talk_title: High-plex 3D microscopy for spatial profiling of tumor microenvironments

models.name: light sheet fluorescence microscopy volumes
models.genotype_or_key_features: up to 2 mm thick human FFPE blocks

MOA_summary: The presenter described cyclic immunofluorescence–based high‑plex 3D microscopy (up to 150 channels) applied to thick (35 µm) FFPE sections and light‑sheet fluorescence imaging, enabling whole‑cell morphology, subcellular organelle localization, and spatial context (e.g., cytotoxic synapse distance, tertiary lymphoid structure architecture) to be captured in three dimensions, which resolves misidentification and provides functional readouts that are impossible in standard 2D sections.
key_findings: Thick (35‑40 µm) FFPE sections retain ~70 % of cells intact versus <5 % in standard 5 µm sections, dramatically reducing lineage misidentification.
3D imaging revealed that ~40 % of CD8+ T cells in tumors exhibit LAG‑3 staining only in deeper z‑planes, explaining false‑negative checkpoint marker detection in 2D slides.
Morphological analysis showed average T cells make 5–10 functional contacts with neighbors; synapse distances contract from ~1 µm to ~30 nm during cytolytic engagement.

Serial reconstruction of ~470 tertiary lymphoid structures in lung adenocarcinoma and tuberculosis demonstrated heterogeneous developmental stages within a single follicle, which 2D sections misclassify.
Light‑sheet 3D imaging of a 2 mm‑thick colon specimen captured ~30 million cells, allowing tracing of nerve‑immune interactions and vascular‑associated immune niches.

talk_title: AI approaches to quantitative analysis of 3D tissue imaging
MOA_summary: The talk outlined how foundation models, segmentation networks, synthetic‑staining GANs and diffusion models, and optical‑flow based slice interpolation are applied to 2D and 3D histopathology data (H&E, cyclic IF, light‑sheet) to generate cell‑level segmentations, virtual multiplex stains, and fill missing z‑planes, thereby enabling quantitative morphometric and spatial analyses that were previously infeasible.
readouts: synthetic multiplex IHC generated by diffusion models
key_findings: Foundation models can embed H&E tiles into high‑dimensional vectors that predict patient outcomes and highlight prognostic regions such as venous invasion without additional training.
A GAN‑based pipeline converts quantitative phase images into realistic H&E, allowing existing H&E‑trained models to be applied to label‑free imaging.
Diffusion models successfully synthesize multiplex IHC (e.g., insulin/glucagon) from a single H&E slide, enabling retrospective multi‑omic analysis of archived specimens.
Optical‑flow algorithms reconstruct missing intermediate sections in CODA and Visium datasets, improving continuity for 3D reconstructions and spatial transcriptomics.
AI‑driven nucleus and tissue segmentation using models like Segment‑Anything achieve >90 % concordance with manual annotations across multiple organ systems.

talk_title: Integrating Digital Pathology, AI, and Molecular Diagnostics for Biomarker Development

biomarkers.name: MSI-high
biomarkers.finding: AI model achieved AUROC ~0.9 for MSI prediction from H&E

biomarkers.name: ALK fusion
biomarkers.finding: Clinical assay sensitivity 100%, specificity 99%, PPV 100%

MOA_summary: John Ifraty reviewed how AI image embeddings derived from H&E slides can capture morphologic correlates of actionable mutations (e.g., ALK fusions, MSI, EGFR) and discussed the need for clinical‑grade performance (high sensitivity, specificity, PPV). He highlighted that current AI models achieve AUROC ~0.9 for MSI but only ~0.6‑0.7 for most other mutations, emphasizing confounders, sample quality, and rare tumor entities as major barriers. He also described emerging multimodal and spatial platforms that combine H&E, multiplex immunofluorescence, and spatial transcriptomics to generate richer biomarkers, while noting throughput and reproducibility challenges.
key_findings: Clinical ALK FISH assay demonstrated 100% sensitivity, 99% specificity, and 100% PPV in validation against RNA‑Seq, setting a high bar for AI‑based predictors.
AI model trained on public colorectal cancer slides achieved AUROC ~0.9 for MSI prediction, the highest performance among mutation‑prediction tasks.
Predictive performance for EGFR, KRAS, PIK3CA, and other mutations ranged from AUROC 0.5‑0.7, indicating limited clinical utility at present.
Foundation models fine‑tuned on institution‑specific data (e.g., Campanella et al.) can raise EGFR prediction AUROC to ~0.9 and enable triage of lung cancer cases for rapid molecular testing.
ctDNA assay (Guardant360) identified ALK fusions in only ~50% of cases, illustrating that sub‑optimal sensitivity can still be useful for longitudinal monitoring.
Spatial multiplex IF and transcriptomics platforms (e.g., Singular Genomics 18‑plex IF + 500‑plex RNA) now achieve 10 samples per slide throughput, moving toward clinical feasibility.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Confounding variables such as biopsy size, sample type (resection vs. fluid cytology), and co‑occurring biomarkers (e.g., BRAF with MSI) can inflate AI performance.
Rare tumor entities (170 soft‑tissue sarcoma subtypes) are under‑represented in training data, limiting model generalizability.
AI models may learn non‑biological shortcuts (e.g., ruler presence in skin lesion images) that do not translate to robust clinical biomarkers.
open_questions: How can standardized pre‑analytical corrections be implemented to improve reproducibility of multiplex immunofluorescence across institutions?
What minimal performance metrics (AUROC, AUPRC, PPV) are required for AI‑based mutation predictors to be adopted in clinical decision support?
Can multimodal models that integrate radiology, H&E, IF, and spatial transcriptomics achieve clinically actionable predictive power for immunotherapy response?

talk_title: Integrating Epidemiology and Basic Science to Elucidate Prostate Cancer Inflammation, Telomere Biomarkers, Statin and Digoxin Repurposing

speaker.name: Elizabeth A. Platz
speaker.affiliation: Johns Hopkins Bloomberg School of Public Health, Department of Epidemiology; Joint appointments in Oncology and Urology, Johns Hopkins University School of Medicine

targets.dependency_context: prostate cancer cells with high telomere length variability

biomarkers.name: Long‑term statin use
biomarkers.finding: associated with reduced risk of metastatic prostate cancer and prostate cancer death (HR approx. 0.5 in pooled analyses)

biomarkers.name: Long‑term digoxin use
biomarkers.finding: inverse association with organ‑confined and lethal prostate cancer; risk declines with longer duration

biomarkers.name: Telomere length variability in cancer cells + short stromal telomeres
biomarkers.assay: fluorescence in situ telomere length assay
biomarkers.finding: 14‑fold increased risk of prostate cancer death compared with low‑risk category

biomarkers.name: CMV seropositivity
biomarkers.finding: positively associated with greater percent area of intraprostatic inflammation

models.name: Health Professionals Follow‑up Study
models.genotype_or_key_features: men with clinically localized prostate cancer, tissue microarrays available

models.name: Prostate Cancer Prevention Trial (PCPT)
models.genotype_or_key_features: end‑of‑study biopsies, finasteride vs placebo arms

models.name: SELECT trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: men from PCPT used as baseline for prospective inflammation analysis

models.name: ARIC cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: long‑term statin and cholesterol data, incident and fatal prostate cancer outcomes

clinical_data.n: n=~2,000 men in HPFS with tissue data
clinical_data.population: men with high telomere variability in cancer cells and short stromal telomeres

MOA_summary: Platz integrates population‑based epidemiology with pathology and molecular assays to show that intraprostatic inflammation, driven in part by infections such as CMV, increases prostate cancer risk; telomere length variability in cancer cells combined with short stromal telomeres predicts lethal disease; and long‑term use of statins or digoxin is inversely associated with aggressive prostate cancer, suggesting drug‑repurposing opportunities.
readouts: fluorescence in situ telomere length assay
key_findings: Intraprostatic inflammation measured in unbiased end‑of‑study biopsies is prospectively associated with increased prostate cancer risk (dose‑response with percent inflamed area).
CMV seropositivity is positively associated with greater intraprostatic inflammation in the PCPT cohort.
High variability of telomere length among cancer cells together with short stromal telomeres confers a 14‑fold higher risk of prostate cancer death, replicated at ~5‑fold in independent cohorts.
Long‑term statin use is linked to a ~50% reduction in metastatic prostate cancer and death, independent of cholesterol levels.
Long‑term digoxin use shows a similar inverse association with both organ‑confined and lethal prostate cancer.
Lower stromal telomere length correlates with reduced lamin B1 expression, supporting a senescent fibroblast hypothesis.
open_questions: What molecular mechanisms link statin‑induced cholesterol lowering to reduced prostate cancer aggressiveness?
Can the telomere variability biomarker be translated into a clinically feasible assay?
What is the causal pathway by which digoxin reduces prostate cancer risk—ion‑transport inhibition or off‑target effects?

talk_title: Highlights of Recent Clinical Trials in Precision Oncology and Emerging Therapies

speaker.name: Dr. Kathy Dombrowsk
speaker.affiliation: University of California, San Francisco, Department of Oncology

targets.dependency_context: KRAS G12C mutant NSCLC after prior KRAS G12C inhibitor
FBXW7‑mutant ovarian cancer with cyclin E1 amplification
Combination with T‑DXd in HER2‑positive tumors
Folate receptor‑expressing endometrial and ovarian cancers

biomarkers.name: ctDNA clearance
biomarkers.finding: 73% of patients treated with zoldonrasib cleared ctDNA

biomarkers.name: Cyclin E1 amplification
biomarkers.finding: Associated with 60% ORR to VE‑1 + PKMYT combination in ovarian cancer

models.name: ZOLDONRASIB‑NSCLC Phase 2
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS G12D mutant NSCLC, prior chemo/IO, n=40

models.name: DERAXONE RASIB PAN‑RAS PANCREATIC Phase 1/2
models.genotype_or_key_features: Metastatic KRAS‑mutant pancreatic adenocarcinoma, gemcitabine + nab‑paclitaxel, n not specified

models.name: ELACESTRANT G12C NSCLC Expansion
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS G12C mutant NSCLC after prior G12C inhibitor, n not specified

models.name: VE‑1 + PKMYT OVARIAN Study
models.genotype_or_key_features: FBXW7‑mutant / cyclin E1‑amplified ovarian cancer, n not specified

models.name: T‑DXd + Olaparib Combination
models.genotype_or_key_features: HER2‑positive solid tumors, sequential dosing, n not specified

models.name: CLDN6‑ADC OVARIAN Trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: Claudin‑6 expressing ovarian cancer, n not specified

models.name: ZW91 Folate Receptor ADC Trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: Folate receptor low‑expressing endometrial/ovarian cancer, n not specified

models.name: CILTA‑CEL SMOLDERING MYELOMA
models.genotype_or_key_features: High‑risk smoldering multiple myeloma, n not specified

clinical_data.population: KRAS G12C mutant NSCLC after prior G12C inhibitor
FBXW7‑mutant ovarian cancer with cyclin E1 amplification
High‑risk smoldering multiple myeloma treated with cilta‑cel

MOA_summary: KRAS G12D covalent inhibitor zoldonrasib forms a tricomplex that blocks RAS‑RAF signaling, achieving high ORR and ctDNA clearance in NSCLC. Pan‑RAS PROTAC deraxone rasib degrades mutant and wild‑type RAS, synergizing with gemcitabine/nab‑paclitaxel in pancreatic cancer. VE‑1 and PKMYT inhibitors arrest G2/M by targeting CDK1, showing activity in FBXW7‑mutant ovarian cancer with cyclin E1 amplification. ADCs (T‑DXd, CLDN6‑ADC, ZW91) deliver topoisomerase or DNA‑damaging payloads, enhanced by sequential PARP inhibition. CAR‑T (cilta‑cel) provides durable MRD‑negative remissions in smoldering myeloma. Emerging targets (CCR8, mesothelin) showed limited efficacy, highlighting resistance mechanisms.
key_findings: Zoldonrasib achieved a 52% ORR and median PFS of 11 months in KRAS G12D mutant NSCLC (n=40) with only minimal grade 3 toxicities.
Deraxone rasib combined with gemcitabine/nab‑paclitaxel yielded a 59% ORR and 90% disease control in first‑line metastatic KRAS‑mutant pancreatic cancer, albeit with 73% grade 3+ adverse events.
Elacestrant produced a 32% partial response rate in KRAS G12C NSCLC patients who had progressed on prior G12C inhibitors.
VE‑1 plus PKMYT inhibition resulted in a 60% ORR in FBXW7‑mutant ovarian cancer harboring cyclin E1 amplification, demonstrating target‑driven efficacy.
Sequential combination of trastuzumab deruxtecan with olaparib achieved a 54% overall response rate, supporting schedule‑optimized ADC‑PARP synergy.
Claudin‑6 and folate receptor ADCs each generated >50% response rates across multiple tumor types, even in low‑expressing cohorts.
Cilta‑cel induced 100% MRD negativity and objective responses in high‑risk smoldering myeloma with no grade 3+ CRS, indicating curative potential in pre‑malignancy.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Mesothelin CAR‑T trials showed low response rates due to tumor microenvironment immunosuppression and T‑cell exhaustion.
CCR8‑targeting antibodies yielded single‑digit response rates, suggesting inadequate target engagement or compensatory pathways.

combination_strategies.rationale: Chemotherapy backbone may enhance tumor dependence on RAS signaling, improving PROTAC efficacy
combination_strategies.phenotype: 59% ORR, 90% DCR in pancreatic cancer

combination_strategies.rationale: Topoisomerase‑based ADC creates DNA lesions that are synthetically lethal with PARP inhibition
combination_strategies.phenotype: 54% ORR with sequential dosing

combination_strategies.rationale: Dual blockade of EGFR and KRAS G12C may overcome resistance mechanisms
combination_strategies.phenotype: Partial responses observed in KRAS G12C colorectal and pancreatic cancers

open_questions: What molecular features define the subset of KRAS G12D NSCLC patients who achieve durable ctDNA clearance?
How can the high grade 3+ toxicity of pan‑RAS PROTAC combos be mitigated while preserving efficacy?
What biomarkers predict response to VE‑1/PKMYT therapy beyond cyclin E1 amplification?
Can ADC payload diversification overcome the class‑wide resistance observed with topoisomerase‑based ADCs?

session_title: AACR/ASCO Joint Session: Next Generation of CAR-T Cell Therapies
talk_title: In Vivo CAR T Cell Platforms and Paradigms

biomarkers.name: MRD
biomarkers.finding: Patients treated with in vivo BCMA CAR-T achieved MRD negative status at day 60.

clinical_data.value: stringent complete response or very good partial response
5/5 patients had grade 3 adverse events (3 grade 3 CRS, 1 grade 2 CRS, 1 grade 1 ICANS)

MOA_summary: The speaker proposes shifting from ex vivo CAR-T manufacturing to in vivo generation using targeted viral-like particles (VLPs) or lipid nanoparticles (LNPs). By blinding the VSV glycoprotein and adding targeting ligands (e.g., anti-CD7 or anti-TCR), the system selectively transduces T cells in vivo, bypassing the need for lymphodepleting chemotherapy and complex GMP facilities.
key_findings: In vivo BCMA CAR-T generation via TCR-targeted VLPs led to stringent complete or very good partial responses in 4 out of 5 multiple myeloma patients.
In vivo BCMA CAR-T therapy showed a bimodal toxicity profile: an initial infusion reaction followed by traditional cytokine release syndrome (CRS).
mRNA-LNP delivery of anti-CD19 CARs achieved transient B-cell aplasia and reduction in SLEDAI scores in refractory SLE patients.
Preclinical NHP data showed persistent B-cell aplasia following CD7-targeted VLP delivery of CAR20, though anti-CAR antibodies developed in 7-14 days.
Calculations suggest that only 0.05% to 2% transduction efficiency of total body T cells is required to reach the therapeutic dose of ~200 million CAR-positive cells.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Immunogenicity of the CAR construct can lead to the development of anti-CAR antibodies, which may cause B-cells to return (observed in NHP models).
open_questions: Whether tissue-resident immune effector cells in lymphoid organs are better suited for cancer immunotherapy than circulating blood T cells.
The exact cause of early inflammatory toxicity in in vivo VLP delivery (innate immune response vs. CD3 cross-linking).

session_title: AACR/ASCO Joint Session: Next Generation of CAR-T Cell Therapies
talk_title: CAR T Cells for Pediatric Brain Tumors

biomarkers.name: anti-CAR antibodies
biomarkers.finding: Anti-CAR antibody levels in plasma were inversely related to CAR T cell persistence and directly impeded CAR killing.

biomarkers.name: cell-free DNA
biomarkers.finding: cfDNA recurrence correlated with the disappearance of circulating CAR T cells.

models.name: Arm A, B, and C trials
models.genotype_or_key_features: Pediatric patients with H3-mutant diffuse midline glioma

clinical_data.value: favorable median overall survival (compared to historical 9-11 months)

MOA_summary: The study evaluates GD2-targeted CAR T cells for H3-mutant diffuse midline gliomas. The research demonstrates that lymphodepletion is critical for CAR T persistence; without it, patients develop robust anti-CAR antibody and T-cell responses (including IgA in the CNS) that reject the therapy. Re-introducing lymphodepletion every four cycles (Arm C) restored durable responses.
key_findings: Intracerebroventricular (ICV) delivery is 10-fold more potent than IV delivery in preclinical models.
Arm A (IV + ICV + lymphodepletion) showed significant tumor shrinkage and improved OS, but high-grade CRS occurred after IV infusion.
Arm B (ICV only, no lymphodepletion) resulted in transient responses (<3 months) due to rapid immune rejection of CAR T cells.
Anti-CAR responses include CD4+ (IL-2, IFN-gamma) and CD8+ (degranulating, 4-1BB+) T cells, as well as IgA antibodies produced by B cells within the CNS.
Arm C (ICV + lymphodepletion every 4 cycles) resulted in durable responses with median OS not reached and a plateau of >50% survival.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Immunogenicity of the mouse-derived scFv and junctional domains (4-1BB/zeta) triggers robust host immune rejection.
IgA antibodies in the CNS suggest that the immune response is initiated within the CNS or peri-CNS tissues.

combination_strategies.rationale: Prevent the emergence of anti-CAR antibodies by depleting B cells
combination_strategies.phenotype: too early to present data

open_questions: Whether myeloid cells in the CNS contribute to a second axis of resistance.
Whether stealth platforms (used in allogeneic CARs) could prevent rejection in autologous CARs for solid tumors.

session_title: AACR/ASCO Joint Session: Next Generation of CAR-T Cell Therapies
talk_title: Precision Targeted Engineering of CAR T Cells
MOA_summary: The speaker describes a CRISPR HDR knock-in strategy to create 'armored' CAR T cells that secrete pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-12, IL-2) under the control of endogenous tumor-specific promoters (NR4A2, RGS16). This approach ensures that potent cytokines are expressed only at the tumor site, reducing systemic toxicity compared to NFAT-inducible promoters while enhancing T cell polyfunctionality and host anti-tumor immunity.
key_findings: NR4A2 is highly tumor-restricted and safe for driving high-toxicity transgenes like IL-12, whereas RGS16 is more potent but less restricted.
IL-12 driven by NR4A2 showed potent anti-tumor effects without the systemic weight loss seen with RGS16 or PD-1 promoters.
IL-2 driven by the RGS16 promoter showed the strongest anti-tumor effect and was well-tolerated.
A 'next-generation' knock-in strategy using a P2A linker to preserve endogenous RGS16 expression significantly reduced peripheral GFP expression while maintaining high tumor expression.
The one-step CRISPR knock-in of both the armoring gene and the CAR into the TRAC locus increased CAR expression without reducing transgene output.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Disruption of the RGS16 gene through standard CRISPR knock-in led to a loss of gene regulation and increased peripheral expression; this was corrected using a P2A linker strategy.
open_questions: Whether this strategy can be used to deliver chemokines (CXCL9, CXCL10) to increase T cell trafficking to the tumor.

talk_title: Mechanistic insights into CTLA-4 blockade and novel immune structures

speaker.name: Dr. James P. Allison
speaker.affiliation: University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center, James P. Allison Institute

biomarkers.name: ICOS-positive CD4 T cells
biomarkers.finding: Presence of ICOS+ CD4 cells after anti‑CTLA‑4 treatment correlated with response; no responders lacked these cells

biomarkers.name: Tetrad structures (CD4 ICOS+, CD8, dendritic cell)
biomarkers.finding: Tetrads were enriched in responders to anti‑CTLA‑4 and absent in non‑responders

models.name: ipilimumab Phase III melanoma long‑term follow‑up

clinical_data.metric: overall survival at 3 years (combined CTLA‑4 + PD‑1)

clinical_data.population: bladder cancer patients receiving neoadjuvant anti‑CTLA‑4

MOA_summary: Blocking CTLA‑4 removes its high‑affinity competition with CD28 for B7 ligands, restoring costimulatory signaling during T‑cell priming. This generates a unique ICOS‑high CD4 effector subset that collaborates with ICOS‑ligand‑expressing dendritic cells to form spatial 'tetrads', which remodel the myeloid compartment and prime stem‑like TCF7+ CD8 T cells with high granzyme expression. The resulting effector‑only immune infiltrate underlies durable tumor regressions and distinguishes CTLA‑4 blockade from PD‑1 inhibition.
key_findings: Anti‑CTLA‑4 treatment expands ICOS‑positive CD4 T cells; absence of these cells predicts lack of response.
CTLA‑4 blockade induces novel spatial 'tetrad' structures (CD4 ICOS+, CD8, dendritic cell) that are enriched in responders and absent with PD‑1 blockade.
In CTLA‑4 knockout mice, CD4 cells co‑express multiple lineage transcription factors (BCL6, GATA3, RORγC), indicating deregulated differentiation.
Adding ICOS‑ligand to a tumor vaccine converts a non‑curative anti‑CTLA‑4 regimen into 100% tumor eradication in a resistant B16 model.
Combination of anti‑CTLA‑4 with anti‑PD‑1 shifts the exhausted CD8 population to a stem‑like TCF7+ phenotype and improves survival (>50% at 3 years in melanoma).
Long‑term follow‑up of ipilimumab trials shows ~20% of melanoma patients achieve 10‑year survival, whereas median pre‑trial survival was 6–7 months.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: PD‑1 monotherapy expands terminally exhausted CD8 cells and shows higher relapse rates (25‑35% at 2 years) compared with CTLA‑4.
CTLA‑4 blockade rarely leads to disease recurrence, but efficacy depends on intact ICOS–ICOSL signaling.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target complementary phases of T‑cell response (priming vs exhaustion) to achieve synergistic tumor regression.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved overall survival (>50% at 3 years) and increased effector CD4/CD8 populations.

combination_strategies.rationale: Provide the necessary ICOS co‑stimulatory signal to amplify CTLA‑4‑induced CD4 effectors.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Conversion of non‑responsive anti‑CTLA‑4 treatment to 100% cure in resistant B16 mouse model.

open_questions: What specific role do macrophages play in the formation and function of the CTLA‑4‑induced tetrads?
Can the tetrad architecture be leveraged as a predictive biomarker in human cancers beyond melanoma?
How can checkpoint strategies be adapted for pediatric fusion‑driven cancers that may lack immunogenic neoantigens?

talk_title: Circular RNAs in cancer: functional landscape and therapeutic potential

speaker.name: Dr. He
speaker.affiliation: Princess Margaret Cancer Center, Department of Medical Biophysics, University of Toronto

targets.dependency_context: neuroendocrine prostate cancer and small cell lung cancer

biomarkers.name: circRMST plasma ratio
biomarkers.finding: circRMST detected at >1,000‑fold higher circular/linear ratio in neuroendocrine tumor plasma versus healthy control

models.name: neuroendocrine prostate cancer GEM model (TKO background)
neuroendocrine prostate cancer and small cell lung cancer lines

MOA_summary: Circular RMST (circRMST) is highly expressed in neuroendocrine prostate and small‑cell lung cancers, where it binds transcription factors NKX2.1 and SOX2, stabilizing NKX2.1 protein by blocking Hsp70‑mediated lysosomal degradation. Loss of circRMST reduces NKX2.1 half‑life, down‑regulates ASL1, and blocks lineage transdifferentiation and tumor growth in mouse models. The covalently closed structure also confers exceptional stability in plasma, enabling biomarker and vaccine applications.
key_findings: Identified ~80,000 circRNAs in 200 prostate cancers; average 7,000 per tumor; up to 400,000 circRNAs cataloged genome‑wide.
Functional screen of 10,000 circRNAs across seven cancer types revealed ~1,300 essential circRNAs, >90% previously uncharacterized and >95% act independently of linear host gene.
circRMST is >100‑fold enriched in neuroendocrine prostate cancer and small‑cell lung cancer versus adenocarcinoma, and is the dominant transcript from its locus.
CRISPR deletion of intronic MIR repeats reduces circRMST expression to ~30% and impairs tumor cell viability in vitro and xenograft growth in vivo.

circRMST knockdown shortens NKX2.1 protein half‑life from 17 h to ~5 h via loss of Hsp70‑binding site protection, leading to loss of ASL1 expression and blockade of neuroendocrine transdifferentiation.
circRMST is detectable in patient plasma with a >1,000‑fold higher circular/linear ratio than in tumor tissue, suggesting a liquid‑biopsy biomarker.
Circular RNA translation can generate novel peptides; immunopeptidomics identified 80 circRNA‑derived peptides, including 12 from reverse‑strand translation, providing new vaccine antigens.
open_questions: Does the circular‑to‑linear RNA ratio vary systematically across disease states and could it serve as a quantitative biomarker?
Can circular RNAs replicate or be amplified in cells, analogous to viroid mechanisms?
What are the functional consequences of the 700+ circRMST‑interacting RNAs identified in the interactome?

talk_title: Developing CAR T‑cell therapies for T‑cell malignancies

speaker.name: Dr. John F. DiPersio
speaker.affiliation: Washington University School of Medicine, St. Louis, Director of the Section of Cellular Therapy and the Center for Gene and Cellular Immunotherapy

biomarkers.name: CD7 loss
biomarkers.finding: Observed in ~20% of relapsed patients after UCART7 therapy, often due to biallelic deletion or splicing abnormalities

biomarkers.name: CD2 loss
biomarkers.finding: Most relapsed cases after CD2‑CAR therapy showed loss of CD2 surface expression

biomarkers.name: CR rate
biomarkers.finding: 82% complete remission at the recommended Phase II dose in the first‑in‑human UCART7 study (n not specified)

models.name: UCART7 Phase I study (Blood 2025)
models.genotype_or_key_features: Patients with relapsed/refractory T‑cell ALL, CD7‑high disease

models.name: T‑cell lymphoma xenograft model
models.genotype_or_key_features: CD2‑positive lymphoma cell lines in immunodeficient mice

clinical_data.population: relapsed/refractory T‑cell ALL, Phase I UCART7 cohort

MOA_summary: The work introduces universal allogeneic CAR‑T platforms that multiplex‑edit donor T cells to delete CD7 (and TCRα) for T‑ALL and to delete CD2 (and TCRα) for T‑cell lymphomas, enabling potent antigen‑specific killing while avoiding fratricide. Epitope‑masking of CD2 or incorporation of the CD2 cytoplasmic tail restores costimulatory signaling lost after CD2 knockout. Long‑acting IL‑7 fusion (NTi‑7) administered early after infusion dramatically expands CAR‑T persistence and cures murine models. Small‑molecule PI3γδ and JAK inhibitors, or CD40L blockade, suppress IL‑6–driven CRS without impairing cytotoxicity.
key_findings: Multiplex CRISPR editing of donor T cells to delete CD7 and TCRα enables universal CAR‑T cells that achieved an 82% complete remission rate in the first‑in‑human UCART7 Phase I trial for relapsed T‑ALL.

Deletion of CD2 in CAR‑T cells caused severe functional exhaustion in vitro and loss of antitumor activity in vivo, which was rescued by epitope‑masking of CD2 or by inserting the CD2 cytoplasmic domain into the CAR construct.

Long‑acting IL‑7 (NTi‑7) given between days 1‑7 after CAR‑T infusion expands CAR‑T cells 10,000‑ to 100,000‑fold and cures murine models of T‑cell lymphoma, B‑cell lymphoma, and AML, whereas later dosing is ineffective.

A high‑throughput screen of 700 kinase inhibitors identified PI3γδ inhibitor (Devolasib) and JAK1/2 inhibitor (Ruxolitinib) as synergistic suppressors of IL‑6 production without impairing CAR‑T cytotoxicity; combined prophylaxis eliminated grade ≥2 CRS in a randomized patient cohort.

CD4⁺ CAR‑T cells are the primary source of IL‑6 via membrane‑bound CD40L, while CD8⁺ cells mediate killing; CD40L knockout or blockade markedly reduces CRS, highlighting a mechanistic link between CD40L signaling and cytokine release.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Relapse after UCART7 is frequently associated with loss of CD7 surface expression through biallelic deletion or splicing alterations.
CD2‑CAR‑T cells lose efficacy when CD2 is knocked out because CD2 provides essential costimulatory signaling; epitope‑masked CD2 restores function.
Targeting CD7 and CD2 depletes normal NK and T cells, leading to profound immunodeficiency and infection risk.

combination_strategies.rationale: Early cytokine support enhances CAR‑T expansion, persistence, and antitumor efficacy
combination_strategies.phenotype: 10,000‑100,000‑fold CAR‑T expansion; complete tumor eradication in murine models

combination_strategies.rationale: Dual inhibition suppresses IL‑6–driven CRS without compromising CAR‑T cytotoxicity
combination_strategies.phenotype: Near‑complete abrogation of grade ≥2 CRS in randomized patient cohort; maintained tumor killing

open_questions: How can the profound immunodeficiency caused by CD7/CD2 depletion be mitigated while preserving antitumor activity?
What are the molecular mechanisms underlying CD7 downregulation in relapsed patients (e.g., splice variants vs. genomic loss)?
Can the optimal timing and dosing of IL‑7 be refined for different CAR‑T products and disease settings?

talk_title: Open-access chemical probes and Target 2035 initiative for the druggable proteome

speaker.name: Dr. Cheryl H. Arrowsmith
speaker.affiliation: Senior Scientist, Princess Margaret Cancer Centre; Professor, University of Toronto

targets.dependency_context: MLL-rearranged leukemia, p53 gain-of-function breast cancer
mutant ALL and multiple myeloma cells

MOA_summary: The SGC develops open-access chemical probes that selectively inhibit epigenetic regulators such as WDR5, PRMT5, PRMT1 and NSD2. Probe binding disrupts protein–protein interactions or catalytic activity, leading to transcriptional reprogramming, loss of oncogenic histone marks, and selective growth suppression in cancer models dependent on these epigenetic pathways. A PROTAC-derived NSD2 degrader recruits FBXO22, forming a covalent adduct that eliminates NSD2 protein and its H3K36me2 mark, restoring dexamethasone sensitivity in mutant ALL cells.
key_findings: Developed ~200 high‑quality chemical probes, including 50–70 epigenetic modulators, all freely available to the community.
Identified OICR-9429 as a potent, selective WDR5‑MLL interaction inhibitor that suppresses growth of MLL‑rearranged leukemia and p53‑gain‑of‑function breast cancer cells.
Discovered a PROTAC that recruits FBXO22 to degrade NSD2, eliminating H3K36me2 and selectively killing NSD2‑mutant ALL and multiple myeloma cells after 18–21 days.
NSD2 degradation restored dexamethasone sensitivity in mutant ALL cell lines, suggesting a combinatorial therapeutic strategy.
Machine‑learning‑guided DNA‑encoded library screens across 18 WDR proteins yielded low‑micromolar, ligand‑efficient hits for ~1/3 of the targets, expanding the druggable proteome.

combination_strategies.rationale: NSD2 degradation re‑sensitizes mutant ALL cells to standard steroid therapy
combination_strategies.phenotype: restored dexamethasone‑induced cell death in NSD2‑mutant lines

talk_title: Epigenomic heterogeneity and chromatin variants in metastatic castration‑resistant prostate cancer

speaker.name: Matthew Loupiain
speaker.affiliation: University of California, San Diego, Department of Medicine

models.name: 71 mCRPC patients
models.genotype_or_key_features: LINE1+, SETL, SINE1+, constant subgroups; AR amplification in LINE1+

clinical_data.population: LINE1+ mCRPC patients treated with AR selective inhibitors

MOA_summary: Chromatin variant analysis using ATAC‑seq identified repetitive element‑driven accessible regions that recruit AR, FOXA1 and other transcription factors, forming de novo regulatory elements that amplify AR expression in LINE1+ metastatic castration‑resistant prostate cancer, leading to intrinsic resistance to AR‑targeted therapy.
key_findings: Unsupervised clustering of ATAC‑seq data from 71 mCRPC samples defined four subgroups based on repetitive element chromatin variants (LINE1+, SETL, SINE1+, constant).
LINE1+ subgroup showed intrinsic resistance to androgen‑receptor selective inhibitors, with markedly shorter survival.
AR locus amplification (up to >150 copies) and ecDNA positivity were enriched in LINE1+ tumors.
Repetitive element‑derived accessible sites in LINE1+ tumors physically interact with the AR promoter (Hi‑C), recruiting AR, FOXA1, and OCTB13.
Chromatin variant classification did not align with gene‑expression‑based subtypes, highlighting orthogonal heterogeneity.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: LINE1+ tumors are intrinsically resistant to AR‑selective inhibitors due to extreme AR overexpression driven by repeat‑derived enhancers.
open_questions: Do repeat‑derived chromatin variants directly generate ecDNA, or are they a consequence of ecDNA formation?
What upstream mechanisms (e.g., p53 loss, metabolic stress) drive the emergence of LINE1+ chromatin variant profiles?

talk_title: SMARCA4 loss drives cell‑state disorder and dedifferentiated endometrial cancer

speaker.name: Unspecified presenter (endometrial cancer work)
speaker.affiliation: University of Alberta, Department of Oncology

models.name: KRAS‑LKB1 mouse lung adenocarcinoma organoids (used for parallel studies)
microdissected well‑differentiated vs dedifferentiated regions of DDEC
models.genotype_or_key_features: SMARCA4 loss, ARID1B loss in dedifferentiated component

models.name: SMARCA4‑KO and WT endometrial cancer cells serially passaged in mice

MOA_summary: Loss of SMARCA4 disrupts the SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex, leading to global DNA hypomethylation and stochastic chromatin accessibility changes that prevent cells from stabilizing in a differentiated state, resulting in histologic dedifferentiation and therapy resistance in endometrial cancer.
key_findings: 80% of dedifferentiated endometrial cancer (DDEC) cases show loss of SMARCA4 and/or ARID1B, components of the SWI/SNF complex.

CRISPR‑mediated SMARCA4 knockout in endometrial cancer cells reduces in‑vitro tumorigenicity but, after serial in‑vivo passaging, restores growth and yields mixed well‑ and poorly‑differentiated histology.
SMARCA4‑deficient tumors display global DNA hypomethylation and distinct chromatin accessibility patterns compared with wild‑type.
Single‑cell multi‑omics reveal that SMARCA4 loss increases cell‑state plasticity, generating an uncommitted transcriptional state without a stem‑cell or embryonic signature.
Patient DDEC samples retain heterogeneous E‑cadherin expression, supporting a model of cell‑state disorder rather than true dedifferentiation.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: SMARCA4‑deficient tumors are resistant to carboplatin chemotherapy in vivo.
open_questions: What upstream signals (e.g., hypoxia, metabolic stress) trigger SMARCA4 loss‑induced chromatin variants?
Can epigenetic drugs restore chromatin stability and re‑sensitize SMARCA4‑deficient DDEC to chemotherapy?

talk_title: LKB1 loss‑driven adenocarcinoma‑to‑squamous transition (AST) and therapeutic vulnerabilities in KRAS‑mutant lung cancer

speaker.name: Hongjun Ji
speaker.affiliation: University of Texas Southwestern, Department of Oncology

biomarkers.name: KLK6A
biomarkers.finding: high KLK6A expression correlates with shorter treatment duration and poor survival in LKB1‑mutant lung adenocarcinoma

models.name: KRAS‑G12D; LKB1‑KO (KL) genetically engineered mouse model
CHRISTOPHER‑1 trial lung cancer samples (n≈8)
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS G12C or EGFR mutant, LKB1 status known

clinical_data.value: shorter in patients with high squamous signature

MOA_summary: LKB1 loss elevates ROS, transiently activating NF‑κB (RELA) which sustains adenocarcinoma survival; prolonged stress leads to a double‑positive p63⁺/RELA⁺ state that secretes IL‑1α, reprograms CAFs to a leukocyte‑like phenotype, induces IL‑1β‑driven neutrophil NET formation, and drives squamous transdifferentiation (AST) that confers resistance to KRAS‑G12C inhibition; combined NF‑κB inhibition, ROS modulation, and IL‑1 blockade synergize with KRAS‑G12C inhibitor to prevent or eradicate squamous lesions.
key_findings: LKB1 loss in KRAS‑mutant mouse models induces spontaneous adenocarcinoma‑to‑squamous transition (AST) that is enriched after targeted therapy.
High squamous transcriptional signature predicts shorter treatment duration in LKB1‑mutant lung cancer patients.
KLK6A expression serves as a predictive biomarker for poor response to aggressive therapy in LKB1‑deficient tumors.
ROS accumulation activates NF‑κB; NF‑κB inhibition accelerates p63⁺ squamous differentiation, while ROS scavenging (NAC) reduces RELA activation.
p63⁺ squamous cells secrete IL‑1α, reprogramming CAFs to IL‑1β‑producing leukocyte‑like CAFs that trigger neutrophil NET formation, promoting squamous tumor growth.
Combined KRAS‑G12C inhibitor (IMC‑6236) with IL‑1 receptor antagonist or NET inhibitor (GSK‑484) markedly reduces squamous tumor burden and improves overall tumor control.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: AST confers resistance to KRAS‑G12C inhibition; NF‑κB activation maintains adenocarcinoma lineage but its inhibition promotes squamous transition.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target oncogenic KRAS while preventing emergence of resistant squamous phenotype
combination_strategies.phenotype: tumor regression of adenocarcinoma; squamous lesions persist unless combined with IL‑1 blockade

combination_strategies.rationale: Block IL‑1α/β signaling that drives CAF reprogramming and NET formation supporting squamous growth
combination_strategies.phenotype: reduced squamous tumor number and NETs in KRAS‑LKB1 mouse model

combination_strategies.rationale: Transient NF‑κB activity sustains adenocarcinoma; inhibition accelerates AST, suggesting timing matters for combination
combination_strategies.phenotype: accelerated p63⁺ squamous differentiation; reduced adenocarcinoma survival in vitro

combination_strategies.rationale: Inhibit neutrophil extracellular trap formation that supports squamous tumor growth
combination_strategies.phenotype: decreased squamous tumor burden and NET levels

open_questions: What determines the threshold of ROS/NF‑κB activation that switches from protective to pro‑AST signaling?
Can early detection of the double‑positive p63⁺/RELA⁺ state predict imminent squamous transition in patients?

talk_title: Dendritic cell subsets, cross‑presentation mechanisms, and mRNA vaccine priming

speaker.name: Kenneth Murphy
speaker.affiliation: Washington University School of Medicine, St. Louis, Professor of Pathology and Immunology and Medicine

models.name: IRF8 enhancer deletion
models.genotype_or_key_features: Deletion of 32 kb IRF8 enhancer sites, DC1-deficient

models.name: Wdfy4 germline knockout
models.genotype_or_key_features: Loss of cross‑presentation despite DC1 presence

models.name: CD11c‑DTR depletion
models.genotype_or_key_features: Diphtheria toxin‑mediated ablation of all conventional DCs

models.name: CD11c‑Cre β2M flox
models.genotype_or_key_features: Class I MHC loss on all classical DCs

models.name: Bone marrow chimera (class I‑deficient DCs)
models.genotype_or_key_features: WT host, class I‑deficient DC bone marrow

MOA_summary: Murphy's work defines transcriptional enhancers that program dendritic cell subset differentiation (DC1s via IRF8 enhancer, DC2s via Notch2, Klf4, ZEB2) and identifies WDFY4 and Rab43 as DC1‑specific factors required for vesicular cross‑presentation. Using these mouse models, he shows that conventional DCs are essential for mRNA‑LNP vaccine‑induced CD8 T‑cell priming, but the response is independent of DC1‑specific cross‑presentation pathways and instead relies on cross‑dressing of peptide‑MHC complexes, a process requiring type I IFN and residual class I on DCs.
key_findings: Batf3‑/- mice lacking DC1s show tumor and viral responses as weak as Rag‑deficient mice, demonstrating DC1 necessity for classic cross‑presentation.
Targeted deletion of three IRF8 enhancers abolishes DC1 development, providing a refined genetic tool superior to Batf3 knockout.
WDFY4 is uniquely expressed in DC1s; Wdfy4‑/- mice fail to cross‑present cell‑associated antigens and cannot reject fibrosarcoma, confirming its essential role in vesicular cross‑presentation.
Rab43 co‑localizes with WDFY4 in DC1s and traffics with phagocytosed Listeria, supporting a vesicular, non‑cytosolic cross‑presentation pathway.
mRNA‑LNP vaccination induces CD8 tetramer responses that persist after DC1 or WDFY4 loss, indicating DC1‑independent priming.
Complete depletion of conventional DCs (CD11c‑DTR) abolishes the mRNA‑vaccine response, proving DC dependence but not subset specificity.
Class I MHC loss on DCs (CD11c‑Cre β2M flox) eliminates tumor‑induced cross‑presentation but only partially reduces mRNA‑vaccine responses, implicating cross‑dressing as a major mechanism.
Single‑cell RNA‑seq of tetramer+ CD8 T cells shows that DC1 priming yields fewer but more proliferative effectors, whereas DC2 priming yields broader effector populations.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: DC1‑specific cross‑presentation (WDFY4‑dependent) is not required for mRNA vaccine efficacy, suggesting potential resistance mechanisms if this pathway is impaired.
High doses of class II peptide can divert DC2s to present CD4 epitopes, suppressing tumor rejection, highlighting a dose‑dependent selectivity issue.

combination_strategies.rationale: Leverage both DC1 and DC2 pathways and cross‑dressing to broaden CD8 priming
combination_strategies.phenotype: Robust tetramer response even in DC1‑deficient mice

combination_strategies.rationale: Enhance cross‑dressing and DC activation for mRNA vaccines
combination_strategies.phenotype: Increased CD8 response in class I‑deficient DCs

open_questions: What cell types provide the peptide‑MHC complexes that cross‑dress DCs after mRNA vaccination?
Does DC2‑primed CD8 T‑cell memory differ qualitatively from DC1‑primed memory in durability or tumor control?
How do route, dose, and lipid composition of mRNA‑LNPs influence the balance between direct DC transfection and cross‑dressing?
Could cross‑dressing contribute to autoimmunity (e.g., myocarditis) by presenting self‑peptides?

talk_title: SWI/SNF chromatin remodeling complex vulnerabilities and therapeutic targeting of DCAF5

speaker.name: Dr. Charles W.M. Roberts
speaker.affiliation: St. Jude Children's Research Hospital, Executive Vice President and Director of the Comprehensive Cancer Center

tumor_types: small cell carcinoma of the ovary

models.name: rhabdoid tumor cell lines (SMARCB1-deficient)
models.genotype_or_key_features: SMARCB1 loss, diploid, low mutational burden

models.name: small cell carcinoma of the ovary cell lines (SMARCA4-deficient)
germline DCAF5 knockout mice
models.genotype_or_key_features: Viable, mild neonatal lethality, smaller size

models.name: DCAF5 degrader xenograft tumors
models.genotype_or_key_features: SMARCB1-deficient tumor implants, treated with degrader

MOA_summary: Roberts demonstrated that loss of the SWI/SNF subunit SMARCB1 destabilizes the complex, leading to degradation of residual subunits by the E3 ligase adaptor DCAF5. Genetic or pharmacologic inhibition of DCAF5 restores SWI/SNF complex abundance at enhancers, rescues transcription of developmental genes, and induces tumor regression in SMARCB1-deficient models without major toxicity, establishing DCAF5 as a synthetic‑lethal vulnerability.
readouts: ChIP‑seq for SWI/SNF and histone marks
in vivo tumor growth assays with degrader
key_findings: CRISPR screens of SMARCB1‑deficient rhabdoid tumor lines identified DCAF5 as a highly selective dependency (Q‑value ~10⁻²²).
Mass‑spec revealed that DCAF5 knockdown increases abundance of nine SWI/SNF subunits, confirming DCAF5‑mediated degradation.
DiGly tagging showed direct ubiquitination of multiple SWI/SNF subunits by DCAF5.
DCAF5 loss restores 30‑70% of wild‑type enhancer‑driven transcription in SMARCB1‑null cells, reactivating developmental gene programs.
In vivo degradation of DCAF5 eradicated established SMARCB1‑deficient tumors; no regrowth observed after 100 days.
Germline DCAF5 knockout mice are viable with only mild growth phenotype, indicating a therapeutic window.
Initial small‑molecule binders of DCAF5 have been identified in collaboration with Gray and Fisher labs, enabling a drug‑discovery program.
open_questions: Can DCAF5 inhibition be combined with existing epigenetic therapies (e.g., EZH2 inhibitors) for synergistic tumor control?
What biomarkers predict sensitivity to DCAF5 targeting beyond SMARCB1 loss?
Will chronic DCAF5 inhibition affect normal tissue homeostasis given its mild phenotype in mice?

talk_title: Chromatin priming drives early onset of BRCA1‑heterozygous basal‑like breast cancer

speaker.name: Carmen Li
speaker.affiliation: University of Pennsylvania, Department of Cancer Biology, Basser Center for BRCA

models.name: BRCA1 heterozygous mammary gland mouse model
models.genotype_or_key_features: BRCA1 flox/+, p53 flox/+, Cre‑induced deletion; also PTEN CRISPR version

models.name: tumor organoids from BRCA1‑/‑;p53‑/‑ mice
models.genotype_or_key_features: derived from basal‑like tumors, used for WNT10A knockdown/overexpression

models.name: pre‑malignant alveolar cell organoid model
models.genotype_or_key_features: normal AB cells from WT or heterozygous mice acutely infected with lenti‑Cre

MOA_summary: Li's group generated a BRCA1‑heterozygous mouse model that reveals early chromatin accessibility changes in alveolar luminal progenitor cells, priming AP1‑dependent transcriptional programs and WNT10A activation. Partial inhibition of AP1 delays tumor onset, while WNT10A overexpression accelerates basal‑like tumor formation, establishing these pathways as early drivers of hereditary breast cancer.
key_findings: BRCA1‑heterozygous mice develop basal‑like mammary tumors ~20 weeks earlier than WT mice after Cre‑mediated BRCA1 loss.
ATAC‑seq of normal alveolar cells shows a distinct subcluster with intermediate chromatin openness that aligns with tumor profiles.
AP1 (c‑Jun, JunD, FOSL2) motifs are enriched in these open regions; c‑Jun protein is up‑regulated in early lesions.
Dominant‑negative Jun expression delays tumor formation only in heterozygous mice, indicating AP1 dependency.
WNT10A locus exhibits increased accessibility and expression; WNT10A knockdown impairs organoid growth and in‑vivo tumorigenicity.
WNT10A overexpression accelerates tumor onset and raises tumor grade in mouse cohorts.
RUNX/CBFB transcription factors are up‑regulated in pre‑malignant and tumor cells; CBFB and RUNX2 knockdown reduces proliferation of derived cancer cell lines.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: AP1 inhibition selectively delays tumorigenesis in BRCA1‑heterozygous background but not in WT, suggesting a context‑specific dependency.

combination_strategies.rationale: Block AP1 transcriptional activity to test dependency in early tumorigenesis
combination_strategies.phenotype: Delayed tumor onset in heterozygous mice, no effect in WT

combination_strategies.rationale: Assess requirement of WNT10A for tumor cell fitness
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced sphere formation and tumor growth in mice

open_questions: What upstream stress signals trigger AP1 activation in BRCA1‑heterozygous alveolar cells?
Can pharmacologic AP1 or WNT10A inhibition prevent tumor onset in high‑risk BRCA1 carriers?
How do physiological stressors (pregnancy, obesity, aging) interact with chromatin priming to modulate risk?
What is the contribution of stromal/immune compartments to the early chromatin changes observed?

talk_title: Epigenetic plasticity drives pancreatic cancer metastasis via KLF5 and chromatin modifiers

speaker.name: Andrew P. Feinberg
speaker.affiliation: Johns Hopkins University, Bloomberg Distinguished Professor of Epigenetics

models.name: PDAC primary and metastatic PDX
models.genotype_or_key_features: paired primary tumor and distant lung metastasis

MOA_summary: Feinberg's work shows that epigenetic plasticity, measured as DNA methylation entropy, creates a transitional chromatin state in early pancreatic lesions and in metastatic clones. A CRISPR screen identified KLF5 as a key transcriptional modulator that drives metastasis by up‑regulating epigenetic modifiers (NCAPD2, MTHFD1) leading to loss of heterochromatin (H3K9me2/3) and increased euchromatin, which promotes EMT and tumor cell migration.
key_findings: PanIN lesions display DNA methylation patterns intermediate between acinar and ductal cells, indicating an epigenetically plastic transition zone.
UHRF1 knockdown abolishes soft‑agar growth of ALL cells, linking it to epigenetic entropy in leukemia.
CRISPR screen of metastatic PDAC PDX identified KLF5 as the top gene whose loss impairs metastatic growth but not primary tumor growth.
KLF5 knockdown restores heterochromatin (H3K9me2/3) in metastatic cells and reduces migration in Boyden assays.
KLF5 regulates NCAPD2 and MTHFD1; their knockdown also restores heterochromatin, placing them downstream of KLF5.
High KLF5 expression in human metastatic PDAC correlates with poor survival in TCGA data.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: KLF5 inhibition selectively impairs metastatic but not primary tumor growth in PDX models, suggesting a metastasis‑specific vulnerability.

combination_strategies.rationale: KLF5 inhibition may prevent emergence of gemcitabine‑resistant metastatic clones.
combination_strategies.phenotype: potentially prolonged survival by delaying metastasis development

open_questions: What microenvironmental stress signals trigger KLF5 up‑regulation in primary tumors?
Can circulating nucleosome signatures reflect the epigenetic entropy state of emerging metastases?
Will pharmacologic KLF5 inhibitors be effective in adjuvant settings for PDAC?

cited_papers: Pena‑Ruiz and Fernandez‑Epico 2024 Molecular Cancer editorial

talk_title: From fetal DNA to cancer early detection: innovations in circulating nucleic acids

speaker.name: Dennis Lo
speaker.affiliation: Vice Chancellor and President, The Chinese University of Hong Kong; Li Ka Shing Professor of Medicine, Department of Chemical Pathology

biomarkers.name: Y chromosome PCR signal
biomarkers.finding: Detected in maternal plasma only when fetus is male, confirming fetal origin of cell‑free DNA

biomarkers.name: Chromosome 21 copy number ratio
biomarkers.finding: 100% accurate discrimination of trisomy 21 vs euploid in 28 pregnancies (14 cases each)

biomarkers.name: Plasma EBV DNA concentration
biomarkers.finding: Detected in >90% of nasopharyngeal cancer patients and <5% of healthy controls

biomarkers.name: Fragment size distribution (shorter fragments)
biomarkers.finding: Cancer‑derived cfDNA shows a median size ~150 bp vs 166 bp for normal cfDNA

biomarkers.name: End‑motif signatures (EM5, EM3, PREM, POEM)
biomarkers.finding: Distinct motif enrichment patterns differentiate liver cancer from controls (AUC up to 0.92 when combined)

models.name: NIPT validation cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: Pregnant women, gestational age 10‑12 weeks, n=~2000

models.name: Liver cancer case‑control set
models.genotype_or_key_features: HCC patients vs healthy controls, n=200 each

MOA_summary: Lo demonstrated that cell‑free DNA released by tumors or fetuses can be quantitatively measured using digital PCR, next‑generation sequencing and novel fragmentomics signatures (size, end‑motif, methylation). By exploiting tumor‑specific nucleic acid markers such as EBV DNA in nasopharyngeal carcinoma or chromosome‑specific copy‑number changes in trisomy 21, the approach enables highly accurate non‑invasive prenatal testing and cancer early‑detection, with fragment length and end‑motif patterns providing additional tissue‑origin information.
key_findings: Discovery of cell‑free fetal DNA in maternal plasma enabled non‑invasive prenatal testing with >99% accuracy using digital PCR of chromosome‑specific targets.
A genome‑wide NIPT based on low‑coverage sequencing accurately detected trisomy 21 in 28 pregnancies (100% sensitivity and specificity).

Plasma EBV DNA serves as a highly specific biomarker for nasopharyngeal carcinoma, detecting 34 cancers in a 20,000‑person Hong Kong screen and shifting 70% of cases to stage I‑II with a 10‑fold mortality reduction (3% vs 40%).
Fragmentomics signatures (shorter fragment size, distinct end‑motif patterns) differentiate cancer‑derived cfDNA from normal cfDNA and improve detection AUC to 0.92 for liver cancer.
Integration of fragment size, end‑motif, and methylation data enables tissue‑of‑origin inference, forming the basis of multi‑cancer early detection platforms such as GRAIL’s Galleri test.
open_questions: How does diurnal or physiological variation (exercise, dialysis, menstrual cycle) affect cfDNA concentration and fragmentomics readouts in longitudinal monitoring?
Can histone modification signatures on cfDNA be systematically leveraged to improve tissue‑origin classification?
What are the limits of viral‑derived cfDNA fragmentomics for distinguishing infection‑related cancers versus benign viral carriage?

patent_or_ip_hints: patents on cfDNA fragmentomics and size‑based quantification
licensing agreements between CUHK and Sequenom, later GRAIL

talk_title: Quantitative fluorescence protein assays and selective biomarkers for ADC choice in breast cancer

speaker.name: David Rim
speaker.affiliation: Yale School of Medicine, Department of Pathology and Medicine, Director of Yale Cancer Center Tissue Microarray Facility

targets.dependency_context: Advanced breast cancer treated with ADCs

biomarkers.name: HER2 quantitative fluorescence (attomoles/mm²)
biomarkers.finding: Higher HER2 levels correlated with longer T‑DXd benefit (median OS 12.8 mo vs 7.5 mo, HR per 50‑unit increase 0.70, p<0.05)

biomarkers.name: Trop‑2 quantitative fluorescence (attomoles/mm²)
biomarkers.finding: High Trop‑2 expression associated with poorer outcomes on T‑DXd (high vs low median OS 7.2 mo vs 13.4 mo, p<0.01)

models.name: Yale breast cancer surgical pathology cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 316 prospectively collected breast cancer cases, HER2 IHC scores available

models.name: Retrospective Dana‑Farber T‑DXd cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 51 metastatic breast cancer cases treated with trastuzumab‑deruxtecan

clinical_data.metric: Hazard ratio per 50‑unit increase in HER2 attomoles
clinical_data.population: metastatic breast cancer treated with T‑DXd

clinical_data.value: Top quartile median OS 6.2 mo vs bottom quartile 13.9 mo
clinical_data.population: metastatic breast cancer treated with T‑DXd

MOA_summary: Rim's laboratory developed the Troplex quantitative immunofluorescence platform that converts fluorescence intensity into absolute protein concentration (attomoles/mm²) for HER2, Trop‑2 and other membrane antigens. By standardizing staining with cell‑line calibrators, the assay achieves <10% CV and enables continuous measurement of target density, which correlates with ADC efficacy and informs a proposed selective biomarker framework for choosing between competing HER2‑ or Trop‑2‑directed ADCs.
key_findings: The Troplex assay quantifies HER2 and Trop‑2 in absolute units with inter‑ and intra‑assay CV <10% using cell‑line standards.
In a retrospective Yale cohort (n=316), quantitative HER2 levels distinguished patients likely to benefit from trastuzumab‑deruxtecan, with a hazard ratio of 0.70 per 50‑unit increase (p<0.05).
High Trop‑2 expression was associated with significantly shorter overall survival on T‑DXd (median OS 6.2 mo vs 13.9 mo for low Trop‑2, p<0.01).
Combined HER2‑high/Trop‑2‑low tumors showed the best outcomes on HER2‑directed ADCs, suggesting a selective biomarker strategy for drug choice.
The assay received New York State Department of Health (NYSDOH) CLIA approval, enabling clinical deployment.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: High Trop‑2 expression appears to blunt response to HER2‑directed ADCs, indicating a potential resistance mechanism or competitive target effect.
Patients with both high HER2 and high Trop‑2 had poorer outcomes than HER2‑high/Trop‑2‑low, supporting the need for a selective biomarker rather than a single target assessment.
open_questions: What is the optimal quantitative cutoff for HER2 or Trop‑2 to guide ADC selection?
How do intrinsic molecular subtypes (e.g., PAM50) influence the quantitative protein thresholds?
What is the contribution of the tumor microenvironment and adaptive immunity to ADC durability beyond target density?

cited_papers: JCO Precision Oncology 2024 Rim et al.

talk_title: Genomics and environmental risk factors in East Asian lung adenocarcinoma

speaker.name: Takashi Kono
speaker.affiliation: National Cancer Center, Japan; Vice President of Japanese Cancer Association; Director of JCGAT

targets.dependency_context: never smoker lung adenocarcinoma, East Asian

models.name: JCGAT national database
models.genotype_or_key_features: 130,000 patient records, 7,000 lung cancer, smoking status collected

MOA_summary: Kono presented large-scale national genomic data showing distinct mutation patterns in never-smoker versus smoker lung adenocarcinoma, with EGFR mutations predominating in never smokers and KRAS in smokers, and highlighted environmental contributors such as passive smoking, air pollution, and germline polymorphisms influencing tumor development.
key_findings: Never smokers comprised ~30% of advanced lung cancer cases in the Japanese national database.
EGFR mutations are the most frequent driver in never-smoker lung adenocarcinoma, while KRAS dominates in smokers.
Passive smoking increases tumor mutational burden via APOBEC signatures but does not affect EGFR mutation frequency.
Air pollution (PM2.5) correlates with higher EGFR-positive lung cancer incidence without directly causing EGFR mutations.
Germline polymorphisms (e.g., TERT, HLA class II) confer greater risk in never smokers, especially for EGFR-positive disease.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: EGFR-positive tumors in smokers often lack SBS4 smoking signature, indicating a smoking-independent development pathway.
open_questions: What are the functional mechanisms by which identified germline variants (TERT, HLA) promote EGFR-driven tumorigenesis?
How does passive smoking interact with APOBEC activity to influence tumor evolution?

talk_title: Germline susceptibility and genomic risk in never-smoker lung cancer

biomarkers.name: Polygenic risk score for lung adenocarcinoma
biomarkers.finding: PRS associated with ~1.3-fold increased risk per SD in both young-onset and never-smoker cohorts

biomarkers.name: TP53 pathogenic variants
biomarkers.finding: Enriched in young-onset lung cancer; rare variant burden significant

biomarkers.name: SMAD6 rare variants
biomarkers.finding: Associated with non-fusion driven lung cancer

biomarkers.name: IREB2 rare variants
biomarkers.finding: Associated with fusion-driven lung cancer

models.name: Young Lung Cancer (YLC) study
models.genotype_or_key_features: 620 patients ≤45 y, 87% never/light smokers, enriched for ALK/ROS1 fusions

models.name: INHERIT study
models.genotype_or_key_features: familial lung cancer, includes EGFR T790M carriers and other hereditary variants

clinical_data.metric: ORR to platinum + PARP inhibitor in BRCA2 germline carrier
clinical_data.value: durable response >4 y to platinum, 16 m on PARP inhibitor
clinical_data.population: stage IV BRCA2 germline mutant lung cancer

MOA_summary: Lo Piccolo described large-scale germline sequencing efforts revealing that rare pathogenic variants in established cancer genes (TP53, SMAD6, IREB2) and polygenic risk scores contribute to lung cancer risk in never smokers, especially in young-onset and EGFR‑positive disease, and highlighted a case where BRCA2 loss of heterozygosity sensitized a patient to platinum and PARP inhibition.
key_findings: Young‑onset lung cancer (≤45 y) is enriched for targetable fusions (ALK, ROS1) and EGFR mutations.
Rare pathogenic germline variants in TP53, SMAD6, and IREB2 are significantly enriched in young‑onset or fusion‑driven tumors.
Polygenic risk scores derived from smoking‑associated GWAS still confer modest risk (OR ≈ 1.3 per SD) in never‑smoker cohorts.
Large (>1 Mb) rare deletions are over‑represented in young‑onset, fusion‑positive cases.
A BRCA2 germline pathogenic variant with tumor LOH predicted exceptional response to platinum chemotherapy followed by PARP inhibition.
open_questions: How do germline variants interact with environmental exposures (e.g., air pollution) to shape somatic driver selection?
Can polygenic risk scores be refined to improve predictive power specifically for never‑smoker lung cancer?

talk_title: Differential treatment response by smoking status in lung cancer

speaker.name: Seung Lee
speaker.affiliation: Samsung Medical Center, Republic of Korea

targets.dependency_context: never smoker adenocarcinoma immune checkpoint response

biomarkers.name: PD-L1 CPS
biomarkers.finding: Higher PD-L1 associated with better ICI response but overall lower in never smokers

biomarkers.name: Tumor mutation burden (TMB)
biomarkers.finding: Never smokers have significantly lower TMB; high TMB rare but predicts ICI benefit

biomarkers.name: PBAP loss‑of‑function mutation
biomarkers.finding: ~10% of never‑smoker NSCLC; associated with favorable ICI response

biomarkers.name: DDR1 expression
biomarkers.finding: High DDR1 linked to hyperprogression on ICI in never smokers

models.name: Immuno‑Oncology Clinical Genomics cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 5,000 lung cancer patients (2,000 never smokers, 3,000 smokers) with genomic and treatment data

clinical_data.metric: EGFR TKI hazard ratio (never smoker vs smoker)

MOA_summary: Lee demonstrated that never‑smoker lung cancer shows superior benefit from EGFR TKIs but diminished response to immune checkpoint inhibitors, correlating with lower TMB and PD‑L1; he identified biomarkers (PBAP loss, DDR1, transcriptional MMR‑like signature) that predict ICI sensitivity or hyperprogression, and highlighted that anti‑angiogenic addition improves TKI efficacy mainly in smokers.
key_findings: EGFR TKI hazard ratio 0.7 in never smokers versus smokers, confirming greater efficacy.
Addition of anti‑angiogenic agents to EGFR TKI improves outcomes more in smokers than never smokers.
Never smokers have significantly lower TMB and PD‑L1 scores, resulting in poor ICI monotherapy response (ORR ~10%).
PBAP loss‑of‑function mutations (~10% of never smokers) predict durable ICI responses (5‑year remission).
High DDR1 expression associates with hyperprogression on ICI in never smokers.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: High DDR1 expression linked to impaired T‑cell infiltration and hyperprogression on ICI.
Low APOBEC signature in never smokers correlates with reduced ICI benefit.

combination_strategies.rationale: Enhance EGFR TKI efficacy; more benefit observed in smokers
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved PFS in smokers, modest effect in never smokers

combination_strategies.rationale: Target EGFR exon 20 insertions and overcome resistance
combination_strategies.phenotype: Promising responses in rare EGFR variants

open_questions: What molecular mechanisms underlie the differential benefit of anti‑angiogenic agents in smokers versus never smokers?
Can DDR1 inhibition restore ICI sensitivity in high‑DDR1 never‑smoker tumors?

talk_title: Molecular evolution and prognostic classification of never‑smoker lung adenocarcinoma (Sherlock study)

targets.dependency_context: co‑occurs with EGFR, drives genomic instability

biomarkers.name: ID2 indel signature (T homopolymer deletions)
biomarkers.finding: Higher ID2 count correlates with aggressive proliferation markers

biomarkers.name: SBS5 clock‑like signature
biomarkers.finding: Enriched in tumors from high PM2.5 regions

biomarkers.name: Methylation‑based AT1 vs AT2 cell‑of‑origin classifier
biomarkers.finding: AT1‑origin signature predicts low‑grade, indolent tumors

biomarkers.name: 60‑gene transcriptomic prognostic signature
biomarkers.finding: Distinguishes steady (alveolar) vs proliferative (fusion) subtypes with significant OS differences

models.name: Sherlock study
models.genotype_or_key_features: ≈5,000 individuals, ~3,000 with multi‑omics; includes never smokers and smokers

MOA_summary: Landi described comprehensive whole‑genome and transcriptomic profiling of ~1,200 lung adenocarcinomas, revealing that never‑smoker tumors follow distinct evolutionary paths: EGFR‑mutant, copy‑number‑rich, AT1‑cell‑origin cancers evolve slowly, whereas KRKR‑mutant, low‑CNV tumors arise rapidly; environmental exposures (PM2.5, indoor coal) imprint specific mutational signatures, and integrated methylation and expression signatures provide prognostic stratification.
key_findings: Three genomic subtypes (piano, mezzo‑forte, forte) correlate with copy‑number burden and driver mutations.
Endogenous signatures (SBS1, SBS5, APOBEC) dominate; exogenous SBS4 and Signature 22A (aristolochic acid) appear in specific regions.
High outdoor PM2.5 exposure associates with increased SBS5 and reduced telomere length.
KRAS‑mutant tumors have shorter latency (≈5 y) and earlier age at diagnosis compared with EGFR‑mutant tumors (≈6 y).
AT1‑origin methylation signature distinguishes low‑grade, indolent tumors and aligns with the steady transcriptomic group.
open_questions: How does the AT1‑cell epigenetic constraint influence response to targeted therapies?
Can the methylation‑based cell‑of‑origin classifier be applied prospectively for early detection?

talk_title: Tumor-agnostic PD-1 blockade, ctDNA‑guided MRD and organ‑preserving immunotherapy

speaker.name: Luis A. Diaz Jr.
speaker.affiliation: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Solid Tumor Oncology Division

biomarkers.name: MSI-H / MMR deficiency
biomarkers.finding: Patients with MSI‑H tumors showed high response rates to pembrolizumab (≥50% ORR in early studies) and formed the basis of the first tumor‑agnostic FDA approval

biomarkers.name: circulating tumor DNA (ctDNA) positivity after definitive therapy
biomarkers.finding: Among 174 MMR‑deficient patients screened, 25 (14%) were ctDNA‑positive; ctDNA‑positive patients who received pembrolizumab had recurrence‑free outcomes comparable to ctDNA‑negative observation, whereas ctDNA‑positive untreated patients progressed rapidly

models.name: Phase I/II pembrolizumab MRD trial (2019‑2023)
models.genotype_or_key_features: R0 resection, MMR‑deficient solid tumors, ctDNA‑positive vs ctDNA‑negative cohorts

models.name: Neoadjuvant PD‑1 blockade in locally advanced mismatch‑repair deficient rectal cancer
models.genotype_or_key_features: 49 patients, MMR‑deficient rectal adenocarcinoma, 6 months pembrolizumab

clinical_data.metric: clinical complete response (cCR) in rectal cohort
overall recurrence‑free rate at 2 years (all non‑rectal cohort)
clinical_data.population: MMR‑deficient non‑rectal solid tumors treated with pembrolizumab

clinical_data.metric: ctDNA‑positive patients receiving pembrolizumab vs observation
clinical_data.population: MMR‑deficient solid tumors, ctDNA‑positive after standard therapy

clinical_data.metric: adverse event rate (any grade) in non‑rectal cohort
clinical_data.population: MMR‑deficient non‑rectal solid tumors receiving pembrolizumab

clinical_data.metric: cancer incidence reduction in Lynch syndrome patients receiving PD‑1 blockade
clinical_data.population: Lynch syndrome carriers with prior cancer

MOA_summary: Pembrolizumab blocks the PD‑1 immune checkpoint, reactivating exhausted T cells against neoantigens generated by the high mutational burden of MMR‑deficient/MSI‑H tumors. In the adjuvant setting, ctDNA detection identifies residual disease; PD‑1 blockade eradicates this microscopic disease, converting ctDNA‑positive patients to long‑term disease‑free status. In the neoadjuvant rectal setting, sustained PD‑1 inhibition induces complete clinical responses, allowing omission of surgery.
key_findings: All 49 MMR‑deficient rectal cancer patients achieved a clinical complete response after 6 months of pembrolizumab, enabling non‑operative management (100% cCR).
Two‑year recurrence‑free survival was 96/49 (≈96%) in the rectal cohort and 85% in the 54‑patient non‑rectal cohort treated with pembrolizumab.

In a ctDNA‑guided MRD trial, 25 ctDNA‑positive patients who received pembrolizumab had recurrence‑free outcomes comparable to 125 ctDNA‑negative patients observed, whereas untreated ctDNA‑positive patients progressed rapidly.
Adverse events of any grade occurred in only 35% of non‑rectal patients, indicating a favorable safety profile for organ‑preserving immunotherapy.
Retrospective analysis of 837 Lynch syndrome carriers showed >80% reduction in secondary cancer incidence among those who had received PD‑1 blockade versus untreated controls.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Lower complete response rates observed in prostate, gastric, and esophageal MMR‑deficient tumors suggest tissue‑specific immune tolerance or microenvironmental barriers.
Barrier tissues (lung, skin, GI mucosa) showed higher responsiveness than non‑barrier sites, indicating possible role of local microbiome or stromal factors.

combination_strategies.rationale: Dual checkpoint inhibition may increase response depth and durability in MMR‑deficient tumors.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Higher complete response rates reported in early combination studies.

open_questions: Is a phase III randomized trial required to establish neoadjuvant PD‑1 blockade as standard of care for organ‑preserving treatment of MMR‑deficient rectal cancer?
What are the mechanistic determinants of the observed tissue‑specific variability in response (e.g., prostate vs. bladder)?
Can earlier or longer duration of PD‑1 blockade further improve cure rates in non‑rectal MMR‑deficient cancers?

company_or_sponsor: Philanthropic donor groups (e.g., Chesapeake Bay swimmers fund)

talk_title: Population-based cancer surveillance informs disparities and policy

speaker.name: Dr. Ahmedin Jemal
speaker.affiliation: Senior Vice President of Surveillance, Prevention, and Health Services Research, American Cancer Society, Atlanta, GA

models.name: US Cancer Statistics (ACS Cancer Facts & Figures) annual reports
models.genotype_or_key_features: nationwide incidence, mortality, survival by age, sex, race, geography

clinical_data.population: US cancer patients overall (2023 Cancer Stats article)

MOA_summary: Dr. Jemal presented integrated analyses of national cancer registry and survey data revealing sex-specific lung cancer incidence reversals, early-onset colorectal cancer birth-cohort effects, and the impact of ACA Medicaid expansion on insurance coverage, stage at diagnosis, and five-year survival, linking surveillance metrics to policy outcomes.
key_findings: Lung cancer incidence in women under age 50 now exceeds that of men, a reversal not explained by smoking prevalence alone.

Birth-cohort analysis shows a fourfold increase in rectal cancer risk and twofold increase in colon cancer risk for cohorts born after 1950, prompting USPSTF to lower colorectal screening start age to 45.
Geographic clusters with 40% higher colorectal cancer mortality identified in the Lower Mississippi Corridor, leading to targeted screening initiatives and legislative action.
Medicaid expansion under the ACA reduced uninsured rates more than private-market subsidies and improved five-year overall survival in high-poverty rural areas.
Dependent coverage expansion increased early-stage diagnosis and fertility-sparing treatment rates for cervical cancer in women aged 21‑25.
open_questions: How can cancer registry data be integrated with genomic and treatment data to better understand rural disparities?
What methods can reduce the lag time in treatment data reporting to inform real-time policy decisions?

talk_title: Extracellular vesicles orchestrate pre‑metastatic niche and systemic disease

speaker.name: David Lyden
speaker.affiliation: Weill Cornell Medicine, Department of Pediatrics and Pediatric Neuro‑Oncology

targets.dependency_context: EVs from lung‑tropic breast cancer cells
EVs from lung‑tropic breast cancer cells
EVs from lung‑tropic breast cancer cells
EVs from brain‑tropic breast cancer cells
EVs from melanoma or pancreatic cancer that induce lung thrombosis
secreted by pancreatic and GI tumors influencing lung macrophages
upregulated by exomeres in liver Kupffer cells
activated T‑cell EVs delivering to tumor nuclei

biomarkers.name: β2 integrin‑positive EVs
biomarkers.finding: high levels predicted postoperative clotting in pancreatic cancer patients

biomarkers.name: CXCL13 plasma level
biomarkers.finding: elevated CXCL13 correlated with lung‑pre‑metastatic niche formation and thrombosis risk

models.name: triple‑negative breast cancer parental and organ‑tropic subclones
naive C57BL/6 mice injected with EVs
liver biopsies from pancreatic cancer patients
pancreatic cancer cohort (n≈30) with longitudinal EV profiling
models.genotype_or_key_features: tracked for clot events and fatty liver development

MOA_summary: Lyden demonstrated that tumor‑derived extracellular vesicles (EVs) carry organ‑specific integrin zip‑codes that direct EV uptake to pre‑metastatic niches; β2‑integrin‑positive EVs trigger lung thrombosis, while exomeres rich in metabolic enzymes induce fatty‑liver inflammation via Kupffer‑cell TNF‑α activation. Blocking β2‑integrin or TNF‑α mitigates these systemic effects. Activated T‑cell EVs loaded with granzyme B enter tumor nuclei, up‑regulating antigen‑presentation genes and synergizing with anti‑PD‑1 to regress pancreatic tumors.
key_findings: Integrin αVβ5‑positive EVs from lung‑tropic breast cancer cells home to the lung and establish pre‑metastatic niches, whereas αVβ3‑positive EVs target the brain.
β2‑integrin‑bearing EVs derived from melanoma or pancreatic cancer induce rapid lung clot formation in naive mice; anti‑β2‑integrin antibody reduces fibrin deposition and improves platelet counts.
Exomeres, the smallest EV fraction, preferentially accumulate in the liver, reprogram Kupffer cells to a TNF‑α‑high, fatty‑acid‑low state, and generate fatty‑liver disease in mice.
Pharmacologic blockade of β2‑integrin or TNF‑α prevents EV‑driven thrombosis and fatty‑liver pathology and modestly delays metastatic tumor growth.

Activated T‑cell EVs package granzyme B, enter tumor cell nuclei, up‑regulate antigen‑presentation pathways, and when combined with anti‑PD‑1 produce durable tumor regressions in pancreatic cancer mouse models (median survival extended 30–40 days).
Circulating β2‑integrin‑positive EV levels prospectively predict clot events in pancreatic cancer patients, outperforming standard coagulation tests.

combination_strategies.rationale: activated T‑cell EVs increase tumor antigen presentation, potentially enhancing checkpoint blockade efficacy
combination_strategies.phenotype: synergistic tumor regression and extended survival in pancreatic cancer mice

combination_strategies.rationale: blocks EV‑mediated platelet binding and lung thrombosis
combination_strategies.phenotype: reduced fibrin deposition and lower clot incidence in melanoma models

combination_strategies.rationale: counteracts exomere‑driven Kupffer cell inflammation and fatty‑liver development
combination_strategies.phenotype: prevention of fatty‑liver phenotype and preservation of cardiac function during chemotherapy

combination_strategies.rationale: inhibits NET formation in pre‑metastatic lung niche
combination_strategies.phenotype: decreased lung thrombosis and metastasis in pancreatic cancer models

open_questions: How long do tumor‑derived EVs persist in circulation after definitive primary‑tumor resection?
What are the precise molecular mechanisms by which granzyme B‑loaded T‑cell EVs access the tumor cell nucleus?
Do CAF‑derived EVs differ functionally from tumor‑cell EVs in organotropism and metabolic reprogramming?
Can chronic exercise‑induced “healthy” EVs competitively displace tumor EVs in patients, and what biomarkers best capture this shift?

talk_title: From fusion biology to functional genomics and therapeutic strategies in pediatric cancers

speaker.name: Dr. Kimberly Stegmaier
speaker.affiliation: Department of Pediatric Oncology, Dana-Farber Cancer Institute

biomarkers.name: STAG2 loss
biomarkers.finding: STAG2 loss associated with higher incidence of metastatic disease and poorer survival in Ewing sarcoma

biomarkers.name: RCST1-ABL2 fusion
biomarkers.finding: Patient with B-ALL harboring RCST1-ABL2 fusion achieved complete remission when imatinib was added to chemotherapy

models.name: LEAP (Leukemia Precision Therapy Consortium)
models.genotype_or_key_features: genomics‑matched targeted therapy for pediatric leukemia

MOA_summary: Stegmaier's work integrates large‑scale functional genomics (CRISPR and chemical screens) with fusion biology to identify pediatric cancer dependencies such as TRIM8, ETV6, and UGT3A2, revealing a Goldilocks dosage requirement for EWS‑FLI1. She leverages degrader technologies (PROTACs, molecular glues) and bifunctional molecules to selectively degrade or rewire the fusion protein, demonstrating that loss of TRIM8 elevates EWS‑FLI1 levels and that targeted degradation of the fusion can induce apoptosis in Ewing sarcoma models.
key_findings: EWS‑FLI1 dosage is a Goldilocks dependency: both over‑expression and complete repression are deleterious to Ewing sarcoma cells.
TRIM8 is a selective Ewing sarcoma dependency; its knockout increases EWS‑FLI1 levels and is lethal in vitro and in vivo.
ETV6 competes with EWS‑FLI1 on chromatin; CRISPR or dTAG‑mediated loss of ETV6 leads to excess EWS‑FLI1 activity and cell death.
STAG2 loss predicts metastatic disease and poor prognosis in Ewing sarcoma, validated in isogenic knockout models.
Chemical PRISM screening identified compound R112 as uniquely active in Ewing sarcoma; activity is rescued by UGT3A2 knockout, implicating UDP‑glucose metabolism as a novel vulnerability.
