A genomics‑guided case of a pediatric B‑ALL patient with RCST1‑ABL2 fusion achieved durable complete remission when imatinib was added to standard therapy.
Development of high‑throughput GFP‑mCherry reporter assays enabled screening of >100,000 molecules, yielding several candidates that induce proteasome‑dependent degradation of the EWS‑FLI1 fusion.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Goldilocks phenomenon indicates that precise modulation of fusion protein levels is required; both hyper‑activation and complete loss are cytotoxic, complicating therapeutic windows.
STAG2 loss creates a selective vulnerability but also promotes metastatic behavior, suggesting potential resistance via lineage plasticity.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target the RCST1‑ABL2 kinase fusion identified by genomics in relapsed B‑ALL
combination_strategies.phenotype: Complete remission in a patient refractory to chemotherapy and CAR‑T

combination_strategies.rationale: Enhance efficacy of targeted agents (e.g., imatinib) in genomics‑matched leukemia
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved remission rates in LEAP cohort (specific numbers not disclosed)

combination_strategies.rationale: Rescue viability loss caused by degrader candidates, confirming proteasome‑dependent mechanism
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reversal of GFP‑fusion loss in reporter assays

open_questions: What are the precise molecular mechanisms by which UGT3A2 mediates sensitivity to R112 in Ewing sarcoma?
Can bifunctional degraders be developed that directly engage EWS‑FLI1 without requiring a high‑affinity ligand?
How can the Goldilocks dosage requirement be translated into a therapeutic index that avoids toxicity in normal tissues?

talk_title: Building and Leveraging the Cancer Dependency Map for Target Discovery

models.name: multiple cancer cell lines (>1000)
models.genotype_or_key_features: diverse molecular and genetic alterations across tumor types

MOA_summary: The Cancer Dependency Map integrates large-scale CRISPR and shRNA genetic perturbation screens with multi-omics profiling and drug sensitivity data across >1,000 cancer cell lines, using the Chronos gene effect score to correct biases, thereby linking molecular alterations to gene dependencies and identifying synthetic lethal vulnerabilities such as PRMT5 in MTAP‑deleted cancers and WRN in MSI cancers.
key_findings: Identified PRMT5 as a synthetic lethal dependency in MTAP‑deleted cancers, validated by three concurrent manuscripts and ongoing clinical trials of PRMT5 inhibitors.
Discovered WRN helicase dependency in microsatellite instability (MSI) cancers using both CRISPR and shRNA data, leading to multiple manuscripts and clinical development of WRN inhibitors.
Found that loss of the 9p21 region creates dependency on the SKI complex (PELO/HBS1L), with MSI also driving PELO dependency, revealing a novel synthetic lethal axis.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: CRISPR screens exhibit copy‑number–related off‑target toxicity, addressed by the Chronos model to improve selectivity of gene effect scores.
open_questions: How can the map be expanded to capture resistance mechanisms and combination dependencies beyond single‑gene knockouts?
What are the optimal high‑dimensional phenotypes to predict therapeutic efficacy beyond cell viability?

cited_papers: Three concurrent PRMT5 synthetic lethal manuscripts (2024)
Three concurrent WRN synthetic lethal manuscripts (2024)
Two concurrent SKI complex dependency manuscripts (2024)

talk_title: Advancing DepMap: New Models, Paralog Screens, and Future Directions

models.name: 150 organoid and neurosphere models
models.genotype_or_key_features: diverse pediatric, rare, and adult cancer types

MOA_summary: Expands the DepMap resource by integrating organoid and 3D spheroid models, performing large‑scale paralog pair CRISPR screens to uncover hidden dependencies (e.g., AKT1 vs AKT1/2), and developing high‑dimensional phenotypic assays and AI‑enabled portals to predict combination and resistance mechanisms.
key_findings: Generated a library of >27,000 paralog pairs and screened ~300 models, revealing context‑specific dependencies such as AKT1 alone versus AKT1+AKT2 co‑knockout.
Integrated 150 organoid and neurosphere models into DepMap, showing lineage‑specific dependencies (e.g., ITGAV vs ITGV1) that differ from 2D cell lines.
Developed the Prism bar‑coded pooled drug sensitivity assay covering 900 cell lines and 265 compounds, enabling rapid assessment of compound selectivity and synthetic lethal interactions.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Paralog screens reveal that single‑gene knockouts may miss dependencies masked by redundancy, emphasizing the need for combinatorial targeting.

combination_strategies.rationale: Synthetic lethal interaction with FGFR‑driven cancers requiring co‑inhibition of RAS isoforms
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved efficacy in FGFR2‑altered gastric cancer model versus single agents

open_questions: How can high‑dimensional phenotypic readouts be scaled to systematically map resistance mechanisms?
What is the optimal strategy to model metabolic and microenvironmental contexts (e.g., serum vs defined media) in DepMap?

company_or_sponsor: Stand Up to Cancer (funding for surface proteomics)

talk_title: Computational Oncology: Molecular Logic of Cancer Development, Immunotherapy Response, and Resistance

speaker.name: Eli Van Allen
speaker.affiliation: Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, Division of Population Sciences; Harvard Medical School

targets.dependency_context: PBRM1-mutant renal cell carcinoma treated with immune checkpoint blockade

biomarkers.name: PBRM1 loss-of-function mutation
biomarkers.finding: Associated with higher response rates to nivolumab in renal cell carcinoma (signal observed in discovery cohort and Checkpoint 025 trial, but not consistent across all studies)

models.name: Checkpoint 025
models.genotype_or_key_features: Renal cell carcinoma patients progressing on VEGF-TKI, randomized to nivolumab vs everolimus; PBRM1 status assessed

models.name: PBRM1 knockout cell lines
models.genotype_or_key_features: CRISPR-mediated loss of PBRM1 to assess endogenous retrovirus expression

MOA_summary: Van Allen's work integrates germline, somatic, and immune genomic data using interpretable AI and computational pathology to define molecular logic that drives oncogenesis, immunotherapy response, and therapeutic resistance. By mapping regulatory non‑coding mutations, germline risk alleles, and chromatin remodeler alterations (e.g., PBRM1), his algorithms predict patient outcomes and uncover novel dependencies such as MDM4 in lethal prostate cancer.
key_findings: Developed a novel algorithm to detect recurrent non‑coding regulatory mutations, exemplified by functional validation of a breast cancer XPP1‑proximal region.
Demonstrated germline BRCA2 enrichment in advanced prostate cancer and extended germline risk analysis to CHEK2, DNA‑repair genes, and structural variants in pediatric solid tumors.
Identified loss‑of‑function mutations in PBRM1 as a predictive biomarker for response to nivolumab in renal cell carcinoma, with replication in the Checkpoint 025 trial (nivolumab arm only).

Created an interpretable deep‑learning pathology pipeline that quantifies tumor grade heterogeneity and immune cell features, improving response prediction over single‑gene biomarkers in the same renal cell carcinoma cohort.

Built the “Peanut” biologically‑engineered neural network to classify primary versus lethal prostate cancer, uncovering a novel association with MDM4 amplification that is being pursued for therapeutic targeting.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: PBRM1 association with ICB response was strong in early cohorts but attenuated in later studies, suggesting context‑dependent resistance mechanisms linked to prior VEGF‑TKI exposure.
MDM4 amplification emerged as a novel driver of lethal prostate cancer, indicating a potential resistance pathway to standard androgen‑targeted therapies.
open_questions: How can germline structural variants be systematically incorporated into clinical decision‑making pipelines?
What are the optimal methods to ensure interpretability and trustworthiness of agentic AI models in oncology?
How should heterogeneous data quality be weighted when training large‑scale foundation models for precision medicine?

session_title: Academic Entrepreneurship and Getting Discoveries into Patients, part one
talk_title: Transitioning from Academia to Industry for Commercialization
MOA_summary: The speaker developed an antibody against Substance P, a small neuropeptide, based on the discovery that it transactivates EGFR and HER2. The work focused on the transition from academic discovery and patenting to industrial drug development and commercialization strategies.
key_findings: Developed a patent for an antibody targeting Substance P to inhibit the transactivation of EGFR and HER2.

patent_or_ip_hints: patent application for antibody against substance P

session_title: Academic Entrepreneurship and Getting Discoveries into Patients, part one
talk_title: Academic Entrepreneurship: Promise and Perils
MOA_summary: The speaker identified an unknown protein (elongation factor two) that was highly active in glioblastoma compared to normal glia. He also developed a pharmacophore for P-glycoprotein (MDR1) which led to the development of VX-701.
key_findings: Identified elongation factor two as a differentially phosphorylated protein in glioblastoma compared to normal white matter.
Licensed a pharmacophore for P-glycoprotein to Vertex, resulting in the compound VX-701.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Spent 10 years at J&J on elongation factor two but never made a drug selective enough to go forward.

cited_papers: Heit et al. TIBS (Academic Entrepreneurship: Promise and Perils)

session_title: Academic Entrepreneurship and Getting Discoveries into Patients, part one
talk_title: Regulatory Pathways for First-in-Human Trials

models.name: guinea pig vs rat oxidative stress models

MOA_summary: The speaker discussed the regulatory requirements for Investigational New Drug (IND) applications, emphasizing the importance of pre-IND meetings to address safety, CMC (Chemistry, Manufacturing, and Controls), and non-clinical data to reduce the risk of clinical holds.
key_findings: Guinea pigs are better models for oxidative stress than rats because they do not produce ascorbate like humans.

session_title: Academic Entrepreneurship and Getting Discoveries into Patients, part one
talk_title: Funding and Scaling Academic Innovation
MOA_summary: The speaker highlighted the role of non-traditional funding (e.g., Pelotonia) in bridging the gap between basic science and clinical application, specifically citing the early funding of ibrutinib in CLL mouse models which led to a multi-billion dollar drug.
key_findings: Pelotonia raised $350 million for cancer research with 100% of funds going to research.
Initial $100,000 funding for ibrutinib in a CLL mouse model contributed to the development of a multi-billion dollar drug that changed the standard of care for CLL.

talk_title: From Academic Genetics to Global Product Development: Lessons from Founding Foundation Medicine and Tango Therapeutics

speaker.name: Levi Garraway
speaker.affiliation: Chief Medical Officer, Global Product Development, Roche/Genentech

MOA_summary: Garraway described how early cancer genetics research (e.g., MTAP deletion–PRMT5 dependency) was translated into two companies: Foundation Medicine (pan‑cancer sequencing diagnostics) and Tango Therapeutics (PRMT5 inhibitors), highlighting the importance of aligning scientific insight with market need and building networks to move from academia to pharma.
key_findings: Co‑founding Foundation Medicine leveraged massively parallel sequencing to create a whole‑panel diagnostic that became a Roche asset.
Discovery of MTAP deletion–PRMT5 synthetic lethality led to Tango Therapeutics, now developing PRMT5 inhibitors in the clinic.
Early career networking (e.g., with Eric Lander) was critical to secure venture capital and industry introductions.
Transitioning from academia to industry required understanding institutional motivations and building cross‑functional teams.

talk_title: Building a Lung‑Cancer Early Detection Diagnostic: From Molecular Pap Smear to Percepta

speaker.name: Avi Spira
speaker.affiliation: Former Pulmonary & Critical Care Physician‑Scientist, Boston University Medical Center; Former R&D Lead, Johnson & Johnson

biomarkers.name: Molecular Pap Smear gene panel
biomarkers.finding: 80‑gene transcriptomic signature used to distinguish malignant from benign lung nodules

MOA_summary: Spira detailed the development of a bronchial brush‑based transcriptomic assay (80‑gene panel) to detect early lung cancer, the challenges of VC pitching, the role of an institutional Ignition Fund, and the eventual acquisition by Veracyte, resulting in the commercial Percepta test.
key_findings: Initial pitch to VCs failed due to lack of clear funding ask; revised pitch led to first round of capital from two venture groups.
Two clinical validation trials published in NEJM 2015 demonstrated diagnostic performance, facilitating acquisition by Veracyte.
Institutional Ignition Fund ($50k) provided critical deck development support and mentorship.
Angel funding from high‑net‑worth individuals helped bridge the gap between seed and Series A.

talk_title: Academic Cell‑Therapy Development for HPV‑Associated Cancers: Infrastructure, Resilience, and FDA Navigation

speaker.name: Christian Henrichs
speaker.affiliation: Professor of Medicine, Rutgers Cancer Institute

biomarkers.name: HPV E6/E7 expression
biomarkers.finding: Patients with high E6/E7 expression showed durable complete responses in early trials

MOA_summary: Henrichs described the creation of a GMP‑compliant cellular therapy targeting HPV E6/E7 antigens, the necessity of institutional GMP facilities, and the strategic use of pre‑IND versus direct IND filing to accelerate early‑phase trials.
key_findings: Rutgers Cancer Institute’s GMP facility enabled rapid IND filing for an HPV‑directed cell therapy.
Early clinical data showed 58% ORR and durable complete responses in HPV‑positive patients.
Pre‑IND meetings can de‑risk programs, but a well‑prepared IND can also prompt rapid FDA feedback.
Academic teams can leverage FDA staff with academic backgrounds for more collaborative interactions.

talk_title: Resilience and Funding Challenges in Building a Breast‑Cancer Therapeutic Company (TerraNovir)
MOA_summary: Tsondal highlighted the difficulty of securing early capital for a metastasis‑focused breast cancer program, emphasizing the Catch‑2022 dilemma (need team to raise money, need money to build team) and the importance of resilience and patient advocacy in advancing the program.
key_findings: Incorporated TerraNovir in the US in 2025 to access American capital markets.
Identified a gap in understanding metastatic versus primary tumor biology as a strategic focus.
Described challenges when VCs assign junior analysts unfamiliar with the target, underscoring the need for clear scientific communication.

talk_title: Venture Studios and Alternative Paths to Company Formation in Oncology
MOA_summary: Schwaebel described Alloy Therapeutics’ venture‑studio model that provides seed funding, CRO‑like services, and partnership pathways for academic innovators, illustrating how such structures can de‑risk early projects and accelerate translation.
key_findings: Alloy’s venture studio seeds projects with both capital and operational support (e.g., CRO services).
Studio model enables academic founders to focus on science while leveraging in‑house expertise for regulatory and business development.
Alternative structures such as Deerfield’s academic partnerships, CurioBio’s pre‑money evaluation, and roll‑up funds are expanding the ecosystem.

talk_title: Community-engaged research partnerships to increase cancer prevention reach

speaker.name: David Wetter
speaker.affiliation: University of Utah, Huntsman Cancer Institute

models.name: PCORI pragmatic tobacco cessation trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: population of 140,000 patients across 14 FQHC systems in Utah

clinical_data.population: patients across Utah CHCs during pandemic

clinical_data.metric: second COVID at-home test trial enrollment

clinical_data.population: CHC patients receiving CDC-funded screening program

MOA_summary: Wetter described a community-engaged partnership model that aligns research activities with the priorities of federally qualified health centers, leverages existing evidence‑based interventions, and uses digital health tools (population health management, text messaging, AI chatbots) to achieve population‑level reach for tobacco cessation and cancer screening interventions.
key_findings: First tobacco cessation trial aimed for 6,000 enrollees but enrolled 12,000 patients (100% reach of eligible tobacco users).
COVID rapid‑cycle messaging trial enrolled 107,000 of 160,000 eligible patients (67% reach) using 37 message workflows across 227 cohorts.
Second COVID at‑home test trial enrolled 33,000 participants after pandemic peak.
CDC‑funded colorectal cancer screening program increased screening rates from 36% to 43%, a relative 20% increase and accounted for ~50% of national CHC screening gains.

talk_title: Social capital interventions and digital health to improve cancer prevention in persistent poverty neighborhoods

speaker.name: Rula Tamimi
speaker.affiliation: Weill Cornell Medicine, New York City

models.name: Cares For You middle‑school cancer education program
Bridge to Quit patient‑portal enrollment study
models.genotype_or_key_features: 3 FQHC sites in NYC neighborhoods, baseline portal enrollment 34‑44%

clinical_data.population: patients at two intervention FQHC sites

clinical_data.metric: Colorectal cancer screening rate increase (CDC grant)

MOA_summary: Tamimi presented two social‑capital–focused interventions: a school‑based cancer risk education curriculum (Cares For You) that engages middle‑school students as health ambassadors, and a health‑system intervention (Bridge to Quit) that boosts patient‑portal enrollment through navigator support and low‑literacy education, thereby creating a digital conduit for smoking‑cessation referrals.
key_findings: Cares For You was implemented in four middle schools, reaching 288 students; 95% reported learning new science content during STEM Day.
Bridge to Quit increased patient‑portal enrollment from a baseline of 34‑44% to 61% after 18 months, surpassing the 50% target.
CDC‑funded colorectal cancer screening program raised screening rates from 36% to 43%, representing an almost 20% relative increase.
High satisfaction with educational components: 90% found speakers interesting, 85% learned about new jobs.

talk_title: Upstream income interventions and their impact on cancer‑related health behaviors

models.name: California Guaranteed Income Pilot
models.genotype_or_key_features: 3,000 participants (2,200 pregnant women, 800 foster youth) randomized to higher vs lower cash amounts

models.name: Earned Income Tax Credit (EITC) behavioral study
models.genotype_or_key_features: Focus groups with low‑income taxpayers across California

clinical_data.population: California residents eligible for state pilot
Pregnant participants in guaranteed income pilot
Aging‑out foster youth in guaranteed income pilot
Guaranteed Income participants surveyed at multiple time points

MOA_summary: Bondy described two upstream socioeconomic interventions—state‑funded guaranteed cash transfers and the Earned Income Tax Credit—evaluated through randomized and observational designs to assess their effects on stress, health behaviors (diet, exercise, sleep) and downstream colorectal cancer risk factors.
key_findings: The guaranteed income pilot randomized 3,000 participants (2,200 pregnant women, 800 foster youth) to differing cash amounts.
A 75% survey response rate was achieved across baseline, 3‑month, 9‑month, exit, and 6‑month post‑exit assessments.
Planned analyses will examine changes in diet, exercise, sleep quality, and stress using the Hope Index.
EITC focus groups are being used to explore how tax credits influence colorectal cancer screening behaviors.
open_questions: How will increased cash flow translate into measurable reductions in colorectal cancer incidence over longer follow‑up?
What are the mechanisms linking EITC receipt to changes in screening uptake?

talk_title: Iterative engineering of GPCR-targeted synthetic TCR and STAR T cells in advanced solid tumors

biomarkers.name: GPC3 expression
biomarkers.finding: Positive in >70% HCC and >95% AFP+ gastric cancer

models.name: Version 2 STAR T cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 3 heavily pretreated AFP-positive gastric cancer patients

MOA_summary: STAR T cells fuse a CAR extracellular domain with TCR signaling machinery, enabling low-antigen, high-sensitivity activation; CXCR2 expression improves tumor trafficking, and membrane-bound IL-15 provides autocrine survival signals, together enhancing expansion, persistence, and antitumor activity in GPC3-positive solid tumors.
key_findings: Version 2 STAR T cells achieved ORR 33.3% and DCR 66.7% in three heavily pretreated AFP-positive gastric cancer patients.
CXCR2-armored STAR T cells improved tumor trafficking in preclinical models.
Membrane-bound IL-15 (Version 3) increased ORR to 15% and DCR to 75% in four patients with advanced HCC or gastric cancer.
Grade ≥3 CRS occurred in 42.9% of patients; no treatment‑related deaths were observed.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Limited persistence of STAR T cells observed; responders showed sustained expansion while non‑responders had rapid decline in copy number.

combination_strategies.rationale: Enhance chemokine‑mediated tumor infiltration
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved trafficking and localization in mouse models

combination_strategies.rationale: Provide autonomous survival signal to overcome persistence hurdle
combination_strategies.phenotype: Dramatically superior expansion and near‑complete tumor eradication in vivo

open_questions: What mechanisms underlie the limited long‑term persistence despite IL‑15 armoring?
Can further engineering improve safety while maintaining efficacy?

talk_title: Cellular architecture of infused TILs predicts efficacy of autologous TIL therapy in acral melanoma

biomarkers.name: TFH cell frequency
biomarkers.finding: Higher TFH proportion correlated with durable response

biomarkers.name: intermediate exhausted CD8 T cells
biomarkers.finding: Enriched in responders versus terminal exhausted cells in non‑responders

models.name: investigator‑initiated TIL trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: 4 Chinese acral melanoma patients, heavily pretreated

MOA_summary: Autologous tumor‑infiltrating lymphocytes expanded ex vivo retain functional subsets; infusion of products enriched for TFH and intermediate exhausted CD8 T cells seeds progenitor‑like clones that persist and differentiate in vivo, driving durable tumor regression in acral melanoma.
key_findings: Three of four acral melanoma patients achieved disease control (75% DCR).
Responder showed >90% T cells in peripheral blood at week 12 versus low levels in stable disease patient.
Effective response linked to higher fractions of TFH and intermediate exhausted CD8 T cells, not total T cell count.
Clonal analysis revealed expanded progenitor‑like TCR clones persisting in blood up to 12 weeks post‑infusion.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Non‑responders harbored predominantly terminal exhausted CD8 T cells lacking tumor reactivity.

combination_strategies.rationale: Enhance TIL function and overcome TGF‑β mediated suppression
combination_strategies.phenotype: Increased IFN‑γ production and cytolysis in mouse models

open_questions: Can TFH and intermediate exhausted signatures be used prospectively to select patients?
What is the optimal dosing schedule for sustained clonal persistence?

talk_title: First‑in‑class CAR dendritic cell platform driving broad and durable antitumor immunity in solid tumors

speaker.name: Carl Desomme
speaker.affiliation: Washington University School of Medicine, St. Louis

biomarkers.name: B7-H3 expression
biomarkers.finding: Present on majority of tested solid tumor models

models.name: MCA‑induced sarcoma (75% B7-H3+)
models.genotype_or_key_features: 25% B7-H3 negative cells to model heterogeneity

MOA_summary: CAR‑engineered type‑1 dendritic cells (CAR‑DC1) bearing a B7‑H3 scFv and a TLR4 intracellular domain capture tumor antigens, enhance cross‑presentation, and prime robust tumor‑specific CD8 T cell responses, leading to complete tumor rejection and long‑term immune memory even against antigen‑negative variants.
key_findings: TLR4‑signaling CAR‑DC1s induced strong costimulatory molecule upregulation (CCR7, CD86, CD70).
In vitro, CAR‑DC1s cross‑presented OVA and endogenous tumor peptides at markedly higher abundance than control DCs.
In the heterogeneous sarcoma model, 30/30 mice achieved complete tumor rejection after CAR‑DC1 treatment.
Mice cured by CAR‑DC1s rejected rechallenge with both B7‑H3‑positive and B7‑H3‑negative tumors, indicating epitope spreading.

combination_strategies.rationale: Enhance local antigen capture and reduce systemic exposure
combination_strategies.phenotype: Planned for Phase I trial (2027) with dose escalation

open_questions: Will CAR‑DC1s be effective against low‑B7‑H3 expressing tumors?
What is the optimal dosing route (IT vs IV) for different solid tumor types?

talk_title: Decoding the medulloblastoma serpinosome prioritizes the oncofetal antigen GPC2 for potent CAR T cell therapy

biomarkers.name: GPC2 density
biomarkers.finding: Median >6,000 molecules/cell in medulloblastoma specimens, above detection threshold

biomarkers.name: CD133 co‑expression
biomarkers.finding: GPC2‑high cells enriched for CD133+ stem‑like phenotype

MOA_summary: GPC2‑directed CAR T cells with CD28 costimulatory domain and c‑Jun overexpression achieve low‑antigen detection, target GPC2‑high tumor‑initiating cells, and overcome antigen‑loss escape, leading to durable tumor control in medulloblastoma models and early clinical safety.
key_findings: GPC2 identified as top oncofetal surface antigen in medulloblastoma via surfaceome analysis.
CAR T cells required CD28 transmembrane domain to detect ~5,000‑6,000 GPC2 molecules/cell.
c‑Jun overexpression reduced antigen‑low escape and enhanced cytokine production.
Phase 1 trial (NCT‑pending) enrolled 4 patients with no dose‑limiting toxicities and detectable CAR T cells in CSF.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Tumor escape observed with reduced GPC2 density; c‑Jun engineering mitigated this.

combination_strategies.rationale: Lower antigen detection threshold and resist exhaustion
combination_strategies.phenotype: Higher IFN‑γ and IL‑2 secretion against low‑GPC2 cells

open_questions: Will GPC2 CAR T cells be effective against heterogeneous CNS tumors with variable GPC2 expression?
What is the optimal intra‑cerebral dosing schedule?

talk_title: NK TCR cells, a next‑generation platform expanding NK cell therapeutic potential

models.name: NK TCR basket trial (myeloma & sarcoma)
models.genotype_or_key_features: Dose escalation, off‑the‑shelf cord‑blood NK product

clinical_data.population: NY-ESO-1 positive multiple myeloma, heavily pretreated

MOA_summary: Cord‑blood NK cells are engineered to express a full CD3 complex and a tumor‑specific TCR (e.g., PRAME or NY‑ESO‑1), coupling antigen‑specific TCR signaling with innate NK cytotoxic receptors and IL‑15 support, resulting in rapid, serial killing of target cells with minimal off‑target activity.
key_findings: TCR‑NK cells killed target tumor spheroids faster than conventional TCR‑T cells (≈2 min vs ≈6 min per target).
In vivo, NK TCR treatment cured 5/5 myeloma‑bearing mice with durable engraftment in blood, bone marrow, and spleen.
No off‑target toxicity observed in peptide‑pulsed LCL assays or in treated patients (no CRS, ICANS, or GvHD).
First patient with NY‑ESO‑1‑positive myeloma achieved complete remission lasting >2 years after a single 22 M dose.
open_questions: Will NK TCR cells retain activity against MHC‑downregulated tumors?
Can additional cytokine support further improve persistence?

talk_title: Universal T cell therapy screening library and preclinical models of CAR T cell therapy

models.name: Toledo xenograft mouse model
models.genotype_or_key_features: IV CAR T infusion, tumor burden monitored by BLI

MOA_summary: CRISPR‑all enables multiplexed knock‑in of CAR constructs and gene perturbations into the TRAC locus of primary T cells; the CACTUS library screens combinatorial edits to identify genetic configurations that enhance CAR T cell proliferation and antitumor activity against lymphoma.
key_findings: Synthetic chimeras (e.g., FAS‑OX40) and stemness factors (TCF7L1) consistently enriched across multiple screens.
Lymphoma‑specific hits (ARID family genes) emerged only in Toledo co‑culture screens.
In vivo screen identified similar top hits, with ~71% concordance between in vitro, in vivo, and deep‑learning model predictions.
open_questions: Which of the identified gene edits translate to improved clinical response in patients?
Can CACTEX2 be refined to a minimal set for rapid clinical testing?

talk_title: Triple disruption of CCDC6, ZEB1B, and ASB2 promotes therapeutic efficacy of GPC3‑targeted CAR T cells

targets.dependency_context: Triple knockout with ASB2 and CBLB

models.name: IL-15 armored GPC3 CAR T trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: Patients with advanced HCC or gastric cancer

models.name: HCC mouse model
models.genotype_or_key_features: IL-15 GPC3 CAR T cells with triple knockout (CBLB, CCDC6, ASB2)

clinical_data.population: IL-15 armored GPC3 CAR T recipient
IL-15 armored GPC3 CAR T trial

MOA_summary: IL‑15‑armed GPC3 CAR T cells achieve high expansion; integration‑mediated disruption of CBLB, CCDC6, and ASB2 further enhances proliferation and antitumor activity, likely by relieving negative regulation of TCR signaling and promoting survival pathways.
key_findings: One patient showed dramatic tumor reduction in 16 days with peak CAR T expansion of 14.7 B cells.
Clonal analysis revealed a dominant TCR clone not present in the infusion product.
Retroviral integrations disrupted CBLB, CCDC6, and ASB2, correlating with the expanded clone.
Triple knockout of these genes in IL‑15 CAR T cells led to greater T cell expansion and complete tumor control in HCC xenograft mice.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Potential autoimmunity risk from CBLB loss; however, monoallelic disruption appeared safe in patient.

combination_strategies.rationale: Provide autonomous survival signal
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced expansion and persistence

combination_strategies.rationale: Remove negative regulators to boost proliferation
combination_strategies.phenotype: Increased expansion and complete tumor eradication in mice

open_questions: Is disruption of all three genes required for maximal benefit?
Does IL‑15 synergize with the knockouts or act independently?

patent_or_ip_hints: patent related to triple knockout strategy

session_title: Advanced Antibody Conjugate and Targeted Therapeutic Platforms (Part 1)
talk_title: The SynthBody Platform: A Multifunctional, Multivalent, Multispecific ADC Platform
MOA_summary: The SynthBody platform utilizes a multivalent, multispecific architecture (Y6, X8, X12 formats) to create combinatorial synthetic targets that mimic high-density antigen expression (like HER2). By employing multi-tiered logic gate control and optimized 3D geometry, the platform increases binding strength, internalization rates, and payload delivery compared to monospecific ADCs. This cooperative binding approach allows for higher potency and resistance to soluble antigen sinks.
key_findings: Y6 synBody targeting CD38, BCMA, and GPRC5D showed in vitro potency approximately 80-fold stronger than BCMA-targeting belantamab in a high-bar myeloma resistance model.
SynBody demonstrated log-order increases in binding strength, internalization, trafficking to lysosomes, and payload delivery compared to monospecific controls.
SynBody is resistant to the presence of soluble targets, whereas belantamab is highly affected.
In vivo dose-ranging experiments showed 100% tumor growth inhibition (TGI) and 2 complete responses (CRs) at the highest dose tested with no body weight loss.
SynBody achieved equivalent efficacy to belantamab MMAE with 11X lower exposure.
A synBody targeting CD19, CD22, and CD79B showed log-order increases in killing potency compared to polatuzumab in both Ramos and HT NHL models.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Designed to overcome soluble antigen sinks that typically reduce the efficacy of monospecific ADCs.

session_title: Advanced Antibody Conjugate and Targeted Therapeutic Platforms (Part 1)
talk_title: M7437: A Novel Anti-LY6E ADC with a Top1 Inhibitor Payload

biomarkers.name: LY6E mRNA
biomarkers.finding: Correlated with poor overall survival

biomarkers.name: LY6E
biomarkers.finding: Activity observed even in models with low to medium expression, suggesting an unselected approach may be possible.

models.name: Phase 1 trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: Range of tumor indications with high LY6E expression

MOA_summary: M7437 is an anti-LY6E ADC utilizing a transglutaminase-conjugated exatecan payload (DAR 4) and an LALA mutation to reduce Fc gamma receptor binding. It targets LY6E-expressing cancer cells, internalizes to the lysosome to release the Top1 inhibitor, and leverages a potent bystander effect to kill neighboring cells regardless of target expression. Preclinical data shows superior activity over MMAE-based benchmarks and other competitors like DATO-DXd in several PDX models.
key_findings: M7437 showed superior anti-tumor activity compared to 9B12 MMAE in head and neck and ovarian PDX models.
In a panel of PDX models, 17 reached the cutoff for preclinical regression and 8 showed complete regression across TNBC, ovarian, head and neck, NSCLC, pancreatic, and gastric cancer.
M7437 demonstrated superior activity compared to DATO-DXd in squamous and SCLC PDX models expressing both LY6E and Trop-2.
M7437 showed promising efficacy in a gastric cancer PDX model that was primary resistant to Enhertu (T-DXd).
GLP toxicity study in cynomolgus monkeys showed the drug was well tolerated, with primary toxicities being hematological (lymphocytes, neutrophils, reticulocytes).
In vitro PBMC assays showed proliferating NK cells were sensitive, while resting NK, B, and T cells were unaffected.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Bystander effect allows for efficacy in tumors with heterogeneous target expression.
open_questions: Discriminating how much bone marrow toxicity is due to LY6E expression on immune cells versus the known toxicity of the exatecan payload.

session_title: Advanced Antibody Conjugate and Targeted Therapeutic Platforms (Part 1)
talk_title: Preclinical Evaluation of Target-Mediated Clearance and Alternative Payloads in Cadherin 17-Directed ADCs

biomarkers.name: P-gp
biomarkers.finding: P-gp expression drives resistance to MMAE payloads but not to exatecan payloads in colorectal cancer.

biomarkers.name: Cadherin 17
biomarkers.finding: Expressed in 90% of colorectal cancer samples; normal expression is limited to lateral membranes of colon/small intestine.

models.name: Humanized mouse (BioCyteGen)
models.genotype_or_key_features: Expresses human Cadherin 17 domain in gut

models.name: CDX models
models.genotype_or_key_features: Cadherin 17 positive colorectal and pancreatic cancer

MOA_summary: The study investigated the impact of target-mediated clearance (TMC) and P-gp efflux on Cadherin 17-directed ADCs. Results showed that normal expression of Cadherin 17 in the gut causes rapid clearance of the ADC (4-fold higher in humanized mice). Additionally, P-gp expression in colorectal cancer cells confers resistance to MMAE payloads, which can be overcome by using a Top1 inhibitor payload (exatecan) or P-gp inhibitors.
key_findings: Humanized mice expressing human Cadherin 17 showed rapid clearance of the ADC, with levels falling below detection within 48 hours, indicating a significant tissue sink in the gut.
In P-gp positive colorectal cancer models, exatecan-bearing ADCs maintained activity while MMAE-bearing ADCs lost activity.
Pharmacological inhibition of P-gp with ensequiror restored the response of MMAE-bearing ADCs in resistant LS513 cells.
Genetic knockout of P-gp in LS513 cells restored sensitivity to MMAE-bearing ADCs in vitro and in vivo.
Switching to an exatecan payload does not mitigate the target-mediated clearance liability caused by normal tissue expression.
TMC is driven by the antibody's binding to normal tissue and is independent of the ADC construct (free antibody showed same clearance rate).
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: P-gp pumps in colorectal cancer act as a primary resistance mechanism for hydrophobic payloads like MMAE.

combination_strategies.rationale: Inhibit P-gp efflux to restore MMAE payload efficacy
combination_strategies.phenotype: restoration of responses in LS513 cells

open_questions: Whether P-gp status can be clinically validated as a biomarker to guide payload choice for Cadherin 17 ADCs.

session_title: Advanced Antibody Conjugate and Targeted Therapeutic Platforms (Part 1)
talk_title: Nuzepha: A Bicycle Drug Conjugate Targeting EphA2 in Solid Tumors
tumor_types: head and neck squamous cell carcinoma (HNSCC)

biomarkers.name: EphA2 expression
biomarkers.finding: Higher expression associated with better response; Cal27 (lowest H-score) was least sensitive.

MOA_summary: Nuzepha is a bicycle drug conjugate (BDC) consisting of a small bicyclic peptide (4.5 kDa) targeting EphA2 linked to an MMAE payload. Due to its small size, it exhibits rapid renal clearance (half-life < 1 hour) and superior tumor penetration compared to antibodies. This pharmacology creates a 'drug-free interval' between weekly doses, which widens the therapeutic window and avoids the severe coagulopathy and hematological toxicities seen with EphA2-targeting antibody-based conjugates.
key_findings: Nuzepha demonstrated significant efficacy in HNSCC xenografts (Fadu, SCC9, Cal27), with doses of 3 and 5 mg/kg producing near complete regressions in aggressive lines.
The BDC format avoids the bleeding toxicity (coagulopathy) associated with antibody-based EphA2 conjugates.
The small size of the molecule allows for rapid delivery and penetration, making episodic dosing sufficient for efficacy.
The binding affinity for EphA2 is 2 nM.
An MMAF version of the construct was found to be decidedly less potent than the MMAE version.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: The short half-life is a design feature to reduce systemic toxicity while maintaining efficacy through rapid tumor penetration.

session_title: Advanced Antibody Conjugate and Targeted Therapeutic Platforms (Part 2)
talk_title: Targeting the Non-Shed Domain of MUC16 with a Proprietary Topo I ADC Platform

biomarkers.name: CA125
biomarkers.finding: CA125 is a cleaved product of MUC16; targeting the non-shed domain avoids target-mediated drug disposition caused by high microgram per ml levels of shed CA125.

MOA_summary: The speaker presents HOX16, an ADC targeting the non-shed domain of MUC16 to avoid target-mediated drug disposition caused by shed CA125. The platform utilizes a proprietary Topo I inhibitor payload with a DAR-6 configuration, PEG masking for hydrophobicity, and a 'carbon bridge cysteine repairing' technology to replace interchain disulfides for enhanced stability.
key_findings: HOX16 antibody binds the non-shed portion of MUC16 with sub-nanomolar affinity and is minimally impacted by exogenous CA125, unlike the DMAC antibody which showed a 22-fold IC50 shift.
HOX16 ADC demonstrated sub-nanomolar internalization efficiency.
Bystander effect was demonstrated by the ability to eliminate 100% of cells in a 3:1 mixture where 33% were antigen-negative (Raji-Luc).
In ovarian CDX models, HOX16 caused tumor regressions at 1 mg/kg, whereas the original DAR-4 MMIE construct (DMAC) was incapable of causing regressions at the same dose.
In vitro plasma stability showed <0.4% payload release over 21 days, which is >10-fold higher stability compared to the DRUG-DXd platform (which showed >4% release).
NHP tox studies showed the ADC was well-tolerated up to 60 mg/kg with no mortalities.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Target-mediated drug disposition (TMDD) is a significant challenge for MUC16 due to high levels of shed CA125 in the bloodstream.
open_questions: What are the lower bounds of MUC16 expression by which efficacy can still be achieved in vivo?

session_title: Advanced Antibody Conjugate and Targeted Therapeutic Platforms (Part 2)
talk_title: The Prism AIR Assay for Characterizing Antibody Internalization and ADC Sensitivity

speaker.name: Gillian Eskra
speaker.affiliation: Prisma Lab at the Broad Institute at MIT

biomarkers.name: SLC46A3
biomarkers.finding: Low expression of the lysosomal transporter SLC46A3 confers resistance to non-cleavable linkers with the MMAF payload.

biomarkers.name: SLAFN11
biomarkers.finding: SLAFN11 expression is associated with sensitivity to the payload in ceticizumab gavitan.

MOA_summary: The speaker describes the Prism AIR (Antibody Internalization Reporter) assay, which uses a universal secondary Fab-MMAF conjugate to mimic ADC function. This allows for the functional validation of any humanized IgG antibody's internalization and killing capacity without needing to synthesize a custom ADC for every target.
key_findings: Screening of 50 ADCs across 25 targets showed that sensitivity strongly correlates with target antigen expression (gene expression and copy number amplification).
Prism AIR showed strong concordance with actual ADC data for the target F3 (tozotamab), validating the AIR format as a proxy for ADC activity.
Differential analysis between datopotamab deruxtecan and datopotamab AIR (MMAF) identified SLC46A3 as a driver of sensitivity for non-cleavable linkers.
Ceticizumab showed poor correlation with its target antigen in the AIR format, which the speaker attributes to the instability of its specific linker chemistry releasing free payload.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Payload-specific differences can lead to outliers in target correlation, such as the difference between SN-38 and MMAF payloads.
open_questions: How do 3D cell culture results differ from the current 2D Prism screening results for ADCs?

session_title: Advanced Antibody Conjugate and Targeted Therapeutic Platforms (Part 2)
talk_title: Preclinical and Clinical Evaluation of STRO-004, a Tissue Factor-Targeted ADC

biomarkers.name: Tissue Factor (F3)
biomarkers.finding: Higher tissue factor expression correlates with greater tumor shrinkage, though activity was observed across the full expression spectrum.

biomarkers.name: Schlafen 11
biomarkers.finding: Combined high Tissue Factor and high Schlafen 11 RNA levels resulted in the highest ORR.

biomarkers.name: CAV1/CAVIN1
biomarkers.finding: High expression of CAV1 and CAVIN1 (involved in internalization) was associated with response to STRO-004.

models.name: Mouse Clinical Trial (MCT)
models.genotype_or_key_features: n=70 models; head and neck, NSCLC, pancreatic, CRC; enriched for TF expression

MOA_summary: STRO-004 is a site-specific DAR8 ADC targeting Tissue Factor (F3) with an exatecan (Topo I inhibitor) payload and a beta-glucuronidase cleavable linker. It is designed to reduce bleeding risk and ILD (via lack of FC gamma function) while increasing payload delivery and bystander killing compared to first-generation TF-MMAE ADCs.
key_findings: STRO-004 achieved an overall ORR of 64% and DCR of 90% across 70 PDX models in a mouse clinical trial.
In head and neck models, STRO-004 showed superior ORR (57% vs 38%) and median PFS (45 vs 27 days) compared to the TF-MMAE benchmark.
In pancreatic models, STRO-004 showed an ORR of 70% compared to 50% for the TF-MMAE benchmark.
STRO-004 was well-tolerated in NHPs up to 50 mg/kg with no significant heme toxicities or bleeding/eye toxicity.
Response is multifactorial; combining Tissue Factor and Schlafen 11 expression levels better captures response heterogeneity than either alone.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: The use of para-azido phenylalanine via code expansion ensures a homogeneous DAR8, which the speaker claims leads to higher stability and lower systemic payload release.
open_questions: Does the distribution of Tissue Factor between epithelial and stromal cells impact efficacy?
Do extra-tumoral beta-glucuronidase levels correlate with response?

talk_title: Phase I intraperitoneal mesothelin-targeted CAR T therapy in esophageal and gastric cancer with peritoneal carcinomatosis

biomarkers.name: Mesothelin IHC ≥25%
biomarkers.finding: Patients required >25% mesothelin positivity for enrollment

models.name: Phase I intraperitoneal CAR T study
models.genotype_or_key_features: mesothelin-positive peritoneal carcinomatosis, median 3-5 prior lines

MOA_summary: An autologous mesothelin-targeted CAR T (M28 Zeta-1XXPD1-DNR) with CD28 costimulation and a PD-1 dominant negative receptor is delivered intraperitoneally, providing regional tumor exposure, intrinsic checkpoint blockade, and prolonged peripheral persistence beyond day 100, but antitumor activity is limited by heterogeneous mesothelin expression.
key_findings: Screened 54 patients, 19 (35%) were mesothelin-positive and eligible.
Seven patients received intraperitoneal CAR T; no dose‑limiting toxicities observed.
CAR T cells persisted in peripheral blood beyond 100 days.
Best radiographic response was stable disease; no objective responses observed.
Low‑grade CRS was common and manageable; one patient experienced grade 3 IE‑CHS resolved with steroids.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Limited efficacy attributed to heterogeneous mesothelin expression across tumor lesions.
Off‑target, on‑tumor toxicities (pharyngitis) linked to mesothelin expression in tonsillar epithelium.

combination_strategies.rationale: Prior intrapleural CAR T plus systemic PD‑1 blockade showed safety and efficacy.
combination_strategies.phenotype: potentially enhanced antitumor activity in mesothelioma

open_questions: Will selecting patients with higher mesothelin expression (>75%) improve responses?
Can combining with systemic checkpoint inhibition overcome heterogeneous antigen loss?

talk_title: Phase I trial of UCMYM802 (Lego‑STIM armored mesothelin CAR T) in advanced solid tumors

biomarkers.name: Mesothelin IHC ≥25%
biomarkers.finding: Patients required mesothelin positivity for enrollment

models.name: First‑in‑human Phase I UCMYM802 study
models.genotype_or_key_features: mesothelin‑positive advanced solid tumors, median 3 prior lines

MOA_summary: UCMYM802 is an mRNA‑CAR T product encoding an anti‑mesothelin scFv with 4‑1BB costimulation and a membrane‑bound CD14‑CD28 Lego‑STIM construct that enhances innate and adaptive immunity, yielding rapid but transient CAR expression and dose‑dependent T‑cell activation.
key_findings: Nine patients received at least one infusion; no dose‑limiting toxicities observed.
ORR 28.6% and DCR 57.1% across evaluable patients, with two partial responses.
Transient CAR expression peaks within 1 hour post‑infusion and declines after 8 hours.
Higher dose levels correlated with increased CAR and Lego expression and with central memory CD8 T‑cell expansion.
Low incidence of high‑grade CRS; cytokine release was modest compared with protein CAR T.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Transient mRNA expression may limit durability of response.
Potential anti‑drug antibodies (ADA) suggested as cause for reduced PK on repeat dosing.

combination_strategies.rationale: Evaluate safety and activity of novel mRNA CAR T alone
combination_strategies.phenotype: demonstrated preliminary efficacy

open_questions: Will repeated dosing overcome transient expression without increasing toxicity?
Can combining with checkpoint blockade enhance durability?

talk_title: First‑in‑human study of KSQ001EX CRISPR‑edited SOX1‑inactivated TIL therapy

targets.dependency_context: SOX1‑knockout in autologous TILs for melanoma

biomarkers.name: CD8 cytotoxic T‑cell infiltration
biomarkers.finding: Increased CD8 cytotoxic phenotype in tumor biopsies post‑treatment

models.name: KSQ001EX Phase I melanoma trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: Patients refractory to PD‑1 inhibitors, 1–10 B TIL dose, ± IL‑2

MOA_summary: Autologous tumor‑infiltrating lymphocytes are edited with CRISPR‑Cas9 to delete SOX1, a negative regulator of cytokine signaling, resulting in enhanced expansion, persistence, and a CD8‑centric cytotoxic phenotype after infusion.
key_findings: Twelve patients received KSQ001EX; no dose‑limiting toxicities observed.
ORR 33% with one patient achieving a durable partial response >12 months.
Higher cell doses (>5.25 B) correlated with tumor reduction in 100% of patients.
Engineered TILs expanded and persisted in peripheral blood for up to 500 days.
Tumor biopsies showed increased CD8 cytotoxic T cells and remodeling of the microenvironment.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Efficacy appears dose‑dependent; lower doses may require IL‑2 support.

combination_strategies.rationale: IL‑2 may boost expansion of edited TILs at lower cell doses
combination_strategies.phenotype: slightly improved responses in IL‑2 cohort

open_questions: Will dual editing of SOX1 and RAG1 improve efficacy without added toxicity?
What is the optimal cell dose to balance efficacy and manufacturing feasibility?

talk_title: Five‑year overall survival of tebentafusp in previously untreated metastatic uveal melanoma (Phase III)

biomarkers.name: Baseline ctDNA detectability
biomarkers.finding: Patients with undetectable baseline ctDNA had superior OS (HR 0.5)

biomarkers.name: ctDNA clearance at week 9
biomarkers.finding: 37% cleared ctDNA; clearance associated with OS HR 0.32

MOA_summary: Tebentafusp is an affinity‑enhanced soluble T‑cell receptor fused to anti‑CD3 that redirects polyclonal T cells to GP100‑presenting uveal melanoma cells, leading to tumor cell killing and durable immune activation independent of radiographic response.
key_findings: Five‑year OS hazard ratio 0.67 with survival doubling from 8% to 16% at 5 years.
Disease control rate improved to 46% versus 27% with investigator's choice.
Baseline ctDNA undetectable patients had markedly better OS (HR 0.5).
ctDNA clearance at week 9 occurred in 37% of patients and predicted superior survival (HR 0.32).
Treatment beyond radiographic progression yielded additional tumor regressions and prolonged post‑progression survival.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Radiographic RECIST responses underestimated survival benefit; ctDNA proved more sensitive.
Heterogeneous benefit across disease burden and LDH subgroups, but overall favorable.

combination_strategies.rationale: Assess additive benefit after progression
combination_strategies.phenotype: 86% of 5‑year survivors crossed over to tebentafusp

open_questions: Can earlier ctDNA monitoring guide treatment continuation versus stopping at progression?
Will combining tebentafusp with other immunomodulators further improve outcomes?

talk_title: Phase 1b/2a study of RZ001 adenoviral HTERT‑targeting ribozyme with atezolizumab‑bevacizumab in HCC

biomarkers.name: hTERT RNA‑scope positivity
biomarkers.finding: All enrolled tumors confirmed hTERT expression

models.name: RZ001 + atezolizumab/bevacizumab Phase 1b/2a
models.genotype_or_key_features: Unresectable HCC, no prior systemic therapy, BCLC B/C

MOA_summary: RZ001 is an adenoviral vector delivering a trans‑splicing ribozyme that replaces hTERT mRNA with a suicide HSV‑TK gene; in the presence of ganciclovir, tumor cells undergo DNA replication arrest and apoptosis while also inducing CD4/CD8 infiltration and VEGF down‑regulation, synergizing with atezolizumab‑bevacizumab.
key_findings: Intratumoral RZ001 combined with atezolizumab‑bevacizumab yielded ORR 40% (RECIST) and 61.5% (mRECIST).
No dose‑limiting toxicities; grade 3/4 treatment‑related AEs occurred in 53% but were manageable.
Rapid onset of responses with durable disease control observed at data cutoff.
All patients expressed hTERT; viral delivery was well tolerated without systemic replication.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Potential resistance via loss of hTERT expression not yet observed.

combination_strategies.rationale: Checkpoint inhibition and anti‑angiogenesis may amplify immune activation from RZ001‑induced tumor cell death
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced tumor regression and ORR

open_questions: Would RZ001 synergize with more potent ICI backbones (e.g., tremelimumab/ durvalumab)?
Can biomarker‑guided selection (e.g., hTERT expression level) refine patient benefit?

talk_title: Phase 1/2 trial of LOAD‑703 adenoviral CD40L/4‑1BBL vector with gemcitabine/nab‑paclitaxel ± atezolizumab in advanced pancreatic cancer

biomarkers.name: CD8 effector memory T cells
biomarkers.finding: Increased in blood after LOAD‑703 administration

biomarkers.name: Treg/CD8 ratio
biomarkers.finding: Decreased ratio indicating favorable immune shift

models.name: LUCON‑001 arm 2 (LOAD‑703 + chemo + atezolizumab)
models.genotype_or_key_features: Advanced PDAC, any line, receiving gemcitabine/nab‑paclitaxel

clinical_data.value: consistent with chemotherapy; no new safety signals

MOA_summary: LOAD‑703 is an oncolytic adenovirus serotype 5/35 encoding membrane‑bound CD40L and 4‑1BBL, delivered intratumorally to activate dendritic cells, convert M2 to M1 macrophages, and provide co‑stimulatory signals to T cells, thereby turning a cold pancreatic tumor microenvironment into a Th1‑type inflamed state that synergizes with chemotherapy and PD‑L1 blockade.
key_findings: In arm 2 (LOAD‑703 + chemo + atezolizumab), ORR was 38% with three complete responses.
First‑line patients (n=13) achieved median OS 14.7 months and 1‑year survival 62%.
Peripheral CD8 effector memory T cells increased while Tregs decreased after treatment.
Tumor biopsies showed upregulation of Th1 cytokines and down‑regulation of fibroblast/TGF‑β signatures.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Patients with rapid progression exhibited high IL‑6 levels, suggesting a potential resistance pathway.

combination_strategies.rationale: Standard chemotherapy may reduce myeloid suppressors and synergize with immune activation
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced response rates vs chemotherapy alone

combination_strategies.rationale: PD‑L1 blockade may overcome up‑regulated checkpoint after viral immune activation
combination_strategies.phenotype: additional complete responses observed

open_questions: Will a randomized four‑arm trial clarify the contribution of LOAD‑703 versus chemo alone?
Can earlier‑line use (neoadjuvant) convert unresectable disease to resectable?

talk_title: Risk stratification and emerging intravesical/systemic therapies for non‑muscle invasive bladder cancer

speaker.name: Dr. Philip Abbash
speaker.affiliation: Fox Chase Cancer Center, Surgeon Scientist

MOA_summary: Abbash highlighted that BCG‑refractory non‑muscle invasive bladder cancer can be treated with checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab), intravesical adenoviral vectors (Adstiladrin), IL‑15 agonists (Anctiva) and FGFR3‑targeted agents, each achieving high complete response rates, and emphasized the need for molecular risk stratification to match patients to these modalities.
key_findings: Pembrolizumab achieved a 41% complete response rate in BCG‑unresponsive NMIBC.
Adstiladrin (intravesical adenoviral) yielded a 53% complete response rate for CIS at 3 months.
Anctiva (IL‑15 agonist) demonstrated a 71% complete response rate in BCG‑unresponsive disease.
Enfortumab vedotin combined with pembrolizumab (EV‑pembro) doubled median overall survival to 31.5 months in metastatic bladder cancer.

combination_strategies.rationale: Combine checkpoint inhibition with intravesical therapy to enhance immune activation in BCG‑unresponsive NMIBC
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved complete response rates (41% CR) compared to historical BCG alone

combination_strategies.rationale: Target FGFR3‑mutant tumors with intravesical gemcitabine pretzel for personalized therapy
combination_strategies.phenotype: High response rates in FGFR3‑positive patients (exact numbers not disclosed)

open_questions: How can molecular subtyping be integrated into routine clinical decision‑making for NMIBC?
What is the cost‑effectiveness threshold for adopting these high‑price bladder‑preserving therapies?

talk_title: Adjuvant ctDNA‑guided immunotherapy and peri‑operative enfortumab‑vedotin + pembrolizumab in muscle‑invasive bladder cancer

speaker.name: Dr. Matthew Galsky
speaker.affiliation: Icahn School of Medicine at Mount Sinai, Lillian and Howard Stratton Professor of Medicine

targets.dependency_context: adjuvant escalation arm of MODERN trial

biomarkers.name: ctDNA (Signatera assay)
biomarkers.finding: Patients with detectable ctDNA after cystectomy derived DFS and OS benefit from atezolizumab versus placebo (HR not disclosed).

biomarkers.name: urine tumor DNA (V96 assay)
biomarkers.finding: 100% of patients with clinical complete response had undetectable urine tumor DNA; detectable utDNA predicted local recurrence.

models.name: CheckMate 274
models.genotype_or_key_features: adjuvant nivolumab vs placebo after cystectomy

models.name: IMvigor11
models.genotype_or_key_features: adjuvant atezolizumab vs placebo in ctDNA‑positive patients

models.name: MODERN
models.genotype_or_key_features: adjuvant nivolumab ± LAG‑3 inhibitor (escalation) or surveillance (de‑escalation) based on ctDNA

models.name: EV‑pembro peri‑operative study
models.genotype_or_key_features: neoadjuvant EV‑pembro followed by cystectomy vs cisplatin‑based chemo or surgery alone

clinical_data.value: significant improvement vs placebo (exact HR not disclosed)

clinical_data.population: chemo + PD‑1 blockade neoadjuvant trial with bladder‑preserving option

clinical_data.value: higher in nivolumab + LAG‑3 arm (phase 2 endpoint)

MOA_summary: Galsky presented that adjuvant checkpoint blockade improves DFS, especially when patients are selected by ctDNA positivity; ctDNA‑guided atezolizumab (IMvigor11) showed benefit, and the MODERN trial tests escalation with LAG‑3 inhibition versus surveillance. In the peri‑operative setting, enfortumab‑vedotin + pembrolizumab yields a 57% pathologic CR, suggesting a bladder‑sparing strategy when combined with systemic therapy.
key_findings: Adjuvant nivolumab improves disease‑free survival after cystectomy (CheckMate 274).
In IMvigor11, ctDNA‑positive patients derived a DFS benefit from atezolizumab versus placebo.
MODERN trial uses ctDNA to randomize patients to nivolumab ± LAG‑3 (escalation) or surveillance (de‑escalation).
Peri‑operative EV‑pembro achieved a 57.1% pathologic complete response, markedly higher than cisplatin‑based neoadjuvant therapy.
Clinical complete response after neoadjuvant chemo‑PD‑1 therapy predicts excellent metastasis‑free survival, and undetectable ctDNA/utDNA correlates with true remission.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Patients with detectable ctDNA after cystectomy are at high risk of recurrence despite surgery, indicating residual micrometastatic disease.
Urine tumor DNA can detect residual bladder disease even when blood ctDNA is negative, highlighting compartment‑specific resistance.

combination_strategies.rationale: Combine PD‑1 blockade with Nectin‑4‑targeted ADC to enhance systemic and local tumor killing
combination_strategies.phenotype: 57% pathologic CR and improved event‑free survival

combination_strategies.rationale: Dual checkpoint blockade may overcome resistance in ctDNA‑positive high‑risk patients
combination_strategies.phenotype: Higher ctDNA clearance rates in phase 2 of MODERN

open_questions: What is the optimal ctDNA threshold and timing for initiating adjuvant immunotherapy?
Can urine tumor DNA replace blood ctDNA for surveillance, or should both be used?

talk_title: Modeling APOBEC‑driven resistance and rational sequential combinations in bladder cancer

speaker.name: Dr. Bishoy Faltas
speaker.affiliation: Weill Cornell Medicine, Medical Oncologist & Physician Scientist

models.name: patient‑derived bladder cancer organoid
models.genotype_or_key_features: wild‑type for HER2/FGFR3, used to test EV resistance

MOA_summary: Faltas demonstrated that transient APOBEC3A expression creates double‑strand DNA breaks, induces late‑S‑phase stasis, opens chromatin at the SOX2 locus, and via FOXM1 binding establishes a durable SOX2‑high state (DASP) that confers multi‑drug resistance, enhanced migration, and EMT‑like plasticity; degradation of SOX2 with a PROTAC restores drug sensitivity.
key_findings: APOBEC3A exposure increased neratinib IC50 4.8‑fold and conferred cross‑resistance to HER2‑ADC (T‑DXd), FGFR3 inhibitors, and enfortumab‑vedotin.
A3A‑exposed cells showed persistent late‑S‑phase stasis, γH2AX and p‑RPA32 elevation, and sustained chromatin accessibility at the SOX2 promoter and distal enhancer.
FOXM1 binds the SOX2 promoter 11‑fold more in A3A‑exposed cells, driving SOX2 transcription.
SOX2‑PROTAC sensitized A3A‑driven resistant cells to trastuzumab‑deruxtecan, confirming SOX2 as a therapeutic vulnerability.
DASP signature (FOXM1, SOX2, S‑phase, EMT genes) predicts poorer progression‑free and overall survival across TCGA pan‑cancer cohorts.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: A3A‑induced DASP state confers broad cross‑resistance to multiple ADCs and TKIs, independent of target expression.
The resistance is not recapitulated by generic DNA damage (doxorubicin) or TGF‑β‑induced EMT, indicating specificity to deaminase activity.

combination_strategies.rationale: Degrade the master regulator SOX2 to reverse the DASP‑mediated multi‑drug resistant phenotype
combination_strategies.phenotype: Restored sensitivity to HER2‑ADC trastuzumab‑deruxtecan in A3A‑exposed cells

open_questions: Can DASP be detected in patient tumors using the transcriptional signature, and does it predict clinical resistance to ADCs?
Would inhibition of FOXM1 upstream of SOX2 provide an alternative strategy to prevent DASP formation?

patent_or_ip_hints: US2025/0123456 – SOX2 PROTAC for bladder cancer

talk_title: CHD7 drives lineage plasticity and replication competence in neuroendocrine prostate cancer

speaker.name: Ace Xie
speaker.affiliation: University of British Columbia, Dr. Weizhu Wan's lab

models.name: 321-321 RPDX
models.genotype_or_key_features: caspase-driven transition from adenoma to NEPC

MOA_summary: CHD7 is progressively up‑regulated during androgen deprivation‑induced NEPC development, where it maintains open chromatin at lineage and replication stress genes, promotes NE transcription factor expression, and sustains ATR checkpoint activity, thereby driving lineage plasticity and cisplatin resistance.
key_findings: CHD7 expression is minimal in pre‑castration prostate tissue, induced early post‑castration, and sustained in terminal NEPC lesions.
CHD7 knockdown reduces NE marker expression, cell growth, DNA synthesis, and increases DNA damage and apoptosis.
CHD7 directly binds and maintains accessibility at ~1,805 loci, regulating 563 transcriptional targets involved in neural fate and DNA replication.
Loss of CHD7 impairs ATR checkpoint phosphorylation and sensitizes NEPC cells to cisplatin (IC50 markedly reduced).
Combining cisplatin with ATR checkpoint inhibition further decreases viability compared with either agent alone.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: CHD7 up‑regulation confers cisplatin tolerance via ATR checkpoint activation.

combination_strategies.rationale: CHD7 maintains ATR checkpoint activity that mitigates cisplatin‑induced replication stress
combination_strategies.phenotype: CHD7 knockdown lowers cisplatin IC50; combination with ATR inhibitor further reduces viability

open_questions: What upstream signals drive early CHD7 induction in NEPC?
Which transcription factors cooperate with CHD7 at its binding sites?

talk_title: KDM5B‑SOX9 signaling drives therapy‑induced neuroendocrine prostate cancer

biomarkers.name: NSE
biomarkers.finding: KDM5B knockout reduces NSE expression in NEPC cells

biomarkers.name: SYN
biomarkers.finding: KDM5B knockout reduces SYN expression

MOA_summary: KDM5B up‑regulation in therapy‑induced NEPC activates SOX9 transcription, which drives neuroendocrine marker expression; loss of KDM5B diminishes SOX9 and NE markers, while overexpression rescues them, establishing a KDM5B‑SOX9 axis essential for NE differentiation.
key_findings: KDM5B and SOX9 are co‑overexpressed in ~46% of NEPC cases and correlate with NSE levels.
KDM5B knockout reduces SOX9, NSE, and SYN expression in NEPC cell lines.
Restoring KDM5B rescues SOX9 and NE markers in a dose‑dependent manner.
Castrated PTEN‑p53 mice develop NE recurrence with high KDM5B, SOX9, NSE, and SYN.
Triple knockout of PTEN‑p53‑KDM5B prevents tumor recurrence and NE marker expression.
open_questions: Does KDM5B directly demethylate the SOX9 promoter or act via co‑factors?
Can pharmacologic inhibition of KDM5B prevent therapy‑induced NEPC in patients?

talk_title: Promoter methylation gains in aging and colon cancer are independent of replication
MOA_summary: Long‑term organoid culture and BRAF V600E induction reveal that CpG‑island promoter hypermethylation accumulates with age independent of cell division, whereas non‑CGI hypomethylation tracks with replication, indicating distinct mechanisms for methylation gains versus losses during tumorigenesis.
key_findings: Global CpG methylation does not change acutely after BRAF V600E induction.
CGI promoters gain methylation over months in both control and BRAF lineages, independent of replication.
Non‑CGI sites lose methylation in a replication‑dependent manner, accelerated in BRAF organoids.
Transcription factor enrichment differs: control lineage shows stress/viral TFs; BRAF lineage enriches cancer‑related TFs.
External datasets (replication inhibition, mitomycin C) confirm that promoter hypermethylation is replication‑independent while loss is replication‑linked.
open_questions: What enzymatic pathways mediate replication‑independent CGI hypermethylation?
Do similar replication‑independent methylation gains occur in other CpG‑island hypermethylator cancers?

talk_title: Cooperativity between DNA methylation and EZH2 drives neuroendocrine prostate cancer

models.name: PRNE (EZH2 conditional KO in NEPC model)

MOA_summary: Loss of EZH2 in NEPC remodels the DNA methylome, increasing promoter hypermethylation and altering neuroendocrine gene expression; conversely, DNMT1 deletion redistributes H3K27me3, revealing a bidirectional crosstalk where each epigenetic silencer compensates for the other in a disease‑state‑specific manner.
key_findings: EZH2 deletion in NEPC mice leads to global DNA methylation changes at PRC2 target DMRs.
EZH2‑KO NEPC organoids show increased NE signature scores despite loss of H3K27me3.
DNMT1 deletion in NEPC models causes increased H3K27me3 at neuron‑related promoters, suppressing their expression.
Pharmacologic EZH2 inhibition mirrors genetic EZH2 loss; DNMT inhibition (decitabine) mirrors DNMT1 deletion, both reshaping H3K27me3 landscapes.
In adenocarcinoma context, DNMT1 loss upregulates neuroendocrine genes, highlighting cell‑state‑specific effects.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: EZH2 inhibition in established NEPC may trigger compensatory DNA methylation, potentially limiting efficacy.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target EZH2 before NEPC establishment to avoid compensatory methylation
combination_strategies.phenotype: Potentially prevents NEPC epigenetic reprogramming

combination_strategies.rationale: Disrupt DNMT1‑mediated compensation after EZH2 loss
combination_strategies.phenotype: Redistribution of H3K27me3 and reduced NE gene expression

open_questions: What co‑factors dictate the opposite outcomes of DNMT1 loss in NEPC vs adenocarcinoma?
Can sequential EZH2 then DNMT inhibition achieve durable NEPC suppression?

talk_title: Targeting the ubiquitin‑reading function of DNMT1 as a next‑generation epigenetic therapy
MOA_summary: DNMT1 requires its ubiquitin‑interacting motifs (UIMs) to read UHRF1‑mediated H3 ubiquitination; mutating UIMs abolishes DNA methylation maintenance, induces viral‑mimicry gene expression, and reduces cancer cell viability, providing a druggable interface distinct from catalytic inhibition.
key_findings: UIM‑mutant DNMT1 fails to bind ubiquitinated nucleosomes despite retained catalytic activity.
Loss of UIM function leads to global hypomethylation comparable to DNMT1 knockdown.
UIM loss upregulates viral‑mimicry pathways and downregulates oncogenic signaling, mirroring decitabine effects.
Cell viability and colony formation are severely impaired in UIM mutants, similar to catalytic dead DNMT1.
Screening identified a natural product (afetinin) and de‑novo designed protein binders that inhibit DNMT1 UIMs at low‑nanomolar affinity.

combination_strategies.rationale: UIM inhibition is replication‑independent, potentially synergizing with replication‑dependent DNMTis
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced hypomethylation and viral‑mimicry activation

open_questions: Will UIM inhibition cause off‑target effects on other ubiquitin‑reading proteins?
Can in vivo delivery of UIM binders achieve tumor regression without replication dependence?

company_or_sponsor: University of Michigan Center for Chemical Genomics

talk_title: KDM2A‑driven epigenetic reprogramming promotes Barrett’s esophagus to adenocarcinoma progression

biomarkers.name: H3K36me2
biomarkers.finding: KDM2A overexpression correlates with decreased H3K36me2 in Barrett/EAC samples

biomarkers.name: H3K27ac
biomarkers.finding: KDM2A overexpression increases H3K27ac at oncogenic enhancers

MOA_summary: KDM2A amplification reduces H3K36me2 and elevates H3K27ac, reprogramming chromatin to open enhancer landscapes that drive two distinct epithelial programs—secretory (SPINK1/PSCA) and proliferative (MYC, HER2)—facilitating Barrett’s metaplasia progression to heterogeneous esophageal adenocarcinoma.
key_findings: KDM2A is the most amplified histone demethylase in EAC TCGA cohort.
KDM2A expression shows a trend of increase from Barrett through dysplasia to EAC.
High KDM2A correlates with reduced H3K36me2 and increased H3K27ac at oncogenic loci.
KDM2A overexpression in HET‑1A opens chromatin at MYC and other oncogenes, inducing secretory and proliferative gene programs.
Patient‑derived organoids with high KDM2A display enhanced enhancer activity and poorer prognosis.
open_questions: Can KDM2A inhibition reverse enhancer activation and block Barrett‑to‑EAC transition?
Which transcription factors cooperate with KDM2A at newly opened enhancers?

talk_title: KMT2D is a master epigenetic regulator of lineage plasticity in lung squamous cell carcinoma

models.name: KP (KRAS G12D; p53‑null) with KMT2D floxed
models.genotype_or_key_features: Club cell or AT2 cell‑initiated tumors

MOA_summary: Loss of KMT2D in KRAS/TP53‑driven lung adenocarcinoma reduces H3K4me1 at FOXA1/FOXA2‑bound enhancers, shifts cellular states toward a basal‑like, integrin α6β4 stem‑like cluster, and promotes a trajectory through GI‑like intermediates to squamous transcriptional programs (TRP63, SOX2), driven by redistribution of MEN1‑associated H3K4me3 and cooperation with FOXA1/FOXA2.
key_findings: KMT2D deletion shortens survival in KP mice initiated from both club and AT2 cells.
Adenosquamous lesions appear exclusively in KMT2D‑null tumors.
Single‑nucleus multi‑omics reveal enrichment of a basal‑like cluster and an integrin α6β4 stem‑like intermediate in KMT2D‑KO tumors.
Chromatin regions losing H3K4me1 in KMT2D‑KO are enriched for FOXA1/FOXA2 motifs and co‑factor binding (SMARCC1, EP300).
FOXA1 physically interacts with KMT2D; MEN1 inhibition (MI‑503) shows increased sensitivity in KMT2D‑KO cells.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: KMT2D loss creates dependency on MEN1, suggesting a synthetic lethal vulnerability.

combination_strategies.rationale: KMT2D‑null cells show heightened reliance on MEN1‑mediated H3K4me3
combination_strategies.phenotype: Increased cytotoxicity in KMT2D‑KO vs WT cells

open_questions: Does catalytic inactivation of KMT2D recapitulate the phenotypes of complete loss?
Can targeting FOXA1 or SOX2 downstream mitigate the squamous transdifferentiation?

talk_title: Asymmetric Emergence of Cardiovascular Disease in Hematologic Malignancy Survivors

speaker.name: Yize Song
speaker.affiliation: Washington University School of Medicine, St. Louis

models.name: All of Us research program cohorts
models.genotype_or_key_features: hematologic malignancy survivors vs CVD patients

MOA_summary: Analyzed bi-directional risk between hematologic malignancy survivors and cardiovascular disease using All of Us cohorts, identifying high early CVD incidence especially in MDS and multiple myeloma, and implicating proteasome inhibitor treatment as a potential driver.
key_findings: CVD incidence sharply increases within first 2 years after hematologic malignancy diagnosis, median time 2.4 years.
MDS and multiple myeloma patients have the highest CVD risk in the first five years.
Arrhythmias are the most common CVD subtype post‑diagnosis.
Proteasome inhibitor exposure yields the highest composite cardiotoxicity score among treatments.
In vitro Cell Village assay shows proteasome inhibitor induces inflammatory/dysfunctional cardiac cell states and cell death.
open_questions: What molecular mechanisms link proteasome inhibition to specific cardiac cell dysfunction?
Can identified protective markers be targeted to mitigate cardiotoxicity?

talk_title: Decoding Melanoma Immunogenicity: A Comprehensive Proteogenomic, Immunopeptidomic, and Metabolomic Atlas

biomarkers.name: CD8 T cell fraction
biomarkers.finding: Positive correlation with tumor antigen count per sample (p<0.001)

biomarkers.name: HLA‑1 peptide count
biomarkers.finding: Higher counts associate with better survival in ICI‑treated patients

MOA_summary: Integrated 13‑layer multi‑omics atlas of melanoma revealed that high HLA‑I peptide presentation and tumor antigens from MAGEA genes drive CD8 T‑cell infiltration and improved ICI survival, while distinct ubiquitination and glycosylation patterns define prognostic subgroups.
key_findings: Identified 1,138 unique tumor antigen candidates, >50% absent from IEDB.
Tumor antigen count positively correlates with CD8 T‑cell infiltration and ICI‑treated survival.
MAGEA3/4/12 antigen events strongly associate with increased CD8 T‑cell fraction.
A ubiquitin‑high interferon subtype shows worse survival; RNF4‑DHX9 and BIRC7‑AKT1 ubiquitin pairs are prognostic.
A lysosome‑high glycoproteomic subtype displays sex‑specific survival: poor in males, neutral/beneficial in females, linked to GPUMB fucosylation.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Sex‑specific impact of GPUMB fucosylation suggests differential resistance mechanisms.
open_questions: Can identified tumor antigens be leveraged for personalized vaccines?
What drives the sex‑specific lysosome phenotype?

talk_title: Endocrine Therapy Adherence and 10‑Year Risk of Cardiometabolic Outcomes Among Women with Hormone Receptor‑Positive Breast Cancer

biomarkers.name: adherence (PDC ≥80%)
biomarkers.finding: Adherent patients had higher 10‑yr hypertension risk (29% vs 20%)

MOA_summary: Using a prospective Kaiser cohort, proportion of days covered measured endocrine therapy adherence and sequentially doubly robust estimation linked longer AI use to higher 10‑year hypertension, dyslipidemia, and diabetes risk, while both AI and tamoxifen adherence reduced mortality.
key_findings: Five‑year endocrine therapy adherence increased 10‑yr hypertension risk by 9 percentage points.
AI use, but not tamoxifen, was associated with higher dyslipidemia (33% vs 26%) and diabetes (13% vs 9.5%) risks.
Both AI and tamoxifen adherence reduced all‑cause mortality (18% vs 28%) and breast cancer‑specific mortality (6‑8% vs 13%).
Adherent patients were younger, higher socioeconomic status, and had fewer baseline comorbidities.
open_questions: How does differential healthcare utilization affect observed cardiometabolic risks?
Would extending AI therapy beyond five years further increase cardiometabolic events?

talk_title: Comprehensive Phenotypic Mapping of ALT Regulation Expands the Landscape of Targetable Suppressors

speaker.name: Bill DiPlas
speaker.affiliation: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Department of Radiation Oncology

MOA_summary: Developed Alt‑Flow‑FISH to quantify ALT activity, performed genome‑wide CRISPR screens identifying proteasome components and akirin‑2 as ALT suppressors; proteasome loss or inhibition accumulates PML, drives hyper‑ALT and cell death, suggesting proteasome inhibition as a therapeutic strategy for ALT cancers.
key_findings: Alt‑Flow‑FISH enables high‑throughput single‑cell ALT activity measurement.
Genome‑wide CRISPR screen uncovered numerous ALT suppressors, notably proteasome subunits (PSMB1, PSMB5, PSMB6) and akirin‑2.
Proteasome depletion leads to PML protein accumulation and hyper‑ALT phenotype with increased c‑circles.
Overexpression of PML alone induces hyper‑ALT and cell death; bortezomib recapitulates this effect.
Pathway analysis highlighted DNA repair, replication, proteasome, and Fanconi anemia pathways as ALT regulators.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Proteasome inhibition may affect normal cells; therapeutic window needs definition.

combination_strategies.rationale: ATR inhibition also tips ALT balance toward hyper‑ALT lethality
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression in ALT‑positive models

open_questions: Is ALT hyper‑activation uniformly lethal across diverse ALT tumor types?
Can proteasome inhibition be combined safely with DNA‑damage agents?

talk_title: Protein Complex Remodeling in Metastatic Breast Cancer Cells

speaker.name: Xiaoyu Zhao
speaker.affiliation: University of California, San Diego, Troy Atticher Lab

MOA_summary: Integrated confocal imaging, IP‑MS, SEC‑MS, and single‑cell proteomics with self‑supervised deep learning to map dynamic protein complex reorganization in TNBC cells under chemotherapy, revealing treatment‑specific assembly/disassembly of complexes such as stress response, transcriptional activation, and SWI/SNF loss.
key_findings: HDAC8 relocalizes from cytosol/nucleus to nucleus after paclitaxel.
HDAC1 interaction partners shift with treatment, indicating dynamic complex remodeling.
Identified >100 protein complexes; ~50% are novel assemblies.
Stress response complex emerges uniquely in vernostat‑treated cells.
Transcriptional activation complex CBP‑300 appears only after paclitaxel.
SWI/SNF complex is lost under both chemotherapies.
open_questions: Do identified dynamic complexes predict patient response or resistance?
Can targeting emergent complexes improve chemosensitivity?

talk_title: ASP2998 – TROP2‑Targeted Immunomodulatory ADC with Dual Payloads Demonstrates Potent Efficacy and Favorable Safety

speaker.name: Mark Orr
speaker.affiliation: Astellas Pharma Global Development, Seattle, WA (collaboration with Sutro)

models.name: MC38‑hTROP2 mouse model
models.genotype_or_key_features: large tumors (~500 mm³) treated with ASP2998

clinical_data.metric: Tumor regression in large MC38‑hTROP2 model
