clinical_data.value: Complete regression at high dose, tumor stasis at lower dose
100% rejection of secondary MC38‑hTROP2 challenge after ASP2998 cure

MOA_summary: ASP2998 is a humanized IgG1 TROP2‑directed ADC delivering a topoisomerase‑I inhibitor and a STING agonist; tumor cell killing releases the STING agonist, activating innate immunity and T‑cell responses, resulting in tumor regression and durable immune memory.
key_findings: Dual‑payload ADC shows enhanced potency in PBMC co‑culture versus cytotoxic‑only ADCs.
In vivo MC38‑hTROP2 model: ASP2998 induces tumor regression and complete protection upon rechallenge.
PK data reveal >99.9% of drug remains intact; free payloads are ~1,000‑fold lower.
GLP repeat‑dose study shows manageable safety profile; target organs at high dose include cornea, kidney, urethra, trachea.
Low IL‑6 induction suggests limited cytokine release syndrome risk.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Efficacy depends on TROP2 expression; threshold not yet defined in trial.

combination_strategies.rationale: STING agonist may convert cold tumors to hot, synergizing with checkpoint blockade
combination_strategies.phenotype: Potentially enhanced anti‑tumor immunity

open_questions: What is the optimal TROP2 expression cutoff for patient selection?
How does the STING agonist payload affect systemic immunity and safety?

talk_title: Targeting EYA4 Disrupts Genome Stability in Triple‑Negative Breast Cancer

speaker.name: Daniela Garcia
speaker.affiliation: University of Texas San Antonio, Greehey Children’s Cancer Center

targets.dependency_context: TNBC cells with high EYA4 expression

MOA_summary: EYA4 binds ssDNA via an N‑terminal basic/AT‑hook region; DNA binding enhances its tyrosine phosphatase activity toward γH2AX and RAD51, promoting homologous recombination. Inhibition or depletion of EYA4 induces HR deficiency, γH2AX accumulation, and tumor growth suppression.
key_findings: EYA4 is hyper‑methylated yet overexpressed in TNBC; highest in MDA‑MB‑231.
EYA4 depletion reduces primary tumor growth and lung metastasis in vivo.
EYA4 binds ssDNA with nanomolar affinity; phosphomimetic mutation reduces binding.
DNA binding markedly stimulates EYA4 tyrosine phosphatase activity.
Small‑molecule inhibitor CID‑XXXX89 reduces HR efficiency (dose‑dependent GFP loss) and cell viability.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Potential selectivity challenge: phosphatase inhibition may affect normal DNA repair.

combination_strategies.rationale: EYA4 inhibition induces HR deficiency, sensitizing cells to PARP blockade
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic reduction in viability in TNBC models

open_questions: Can EYA4 inhibition overcome PARP resistance in BRCA‑wildtype TNBC?
What biomarkers predict response to EYA4 inhibitors in patient tumors?

talk_title: Neural integration and synaptic networks drive glioblastoma progression

biomarkers.name: RNA‑Seq connectivity score
biomarkers.finding: used as primary endpoint to assess perampanel effect

biomarkers.name: AI‑based MRI growth dynamics
biomarkers.finding: used as primary endpoint in PERSECH trial

models.name: rabies‑based retrograde tracing in mouse glioma
models.genotype_or_key_features: tumor cells infected with pseudotyped rabies virus

MOA_summary: Perampanel, a non‑competitive AMPA‑receptor antagonist, blocks excitatory synaptic inputs from neurons to glioblastoma cells, reducing calcium‑mediated proliferation and invasion; additional neuronal‑derived signals (acetylcholine) also promote tumor growth, and GPM6A‑driven tumor microtubules facilitate invasion.
key_findings: Synaptic contacts from neurons to glioblastoma cells were visualized by EM in patient samples and xenografts.
Neuronal AMPA‑receptor activity drives calcium influx, proliferation, and invasion of glioblastoma cells.
Perampanel is being tested in the phase I/II PERSECH trial using RNA‑Seq connectivity scores and AI‑based MRI growth dynamics as primary endpoints.
Rabies‑based retrograde tracing reveals widespread, dynamic neuron‑to‑tumor connectivity across the whole brain.
Cholinergic signaling and GPM6A‑mediated tumor microtubules represent additional mechanisms of invasion.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Perampanel requires careful titration due to neuropsychiatric side effects, indicating a narrow therapeutic window.

combination_strategies.rationale: disrupt neuronal signaling may sensitize tumors to DNA damage
combination_strategies.phenotype: potential synergistic reduction in tumor growth

open_questions: What immune cells (e.g., microglia) modulate neuron‑glioma synapses?
Can the synaptic network be fully recapitulated in immunocompetent mouse models?

talk_title: Screening platform to discover tumor‑microtubule disconnecting agents

models.name: immunocompromised mouse with chronic cranial window

MOA_summary: PKC activation by TPPB reduces expression of the tumor‑microtubule marker TTYH2, leading to morphological disconnection of glioblastoma tumor microtubules and silencing of calcium‑mediated pacemaker activity, thereby sensitizing tumors to radiotherapy.
key_findings: A high‑content 2D tumor‑microtubule assay enabled quantitative segmentation of microtubule number, length, and cell viability.
Screen of 71 compounds identified PKC activators (PMA, TPPB) that disconnect microtubules without killing cells.
TPPB treatment reduced TTYH2 expression, decreased microtubule length, and silenced periodic calcium activity in three glioblastoma cell lines.
In vivo, TPPB combined with radiotherapy eradicated tumor cells in the invasion zone and extended mouse survival.
Spatial COMET analysis showed TTYH2 down‑regulation in the invasion zone and increased apoptosis (caspase‑3) after TPPB treatment.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: PKC activators have broad downstream effects; specificity for tumor‑microtubule disruption versus other PKC‑mediated pathways remains unclear.

combination_strategies.rationale: disconnected microtubules render tumor cells more radiosensitive
combination_strategies.phenotype: dramatic tumor eradication in mouse invasion zone

open_questions: What downstream PKC substrates mediate microtubule disassembly?
Can TPPB be combined with temozolomide or immunotherapies for additive benefit?

talk_title: Monocyte and neutrophil dynamics shape glioblastoma myeloid microenvironment

targets.dependency_context: neutrophil netosis in GBM necrotic core

biomarkers.name: MPO
biomarkers.finding: high MPO signal in necrotic core macrophages indicates neutrophil netosis

biomarkers.name: ARG1
biomarkers.finding: upregulated in macrophages exposed to neutrophil supernatants

MOA_summary: In hypoxic necrotic GBM cores, neutrophils undergo MPO‑dependent NETosis; macrophages phagocytose NET debris, upregulating immunosuppressive markers (ARG1, PD‑L1) and suppressing T‑cell activity, thereby promoting tumor progression. MPO deficiency blocks NETosis, increases neutrophil numbers, reduces immunosuppression, and extends survival.
key_findings: Neutrophil infiltration occurs late in GBM progression and is enriched in necrotic, hypoxic cores.
Neutrophils undergo MPO‑dependent NETosis; macrophages engulf NETs and become immunosuppressive (ARG1, PD‑L1 upregulation).
MPO‑deficient tumors show increased neutrophil numbers, reduced immunosuppressive phenotype, and improved mouse survival.
Human GBM samples display macrophage engulfment of MPO, especially in mesenchymal subtype.
Neutrophil‑derived factors suppress T‑cell proliferation in vitro, an effect reversed in MPO‑knockout conditions.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: MPO deficiency leads to accumulation of neutrophils with an APC‑like phenotype, altering the immune landscape.

combination_strategies.rationale: steroid reduces IL‑1 and monocyte recruitment; potential impact on neutrophil dynamics
combination_strategies.phenotype: unknown; suggested for future study

open_questions: How does targeting MPO or NETosis affect overall tumor immunity and response to checkpoint blockade?
What are the sex‑specific effects of myeloid manipulation in GBM?

talk_title: Single‑cell and spatial genomics map glioma heterogeneity and evolution

models.name: 100 glioblastoma samples (single‑nucleus RNA‑seq)
models.genotype_or_key_features: diverse driver alterations, mesenchymal, OPC‑like, NPC‑like, astrocytic‑like states

models.name: Visium HD glioblastoma sections
models.genotype_or_key_features: 2‑µm capture, copy‑number mapping, subclone spatial distribution

models.name: PDX glioblastoma xenografts for invasion studies
models.genotype_or_key_features: tracking OPC/NPC‑like cell migration across midline

MOA_summary: High‑resolution single‑cell and spatial transcriptomics reveal four developmental‑like glioma states (OPC‑like, NPC‑like, astrocytic‑like, mesenchymal‑like) whose relative abundance is shaped by genetics, microenvironmental cues (hypoxia, vasculature), and therapy; spatial mapping links necrotic cores to mesenchymal/inflammatory niches and peripheral zones to neurodevelopmental states, informing region‑specific therapeutic strategies.
key_findings: Large cohort (n>100) confirms four dominant glioblastoma cell states; mesenchymal compartment further subdivides into hypoxia‑ and stress‑associated programs.
Visium HD spatial analysis shows layered organization: necrotic core → mesenchymal/inflammatory → angiogenic hub → neurodevelopmental (OPC/NPC) zones.
Subclonal copy‑number events are spatially confined, with distinct clones populating leptomeningeal versus parenchymal regions.
Phylogenetic trees based on SNVs reveal that advanced clones are enriched for OPC/NPC‑like states, suggesting state‑specific evolutionary pressure.
IDH‑mutant gliomas display hypomethylation‑driven loss of differentiation; IDH inhibitors promote cancer‑cell differentiation, correlating with clinical response.
open_questions: How can region‑specific therapies be designed to target distinct microenvironmental niches within glioblastoma?
What mechanisms underlie the preferential evolution of OPC/NPC‑like cells in advanced clones?

talk_title: Definitive non-operative management with tremelimumab plus durvalumab in resectable MSI‑high gastric/GEJ cancer (INFINITY cohort 2)

speaker.name: Alberto Giovanni Leone
speaker.affiliation: Fondazione Istituto Nazionale dei Tumori di Milano, Milan, Italy

biomarkers.name: MSI‑high / dMMR
biomarkers.finding: All enrolled patients were MSI‑high; this defined the trial population

biomarkers.name: tissue‑informed ctDNA
biomarkers.finding: Negative ctDNA at restaging predicted clinical complete response with 100% specificity

models.name: INFINITY cohort 2
models.genotype_or_key_features: resectable MSI‑high gastric/GEJ adenocarcinoma, T2+, any N

MOA_summary: Dual checkpoint blockade with a single dose of anti‑CTLA‑4 (tremelimumab) and three cycles of anti‑PD‑L1 (durvalumab) induces deep tumor immune activation in MSI‑high gastric/GEJ cancer, enabling a composite clinical complete response that permits omission of surgery in the majority of patients.
key_findings: 71% (13/17) of patients achieved a composite clinical complete response after 3 months of tremelimumab + durvalumab and avoided surgery.
24‑month PFS was 94.1% and OS was 100% with a median follow‑up of 27.1 months.
Gastrectomy‑free survival at 2 years was 70.6%, demonstrating feasibility of a non‑operative approach.
Grade 3 treatment‑related adverse events occurred in 17% of patients; no grade 4/5 events were observed.
Baseline ctDNA was detectable in 13/17 patients; ctDNA clearance correlated with clinical complete response (specificity 100%).
RNA‑seq revealed immune‑permissive signatures in responders versus stromal/EMT signatures in non‑responders.

combination_strategies.rationale: CTLA‑4 blockade expands and re‑activates tumor‑specific T cells; PD‑L1 blockade prevents adaptive immune escape.
combination_strategies.phenotype: synergistic induction of clinical complete responses enabling organ preservation

open_questions: Optimal duration and dosing schedule of dual checkpoint blockade for durable non‑operative control.
Whether adding chemotherapy or other agents could further improve response rates.
Implementation of ctDNA‑guided surveillance in community practice.

cited_papers: previous publication in Annals of Oncology

talk_title: Phase 2 MATIS trial of CD39 inhibitor IPH5201 combined with durvalumab and platinum‑based chemotherapy in resectable NSCLC

speaker.name: Fabrice Barlisi
speaker.affiliation: Gustave Roussy, University of Paris, France

biomarkers.name: PD‑L1 TPS
biomarkers.finding: PCR rate 35.7% in PD‑L1 >1% and 50% in PD‑L1 >50% subgroup

biomarkers.name: CD39 cell density
biomarkers.finding: Higher baseline CD39 density associated with higher PCR/MPR rates

models.name: MATIS
models.genotype_or_key_features: resectable stage II‑III NSCLC, EGFR/ALK wild‑type, any PD‑L1

MOA_summary: Inhibition of CD39 with IPH5201 blocks extracellular ATP conversion to immunosuppressive adenosine, enhancing dendritic cell activation and T‑cell priming; combined with PD‑L1 blockade (durvalumab) and platinum‑based chemotherapy, this strategy increases pathological complete responses in resectable NSCLC.
key_findings: pCR was 27.5% in the intent‑to‑treat cohort (n=40), exceeding historical 25% benchmarks for chemo‑immunotherapy alone.
Patients with PD‑L1 >1% achieved a pCR of 35.7%; those with PD‑L1 >50% reached 50% pCR.
Higher baseline CD39 stromal density correlated with higher pCR/MPR rates.
87.5% of patients proceeded to surgery; peri‑operative safety was comparable to standard chemo‑immunotherapy.
Grade 3 AEs occurred in 55% of patients, with 32% deemed drug‑related; one postoperative death due to pneumonia was reported.

combination_strategies.rationale: CD39 blockade reduces adenosine‑mediated immunosuppression, potentially synergizing with checkpoint inhibition and cytotoxic DNA damage.
combination_strategies.phenotype: increased pCR and MPR rates without compromising surgical feasibility

open_questions: Will enrichment for high CD39 density improve patient selection?
Is CD39 inhibition additive to anti‑CD73 strategies in the peri‑operative setting?
What is the optimal adjuvant duration of IPH5201 after surgery?

talk_title: Phase 1 first‑in‑human study of anti‑CCR8 antibody denicitug in advanced solid tumors

speaker.name: Bruno Boccorni
speaker.affiliation: Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center & Dana‑Farber Cancer Institute, Boston, USA

tumor_types: head and neck squamous cell carcinoma

biomarkers.name: CCR8 expression (tumor IHC)
biomarkers.finding: Responders and non‑responders displayed a wide range of CCR8 levels; no clear correlation observed in this heterogeneous cohort

models.name: Denicitug Phase 1 dose‑escalation and paired‑biopsy cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: advanced solid tumors, median 3 prior lines, 2/3 prior PD‑1/PD‑L1 exposure

MOA_summary: Denicitug is an afucosylated anti‑CCR8 monoclonal antibody that depletes intratumoral CCR8‑positive regulatory T cells via enhanced ADCC, leading to reduced immunosuppression and a concurrent increase in CD8‑effector T‑cell infiltration.
key_findings: Denicitug monotherapy achieved an ORR of 8% and a DCR of 46% in a heavily pre‑treated cohort (median 3 prior lines).
Partial responses were observed at the lowest biologically active dose (10 mg) and were durable up to 14.6 months in one NSCLC patient.
Pharmacodynamic analyses showed near‑complete depletion of CCR8+ intratumoral Tregs and a concurrent expansion of CD8+ effector T cells across all dose levels.
Safety profile was manageable; most common treatment‑related AEs were rash, pruritus, diarrhea, and fatigue, with no grade 4/5 events.
No clear relationship between baseline CCR8 expression and clinical response was identified in this heterogeneous population.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: CCR8 is preferentially expressed on intratumoral Tregs, potentially sparing peripheral Tregs and reducing systemic autoimmunity.

combination_strategies.rationale: CCR8‑mediated Treg depletion may overcome resistance to checkpoint blockade by relieving local immunosuppression.
combination_strategies.phenotype: expected synergistic tumor regressions; combination studies ongoing

open_questions: What is the optimal dose for maximal Treg depletion without excess skin toxicity?
Will baseline CCR8 expression become a predictive biomarker in a more homogeneous tumor cohort?
Can combination with PD‑1 blockade improve response rates beyond the modest monotherapy activity?

talk_title: Multi‑omic dissection of primary versus acquired immunotherapy resistance in NSCLC (HUDSON trial cohort)

speaker.name: Archana Balan
speaker.affiliation: Sidney Kimmel Comprehensive Cancer Center at Johns Hopkins University, Baltimore, USA

biomarkers.name: STK11 / KEAP1 / KRAS co‑mutations
biomarkers.finding: Present in ~40% of both primary and acquired resistance groups

biomarkers.name: PD‑L1 expression
biomarkers.finding: Used for subgrouping but not directly linked to resistance type

biomarkers.name: EMT gene signature
biomarkers.finding: Enriched in acquired resistance tumors

biomarkers.name: FGF / mTOR pathway mutations
biomarkers.finding: Enriched in acquired resistance group

models.name: HUDSON screening population
models.genotype_or_key_features: 951 advanced NSCLC patients progressed on prior anti‑PD‑L1; 951 patients, >1800 biospecimens

MOA_summary: Comprehensive multi‑omic profiling of IO‑resistant NSCLC reveals that acquired resistance is driven by genomic instability, EMT, lineage plasticity, and metabolic reprogramming, whereas primary resistance is associated with canonical driver alterations (STK11, KEAP1, KRAS) and immune‑exclusion signatures.
key_findings: Acquired resistance tumors displayed higher tumor mutation burden and greater subclonal diversity than primary resistance tumors.
Genomic scarring (large‑scale transitions, telomeric allele imbalance, HRD) was enriched in post‑IO samples of both resistance groups.
Mutations in FGF, mTOR, NRAS, RBM10, DNMT3A, and DNA‑damage‑repair genes were significantly enriched in the acquired resistance cohort.
Bulk RNA‑seq showed up‑regulation of interferon‑γ, inflammatory, and EMT pathways in acquired resistance tumors.
Single‑cell analysis identified expanded pluripotent stem‑like epithelial cells and tissue‑resident memory CD8 T cells in acquired resistance, alongside reduced antigen presentation.
open_questions: Can targeting EMT or metabolic pathways reverse acquired IO resistance?
What is the therapeutic relevance of DNMT3A‑mutated clonal hematopoiesis in this setting?
How might epigenetic modulators be integrated with immunotherapy to overcome resistance?

talk_title: Mechanisms of early dissemination and phenotypic plasticity in melanoma metastasis

models.name: melanocytic and mesenchymal melanoma cell lines
c-kit driven melanoma model
models.genotype_or_key_features: c-kit promoter activation in melanocyte stem cells

MOA_summary: Hernando demonstrated that mechanical compression and narrow-pore migration induce loss of heterochromatin (H3K9me3) and nuclear de‑density, converting differentiated melanocytic cells into a mesenchymal‑like state with increased deformability and metastatic potential, mediated in part by RUNX1‑driven gene networks and metabolic adaptations such as FATP1/2‑mediated lipid uptake and CNDP1‑driven mitochondrial protection in brain metastases.
key_findings: Constriction through 3 µm pores converts melanocytic melanoma cells into a mesenchymal‑like phenotype with reduced H3K9me3 heterochromatin and increased nuclear diffusion.
Constricted cells exhibit significantly higher liver metastasis after intracardiac injection compared with unconstricted controls.
RUNX1 is required for emergence of intra‑tumor heterogeneity; RUNX1‑deficient mice retain a melanocytic tumor phenotype.
CNDP1 is up‑regulated in brain metastases, and its silencing reduces brain colonisation, mitochondrial function, and oxygen consumption, while overexpression enhances brain metastasis.
Mechanical stress induces chromatin hub re‑wiring via CTCF relocation, promoting expression of mesenchymal genes.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Mechanical compression creates a reversible epigenetic state that confers metastatic advantage without altering proliferative capacity.
CNDP1‑mediated carnosine cleavage creates a metabolic vulnerability that can be therapeutically targeted.
open_questions: What are the upstream signals that trigger RUNX1 activation during early dissemination?
Can pharmacologic inhibition of FATP1/2 or CNDP1 prevent metastasis in vivo?

company_or_sponsor: National Cancer Institute Metastasis Research Network

talk_title: Accelerating drug development in melanoma: neoadjuvant platforms and the third‑space hypothesis

biomarkers.name: IFN‑γ signature
biomarkers.finding: High baseline IFN‑γ signature predicts higher major pathological response rates

biomarkers.name: PD‑L1 CPS
biomarkers.finding: Combined high PD‑L1 and TMB yields 100% 3‑year EFS in neoadjuvant cohorts

biomarkers.name: Tumor mutation burden
biomarkers.finding: High TMB correlates with superior event‑free survival after neoadjuvant therapy

models.name: CheckMate 067
models.genotype_or_key_features: ipilimumab + nivolumab vs nivolumab alone, advanced melanoma

models.name: ABC study
models.genotype_or_key_features: ipilimumab + nivolumab vs nivolumab in untreated asymptomatic brain mets

models.name: NeoIreni (NCTxxxx)
models.genotype_or_key_features: neoadjuvant stage III melanoma, biomarker‑guided arms

MOA_summary: Long outlined a biomarker‑driven neoadjuvant framework that uses early pathological response to stratify patients into responders (first/second space) and non‑responders (third space), enabling rapid go/no‑go decisions for novel agents; she introduced NeoPredict and NeoIreni trials that integrate IFN‑γ, PD‑L1, and TMB to predict resistance and guide combination immunotherapy or targeted approaches.
key_findings: 10‑year melanoma‑specific survival now exceeds 50% in advanced disease.
Neoadjuvant checkpoint blockade yields ~60% major pathological response, translating to near‑100% overall survival in responders.
Biomarker triad (high IFN‑γ, PD‑L1, TMB) predicts 100% 3‑year event‑free survival.
Multiple phase‑3 adjuvant trials (CheckMate 915, RELATIMAB, vibostalimab) failed to improve outcomes, highlighting the need for better patient selection.
NeoPredict test can identify third‑space patients before therapy, allowing early enrollment in experimental neoadjuvant trials.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Low-dose ipilimumab in adjuvant trials likely contributed to negative outcomes due to dose‑response relationship.
Anti‑TIGIT arm showed trend toward poorer survival, suggesting possible antagonistic interaction.

combination_strategies.rationale: enhance interferon‑γ signature response
combination_strategies.phenotype: no improvement over nivolumab alone in neoadjuvant setting

combination_strategies.rationale: target multiple checkpoints in high‑TMB, BRAF‑mutant subgroup
combination_strategies.phenotype: no superiority to ipi/nivo in randomized neoadjuvant trial

open_questions: What is the optimal biomarker threshold for defining the third space?
Can early neoadjuvant biomarker data predict long‑term resistance mechanisms beyond checkpoint blockade?

talk_title: IL‑13Rα2 CAR‑T therapy in melanoma and neuroendocrine cancers

models.name: IL‑13Rα2 CAR‑T multi‑center trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: Patients with IL13RA2‑positive melanoma (10‑15%) and neuroendocrine tumors

clinical_data.value: robust expansion within first days post‑infusion
partial response in lung metastasis, progression in liver lesion

MOA_summary: The speaker reported a phase‑I study of an IL‑13Rα2‑directed CAR‑T cell therapy, showing rapid in‑vivo expansion after lymphodepletion and occasional tumor regressions in IL13RA2‑expressing melanoma lesions, while highlighting heterogeneous responses due to variable antigen expression and tumor heterogeneity.
key_findings: IL13RA2 is expressed homogeneously in 10‑15% of melanomas and highly in neuroendocrine cancers.
CAR‑T cells achieve brisk peripheral expansion after low‑dose lymphodepletion.
A lung metastasis showed radiographic response, whereas a concurrent liver lesion progressed, underscoring intrapatient heterogeneity.
The trial has enrolled 19 patients with ongoing safety and efficacy assessment.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Heterogeneous IL13RA2 expression leads to mixed lesion responses within the same patient.
open_questions: Can combination with metabolic modulators improve CAR‑T activity in low‑IL13RA2 expressing lesions?
What is the optimal lymphodepletion regimen for melanoma CAR‑T?

talk_title: Ultrasensitive ctDNA MRD detection, lymphatic fluid sampling, and liquid spatial ecotypes for immunotherapy response prediction

speaker.name: Adel Chaudhry
speaker.affiliation: Mayo Clinic, Department of Radiation Oncology

biomarkers.name: Lymph ctDNA MRD
biomarkers.finding: Presence in surgical drain fluid at 24h post-op associated with worse progression-free survival and local-regional progression-free survival

models.name: head and neck cancer surgical drain fluid

MOA_summary: Multi-mutation tracking and tumor-informed sequencing enable detection of ctDNA at parts-per-million levels; sampling lymphatic fluid from surgical drains enriches ctDNA, improving MRD detection and prognostication; cfDNA methylation patterns inferred from spatial transcriptomics define liquid spatial ecotypes that predict immunotherapy response or resistance.
key_findings: Multi-mutation tracking lowers MRD detection limit to ~1 part per million
Lymphatic fluid ctDNA detection at 24h post-surgery predicts worse PFS and outperforms pathology features
Liquid spatial ecotype 7 inferred from cfDNA methylation predicts immunotherapy response and improved survival across melanoma and NSCLC
Spatial ecotype 4 predicts resistance and worse survival, outperforming PD-L1 and TMB
open_questions: How does tumor fraction affect accuracy of liquid ecotype inference?
Can serial monitoring of liquid ecotypes track pseudoprogression?

talk_title: Ultra-sensitive tumor-informed whole genome ctDNA MRD assays and adaptive clinical trial designs

speaker.name: Anagnostou
speaker.affiliation: Johns Hopkins University, Department of Oncology

biomarkers.name: ctDNA clearance
biomarkers.finding: Associated with better outcomes in neoadjuvant gastroesophageal cancer

models.name: TICTACTOE
models.genotype_or_key_features: early-stage NSCLC, ctDNA MRD assessed 4-6 weeks post definitive therapy

MOA_summary: Tumor-informed whole-genome sequencing tracks thousands of SNVs to achieve ultra-sensitive MRD detection; integrating machine learning reduces error rates, enabling ctDNA monitoring to guide adaptive trial designs and therapeutic escalation in early-stage cancers.
key_findings: Whole-genome tumor-informed ctDNA assay detects MRD at parts-per-million sensitivity across multiple cancer types
ctDNA persistence pre-surgery predicts inferior outcomes in low-TMB mesothelioma
Adaptive trial designs (e.g., TICTACTOE) will assess ctDNA MRD concordance with recurrence-free survival in early-stage NSCLC
ctDNA molecular response at 6-9 weeks predicts long-term outcomes in metastatic NSCLC treated with pembrolizumab
open_questions: What is the optimal ctDNA MRD timepoint for guiding adjuvant therapy?
How to improve clinical sensitivity of MRD assays in low-shedding tumors?

talk_title: Improving ctDNA assay sensitivity via increased cfDNA sampling and diagnostic priming agents

speaker.name: Victor Adelsten
speaker.affiliation: Broad Institute, Gerstner Center for Cancer Diagnostics

MOA_summary: MAESTRO enrichment uses allele-specific oligos to boost low-frequency mutations, enabling detection below 1 ppm; diagnostic priming agents (nanoparticles or Fc-silenced antibodies) transiently inhibit cfDNA clearance, increasing ctDNA yield for downstream assays.
key_findings: MAESTRO converts low VAF mutations to high VAF, achieving detection down to 0.25 ppm with linearity
open_questions: Safety profile and optimal dosing of cfDNA priming agents in humans?
Can priming improve detection in low-shedding cancers like renal cell carcinoma?

talk_title: Co-evolution of tumor and stromal microenvironment in bone metastasis

biomarkers.name: VK1, CD163, CCR3 triple positive macrophage (IMAC)
biomarkers.finding: Enriched in bone metastatic niche; not increased in number but relocalized

biomarkers.name: phospho-Smad2
biomarkers.finding: High staining in tumor cells adjacent to bone matrix indicating active TGF-beta signaling

models.name: bone metastasis model (GFP tumor + secreted mCherry niche labeling)
models.genotype_or_key_features: breast cancer cells engineered to express GFP and secreted mCherry

MOA_summary: Tumor cells exploit pre-existing bone niches via CXCL12/CXCR4 chemotaxis and E-selectin adhesion, inducing a non-canonical MET and Wnt activation for early seeding; later, tumor‑derived TGF‑β and JAG1 create a feedback loop with osteoclasts and osteoblasts, driving osteolysis and chemotherapy resistance, while DACH1‑mediated condensates suppress Wnt to switch signaling states.
key_findings: E-selectin engagement induces non‑canonical MET and Wnt signaling, promoting early bone seeding.
Tumor‑derived JAG1 activates osteoclast maturation and osteoblast cytokine production, establishing a TGF‑β/JAG1 positive feedback loop.
Neutralizing antibody 15d11 targeting JAG1 combined with chemotherapy reduces bone metastasis development by ~100% in mouse models.
DACH1 forms biomolecular condensates that suppress Wnt signaling, mediating the transition from early seeding to osteolytic growth.
IMAC (VK1⁺/CD163⁺/CCR3⁺) macrophages are relocalized to bone metastatic niches, supporting tumor growth and causing anemia by disrupting erythropoiesis islands.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: JAG1 expression in osteoblasts confers chemotherapy resistance to tumor cells.
Bone metastases show depletion of adaptive immune cells (B, T, DC) contributing to immune evasion.

combination_strategies.rationale: JAG1 neutralization sensitizes tumor cells to chemotherapy
combination_strategies.phenotype: Combined treatment nearly abolishes bone metastasis growth

open_questions: Can CCR3 blockade effectively prevent IMAC recruitment without inducing anemia?
What are the precise molecular mechanisms by which DACH1 condensates suppress Wnt signaling?

talk_title: Targeting and detecting the pre‑metastatic niche in brain metastasis

targets.dependency_context: astrocyte‑mediated MHC‑I downregulation in brain metastasis

biomarkers.name: MLIN1
biomarkers.finding: Secreted by astrocytes, upregulates CDK5 leading to MHC‑I downregulation in tumor cells

biomarkers.name: VCAM-1 expression on brain endothelium
biomarkers.finding: Upregulated in pre‑metastatic niche; enables immunoPET detection

models.name: ADORATION trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: Phase 1/2 REG inhibitor + radiation in patients with brain metastasis

clinical_data.population: brain metastasis patients in ADORATION trial
brain metastasis patients in ADORATION trial

MOA_summary: Tumor‑derived extracellular vesicles bearing CEACAM1/MIP direct cancer cells to the brain, where they disrupt the BBB via miR‑181c or VEGF‑A, induce astrocyte STAT3‑driven CDK5 upregulation (MLIN1 mediated) to suppress MHC‑I, and create a metabolic niche rich in GABA and glutamate; targeting VCAM‑1 with radiolabeled antibodies enables immunoPET detection and alpha‑particle therapy, while REG inhibition remodels the immune microenvironment, reducing M2 macrophages and MDSCs and enhancing CD8 T‑cell activity.
key_findings: CEACAM1/MIP on tumor EVs serves as a zip code directing metastasis to the brain.
miR‑181c and VEGF‑A from tumor EVs disrupt BBB tight junctions, facilitating tumor cell entry.
Astrocyte‑derived MLIN1 upregulates CDK5, leading to MHC‑I downregulation and immune evasion; rosuvastatin restores antigen presentation.
Neuronal GABA and glutamate fuel brain metastatic tumor cells, promoting proliferation.
Radiolabeled anti‑VCAM‑1 immunoPET detects pre‑metastatic niches before MRI-visible lesions.
REG inhibitor combined with radiation shows 70% partial and 24% complete responses in a Phase 1/2 brain metastasis trial.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Astrocyte‑mediated CDK5 activation creates an immune‑cold niche resistant to anti‑PD‑1 therapy.
REG pathway inhibition overcomes M2 macrophage–driven immunosuppression.

combination_strategies.rationale: REG inhibition sensitizes tumors to radiation‑induced immune activation
combination_strategies.phenotype: High response rates in brain metastasis patients

combination_strategies.rationale: Inhibits astrocyte CDK5 to restore MHC‑I expression
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced T‑cell recognition of brain metastatic cells in preclinical models

open_questions: Can VCAM‑1–targeted alpha‑particle therapy eradicate microscopic pre‑metastatic niches without toxicity?
What are the downstream effectors of REG signaling that mediate MDSC recruitment?

talk_title: Telatrexib in TKI‑naive ROS1‑rearranged NSCLC: updated TRUST‑1 and TRUST‑2 data

speaker.name: Luda Bazhanova
speaker.affiliation: UC San Diego Moores Cancer Center, San Diego

biomarkers.name: intracranial ORR
biomarkers.finding: pooled intracranial ORR 76.5% (n=157)

models.name: TRUST-1 and TRUST-2
models.genotype_or_key_features: ROS1 fusion, TKI‑naive, stage IV NSCLC

clinical_data.population: TKI‑naive ROS1‑rearranged NSCLC with brain mets

MOA_summary: Telatrexib is a CNS‑penetrant, selective ROS1 tyrosine‑kinase inhibitor that achieves high ORR and durable responses in ROS1‑rearranged NSCLC by potently inhibiting ROS1 while sparing TRKB, resulting in low neurologic toxicity.
key_findings: Pooled ORR in TKI‑naive ROS1‑rearranged NSCLC was 89.8% (n=157).
Intracranial ORR reached 76.5% in patients with brain metastases.
Median progression‑free survival was 46.1 months with 48.9% progression‑free at 4 years.
Median duration of response was 49.7 months; 53.4% of responders remained on therapy at 4 years.
Grade ≥3 AST/ALT elevations were low (8.3% and 11% respectively) and neurologic adverse events were rare.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Lower neurologic toxicity attributed to 11‑fold selectivity for ROS1 over TRKB.
open_questions: Long‑term outcomes beyond 4 years and mechanisms of eventual resistance.

talk_title: SHR‑A‑1811 ± atezolizumab as first‑line therapy for HER2‑mutant NSCLC: randomized phase II cohort

speaker.name: Shun Lu
speaker.affiliation: Shanghai Lung Cancer Center, Shanghai Chest Hospital, Shanghai

biomarkers.name: PD‑L1 expression
biomarkers.finding: randomization stratified by PD‑L1; response rates similar across PD‑L1 subgroups

MOA_summary: SHR‑A‑1811 is a HER2‑directed ADC delivering a topoisomerase I payload; when combined with atezolizumab, it may enhance immunogenic cell death and improve durability of disease control in HER2‑mutant NSCLC.
key_findings: SHR‑A‑1811 monotherapy achieved ORR 81.3% (n=32) in HER2‑mutant NSCLC.
Adding atezolizumab increased 12‑month PFS to 79.2% versus 62.3% with monotherapy.
Grade ≥3 treatment‑related AEs were higher in the combination (77.8%) than monotherapy (40.6%).
Responses were consistent across subgroups (age, sex, brain mets, PD‑L1 status).
Dose of 4.8 mg/kg was selected as RP2D based on phase 1 dose‑exploration.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Higher hematologic toxicity observed with combination; dose optimization discussed.

combination_strategies.rationale: ADC‑induced immunogenic cell death may synergize with checkpoint blockade
combination_strategies.phenotype: higher 12‑month PFS (79.2% vs 62.3%) despite lower ORR

open_questions: Would sequencing monotherapy followed by combo at progression improve overall benefit?
How does HER2‑ADC efficacy compare head‑to‑head with HER2 TKIs or other ADCs?

talk_title: HS10504, a fourth‑generation EGFR TKI targeting C797S, phase I first‑in‑human results

speaker.name: Zhang Hongxe
speaker.affiliation: First Affiliated Hospital of Guangzhou Medical University

biomarkers.name: ctDNA EGFR mutation allele frequency
biomarkers.finding: rapid decline after one cycle; C797S and T790M levels decreased by cycle 2

MOA_summary: HS10504 is a fourth‑generation EGFR inhibitor designed to bind both the sensitizing EGFR mutations and the C797S resistance mutation, retaining activity irrespective of T790M status, leading to rapid ctDNA clearance and tumor shrinkage.
key_findings: ORR at 400 mg was 52.9% versus 31.8% at 300 mg.
Median progression‑free survival reached 9.6 months in the 400 mg cohort.
Rapid ctDNA allele‑frequency reductions observed after one treatment cycle.
Grade ≥3 AEs occurred in 65.7% of 400 mg patients, most commonly lymphopenia and anemia.
One discontinuation due to decreased appetite; no treatment‑related deaths.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Dose‑dependent hematologic toxicity; 400 mg selected as RP2D despite higher AE rate.
open_questions: Will HS10504 demonstrate CNS activity in patients with brain metastases?
Can combination with EGFR TKIs or downstream inhibitors overcome residual resistance?

talk_title: Elirasib (D3S001), a next‑generation GDP‑bound KRASG12C inhibitor alone or with cetuximab in refractory CRC and PDAC: phase 1/2 results

speaker.name: Kanwal Raghav
speaker.affiliation: University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center, Houston

targets.dependency_context: KRAS G12C‑mutant CRC or PDAC, refractory

biomarkers.name: KRAS G12C allele frequency (ctDNA)
biomarkers.finding: rapid and deep molecular responses; near‑complete clearance in many patients

biomarkers.name: acquired MET amplification
biomarkers.finding: observed in combo arm as a common resistance mechanism

models.name: Phase 1/2 Elirasib study (colorectal and pancreatic cohorts)

MOA_summary: Elirasib binds the GDP‑bound KRASG12C pocket with high affinity, outcompeting nucleotide exchange and depleting active KRAS, while cetuximab blocks EGFR‑mediated feedback, together producing higher response rates in KRASG12C colorectal cancer.
key_findings: Monotherapy ORR in KRASG12C CRC was 40.5%; combination with cetuximab increased ORR to 62.1%.
In KRASG12C PDAC, monotherapy ORR reached 69.6% with median PFS 13.5 months.
Grade ≥3 TRAEs were low in monotherapy (15% CRC, 8.7% PDAC) but higher in combo (48.4%).
Rapid and deep ctDNA KRASG12C allele‑frequency reductions observed across cohorts.
Acquired resistance in combo arm frequently involved MET amplification, BRAF V600E, and MAP2K1 mutations.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Combination induced higher RTK‑MAPK pathway alterations (MET amp, BRAF V600E) as resistance mechanisms.

combination_strategies.rationale: Block EGFR feedback that reactivates KRAS signaling after KRASG12C inhibition
combination_strategies.phenotype: higher ORR (62.1% vs 40.5%) but increased grade ≥3 TRAEs

combination_strategies.rationale: Target downstream MAPK reactivation observed in resistance
combination_strategies.phenotype: proposed to overcome MET/BRAF/MAP2K1‑mediated resistance

open_questions: Will upfront combination with cetuximab improve overall survival despite higher toxicity?
Can early detection of MET amplification guide addition of MET inhibitors?

talk_title: Namifotenib + gemcitabine/nab‑paclitaxel in first‑line metastatic pancreatic cancer: ACCENT trial

models.name: ACCENT phase 1b/2a
models.genotype_or_key_features: metastatic PDAC, chemo‑naïve, 400 mg namifotenib intermittent dosing

MOA_summary: Namifotenib selectively inhibits focal adhesion kinase, reducing tumor‑cell survival signals and stromal fibrosis, thereby enhancing the efficacy of gemcitabine/nab‑paclitaxel in metastatic pancreatic cancer.
key_findings: Confirmed ORR of 36% (vs 23% historical) with namifotenib + gem‑nab.
Five complete responses observed (8%).
Median PFS improved to 7.7 months compared with 5.5 months for gem‑nab alone.
Median OS reached 11.1 months, comparable to FOLFIRINOX historical data.
Safety profile comparable to standard gem‑nab; grade ≥3 neutropenia 39%.

combination_strategies.rationale: FAK inhibition may reduce stromal barrier and enhance chemotherapy delivery
combination_strategies.phenotype: improved ORR and PFS without added severe toxicity

combination_strategies.rationale: Potential synergistic effect with more intensive chemotherapy
combination_strategies.phenotype: preclinical synergy; clinical trial on hold

open_questions: Will continuous daily dosing of namifotenib further improve efficacy?
Can biomarkers of fibrosis predict responders?

talk_title: Fractionated dose‑dense actinium‑225 J591 anti‑PSMA therapy in metastatic prostate cancer: phase 1 study

biomarkers.name: PSMA PET uptake
biomarkers.finding: used as baseline biomarker; not for eligibility

biomarkers.name: PSA decline ≥50%
biomarkers.finding: ≈70% of patients achieved ≥50% PSA decline

models.name: Phase 1 fractionated vs multiple‑cycle actinium‑225 J591
models.genotype_or_key_features: metastatic CRPC, prior lutetium‑PSMA exposure allowed

MOA_summary: Actinium‑225 labeled anti‑PSMA antibody J591 delivers high‑LET alpha particles directly to PSMA‑expressing prostate cancer cells, causing potent DNA damage while sparing surrounding tissue due to the short range of alpha emissions.
key_findings: Recommended phase 2 dose established at 60 kBq/kg for fractionated regimen.
≈70% of patients achieved ≥50% PSA decline; 20% had partial radiographic responses.
Median overall survival was ~12 months in a heavily pre‑treated cohort.
Dose‑dense fractionated schedule showed acceptable hematologic toxicity (mainly thrombocytopenia).
Multiple‑cycle regimen was not recommended due to higher DLT rate.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Thrombocytopenia was dose‑limiting; no non‑hematologic DLTs observed.

combination_strategies.with_agent: future combination with systemic therapies (e.g., ARPI)
combination_strategies.rationale: Potential additive efficacy after establishing safety of fractionated dosing
combination_strategies.phenotype: not yet tested

open_questions: Will earlier use of fractionated actinium‑225 improve survival compared with beta‑emitters?
Optimal sequencing with lutetium‑PSMA therapy?

talk_title: A Primer for Geospatial Science Analytics in Cancer Research and Translation

speaker.name: Trang Vo-Pham
speaker.affiliation: Fred Hutchinson Cancer Center, University of Washington

clinical_data.population: Washington state patients at high risk for air pollution-related health effects

MOA_summary: Geospatial science links residential locations to environmental exposure datasets (e.g., air pollution, green space, socioeconomic indices) enabling epidemiologic analyses of cancer incidence, mortality, and disparities; translational tools such as the GeoExMap web app and EHR-linked outreach convert these insights into community‑focused interventions.
key_findings: Nearly 3,000 cancer‑related geospatial publications were indexed in 2025, demonstrating rapid growth of the field.
Geospatial analyses identified cancer mortality hotspots linked to lower education and mammography screening rates.
Neighborhood deprivation index was positively associated with breast cancer‑specific mortality among non‑Hispanic White women but not Black women.
Higher county‑level trauma measures correlated with increased all‑cancer mortality and lower cancer‑prevention behaviors.
Neighborhood socioeconomic status explained most variation in lung cancer mortality, whereas genetics dominated breast and prostate cancer mortality.
County‑level wildfire‑related PM2.5 exposure was associated with ~490 additional cancer deaths per year per 0.1 µg/m³ increase.
The GeoExMap open‑source web app provides interactive mapping of >175 health and environmental variables with community‑tailored mitigation tips.

talk_title: Environmental Exposures Impact on Cancer Risk and Survival

speaker.name: Jamie Hart
speaker.affiliation: Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health, Brigham and Women's Hospital

clinical_data.population: cancer patients exposed to wildfire smoke

clinical_data.value: suggestive elevated risk, driven by premenopausal and Black/Non‑Hispanic White women

MOA_summary: Fine particulate matter (PM2.5) and traffic‑related nitrogen dioxide (NO2) generate oxidative stress, chronic inflammation, DNA adducts and epigenetic alterations that increase cancer initiation and progression; wildfire‑derived PM2.5 appears more toxic, amplifying mortality risk in exposed cancer patients.
key_findings: A 10 µg/m³ increase in PM2.5 is associated with a 13% higher lung cancer incidence across global cohorts.
Wildfire‑specific PM2.5 exposure adds ~490 cancer deaths per year per 0.1 µg/m³ increase, indicating higher toxicity than background PM2.5.
NO2 exposure shows a suggestive elevated risk for premenopausal breast cancer, especially among Black and non‑Hispanic White women.
Recent studies are shifting from total PM2.5 to source‑specific components (e.g., metal content, wildfire smoke) to pinpoint carcinogenic drivers.
Survival analyses using SEER‑Medicare data reveal that patients in high‑wildfire PM2.5 regions experience worse cancer‑specific mortality.
open_questions: How can source‑specific air pollution components be integrated into risk prediction models for diverse cancer types?

talk_title: Impact of Structural and Social Drivers of Health on Cancer Risk and Survival

models.name: RESPOND Study (U19)
models.genotype_or_key_features: 10 cancer registries across 8 states, >82,000 Black men, >314,000 White men

clinical_data.value: up to ~2.0 in highest redlining quintile
clinical_data.n: n=82,000 Black men; n=314,000 White men
clinical_data.population: African American and non‑Hispanic White men with prostate cancer
Black breast cancer patients in North Carolina

MOA_summary: Structural racism manifested as redlining and multi‑domain segregation creates concentrated socioeconomic deprivation, leading to higher exposure to adverse environmental factors and limited healthcare access, which together increase tumor aggressiveness and mortality in affected populations.
key_findings: Black men with prostate cancer residing in the highest redlining neighborhoods have nearly double the hazard of prostate cancer mortality compared with White men in non‑redlined areas.

Across multiple states (e.g., California, Georgia, Louisiana, Detroit, New Jersey, Texas, Florida), a dose‑response relationship between redlining intensity and prostate cancer mortality was observed.

A composite county‑level structural racism index (housing, education, employment, income, health) was associated with higher odds of treatment delay among Black breast cancer patients, but not among White patients.
Multi‑domain structural racism measures reveal heterogeneous impacts across cancer types, underscoring the need for race‑specific composite indices.
Community advisory boards guided the selection of geospatial variables, ensuring relevance to lived experiences and facilitating translation of findings.
open_questions: Do historical redlining patterns produce similar mortality gradients as contemporary redlining?
Can interventions targeting housing discrimination reduce observed survival disparities?

talk_title: BIGHAT-299 first-in-class CCAM6-directed TCE for solid malignancies

targets.dependency_context: high CCAM6 expression on solid tumor cells vs neutrophils

models.name: primary neutrophils and cancer cell lines
models.genotype_or_key_features: CCAM6 high expression in tumor cells, 28k copies on neutrophils

models.name: PDX panel (12-15 models)
models.genotype_or_key_features: Diverse solid tumor histologies with CCAM6 expression

models.name: CCAM6 humanized CD34+ mouse
models.genotype_or_key_features: human CCAM6 expression on myeloid cells

MOA_summary: BIGHAT-299 is a conditionally active bispecific T‑cell engager that binds a low‑affinity VHH to CCAM6 on tumor cells and a pH‑conditional CD3 arm, achieving selective cytotoxicity against high‑density CCAM6 tumor cells while sparing neutrophils, leading to potent tumor regression in preclinical models.
key_findings: BIGHAT-299 exhibits a 1,000‑fold IC50 differential between CCAM6‑high tumor cells and neutrophils (20‑100 nM vs 1‑10 pM).
In CCAM6‑humanized mice, weekly dosing up to 2 mg/kg showed no neutrophil depletion or anemia, unlike high‑affinity controls.
Complete tumor regressions were observed in multiple CCAM6‑positive PDX models at 50 µg/kg weekly.
Pharmacokinetic studies in cynomolgus monkeys demonstrated multi‑day half‑life and sub‑Q bioavailability >120 mg/mL.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity achieved by exploiting >80‑fold antigen density difference between tumor and normal neutrophils.
No activity observed against monocytes/macrophages with 3‑10 k copies per cell.
open_questions: What is the minimal CCAM6 copy number required for therapeutic efficacy in heterogeneous patient tumors?
Can the conditional CD3 affinity be further tuned to improve safety in humans?

talk_title: ENA-101 first-in-class bispecific T‑cell engager targeting DART‑fox peptide‑HLA A3

targets.dependency_context: HLA‑A3 positive tumors expressing DART‑fox peptide

biomarkers.name: HLA‑A3 positivity
biomarkers.finding: 20‑30% of patients in US/EU are HLA‑A3 positive, enabling enrollment

biomarkers.name: DART‑fox A3 peptide expression
biomarkers.finding: RNA‑ISH score ≥2 in >70% of lung squamous, TNBC, head‑and‑neck samples

models.name: DART‑fox positive cancer lines
models.genotype_or_key_features: HLA‑A3 positive, endogenous DART‑fox peptide presentation

models.name: CRISPR DART‑fox knockout colorectal line
models.genotype_or_key_features: no DART‑fox peptide, serves as negative control

models.name: NSG mouse tumor model
models.genotype_or_key_features: human tumor cells + human PBMCs, doses 50 µg/kg‑1 mg/kg

MOA_summary: ENA‑101 is a bispecific T‑cell engager that binds a high‑affinity antibody specific for the DART‑fox A3 peptide presented by HLA‑A3 and an intermediate‑affinity anti‑CD3 scFv, delivering potent, peptide‑specific T‑cell activation and tumor cell killing while sparing normal HLA‑A3 positive tissues.
key_findings: ENA‑101 binds DART‑fox A3/HLA‑A3 with double‑digit picomolar affinity and shows no off‑target binding to HLA‑A3 alone.
In vitro cytotoxicity reaches sub‑10 pM EC50 against DART‑fox positive lines, with no activity against knockout controls.
No cytokine release syndrome signals observed in whole‑blood assays up to high concentrations.
In NSG xenograft models, weekly dosing (50 µg/kg‑1 mg/kg) achieved complete tumor regressions without detectable toxicity.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity driven by peptide‑HLA presentation; loss of HLA‑A3 or peptide expression could confer resistance.
HLA‑A3 loss of heterozygosity occurs in ~7‑10% of patients, representing a potential escape mechanism.
open_questions: Can ENA‑101 synergize with checkpoint inhibitors in HLA‑A3 positive patients?
What is the durability of T‑cell memory responses after peptide‑specific killing?

talk_title: NRP1‑targeted peptide‑linked antibodies redirect viral memory T cells

biomarkers.name: NRP1 expression
biomarkers.finding: High NRP1 IHC in >70% of tested tumor lines

biomarkers.name: CMV NLV peptide/HLA‑A2
biomarkers.finding: Detectable surface presentation after 5D6‑NLV treatment

models.name: NSG tumor xenograft with human PBMCs
HLA‑A2 transgenic mouse
models.genotype_or_key_features: systemic expression of HLA‑A2 for safety assessment

MOA_summary: A NRP1‑binding antibody (5D6) chemically or recombinantly linked to viral peptides (e.g., CMV NLV) is internalized, processed, and cross‑presented on HLA‑A2, enabling circulating virus‑specific T cells to recognize and kill NRP1‑positive tumor cells while sparing normal NRP1‑expressing tissues.
key_findings: 5D6‑NLV delivers >200 copies of CMV peptide per tumor cell, sufficient for TCR‑mediated killing.
In vitro, peptide‑linked 5D6 induces dose‑dependent IFN‑γ and TNF‑α release from CMV‑specific T cells.
In NSG xenograft models, a single dose of peptide‑linked 5D6 plus 5 × 10⁶ TCR‑T cells eradicated tumors without off‑target toxicity.
No cytokine release or cytotoxicity observed against NRP1‑positive endothelial or hematopoietic cells.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Potential resistance if tumor down‑regulates NRP1 or HLA‑A2.
Cross‑presentation efficiency varies with antigen density; low NRP1 may limit peptide loading.
open_questions: Can this platform be extended to other viral or tumor‑derived peptides?
What is the durability of redirected viral memory T‑cell responses in vivo?

talk_title: PrimeBody platform enables tumor‑activated CD47 targeting with superior therapeutic index

targets.dependency_context: ubiquitous CD47 expression; tumor activation via protease‑cleavable linker

biomarkers.name: CD47 expression
biomarkers.finding: High CD47 on all solid tumors evaluated

models.name: CD47‑humanized + SIRPα‑humanized mouse
models.genotype_or_key_features: allows assessment of anemia and PK

MOA_summary: VORA101 is a high‑affinity SIRPα‑based CD47 blocker fused to an enhanced Fc and masked with a protease‑cleavable linker; tumor‑specific proteases remove the mask, restoring CD47 blockade and Fc‑mediated ADCP/ADCC while sparing normal tissues, thereby eliminating anemia and improving exposure.
key_findings: Masked VORA101 shows >500‑fold tumor‑selective activation in ex vivo human tumor tissue versus serum.
In CD47‑humanized mice, VORA101 prevented anemia at doses where unmasked migrolimab caused severe anemia.
VORA101 achieved 2‑fold higher serum concentrations than migrolimab due to reduced tissue sink.
In Raji xenografts, masked VORA101 induced complete tumor regressions, outperforming migrolimab.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Efficacy depends on presence of tumor‑associated proteases; protease down‑regulation could reduce activation.
Mask designed for human proteases; mouse models show lower off‑target cleavage.

combination_strategies.rationale: CD47 blockade synergizes with Fc‑active HER2 antibody to promote ADCP
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved ORR in HER2‑positive gastric model

open_questions: Which protease signatures best predict activation across the selected indications?
Can the mask be further optimized to reduce any residual peripheral activation?

talk_title: Integrated CD2 costimulation in trispecific T‑cell engagers improves T‑cell fitness

targets.dependency_context: CD3 on T cells; CD2 costimulation via CD58 on tumor

biomarkers.name: CD2 expression
biomarkers.finding: Detected on >80% of tumor‑infiltrating T cells across scRNA‑seq datasets

MOA_summary: Evolve‑104 is a trispecific engager that combines anti‑CD3, tumor antigen binding, and a CD2 agonist (via CD58 engagement), delivering signal‑1 and signal‑2 simultaneously to T cells, resulting in enhanced proliferation, reduced exhaustion, and superior antitumor activity.
key_findings: CD2‑integrated Evolve‑104 induced 2‑fold higher CD8 proliferation and Ki67 positivity at day 14 versus conventional bispecifics.
Affinity‑tuned Evolve‑104 achieved ~60% tumor cell killing at 1:10 E:T ratio, outperforming bispecifics.
In NCI‑H292 xenografts, Evolve‑104 produced ~60% tumor growth inhibition, whereas bispecifics achieved ~40%.
Ex vivo TIL assays showed the greatest up‑regulation of CD25 and CD137 with affinity‑tuned Evolve‑104.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Potential for increased CRS mitigated by CD3 affinity tuning; preclinical assays show reduced cytokine release.
open_questions: Will CD2 costimulation affect NK‑cell activity in patients?
What is the optimal CD3 affinity to balance efficacy and safety in humans?

talk_title: Preclinical evaluation and safety of CVX250, a bispecific T‑cell engager targeting Cathepsin G peptide‑HLA in AML

speaker.name: Jennifer Hibel
speaker.affiliation: Crossbow Therapeutics / MD Anderson Cancer Center

targets.dependency_context: HLA‑A2 positive AML cells presenting CG1 peptide

biomarkers.name: HLA‑A2 positivity
biomarkers.finding: Present in ~40% of US population; required for enrollment

biomarkers.name: CG1 peptide copy number
biomarkers.finding: Potent killing observed down to ~33 copies per cell

models.name: NSG mouse with human PBMCs and ML2 AML

MOA_summary: CVX250 is a TCR‑mimetic bispecific that binds the CG1 peptide presented by HLA‑A2 on AML cells and CD3 on T cells, mediating picomolar‑potent cytotoxicity and bystander killing of CG1‑negative AML cells via cytokine‑mediated up‑regulation of death receptors.
key_findings: CVX250 kills AML cell lines with EC50 values in the single‑digit picomolar range, even at 33 CG1 copies per cell.
Bystander killing observed when target‑positive cells were mixed with target‑negative cells, indicating cytokine‑mediated effects.
No significant cytokine release or platelet activation detected in whole‑blood assays up to high concentrations.
PK in cynomolgus monkeys showed IgG‑like half‑life (7‑14 days) with minimal immunogenicity.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Potential resistance if AML down‑regulates HLA‑A2 or CG1 processing.
FcγR2A binding observed at high concentrations but unlikely to be functional at therapeutic levels.
open_questions: Will CVX250 retain activity in the bone‑marrow microenvironment where HLA expression may be lower?
Can combination with hypomethylating agents increase CG1 presentation?

talk_title: First‑in‑class monoclonal antibody targeting TM4SF5 for gastrointestinal cancers

biomarkers.name: TM4SF5 expression
biomarkers.finding: High TM4SF5 correlates with ~2‑fold worse overall survival in HCC and CRC

models.name: CRC patient‑derived xenograft
models.genotype_or_key_features: Variable response; up to 60% TGI in responders

MOA_summary: RCT1213 binds a conformational epitope spanning both extracellular loops of TM4SF5, disrupting its interaction with IL‑6R/STAT3 and SLAMF7, thereby reducing immunosuppressive cytokine signaling, restoring NK‑cell recognition, and reshaping the tumor microenvironment toward immune activation.
key_findings: RCT1213 binds EC1/EC2 loops with sub‑nanomolar affinity and blocks TM4SF5‑IL‑6R interaction, reducing STAT3 phosphorylation.
In HCC mouse models, RCT1213 achieved ~80% tumor growth inhibition, comparable to atezolizumab + bevacizumab.
Combination of RCT1213 with atezolizumab and bevacizumab yielded complete responses in all tested mice.
In CRC PDX, dose‑dependent tumor suppression observed; responders showed increased CD8⁺ T cells and decreased Tregs.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Potential resistance if tumor down‑regulates TM4SF5 or compensates via alternative tetraspanins.
Selectivity confirmed by minimal off‑target binding in biodistribution studies.

combination_strategies.rationale: Checkpoint blockade may synergize with TM4SF5‑mediated TME remodeling
combination_strategies.phenotype: Complete tumor regression in HCC models

combination_strategies.rationale: Anti‑angiogenic effect complements TM4SF5‑driven STAT3 inhibition
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced tumor control versus monotherapy

open_questions: Will TM4SF5 expression serve as a reliable predictive biomarker across diverse GI tumor subtypes?
Can RCT1213 be engineered into a bispecific format to further enhance efficacy?

talk_title: Targeting Dormant Residual Tumor Cells with Autophagy and mTOR Inhibition (CLEVER Trial)

speaker.name: Louis Chodosh
speaker.affiliation: University of Pennsylvania, Abramson Cancer Center

targets.dependency_context: dormant residual tumor cells in breast cancer

biomarkers.name: pan-cytokeratin positive DTCs in bone marrow
biomarkers.finding: presence associated with increased recurrence risk

models.name: doxycycline-inducible HER2 mammary model (MTB-TAN)
models.genotype_or_key_features: HER2 on/off toggling, residual tumor cells

models.name: CLEVER phase II trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: high-risk early-stage breast cancer patients with bone marrow DTCs

clinical_data.population: high-risk breast cancer patients with DTCs

MOA_summary: In mouse models, transient inhibition of autophagy (chloroquine) or mTOR signaling (everolimus) selectively depletes dormant residual tumor cells, leading to dose‑dependent improvements in recurrence‑free survival; the parallel phase II CLEVER trial demonstrated feasibility, safety, and significant DTC burden reduction with similar agents, suggesting that targeting dormancy‑specific dependencies can prevent breast cancer recurrence.
key_findings: Gamma‑secretase inhibition of Notch during dormancy reduced recurrence in 50% of mice.
Genetic or pharmacologic autophagy inhibition (ATG5/7 knockdown or chloroquine) decreased residual tumor cell numbers and improved recurrence‑free survival in mice.
mTOR inhibition with everolimus, even transiently (3‑9 weeks), markedly prolonged recurrence‑free survival in mice.
In the CLEVER phase II trial (n=51), hydroxychloroquine, everolimus, or their combination reduced bone‑marrow DTC burden by ~80% versus observation.
Five‑year recurrence‑free survival across all CLEVER arms was ~96%, exceeding expectations for this high‑risk cohort.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Notch inhibition accelerated growth of established recurrent tumors, indicating stage‑specific selectivity.
mTOR and autophagy inhibitors had no effect on primary tumor formation or established recurrent tumors, highlighting dormancy‑specific vulnerability.

combination_strategies.rationale: dual inhibition of autophagy and mTOR may synergistically deplete dormant cells
combination_strategies.phenotype: modest improvement over everolimus alone in mouse recurrence‑free survival

open_questions: What biomarkers can reliably predict which patients will benefit most from dormancy‑targeted therapy?
Can real‑time pharmacodynamic monitoring of DTC depletion be standardized for clinical use?

talk_title: Residual Breast Cancer Cells Co‑opt SOX5‑Driven Endochondral Ossification to Maintain Dormancy

targets.dependency_context: dormant residual tumor cells in HER2‑driven mouse model

models.name: MTB‑TAN doxycycline‑inducible HER2 model
models.genotype_or_key_features: HER2 on/off, residual lesions, CRISPR‑Cas9 screen

MOA_summary: CRISPR‑Cas9 screening of dormant residual tumor cells identified SOX5 as a suppressor of proliferation; loss of SOX5 accelerates recurrence by increasing proliferative outgrowth of dormant cells and impairs a dormancy‑associated endochondral ossification program marked by proteoglycan accumulation and 18F‑NaF PET/CT signal, linking developmental ossification pathways to tumor cell quiescence.
key_findings: SOX5 loss (via two independent guide RNAs) accelerated tumor recurrence in orthotopic and experimental lung metastasis models (hazard ratio ~2.5‑3).
SOX5 knockout increased Ki‑67 positivity specifically in late residual lesions, indicating loss of dormancy‑enforced quiescence.
Dormant residual lesions displayed endochondral ossification signatures (proteoglycan and calcification staining) and progressive 18F‑NaF PET/CT uptake.
SOX5 knockdown markedly reduced calcification and PET tracer uptake in residual lesions, linking SOX5 to the ossification program.
Enrichment of chondrogenic/osteogenic gene signatures in post‑neoadjuvant patient samples correlated with delayed time to recurrence.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: SOX5 loss specifically affected dormant cells without altering proliferation in primary tumor phases.
open_questions: What upstream signals regulate SOX5 activation during dormancy?
Can pharmacologic modulation of the ossification pathway sustain dormancy and prevent recurrence?

talk_title: Molecular Characterization of Human Disseminated Tumor Cells Reveals Embryonic‑Like Stemness and Early Dissemination

biomarkers.name: EpCAM+ cytokeratin+ DTCs in bone marrow
biomarkers.finding: presence predicts higher risk of metastasis across cancer types

models.name: bone marrow aspirates from early‑stage cancer patients (M0) and metastatic patients (M1)
models.genotype_or_key_features: single‑cell whole‑genome amplification, RNA‑seq, phospho‑protein profiling

models.name: spontaneous tumor models for early dissemination studies
models.genotype_or_key_features: tracking DTC appearance before primary tumor detection

MOA_summary: Single‑cell genomic and transcriptomic analyses of bone‑marrow disseminated tumor cells (DTCs) from early‑stage (M0) and metastatic (M1) patients reveal that M0 DTCs lack late‑stage driver mutations, exhibit high stemness and embryonic‑like gene expression (similar to zygote/early‑embryo signatures), and their stemness scores strongly predict poor survival, indicating that early dissemination involves a primitive, pluripotent‑like state distinct from overt metastases.
key_findings: Dissemination of tumor cells occurs early, often before a detectable primary tumor, in both mouse models and patients.
M0 DTCs display embryonic stem‑cell–like transcriptional programs and highest stemness scores, exceeding those of adult erythroid progenitors.
High stemness scores in EpCAM+ DTCs correlate with markedly reduced overall survival in lung cancer patients.
M0 DTCs cluster transcriptionally with human embryonic stem cells, whereas M1 DTCs align with differentiated epithelial/mesenchymal signatures.
Phospho‑protein profiling of DTCs from a HER2‑positive patient after neoadjuvant therapy showed persistent activation of HER2, AKT, ERK, and STAT3 pathways, indicating therapy‑resistant signaling.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Activated HER2‑AKT‑ERK‑STAT3 signaling observed in residual DTCs despite HER2‑targeted therapy suggests intrinsic resistance mechanisms.
open_questions: What molecular mechanisms drive the embryonic‑like stemness program in early DTCs?
Can targeting the identified IL‑6/STAT3 axis eradicate embryonic‑like DTCs without harming normal stem cells?

talk_title: Pan-cancer analysis of rare RAS alleles using AACR Project Genie

models.name: AACR Project Genie v19
models.genotype_or_key_features: 271,000 tumor samples, 220,000 patients, RAS mutant status

MOA_summary: Analysis of >270k tumors from Project Genie revealed that non‑G12C KRAS alleles dominate (≈60% of KRAS mutations) and display lineage‑specific distribution, while HRAS and NRAS show distinct tissue enrichment and co‑mutation architectures, indicating isoform‑specific biological contexts that can inform lineage‑tailored RAS‑targeted strategies.
key_findings: Non‑G12C KRAS alleles comprise ~60% of KRAS mutations across 42,000 KRAS‑altered tumors, outnumbering G12C (≈16%).
KRAS G12D/V are broadly distributed in pancreatic, colorectal, and lung adenocarcinomas; G12C/A enriched in lung adenocarcinoma; G12R restricted to pancreas; Q61 spread across multiple lineages.
KRAS co‑mutations show tissue‑specific patterns (e.g., SMAD4, APC in colon) and mutual exclusivity with NF1, PTEN, RB1, EGFR, MET, BRAF.

HRAS mutations (<1% of tumors) are enriched in bladder, urothelial, lung, melanoma, thyroid, colorectal, and head‑and‑neck cancers, with codon 61 predominance and multi‑pathway co‑activations (PI3K, RTK, NOTCH1, CDK4/6).
NRAS mutations are enriched in melanoma, myeloid, colorectal, lung, and thyroid cancers; Q61 accounts for ~56% of NRAS mutations and display both pathway redundancy and lineage‑specific co‑mutations.
open_questions: How do rare RAS alleles (e.g., A146, Q22) influence response to emerging RAS inhibitors?
Do allele frequencies differ between treatment‑naïve and post‑therapy samples?

talk_title: Neomorphic p53 alleles drive organ‑specific metastatic programs

speaker.name: Gizem Efe
speaker.affiliation: Columbia University Medical Center, Irving Scholar

biomarkers.name: TP53 missense vs. DNA‑contact vs. conformational mutation type
biomarkers.finding: Missense TP53 mutations associate with lung metastasis and better OS than conformational mutations; specific hotspot (R175) shows superior OS vs R282

models.name: 4‑NQO induced esophageal SCC with TP53 WT, null, or R172H
models.genotype_or_key_features: TP53 status defined; metastatic lesions harvested from lung and liver

models.name: AACR GENIE SCC cohort (v19)
models.genotype_or_key_features: TP53 mutation spectrum correlated with metastatic site and survival

clinical_data.value: HR 0.68 for missense vs. conformational TP53 mutations
better OS for lung metastasis vs. liver metastasis

MOA_summary: Mutant TP53 R172H occupies novel genomic loci in metastatic lesions, directly up‑regulating CSF1 via promoter and enhancer binding, which promotes macrophage‑mediated metastasis; organ‑specific mutant TP53 programs (e.g., EMT/glycolysis in lung, Hedgehog/STAT3 in liver) drive distinct metastatic phenotypes and correlate with survival differences across mutation classes.
key_findings: Mutant TP53 R172H binds novel promoter/enhancer regions at the CSF1 locus in metastatic tumors, increasing H3K27ac without changing TP53 protein levels.
CRISPR‑mediated CSF1 deletion in TP53‑mutant cells markedly reduces metastatic burden, an effect not seen in TP53‑null cells.
Organ‑specific metastatic signatures: lung metastases enriched for EMT and glycolysis; liver metastases for Hedgehog and IL6/STAT3 pathways.

In AACR GENIE SCC cohort, missense TP53 mutations associate with lung metastasis, while splice/nonsense variants show different patterns; TP53‑mutant patients have worse OS overall, but lung‑metastatic TP53‑mutants have better prognosis than liver‑metastatic ones.
Hotspot TP53 variants display distinct survival outcomes (e.g., R175 better OS than R282).
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: CSF1 mediates both tumor‑intrinsic and macrophage‑dependent mechanisms, suggesting dual resistance pathways.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target macrophage recruitment driven by mutant TP53‑induced CSF1 expression
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced metastatic burden in TP53‑mutant mouse models

open_questions: Do different TP53 mutation classes confer distinct sensitivities to CSF1R blockade?
What epigenetic co‑factors partner with mutant TP53 at organ‑specific enhancers?

talk_title: KMT2C mutation predicts platinum sensitivity across solid tumors

speaker.name: Bruno Bacorni
speaker.affiliation: Dana‑Farber Cancer Institute / Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center

biomarkers.name: KMT2C mutation
biomarkers.finding: Associated with 3.9‑month OS improvement on platinum (51 mo vs 25.3 mo in colorectal cancer, HR≈0.5)

models.name: Tempus Lens real‑world database
models.genotype_or_key_features: 143k sequenced patients; 19k received platinum; KMT2C status known

models.name: AACR Project Genie colorectal cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 1,500 CRC patients; 800 with KMT2C data; platinum vs irinotecan outcomes

models.name: CRISPR KMT2C‑KO in colorectal, head‑and‑neck, endometrial lines
Colorectal cancer xenograft with KMT2C KO

clinical_data.population: all solid tumors, KMT2C mutant vs WT

clinical_data.value: Improved in KMT2C‑mutant across GI, endometrial, head‑and‑neck
No difference between KMT2C mutant and WT

MOA_summary: Loss‑of‑function KMT2C impairs homologous recombination repair, creating a functional HRD phenotype that sensitizes tumors to DNA cross‑linking platinum agents, as demonstrated by superior OS and PFS in KMT2C‑mutant patients, especially in colorectal cancer.
key_findings: KMT2C loss‑of‑function occurs in ~5‑6% of solid tumors and >10% in bladder, cervical, melanoma, endometrial cancers.
Across 19,000 platinum‑treated patients, KMT2C mutants showed a 2.9‑month OS advantage (19.6 mo vs 16.7 mo).
In colorectal cancer, KMT2C mutation doubled median OS (51 mo vs 25.3 mo) and improved PFS.
Benefit was specific to platinum agents; no OS advantage observed with irinotecan‑based therapy.
CRISPR KMT2C knockout increased oxaliplatin sensitivity in colorectal, head‑and‑neck, and endometrial cell lines; xenograft models confirmed enhanced tumor regression.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: No platinum benefit observed in breast, lung, or GU cancers despite KMT2C mutation.

combination_strategies.rationale: KMT2C loss creates HRD, potentially sensitizing to PARP blockade
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic cytotoxicity in KMT2C‑KO cell lines (preliminary)

open_questions: Does high tumor mutation burden in KMT2C‑mutant tumors contribute to immunotherapy response?
Can a prospective trial stratify patients to platinum vs non‑platinum based on KMT2C status?

talk_title: Rare class‑II BRAF alterations and fusions in pancreatic cancer

speaker.name: Dr. Ochi
speaker.affiliation: Institution not specified (presenter of BRAF study)

models.name: GECAN (Japan) and AACR Project Genie datasets
models.genotype_or_key_features: Pancreatic cancer cases with BRAF alterations classified by OncoKB

clinical_data.value: trend toward worse OS for class‑II vs class‑I (not statistically significant)
clinical_data.population: pancreatic cancer patients with BRAF alterations

MOA_summary: Pancreatic cancers harbor a distinctive spectrum of class‑II BRAF alterations (predominantly N486K/P190 and fusions) that signal as dimers and are resistant to monomeric BRAF inhibitors, suggesting a need for dimer‑targeted or MEK‑based therapeutic strategies.
key_findings: Class‑II BRAF alterations constitute the majority of BRAF events in pancreatic cancer, unlike other tumors where class‑I V600E dominates.
N486K/P190 alterations account for 82% of class‑II events in pancreatic cancer (GECAN) and 36% in GENIE.
Class‑II BRAF alterations trend toward poorer overall survival compared with class‑I, though not statistically significant.
Class‑II alterations are rare in other solid tumors, highlighting a pancreas‑specific genomic pattern.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Class‑II BRAF dimers are resistant to first‑generation BRAF inhibitors, requiring alternative strategies.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target downstream MAPK signaling in BRAF dimer‑driven tumors
combination_strategies.phenotype: Potential therapeutic activity (hypothesized)

combination_strategies.rationale: Direct inhibition of BRAF dimers overcoming paradoxical activation
combination_strategies.phenotype: Preclinical efficacy suggested

open_questions: Do class‑II BRAF alterations confer distinct sensitivity to MEK versus pan‑RAF inhibitors in pancreatic models?
What is the underlying mutagenic process driving the N486K/P190 hotspot in pancreas?

talk_title: Ultima Genomics duplex whole‑genome cfDNA for clonal evolution tracking

models.name: Rapid autopsy cohort (6 breast cancer patients)
models.genotype_or_key_features: Longitudinal cfDNA samples (24 high‑tumor‑fraction) + tissue WES

MOA_summary: High‑depth duplex whole‑genome sequencing (Ultima PPMseq) of cfDNA provides orders‑of‑magnitude more mutations than targeted panels, enabling precise reconstruction of clonal phylogenies, detection of low‑frequency driver events (e.g., TP53), and assignment of mutational processes, thereby improving longitudinal monitoring of tumor evolution.
key_findings: PPMseq cfDNA generated high‑confidence phylogenetic trees matching tissue‑derived trees, with greater clone resolution than whole‑exome cfDNA.
Whole‑genome cfDNA identified low‑frequency TP53 mutations and assigned them to specific subclones, which were missed by exome sequencing.
In a breast cancer patient on PI3K and CDK4/6 inhibitors, distinct subclones harboring different driver mutations were tracked over time, revealing dynamic clonal shifts.
Mutational signature analysis of cfDNA trees uncovered dominant APOBEC activity in specific branches.
Comparative analysis showed higher concordance between cfDNA whole‑genome trees and combined tissue+cfDNA exome trees than cfDNA exome alone.
open_questions: What is the minimal duplex coverage needed to achieve comparable clone resolution with Illumina platforms?
Can structural variant detection from cfDNA whole‑genome sequencing improve therapeutic decision‑making?

talk_title: AI‑integrated gut microbiome analysis in early vs. late onset colorectal cancer

speaker.name: Sofia Manjarrez
speaker.affiliation: City of Hope, Velasquez‑Villareal Lab, PhD student

biomarkers.name: Bacteroides abundance
biomarkers.finding: Significantly enriched in early‑onset CRC fecal samples

biomarkers.name: Veillonella abundance
biomarkers.finding: Higher in early‑onset CRC, especially in low BMI individuals

biomarkers.name: Segotella abundance
biomarkers.finding: More common in late‑onset CRC

models.name: AACR Project Genie + PECGS + USC COPEC (2,715 samples)
23 fecal 16S rRNA samples from Hispanic‑Latino CRC patients (LA)

MOA_summary: Integrating AI agents with multi‑omic (WES, RNA‑seq) and social determinants data reveals that early‑onset colorectal cancer in Hispanic‑Latino patients exhibits reduced gut microbial diversity and specific enrichment of Bacteroides and Veillonella, linking microbiome shifts to clinical and socioeconomic factors such as BMI.
key_findings: Early‑onset CRC patients show significantly lower gut microbial alpha diversity compared with late‑onset patients.
Bacteroides and Veillonella are enriched in early‑onset cases; Segotella is enriched in late‑onset cases.
Veillonella abundance correlates with lower BMI, while Faecalibacterium is higher in obese individuals.
AI agents can query integrated datasets to rapidly assess relationships (e.g., Fusobacterium vs. onset) and generate hypothesis‑driven analyses.
open_questions: Will expanding the fecal cohort to >500 samples validate the observed microbial signatures?
How do generational differences within Hispanic‑Latino populations influence microbiome composition and CRC risk?

session_title: Advancing Early Phase Pediatric Clinical Trials to Turn Promising Agents into Actionable Evidence
talk_title: Menin inhibitor revumenib development and pediatric integration

speaker.name: Dr. Jerry McGean
speaker.affiliation: Syndax Pharmaceuticals, Vice President of the Menin Program

biomarkers.name: blast count reduction
biomarkers.finding: weekly reductions in peripheral blast percentage correlated with on‑target activity and clinical responses

models.name: AUGMENT101 (revumenib monotherapy)
models.genotype_or_key_features: relapsed/refractory KMT2A‑rearranged or NPM1‑mutant leukemia, ages >30 days

clinical_data.metric: patients enrolled in pediatric single‑patient protocols
time from first adult dose to NDA filing

MOA_summary: Revumenib is a high‑affinity protein‑protein interaction inhibitor that binds the scaffold protein menin, displacing the KMT2A‑menin transcription complex. This collapses the HOX/MEIS leukemic transcription program, leading to differentiation or apoptosis of KMT2A‑rearranged or NPM1‑mutant leukemia cells.
key_findings: Revumenib binds menin with high affinity and blocks KMT2A‑menin interaction, collapsing the HOX/MEIS transcription program in preclinical models.
In eight pediatric B‑ALL PDX models, seven showed significant survival benefit (2–12 months extension, some >12 months).
A single‑patient protocol enabled the first pediatric dose (1.5‑year‑old) in October 2019, before formal pediatric enrollment, providing early PK, safety, and efficacy data.
Exposure across the full pediatric weight range (5 kg–75 kg) was proportional to mg/kg dosing, supporting a unified dosing strategy.
Over 130 single‑patient protocols (25 infants, 78 children, 29 adolescents) were collected, demonstrating feasibility of rapid pediatric data acquisition.

Regulatory engagement (pre‑IND meeting, real‑time oncology review) allowed IND filing in mid‑2019, first adult dosing November 2019, and FDA approval by end‑2024 for KMT2A‑rearranged AML and 2025 for NPM1‑mutant AML.

combination_strategies.rationale: preclinical synergy of revumenib with standard ALL/AML chemotherapy regimens
