combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced survival in pediatric PDXs when combined with chemotherapy

talk_title: Pragmatic Lung S2302 Phase 3 Registrational Intent Trial

speaker.name: Constantin Dragnev
speaker.affiliation: Dartmouth Cancer Center, Interim Cancer Center Director and Associate Director for Clinical Research

models.name: Pragmatic Lung S2302
models.genotype_or_key_features: Previously treated lung cancer, any histology

MOA_summary: A pragmatic, registrational Phase 3 trial compared ramucirumab plus pembrolizumab versus standard chemotherapy in previously treated NSCLC, using simplified eligibility and minimal data collection to assess overall survival and safety in routine practice.
key_findings: Pragmatic Lung enrolled 800 participants across >600 sites, exceeding accrual targets (40-60 patients/month).
Overall survival was not superior for ramucirumab/pembrolizumab versus standard chemotherapy.
Serious adverse events leading to discontinuation were lower in the chemotherapy-free arm.
Protocol development and activation completed in 200 days, half the typical timeline.

combination_strategies.rationale: Combine anti‑angiogenic and immune checkpoint inhibition to improve survival without chemotherapy.
combination_strategies.phenotype: no OS benefit but reduced toxicity compared with chemotherapy.

talk_title: Hybrid Decentralized Model for Early‑Phase Trial Recruitment

speaker.name: Pat Lorusso
speaker.affiliation: Yale Cancer Center, Amy and Joseph Perella Professor of Medicine

models.name: Yale Early‑Phase Trial Cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: Diverse adult cancer patients across Connecticut, MA, RI

MOA_summary: Describes a hybrid decentralized approach that brings early‑phase trial procedures to community sites, using multilingual educational tools and patient navigators to increase enrollment of underrepresented populations.
key_findings: Underrepresented patient enrollment in early‑phase trials increased from 2% to ~22% after implementing hybrid decentralization.
Multilingual "Golden Book" myth‑busting tool was created in English and Spanish to improve trial literacy.
Community health workers and navigators facilitated trial participation despite transportation and language barriers.

talk_title: WIN Program: Training Diverse Clinical Trialists

speaker.name: Robert Winn
speaker.affiliation: Virginia Commonwealth University Cancer Center (soon Fox Chase Cancer Center)

MOA_summary: The WIN program provides a two‑year curriculum, protected time, and mentorship to underrepresented investigators, resulting in increased trial enrollment from minority and rural communities.
key_findings: Over 321 scholars trained; 98% remain engaged with community partners years after completion.
Scholars report >60% increase in understanding of trial design and community outreach.
Participating sites show higher minority accrual rates compared with national averages.

talk_title: Health Equity Leadership in Biotech: Embedding Diversity in Drug Development
MOA_summary: Describes industry strategies to build affective and cognitive trust through repeated community presence, multilingual education, and partnership with patient advocates to improve trial diversity.
key_findings: Implementing community‑based education and screening programs increased awareness of clinical trials in target populations.
Balancing affective trust (relationship building) with cognitive trust (data sharing) was identified as critical for trial recruitment.

talk_title: Community Outreach and Rural Trial Accrual in Appalachia

speaker.name: Electra Paskett
speaker.affiliation: Ohio State University, Professor and Deputy Director for Population Sciences and Community Outreach

MOA_summary: Highlights successful community‑based interventions, including mobile screening units and multilingual outreach, that achieved >20% minority accrual in Alliance cancer control and treatment trials across Appalachian regions.
key_findings: Consistently >20% racial/ethnic minority accrual to Alliance trials over three years in Appalachian sites.
Mobile mammography and lung‑van programs detected early‑stage cancers in remote communities lacking hospitals.
Using non‑clinical language (e.g., "lung screening" instead of "cancer") improved community receptivity.

talk_title: WIN Scholar Experience: Community‑Engaged Cervical Cancer Trial Design

speaker.name: Chika Awajiku
speaker.affiliation: University of California San Diego, Assistant Professor and Radiation Oncologist

models.name: Phase I Cervical Cancer Trial (WIN Cohort 2)

MOA_summary: Describes how WIN training enabled design of a patient‑centered phase I cervical cancer trial with simplified logistics, multilingual materials, and community health worker support to improve enrollment of underserved patients.
key_findings: Trial design incorporated patient‑friendly consent and reduced visit burden.
Community health workers facilitated enrollment of patients from low‑income neighborhoods.
Scholars reported increased confidence in integrating community input into protocol development.

talk_title: Community‑Based Engagement for GU Cancer Patients in Rural California

speaker.name: Yue Chen
speaker.affiliation: University of California San Diego, Genitourinary Medical Oncologist

MOA_summary: Outlines use of patient summits, multilingual outreach, and patient‑led storytelling to build trust and increase trial participation among rural GU cancer patients traveling long distances for care.
key_findings: Patient summits featuring trial participants increased willingness to enroll among rural GU cancer patients.
Travel distances up to 2.5 hours were mitigated through coordinated transportation and community partner support.

talk_title: Designing a Pragmatic Registry Trial for Metastatic Breast Cancer Across NY Registries

speaker.name: Chu‑Chen Hong
speaker.affiliation: Roswell Park Comprehensive Cancer Center, Breast Cancer Epidemiology

models.name: PCORI Pragmatic Trial of Metastatic Breast Cancer
models.genotype_or_key_features: Patients recruited via five New York State cancer registries

MOA_summary: Proposes a pragmatic trial leveraging state cancer registries and patient advisory boards to recruit metastatic breast cancer patients, using remote consent, digital surveys, and community‑driven materials to ensure rural representation.
key_findings: Community advisory boards co‑designed patient‑facing materials and surveys.
Remote recruitment via registry data and REDCap enabled enrollment of patients from rural counties.

talk_title: Precision Approaches to HPV-Independent Head and Neck Cancer

speaker.name: Barbara Bertness
speaker.affiliation: Yale School of Medicine, Medical Oncology

tumor_types: head and neck squamous cell carcinoma

biomarkers.name: PD‑L1 CPS
biomarkers.finding: Pembrolizumab plus chemo HR 0.63 in PD‑L1‑rich HPV‑negative HNSCC

biomarkers.name: Tumor Mutation Burden
biomarkers.finding: TMB ≥15 associated with higher response to pembrolizumab in HPV‑negative HNSCC

biomarkers.name: p53 mutation (disruptive)
biomarkers.finding: Disruptive p53 mutations correlate with higher TMB

biomarkers.name: CDKN2A alteration
biomarkers.finding: Co‑mutation with p53 further elevates TMB

models.name: SCC61
models.genotype_or_key_features: CDC25 mutation, resistant to WEE1 inhibition

MOA_summary: Bertness highlighted that HPV‑negative HNSCC harbors EGFR overexpression and frequent loss of tumor suppressors (TP53, CDKN2A) leading to high TMB. She demonstrated that EGFR‑targeted therapies are limited by downstream resistance (PIK3CA, RAS, PTEN loss) and nuclear EGFR. Bispecific agents (EGFR/TGF‑β, EGFR/LGR5, EGFR/MET) improve response when combined with pembrolizumab. She also presented a synthetic‑lethal strategy where p53 loss creates dependence on Aurora A; combined Aurora A and WEE1 inhibition induces replication stress, mitotic catastrophe, and tumor regression in p53‑mutant models, including EGFR‑TKI‑resistant cells.
key_findings: Pembrolizumab plus chemotherapy improves HR to 0.63 in PD‑L1‑rich HPV‑negative HNSCC (KEYNOTE‑048).
Ficaserafusp + pembrolizumab yields 54% response and median OS >20 months in HPV‑negative PD‑L1+ disease, now in phase III FORTIFY‑HN01.
Pitaseptumab + pembrolizumab achieves 66% ORR in HPV‑negative HNSCC, supporting two ongoing phase III trials.
Amivantamab monotherapy shows 45% response in refractory HPV‑negative HNSCC; combination with pembrolizumab reaches 56% response in first line.
Aurora A inhibition (VIC‑1911) blocks replication fork progression; combined with WEE1 inhibitor (davisertib) induces mitotic catastrophe and tumor regression in p53‑mutant HNSCC xenografts and PDXs.
Synergy of Aurora A + WEE1 inhibition is lost in CDC25‑mutant SCC61, indicating genotype‑specific resistance.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Nuclear EGFR evades cetuximab; adding erlotinib restores intracellular EGFR inhibition.
Loss of PTEN, PIK3CA or RAS mutations confer resistance to EGFR‑targeted therapy.
CDC25 mutation confers resistance to WEE1 inhibition, limiting Aurora‑WEE1 synergy in SCC61.

combination_strategies.rationale: PD‑1 blockade synergizes with EGFR bispecifics by enhancing anti‑tumor immunity
combination_strategies.phenotype: Response rate 54% (ficaserafusp) and 66% (pitaseptumab) in HPV‑negative PD‑L1+ HNSCC

combination_strategies.rationale: Standard of care backbone for KEYNOTE‑048
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved HR to 0.63 in PD‑L1‑rich cohort

combination_strategies.rationale: Aurora inhibition activates WEE1‑mediated arrest; dual inhibition forces mitotic entry with defective spindles
combination_strategies.phenotype: Mitotic catastrophe and tumor regression in p53‑mutant models

combination_strategies.rationale: Eradicates nuclear EGFR that cetuximab cannot bind
combination_strategies.phenotype: ORR increased from 33% to 62.5% when added after cetuximab+chemo

open_questions: Which specific p53 disruptive mutations predict maximal synergy with Aurora‑WEE1 inhibition?
Can biomarkers such as CDC25 status be used to pre‑exclude non‑responders to the Aurora‑WEE1 combo?

talk_title: HPV‑Associated Head and Neck Cancer Subtypes and Radiation Sensitivity

speaker.name: Dale Yarbrough
speaker.affiliation: University of North Carolina, Chair of Otolaryngology

biomarkers.name: NF‑kappaB activation signature
biomarkers.finding: Defines low‑risk (high NF‑kappaB) vs high‑risk subtypes with HR >3.5 for survival

biomarkers.name: TRAF3/CYLD mutation status
biomarkers.finding: Mutations associate with improved survival and low‑risk subtype

biomarkers.name: Circulating tumor DNA (HPV ctDNA)
biomarkers.finding: Higher baseline ctDNA in high‑risk subtype; potential stratifier for de‑escalation

clinical_data.metric: Hazard Ratio for survival (high vs low risk)
clinical_data.population: HPV+ HNSCC patients across multiple cohorts

clinical_data.value: significant increase in cell kill after TRAF3 knockout
cure in 1 mouse, improved survival in cohort

MOA_summary: Yarbrough defined two HPV‑positive HNSCC subtypes based on NF‑kappaB activation driven by TRAF3 or CYLD loss; low‑risk tumors exhibit high NF‑kappaB activity and intrinsic radiation sensitivity, while high‑risk tumors are radio‑resistant. Activation of NF‑kappaB via TLR5 agonist entolimod sensitizes high‑risk tumors to radiation, offering a therapeutic avenue to improve the therapeutic ratio.
key_findings: HPV+ HNSCC segregates into low‑risk (high NF‑kappaB, TRAF3/CYLD loss) and high‑risk subtypes with hazard ratios >3.5 for overall survival.
Low‑risk subtype shows markedly increased radiation sensitivity in vitro and in vivo.
NF‑kappaB activation via TLR5 agonist entolimod synergizes with radiation to cure high‑risk mouse tumors while protecting normal tissue.
Birinapant (SMAC mimetic) plus chemoradiation worsened survival, leading to abandonment of that strategy.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: High‑risk tumors resist radiation due to lack of NF‑kappaB‑driven pro‑apoptotic signaling.
SMAC mimetic trial demonstrated unexpected detrimental effect, highlighting context‑dependent NF‑kappaB modulation.

combination_strategies.rationale: NF‑kappaB activation may lower the threshold for radiation‑induced cell death
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced tumor control and cure in high‑risk mouse models

combination_strategies.rationale: Activate NF‑kappaB to sensitize tumors
combination_strategies.phenotype: Worse overall survival in phase II trial (TrialNEX)

open_questions: Can entolimod be safely combined with definitive radiation in humans and improve outcomes for high‑risk HPV+ HNSCC?
What additional biomarkers (e.g., integration status, episomal HPV) refine risk beyond NF‑kappaB signature?

talk_title: Minimal Residual Disease and Treatment Optimization in Head and Neck Cancer

speaker.name: Ari Rosenberg
speaker.affiliation: University of Chicago, Associate Professor of Medical Oncology

tumor_types: HPV-negative head and neck squamous cell carcinoma

biomarkers.name: HPV ctDNA
biomarkers.finding: Baseline detection 86%; clearance by mid‑treatment predicts superior PFS

biomarkers.name: Tumor‑informed ctDNA panel
biomarkers.finding: Post‑operative ctDNA positivity predicts recurrence with 100% PPV in small cohorts

biomarkers.name: Methylation‑based ctDNA assay
biomarkers.finding: Detects MRD in HPV‑negative HNSCC with high specificity when multiple time points used

models.name: DART de‑escalation study
models.genotype_or_key_features: Post‑op HPV+ HNSCC, ctDNA‑stratified radiation dose

clinical_data.population: HPV+ HNSCC, low clinical risk or favorable ctDNA kinetics
Post‑op HPV+ HNSCC, ctDNA‑negative received 36 Gy

MOA_summary: Rosenberg presented ctDNA as a minimally invasive biomarker for MRD across HPV‑positive and HPV‑negative HNSCC. Early clearance of HPV ctDNA during neoadjuvant therapy predicts favorable PFS, while post‑treatment ctDNA positivity forecasts recurrence. Adaptive trial designs using ctDNA dynamics to de‑escalate radiation or chemotherapy, or to intensify immunotherapy, demonstrate improved response rates and survival, illustrating how real‑time molecular monitoring can guide treatment optimization.
key_findings: Baseline HPV ctDNA detected in 86% of patients; mid‑treatment clearance (>95%) identifies a cohort with superior PFS.
In the OPTIMA 2 neoadjuvant trial, ctDNA clearance after neoadjuvant chemo‑immunotherapy correlated with improved PFS.
SYNERGY phase II trial using ctDNA‑guided adaptive chemo‑immunotherapy achieved 63% ORR, median OS 30.9 months, and allowed de‑escalation of chemotherapy in ~75% of patients.
Post‑operative ctDNA positivity predicts recurrence with 100% specificity in early studies, supporting its use for risk‑adapted radiation de‑escalation (DART trial).
Methylation‑based ctDNA assays provide MRD detection in HPV‑negative HNSCC, especially when serial sampling is employed.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: A subset of patients remained ctDNA‑negative despite eventual recurrence, indicating limits of assay sensitivity.
SMAC mimetic trial failure underscores that not all NF‑kappaB activation strategies translate to benefit.

combination_strategies.rationale: Patients with low‑risk ctDNA profile may tolerate radiation de‑escalation without compromising control
combination_strategies.phenotype: 90% 2‑year PFS in low‑risk or favorable ctDNA groups

combination_strategies.rationale: Target MRD‑positive patients post‑definitive therapy to prevent recurrence
combination_strategies.phenotype: Trial ongoing; early data suggest clearance in treated patient

combination_strategies.rationale: ctDNA rise after initial PD‑1 monotherapy triggers addition of chemotherapy in SYNERGY trial
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved ORR and survival compared to static treatment

open_questions: What is the optimal ctDNA assay (tumor‑informed vs tumor‑agnostic) for early detection of MRD in HPV‑negative HNSCC?
Can ctDNA dynamics be integrated with imaging and other biomarkers to refine adaptive treatment algorithms?

talk_title: Agentic AI for Clinical Development in Oncology

speaker.name: Dr. Daniela Bittermann
speaker.affiliation: Brigham and Women's Hospital / Dana-Farber Cancer Institute

MOA_summary: Developed an agentic AI system that ingests unstructured clinical notes, extracts immunotherapy-related adverse events, grades severity, attributes causality, and provides evidence-backed JSON output, achieving macro F1 0.93 and reducing curation time by 40% while maintaining accuracy and inter-annotator agreement.
key_findings: The agentic AI pipeline achieved a macro F1 of 0.93 for immunotherapy adverse event extraction from clinical notes.
Automated curation reduced manual review time from 10 minutes per note to approximately 0.2 minutes (two cents per note), a 40% time reduction.
Accuracy of adverse event determination was comparable to expert oncologists, with significantly improved inter-annotator agreement.
open_questions: How can the adverse event extraction system be extended to prospectively predict adverse events and guide patient selection in trials?
What governance frameworks are needed to benchmark and audit agentic AI outputs for regulatory submissions?

talk_title: Agentic AI in Clinical Tissue Medicine and Pathology

speaker.name: Dr. Sebastian Firsch
speaker.affiliation: University Medical Center Mainz, Institute of Pathology

MOA_summary: Designed a modular agentic AI workflow for pathology that sequentially uses a foundation model to generate microscopic descriptions of whole-slide images, an LLM with retrieval-augmented generation to propose diagnoses and recommend immunohistochemical tests, and downstream agents to interpret IHC results and produce structured reports, all with on-premise deployment and reasoning traceability.
key_findings: A prototype system successfully described a lung squamous cell carcinoma slide, identified p40 positivity and TTF-1 negativity, and generated a final diagnostic report.
The workflow operates on open-source models with low computational requirements and can run on-premise, preserving data privacy.
Incorporating reasoning traces provides auditability for clinical quality management.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Potential bias from handwritten annotations on slides; ~25% of slides contain marks that may affect model predictions.
open_questions: How can the system be hardened against adversarial prompt injection attacks in clinical settings?
What standards are needed for validation and regulatory approval of multi-agent pathology AI pipelines?

session_title: Agentic AI as the Oncologist: Clinical Decision Support and Human AI Collaboration
talk_title: Agentic AI for Radiation Oncology: Imaging, Planning, and Operational Optimization

speaker.name: Dr. Dorin Comaniciu
speaker.affiliation: Siemens Healthineers, Senior Vice President and Chief Expert for Healthcare AI

MOA_summary: AI-driven imaging reconstruction (DeepResolve) accelerates MRI acquisition by 73% and has processed >44 M scans; AI auto‑contouring covers >200 organs at risk; end‑to‑end AI radiation‑therapy planning generates deliverable plans in under a second; an operational‑twin AI schedules patients, machines, and staff in real time to reduce treatment delays.
key_findings: DeepResolve AI reconstruction makes MRI scans up to 73% faster, enabling >44 M AI‑reconstructed scans in 2.5 years.
Regulatory‑approved auto‑contouring system identifies >200 organs at risk and clinical target volumes across disease sites.
AI‑driven radiation‑therapy planning produces a full treatment plan in <1 second on standard hardware for head‑and‑neck cases.
Operational‑twin AI scheduling integrates patient demand, machine capacity, and clinical priority to generate optimized appointment recommendations in one click.

session_title: Agentic AI as the Oncologist: Clinical Decision Support and Human AI Collaboration
talk_title: 4D Oncology Platform: Real‑Time ADC Evaluation and Agentic AI Treatment Selection

speaker.name: Dr. Taehyong Hwang
speaker.affiliation: Vanderbilt University Medical Center, Founding Director, Center of Molecular Artificial Intelligence

models.name: patient tumor organoid
models.genotype_or_key_features: HER2‑positive, FGFR2‑b expression, variable lysosome activity

models.name: NCI Cancer Moonshot gastric cancer cohort

MOA_summary: Agentic AI integrates pathology, NCCN/ESMO guidelines, and real‑time organoid drug testing to prioritize FDA‑approved ADCs and targeted agents; live imaging of drug internalization, lysosomal cleavage, and payload release reveals functional resistance (e.g., lack of lysosome for Trop2‑ADC) and guides selection of HER2‑ADC or FGFR2‑targeted therapy within 30 days.
readouts: live‑cell fluorescence imaging of ADC internalization
key_findings: Deep‑learning AI suggested FGFR2 IHC staining in addition to standard HER2, leading to identification of an off‑label FGFR2‑targetable lesion.
Organoid assay showed Trop2‑ADC failed to kill patient tumor due to absent lysosomal activity, despite target expression.
HER2‑ADC demonstrated robust tumor cell killing in organoid within 24 h, confirming efficacy before patient treatment.
The platform generated a ranked list of 56 FDA‑approved ADCs and predicted penetration, toxicity, and resistance mechanisms for each within 30 days.
Agentic AI integrated guideline‑based recommendations with experimental data to produce a treatment plan that was reviewed by the tumor board.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Trop2‑ADC resistance attributed to tumor cells lacking functional lysosomes, preventing linker cleavage and payload release.

combination_strategies.rationale: FGFR2b overexpression identified by AI‑driven IHC suggests sensitivity to FGFR2‑targeted therapy in HER2‑negative gastric cancer.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Potential synergistic effect when combined with HER2‑ADC in heterogeneous tumors

open_questions: How will regulatory pathways accommodate AI‑generated, patient‑specific organoid drug‑response data for clinical decision making?
Can the 4D monitoring platform be scaled to routine use across multiple cancer types beyond gastric and breast cancer?

talk_title: Clonal hematopoiesis, TET2 deficiency and IL-1β inhibition in lung cancer

speaker.name: Janghee Wu
speaker.affiliation: Emory University Winship Cancer Institute, Department of Hematology

targets.dependency_context: TET2 mutated clonal hematopoiesis in NSCLC

biomarkers.name: TET2 mutation
biomarkers.finding: Associated with decreased lung cancer incidence in canakinumab-treated patients

biomarkers.name: Clonal hematopoiesis (CH) status
biomarkers.finding: 24% of neoadjuvant trial patients carried CH mutations

models.name: KP orthotopic lung cancer model
models.genotype_or_key_features: TET2 heterozygous or homozygous knockout, young (10-12 wk) vs aged (18-20 mo) mice

models.name: REAL-NADIM neoadjuvant chemoimmunotherapy trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: Early-stage NSCLC patients, CH+ vs CH-

models.name: 59 NSCLC patient mononuclear cells
models.genotype_or_key_features: CH mutation profiling, scRNA-seq of >200k cells

clinical_data.value: significantly prolonged in CH+ patients after neoadjuvant chemoimmunotherapy
no significant difference between CH+ and CH-
no significant difference between CH+ and CH-

MOA_summary: TET2-deficient clonal hematopoiesis skews the immune microenvironment toward immunosuppression, increasing myeloid and exhausted T cell populations, which promotes lung tumor development; IL-1β inhibition with canakinumab reduces cancer incidence in CH-negative mice but not in TET2-mutant contexts, highlighting a gene‑environment interaction.
key_findings: TET2-deficient hematopoiesis accelerates lung tumor growth in aged mice but not in young mice.
Aged TET2‑mutant mice show increased tumor‑infiltrating CD45+ cells, reduced NK cells, and exhausted PD‑1+ CD8 T cells.
Human NSCLC patients with TET2‑mutated CH display similar immune remodeling (increased monocytes, reduced NK cells).
In the REAL‑NADIM neoadjuvant trial, CH+ patients had significantly longer overall survival after chemoimmunotherapy despite similar pathologic response and PFS.
CH clones expand over disease course, especially in patients not receiving adjuvant therapy.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: TET2 mutation confers resistance to IL‑1β blockade in later‑stage lung cancer (CANOPE study).

combination_strategies.rationale: Test whether IL‑1β inhibition mitigates CH‑driven tumor promotion
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced lung cancer incidence in CH‑negative mice; no benefit in TET2‑mutant mice

open_questions: Does clonal hematopoiesis acquire new mutations after therapy, converting blood‑only CH to tumor‑infiltrating CH?
What are the mechanisms by which TET2‑mutant CH cells remodel the tumor microenvironment beyond cytokine secretion?

talk_title: Tumor‑infiltrating clonal hematopoiesis (TICH) in the TRACERx NSCLC cohort

speaker.name: Aino-Maija Leppa
speaker.affiliation: University of Cambridge, Department of Oncology

biomarkers.name: TICH status
biomarkers.finding: Patients with tumor‑infiltrating CH have worse disease‑free survival than CH‑negative patients

models.name: TRACERx NSCLC cohort (completed)
models.genotype_or_key_features: 800 patients, 33% CH prevalence, 43% of CH infiltrates tumors

models.name: 37 patients with longitudinal plasma cfDNA
models.genotype_or_key_features: Tracked CHIP clone VAF over time

MOA_summary: Clonal hematopoiesis clones, especially those with DNMT3A or TET2 mutations, can infiltrate NSCLC tumors (TICH), where they expand preferentially and remodel the immune microenvironment toward an inflammatory, interferon‑rich state, leading to poorer recurrence‑free survival, particularly in patients not receiving adjuvant therapy.
key_findings: 33% of TRACERx NSCLC patients harbor CH mutations; 43% of those infiltrate tumors (TICH).
TICH patients have significantly reduced disease‑free survival compared with CH‑negative patients.
CH clones expand over time in patients who do not receive adjuvant therapy.
TET2 and DNMT3A mutant clones achieve larger relative tumor‑infiltrating clone sizes than other CH mutations.
Single‑cell analyses reveal TET2‑mutant cells enriched in myeloid lineages and NK cells, while TICH tumors show increased γδ T cells, cycling T cells, and interferon response genes.
open_questions: Do CH clones acquire additional driver mutations during disease progression?
What therapeutic strategies can specifically target TICH‑associated immune remodeling?

talk_title: TFF2 deficiency amplifies IL‑1β inflammation and drives aging‑associated gastric tumor progression

targets.dependency_context: MDSC‑derived cytokine in aged gastric tumors

biomarkers.name: PMN‑MDSC frequency
biomarkers.finding: Increased in aged stomach and blood; reduced after TFF2‑MSA treatment

biomarkers.name: CD8+ T cell proportion
biomarkers.finding: Decreased in aged mice; partially restored by TFF2‑MSA

models.name: TFF2 heterozygous/knockout mice
models.genotype_or_key_features: TFF2+/‑ and TFF2‑/‑, young (3 mo) vs aged (18 mo)

models.name: ACKP orthotopic gastric tumor model
models.genotype_or_key_features: Implanted into young vs aged mice

models.name: MNU‑induced gastric cancer model
models.genotype_or_key_features: Carcinogen‑induced tumors in young vs aged mice

MOA_summary: Aging reduces gastric TFF2‑expressing epithelial cells, leading to expansion of PMN‑MDSCs that secrete IL‑1β, which activates IL‑1R1‑positive CAFs and creates an immunosuppressive, tumor‑promoting niche; systemic administration of a stabilized TFF2‑MSA fusion reverses MDSC expansion, restores CD8⁺ T cells, and suppresses tumor growth in aged mice.
key_findings: Aged mouse stomachs show a marked loss of TFF2⁺ epithelial cells.
Aging drives systemic inflammaging with increased PMN‑MDSCs, bone‑marrow myeloid bias, and elevated IL‑6/TNF‑α.
TFF2‑deficient mice recapitulate the aged immune phenotype (MDSC expansion, CD8⁺ T cell reduction).
TFF2‑MSA treatment reduces MDSC accumulation, restores CD8⁺ T cells, and normalizes bone‑marrow myelopoiesis.
In both ACKP orthotopic and MNU‑induced gastric cancer models, aged mice develop larger tumors and shorter survival, which are mitigated by TFF2‑MSA pretreatment.

combination_strategies.rationale: TFF2‑MSA synergizes with checkpoint inhibition in prior studies
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced tumor suppression in aged mice

open_questions: Does TFF2‑MSA affect other stromal compartments beyond CAFs?
Can TFF2‑MSA improve responses to standard chemotherapy in aged gastric cancer models?

talk_title: Aging‑mediated reactivation of metastatic melanoma via γδ T‑cell dysfunction

speaker.name: Kelly Coutant
speaker.affiliation: University of California, Postdoctoral Fellow in Mitchison Lab

biomarkers.name: γδ T‑cell frequency
biomarkers.finding: Decreased in middle‑aged mice; associated with increased liver metastasis

biomarkers.name: PD‑1 on γδ T cells
biomarkers.finding: Increased in aged liver metastases

models.name: C57BL/6 middle‑age (12‑mo) and geriatric (24‑mo) mice
models.genotype_or_key_features: Tail‑vein or hydrodynamic tail‑vein injection of melanoma cells

models.name: γδ T‑cell depletion model (DTR‑expressing γδ T cells + diphtheria toxin)

MOA_summary: Aging creates a liver microenvironment that reduces functional γδ T cells, increases POSN1 expression in melanoma cells, and expands PMN‑MDSCs, collectively driving immunosuppressive γδ T‑cell phenotypes (PD‑1⁺, Vγ1⁺) and promoting metastatic outgrowth; depletion of γδ T cells further exacerbates metastasis, confirming their protective role in young hosts.
key_findings: Middle‑aged mice develop significantly more liver metastases than young or geriatric mice.
γδ T‑cell numbers drop specifically in middle‑aged livers, correlating with metastasis burden.
Depleting γδ T cells reduces primary tumor growth but increases liver metastasis, indicating site‑specific roles.
POSN1 overexpression in melanoma cells enhances both lung and liver metastasis and expands PMN‑MDSCs while decreasing γδ T‑cell infiltration.
Aged livers show increased arginase‑1 expression and MDSC accumulation, suggesting metabolic suppression of γδ T cells.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Aged γδ T cells display higher PD‑1 and Vγ1 (protumor) and lower Vγ6 (antitumor) expression.

combination_strategies.rationale: Restore functional γδ T cells in aged hosts to limit metastasis
combination_strategies.phenotype: Proposed therapeutic approach

open_questions: What upstream signals drive POSN1 upregulation in aged melanoma cells?
Can senolytic targeting of MDSCs restore γδ T‑cell function in aged livers?

cited_papers: Mitchison et al. 2023 Nat Immunol (POSN1 study)

talk_title: Age‑stratified therapeutic strategies targeting chemo‑resistant subclones in triple‑negative breast cancer

speaker.name: M Spacek
speaker.affiliation: University of XYZ, Department of Oncology

biomarkers.name: Barcode 25 clone frequency
biomarkers.finding: Enriches after paclitaxel treatment in both young and aged mice

biomarkers.name: PD‑L1 expression
biomarkers.finding: Increased in tumors containing chemo‑resistant clone

models.name: FVB mice (young 8‑10 wk, aged 14 mo) with Met1 TNBC tumors
Met1 TNBC cells with 31 DNA‑barcoded subclones
models.genotype_or_key_features: Unique barcode per clone; barcode 25 chemo‑resistant

MOA_summary: A DNA‑barcode tracking system identifies a minor subpopulation (barcode 25) that survives paclitaxel, drives PD‑L1 up‑regulation, interferon‑γ signaling, EMT, and immunosuppression; pan‑HDAC inhibition selectively targets this clone, restoring chemosensitivity and enhancing anti‑PD‑L1 efficacy, especially in aged mice.
key_findings: Aged mice respond less to paclitaxel and gain no additional benefit from anti‑PD‑L1 combination.
Only one barcode (25) expands after paclitaxel, conferring resistance in vitro and in vivo.
Barcode 25 tumors exhibit higher PD‑L1, CXCL10, vimentin, and EBI3 expression.
Pan‑HDAC inhibitor panobinostat is the top hit in a drug screen against barcode 25 cells.
Combining panobinostat with anti‑PD‑L1 markedly improves survival in aged mice, and triple therapy (paclitaxel + panobinostat + anti‑PD‑L1) achieves near‑complete control in young mice.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Chemo‑resistant clone constitutes <5% of tumor yet drives immunosuppressive phenotype.

combination_strategies.rationale: HDAC inhibition up‑regulates immune visibility of resistant clone
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor control in aged mice

combination_strategies.rationale: Standard of care chemotherapy; triple combo yields near‑complete response in young mice
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced tumor regression

open_questions: Will selective HDAC inhibitors retain efficacy with reduced toxicity in older patients?
What is the molecular mechanism by which HDAC inhibition overcomes barcode 25 resistance?

cited_papers: Spacek et al. 2024 Cancer Cell (DNA barcode platform)

talk_title: Age‑dependent neuroimmune changes in glioblastoma reveal senescent microglia as a therapeutic target

targets.dependency_context: Age‑related microglial senescence in GBM peritumoral brain

biomarkers.name: β‑galactosidase+ microglia
biomarkers.finding: Increased in extracranial brain of aged GBM mice; reduced by TQ senolytic

biomarkers.name: senescence score (gene signature)
biomarkers.finding: Higher in peritumoral region of older GBM patients, especially in microglia

models.name: C57BL/6 young (2 mo) vs old (24 mo) intracranial SB28 GBM model
Aged C57BL/6 immune‑depleted mice with GBM43 PDX
models.genotype_or_key_features: Human GBM PDX in aged immunocompromised host

MOA_summary: Aging increases senescent microglia in the peritumoral brain, creating a pro‑inflammatory niche that limits the efficacy of radiation plus immunotherapy; senolytic treatment (dasatinib + quercetin) selectively clears these senescent microglia, enhancing survival in aged GBM models when combined with standard radiotherapy and immunotherapy.
key_findings: Patient glioblastoma cores show no age‑related transcriptional differences, but peritumoral tissue in older patients is enriched for senescent microglia.
Aged mice exhibit higher β‑galactosidase+ microglia in extracranial brain, not within the tumor core.
Senolytic TQ reduces senescent microglia in aged brains and improves survival when combined with radiation + anti‑PD‑1, but not in young mice.
In aged humanized GBM PDX mice, adding TQ to standard radiation + temozolomide further extends survival for a subset of animals.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Senolytic effect observed only in extracranial brain, not within tumor core, suggesting limited drug penetration or lower senescent cell burden inside tumor.

combination_strategies.rationale: Standard of care induces tumor cell death; senolytic removes age‑associated immunosuppressive microglia
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved overall survival in aged mice

open_questions: Why does the senolytic fail to clear senescent cells within the tumor core?
Does radiation itself increase senescence in the peritumoral brain, thereby creating a therapeutic window for senolytics?

talk_title: Multimodal and Agentic AI in Biomarker Discovery

speaker.name: Jakob Kata
speaker.affiliation: University of Dresden and University of Heidelberg, Germany

MOA_summary: Kata described the evolution from classical machine learning to deep learning and foundation models for cancer biomarker development, defining three classes of AI‑based biomarkers (quantification, indirect inference, and outcome prediction) and introduced AI agents that can autonomously integrate multimodal clinical data to generate diagnostic and therapeutic recommendations.
key_findings: Class A AI biomarkers replicate human scoring (e.g., PD‑L1 IHC) with higher reproducibility.
Class B AI biomarkers infer genetic alterations (e.g., MSI, EGFR mutations) from pathology images for pre‑screening.
Class C AI biomarkers predict treatment outcomes directly from multimodal data, enabling new clinical decision tools.

talk_title: AI‑Enabled Clinical Stage Programs at Recursion

speaker.name: Tirup Behanie
speaker.affiliation: Recursion Pharmaceuticals, VP of Translational Strategy

targets.dependency_context: ovarian cancer with high CDK7 expression

biomarkers.name: MSI‑high status
biomarkers.finding: MSI‑high ovarian models showed dose‑dependent RBM39 degradation and greater sensitivity to REC‑1245

biomarkers.name: CDK7 expression
biomarkers.finding: High CDK7 expression correlated with poorer overall survival in thousands of Tempus patient records

biomarkers.name: Polyp burden change
biomarkers.finding: 75% of FAP patients responded with median 43% reduction after 12 weeks of REC‑4881; 82% maintained response (53% reduction) 12 weeks off therapy

models.name: DAHLIA (REC‑1245) Phase 1/2
models.genotype_or_key_features: Patients with MSI‑high or high genomic instability tumors

models.name: ELUCIDATE (REC‑617) Phase 1/2
models.genotype_or_key_features: Advanced solid tumors; ovarian cancer cohort

clinical_data.population: FAP patients treated 12 weeks with REC‑4881

clinical_data.n: n=1 ovarian cancer patient in ELUCIDATE

MOA_summary: Recursion leveraged AI‑driven phenomaps to identify RBM39 as a functional analog of CDK12, enabling rapid degrader development (REC‑1245) that exploits splicing defects in MSI‑high, genomically unstable tumors; AI‑guided design produced a CDK7 inhibitor (REC‑617) optimized for ovarian cancer vulnerability; and AI‑derived repurposing identified a MEK1/2 inhibitor (REC‑4881) that reduces polyp burden in APC‑loss FAP patients.
key_findings: RBM39 degradation by REC‑1245 showed dose‑dependent activity in MSI‑high ovarian models and correlated with genomic instability across multiple preclinical models.
REC‑1245 entered the DAHLIA Phase 1/2 trial targeting MSI‑high and other high‑instability tumor types, with dose escalation ongoing.

REC‑617 demonstrated in vitro ovarian cell line growth inhibition and in vivo tumor regressions; causal modeling of >10,000 Tempus records linked high CDK7 expression to poor survival, guiding ovarian cancer selection.
A partial response was observed in an ovarian cancer patient treated with REC‑617 in the ELUCIDATE trial.
REC‑4881 achieved a 75% response rate and 43% median polyp burden reduction after 12 weeks in FAP patients, with 82% maintaining response (53% reduction) after a drug‑free interval.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: RBM39 degraders may be more selective than CDK12 inhibitors that also hit CDK13, potentially improving therapeutic window.

combination_strategies.rationale: to enhance efficacy in ovarian cancer beyond monotherapy
combination_strategies.phenotype: planned combination cohorts in ELUCIDATE

open_questions: What biomarker thresholds (e.g., MSI‑high vs. quantitative genomic instability scores) best predict response to RBM39 degradation?
Will CDK7 expression become a prospective selection marker for REC‑617 based on larger patient datasets?

talk_title: AI Transformation of Pharmaceutical R&D at AstraZeneca

speaker.name: George Reis-Filho
speaker.affiliation: AstraZeneca, Chief AI and Science Innovation

biomarkers.name: Normalized membrane ratio (NMR)
biomarkers.assay: quantitative continuous scoring on pathology images
biomarkers.finding: NMR correlates with Trop2 internalization and predicts response to Trop2‑targeted ADCs in NSCLC

MOA_summary: Reis‑Filho outlined AstraZeneca's strategy to fine‑tune large multimodal foundation models with proprietary oncology data, enabling AI‑driven patient selection (e.g., Trop2 NMR) and accelerating ADC development, while highlighting the shift from handcrafted annotation to foundation‑model‑based quantification that reduced annotation effort from 40,000 to <5 hours.
key_findings: A quantitative continuous scoring system can measure subcellular Trop2 expression and derive a normalized membrane ratio that serves as a surrogate for ADC internalization.
Development of the Trop2 ADC biomarker required ~40,000 pathologist annotation hours for one indication; foundation models can reduce this to <5 hours or eliminate it.
AstraZeneca is building multimodal foundation models through partnerships (Tempus, Pathos, Model AI) to improve patient selection across diverse therapeutic classes.
open_questions: How can multimodal foundation models integrate time‑dimension data to improve longitudinal patient selection?
What validation frameworks are needed to satisfy regulators for AI‑derived biomarkers with limited explainability?

talk_title: CODA: 3D Mapping of Tumors at Single‑Cell Resolution Across Organs and Species
MOA_summary: CODA reconstructs large tissue volumes (multi‑centimeter cubes) at sub‑micron resolution by iteratively registering serial H&E sections, segmenting tissue components with a CNN, and deconvolving cellular morphology, enabling 3D mapping of individual cells, vessels, nerves, and precancerous lesions across organs and species.
key_findings: Reconstructed a 3‑centimeter‑cube of human pancreas at sub‑micron resolution, positioning ~1 billion cells with >97% segmentation accuracy.
Identified 100–500 microscopic precursor lesions per healthy pancreas sample; disease‑adjacent pancreas contained up to 1 000 lesions.
Laser‑capture sequencing of individual lesions revealed distinct point‑mutation profiles, demonstrating multifocality in precancerous lesions.
Mapped entire fallopian tubes from seven healthy women and found STIC‑like lesions in all, challenging the prevailing belief that such lesions are rare in normal tissue.
Demonstrated that blood vessels and nerves follow a scale‑free fractal geometry across species (human, mouse, rhesus macaque) and developmental stages.
Developed generative AI tools (InterpolAI) to interpolate missing histology sections, achieving Pearson correlation >0.99 with ground‑truth images.
open_questions: What is the smallest length scale at which CODA provides biologically meaningful information for different research questions?
Can 3D snapshots of T‑cell spatial organization be used to predict tumor clearance or response to immunotherapy?

talk_title: AI‑Enabled Clinical Genomics Workflows for Precision Oncology

clinical_data.population: all solid tumors and hematologic malignancies

MOA_summary: AI methods are integrated into the MSK‑IMPACT clinical genomics pipeline to improve variant detection (deep learning callers, error‑modeling), functional interpretation (deep neural networks, AlphaMisSense, SpliceAI), and clinical decision support (LLMs with retrieval‑augmented generation, AI‑driven trial matching), thereby increasing sensitivity, reducing turnaround time, and expanding actionable insights across cancer types.
readouts: LLM‑based knowledge‑base querying (OncoKB, Molecular Oncology Almanac)
key_findings: Sequenced >100,000 patients using the MSK‑IMPACT 500‑gene tumor‑normal panel, generating a dataset that enables AI‑driven analyses.
Deep learning variant callers (e.g., DeepVariant) achieve higher sensitivity in difficult genomic regions compared with traditional callers.
AI‑based MSI detection (myMSI) reduced indeterminate cases relative to MSI‑Sensor, improving diagnostic confidence.
AlphaMisSense and SpliceAI improve pathogenicity prediction for missense and splice variants by incorporating protein structure and splicing models.
A neural network trained on 40,000 MSK‑IMPACT profiles predicts cancer type with ~80% accuracy overall and >90% for high‑confidence predictions.
LLM‑augmented tumor board agents (GPT‑4‑based) doubled decision‑support accuracy compared with GPT‑4 alone in a prospective evaluation.
open_questions: How can AI models be validated and regulated to ensure safety and equity across diverse patient populations?
What are the best practices for integrating AI‑generated confidence intervals into clinician decision‑making?

talk_title: AI-assisted mammography screening improves hard patient outcomes – evidence from randomized trials
MOA_summary: Deep learning models applied to digital mammograms generate cancer detection scores that are integrated into radiology workflow, leading to higher detection of malignancies and improved downstream patient outcomes compared with standard radiologist-only interpretation.
key_findings: European randomized controlled trials of AI-assisted mammography screening demonstrated a statistically significant increase in cancer detection rates versus standard screening.
AI integration did not increase false-positive recall rates, maintaining comparable specificity to radiologist-only reads.
The trials showed downstream improvement in patient outcomes, including earlier-stage diagnosis and reduced mortality.

talk_title: Agentic AI for accelerating biomedical discovery
MOA_summary: Bi Omni is an open‑source AI agent that integrates a curated environment of >100 biomedical tools, databases, and wet‑lab robots, enabling it to plan, execute, and iteratively refine experimental protocols, data analyses, and hypothesis generation, thereby automating and accelerating the scientific discovery loop.
key_findings: Bi Omni performed a full plasmid cloning design in ~10 minutes, producing a protocol that was experimentally validated as correct.
The agent completed a 30‑human multimodal data analysis (458 Excel files) in ~35 minutes, a task estimated to take weeks by a post‑doc.

In internal benchmarks across variant prioritization, GWAS gene discovery, perturbation screen design, scRNA‑seq analysis, drug repurposing, microbiome taxa analysis, and rare‑disease diagnosis, Bi Omni achieved accuracy comparable to or exceeding specialist tools despite no task‑specific engineering.
Over a three‑month public beta, >10,000 users executed ~70,000 automated research tasks, demonstrating broad utility across academia, pharma, and clinical settings.

talk_title: Foundation and virtual cell models for single‑cell biology

models.name: XCell Ultra
models.genotype_or_key_features: 5 billion parameters, trained on Pisces dataset (≈26 M perturbed scRNA‑seq cells, 16 contexts)

models.name: BioReason Pro
models.genotype_or_key_features: 4 billion‑parameter language model fine‑tuned on protein GO reasoning data

MOA_summary: XCell extends the first‑generation scGBT foundation model by training a diffusion‑based language model on massive, diverse perturbation scRNA‑seq datasets (Pisces) to learn causal gene‑expression responses, while BioReason Pro aligns DNA‑ or protein‑sequence foundation models with large‑language‑model reasoning to predict pathway or GO‑term functions, using reinforcement learning and multimodal tokenization.
readouts: benchmark accuracy on perturbation prediction tasks
key_findings: Training on 26 M perturbed cells across 16 biological contexts enabled XCell to recover known protein‑protein interaction networks and improve causal inference over descriptive models.
Scaling XCell from 50 M to 5 B parameters yielded a monotonic decrease in training loss and increase in test‑set accuracy, establishing a clear scaling law for virtual‑cell models.
BioReason Pro achieved >80 % of predictions judged superior to human expert annotations on 200 novel proteins, outperforming baseline methods across GO‑term categories.
Both XCell and BioReason Pro were released open‑source; within two weeks, tens of thousands of users accessed the models, indicating strong community uptake.
open_questions: How will virtual‑cell models generalize to patient‑level organoid or in‑vivo perturbation data with limited training examples?
Can DNA‑sequence reasoning models be extended to predict drug‑response phenotypes directly from genomic variants?

talk_title: Real‑time AI augmentation for sepsis and palliative‑care in the clinic

speaker.name: Suchi Saria
speaker.affiliation: Johns Hopkins University; Founder & CEO, Bayesian Health

models.name: Sepsis detection AI (Cleveland Clinic deployment)
models.genotype_or_key_features: mixture‑of‑experts transformer on multimodal EMR data

models.name: Palliative‑care early‑alert AI (Mayo Clinic deployment)
models.genotype_or_key_features: same architecture applied to oncology‑related admissions

clinical_data.value: significant decrease (p<0.01) in in‑hospital mortality for sepsis cohort
≈15% relative reduction in oncology palliative‑care cohort
clinical_data.population: oncology patients eligible for palliative care

MOA_summary: A real‑time multimodal transformer model ingests continuous EMR streams (labs, vitals, notes, meds) and outputs patient‑specific risk scores; integrated as an EHR‑embedded modular agent, it surfaces transparent alerts, auto‑generates order‑sets and documentation, and feeds back clinician responses for continuous monitoring and drift detection.
key_findings: AI‑augmented sepsis detection achieved 80%‑90% sensitivity while reducing false‑positive alerts by ~10‑fold compared with legacy rule‑based systems.
Median time‑to‑treatment was advanced by 3.5 hours, leading to a statistically significant reduction in in‑hospital mortality (p<0.01).
Clinician adoption of AI alerts reached 80%‑90% across four health systems, demonstrating high workflow integration.
In oncology‑focused palliative‑care deployment, early alerts increased appropriate consults and cut 30‑day readmissions by ~15%, also lowering total cost of care.
A governance layer continuously monitors model drift, enabling rapid root‑cause analysis and model re‑training without interrupting care.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Initial rule‑based alerts suffered from high nuisance‑alert rates (≈20:1 false‑positive to true‑positive), which the AI system reduced to ~3:1.

combination_strategies.rationale: Embed risk scores directly into clinician order‑sets to reduce friction and improve adoption
combination_strategies.phenotype: higher alert acknowledgment and faster treatment initiation

open_questions: How does model performance vary across demographic sub‑populations and rare disease cohorts?
What are the optimal strategies for continual learning while preserving patient safety?

talk_title: Foundation models for pathology and multimodal patient representation

speaker.name: Faisal Mahmood
speaker.affiliation: Harvard Medical School, Department of Pathology

models.name: Titan
models.genotype_or_key_features: whole‑slide representation model trained with iBOT, contrastive image‑text, 1‑B+ parameters

models.name: Threads
models.genotype_or_key_features: multimodal contrastive model aligning H&E with molecular (IHC, transcriptomics) data

models.name: Virtual Patient Representation Model
models.genotype_or_key_features: trained on 7.2 M patients, 18 modalities, dynamic embedding updated over time

MOA_summary: Self‑supervised foundation models (e.g., Titan) learn dense embeddings of whole‑slide pathology images; multimodal contrastive training (Threads) aligns these embeddings with molecular assays, while a large‑scale multimodal patient encoder integrates structured and unstructured clinical data into a single dynamic vector for downstream prediction of diagnosis, prognosis, and treatment response.
key_findings: Titan achieved state‑of‑the‑art performance on slide‑level classification tasks while using a single vector per slide, outperforming prior region‑based models.
Contrastive training with IHC and transcriptomic data (Threads) improved few‑shot classification accuracy by >20 % on external cancer cohorts.
The 7.2 M‑patient multimodal encoder clustered known disease ontologies without supervision, demonstrating emergent medical knowledge.
Similarity search using the patient embeddings retrieved clinically relevant matches for rare‑cancer cases and potential trial candidates.
open_questions: How will dynamic patient embeddings handle longitudinal data drift and evolving clinical practice?
Can the virtual patient model be prospectively validated for trial matching in real time?

talk_title: Phase 2 neoadjuvant trastuzumab tucatinib plus chemotherapy in HER2‑amplified locally advanced rectal cancer

speaker.name: Mike Foote
speaker.affiliation: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, GI Oncology

targets.dependency_context: HER2‑amplified KRAS wild‑type locally advanced rectal cancer
HER2‑amplified KRAS wild‑type locally advanced rectal cancer

biomarkers.name: HER2 IHC 3+
biomarkers.finding: All patients achieving clinical complete response had HER2 IHC 3+ (100% vs 10% in non‑responders)

biomarkers.name: ctDNA clearance
biomarkers.finding: Patients with ctDNA clearance after neoadjuvant therapy had higher cCR rates (70% vs 0%)

models.name: Phase 2 HER2‑targeted neoadjuvant rectal cancer trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: HER2‑amplified, KRAS wild‑type, MMR proficient, locally advanced rectal adenocarcinoma (n=10 enrolled, 9 evaluable)

clinical_data.metric: overall response rate (ORR) after HER2‑only lead‑in

MOA_summary: Dual HER2 blockade with trastuzumab (antibody) and tucatinib (small‑molecule kinase inhibitor) inhibits HER2‑driven signaling in HER2‑amplified KRAS‑wild‑type rectal tumors, leading to high response rates and a 50% clinical complete response, enabling organ preservation in a subset of patients.
key_findings: HER2‑amplified locally advanced rectal cancer patients achieved a 78% ORR during a 6‑week HER2‑only lead‑in.
50% of patients attained a clinical complete response after combined HER2 blockade and chemotherapy, allowing non‑operative management.
HER2 IHC 3+ staining predicted clinical complete response, whereas lower IHC scores correlated with residual disease.
ctDNA dynamics tracked response and early recurrence; persistent ctDNA after neoadjuvant therapy identified patients at high risk of relapse.
No grade ≥3 toxicities were observed, confirming safety of the HER2‑targeted neoadjuvant regimen.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Patients with KRAS mutations were excluded as they confer resistance to HER2‑targeted therapy.
Three patients with initial cCR later recurred locally, all retaining HER2 positivity, suggesting possible need for prolonged HER2 inhibition.

combination_strategies.rationale: Add cytotoxic backbone to enhance tumor shrinkage and eradicate HER2‑negative clones.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Increased cCR rate to 50% versus historical 20% with chemo alone

combination_strategies.rationale: Consolidate local control for patients with residual disease after HER2‑targeted therapy.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved local control; 63% organ preservation at 2.5 years

open_questions: Can HER2‑targeted neoadjuvant therapy replace chemotherapy entirely in HER2‑amplified rectal cancer?
What is the optimal duration of HER2 blockade to prevent late local recurrences?
Would other HER2 agents (e.g., ADCs) further improve cCR and organ preservation rates?

talk_title: Phase 2 neoadjuvant zenodatumab in early‑stage HER2‑positive breast cancer (NEO3NHER)

biomarkers.name: HER2 IHC 3+
biomarkers.finding: All patients achieving pCR were HER2 IHC 3+ (100% vs 0% in IHC 2+)

biomarkers.name: ERBB2 amplification
biomarkers.finding: All four pCR patients harbored ERBB2 amplification

models.name: NEO3NHER phase 2 neoadjuvant study
models.genotype_or_key_features: HER2‑positive (IHC 3+ or 2+ with amplification), ER‑positive 80%, n=20

MOA_summary: Zenodatumab is a biparatopic HER2 antibody that simultaneously engages the trastuzumab and pertuzumab epitopes, enhancing HER2 clustering, blocking downstream signaling, and mediating ADCC, resulting in a 30% pCR rate in HER2‑positive early breast cancer without chemotherapy.
key_findings: Zenodatumab monotherapy achieved a 30% pCR rate in HER2‑positive early breast cancer.
An additional 20% of patients achieved RCB‑1, indicating near‑complete responses.
All pCRs occurred in HER2 IHC 3+ tumors; HER2 IHC 2+ patients did not achieve pCR.
No grade ≥3 treatment‑related adverse events were observed, confirming a favorable safety profile.
Higher HER2 RNA expression and PAM50 HER2‑enriched subtype correlated with response, though not statistically significant.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Patients with HER2 IHC 2+ showed lower response rates, suggesting a threshold effect for zenodatumab efficacy.

combination_strategies.rationale: Standard of care for ER‑positive HER2‑positive disease; may synergize with HER2 blockade.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Allowed per investigator discretion; no safety signal observed

open_questions: Will extending zenodatumab duration beyond 5 months increase pCR rates?
Can zenodatumab replace chemotherapy in node‑positive HER2‑positive disease?
What is the predictive value of HER2‑DX or other quantitative HER2 assays for patient selection?

talk_title: Phase III perioperative atezolizumab plus chemotherapy in resectable stage II/III NSCLC (interim EFS and MRD analysis)

biomarkers.name: ctDNA clearance (pre‑surgery)
biomarkers.finding: 75% clearance in atezolizumab arm vs 46% in placebo; clearance associated with higher PCR (70% vs 18%)

biomarkers.name: PD‑L1 expression
biomarkers.finding: Stratification factor; not directly linked to efficacy in interim analysis

models.name: Phase III atezolizumab + chemo vs placebo + chemo
models.genotype_or_key_features: Stage II/III NSCLC, EGFR/ALK wild‑type, PD‑L1 assessed, n=501

MOA_summary: Atezolizumab blocks PD‑L1, restoring anti‑tumor T‑cell activity when combined with standard chemotherapy in the peri‑operative setting, leading to improved EFS, higher MPR/pCR rates, and ctDNA clearance that predicts favorable outcomes.
key_findings: Atezolizumab plus chemotherapy improved 2‑year EFS to 74.8% versus 55.5% with placebo (HR 0.45).
MPR increased to 53.8% and pCR to 31.1% in the atezolizumab arm, far exceeding placebo.
Pre‑surgery ctDNA clearance occurred in 75% of atezolizumab patients versus 46% of placebo and correlated with higher PCR rates.
Grade ≥3 immune‑related adverse events were low (4.4%) and manageable.
ctDNA clearance was prognostic for EFS regardless of treatment arm.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Patients with baseline ctDNA positivity who failed to clear ctDNA had markedly lower PCR and higher MRD rates.

combination_strategies.rationale: Chemotherapy provides cytoreduction and may increase neoantigen release, synergizing with PD‑L1 blockade.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved EFS, MPR, and pCR compared with chemo alone

open_questions: Will ctDNA‑guided adjuvant therapy escalation improve long‑term survival?
What is the optimal duration of atezolizumab after surgery?

talk_title: Post‑neoadjuvant ctDNA predicts non‑pCR and distant recurrence in early triple‑negative breast cancer (NSABP B‑59/GEPRDUOS substudy)

biomarkers.name: ctDNA positivity post‑neoadjuvant
biomarkers.finding: 97.9% specificity for non‑pCR; HR 10.2 for distant recurrence (p=0.0001)

models.name: NSABP B‑59 / GEPRDUOS ctDNA substudy
models.genotype_or_key_features: 212 patients total; 155 with post‑neoadjuvant ctDNA data, early TNBC, randomized atezolizumab vs placebo

clinical_data.metric: hazard ratio for distant recurrence (ctDNA‑positive vs negative)

MOA_summary: Tumor‑informed ctDNA sequencing detects residual molecular disease after neoadjuvant therapy; persistent ctDNA strongly predicts lack of pathological complete response and a >10‑fold increased risk of distant recurrence in early triple‑negative breast cancer.
key_findings: Only 9% of patients remained ctDNA‑positive after neoadjuvant therapy.
ctDNA‑negative patients had an 85% pCR rate versus 2% in ctDNA‑positive patients.
Persistent ctDNA conferred a hazard ratio of 10.2 for distant recurrence (p=0.0001).
ctDNA clearance showed 97.9% specificity for predicting non‑pCR.
open_questions: Can ctDNA‑guided escalation (e.g., additional immunotherapy) improve outcomes for ctDNA‑positive patients?
What is the optimal timing for ctDNA assessment to inform adjuvant decisions?

cited_papers: Liu et al. 2024 J Clin Oncol

talk_title: Perioperative nivolumab: EFS and ctDNA dynamics in resectable NSCLC (CheckMate 770 exploratory analysis)

biomarkers.name: ctDNA clearance (pre‑surgery)
biomarkers.finding: 75% clearance in nivolumab arm vs 46% in placebo; clearance associated with 70% PCR vs 18% in placebo

biomarkers.name: KEAP1 mutation
biomarkers.finding: Nivolumab improved EFS in KEAP1‑mutant patients (HR 0.6) compared with placebo

models.name: CheckMate 770 phase 3 perioperative nivolumab vs placebo
models.genotype_or_key_features: Stage II/III NSCLC, EGFR/ALK wild‑type, 41% biomarker evaluable (n≈190), median follow‑up 41 mo

clinical_data.metric: EFS hazard ratio in KEAP1‑mutant subgroup

MOA_summary: Nivolumab blocks PD‑1, reactivating anti‑tumor T‑cell responses; combined with chemotherapy in the peri‑operative setting it improves event‑free survival, especially in patients achieving ctDNA clearance and in tumors harboring KEAP1, SDK11, CDKN2A, or SMARCA4 alterations.
key_findings: Perioperative nivolumab improved EFS (HR 0.45) and increased 2‑year EFS to 74.8% versus 55.5% with placebo.
ctDNA clearance before surgery occurred in 75% of nivolumab patients versus 46% of placebo and correlated with higher PCR rates.
Nivolumab benefit persisted across genomic subgroups; patients with KEAP1, SDK11, CDKN2A, or SMARCA4 alterations derived greater relative benefit versus placebo.
Machine‑learning analysis identified ctDNA clearance, non‑N2 nodal status, PCR, squamous histology, and nivolumab treatment as top predictors of prolonged EFS.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Patients with persistent ctDNA after neoadjuvant therapy had markedly lower PCR and higher MRD rates, indicating residual disease.

combination_strategies.rationale: Chemotherapy may increase tumor antigen release, synergizing with PD‑1 blockade.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved EFS and pCR compared with chemo alone

open_questions: How should ctDNA‑positive, non‑clearing patients be managed post‑surgery?
Can integrating additional biomarkers (e.g., TMB) refine patient selection for peri‑operative nivolumab?

talk_title: Feasibility and efficacy of perioperative nivolumab ± relatlimab in potentially resectable HCC

speaker.name: Howard Lee
speaker.affiliation: Johns Hopkins University / Ohio State University

biomarkers.name: AFP dynamics
biomarkers.finding: Patients with decreasing AFP pre‑surgery had no recurrences to date

models.name: Phase II perioperative nivolumab ± relatlimab in HCC
models.genotype_or_key_features: 15 patients per arm, BCLC A‑C, diverse etiologies, median follow‑up 26.3 mo

MOA_summary: Nivolumab blocks PD‑1 while relatlimab blocks LAG‑3, together releasing T‑cell inhibition in the liver microenvironment; this dual checkpoint inhibition yields modest pathologic responses and favorable safety in resectable HCC.
key_findings: Both arms met feasibility criteria with no surgery‑delaying toxicities.
Pathologic or major response rates were 20% with nivolumab alone and 26.7% with the combination.
One‑year DFS was 73.3% (nivolumab) versus 100% (combo); two‑year OS favored the combination (84.8% vs 68.5%).
Grade 3+ immune‑related adverse events occurred in 46.7% (nivolumab) and 73.3% (combo), consistent with known safety profiles.
Decreasing AFP during neoadjuvant therapy correlated with absence of recurrence.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: LAG‑3 receptor occupancy was lower in tumor‑infiltrating lymphocytes than peripheral blood, suggesting limited tumor penetration at the tested dose.

combination_strategies.rationale: Dual checkpoint blockade may overcome adaptive immune resistance mediated by FGL1‑LAG‑3 in the liver.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Higher pathologic response and DFS compared with nivolumab alone

open_questions: Would higher or intra‑arterial dosing of relatlimab improve tumor penetration and efficacy?
Can ctDNA or other molecular markers further stratify patients who benefit from dual checkpoint blockade?

talk_title: Single-cell genome sequencing reveals low-frequency copy number alterations in normal breast epithelium

models.name: normal breast epithelial cells
models.genotype_or_key_features: BRCA1/2 heterozygous and wild-type, luminal vs basal lineages

MOA_summary: Using shallow single-cell genome sequencing, the study identified rare aneuploid cells in normal breast epithelium, predominantly luminal, with recurrent 1q gains and 10q/16q losses that mirror early breast cancer copy-number patterns, suggesting these events arise early and may represent founder lesions.
key_findings: Detected <1% of normal breast epithelial cells harboring copy-number gains (1q) or losses (10q, 16q) across BRCA1/2 carriers and wild‑type donors.
Aneuploid events were lineage‑restricted, occurring mainly in luminal cells and rarely in basal myoepithelium.
BRCA1/2 heterozygous samples showed higher frequency of high‑ploidy cells compared with wild‑type.
Spatial analysis of 537 reduction mammoplasties (3 million cells) revealed age‑correlated decline in cellularity and increased inflammatory macrophages and adipogenic fibroblast signatures.
open_questions: What functional advantage does 1q gain confer in normal luminal cells?
Do aneuploid cells influence local immune surveillance in aging breast tissue?

talk_title: Single-cell DNA and ATAC profiling of normal breast epithelium reveals lineage‑specific aneuploidy and its correlation with age

models.name: normal breast epithelial cells
models.genotype_or_key_features: 49 healthy women, ~83,000 cells, age 20‑80, luminal HR, luminal secretory, basal

models.name: nanowell dual‑index assay
models.genotype_or_key_features: simultaneous DNA copy‑number and chromatin accessibility per cell

models.name: Visium/Xenium sections
models.genotype_or_key_features: inferred CNV from spots, mapping of aneuploid clones to ducts/TDLUs

MOA_summary: High‑throughput single‑cell DNA and ATAC profiling of normal breast tissue identified that ~3‑4% of epithelial cells are aneuploid, with age‑dependent expansion of clones bearing 1q gain, 10q loss, and 16q loss, predominantly in luminal lineages, and that these events spatially cluster in ducts/TDLUs without histologic abnormality.
key_findings: Across 49 donors (≈83 k cells) ~3.9% of epithelial cells were aneuploid; 1q gain, 10q loss, and 16q loss were the most frequent events.
Aneuploid frequency positively correlated with donor age; expanded clones (vs sporadic) showed the strongest age association.
Aneuploid cells were enriched in luminal hormone‑responsive and luminal secretory lineages; basal cells harbored few events, mainly X‑chromosome loss.
Spatial transcriptomics mapped 1q‑gain/10q‑loss clones to ductal/TDLU regions that appeared histologically normal.
ATAC data linked 1q gain to increased accessibility at MDM4 and NF2; 10q loss associated with PTEN and CDH1 regulatory changes.
open_questions: Do specific aneuploid clones predispose to ER‑positive vs ER‑negative breast cancer subtypes?
What are the functional consequences of 1q‑gain‑driven chromatin changes in luminal cells?

talk_title: Aneuploidy patterns as predictors of immune evasion and immunotherapy response across cancers

biomarkers.name: 9p loss
biomarkers.finding: Associated with decreased cytotoxic immune score and poorer survival on checkpoint inhibitors in HPV‑negative head‑and‑neck cancer

biomarkers.name: 1q gain
biomarkers.finding: Predicts reduced response to immune checkpoint inhibitors in MSI‑high endometrial cancer and melanoma, independent of TMB and PD‑L1

models.name: Caris Therapeutics real‑world dataset
models.genotype_or_key_features: ~300 endometrial cancer patients treated with ICB, ~1000 non‑ICB controls

models.name: mini‑mouse TCGA (PDAC)
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS/TP53 mutant PDAC lines injected into immunocompetent mice

clinical_data.population: HPV‑negative head‑and‑neck cancer patients on ICB
MSI‑high endometrial cancer treated with pembrolizumab

MOA_summary: Retrospective analyses across TCGA, CPTAC, and real‑world cohorts identified chromosome‑9p loss and chromosome‑1q gain as recurrent aneuploidies that correlate with reduced cytotoxic T‑cell signatures and innate immune cell abundance, respectively, and predict poorer outcomes to checkpoint blockade in multiple tumor types; functional CRISPR‑engineered 9p13 deletions confer resistance to antigen‑specific T‑cell killing in vitro.
key_findings: 9p loss is strongly associated with an immune‑cold microenvironment and inferior survival on ICB in HPV‑negative head‑and‑neck cancer.
1q gain correlates with reduced macrophage infiltration and predicts lack of response to ICB in MSI‑high endometrial cancer and melanoma, independent of TMB and PD‑L1.
Pan‑cancer regression controlling for overall aneuploidy identified 9p loss and 1q gain as top loci linked to immune evasion.
Isogenic deletion of a 15 Mb 9p13 region in oral cancer cells confers resistance to CAR‑T cell killing, implicating antigen‑processing genes in the region.
Mouse PDAC mini‑TCGA recapitulates human findings: 9p loss ↔ lower CD8⁺ T‑cells; 1q gain ↔ lower macrophage abundance.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: 9p loss appears to impair CD8⁺ T‑cell mediated cytotoxicity without affecting innate immune cells.
1q gain preferentially reduces anti‑tumor macrophage signatures rather than adaptive immunity.

combination_strategies.rationale: Patients with 9p loss or 1q gain may benefit from combination therapies that restore immune infiltration.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Potentially improved response rates when combined with agents that boost CD8⁺ T‑cells or macrophage activation

open_questions: Which specific genes within 9p13 mediate resistance to CD8⁺ T‑cell killing?
Can therapeutic reversal of 1q‑driven innate immune suppression sensitize tumors to ICB?
Will longitudinal monitoring of 9p/1q status predict emergence of resistance during therapy?

talk_title: Regulatory perspective on rare cancer drug development

speaker.name: Martha Donahue
speaker.affiliation: U.S. Food and Drug Administration, Oncology Center of Excellence

MOA_summary: The speaker outlined FDA regulatory incentives, evidentiary standards, and flexible trial design options to accelerate development of therapies for rare cancers, emphasizing that substantial evidence is required regardless of prevalence and that randomized designs are preferred when feasible.

talk_title: Barriers to clinical development of therapeutics for chronic myelomonocytic leukemia

targets.dependency_context: CMML patients with GM-CSF signaling activation

MOA_summary: The presenter highlighted the heterogeneous mutational landscape of CMML, identified convergent GM‑CSF signaling as a therapeutic vulnerability, and described early proof‑of‑concept single‑arm trials targeting JAK2 (ruxolitinib) and GM‑CSF (lenzilumab) that suggest disease‑modifying potential across genetic subsets.
key_findings: CMML incidence is comparable to other myeloproliferative neoplasms that have approved therapies, supporting a viable economic case for drug development.
Single‑arm studies of ruxolitinib (JAK2 inhibitor) and lenzilumab (anti‑GM‑CSF) demonstrated proof‑of‑concept activity in CMML despite small patient numbers.
Phase III randomized trials in Europe (84 patients per arm) completed accrual in 18 months, showing that adequately powered studies are feasible for rare hematologic malignancies.
open_questions: What is the optimal primary endpoint for heterogeneous diseases like CMML—overall survival, transfusion independence, or patient‑reported outcomes?

talk_title: Development of the first FDA‑approved treatment for KRAS‑mutant low‑grade serous ovarian cancer

speaker.name: Rachel Grisham
speaker.affiliation: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Gynecologic Medical Oncology

targets.dependency_context: KRAS‑mutant low‑grade serous ovarian cancer (~33% of cases)
Low‑grade serous ovarian cancer; synergizes with MAPK inhibition

biomarkers.name: KRAS mutation
biomarkers.finding: Patients with KRAS‑mutant disease had ORR 44% vs 31% overall and median PFS 22 months

models.name: NGOD‑OV60 GOG 3052 RAMP 201
models.genotype_or_key_features: Recurrent low‑grade serous ovarian cancer, ≥1 prior chemotherapy line, stratified by KRAS status

MOA_summary: Avutametinib, a first‑in‑class RAF/MEK clamp, inhibits MAPK signaling downstream of KRAS, while defactinib blocks focal adhesion kinase; together they produce synergistic tumor regression in KRAS‑mutant low‑grade serous ovarian cancer, achieving >40% response rates and durable disease control.
key_findings: Combination avutametinib + defactinib yielded an overall response rate of 31% in recurrent low‑grade serous ovarian cancer, exceeding the 16‑26% rates seen with prior single‑agent MEK inhibitors.
KRAS‑mutant patients experienced a higher ORR of 44% and a median progression‑free survival of 22 months, establishing KRAS mutation as a predictive biomarker.
The phase 2 RAMP 201 study demonstrated durable responses with a median duration of response >31 months, supporting accelerated approval.
Breakthrough therapy and orphan drug designations facilitated rapid FDA review, leading to accelerated approval within six months of NDA submission.
A seamless phase 2/3 design allowed rapid accrual (>100 patients) and immediate transition to the confirmatory RAMP 301 randomized trial.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Single‑agent avutametinib at a higher dose showed lower efficacy than the combination, indicating that FAK inhibition contributes essential synergistic activity.

combination_strategies.rationale: FAK inhibition synergizes with MAPK blockade to enhance tumor regression and mitigate toxicity of continuous MEK inhibition
combination_strategies.phenotype: Higher response rate (31% vs 16‑26% historical) with comparable safety

open_questions: Will the confirmatory RAMP 301 trial demonstrate overall survival benefit sufficient to expand approval to KRAS‑wild‑type patients?
Can the intermittent dosing schedule be optimized further to improve tolerability without compromising efficacy?

talk_title: Revolutionizing Phase I Trials Using the BOIN Framework and Backfill Implementation

speaker.name: Betty Anne Ash Murray
speaker.affiliation: MD Anderson Cancer Center, Department of Biostatistics

MOA_summary: Murray presented a Bayesian adaptive dose-finding framework (BOIN) with backfilling to optimize dose escalation, safety, and efficacy decisions in early-phase oncology trials, incorporating multidimensional toxicity scores, patient-reported outcomes, and flexible cohort sizes.
key_findings: Introduced a multidimensional toxicity scoring system ranging 0-1 to quantify cumulative adverse events across cycles.
Demonstrated that incorporating patient-reported CTCAE data alongside clinician-assessed AEs can inform dose recommendation.
Described the BOIN decision boundaries (lambda_e, lambda_d) for escalation/de-escalation and highlighted flexibility in cohort size.
Presented a backfill BOIN design (BFBOIN/BEBOIN) to allow safety‑cleared dose levels to be populated with additional patients, improving dose recommendation robustness.
open_questions: How to model genetic polymorphisms influencing toxicity slopes across cycles?
Best practices for integrating PRO CTCAE data without introducing bias in non‑randomized early-phase settings?

talk_title: Bayesian Optimal Phase 2 Design (BOP2) for Simultaneous Toxicity and Efficacy Monitoring
MOA_summary: The speaker introduced the Bayesian Optimal Phase 2 (BOP2) design, a model‑assisted approach that uses posterior probabilities to make real‑time stopping decisions for toxicity and futility in single‑arm and randomized trials, with flexible interim monitoring schedules.
key_findings: BOP2 provides decision tables pre‑tabulated for clinicians, eliminating on‑the‑fly calculations during the trial.
Demonstrated information‑dependent cutoffs that balance early stopping for unsafe or ineffective treatments while preserving power for promising agents.
Showed extensions to handle multiple efficacy endpoints, differing monitoring times for toxicity and efficacy, and incorporation of historical control data.
Implemented via the trialdesign.org web tool, generating protocol templates and operating characteristic simulations acceptable to regulatory reviewers.
open_questions: How to best incorporate predictive biomarkers into the BOP2 posterior probability calculations?
Strategies for adapting BOP2 decision boundaries in platform trials with shifting control arm response rates.

talk_title: 27-hydroxycholesterol links hypercholesterolemia to estrogen receptor-positive breast cancer and metastasis
MOA_summary: 27-hydroxycholesterol, a cholesterol metabolite, acts as a partial estrogen receptor agonist promoting ER+ breast tumor growth and, through selection pressure, generates 27HC‑resistant subclones that acquire high lipid uptake, ferroptosis resistance via GPX4 upregulation, and enhanced metastatic potential.
key_findings: 27HC binds ER as a partial agonist at physiologic concentrations and drives ER+ tumor growth in mice.
Genetic knockout of CYP27A1 reduces tumor growth and delays latency in MMTV-PyMT mice.
27HC‑resistant subclones exhibit dramatically increased tumorigenicity and metastasis across breast cancer and melanoma models.
Resistant cells display non‑selective lipid uptake and become highly resistant to GPX4 inhibition, linking ferroptosis resistance to metastasis.
GPX4 knockout abolishes the metastatic advantage of 27HC‑selected cells, confirming GPX4 dependence.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: 27HC‑selected cells develop resistance to GPX4 inhibitors and ferroptosis.

combination_strategies.rationale: lower LDL and 27HC levels
combination_strategies.phenotype: reduced LDL and 27HC in breast cancer patients after 8 weeks

combination_strategies.rationale: inhibit CYP27A1 activity
combination_strategies.phenotype: correlated decrease in 27HC with higher 25‑hydroxyvitamin D

open_questions: What are the specific transporters mediating increased lipid uptake in 27HC‑resistant cells?
Can targeting GPX4 be therapeutically safe in patients?

cited_papers: Brown and Goldstein studies on CYP27A1

talk_title: Epidemiology and shared genetics of atherosclerosis and cancer

models.name: 30 million claim dataset
models.genotype_or_key_features: large population, multiple cancer and CVD outcomes

MOA_summary: Large‑scale claim analyses reveal that atherosclerotic cardiovascular disease, but not non‑atherosclerotic forms, is associated with increased incidence of multiple cancers, suggesting shared pathogenic mechanisms beyond traditional risk factors.
key_findings: Non‑atherosclerotic cardiovascular conditions (valve disease, arrhythmia) do not increase cancer risk.
Atherosclerotic diseases (CAD, PAD, aortic aneurysm) show stepwise increased cancer incidence across multiple malignancies.
Meta‑analysis of Life’s Essential Eight risk factor control predicts up to 50% reduction in future cancer risk.
open_questions: What molecular pathways link atherosclerotic plaque biology to tumor initiation?
Can targeted lipid‑lowering therapies reduce cancer incidence in high‑risk CVD patients?

talk_title: Cancer‑driven atherosclerosis via TNF‑LRG1 axis and therapeutic targeting

biomarkers.name: circulating TNF
biomarkers.finding: higher in cancer patients vs controls (p<0.01)

biomarkers.name: LRG1 in plaque
biomarkers.finding: upregulated in plaques from cancer patients

MOA_summary: Tumor‑derived TNF induces endothelial LRG1 expression, promoting intraplaque neovascularization and hemorrhage, thereby destabilizing atherosclerotic lesions; TNF blockade or LRG1 inhibition reduces plaque angiogenesis and size in mouse models.
key_findings: Mice bearing colon tumors develop plaques with abundant fragile neovessels and intraplaque hemorrhage.
