RNA‑seq identifies LRG1 as the sole consistently up‑regulated angiogenic factor in tumor‑bearing mice.
Tumor‑derived TNF drives LRG1 expression in vascular tissue.
Etanercept treatment abolishes tumor‑induced plaque neovascularization and reduces plaque size.
shRNA knockdown of LRG1 recapitulates the protective effect of TNF blockade.

combination_strategies.rationale: block tumor‑derived TNF that induces LRG1‑mediated angiogenesis
combination_strategies.phenotype: reduced plaque neovascularization and smaller plaques

combination_strategies.rationale: directly inhibit the angiogenic cytokine downstream of TNF
combination_strategies.phenotype: similar plaque stabilization as TNF blockade

open_questions: Can LRG1 inhibition be safely applied in humans given its role in normal angiogenesis?
Does the TNF‑LRG1 axis operate in other cancer types beyond colon cancer?

talk_title: Ischemic cardiovascular events reprogram bone marrow to promote tumor growth

biomarkers.name: Ly6C^high monocytic cells
biomarkers.finding: increased frequency in tumors after MI

biomarkers.name: FoxP3+ Tregs
biomarkers.finding: elevated in MI‑associated tumors

models.name: LACE and Pathways breast cancer studies
E0771 orthotopic breast cancer + LAD ligation
Bone marrow transplant from MI donors

clinical_data.population: breast cancer survivors with post‑diagnosis CV event

MOA_summary: Myocardial infarction induces long‑lasting epigenetic reprogramming of bone‑marrow myeloid progenitors, expanding Ly6C^high monocytic MDSCs that suppress T‑cell activation and increase regulatory T cells in tumors, thereby accelerating tumor growth and metastasis.
key_findings: Breast cancer survivors who experience a cardiovascular event have a 60% higher risk of recurrence and cancer‑specific death.
MI in mice doubles orthotopic breast tumor volume and increases lung metastases in melanoma models.
Tumors after MI show increased Ly6C^high monocytic MDSCs and FoxP3+ Tregs with reduced CD8+ T‑cell activation.
Depletion of Ly6C^high monocytes (Ccr2‑DTR) abolishes the MI‑induced tumor growth advantage.
Bone‑marrow from MI mice transferred to naïve recipients recapitulates accelerated tumor growth, indicating durable bone‑marrow imprinting.
ATAC‑seq reveals altered chromatin accessibility in myeloid progenitors, especially NLRP3‑positive MDPs, with reduced IRF8 motifs.

combination_strategies.rationale: prevent recruitment of Ly6C^high monocytes
combination_strategies.phenotype: loss of MI‑induced tumor acceleration in mice

combination_strategies.rationale: reduce systemic inflammation post‑MI
combination_strategies.phenotype: potentially attenuate bone‑marrow reprogramming

open_questions: Which specific cytokines from the infarcted heart drive myeloid progenitor reprogramming?
Can pharmacologic reversal of epigenetic changes in HSPCs mitigate the pro‑tumor effect?

talk_title: Reverse cardio‑oncology: cardiovascular events accelerate cancer recurrence via immune reprogramming
MOA_summary: Cancer elevates systemic TNF, which induces LRG1 expression in vascular tissue, promoting intraplaque neovascularization and hemorrhage, thereby linking tumor presence to plaque vulnerability.
key_findings: Cancer patients have higher circulating TNF levels (UK Biobank).
LRG1 is upregulated in atherosclerotic plaques from cancer patients.
Machine‑learning identifies prior cancer as a top predictor of intraplaque hemorrhage.

combination_strategies.rationale: block cancer‑derived TNF to reduce plaque neovascularization
combination_strategies.phenotype: plaque size reduction in mouse models

open_questions: Does long‑term TNF blockade improve cardiovascular outcomes in cancer survivors?
Are other tumor‑derived cytokines contributing to plaque instability?

talk_title: Micronuclei-mediated horizontal DNA transfer and ecDNA formation

models.name: DLD1
models.genotype_or_key_features: human colon cancer, Y chromosome centromere can be turned off

MOA_summary: Induced missegregation of a chromosome into micronuclei triggers chromothripsis, generating ecDNA fragments that can be transferred to neighboring cells via tunneling nanotubes; transferred DNA can integrate into recipient genomes, become transcriptionally active, and confer new phenotypes.
key_findings: Targeted Y‑chromosome missegregation in DLD1 cells produces ~900 kb ecDNA fragments that amplify a neomycin resistance marker.
Micronuclei formation leads to ~30% of cells containing micronuclei, of which ~4% show mismatched fluorescent labeling, indicating intercellular DNA transfer.
DNA transfer occurs via tunneling nanotubes; transfer events are observed in ~1–4% of micronuclei across multiple cell types.
Co‑culture experiments demonstrate that female recipient cells acquire neomycin resistance after receiving Y‑chromosome fragments from male donor cells.
Single‑cell RNA‑seq confirms transcription of transferred neomycin resistance gene and adjacent Y‑linked genes in recipient cells, confirming functional integration.
DNA transfer is enhanced by mitotic errors and DNA damage, but is independent of genomic instability alone.
open_questions: How can the frequency of intercellular DNA transfer be quantified in patient tumor samples?
Does chemotherapy‑induced DNA damage increase the rate of ecDNA transfer and contribute to secondary malignancies?
Can the cGAS‑STING pathway limit the survival of recipient cells after DNA transfer?

talk_title: Retention elements enable ecDNA propagation and therapeutic targeting

models.name: COLO 320DM
models.genotype_or_key_features: MYC ecDNA with multiple retention elements

MOA_summary: Retention elements on ecDNA bind mitotic chromosomes via mitotic bookmarking sites, enabling high‑efficiency hitchhiking; pharmacologic disruption of these elements impairs ecDNA tethering, induces micronucleation, and causes tumor regression in ecDNA‑driven xenografts.
key_findings: Thousands of retention element sequences were identified genome‑wide using Retain‑seq, many of which overlap mitotic bookmark promoters.
A single retention element increases ecDNA retention by ~50%; multiple elements raise retention >95% in live‑cell imaging.
Mathematical modeling predicts >95% retention efficacy is required for long‑term ecDNA propagation, matching experimental data.
In neuroblastoma patients, presence of ecDNA within micronuclei correlates with improved overall survival compared to patients without such micronuclei.
An Econic Biosciences inhibitor that disrupts ecDNA‑chromosome tethering causes dramatic tumor regressions in ecDNA‑positive PDX models but not in HSR‑positive controls.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Loss of retention elements via DNA methylation reduces ecDNA tethering, suggesting epigenetic regulation of ecDNA stability.

combination_strategies.rationale: BRD4 is required for ecDNA hub formation; combined inhibition may further destabilize ecDNA transcriptional advantage.
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced reduction of MYC expression and tumor growth inhibition

open_questions: Can retention element methylation status predict ecDNA stability and patient prognosis?
What are the downstream effectors of the retention‑element‑mediated mitotic bookmarking mechanism?
Will systemic delivery of retention‑disrupting agents be tolerable in patients?

talk_title: Homologous recombination deficiency as a genomic risk factor linked to radon exposure and health disparities in African American lung cancer

models.name: gnomAD Discovery and All of Us validation cohorts
models.genotype_or_key_features: 1.1 million individuals across six ancestry groups, 88 HR pathway genes

MOA_summary: The work identifies a higher prevalence of homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) signatures and ancestry-enriched germline pathogenic variants in DNA repair genes among African American NSCLC patients, linking these genomic susceptibilities to radon and air pollution exposures that cause double-strand DNA breaks.
key_findings: African American NSCLC patients show a higher frequency of HRD mutational signatures compared to European Americans, especially in squamous cell carcinoma.
Pan‑cancer analysis revealed African ancestry associated with higher somatic HRD frequencies in 13 of 23 cancer types.
Identified 37 ancestry‑enriched germline pathogenic variants across 27 HR pathway genes, predominantly frameshift alterations.
Enrichment analysis showed HR and non‑homologous end joining pathways were significantly associated with lung cancer risk in African ancestry groups.
Radon exposure contributes to 3‑4% of lung cancer cases; high radon levels (≥4 pCi/L) equate to ~½ pack‑day smoking exposure.
open_questions: How do epigenetic changes induced by radon interact with HRD to influence lung cancer risk?
What are the functional consequences of the identified ancestry‑enriched germline variants on DNA repair capacity?

company_or_sponsor: Harvard Chan School of Public Health

talk_title: Environmental risk factors and exposomics in prostate cancer among men of African ancestry

speaker.name: Dr. Maria Argos
speaker.affiliation: Boston University School of Public Health, Department of Environmental Health

models.name: Prostate Cancer Precision Health Africa 1K project
models.genotype_or_key_features: Men of African ancestry with recent prostate cancer diagnosis; includes clinical, pathology, lifestyle, biospecimens

MOA_summary: The consortium integrates whole‑genome sequencing, epigenomic profiling, and untargeted exposomics to characterize how environmental chemicals (e.g., metals, DDT/DDE, PFAS) and endocrine‑disrupting exposures interact with genetic susceptibility to drive higher tumor mutational burden and aggressive prostate cancer phenotypes in African ancestry populations.
key_findings: Men of African ancestry exhibit markedly higher tumor mutational burden than European ancestry controls (123 SA, 7 Brazil, 53 Australia).
63 epigenetic‑related driver mutations were specific to African ancestry, with only one European‑specific driver.
Prostate tumors from African ancestry individuals show widespread hypermethylation compared with European ancestry tumors.
Geographic variation in metal exposure (Al, Co, Cr, Fe, Mn) was observed, with higher levels in rural recruitment sites.
Antimony exposure correlated with higher ISUP grade in Chicago participants, while copper and zinc showed protective associations.
Mixture analysis identified manganese as a strong contributor to cancer risk models and arsenic to PSA levels.
open_questions: How do specific endocrine‑disrupting chemicals modulate tumor epigenetics to influence aggressiveness?
What are the critical exposure windows (prenatal, perinatal, adult) that most impact prostate cancer risk in African ancestry men?

company_or_sponsor: Department of Defense Health Equity Research and Outcomes Improvement Consortium

talk_title: Spatially organized CXCL3 ligand immune hubs as biomarkers of immunotherapy response and premalignant progression

speaker.name: Dr. Karin Pelka
speaker.affiliation: Gladstone Institutes, University of California, San Francisco, Parker Institute for Cancer Immunotherapy

biomarkers.name: CXCL3 ligand hub density at tumor epithelium‑stroma interface
biomarkers.finding: High density correlates with response to anti‑PD‑1 therapy in mismatch‑repair deficient colorectal cancer and NSCLC

biomarkers.name: CXCL3‑positive CD8 T‑cell infiltration in oral dysplasia
biomarkers.finding: Enrichment predicts progression to oral squamous cell carcinoma

models.name: MSS and MSI‑high colorectal cancer patients (n≈64)
models.genotype_or_key_features: Mismatch repair deficient vs proficient tumors

models.name: NSCLC cohort (John Chen study)
models.genotype_or_key_features: Patients treated with standard anti‑PD‑1 therapy

MOA_summary: Spatial transcriptomics reveals CXCL3‑rich immune hubs at the tumor epithelium‑stroma interface, where interferon‑γ‑stimulated myeloid cells and CXCL13‑positive exhausted CD8 T cells co‑localize, facilitating T‑cell infiltration and predicting immunotherapy responsiveness; the same architecture in premalignant oral lesions marks lesions at high risk of malignant transformation.
key_findings: CXCL3 ligand hubs are enriched at the epithelial‑stromal interface in mismatch‑repair deficient colorectal cancers and are absent in proficient tumors.
CD8⁺ CXCL3⁺ T cells infiltrate the cancer epithelium within these hubs, while myeloid cells remain stromal.
In NSCLC, CXCL3 hub presence predicts response to anti‑PD‑1 therapy; a stem‑immunity subtype (TCF7⁺) further refines prediction.
In oral dysplasia, early lesions with high CXCL3‑positive CD8 T‑cell infiltration progress to cancer, whereas non‑progressors lack this feature.
CXCL3 hubs are distinct from tertiary lymphoid structures and do not contain CXCR5⁺ follicular helper cells.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Heterogeneous distribution of CXCL3 hubs within a single tumor may explain variable response to checkpoint blockade.
Chronic interferon‑stimulated gene signatures without accompanying CXCL3 hubs may reflect resistance mechanisms.

combination_strategies.rationale: CXCL3 hubs indicate pre‑existing T‑cell activation that can be unleashed by PD‑1 blockade
combination_strategies.phenotype: Higher response rates in MSI‑high colorectal and NSCLC patients with hubs

open_questions: What determines the formation versus absence of CXCL3 hubs in otherwise similar tumors?
Can therapeutic modulation of CXCL3 signaling enhance hub formation and improve immunotherapy efficacy?

talk_title: Hematopoietic loss of Y chromosome and its impact on mortality and disease

biomarkers.name: Loss of Y in >5% blood cells
biomarkers.finding: Associated with 2-fold increased all-cause mortality and higher cancer risk

models.name: LOY CRISPR mouse
models.genotype_or_key_features: CRISPR-induced Y chromosome loss in hematopoietic cells

models.name: UK Biobank
models.genotype_or_key_features: Men with >5% LOY in blood, n~20,000

clinical_data.value: increased risk across multiple cancer types

MOA_summary: Loss of the Y chromosome in hematopoietic cells creates mosaicism that drives increased fibrosis via TGF‑beta signaling and impairs immune surveillance, leading to higher mortality and cancer risk; PD‑1 blockade can reverse LOY‑induced phenotypes in mouse models.
key_findings: Approximately 20% of men in UK Biobank have >5% LOY in blood at baseline, increasing linearly with age.
LOY mice generated by CRISPR exhibit reduced lifespan and increased cardiac fibrosis, reversible by TGF‑beta inhibition.
LOY monocytes display upregulated immune‑checkpoint receptors; PD‑1 inhibition reverses LOY‑induced phenotypes in mice.
Loss of Y is present in all studied men at the single‑cell level, with up to 80% mosaicism in some individuals.
LOY is associated with increased mortality (≈2‑fold) and higher incidence of solid and hematologic cancers.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: LOY cells show heightened TGF‑beta–driven fibrosis, suggesting a selective vulnerability to TGF‑beta inhibitors.

combination_strategies.rationale: Reverses fibrosis caused by LOY macrophages
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced cardiac fibrosis and improved survival in LOY mice

combination_strategies.rationale: Restores immune surveillance impaired by LOY immune cells
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reversal of LOY‑induced immune dysfunction in mouse models

open_questions: What is the precise mechanism linking LOY in specific immune cell subsets to cancer progression?
Can LOY be used as a clinically actionable biomarker for patient stratification in immunotherapy?

talk_title: Y chromosome loss as a driver of aggressive bladder cancer and response to checkpoint blockade

biomarkers.name: Y chromosome expression signature
biomarkers.finding: LOY tumors show improved response to PD‑1/PD‑L1 inhibitors (HR 0.56, 95% CI 0.38‑0.82) in IMvigor210

models.name: BBN carcinogen model
models.genotype_or_key_features: YY vs XY mice, LOY vs Y‑positive bladder tumors

models.name: IMvigor210
models.genotype_or_key_features: Advanced bladder cancer patients treated with atezolizumab, n~348

models.name: Tempus real‑world cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 171 men with bladder cancer treated with PD‑1/PD‑L1 inhibitors

MOA_summary: Loss of the Y chromosome in bladder cancer creates an immunosuppressive microenvironment with exhausted T cells and M2 macrophages, but paradoxically renders tumors more responsive to PD‑1/PD‑L1 checkpoint blockade, as demonstrated in mouse BBN models and human cohorts.
key_findings: Bladder tumors with LOY have poorer baseline prognosis but respond better to PD‑1/PD‑L1 inhibitors.
Isogenic LOY and Y‑positive bladder cancer cell lines show similar in vitro growth but LOY tumors grow faster in immunocompetent mice.
LOY tumors exhibit increased CD8+ T‑cell exhaustion and M2 macrophage infiltration.
CRISPR‑mediated Y chromosome deletion recapitulates enhanced checkpoint inhibitor response.
In IMvigor210, LOY signature stratifies patients with improved survival (HR 0.56, 95% CI 0.38‑0.82).
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: LOY tumors show increased expression of multiple immune‑checkpoint molecules, indicating potential resistance mechanisms that are overcome by checkpoint blockade.

combination_strategies.rationale: LOY tumors are immunosuppressed yet checkpoint‑dependent
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved response rates and survival

open_questions: Does longitudinal increase in LOY drive acquired resistance to checkpoint inhibitors?
What is the role of Y‑reactive T cells in shaping LOY dynamics?

talk_title: Comprehensive analysis of Y chromosome loss across adult and pediatric cancers and therapeutic implications

biomarkers.name: Y chromosome loss signature (RNA expression)
biomarkers.finding: Associated with poorer overall survival in uveal melanoma, prostate cancer, and mesothelioma

clinical_data.population: Male patients with LOY in uveal melanoma, prostate cancer, mesothelioma

clinical_data.value: 25% LOY in stage IV neuroblastoma vs 5% in lower stages

MOA_summary: Loss of the Y chromosome occurs via whole‑chromosome loss or structural rearrangements, often co‑occurring with p53 mutations or X‑linked tumor suppressor loss, leading to altered gene dosage (e.g., DDX3X/EIF1AX dependencies) and an immunosuppressive microenvironment that correlates with poorer outcomes across multiple adult and pediatric cancers.
key_findings: Y chromosome loss is present in 30‑40% of adult male tumors and ~18% of pediatric tumors.
Complete loss of Y is the most frequent Y alteration; structural variants involving Y occur in 25‑30% of sarcomas and bladder cancers.
In uveal melanoma, LOY is clonal and associated with poor prognosis, whereas in lung squamous carcinoma LOY is subclonal and linked to p53 mutations.
Loss of Y co‑occurs with X‑linked tumor suppressor mutations (KDM6A, KDM5C), suggesting a two‑hit mechanism.
DepMap analysis reveals LOY cell lines are uniquely dependent on X‑linked paralogs DDX3X and EIF1AX.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: LOY tumors show enrichment of EMT and immune‑checkpoint pathways, indicating potential resistance to conventional therapies.

combination_strategies.rationale: Synthetic lethality in LOY cells lacking Y‑linked backup
combination_strategies.phenotype: Selective killing of LOY cancer cell lines

open_questions: How does individual Y chromosome size variation affect LOY detection and functional impact?
What is the relationship between mosaic LOY in blood and tumor LOY in the same patient?
Can LOY be leveraged as a therapeutic vulnerability across cancer types?

cited_papers: Corchete Sanchez et al. 2025 Cancer Res

talk_title: Clinical outcomes of HIF‑2α inhibition in von Hippel‑Lindau disease

models.name: LightSpark‑005 (VHL syndrome phase 2 single‑arm)
models.genotype_or_key_features: germline VHL mutation, measurable renal RCC lesion ≥1 cm

clinical_data.population: VHL patients with measurable renal RCC
VHL patients with pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors

clinical_data.metric: Procedures per patient per year (on treatment)

MOA_summary: Belzutifan binds the PAS‑B pocket of HIF‑2α, allosterically preventing heterodimerization with HIF‑1β, thereby suppressing HIF‑2‑driven transcription of angiogenic and proliferative genes, which leads to shrinkage of VHL‑associated lesions across kidney, CNS, pancreas, and retina.
key_findings: In a single‑arm phase 2 study of 61 VHL patients, belzutifan achieved a 70% ORR in renal RCC lesions, including 11% CRs.
CNS hemangioblastomas responded in 50% of cases (≥30% shrinkage), a dramatic improvement over prior VEGFR TKIs (<4% response).
Pancreatic neuroendocrine tumors showed a 90% ORR, with most lesions achieving near‑complete shrinkage.
Treatment reduced the annual number of surgical procedures from ~20 per patient to <5, indicating a major quality‑of‑life impact.
Anemia was the most common on‑target toxicity (93% any grade, 13% grade ≥ 3) but was manageable with ESA support.
No significant difference in response was observed between VHL deletion versus point‑mutation genotypes.
open_questions: What mechanisms drive the subset of VHL lesions that are intrinsically resistant to HIF‑2α inhibition?
How durable are responses beyond the 5‑year follow‑up, especially for CNS lesions?
Can biomarkers (e.g., baseline HIF‑2α IHC, circulating IPAG) predict which lesions will respond?

talk_title: HIF‑2α inhibition across sporadic RCC settings: phase 3 and combination data

speaker.name: Brian Ream
speaker.affiliation: Vanderbilt University Medical Center, Geomedical Oncology

targets.dependency_context: sporadic clear‑cell RCC, refractory to prior IO/TKI

models.name: LightSpark‑005 (refractory RCC, belzutifan vs everolimus)
models.genotype_or_key_features: post‑IO, ≥1 prior VEGFR TKI, clear‑cell RCC

MOA_summary: Belzutifan allosterically blocks HIF‑2α/HIF‑1β dimerization, suppressing HIF‑2‑driven transcription; when combined with VEGFR TKIs (lenvatinib) or immunotherapy (pembrolizumab) it adds durable disease control beyond what TKIs alone achieve, producing a tail of long‑term progression‑free survivors.
key_findings: In the phase 3 LightSpark‑005 trial, belzutifan monotherapy achieved an ORR of 23% with a 34% primary progression rate, comparable to everolimus but with a distinct safety profile.
Combination of lenvatinib + belzutifan (LightSpark‑011) improved median PFS by ~4 months versus cabozantinib (HR 0.70) and yielded ~50% of patients progression‑free at 2 years.
Adjuvant belzutifan + pembrolizumab (LightSpark‑022) reduced risk of recurrence (DFS HR 0.72) versus pembrolizumab alone in high‑risk resected RCC.
Anemia (93% any grade) and early hypoxia are on‑target toxicities; most occur within the first 1–2 months and are manageable with ESA support.
Serum IPAG reduction correlates with deeper tumor responses, suggesting a pharmacodynamic biomarker for HIF‑2 inhibition.
The frontline triplet trial (LightSpark‑012) failed to meet PFS/OS endpoints, highlighting unknown biology limiting HIF‑2α inhibition in first‑line disease.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: High primary progression (34%) in monotherapy suggests a subset of RCCs are HIF‑2α independent.
Frontline triplet negative result raises questions about context‑dependent HIF‑2α dependency.

combination_strategies.rationale: standard second‑line TKI comparator
combination_strategies.phenotype: similar median PFS but inferior 2‑year tail compared to lenvatinib + belzutifan

combination_strategies.rationale: additive anti‑angiogenic effect and broader kinase coverage
combination_strategies.phenotype: median PFS gain ≈4 months, 2‑year PFS ≈50% vs cabozantinib

combination_strategies.rationale: potential synergy by targeting HIF‑2‑driven immunosuppressive pathways
combination_strategies.phenotype: DFS improvement in adjuvant high‑risk RCC (HR 0.72)

open_questions: Why did the frontline triplet (belzutifan + pembro + TKI) fail to improve PFS/OS?
What biomarkers (e.g., HIF‑2 signature, BAP1 mutation) predict durable responders?
Can earlier integration of HIF‑2 inhibition prevent emergence of HIF‑2‑independent resistant clones?

talk_title: Drosophila and mouse models reveal autophagy‑driven nutrient mobilization and SLC7A9‑dependent ferroptosis suppression in cachexia

targets.dependency_context: pancreatic cancer cell lines, Drosophila tumor model

models.name: RasV12 scribbled (Ras^V12; scrib‑) Drosophila tumor model
models.genotype_or_key_features: RasV12 + scrib loss, induces autophagy and cachexia

models.name: HPAF‑2 pancreatic cancer cell line
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS wild‑type, used for SLC7A9/A11 knockdown

models.name: Pancreatic cancer xenografts in mouse pancreas
models.genotype_or_key_features: stable knockdown of SLC7A9 or SLC7A11

MOA_summary: Loss of autophagy in host tissues reduces systemic release of cysteine, limiting tumor access to this nutrient; tumor‑derived SLC7A9 (subermesa ortholog) imports cysteine to sustain glutathione synthesis and prevent ferroptosis, thereby supporting malignant growth in a cachectic environment.
key_findings: Autophagy‑deficient Drosophila hosts are protected from organ wasting and tumor growth, indicating host autophagy fuels tumor progression.
Stable‑isotope tracing shows >70% of tumor biomass derives from nutrients mobilized by host autophagy rather than dietary intake.
Genetic screen identified the fly transporter subermesa, orthologous to mammalian SLC7A9, as uniquely required for malignant tumor growth without affecting benign RasV12 clones.

SLC7A9 knockdown in pancreatic cancer cells reduces glutathione levels, induces ferroptosis markers, and impairs tumor growth in vitro and in xenograft models, whereas SLC7A11 knockdown has a milder effect.
Rescue experiments with N‑acetylcysteine or glutathione restore tumor growth only when SLC7A9 is functional, confirming cysteine import via SLC7A9 is critical for ferroptosis suppression.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: SLC7A9 inhibition selectively impairs malignant tumor growth while sparing benign RasV12 clones and normal tissue development.

combination_strategies.rationale: To determine whether ferroptosis underlies growth inhibition upon SLC7A9 loss
combination_strategies.phenotype: Rescues cell viability and tumor growth in SLC7A9‑deficient cells

combination_strategies.rationale: Provide exogenous cysteine/glutathione to bypass SLC7A9 transport
combination_strategies.phenotype: Restores tumor growth in SLC7A9‑deficient flies and mice

open_questions: What upstream signals regulate subermesa/SLC7A9 expression during cachexia‑induced autophagy?
Can pharmacologic inhibition of SLC7A9 be achieved safely in patients without affecting normal tissue cysteine homeostasis?

talk_title: VHL loss drives HIF‑2α‑dependent PTHrP secretion and cachexia in clear‑cell renal carcinoma

biomarkers.name: PTHrP plasma level
biomarkers.finding: Elevated in RCC patients vs controls; correlates with hypercalcemia and lower muscle mass

biomarkers.name: Serum calcium
biomarkers.finding: Higher calcium associated with high PTHrP; decreases after HIF‑2α inhibition

models.name: 786O (non‑cachexia ccRCC)
models.genotype_or_key_features: VHL loss but does not induce cachexia

models.name: Orthotopic OSRC2 mouse model
models.genotype_or_key_features: Implanted into mouse kidney; induces weight loss independent of tumor size

models.name: Dana‑Farber RCC trial cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: Patients treated with belzutifan or NKT2152; baseline PTHrP measured

clinical_data.value: decrease from 11.2 mg/dL to 9.4 mg/dL after belzutifan

MOA_summary: Loss of VHL stabilizes HIF‑2α, which directly binds the PTHLH promoter and drives its transcription and translation; secreted PTHrP acts on peripheral tissues to induce hypercalcemia and cachexia, while HIF‑2α inhibition suppresses PTHrP production, normalizing calcium and improving body weight independent of tumor burden.
key_findings: Orthotopic OSRC2 tumors cause severe weight loss and hypercalcemia in mice, independent of tumor size.
Pharmacologic HIF‑2α inhibition (BT‑2399) prevents cachexia and normalizes calcium without affecting tumor growth.
Proteomic secretome analysis identified PTHLH as the only factor down‑regulated by HIF‑2α inhibition in cachexia‑inducing OSRC2 cells.
ChIP‑seq and transcriptional assays confirm HIF‑2α directly activates PTHLH expression.
CRISPR knockout of PTHLH in OSRC2 abolishes cachexia and hypercalcemia in mice; doxycycline‑inducible rescue restores the phenotype.
In a retrospective RCC patient cohort, baseline PTHrP levels correlate with higher calcium and lower muscle mass.
Treatment with FDA‑approved HIF‑2α inhibitor belzutifan (and investigational NKT2152) reduces plasma PTHrP, lowers calcium, and increases BMI, regardless of tumor response.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Cachexia phenotype is independent of tumor burden, suggesting resistance mechanisms are not linked to tumor size.

combination_strategies.rationale: Suppresses HIF‑2α‑driven PTHrP production to mitigate cachexia
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved BMI and calcium levels without tumor shrinkage

combination_strategies.rationale: Same as belzutifan; validates target class effect
combination_strategies.phenotype: Sustained low PTHrP and weight gain over 41 weeks

open_questions: Does long‑term HIF‑2α inhibition improve overall survival in ccRCC patients by mitigating cachexia?
What are the downstream signaling pathways of PTHrP in muscle and adipose that mediate wasting in RCC?

talk_title: Whole genome duplication as a pan-cancer predictor of metastasis

biomarkers.name: Whole genome duplication (WGD)
biomarkers.finding: WGD present in primary early-stage tumors predicts higher odds of future metastasis (overall ~22% prevalence, up to 2x risk in breast cancer) and higher metastatic burden

models.name: MSK-IMPACT early-stage cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 14,000 early-stage tumors with copy-number data, radiology-inferred metastasis status

clinical_data.metric: Relative risk of metastasis for WGD+ vs WGD-

MOA_summary: Whole genome duplication, inferred from copy-number profiles, marks tumors with increased genomic instability that independently predicts higher likelihood of future metastatic events and greater metastatic burden across cancer types, regardless of TP53 status.
key_findings: WGD is present in ~22% of early-stage pan-cancer tumors and varies by cancer type (10% glioma to >30% esophageal/gastric/colorectal).
Primary tumors with WGD have a significantly higher probability of developing metastasis compared to non‑WGD tumors.
WGD+ tumors exhibit higher metastatic burden (≥2 metastatic sites) within the first year after diagnosis.
WGD predicts increased risk of brain metastasis (2‑2.5x higher) across pan‑cancer and NSCLC cohorts.
The association between WGD and metastasis persists in both TP53‑wildtype and TP53‑mutant groups, indicating independence from TP53 status.
open_questions: What biological mechanisms link WGD to organ‑specific metastatic tropism, especially brain metastasis?
Can therapeutic interventions targeting aneuploidy or WGD‑associated pathways mitigate metastatic risk?

talk_title: Tissue‑based genomic instability score (GIS) for HRD prediction in breast, ovarian and pancreatic cancer

biomarkers.name: Genomic Instability Score (GIS)
biomarkers.finding: AUC 0.98 in validation (n=164); sensitivity 88‑96% and specificity ≥93% for HRD detection across breast, ovarian, pancreatic cancers

models.name: Guardant360 tissue clinical cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 741‑gene panel with SNV, CNV, fusion, methylation, whole transcriptome

MOA_summary: An ensemble model integrates SNV‑derived COSMIC signatures (SBS3, SBS39) and CNV‑based scar metrics to generate a probabilistic GIS that predicts homologous recombination deficiency, enabling broader identification of patients who may benefit from PARP inhibitor therapy beyond BRCA mutation testing.
key_findings: GIS model achieved AUC 0.98 in a 164‑sample test set.
Sensitivity ranged from 78%‑96% across breast, ovarian, and pancreatic cancers; specificity ≥93% across non‑HRD cohorts.
GIS detected HRD in ~1/3 of breast, 45% of ovarian, and 14% of pancreatic cancers, extending beyond BRCA1/2 pathogenic mutations.
Only 2‑3% of GIS‑positive ovarian/breast cases were explained by non‑BRCA HRR gene alterations, indicating added value of the signature.
GIS is available on both tissue and liquid Guardant360 assays, supporting longitudinal monitoring.
open_questions: How will GIS perform prospectively as a predictive biomarker in PARP inhibitor trials?
Can cancer‑type specific models (e.g., prostate) improve GIS accuracy?

talk_title: Integrated multi‑omic analysis of microvascular invasion in resectable hepatocellular carcinoma

biomarkers.name: MVI prediction score
biomarkers.finding: Score correlates with higher recurrence, poorer survival, and reduced response to atezolizumab (HR 0.56) in HCC

models.name: Primary HCC cultures
models.genotype_or_key_features: Cell doubling time correlated with MVI score

clinical_data.metric: Response to atezolizumab (high vs low MVI score)

MOA_summary: A transcriptomic signature derived from microvascular invasion (MVI) tumors identifies cell‑cycle activation, metabolic reprogramming, and EMT‑related transcription factors; high MVI scores predict aggressive biology, poorer immunotherapy response, and highlight TOP2A as a therapeutic vulnerability treatable with doxorubicin.
key_findings: An MVI‑associated gene signature robustly predicts microvascular invasion across five independent HCC cohorts.
High MVI scores associate with increased cell‑cycle pathway activity (G2M, E2F, MYC) and reduced hepatic metabolic function.
Patients with high MVI scores have inferior outcomes on atezolizumab‑based therapy (HR 0.56).
Serum protein panel (six markers) combined with albumin/bilirubin yields AUC 0.856 for non‑invasive MVI detection.
TOP2A emerged as a top dependency; doxorubicin showed strongest in‑vitro efficacy in MVI‑high models.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Standard systemic agents (second/third line) show limited efficacy in MVI‑positive HCC, indicating a resistance phenotype.

combination_strategies.rationale: TOP2A dependency in MVI‑high HCC
combination_strategies.phenotype: Significant tumor cell kill in vitro; potential to overcome resistance to checkpoint inhibitors

open_questions: Will prospective testing of the serum biomarker panel improve surveillance outcomes?
Can TOP2A‑targeted therapy improve survival when combined with immunotherapy in MVI‑high patients?

talk_title: Spatial immune profiling of colorectal adenomas predicts recurrence and progression to cancer

biomarkers.name: IgA plasma cell abundance
biomarkers.finding: Reduced IgA plasma cells in index polyps associate with recurrence and progression to cancer

biomarkers.name: MUC2 expression
biomarkers.finding: Lower MUC2 in polyps predicts higher risk of recurrence/progression

models.name: NanoString GeoMx spatial transcriptomics cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 6‑12 donors per outcome phenotype, multiple regions per polyp

clinical_data.metric: Immune cell reduction in progressive polyps
clinical_data.value: Significant decrease (p<0.05) in IgA plasma cells, IgM plasma cells, macrophages
clinical_data.population: polyps progressing to cancer vs non‑recurring

clinical_data.value: Elevated TUFT cell and claudin‑10+ cells in progressive polyps

MOA_summary: Spatial transcriptomics reveals that loss of mucosal immune surveillance (IgA/M plasma cells, microfold cells) and barrier proteins (MUC2, KRT20) together with increased stem‑cell‑like epithelial states drive adenoma recurrence and malignant transformation.
key_findings: Progressive polyps show marked reduction of IgA/M plasma cells and macrophages, indicating compromised mucosal immunity.
Loss of barrier genes (MUC2, KRT20) and microfold cells correlates with higher recurrence risk.
Stem‑cell‑like transcriptional programs (TUFT cells, claudin‑10+) are enriched in polyps that progress to cancer.
Stromal compartments of progressive polyps display reduced PPAR signaling and DNA repair (HR, base excision).
Shared immune deficits are observed between polyps that recur (with or without cancer) suggesting a common early risk signature.
open_questions: Can a combined spatial biomarker panel be translated into a clinically actionable risk score for surveillance intervals?
Would immune‑modulating interventions (e.g., mucosal vaccines) restore barrier immunity and prevent progression?

talk_title: Whole‑exome HRD scoring predicts PARP inhibitor benefit in ovarian cancer

biomarkers.name: Whole‑exome HRD score (cutoff >50)
biomarkers.finding: HRD‑High patients receiving PARP inhibitors had significantly longer PFS vs bevacizumab (first‑line and second‑line settings)

models.name: Seoul National University Hospital ovarian cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 81 patients, paired tumor‑normal WES, HRD score, BRCA status, maintenance therapy (PARPi or bevacizumab)

clinical_data.metric: PFS HR for PARPi vs bevacizumab (HRD‑High, first‑line)
PFS HR for PARPi vs bevacizumab (HRD‑High, second‑line)

MOA_summary: A whole‑exome derived HRD scar score (integrating LOH, TAI, LST) identifies tumors with homologous recombination deficiency; HRD‑High status predicts superior progression‑free survival with PARP inhibitor maintenance compared with anti‑angiogenic therapy.
key_findings: Two‑thirds of the cohort were HRD‑High (score >50).
HRD‑High correlated strongly with BRCA pathogenic alterations.
In first‑line maintenance, PARP inhibitors improved PFS over bevacizumab in HRD‑High patients (HR 0.56).
In second‑line maintenance, HRD‑High remained the dominant predictor of PARPi benefit, whereas BRCA status lost significance.
Whole‑exome HRD scoring provides independent prognostic information beyond BRCA mutation.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: BRCA mutation alone was less predictive in the recurrent (second‑line) setting, highlighting the need for broader HRD assessment.
open_questions: What is the optimal HRD score cutoff across different tumor types and treatment lines?
Can the WES‑based HRD assay be prospectively validated in larger, multi‑center trials?

talk_title: CA19‑9 for early detection of pancreatic cancer in new‑onset diabetes

biomarkers.name: CA19‑9
biomarkers.finding: In new‑onset diabetes cohort, CA19‑9 achieved 83% sensitivity at >97% specificity for pancreatic cancer (PPV ~70%)

models.name: NOD cohort (CPDPC)
models.genotype_or_key_features: 2,263 participants with new‑onset diabetes, 12 pancreatic cancer cases within 3 years

MOA_summary: Elevated serum CA19‑9 levels rise up to two years before clinical diagnosis of pancreatic cancer; longitudinal monitoring improves lead time by ~6 months and captures cases missed by a single threshold.
key_findings: CA19‑9 alone identified 83% of pancreatic cancers in a new‑onset diabetes cohort with >97% specificity.
A longitudinal algorithm incorporating CA19‑9 velocity increased AUC by ~6% and added ~6 months lead time.
One missed case was captured by the longitudinal rise despite a negative single threshold.
PPV in this high‑risk NOD population was ~70%, substantially higher than in the general population.

combination_strategies.rationale: Improve sensitivity for CA19‑9‑negative cases
combination_strategies.phenotype: 13% increase in sensitivity without loss of specificity

open_questions: How does CA19‑9 performance vary across pancreatic cancer stages at diagnosis?
Can integration with imaging or other biomarkers further improve early detection?

talk_title: Spatial biology and AI‑guided image analysis for early melanoma detection

speaker.name: Wendy Cao
speaker.affiliation: University of Queensland / QIMR Berghofer Medical Research Institute

biomarkers.name: Melanocyte cell‑state module scores (nevus vs melanoma)
biomarkers.finding: Red melanocyte module enriched in benign nevi; purple module enriched in thin melanoma, enabling classification of ambiguous lesions

models.name: Visium Cytospace cohort (261 samples)
models.genotype_or_key_features: Spatial transcriptomics of benign nevi, atypical nevi, melanoma in situ, thin invasive melanoma

clinical_data.value: Not quantified; clear separation between benign and malignant lesions in validation set

MOA_summary: Spatial transcriptomics deconvolution identifies distinct melanocyte transcriptional states; a melanoma‑specific module (purple) correlates with malignant lesions, while a nevus‑specific module (red) marks benign nevi, enabling AI models to predict gene expression from H&E and guide diagnostic decisions.
readouts: AI model predicting SOX10/S100B from H&E
key_findings: Two melanocyte cell states were discovered: red (nevus‑associated) and purple (melanoma‑associated).
Module scores derived from these states accurately differentiate benign nevi from thin melanoma.
BRAF V600E mutation signal was higher in benign nevi than in thin melanoma.
AI model trained on spatial data can predict SOX10 and S100B expression directly from H&E images with high correlation.
In ambiguous lesions, high melanoma module scores aligned with expert recommendation for excision.
open_questions: Can the H&E‑based AI prediction be scaled to routine pathology workflows?
Will integration of protein markers (e.g., via Orion) further improve diagnostic accuracy for intermediate lesions?

talk_title: ACT Wonder Squared S: Advancing Clinical Trials Working Through Outreach Navigation and Digitally Enabled Referral and Recruitment Strategies

speaker.name: Dr. Susan Vetter Bramble
speaker.affiliation: Moffitt Cancer Center and Research Institute

MOA_summary: Implemented a cluster‑stratified, multilevel intervention across 14 priority zones to increase trial referral and enrollment, combining community education sessions, a TrialConnect portal for community physicians, and digital tools (portfolio profiler, eligibility calculator, recruitment dashboard) to align trial availability with cancer burden and streamline referral workflows.
key_findings: Delivered 36 community education sessions reaching ~500 participants, with post‑intervention increases in trial familiarity, intention, and knowledge.
Portfolio profiler and recruitment dashboard were the most frequently used digital tools among internal staff; eligibility calculator had the highest visit count.
80% of users who accessed a digital tool returned for additional use.
TrialConnect portal had low external physician uptake despite extensive marketing.
Patient decision‑aid tool (Choices DA) was accessed by 25% of eligible patients, with higher usage among White and Hispanic users.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Low adoption of the TrialConnect portal by community physicians highlighted challenges in external stakeholder engagement.
Digital tool usage varied by provider type, indicating potential barriers to broader implementation.

talk_title: Support Trial (ECCOACRN): Community Oncology Practice Sites Clinical Trial Referral Initiative
MOA_summary: Conducted a hybrid type‑1 cluster‑randomized rollout across seven NCORP community oncology sites, using stakeholder‑driven tools (web‑based health literacy resources, trial navigators) to improve referral of Black and Hispanic patients, demonstrating feasibility of enrolling underrepresented populations into therapeutic trials.
key_findings: Across sites, 48 of 161 referred Black/Hispanic patients at Baptist site consented (30% conversion).
Carle site: 59 of 193 referred consented (30%).
Geisinger: 24 of 40 referred consented (60%).
Montefiere: 9 of 19 referred consented (47%).
Provider surveys showed 90% reported referring minority patients, yet only ~20% of patients recalled being referred.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: IRB delays and site‑specific workflow variability limited timely data sharing.
Staff turnover and bandwidth constraints impeded consistent implementation.

talk_title: ME Alex: Tailored AI‑Driven Communication Intervention to Facilitate Cancer Clinical Trial Referrals
MOA_summary: Developed an AI‑generated virtual community health educator platform (ME Alex) that delivers plain‑language trial information via video and chat, personalizing messaging based on demographic and linguistic cues to improve trial awareness and referral among underserved patients.
key_findings: Over 1,500 users engaged with the platform to date.
Participants rated content clarity and engagement between 80–90 on a 0‑100 scale.
Higher usage observed among White and Hispanic users; lower among Asian and unknown ethnicity groups.
Iterative feedback led to incorporation of voice‑cloned AI avatars and a chat function for real‑time Q&A.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Initial AI‑generated voices were perceived as lacking warmth and empathy, prompting redesign with voice clones.
Chat functionality was not available at launch, delaying full interactivity.

talk_title: Enabling a Cross‑Site Network (CUS2CT) Through a Learning Collaborative Data Coordination and Evaluation
MOA_summary: Established a centralized data coordination hub (DEC) for the CP2CT network, standardizing common data elements, REDCap submission, and governance structures to enable consistent evaluation and rapid dissemination of multilevel trial‑access interventions across four U01 sites.
key_findings: Implemented a unified REDCap platform with common data elements aligned to NCI standards across all network sites.
Created three working groups (data, program evaluation, learning collaborative) and regular steering committee meetings to ensure alignment.
Developed a logic model and standardized outcome metrics now available for external researchers.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: IRB variability across sites slowed data sharing and limited access to granular clinic screening metrics.

talk_title: Accrual to Clinical Trials (ACT) at Ohio State University: Baseline Assessment and Multilevel Stepped‑Wedge Intervention

speaker.name: Dr. Cheryl Lee
speaker.affiliation: Ohio State University Comprehensive Cancer Center

clinical_data.value: 40% of 2,000 patients had a trial for their disease
clinical_data.population: All oncology patients screened at OSU clinics

clinical_data.metric: Eligibility among those with a trial

MOA_summary: Performed a baseline audit of referral pathways across OSU oncology clinics, identified multilevel barriers, and launched a stepped‑wedge, group‑randomized intervention targeting patient, provider, system, and community levels with outreach, EMR prompts, navigation, and education to improve trial referral and enrollment.
readouts: percentage of patients at each step
key_findings: Baseline data showed only 5% of screened patients ultimately consented to a trial.
Key barriers identified: low health literacy, provider time constraints, strict eligibility criteria, IRB delays, and lack of centralized referral contacts.
Proposed interventions include trial point‑of‑contact numbers, EMR alerts, patient navigators, and community education.
Hypothesized that tailored multilevel interventions could raise overall consent rates from 5% toward 8–10%.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Variability in A‑D step percentages across clinics highlighted site‑specific challenges.

talk_title: Zinc transporter ZIP4 drives pancreatic cancer cachexia via muscle wasting signaling cascade

speaker.name: Min Li
speaker.affiliation: University of Pennsylvania, Department of Surgery

targets.dependency_context: downstream of KRAB in ZIP4-high tumors

models.name: KPC model
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS G12D; TP53 R172H; pancreatic tumor

models.name: ASPC mouse model
models.genotype_or_key_features: human pancreatic cancer cell line injected into immunodeficient mice

models.name: BxPC3 mouse model
models.genotype_or_key_features: moderate ZIP4 expression pancreatic cancer line

MOA_summary: Overexpression of the zinc transporter ZIP4 in pancreatic tumor cells increases intracellular zinc, activating the KRAB transcription factor, which up‑regulates Rab27b‑mediated secretion of Hsp70‑90 proteins that bind muscle cell receptors, triggering p38 MAPK, eBPβ, UBR2 and ATGL1 signaling and resulting in skeletal muscle atrophy and systemic cachexia.
key_findings: ZIP4 is markedly up‑regulated in human pancreatic tumors versus benign tissue.
ZIP4 knock‑down in mice extends median survival from 15 to 30 days (ASPC model) and from 57 to 92 days (BxPC3 model).
ZIP4‑silenced mice retain body weight and show rescued muscle fiber size and grip strength despite persistent primary tumor burden.
Mechanistically, ZIP4‑driven zinc influx activates KRAB, inducing Rab27b‑mediated Hsp70‑90 release that engages muscle P38 MAPK signaling and atrophy pathways.

combination_strategies.rationale: potential synergistic tumor control with ZIP4 targeting
combination_strategies.phenotype: concern for overlapping toxicities

open_questions: Can pharmacologic inhibition of ZIP4 be achieved safely in patients?
What is the impact of ZIP4 targeting on tumor growth and metastasis beyond cachexia phenotypes?

talk_title: Dynamic phenotyping of cancer cachexia using longitudinal weight, imaging, and symptom data

speaker.name: Elizabeth Cespedes Feliciano
speaker.affiliation: Kaiser Permanente Northern California, Department of Oncology

biomarkers.name: GDF15
biomarkers.finding: higher baseline GDF15 associated with severe wasting phenotype and shorter survival

biomarkers.name: IL-6
biomarkers.finding: elevated IL-6 levels in splenomegaly and severe wasting clusters

models.name: Kaiser Permanente advanced cancer cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: n≈6000 patients with unresectable lung, pancreatic, or colorectal cancer

models.name: UNCOVER prospective observational study
models.genotype_or_key_features: ≈1600 patients, baseline and 3‑month visits with blood, PROs, accelerometry, CT imaging

clinical_data.n: n=157 (high‑risk melanoma example from other talk, not applicable)

MOA_summary: Longitudinal electronic health record data and serial CT imaging were used to define dynamic cachexia phenotypes (pre‑cachexia, active cachexia, recovery) and to link body‑composition clusters with distinct inflammatory biomarker signatures, revealing subgroups with divergent survival and symptom burden.
key_findings: Applying a sliding‑window BMI trajectory algorithm to ~6,000 advanced cancer patients identified dynamic cachexia episodes, including pre‑cachexia, recovery, and recurrent weight loss.

Hierarchical clustering of CT‑derived body composition changes revealed five phenotypes (early adipose remodeling, moderate wasting, severe wasting, splenomegaly, mixed), each with distinct inflammatory profiles (elevated GDF15, IL‑6, IL‑8).
Patients in severe and splenomegaly clusters had the shortest overall survival, independent of cancer type.
One‑quarter of weight‑stable patients harbored high‑risk imaging phenotypes, suggesting subclinical cachexia detectable before weight loss.

Two symptom‑based latent classes emerged: a high‑symptom burden group with greater fatigue, depression, low activity, and higher cortisol, low leptin, and a low‑symptom group resembling non‑cachectic controls.
open_questions: Can early imaging‑derived phenotypes predict response to anti‑cachexia interventions?
What are the causal relationships between specific inflammatory mediators (e.g., GDF15) and the identified body‑composition clusters?

talk_title: Targeted Cancer Interception Vaccines and p53 Reactivation Strategies

speaker.name: Margie Lee Clapper
speaker.affiliation: Fox Chase Cancer Center, Philadelphia, PA, USA, Director of the Cancer Prevention Interception Targeted Agent Discovery Program

targets.dependency_context: EGFR L858R mutant ground-glass opacity lesions
KRAS G12C mutant ground-glass opacity lesions
p53 hotspot mutations (R175H, R273) in Li-Fraumeni syndrome models

biomarkers.name: IFN-γ ELISpot response
biomarkers.finding: 85% of vaccinated Lynch syndrome individuals showed a positive IFN-γ response at 1 year

biomarkers.name: Nox and Puma mRNA induction
biomarkers.finding: Significant upregulation in p53 mutant Cal-33 cells treated with REM-491, not in WT or p53-null cells

models.name: EGFR L858R tail‑vein injection model
models.genotype_or_key_features: Mouse tail‑vein injection of EGFR L858R‑mutant cells

models.name: Breast carcinoma R175H PDX
models.genotype_or_key_features: TP53 R175H mutant breast cancer cells

MOA_summary: Clapper presented mRNA‑based vaccines targeting EGFR L858R and KRAS G12C mutations found in premalignant lung ground‑glass opacities, demonstrating CD8+ T‑cell activation and reduced tumor formation in mouse models. She also described the small‑molecule p53 reactivator REM‑491, which restores transcriptional activity of multiple hotspot p53 mutants (R175H, R273) in vitro (EC50 ~40 nM) and suppresses tumor growth in xenograft models without observable toxicity.
key_findings: In a cohort of Lynch syndrome individuals, 85% maintained a positive IFN‑γ ELISpot response one year after two doses of the 209‑peptide NU209 vaccine.
Whole‑exome sequencing of 200 ground‑glass lung nodules revealed 25% harbor EGFR L858R and 40% KRAS G12C mutations.
EGFR L858R mRNA vaccine achieved >90% lipid nanoparticle encapsulation efficiency and induced robust CD8+ T‑cell responses in mice.
Mice vaccinated with EGFR L858R mRNA vaccine showed a dramatic reduction in tumor formation compared with empty nanoparticle controls.
REM‑491 induced Nox and Puma transcription in TP53‑mutant Cal‑33 cells but not in WT or p53‑null cells, confirming mutant‑specific activity.
REM‑491 achieved EC50 ≈40 nM for mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization in TP53 R175H cells and lower EC50 in heterozygous R273 cells.
In vivo, REM‑491 administered daily for 17 days reduced tumor volume and weight in TP53 R175H breast cancer xenografts without weight loss or biochemical toxicity, and MTD >400 mg/kg was not reached.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: REM‑491 showed activity across ~25‑30% of p53 hotspot mutations, suggesting broad but not universal reactivation.
Preliminary clinical data on Y220C‑targeted agents (e.g., rosatapopt) indicate emerging resistance, underscoring the need for agents like REM‑491 with broader mutant coverage.

combination_strategies.rationale: Negative control to demonstrate vaccine specificity
combination_strategies.phenotype: No tumor formation reduction

open_questions: What are the pharmacodynamic biomarkers that can be used to monitor REM‑491 target engagement in patients?
Can the EGFR/KRAS mRNA vaccines be adapted to target additional driver mutations found in premalignant lesions?

cited_papers: Phase 1/2a trial of frameshift peptide vaccine in Lynch syndrome (Matthias Kloor)

talk_title: Precision Cancer Prevention: Risk Assessment, Implementation, and Public Health Strategies

speaker.name: Suzette Delaloge
speaker.affiliation: Gustave Roussy, Paris, France, Director of the Interception Personalized Cancer Prevention Program

biomarkers.name: Breast density
biomarkers.finding: Integrated into risk models to stratify women into low, average, and high risk categories

biomarkers.name: Polygenic risk scores (PRS)
biomarkers.finding: Combined with clinical factors to achieve 5‑year invasive breast cancer risk estimates (high risk ≈1.67%)

models.name: MIPEPS trial cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 28,000 European women, risk stratified by PEARS score, ages 40‑70

models.name: LIBER trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: BRCA/Lynch carriers, letrozole vs placebo, n≈? (low accrual 18%)

clinical_data.value: Low risk 36%, High risk 33% of participants

clinical_data.metric: 5‑year invasive breast cancer risk (high risk group)
Adherence to screening in interception program
clinical_data.population: Participants in French interception pilot program

MOA_summary: Delaloge outlined a public‑health‑driven precision prevention framework that integrates individual risk assessment (clinical scores, breast density, polygenic risk scores) with AI‑enhanced imaging, digital tools, and coordinated care pathways to deliver risk‑adapted screening, surveillance, and risk‑reduction interventions across multiple cancer types.
key_findings: In the MIPEPS trial, 36% of 28,000 women were classified as low risk and 33% as high risk, with a 5‑year invasive breast cancer risk of 1.67% in the high‑risk group.
Turnaround time for the PEARS risk score was a median of 7 weeks, demonstrating feasibility of large‑scale academic risk profiling.
The US Wisdom trial (non‑inferiority) met its primary endpoint of Stage 2B breast cancer incidence, supporting risk‑adapted screening.
The LIBER letrozole prevention trial suffered low accrual (18% acceptance) and 75% 5‑year adherence, resulting in an underpowered study with no observed risk reduction.
The French interception pilot program achieved a 1‑point improvement in the WCRF lifestyle score in 41% of participants and high adherence to recommended screening.

combination_strategies.rationale: Improve accuracy and feasibility of risk stratification compared with traditional clinical scores
combination_strategies.phenotype: Potentially comparable predictive performance to classical models

combination_strategies.rationale: Facilitate patient engagement, education, and adherence to prevention interventions
combination_strategies.phenotype: Increased WCRF score and screening adherence in pilot participants

open_questions: How can risk assessment tools be standardized and integrated across different European health systems?
What are the most effective strategies to improve participation of low‑education and socio‑economically deprived populations in risk‑based screening?
Which biomarkers can serve as actionable, longitudinal surrogates to motivate lifestyle change and monitor prevention efficacy?

talk_title: Metabolic vulnerabilities of Nrf2-activated cancer cells and cysteine availability in vivo

models.name: KRAS; p53; Nrf2 mutant lung tumor model
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS G12D, p53 loss, Nrf2 mutation

models.name: Myc overexpression, p53 deletion liver tumor model
models.genotype_or_key_features: hydrodynamic tail vein Myc OE, p53 KO

MOA_summary: Gina demonstrated that Nrf2 activation rewires cysteine and glutathione metabolism, creating dependencies on cystine uptake, glutamine metabolism, and NADPH production; she showed tissue-specific cysteine sourcing via transsulfuration in liver versus reliance on extracellular cystine in lung tumors, and highlighted how microenvironmental nutrient gradients shape these vulnerabilities.
key_findings: Liver tumors retain transsulfuration activity, while KRAS-driven lung tumors lack cysteine synthesis and depend on extracellular cystine.
Interstitial fluid of lung tumors contains ~40 µM reduced cysteine and ~1 mM reduced glutathione, indicating a shift to cysteine uptake.
Blocking cystine uptake with GGSTOP impairs tumor growth in vivo, but technical issues limited chronic dosing.
Nrf2-active cells become glutamate‑limited and are sensitized to glutaminase inhibition (CB‑839) in standard RPMI but not in tumor‑derived sTIF media.
sTIF media, mimicking interstitial fluid, rescues cystine‑starved cells and abolishes CB‑839 sensitivity, revealing context‑dependent metabolic vulnerabilities.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Nrf2‑active cells show selective sensitivity to glutaminase inhibition only under artificial cystine‑rich conditions.
Tumor microenvironment‑derived cysteine reduces reliance on glutamine metabolism, potentially conferring resistance to CB‑839.

combination_strategies.rationale: Exploit glutamate limitation in Nrf2‑active cells
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor cell death in RPMI but not in sTIF

combination_strategies.rationale: Block extracellular glutathione‑derived cysteine supply
combination_strategies.phenotype: Impaired tumor growth in mice

open_questions: What are the precise transporters mediating cysteine uptake in lung tumors?
Can chronic inhibition of GGT or cystine transport be achieved without immune neutralization?
How does spatial heterogeneity of interstitial cysteine affect therapeutic windows?

talk_title: Mitochondrial protein degradation drives heme‑mediated CD8 T‑cell exhaustion

biomarkers.name: PD‑1
biomarkers.finding: Higher expression on heme‑treated CD8 T cells (n=multiple donors)

biomarkers.name: TCF1
biomarkers.finding: Reduced expression after heme exposure

biomarkers.name: BACH2
biomarkers.finding: Proteasome inhibition restores BACH2 protein levels

models.name: tumor‑bearing mice CD8 T‑cell isolates
models.genotype_or_key_features: MDR high vs MDR low mitochondrial phenotype

clinical_data.metric: Proliferation of heme‑treated T cells in vivo

MOA_summary: Ho showed that chronic antigen exposure in tumors leads to accumulation of depolarized mitochondria in CD8 T cells; blocked mitophagy forces proteasomal degradation of mitochondrial proteins, releasing heme that binds and degrades BACH2, relieving repression of BLIMP1 and driving terminal exhaustion.
readouts: In vivo adoptive transfer proliferation tracking
key_findings: MDR‑low CD8 T cells in tumors have high mitochondrial mass but low membrane potential and upregulated proteasome genes.
Proteasome inhibition (MG132) restores BACH2 and reduces BLIMP1, partially reversing exhaustion markers.
Heme released from degraded mitochondrial hemoproteins increases in exhausted T cells and correlates with exhaustion severity in vivo.
Exogenous heme treatment in vitro drives PD‑1 up‑regulation, TCF1 loss, and cytokine production decline.
BACH2 mutant resistant to heme binding preserves stemness markers even in tumor‑derived damaged‑mitochondria T cells.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Proteasome activity is selectively up‑regulated in mitochondria‑damaged T cells, not in cytosol.
Heme‑mediated BACH2 degradation appears specific to hemoproteins released from mitochondria.

combination_strategies.rationale: Prevent mitochondrial protein degradation and heme release
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved proliferation and cytokine production of CD19 CAR T cells in vitro

combination_strategies.rationale: Validate heme‑dependent exhaustion mechanism
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced regulatory heme signal when proteasome blocked

open_questions: What nuclear heme‑binding partners mediate transcriptional reprogramming beyond BACH2?
Can selective modulation of mitochondrial protein turnover be achieved without global proteasome inhibition?
How does this pathway operate in chronic viral infections versus cancer?

patent_or_ip_hints: Method for modulating T‑cell exhaustion via proteasome inhibition

talk_title: TIM‑4/AMPK‑TREM1 axis controls mitochondrial metabolism and immunosuppression in tumor‑associated macrophages

biomarkers.name: CD206
biomarkers.finding: Increased in TREM1‑negative TAMs

biomarkers.name: ARG1
biomarkers.finding: Elevated in TIM4‑high TAMs

biomarkers.name: CD86
biomarkers.finding: Higher in TREM1‑positive TAMs

MOA_summary: Huang identified that tumor‑derived signals up‑regulate TIM4, which activates AMPK, suppresses TREM1 expression, and enhances mitochondrial complex II activity and glutamine‑derived α‑KG production in tumor‑associated macrophages; the resulting α‑KG fuels JMJD3‑mediated H3K27 demethylation, driving an immunosuppressive M2‑like phenotype.
readouts: Western blot for phospho‑AMPK, TREM1, TIM4
Flow cytometry for CD206, CD86, ARG1
key_findings: Tumor‑associated macrophages show ~70% reduction of TREM1 protein compared with splenic macrophages.
TIM4 deficiency or blockade restores TREM1 expression, reduces mitochondrial complex II activity, and diminishes α‑KG production.
Myeloid‑specific TREM1 deletion hyperactivates mitochondrial respiration, increases immunosuppressive markers, accelerates tumor growth, and shortens mouse survival.
AMPK activation downstream of TIM4 suppresses TREM1; AMPK knockdown rescues TREM1 and lowers mitochondrial activity.
GMG3 loss in myeloid cells reduces tumor size, TAM numbers, and immunosuppressive markers while enhancing CD8 T‑cell infiltration.
Combined TIM4 antibody and GMG3 inhibitor (GSKJ4) synergistically shrink tumors, decrease TAM immunosuppression, and improve survival in mouse melanoma.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: TIM4‑mediated AMPK activation selectively suppresses TREM1 in TAMs without affecting peripheral macrophages.
GMG3 inhibition preferentially impacts TAMs with high α‑KG, sparing normal tissue macrophages.

combination_strategies.rationale: Block TIM4‑AMPK signaling to restore TREM1 and reduce mitochondrial hyperactivity
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced tumor growth and TAM immunosuppression in mice

combination_strategies.rationale: Prevent α‑KG‑driven H3K27 demethylation and M2 gene expression
combination_strategies.phenotype: Decreased ARG1/CD206, increased CD86/MHC‑II, enhanced CD8 T‑cell infiltration

combination_strategies.rationale: Dual blockade of upstream signaling and epigenetic effector
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression and prolonged survival

open_questions: Can TIM4 blockade be combined safely with PD‑1/PD‑L1 checkpoint inhibitors?
What is the impact of systemic AMPK inhibition on other immune subsets?
Are there biomarkers (e.g., circulating α‑KG) predictive of response to TIM4/GMG3 targeting?

cited_papers: Zhao et al. 2024 Cancer Immunology Research
patent_or_ip_hints: Methods of modulating TAM metabolism via TIM4‑AMPK‑TREM1 axis

talk_title: Cancer risk and genotype‑phenotype correlations in telomere biology disorders

speaker.name: Dr. Sharon Savage
speaker.affiliation: National Cancer Institute, Clinical Genetics Branch, Division of Cancer Epidemiology and Genetics

tumor_types: head and neck squamous cell carcinoma

biomarkers.name: telomere length (flow cytometry in lymphocytes)
biomarkers.finding: Patients with dyskeratosis congenita have telomere lengths below the 1st percentile for age, distinguishing them from the general population

biomarkers.name: clonal hematopoiesis mutations (POT1, TERT promoter, PPM1D)
biomarkers.finding: Presence of non‑cancer‑associated somatic mutations correlated with better outcomes, whereas TP53 loss, MDM4 overexpression, or U2AF1 mutations were linked to cancer development

models.name: Inherited Bone Marrow Failure Syndrome Study
models.genotype_or_key_features: 231 patients from 124 families with telomere biology disorders, including heterozygous, biallelic, X‑linked variants

clinical_data.metric: cancer risk pre‑transplant vs general population

MOA_summary: Germline mutations in telomere maintenance genes (e.g., TINF2, RTEL1, TERT) cause critically short or dysfunctional telomeres, leading to stem‑cell failure and a strong selective pressure for clonal hematopoiesis; specific somatic mutations (TP53 loss, MDM4 overexpression, U2AF1) further drive malignant transformation, resulting in markedly elevated risks of head‑and‑neck SCC, AML, and MDS.
key_findings: Median overall survival of the entire TBD cohort was 52 years, but carriers of biallelic or X‑linked/TINF2 variants had median survival only in the 30s.
Overall cancer risk was ~3‑fold higher than the general population, rising to ~4‑fold before transplant.
Head‑and‑neck squamous cell carcinoma risk was ~50‑fold higher (median onset age 14.7 years) in TINF2 mutation carriers.
AML risk was ~50‑fold and MDS risk ~500‑fold higher than expected in age‑sex matched controls.

Clonal hematopoiesis with non‑cancer‑associated mutations (POT1, TERT promoter, PPM1D) correlated with better prognosis, whereas TP53 loss, MDM4 overexpression, or U2AF1 mutations predicted cancer development.
Telomere length measured by flow cytometry reliably distinguishes TBD patients from the general population and aids genotype‑phenotype counseling.
open_questions: How can somatic mutation profiling be integrated into routine surveillance to predict imminent cancer transformation?
What are the optimal telomere‑length thresholds for initiating intensified cancer screening in heterozygous carriers?
Can targeted therapies mitigate the high cancer risk in specific genotype subgroups (e.g., TINF2 vs. RTEL1)?

talk_title: Genotype‑specific risk assessment and screening strategies for Lynch syndrome

speaker.name: Dr. Faye Kastrinos
speaker.affiliation: Columbia University, Department of Medicine, GI Cancer Genetics and Prevention Program

biomarkers.name: MSI‑high (tumor)
biomarkers.assay: IHC for MMR loss / PCR MSI testing
biomarkers.finding: MSI‑high tumors have a high positive predictive value for germline Lynch syndrome across cancer types

biomarkers.name: PREM5 score
biomarkers.assay: online logistic regression tool using personal/family cancer history
biomarkers.finding: Score ≥2.5 % predicts ≥2.5 % probability of a pathogenic MMR variant and triggers insurance‑covered germline testing

models.name: All of Us research cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 200,000+ participants with WGS; identified 1/354 prevalence of pathogenic MMR variants

models.name: International Mismatch Repair Consortium registry
models.genotype_or_key_features: Multi‑national family registries with genotype‑specific cancer incidence data

clinical_data.metric: prevalence of Lynch syndrome in unselected population
cancer risk reduction with cascade testing & screening (first‑degree relative)
cancer risk reduction with cascade testing & screening (sibling)
colorectal cancer incidence in carriers vs non‑carriers (age 50)
colorectal cancer incidence in carriers vs non‑carriers (age 70)

MOA_summary: Germline pathogenic variants in mismatch‑repair genes (MLH1, MSH2, MSH6, PMS2) cause microsatellite instability, markedly increasing risk of colorectal, endometrial and other cancers; genotype‑specific modeling shows earlier onset for MLH1/MSH2 and later, lower‑penetrance disease for MSH6/PMS2, informing age‑adjusted colonoscopy intervals and the potential integration of non‑invasive FIT screening to reduce colonoscopy burden.
key_findings: Lynch syndrome prevalence is ~1/350 in the US, with ~63 % of carriers lacking any personal or family cancer history that would trigger clinical suspicion.
MSH6 and PMS2 carriers constitute the majority of pathogenic variants in population‑based cohorts, yet they have later onset and lower absolute cancer risks compared with MLH1/MSH2.

Genotype‑specific colonoscopy recommendations (e.g., start at 25 y for MLH1/MSH2 annually, at 35 y for MSH6 every 2 y, at 40 y for PMS2 every 3 y) improve risk‑benefit balance and reduce colonoscopy burden.

Modeling shows that integrating biennial FIT with extended colonoscopy intervals (e.g., every 4 y) for PMS2 carriers maintains cancer prevention while improving quality‑adjusted life years and cost‑effectiveness.
Aspirin chemoprevention at 161 mg daily is recommended for all carriers, based on emerging evidence, despite limited data on genotype‑specific efficacy.
open_questions: How will emerging blood‑based multi‑cancer early detection tests perform in Lynch syndrome carriers?
What are the optimal aspirin dosing and duration for each MMR genotype?
Can universal germline testing be operationalized in routine oncology practice to capture the ~1 million undiagnosed carriers?

talk_title: Precision modeling and targeted therapy testing in Fanconi anemia‑associated head and neck cancer

speaker.name: Unnamed Head & Neck Surgeon
speaker.affiliation: Columbia University, Department of Otolaryngology‑Head & Neck Surgery

tumor_types: head and neck squamous cell carcinoma

biomarkers.name: EGFR protein expression
biomarkers.finding: High EGFR expression correlated with strong cetuximab and dacomitinib sensitivity in PDX model #1

biomarkers.name: PIK3CA E545K mutation
biomarkers.finding: Model #3 harboring PIK3CA E545K responded to alpelisib; combination with cetuximab enhanced tumor regression

biomarkers.name: BCL2/BCLXL protein levels
biomarkers.finding: Model #2 showed high BCL2/BCLXL expression and was sensitive to venetoclax monotherapy

models.name: Fanconi anemia HNSCC PDX #3
models.genotype_or_key_features: PIK3CA E545K, phosphorylated AKT, FANCA deficiency

models.name: Fanconi anemia HNSCC organoid line (in development)

MOA_summary: Patient‑derived Fanconi anemia head‑and‑neck cancer xenografts retain the underlying DNA‑repair deficiency and display heterogeneous driver alterations; EGFR overexpression predicts response to cetuximab and dacomitinib, PIK3CA E545K predicts sensitivity to alpelisib (with additive effect when combined with EGFR blockade), and high BCL2/BCLXL expression confers venetoclax sensitivity, providing a precision‑medicine framework for this ultra‑rare population.
readouts: Western blot for phospho‑EGFR, phospho‑AKT, PARP
key_findings: Two of three Fanconi anemia HNSCC PDX models were the most platinum‑sensitive among >30 sporadic HNSCC models tested.
Model #1 (high EGFR/HER2/HER3) showed exquisite sensitivity to cetuximab and dacomitinib, with dose‑dependent tumor regression.
Model #3 (PIK3CA E545K) was resistant to cetuximab but responded to alpelisib; the combination of alpelisib plus cetuximab produced greater tumor shrinkage than either agent alone.
Model #2 (high BCL2/BCLXL) was resistant to EGFR inhibitors but displayed marked sensitivity to the BCL2 inhibitor venetoclax, suggesting a novel therapeutic avenue.
Heterogeneity across the three PDXs underscores the need for genotype‑guided therapy selection rather than a one‑size‑fits‑all approach in Fanconi anemia‑associated HNSCC.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Model #2 lacked EGFR expression and was intrinsically resistant to EGFR‑targeted antibodies and TKIs.
Model #3’s PIK3CA mutation conferred resistance to EGFR blockade but created a dependency on PI3K signaling.

combination_strategies.rationale: EGFR blockade in high‑EGFR expressing tumor
combination_strategies.phenotype: dose‑dependent tumor regression in PDX #1

combination_strategies.rationale: Target PIK3CA‑driven AKT activation
combination_strategies.phenotype: tumor shrinkage in PDX #3; enhanced when combined with cetuximab

combination_strategies.rationale: Inhibit BCL2‑mediated survival in BCL2‑high tumor
combination_strategies.phenotype: significant tumor regression in PDX #2

open_questions: Can low‑dose platinum regimens be safely administered to Fanconi anemia patients based on preclinical sensitivity?
What biomarkers beyond EGFR, PIK3CA, and BCL2 can predict response to targeted agents in this population?
How can these PDX‑guided therapeutic recommendations be translated into individualized clinical trials for ultra‑rare patients?

talk_title: Tobacco use trends and regulatory landscape

clinical_data.value: decreasing toward Healthy People 2030 goal of 5%

clinical_data.metric: youth cigarette use after Juul launch

MOA_summary: The speaker presented epidemiologic data on declining cigarette use, stable cigar use, and rising e‑cigarette use, highlighting the impact of FDA regulatory pathways (PMTA, MRTP) and the need for balanced risk‑benefit assessment of novel nicotine products.
key_findings: U.S. cigarette prevalence is trending downward toward the Healthy People 2030 target of 5%
Cigar use rates have remained flat over recent years
E‑cigarette use has steadily increased since market entry in 2009, both in the U.S. and globally
Youth cigarette use spiked after the introduction of Juul in 2014‑2015 before declining with subsequent regulations

talk_title: Lung cancer screening impact and policy gaps

speaker.name: Doug Wood
speaker.affiliation: University of Washington, Fred Hutch Cancer Research Center

clinical_data.population: high‑risk smokers screened with low‑dose CT

clinical_data.metric: potential lives saved with expanded criteria
clinical_data.population: U.S. adults if inclusion criteria broadened
lung cancer patients in IELCAP vs historical

MOA_summary: Low‑dose CT screening of high‑risk smokers reduces lung cancer mortality by 20% (NLST) and improves 5‑year survival dramatically; however, current policy limits uptake to ~19% of eligible individuals, suggesting that simplifying criteria and expanding eligibility could save tens of thousands of lives.
key_findings: NLST demonstrated a 20% mortality reduction with low‑dose CT in high‑risk smokers
Only 19% of eligible U.S. adults are currently screened, yet this still yields ~15,000 lives saved over five years
Modeling suggests expanding criteria could increase lives saved to ~90,000 in five years
Simplifying smoking metrics (duration vs pack‑years) improves equitable identification of high‑risk individuals
Current upper age limit of 80 and 15‑year smoking cessation rule exclude many who could benefit
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Stigma and nihilism limit lung cancer screening uptake
Complex inclusion criteria reduce access for minority and older populations

combination_strategies.rationale: easier identification of high‑risk individuals and more equitable screening
combination_strategies.phenotype: increased eligible screened population

combination_strategies.rationale: captures older high‑risk smokers currently excluded
combination_strategies.phenotype: potentially higher detection of early-stage disease

open_questions: What is the optimal balance between expanding eligibility and minimizing over‑screening harms?
How can policy effectively address stigma and nihilism to improve uptake?

talk_title: Screening test implementation and follow‑up gaps

speaker.name: Electra Paskett
speaker.affiliation: Ohio State University, Division of Cancer Prevention and Control

models.name: U.S. screening programs (breast, cervical, colorectal, lung)

clinical_data.population: U.S. women with abnormal cervical screen
U.S. individuals with positive stool test

MOA_summary: The presenter highlighted gaps between screening test availability and completion of the screening process, showing low follow‑up rates for abnormal results across breast, cervical, colorectal, and lung cancer screening, especially in underserved populations, and argued for policy changes to support navigation and coverage.
key_findings: U.S. screening uptake is high for breast (80%) but low for lung cancer (14%)
Timely follow‑up after abnormal screens ranges from 60% (cervical) to 66% (mammography) and is lower for lung (48‑58%) and FIT/FOBT (18‑57%)
Out‑of‑pocket costs for follow‑up (e.g., $800‑$1,600 for abnormal mammogram) hinder completion
Medicaid expansion improves screening rates and cancer survival in rural, high‑poverty areas
Implementation barriers, not just test availability, limit effective screening worldwide
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: High copays deter follow‑up after abnormal screens
Lack of health‑system tracking of test results impedes completion

combination_strategies.rationale: improve tracking and follow‑up of abnormal results
combination_strategies.phenotype: increase follow‑up rates to >80% in underserved settings

combination_strategies.rationale: increase access to screening and downstream care
combination_strategies.phenotype: higher screening uptake and improved survival

open_questions: How can reimbursement policies be restructured to eliminate copays for follow‑up care?
What health‑system designs best support real‑time tracking of screening results?

talk_title: NCI cancer prevention research pipeline and population impact

speaker.name: Phil Castle
speaker.affiliation: National Cancer Institute, Division of Cancer Prevention
