models.name: Luciana phase 3
models.genotype_or_key_features: claudin‑18.2 positive gastric cancer, ZOLPI + chemo + PD‑1

clinical_data.value: survival benefit leading to regulatory approval

MOA_summary: Zorvotaximab is an IgG1 monoclonal antibody that binds the extracellular loop 1 of claudin‑18.2 and mediates tumor cell killing primarily through ADCC and CDC; cerpituximab adds ADCP activity and can modulate the tumor microenvironment by increasing NK and macrophage infiltration and chemokine release, thereby enhancing T‑cell recruitment.
key_findings: Zorvotaximab monotherapy achieved <10% ORR but 30‑40% disease stabilization in claudin‑18.2 positive gastric cancer.
Randomized FASTO phase 2 showed improved PFS when Zorvotaximab was combined with fluoropyrimidine chemotherapy.
Global phase 3 trials demonstrated an overall survival benefit leading to regulatory approval of the Zorvotaximab‑chemo combination.
Cerpituximab monotherapy induced ADCP and increased tumor‑infiltrating NK cells, macrophages, and chemokines (CXCL9/10) in gastric cancer biopsies.
In pancreatic cancer, cerpituximab plus chemotherapy failed to improve OS despite 28% ORR, suggesting that subcellular localization and KRAS mutation status affect efficacy.
Steroid pre‑medication appeared to improve tolerability of cerpituximab without diminishing ADCP activity.
Claudin expression is heterogeneous and can be lost after chemotherapy in ~40% of patients, highlighting the need for dynamic biomarker monitoring.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Spatial heterogeneity of claudin‑18.2 expression leads to variable antibody access; peritoneal metastases may show reduced expression.
KRAS mutations correlate with cytoplasmic accumulation of claudin‑18.2, potentially reducing antibody efficacy in pancreatic cancer.
Long‑term exposure of claudin‑18.2 in normal gastric mucosa causes transient gastritis and GI toxicity.

combination_strategies.rationale: enhance tumor cell killing and improve PFS when combined with claudin‑targeted antibody
combination_strategies.phenotype: improved PFS and OS versus chemotherapy alone

combination_strategies.rationale: claudin‑targeted antibodies increase CD8 T‑cell infiltration, potentially synergizing with checkpoint blockade
combination_strategies.phenotype: higher response rates in high claudin‑expressing, PD‑L1 CPS ≥1 patients

combination_strategies.rationale: reduce infusion‑related GI toxicity without compromising ADCP activity
combination_strategies.phenotype: better tolerability, lower discontinuation rates

open_questions: What molecular mechanisms drive loss of claudin‑18.2 expression after chemotherapy or targeted therapy?
Can serum‑based methylation or exosome assays reliably substitute tissue IHC for patient selection?
How can we best sequence claudin‑targeted antibodies, ADCs, T‑cell engagers, and CAR‑T therapies to maximize efficacy?

talk_title: Translational rationale for claudin‑targeted ADCs and bispecific T‑cell engagers

targets.dependency_context: claudin‑18.2 high gastric cancer cell lines

models.name: claudin‑positive/negative mixing organoids
models.genotype_or_key_features: mixed claudin expression to assess bystander killing

MOA_summary: Claudin‑18.2 ADCs deliver high‑potency cytotoxic payloads (e.g., topoisomerase inhibitors) directly to antigen‑positive tumor cells, while bispecific T‑cell engagers (2+1 format) recruit CD3‑positive T cells to form immune synapses independent of HLA, enabling cytotoxicity even at moderate antigen density and providing bystander killing of neighboring claudin‑negative cells.
key_findings: ASP2138 ADC induced potent killing across gastric cancer cell lines with varying claudin‑18.2 density, though higher expression correlated with greater potency.
Claudin‑18.2 bispecific TCE showed cytotoxicity in vitro even at low antigen levels and mediated bystander killing in mixed claudin‑positive/negative organoid cultures.
Pre‑clinical data suggest that lower CD3 affinity bispecifics can retain efficacy while potentially reducing cytokine release syndrome risk.
Clinical data from an Astellas claudin‑18.2 ADC (phase 3) demonstrate activity comparable to HER2‑targeted ADCs, supporting the therapeutic window in gastric cancer.
Heterogeneous claudin expression (≈87% of gastric tumors) underscores the need for strategies such as dual‑antigen targeting or bystander effects to overcome antigen loss.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Potential on‑target resistance via mutations in the extracellular loop 1 of claudin‑18.2 could impair binding of ADCs/TCEs, analogous to GPRC5D resistance in myeloma.
Antigen density remains a determinant of TCE efficacy; very low claudin expression reduces cytotoxicity.

combination_strategies.rationale: chemotherapy may increase claudin surface exposure and modulate TME to enhance ADC/TCE activity
combination_strategies.phenotype: preclinical synergy observed in xenograft models

combination_strategies.rationale: TCE‑induced T‑cell activation could be amplified by checkpoint blockade
combination_strategies.phenotype: hypothesized increased tumor infiltration

open_questions: What is the frequency and functional impact of CLDN18 extracellular domain mutations in patients progressing on claudin‑targeted ADCs or TCEs?
Can dual‑antigen bispecific formats (e.g., claudin‑18.2 + CDH17) improve therapeutic index in heterogeneous gastric tumors?
How does baseline immune‑rich versus immune‑desert microenvironment influence response to claudin‑targeted TCEs?

talk_title: Claudin‑18.2 CAR‑T cell therapy in gastric and pancreatic cancer

targets.dependency_context: claudin‑18.2 high gastric or pancreatic tumors

biomarkers.name: Claudin‑18.2 IHC intensity
biomarkers.finding: High expression correlated with longer overall survival after CAR‑T (median OS 18 mo vs 5.5 mo for low expression)

biomarkers.name: CPP score (claudin‑18.2, PD‑1, PD‑L2 protein levels)
biomarkers.finding: Low CPP score associated with higher response rate and prolonged PFS

biomarkers.name: TZMK+ TPX+ T‑cell infiltration
biomarkers.finding: Patients with this immune signature had median OS 18 mo versus 5.5 mo

models.name: Phase 1 IIT claudin‑18.2 CAR‑T monotherapy cohort
Randomized controlled phase 2/3 claudin‑18.2 CAR‑T vs standard therapy

MOA_summary: Claudin‑18.2 CAR‑T cells are engineered to recognize the extracellular loop of claudin‑18.2, leading to antigen‑specific T‑cell activation, cytokine release, and tumor cell lysis; persistence is limited by rapid exhaustion, prompting strategies such as naive T‑cell enrichment, novel lymphodepletion, and repeat infusions to improve durability.
key_findings: Monotherapy claudin‑18.2 CAR‑T achieved a 55% ORR and ≥19% disease control rate in gastric cancer.
Sequential treatment (chemo → PD‑1 → CAR‑T) yielded an 80% ORR and 15‑month median PFS, suggesting benefit of tumor debulking before CAR‑T.
Randomized trial showed a four‑fold higher response rate and a two‑fold OS improvement versus standard therapy, reducing mortality risk by 63%.
High claudin‑18.2 expression, absence of liver/bone metastases, and a TZMK⁺/TPX⁺ immune‑rich microenvironment predicted long‑term responders.
Grade ≥3 gastric mucosal injury occurred in ~10% of patients but was manageable with PPIs and fasting; pulmonary toxicity was rare and linked to CRS.
Repeat CAR‑T infusion showed diminished expansion and persistence, highlighting the need for improved persistence strategies.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Rapid decline of CAR‑T cells 4‑8 weeks post‑infusion limits durability.
Tumor heterogeneity and loss of claudin‑18.2 after prior therapies can lead to intrinsic resistance.
Exhausted T‑cell phenotype in the tumor microenvironment may blunt CAR‑T efficacy.

combination_strategies.rationale: potential synergy by relieving T‑cell exhaustion
combination_strategies.phenotype: unexpectedly lower response rates in early cohort, possibly due to Treg activation

combination_strategies.rationale: enhance CAR‑T trafficking by disrupting stroma and creating space
combination_strategies.phenotype: improved early expansion and tumor shrinkage

combination_strategies.rationale: increase safety by requiring dual antigen expression
combination_strategies.phenotype: reduced gastric toxicity in preclinical models

open_questions: What biomarkers best predict durable CAR‑T persistence and long‑term survival in gastric cancer?
How can CAR‑T cell exhaustion be mitigated—e.g., via gene editing of inhibitory receptors or cytokine support?
What is the optimal sequencing of debulking chemotherapy, checkpoint blockade, and CAR‑T infusion to maximize efficacy?

talk_title: EHR-based phenotyping of cancer cachexia and identification of anatomical subtypes

speaker.name: Ed Reznik
speaker.affiliation: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Computational Oncology Lab

biomarkers.name: P53 mutation
biomarkers.finding: significant association with increased risk of cachexia (p<0.001) across diseases

biomarkers.name: Neutrophil to lymphocyte ratio (NLR)
biomarkers.finding: type A cachexia patients show marked increase in NLR during cachexia episode

biomarkers.name: Albumin
biomarkers.finding: albumin declines during cachexia, reaching hypoalbuminemia in type A patients

models.name: MSK EHR weight trajectory cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 60,000 patients with longitudinal weight data, 5,000 with paired pre/post CT

MOA_summary: Reznik's group defined cachexia windows using longitudinal weight loss criteria in EHR data, aligned these windows with serologic, imaging, and genomic measurements, and identified three CT-based anatomical subtypes (A, B, C) distinguished by organ volume changes and systemic inflammation, with TP53 mutations emerging as a genomic risk factor when modeled as a time-to-event.
key_findings: Implemented a 5% weight loss over 6‑month EHR algorithm to define cachexia windows in >60,000 cancer patients.
Found ~30% prevalence of cachexia episodes in melanoma and high prevalence in pancreatic cancer.
Identified three CT‑based cachexia subtypes: Type A (fat loss + spleen/liver enlargement), Type B (fat loss + organ atrophy and radiodensity increase), Type C (mixed).
Type A subtype associated with worst overall survival across diseases.
TP53 mutation is the most significant genomic correlate of cachexia risk when modeled as a time‑to‑event, validated in an independent cohort.
Type A patients exhibit rising NLR and dropping albumin during cachexia episodes, indicating systemic inflammation.
open_questions: What molecular mechanisms drive the divergent organ volume changes in cachexia subtypes?
Can early detection of the Type A inflammatory signature guide therapeutic intervention?

talk_title: Observational study of cachexia phenotypes using patient-reported outcomes and biomarkers

speaker.name: Richard Dunn
speaker.affiliation: University of Rochester Medical Center, GI Oncology

targets.dependency_context: elevated serum GDF15 in cachectic patients

biomarkers.name: GDF15
biomarkers.finding: higher levels in high symptom burden cachexia cluster

biomarkers.name: IL6
biomarkers.finding: elevated in high symptom burden cluster

biomarkers.name: IL8
biomarkers.finding: elevated in high symptom burden cluster

models.name: Lotus study
models.genotype_or_key_features: ≈1,000 patients with advanced lung, pancreatic, or colorectal cancer, longitudinal PROs, imaging, blood, and tumor tissue

clinical_data.population: advanced lung, pancreatic, colorectal cancer patients

clinical_data.value: shorter in high symptom burden cluster
clinical_data.n: subset of high symptom burden patients

MOA_summary: Dunn presented the design and early baseline findings of two prospective observational cohorts (Lotus and UNCOVER) that longitudinally collect patient-reported outcomes, body composition imaging, and blood/tumor biomarkers to identify cachexia phenotypes; preliminary symptom clustering revealed high‑symptom and low‑symptom groups, with the high‑symptom group showing elevated GDF15, IL‑6, and IL‑8 and worse early disease progression.
key_findings: Enrolled ~1,000 patients in Lotus (U Rochester) and ~800 in UNCOVER (Kaiser) to prospectively phenotype cachexia.
Baseline: >50% of patients already have ≥5% weight loss; pancreatic cancer patients show highest prevalence.
Symptom clustering identified three groups: low symptom burden (similar to weight‑stable), high symptom burden (fatigue, anorexia, emotional distress), and intermediate.
High symptom burden cluster associated with higher serum GDF15, IL‑6, and IL‑8 levels.
Patients in high symptom burden cluster had lower physical activity, higher appetite stimulant use, and higher 3‑month disease progression rates.
Preliminary data suggest that symptom clusters may correlate with body‑composition clusters (e.g., low symptom = minimal wasting).

combination_strategies.rationale: Targeting elevated GDF15 may improve weight and activity in a molecularly defined cachexia subtype
combination_strategies.phenotype: weight gain and increased physical activity in phase‑2 trial

open_questions: Do symptom‑based clusters align with the CT‑based anatomical subtypes described by Reznik?
Can early GDF15 elevation predict response to anti‑GDF15 therapy across tumor types?

talk_title: Prostaglandin E2–driven cachexia and immunotherapy resistance in lung cancer models

speaker.name: Dr. Gonsalves
speaker.affiliation: New York University School of Medicine, Cancer Immunology Lab

biomarkers.name: Prostaglandin E2 (PGE2)
biomarkers.assay: mass spectrometry of tumor lysate / BAL fluid
biomarkers.finding: significantly higher in cachectic mice and in BAL fluid of cachectic lung cancer patients

biomarkers.name: PD‑L1 expression
biomarkers.finding: high PD‑L1 tumors enriched in patients whose cachexia reversed with ICI therapy

models.name: KL mouse (KRAS^G12D; LKB1 loss)
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS activating mutation + LKB1 loss, develops lung tumors and cachexia in ~80% of mice

models.name: NYU advanced lung adenocarcinoma ICI cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: serial weight data, PD‑L1 status, outcomes

MOA_summary: Gonsalves demonstrated that elevated prostaglandin E2 production by tumor‑associated neutrophils and tumor cells drives cachexia and impairs anti‑PD‑L1 efficacy; CRISPR‑mediated knockdown of PTGES or PTGS2 reduces PGE2, delays weight loss in KRAS/LKB1 mouse models and C26 allografts, linking PGE2 to both wasting and immunotherapy resistance, with dysbiotic lung microbiota further amplifying neutrophil activation.
readouts: single‑cell RNA‑seq of tumor and BAL cells
key_findings: In KRAS/LKB1 (KL) lung cancer mice, ~80% develop cachexia with up to 20% weight loss; non‑cachectic littermates retain weight despite similar tumor burden.
Cachectic mice show higher tumor and serum IL‑6 family cytokines and enrichment of arginase‑positive macrophages and prostaglandin‑producing neutrophils.
Prostaglandin E2 levels are markedly increased in cachectic mouse tumors and in bronchoalveolar lavage fluid of cachectic lung cancer patients.
CRISPR knockdown of PTGES in KL tumors suppresses PGE2 and significantly delays cachexia onset.
CRISPR knockout of PTGS2 in C26 cachectic cells eliminates tumor PGE2 and protects mice from weight loss.
Lung tumor dysbiosis (higher 16S density) correlates with cachexia and neutrophil infiltration; introducing dysbiotic bacteria to non‑cachectic tumor‑bearing mice induces weight loss.
Clinical cohort analysis shows cachexia reversal after ICI correlates with high PD‑L1 expression and partial responses, whereas continued cachexia associates with progressive disease and early death.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Cachectic mice exhibit resistance to anti‑PD‑L1 therapy despite similar tumor burden.
Neutrophil‑derived PGE2 appears to mediate both muscle wasting and immune suppression.

combination_strategies.rationale: Reduce neutrophil numbers that produce PGE2 and suppress immunity
combination_strategies.phenotype: preclinical testing ongoing

combination_strategies.rationale: Block prostaglandin synthesis upstream of PGE2
combination_strategies.phenotype: potential to mitigate cachexia, but safety concerns noted

combination_strategies.rationale: Block central appetite suppression without systemic NSAID toxicity
combination_strategies.phenotype: preclinical proof‑of‑concept

open_questions: Will pharmacologic inhibition of PGE2 restore anti‑PD‑L1 efficacy in cachectic tumor models?
What is the relative contribution of tumor‑derived vs. neutrophil‑derived PGE2 to systemic wasting?
Can modulation of lung microbiota prevent neutrophil activation and cachexia development?

talk_title: Methodological considerations for incorporating real-world data into clinical trial design

speaker.name: Rebecca Hubbard
speaker.affiliation: Brown University, Carl Kawaja and Wendy Holcomb Professor of Public Health, Professor of Biostatistics and Data Science

clinical_data.value: inflated with full pooling and static borrowing
increased with full pooling but biased; restored with propensity weighting

MOA_summary: Hubbard presented a framework for hybrid control trial designs that augment internal control arms with external real-world data, comparing analysis strategies (full pooling, static borrowing, propensity score weighting) via simulations to assess bias, type I error, and power, highlighting propensity weighting as a method that controls bias while improving efficiency.
key_findings: Full pooling of external control data introduces substantial bias in treatment effect estimates.
Static borrowing reduces bias but still shows inflated type I error under strong confounding.
Propensity score weighting eliminates bias and restores type I error to nominal levels while providing a power boost.
Dynamic borrowing yields estimates close to trial-only results with minimal efficiency gain.
open_questions: How to ensure all relevant confounders are captured for propensity score adjustment in real-world datasets?
What are optimal methods for dynamic borrowing when external data quality varies?

talk_title: Real-world data curation from EHRs and applications to clinical trials

speaker.name: Neil Meropol
speaker.affiliation: Flatiron Health, Principal Investigator Elect of the ASCO TAPER study

models.name: Flatiron Health EHR-derived cohorts
models.genotype_or_key_features: various cancers, includes structured and unstructured data

MOA_summary: Meropol described methods for extracting structured variables from EHRs using human abstraction, machine learning, and large language models, presented quality assessment frameworks, and illustrated applications such as external control arm construction for the RESOLVE pancreatic trial and outcome analyses in NSCLC and bladder cancer.
key_findings: LLM-extracted progression dates showed high reliability compared to human abstraction across multiple tumor types.
External control arms built from multiple real-world data sources closely matched the RESOLVE trial control arm after propensity score adjustment.
In bladder cancer, patients receiving cisplatin off-label had better outcomes than those who did not.
In early-stage NSCLC, neoadjuvant immunotherapy improved distant metastasis-free survival, but additional adjuvant therapy did not show further benefit.
open_questions: How to standardize LLM validation across institutions for regulatory acceptance?
What are optimal strategies for handling missingness in key covariates during propensity score construction?

talk_title: Prospective real-world data registry embedded in an ECOG-ACRIN phase III trial

speaker.name: Peter O'Dwyer
speaker.affiliation: University of Pennsylvania, Medical Oncologist, co-chair of ECOG-ACRIN Research Group

models.name: ECOG-ACRIN EA5182
models.genotype_or_key_features: EGFR-mutant NSCLC, arms: osimertinib vs osimertinib + bevacizumab

models.name: Prospective real-world registry (PRECOG 1702)
models.genotype_or_key_features: Patients receiving same therapies outside trial, same eligibility criteria

MOA_summary: O'Dwyer outlined the design and early feasibility results of a prospective real-world data registry (PRECOG 1702) run in parallel with the ECOG-ACRIN phase III trial EA5182, demonstrating high data completeness (>85%) and rapid accrual, and discussed operational challenges such as site CRA engagement, IRB barriers, and EHR documentation gaps.
key_findings: Prospective real-world registry achieved >85% capture of predefined key variables after minimal data cleaning.
Accrual reached target of 25 patients/month after an initial slow start, now active at 40 institutions.
CRAs are critical for data collection; engagement and regular communication improve site performance.
Regulatory challenges include IRB exemptions for patient-reported outcomes and social determinants of health.
open_questions: How can EHR-integrated smart forms (e.g., Chart Hero, OncAssessment) be scaled across diverse health systems?
What regulatory pathways best accommodate prospective real-world registries as external controls?

talk_title: PACER method reveals TCL1A as a driver of clonal hematopoiesis and leukemia

biomarkers.name: IL6
biomarkers.assay: polygenic risk score (genetically predicted cytokine level)
biomarkers.finding: Modest association between genetically predicted IL6 levels and faster DNMT3A clone expansion

models.name: UK Biobank
models.genotype_or_key_features: large population with whole-genome sequencing, CHIP status, smoking data

models.name: TOPMed
models.genotype_or_key_features: GWAS of clonal expansion rate, identified rs2887399 in TCL1A promoter

models.name: TCL1A transgenic (H-11 locus) crossed with Cre strains
models.genotype_or_key_features: human TCL1A ORF expressed in HSCs, leads to HSPC expansion and 100% mortality by 12 months

MOA_summary: Jaiswal introduced the PACER (passenger approximated clonal expansion rate) method to infer clone age from passenger mutations, identified TCL1A promoter SNP rs2887399 as a regulator of clonal fitness, and demonstrated that ectopic TCL1A expression drives HSC expansion and myeloid leukemia in transgenic mice, establishing TCL1A as a causal driver of CHIP.
key_findings: PACER can quantify clonal expansion rates from bulk WGS data using passenger mutation counts.
Driver mutation identity accounts for most variance in clone fitness; splicing factor mutations (U2AF1, SRSF2, SF3B1) and JAK2 show highest fitness.
Smoking is the only measured environmental factor positively correlated with clonal expansion rate in humans.
GWAS identified rs2887399 in the TCL1A promoter; the T allele slows expansion of multiple CHIP drivers.
TCL1A is normally silent in HSCs; driver mutations de‑repress TCL1A, leading to HSC fitness advantage.
Transgenic mice with HSC‑specific TCL1A expression develop HSPC expansion and 100% mortality from myeloid leukemia by 12‑13 months.
Carriers of the protective T allele have reduced risk of myeloid malignancies, especially CMML.
open_questions: What cooperating mutations underlie the rapid leukemic transformation observed in TCL1A transgenic mice?
Is the narrow mortality window driven by a specific epigenetic state or additional genetic events?

talk_title: Phylogenetic and methylation mapping of somatic evolution in blood

speaker.name: Jyoti Nangalia
speaker.affiliation: Cambridge University Hospital & Wellcome Sanger Institute

targets.dependency_context: epigenetic silencing in blood and breast tissue

models.name: single-cell derived colonies (blood, bone marrow)
models.genotype_or_key_features: up to 1,200 colonies from healthy donors (age 17 weeks gestation to 77 years) and 6 hematologic cancer patients

models.name: UK Biobank & other cohorts
models.genotype_or_key_features: used for validation of clonal expansion rate and environmental associations

MOA_summary: Nangalia described a combined whole‑genome and whole‑methylome sequencing pipeline for single‑cell colonies, enabling reconstruction of somatic mutation phylogenies and assignment of CpG methylation changes to specific ancestral branches, revealing massive early‑life epiallelic remodeling and that driver mutations both shape and are shaped by these epigenetic landscapes, with some epigenetic changes acting as oncogenic drivers themselves.
key_findings: Developed an automated low‑input pipeline to obtain both WGS and whole‑genome methylomes from ~2 ng DNA per colony.
Mapped ~5,000 CpG‑segment epialleles that change early in embryogenesis (≈10–15 somatic mutations) with ~10,000‑fold higher rate than later life.
Identified ~4,000–5,000 early‑life CpG segments (mostly promoters) that acquire stable methylation states, many showing allele‑specific patterns beyond X‑inactivation.
Demonstrated that CHIP driver mutations (e.g., DNMT3A, TET2, 1q+, DNMT3A Y66F) are associated with increased numbers of epimutations per branch.
Showed that epigenetic silencing of one BRCA1 allele can occur pre‑gastrulation, creating a mosaic that predisposes to BRCA‑like breast cancer.
Found that somatic methylation changes can act as drivers, with certain embryonic epialleles preferentially selected by specific mutations.
Established that methylation trees can resolve polytomies where SNV trees cannot, providing finer temporal resolution of early clonal events.
open_questions: Which specific early‑life epialleles confer selective advantage to particular driver mutations?
Do epigenetic changes directly contribute to disease phenotypes beyond serving as lineage markers?

talk_title: Inflammatory and metabolic consequences of CHIP and therapeutic implications

speaker.name: Ben Ebert
speaker.affiliation: Dana‑Farber Cancer Institute, Harvard Medical School

targets.dependency_context: interaction with TET2 in myeloid cells

biomarkers.name: Clone VAF ≥10%
biomarkers.finding: Large clones (VAF ≥10%) confer highest risk of inflammatory disease and myeloid malignancy

biomarkers.name: RDW
biomarkers.finding: Higher RDW independently predicts progression to myeloid malignancy and overall mortality

models.name: Tet2 knockout bone‑marrow transplant into LDLR‑/‑ mice
models.genotype_or_key_features: Tet2‑/‑ hematopoietic cells increase atherosclerosis on high‑fat diet

models.name: Tet2 knockout exposed to cigarette smoke
Tet2 knockout on NASH diet
models.genotype_or_key_features: Accelerated liver fibrosis and reticulin staining

models.name: UK Biobank (≈500,000 participants)
models.genotype_or_key_features: CHIP status, genotype, outcomes, risk‑score development

clinical_data.value: significant increase vs non‑CHIP (exact HR not quoted)

MOA_summary: Ebert showed that loss of TET2 in hematopoietic cells reprograms lipid metabolism, leading to accumulation of triglycerides, diacylglycerols and cholesterol esters, which drives heightened IL‑1β‑mediated inflammation in atherosclerosis, COPD, NASH and other age‑related diseases; genetic screens identified ACLY and OGT as key mediators, and pharmacologic inhibition of lipid pathways normalizes the inflammatory phenotype.
readouts: CRISPR in vivo screen with barcoded guides
clinical risk‑score modeling (age, VAF, genotype, RDW, MCV, cytopenias)
key_findings: CHIP carriers have increased risk of atherosclerosis, myocardial infarction, heart failure, arrhythmias, liver disease, kidney disease, COPD, osteoporosis and lung cancer.
TET2 loss leads to lipid droplet accumulation in neutrophils/macrophages, driving IL‑1β‑dependent inflammation.
In vivo CRISPR screen in Tet2‑deficient mice identified ACLY and OGT as essential for the inflammatory phenotype; knockout of these genes restores wild‑type inflammation levels.
TET2 directly binds OGT; loss of TET2 liberates OGT, causing aberrant protein O‑GlcNAcylation and metabolic dysregulation.
A simple risk score incorporating genotype, clone size, RDW, MCV, cytopenias and age stratifies CHIP individuals into low, intermediate and high risk for myeloid malignancy and overall mortality.
High‑risk CHIP (~1% of population) has ~50% overall survival, whereas low‑risk/CHIP‑negative individuals have ~95% survival.
IL‑1β inhibition (e.g., canakinumab) reduces lung cancer incidence, suggesting inflammatory CHIP contributes to early tumorigenesis.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Tet2‑deficient cells show selective advantage only under inflammatory or metabolic stress (high‑fat diet, cigarette smoke, NASH diet).
ACLY is essential for all cells, making selective targeting of Tet2‑driven inflammation challenging.

combination_strategies.rationale: Block downstream inflammatory cytokine mediating CHIP‑driven disease
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced atherosclerosis and lung cancer incidence in preclinical models and human trials

combination_strategies.rationale: Prevent lipid droplet accumulation in Tet2‑deficient myeloid cells
combination_strategies.phenotype: Restores wild‑type inflammatory gene expression profile in vitro and in mice

open_questions: Can selective ACLY inhibition be achieved in hematopoietic cells without systemic metabolic toxicity?
What are the long‑term effects of chronic IL‑1β blockade in CHIP carriers without overt disease?
How do specific CHIP genotypes (e.g., splicing factor vs DNMT3A) differ in their metabolic rewiring?

talk_title: What Do We Do Now That We've Machine Learned Everything?

speaker.name: Jeffrey Leake
speaker.affiliation: Fred Hutchinson Cancer Center; Synthesize Bio (co‑founder, board member)

MOA_summary: Leake outlined the current landscape of AI applications across oncology drug development, emphasizing the distinction between supervised and generative models, the regulatory uncertainty surrounding generative AI, and the need for robust benchmarks, representation, and guardrails to ensure safe, responsible deployment.
key_findings: AI is now embedded in >1,500 FDA regulatory submissions over the past decade, with oncology accounting for ~27% of AI‑containing submissions.
The FDA received 307 AI‑related oncology submissions in 2024 alone, reflecting rapid growth.
Four major impact areas for AI in oncology were identified: care/operations, drug discovery, drug development, and consumer health.
Generative AI models present greater regulatory challenges than supervised models due to broader use cases and lack of clear pathways.
Benchmarking, representation across diverse populations, and external validation were highlighted as critical gaps to address.

talk_title: AI in Pharma R&D: Regulatory Perspectives and Guardrails

speaker.name: George Visfilo
speaker.affiliation: AstraZeneca, Chief of AI for Science Innovation

MOA_summary: Visfilo described AstraZeneca's experience with AI‑enabled pathology devices, the three classes of AI‑driven biomarkers (replication, inference, outcome prediction), and advocated for third‑party benchmarking, clear guardrails, and high levels of evidence to ensure safe clinical deployment.
key_findings: AstraZeneca achieved the first Class 2 FDA clearance for an AI pathology device in 2021 and a breakthrough device designation for an AI‑derived predictive biomarker for an ADC.
Three AI biomarker classes were defined: Class A (task replication), Class B (inferred biomarkers), Class C (prognostic or predictive outcomes).
For Class C2 (predictive of therapeutic response), prospective trial validation is required; explainability is not mandatory if performance is demonstrated.
Emphasis on multi‑stakeholder partnerships (academia, industry, payers, regulators, patients) to set evaluation standards and avoid a “Wild West” scenario.

talk_title: FDA Frameworks for AI in Oncology Drug Development

speaker.name: Jeevan Puthenvedu
speaker.affiliation: Office of Oncologic Diseases, CDER, FDA; Oncology Center of Excellence AI Program

MOA_summary: Puthenvedu presented the FDA’s risk‑based credibility framework for AI models used to support regulatory decisions in drug and biologic development, outlining the seven‑step process, the distinction between AI used for data generation versus decision support, and the 2025 draft guidances on model risk, lifecycle maintenance, and international harmonization.
key_findings: The FDA received >1,000 AI‑containing submissions (CDER + CBER) to date, with oncology representing ~27% of AI submissions.
The draft guidance (Jan 2025) introduces a seven‑step risk‑based credibility assessment, calibrated by model influence and decision consequence.
High‑risk use cases (e.g., AI‑driven inpatient monitoring decisions) require extensive validation, documentation, and early FDA engagement.
AI models that meet the statutory device definition are subject to separate AI‑enabled device guidance (Jan 2025).
Key regulatory principles: methodological transparency, external validation on representative data, alignment with Good Machine Learning Practices, and early sponsor‑FDA communication.

talk_title: DPP4 as a novel checkpoint for CD8 T cells in glioblastoma

biomarkers.name: DPP4 expression in CD8 T cells
biomarkers.finding: Higher DPP4 levels correlate with terminal exhaustion phenotype

biomarkers.name: TOX expression
biomarkers.finding: TOX upregulated in exhausted CD8 T cells; reduced after DPP4 inhibition

models.name: GPR model
models.genotype_or_key_features: human GBM cells implanted in mouse brain

clinical_data.comparator: standard of care without DPP4 inhibitors

clinical_data.population: orthotopic GBM mice treated with sitagliptin

MOA_summary: DPP4 acts as an immunometabolic checkpoint on CD8 T cells; its inhibition by sitagliptin restores mitochondrial respiration and glycolysis, enhances proliferation, cytotoxic mediator production, and degranulation, partly via up‑regulation of GAD1‑mediated TCA cycle flux, leading to improved anti‑GBM activity.
key_findings: Retrospective analysis of Medicare data showed GBM patients on DPP4 inhibitors had significantly longer survival than matched controls.
CD8 T cells in human GBM express high DPP4, especially terminally exhausted (TOX‑high) subsets.
Pharmacologic or genetic DPP4 inhibition increased CD8 proliferation, cytokine production, and cytotoxicity in vitro.
DPP4 inhibition up‑regulated mitochondrial respiratory capacity and glycolysis, linked to GAD1 activation.
In orthotopic GBM mouse models, sitagliptin prolonged survival, an effect abolished by CD8 depletion.
Global DPP4 knockout mice recapitulated the pharmacologic phenotype, confirming target specificity.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: DPP4 inhibition had minimal impact on myeloid chemotaxis function, suggesting cell‑type selective effects.

combination_strategies.rationale: DPP4 inhibition restores T cell function that may synergize with PD‑1 blockade
combination_strategies.phenotype: potential additive tumor control (not yet tested)

open_questions: What are the long‑term effects of chronic DPP4 inhibition on T cell memory formation?
Can on‑demand DPP4 inhibition be achieved to avoid systemic metabolic effects?

talk_title: Molecular mechanisms of differentiation therapy and arsenic in acute promyelocytic leukemia

clinical_data.population: APL patients treated with arsenic alone

MOA_summary: ATRA binds the RARα moiety of the PML‑RARα fusion, converting it from a transcriptional repressor to an activator, while arsenic binds a cysteine triad in the PML moiety, inducing SUMO‑dependent ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation of PML‑RARα and restoration of PML nuclear bodies, leading to p53 activation, senescence, and terminal differentiation of leukemic promyelocytes.
key_findings: ATRA alone induces differentiation but rarely cures APL; arsenic alone cures ~2/3 of patients; the combination cures virtually all patients.
Both agents directly degrade the PML‑RARα oncoprotein via distinct ubiquitin‑proteasome pathways (UBR5 for ATRA, SUMO‑dependent ligase for arsenic).
Restoration of PML nuclear bodies after treatment correlates with p53 activation and loss of self‑renewal, essential for cure in mouse models.
Mutations in the arsenic‑binding cysteine triad confer resistance, confirming the binding site’s importance.
PML is required for response to conventional chemotherapy in non‑APL leukemias, indicating a broader role in therapy response.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Arsenic‑resistant APL patients harbor mutations in the PML cysteine triad that prevent arsenic binding.
FLT3 activation influences retinoic acid response but not the ATRA‑arsenic combination.

combination_strategies.rationale: Both agents degrade PML‑RARα via complementary mechanisms, synergizing to restore nuclear bodies and activate p53.
combination_strategies.phenotype: dramatic tumor burden reduction and cure in mouse models; near‑100% cure in patients

open_questions: What downstream retinoic acid target genes enforce self‑renewal in APL?
Can the PML‑senescence hub be exploited to sensitize other cancers to therapy?

cited_papers: Lococo et al. historical APL trial

talk_title: Apoptotic priming as a determinant of chemotherapy sensitivity across solid and liquid cancers

biomarkers.name: BH3 profiling priming score
biomarkers.finding: Higher priming correlates with faster CA125 normalization and improved PFS in ovarian cancer

models.name: DepMap panel
models.genotype_or_key_features: various solid and hematologic cancer lines

models.name: CLL, follicular lymphoma, AML primary specimens
pediatric rhabdomyosarcoma (neonatal vs adult satellite cells)

clinical_data.value: significant in pediatric rhabdomyosarcoma, none in adult

MOA_summary: Differential apoptotic priming, measured by BH3 profiling, determines how readily cancer cells undergo mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization upon chemotherapy; hematologic and pediatric CNS cancers are highly primed and thus chemo‑sensitive, whereas most solid tumors are unprimed and require inhibition of multiple BCL‑2 family members to trigger death.
key_findings: Blood cancers and testicular germ cell tumors are intrinsically more primed for apoptosis than most solid tumors, correlating with clinical chemosensitivity.
Baseline BH3 profiling priming scores predict faster CA125 normalization and longer PFS in ovarian cancer patients.
CRISPR knockout of Bax/Bak in HeLa cells reduces priming and confers broad chemoresistance, confirming causality.
Solid tumor cell lines require simultaneous inhibition of multiple pro‑survival BCL‑2 proteins (BCL‑XL, MCL‑1) for cell death, unlike hematologic lines.
Neonatal muscle‑derived rhabdomyosarcomas retain higher Bax/Bim levels, are more primed, and respond better to vincristine; BCL‑XL degrader DT2216 synergizes to improve response.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Adult rhabdomyosarcoma shows low priming and resistance to vincristine alone.
BCL‑2 family redundancy (upregulation of MCL‑1/BCL‑XL) limits efficacy of single BCL‑2 inhibitors.

combination_strategies.rationale: Sensitize low‑primed tumors by removing a key pro‑survival protein
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced vincristine response in adult rhabdomyosarcoma mouse model

open_questions: What molecular determinants beyond cell‑of‑origin set the basal priming level in different tissues?
Can dynamic changes in priming after radiation or targeted therapy be leveraged for adaptive treatment scheduling?

talk_title: NF1 as a microtubule‑repair factor and predictor of maytansinoid ADC efficacy in HER2‑positive breast cancer

biomarkers.name: NF1 mutation status
biomarkers.finding: NF1‑mutated patients showed significantly longer progression‑free survival on T‑DM1 (p<0.01, n=34)

models.name: HER2‑positive breast cancer lines (WT and NF1‑KO)
NSG mice bearing WT or NF1‑KO tumors
34 HER2‑positive breast cancer patients
models.genotype_or_key_features: NF1 mutation vs wild‑type, treated with T‑DM1

models.name: neonatal vs adult muscle satellite cell‑derived rhabdomyosarcoma

clinical_data.population: NF1‑mutated HER2‑positive breast cancer patients on T‑DM1

MOA_summary: NF1 binds directly to polymerized microtubules, accelerates tubulin polymerization and promotes self‑repair of intra‑microtubule damage; loss of NF1 impairs this repair, creating more drug‑binding sites for maytansinoid payloads (DM1) on exposed protofilament ends, thereby sensitizing NF1‑deficient cells to T‑DM1 ADCs.
readouts: co‑sedimentation and EM of NF1‑microtubule complexes
thermal shift assay for DM1 binding
key_findings: NF1 loss does NOT affect Ras signaling in this context but dramatically increases sensitivity to the maytansinoid ADC T‑DM1.
Re‑expression of wild‑type NF1 rescues mitotic timing defects and reduces DM1 sensitivity, confirming specificity.
NF1 directly binds microtubules and accelerates polymerization; in vitro, NF1 counteracts DM1‑induced polymerization block.
NF1‑deficient cells display impaired microtubule self‑repair, leading to increased DM1 binding sites and heightened cytotoxicity.
Clinical cohort analysis shows NF1‑mutated HER2‑positive breast cancer patients have significantly longer PFS on T‑DM1.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: NF1 loss creates a vulnerability to maytansinoid payloads but may also increase genomic instability (higher aneuploidy scores).
Ras‑GAP deficient NF1 mutants still bind microtubules but fail to promote repair, separating Ras and microtubule functions.

combination_strategies.rationale: To determine if the payload or antibody component drives differential sensitivity
combination_strategies.phenotype: No differential effect; sensitivity linked to DM1 payload

open_questions: Which NF1 domains mediate microtubule binding versus Ras GAP activity?
Can NF1 expression levels (beyond mutation) serve as a biomarker for maytansinoid ADC response?
Does p53 status modulate the relative contribution of NF1’s Ras versus microtubule functions in therapy response?

patent_or_ip_hints: Potential IP on NF1‑microtubule interaction modulators

session_title: Contextual Challenges in Blood-Based Early Cancer Detection Tests
talk_title: Statistical considerations for early cancer detection test performance
MOA_summary: Bob Smith explained that sensitivity and specificity for single-cancer early detection tests differ from multi-cancer tests, highlighted the concept of program sensitivity, and argued that MCEDs have lower aggregate sensitivity but broader public health impact, emphasizing the need for contextual interpretation of performance metrics.
key_findings: Program sensitivity can equalize comparisons between frequent low-cost tests and less frequent high-cost tests.
Sensitivity and specificity can move together when test quality improves, contrary to the assumed inverse relationship.
MCED aggregate sensitivity is lower than single-cancer tests, but modeling suggests annual MCED screening could prevent 17% of cancer deaths.
Stage shift is a blunt endpoint; within-stage tumor size changes can drive survival benefits.
False positive rates for MCEDs are often mischaracterized; a 1% rate reflects the complement of PPV at biopsy.

session_title: Contextual Challenges in Blood-Based Early Cancer Detection Tests
talk_title: Defining target condition and cancer signal origin in MCED tests
MOA_summary: Nima Visvade argued that the target condition for MCEDs should be defined by clinically actionable cancer signal origin (CSO), emphasizing biologic parity and diagnostic pathway grouping to improve workup efficiency and clinical utility.
key_findings: Target condition for MCEDs is inseparable from cancer signal origin, which guides diagnostic pathways.
Methylation‑based MCEDs provide accurate CSO predictions that are clinically useful for directing workup.
Biologic families (e.g., HPV‑related tumors) may be more appropriate grouping than strict organ boundaries.
Pathway‑based grouping aligns workup burden with clinical benefit, allowing a single imaging study to evaluate multiple CSOs.
High CSO accuracy transforms MCEDs from vague screening signals to actionable diagnostic starting points.

session_title: Contextual Challenges in Blood-Based Early Cancer Detection Tests
talk_title: Generalizability and implementation considerations for MCED and single‑cancer tests
MOA_summary: Asma Shukat highlighted how trial enrichment (age, stage, risk) inflates performance metrics of early detection tests and advocated for age‑sex adjustment, detailed subgroup reporting, and likelihood ratios to improve generalizability to real‑world populations.
key_findings: Trial populations often enrich for older age, higher stage, or known cancers, leading to inflated sensitivity and PPV.
Healthy‑volunteer bias can produce lower prevalence than intended use, underestimating PPV.
Age and sex standardization to the US census modestly shifts sensitivity and specificity, as shown in the PREEMPT CRC cohort.
Granular reporting of performance by cancer type, stage, lesion size, and advanced precursor lesion subcategories is needed but limited by power.
Likelihood ratios and number‑needed‑to‑screen are valuable metrics for clinicians to assess post‑test probability.

talk_title: Consumer wearable data for functional monitoring and outcome prediction in cancer patients

speaker.name: Charissa
speaker.affiliation: University of Pittsburgh and UPMC Hillman Cancer Center

models.name: Fitbit chemotherapy cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 162 patients, any solid tumor, stage IV 2/3, median age 60

models.name: Fitbit surgical peritoneal cancer cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 54 patients undergoing cytoreductive surgery with HIPEC

clinical_data.metric: Peak gait cadence threshold for fall risk

MOA_summary: The study demonstrates that continuous consumer‑wearable metrics (step count, peak gait cadence, activity fragmentation) can be reliably collected during active cancer treatment and are associated with clinical outcomes such as readmission, fall risk, and overall survival, providing prognostic information beyond clinician‑rated performance status.
key_findings: Patients undergoing chemotherapy wore Fitbits ≥8 h/day on 70% of study days over 90 days, with no decline over time.
Older patients showed higher adherence than younger patients; non‑white participants had significantly lower adherence.
Higher inpatient step counts after cytoreductive surgery (≈1,250 steps/day) were associated with lower 60‑day readmission versus 500 steps/day in readmitted patients.
Peak gait cadence ≥100 steps/min predicted reduced fall risk in older cancer survivors and differentiated fallers in ROC analysis.

In a 2.5‑year follow‑up of 213 chemotherapy patients, peak gait cadence and activity fragmentation remained independent predictors of overall survival after adjusting for age, stage, cancer type, ECOG, and step count.
Clinicians reported increased confidence in decision‑making when provided with wearable‑derived data alongside standard clinical information.
open_questions: How early can gait cadence or activity fragmentation metrics provide actionable warnings for falls or clinical deterioration?
What strategies can improve wearable adherence among non‑white and cognitively impaired patients?

talk_title: Wearable sensors and AI for digital trials across disease domains

models.name: COVID‑19 early detection study
models.genotype_or_key_features: 40,000+ participants, remote recruitment, wearable RHR monitoring

models.name: Detect Ahead trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: ≈450 participants randomized to wearable‑alert vs symptom‑only arm

models.name: Pre‑diabetes multimodal digital trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: 1,000 participants, CGM, wearable, EHR, saliva, stool

MOA_summary: By leveraging large‑scale passive wearable data (resting heart rate, activity, sleep) combined with AI algorithms, the presented digital trials demonstrate early detection of infectious disease, risk stratification for pre‑diabetes, and the feasibility of fully remote, multimodal clinical studies across diverse health conditions.
key_findings: A wearable‑based algorithm achieved AUC 0.8 for early COVID‑19 detection in a 40,000‑person cohort.
In the Detect Ahead trial, delivering wearable‑generated alerts did not significantly increase testing adherence, highlighting behavioral barriers to action.
A multimodal classifier integrating wearable, CGM, EHR, saliva, and stool data distinguished normoglycemic from type 2 diabetic individuals with AUC 0.96.
The same classifier generated a continuous risk score that refined risk stratification among pre‑diabetic participants beyond HbA1c alone.
Remote digital trials enrolling up to 1,000 participants were executed without in‑person visits, demonstrating scalability of fully virtual study designs.
open_questions: What is the optimal timing and modality for delivering actionable alerts to maximize participant compliance?
How can wearable‑derived signals be harmonized across device brands to enable broader population coverage?

talk_title: Integrating wearable sensor data and AI into oncology practice

models.name: Fitbit lung cancer remote monitoring cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: patients receiving PD‑1 inhibitor at home, telemedicine visits

models.name: Multimodal cancer outcome prediction cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: integrated wearable, PRO, lab, imaging data across several tumor types

clinical_data.metric: Remote patient monitoring billing rate in Medicare cancer patients
Weight loss in breast cancer patients using AI‑driven coaching
AUC for predicting urgent care visits using multimodal data

MOA_summary: Wearable‑derived continuous physiologic streams (steps, heart rate, sleep) combined with AI models can augment traditional oncology assessments, improving prediction of adverse events, treatment toxicity, and health‑care utilization, while highlighting the need for patient adherence and health‑system integration.
key_findings: Only <1% of Medicare cancer patients had billed remote patient monitoring codes, indicating poor system adoption.
AI‑driven coaching achieved a 5% body‑weight reduction in breast cancer patients, but overall adherence to wearable wear time declined over study duration.
Multimodal models integrating wearable, PRO, lab, and imaging data reached AUC 0.86 for predicting urgent care visits, outperforming EHR‑only models.
Physician‑patient performance‑status assessments show high variability; AI analysis of movement videos can provide more objective scores.
Patients receiving subcutaneous PD‑1 therapy at home showed higher compliance with vital‑sign uploads than with step‑count data, underscoring task‑specific adherence challenges.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Clinician alert fatigue and lack of reimbursement hinder integration of continuous data into workflows.
Patient digital literacy and device ownership disparities limit enrollment of underserved populations.
open_questions: How can health‑system IT and reimbursement structures be aligned to support scalable wearable integration?
What are the optimal AI‑derived thresholds and time windows for actionable alerts without causing alert fatigue?

talk_title: AI‑Guided Discovery and Targeting of Plexin B2 in Circulating Tumor Cell Clusters

targets.dependency_context: CTC clusters in triple‑negative breast cancer

biomarkers.name: PLEXIN B2 expression
biomarkers.finding: High plexin B2 in CTC clusters correlates with reduced distant metastasis‑free survival (HR 1.8, p=0.02)

models.name: BALB/c spontaneous metastasis model
models.genotype_or_key_features: 4T1 breast cancer cells, plexin B2 knockout vs control

MOA_summary: AI‑driven ranking (R‑score) identified plexin B2 as a top adhesion molecule driving homotypic and heterotypic CTC cluster formation; neutralizing antibodies block plexin B2, reducing cluster size and number, decreasing lung metastasis in mice, and correlating with improved patient outcomes.
key_findings: R‑score prioritized plexin B2 among 30 adhesion proteins as the top CTC‑cluster driver.
Genetic knockout of plexin B2 in 4T1 cells reduces lung micro‑metastases by ~70% (p<0.001).
Anti‑plexin B2 antibodies decrease both homotypic tumor‑tumor and heterotypic tumor‑myeloid clusters in vitro.
Single‑dose anti‑plexin B2 treatment in BALB/c mice cuts metastatic burden by 60% (p<0.01).
High plexin B2 expression in patient CTC clusters associates with worse distant metastasis‑free survival.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Antibody cross‑reacts with mouse plexin B2, enabling preclinical testing; no off‑target toxicity observed in normal tissues.

combination_strategies.rationale: Potential additive effect by reducing CTC‑mediated dissemination
combination_strategies.phenotype: Preliminary in vivo data suggest enhanced tumor control (not yet quantified)

open_questions: Can anti‑plexin B2 therapy overcome resistance to existing ADCs in patients with low nectin‑4?
What is the durability of the anti‑cluster effect after repeated dosing?

talk_title: SWALL‑1 Controls Epigenetic Methylation, Mitochondrial Metabolism, and Antitumor Immunity in IDH‑Mutant Gliomas

biomarkers.name: SWELL1 expression
biomarkers.finding: IDH‑mutant gliomas show ~1‑fold higher SWELL1 mRNA vs wild‑type (TCGA).

biomarkers.name: D‑2HG extracellular level
biomarkers.finding: SWELL1 knockout reduces extracellular D‑2HG by ~45% in vitro.

MOA_summary: SWELL1 forms the LRRC8A‑C VRAC complex that mediates passive export of the oncometabolite D‑2HG from IDH‑mutant glioma cells; loss of SWELL1 reduces extracellular D‑2HG, enhances cGAS‑STING activation, increases CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration, and suppresses tumor proliferation via hyper‑methylation of MYC/SOX2 pathways and impaired mitochondrial respiration.
key_findings: SWELL1 knockdown decreases extracellular D‑2HG release by ~40% without affecting LDH release.
LRRC8C knockdown reduces D‑2HG release ~25%; LRRC8D knockdown increases release, indicating subunit specificity.
SWELL1‑KO IDH‑mut glioma xenografts show prolonged mouse survival (median 45 vs 30 days, p=0.004).
SWELL1 loss leads to increased microglial CD86⁺ activation and CD8⁺ T‑cell IFN‑γ⁺ frequency.
SWELL1‑KO cells accumulate intracellular D‑2HG (~2‑fold) and display increased H3K27me3 and DNA hyper‑methylation, down‑regulating MYC and SOX2.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: SWELL1 is ubiquitously expressed; tumor specificity derives from reliance on D‑2HG export in IDH‑mut context.

combination_strategies.rationale: SWELL1 loss enhances antitumor immunity via cGAS‑STING
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor control in orthotopic models (not yet quantified)

open_questions: Will pharmacologic inhibition of VRAC recapitulate genetic SWELL1 loss?
Can SWELL1 expression serve as a biomarker for response to IDH inhibitors?

talk_title: Lineage Plasticity Drives Antibody‑Drug Conjugate Resistance in Bladder Cancer

biomarkers.name: NECTIN‑4 expression
biomarkers.finding: High NECTIN‑4 tumors have median EV treatment duration 9.2 months vs 3.1 months for low‑expressors (p=0.001).

biomarkers.name: ASCL1 activity
biomarkers.finding: ASCL1 overexpression suppresses NECTIN‑4 transcription in HT1376 cells (−70% mRNA, p<0.01).

models.name: 300‑patient bladder tumor cohort (MSK)
models.genotype_or_key_features: Urothelial vs variant histology, NECTIN‑4 IHC

MOA_summary: Lineage plasticity driven by ASCL1/NEUROD1 reprogramming suppresses NECTIN‑4 expression, rendering enfortumab vedotin ineffective; variant histology tumors up‑regulate alternative surface antigens such as DL3, offering new ADC targets for combination therapy.
key_findings: ASCL1/NEUROD1 overexpression in HT1376 cells reduces NECTIN‑4 mRNA by 70% and abolishes EV‑mediated cytotoxicity.
Spatial transcriptomics shows NECTIN‑4 loss confined to neuroendocrine regions, while DL3 is up‑regulated there.
Patients with NECTIN‑4‑low variant histology have significantly shorter EV treatment duration.
Proposed combination of NECTIN‑4 ADC with DL3‑targeted ADC could address heterogeneous antigen expression.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Plasticity‑mediated antigen loss is reversible in vitro upon ASCL1 knockdown, suggesting epigenetic regulation.

combination_strategies.rationale: Compensate for NECTIN‑4 loss in neuroendocrine/sarcomatoid subclones
combination_strategies.phenotype: Predicted synergistic tumor control in mixed‑histology models

open_questions: Can epigenetic drugs restore NECTIN‑4 expression in resistant tumors?
What is the optimal sequencing of dual‑ADC therapy to prevent clonal escape?

talk_title: eIF4A1 Inhibition Suppresses AML via Serine Biosynthesis Blockade

targets.dependency_context: AML cells with high eIF4A1 expression
AML cells sensitized by eIF4A1 inhibition

biomarkers.name: PHGDH expression
biomarkers.finding: eIF4A1 knockout reduces PHGDH mRNA by 65% (p<0.001).

biomarkers.name: Serine levels
biomarkers.finding: Intracellular serine drops 45% after zoledronate treatment.

MOA_summary: Zoledronate inhibits eIF4A1, destabilizing YB‑1‑bound mRNAs such as PHGDH, leading to reduced serine biosynthesis, impaired oxidative phosphorylation, and AML cell death; combined with MCL1 inhibition yields synergistic cytotoxicity.
key_findings: eIF4A1 is the most overexpressed translation initiation factor in AML vs normal hematopoietic cells.
eIF4A1 knockout prolongs survival in immunocompetent AML mouse model (median 60 vs 38 days, p=0.003).
Zoledronate kills AML cell lines with nanomolar IC50 while sparing normal CD34⁺ cells.
PHGDH down‑regulation mediates serine depletion; overexpression rescues growth inhibition.
CRISPR screen identifies YB‑1 and MCL1 as top sensitizers; zoledronate + MCL1 inhibitor shows >2‑fold synergy (CI<0.5).
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Zoledronate shows minimal toxicity to normal hematopoietic cells; selectivity linked to AML‑specific eIF4A1 dependence.

combination_strategies.rationale: MCL1 knockdown sensitizes AML to eIF4A1 inhibition
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic reduction of AML viability (CI 0.4)

combination_strategies.rationale: YB‑1 is a co‑factor for eIF4A1‑mediated mRNA stability
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced AML cell death in vitro

open_questions: Will zoledronate retain selectivity in a broader panel of normal tissues in vivo?
Can eIF4A1 inhibition overcome resistance to standard AML therapies (e.g., venetoclax)?

talk_title: Targeting Rho1/2 to Overcome ADC Resistance in Triple‑Negative Breast Cancer

biomarkers.name: RHO2 expression
biomarkers.finding: 70% of HR‑negative breast cancers express surface RHO2 by CITE‑seq.

models.name: RHO2+ sorted cells
models.genotype_or_key_features: 5,000 cells injected generate tumors; RHO2‑ cells do not

MOA_summary: Anti‑RHO1/2 antibodies bind surface oncogenic embryonic proteins re‑expressed in TNBC stem‑like cells, suppressing stemness signatures, NF‑κB signaling, and NRF2‑driven antioxidant response, thereby sensitizing cells to the redox‑modulating agent APR‑246.
key_findings: RHO2+ sorted TNBC cells generate tumors at 5,000 cells; RHO2‑ cells fail to engraft.
Anti‑RHO2 treatment reduces NF‑κB p‑p65 and NQO1 levels in vitro.
Combination of anti‑RHO2 antibody with APR‑246 lowers APR‑246 IC50 by 2.5‑fold in TNBC cell lines.
In vivo, anti‑RHO2 alone modestly slows tumor growth; combined with APR‑246 yields significant tumor regression (p<0.01).
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: RHO1/2 are embryonic proteins absent in adult normal tissues, suggesting a favorable therapeutic window.

combination_strategies.rationale: RHO2 blockade lowers NRF2 activity, increasing ROS susceptibility to APR‑246
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced tumor regression in TNBC PDX (p<0.01)

open_questions: What is the ligand (if any) that drives RHO1/2 signaling in TNBC?
Can anti‑RHO antibodies be humanized for clinical development?

talk_title: Potentiating venetoclax response in AML with the NAMPT inhibitor OT-82

models.name: primary AML cells from 10 adult patients

MOA_summary: OT-82 inhibits NAMPT, reducing NAD biosynthesis and creating a metabolic vulnerability in venetoclax‑resistant AML cells; combined with venetoclax it restores apoptotic sensitivity and yields durable tumor regressions in diverse AML PDX models.
key_findings: OT-82 showed the lowest LC50 among tested AML drugs across 10 adult AML samples, with a wide therapeutic window versus normal bone marrow cells.
In pediatric AML PDXs (PRR485, AML49), OT-82 alone extended event‑free survival, and the combination with venetoclax further improved survival without added benefit from azacitidine.
Across 16 heterogeneous AML PDXs, the venetoclax + OT‑82 combination produced the most robust and durable responses, achieving maintained complete remissions.
Response was observed irrespective of adverse genomic features (TP53, FLT3, KMT2A rearrangements), suggesting broad applicability.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: No significant toxicity observed in normal bone marrow cells at concentrations lethal to AML cells.
Three PDXs were non‑responsive, all also venetoclax‑resistant, indicating possible intrinsic resistance mechanisms.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target metabolic dependency (NAMPT) to overcome venetoclax resistance mediated by apoptotic rewiring.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression and increased event‑free survival in AML PDXs.

combination_strategies.rationale: Standard venetoclax‑based regimen for AML.
combination_strategies.phenotype: No additional benefit over venetoclax + OT‑82 in PDX models.

open_questions: What molecular biomarkers predict response to OT‑82‑based combinations in patients?
Can OT‑82 be safely combined with venetoclax in early‑phase clinical trials?

talk_title: Non‑canonical CAR T cell states associated with durable responses in multiple myeloma

targets.dependency_context: multiple myeloma patients receiving BCMA CAR T

biomarkers.name: FLC levels
biomarkers.finding: Transient responders showed rising FLC at 6‑12 mo, durable responders maintained low FLC

models.name: 22 MM patients receiving 4 different BCMA CAR T products
models.genotype_or_key_features: baseline and serial PBMC/bone marrow samples

models.name: CAR T infusion with longitudinal sampling

clinical_data.value: median >12 mo for durable responders, <12 mo for transient responders
clinical_data.population: multiple myeloma receiving BCMA CAR T

clinical_data.value: significantly higher %CAR T cells at ≥3 mo in durable responders
higher in TR CAR T cells, especially CD8 compartment

MOA_summary: Single‑cell multi‑omics of BCMA CAR T therapy revealed that durable responders harbor proliferative, early‑memory CAR T cells and a unique ‘T persistor’ state marked by AP‑1 and NF‑κB activation, low exhaustion, and bone‑marrow residency, which correlates with sustained CAR T persistence and improved clinical outcomes.
key_findings: Durable responders showed enrichment of proliferating and early‑memory CAR T cells early post‑infusion, whereas transient responders displayed higher CD8 exhaustion signatures.

A novel ‘T persistor’ CAR T state emerged at later time points in durable responders, characterized by high AP‑1, NF‑κB, cytokine (IFN‑γ, TNF‑α, IL‑2) expression and CXCR4/CD69 bone‑marrow residency markers.
Clonal tracking demonstrated that T persistor cells arise from multiple early CAR T states, including both CD4 and CD8 subsets.
Effective CAR T therapy reshaped the bone‑marrow microenvironment, reducing immunosuppressive monocyte signatures in durable responders.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Transient responders exhibited persistent CD8 exhaustion signatures and reduced CAR T persistence.
T persistor cells displayed low exhaustion despite long‑term residence, suggesting a selective survival program.

combination_strategies.rationale: Enhance CAR T infiltration by modulating fibroblast‑derived TGF‑β
combination_strategies.phenotype: Increased CAR T circulation and tumor clearance in resistant PDX model

open_questions: What upstream signals drive differentiation into the T persistor state?
Can pharmacologic modulation of AP‑1/NF‑κB pathways enhance CAR T durability?

talk_title: KatTCIP molecular glue recruiting P300/CBP to BCL6 induces death in DLBCL

biomarkers.name: BCL6 expression level
biomarkers.finding: Cell lines with highest BCL6 were most sensitive to TCIP3 (IC50 0.8 nM)

models.name: DLBCL cell line xenograft in mice
sheep red blood cell immunized mice
models.genotype_or_key_features: GC B cells enriched for BCL6

clinical_data.value: dose‑dependent loss up to near‑elimination at 5 mg/kg BID

MOA_summary: TCIP3 is a bivalent molecular glue that simultaneously binds the BCL6 BTB domain (via BI‑3812) and the P300/CBP bromodomain (via GNE‑781), inducing a cooperative ternary complex that recruits acetyltransferase activity to BCL6 loci, leading to localized histone and BCL6 acetylation, transcriptional activation of cell‑death genes, and rapid apoptosis of BCL6‑dependent DLBCL cells.
key_findings: TCIP3 forms a cooperative ternary complex between BCL6 and P300/CBP, confirmed by ITC and X‑ray crystallography.
TCIP3 induces potent GFP reporter activation and apoptosis in high‑BCL6 DLBCL lines with an IC50 of 0.8 nM, outperforming BCL6 degraders.
ChIP‑seq shows selective recruitment of p300 and increased H3K27ac at BCL6 target sites without global acetylation loss.
In vivo, TCIP3 depletes germinal‑center B cells and drives near‑complete tumor regression in a DLBCL xenograft model with minimal toxicity.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity driven by BCL6 expression; negative control compounds lacking either binder showed no activity.
No global HAT inhibition observed, indicating target‑specific action.

combination_strategies.rationale: Compare efficacy of different transcriptional co‑activator recruitment
combination_strategies.phenotype: Similar potency; BRD4 TCIP induces faster cell death

open_questions: Can KatTCIP be adapted to other oncogenic transcription factors?
What is the durability of response and potential resistance mechanisms in vivo?

talk_title: FGFR1 inhibition remodels fibroblast‑derived TGF‑β niche to enhance CAR‑T efficacy in DLBCL

biomarkers.name: MCP chemokines (CCL2/7/8)
biomarkers.finding: Up‑regulated in CAFs after FGFR1 inhibition, correlating with macrophage infiltration

clinical_data.value: significant reduction with FGFR1 inhibitor + CAR‑T vs CAR‑T alone
marked increase in blood and spleen with combination therapy

MOA_summary: FGFR1 inhibition in cancer‑associated fibroblasts suppresses TGF‑β production and induces MCP chemokine secretion, leading to recruitment of macrophages and enhanced infiltration of CAR‑T cells; the remodeled microenvironment synergizes with anti‑CD19 CAR‑T to eradicate resistant DLBCL in vivo.
key_findings: FGFR1 inhibition reduced CAF‑derived TGF‑β and simultaneously increased CCL2/7/8 expression, driving macrophage recruitment.
Macrophage depletion partially reversed the anti‑lymphoma effect of FGFR1 inhibition, indicating a contributory role of macrophages.
Combination of FGFR1 inhibitor with anti‑CD19 CAR‑T markedly increased circulating CAR‑T cells and eliminated tumor burden in a resistant DLBCL PDX model.
FGFR1 inhibition did not impair normal hematopoiesis in treated mice, suggesting a therapeutic window.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: FGFR1 is selectively expressed on CAFs in the DLBCL niche, limiting off‑target effects.
No overt toxicity observed in normal tissues.

combination_strategies.rationale: Improve CAR‑T infiltration by alleviating CAF‑mediated immunosuppression
combination_strategies.phenotype: Complete tumor clearance and increased CAR‑T persistence

open_questions: Will FGFR1 inhibition synergize with CAR‑T in other hematologic malignancies?
What biomarkers predict patients who will benefit from CAF‑targeted therapy?

talk_title: Condensate dynamics drive adaptive METTL3 inhibitor resistance

targets.dependency_context: AML and solid tumor cells dependent on m6A
cells with adaptive resistance to METTL3 inhibition

biomarkers.name: YTHDC1 protein level
biomarkers.finding: Up‑regulated after METTL3 inhibitor treatment in AML and melanoma models

models.name: AML (MOLM‑13, etc.), pancreatic cancer, melanoma lines

clinical_data.value: synergistic reduction with YTHDC1 inhibitor + METTL3 inhibitor
85 days vs 50 days with combo vs METTL3 inhibitor alone

MOA_summary: METTL3 inhibition reduces m6A on oncogenic transcripts (MYC, BCL2) but induces adaptive up‑regulation and nuclear condensate formation of the m6A reader YTHDC1; gel‑like YTHDC1 condensates sequester residual m6A‑modified RNAs, restoring translation of survival genes and conferring resistance, which can be overcome by YTHDC1 inhibition or venetoclax co‑treatment.
key_findings: CRISPR screens identified YTHDC1 as a top sensitizer to METTL3 inhibition; its loss synergizes with METTL3 inhibitors in AML and melanoma.
METTL3 inhibition enlarges YTHDC1 nuclear condensates, shifting them to a less liquid, gel‑like state correlated with resistance.
Overexpression of YTHDC1 or PABPN1 disrupts condensate dynamics and confers resistance; YTHDC1 inhibition restores sensitivity.
Combining a YTHDC1 inhibitor or venetoclax with METTL3 inhibition yields strong synergistic anti‑tumor effects with minimal impact on normal CD34+ cells.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Resistance linked to YTHDC1 condensate dynamics rather than genetic mutations.
Normal hematopoietic CD34+ cells remain sensitive to METTL3 inhibition, indicating a therapeutic window.

combination_strategies.rationale: Disrupt adaptive YTHDC1 condensates that rescue MYC/BCL2 translation
combination_strategies.phenotype: Restores METTL3 inhibitor efficacy in resistant models

combination_strategies.rationale: Target BCL2 downstream of YTHDC1‑mediated rescue
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression and extended survival

open_questions: What are the molecular constituents of the gel‑like YTHDC1 condensates?
Can biomarkers of condensate dynamics predict patient response to METTL3 inhibitors?

talk_title: Lymphotoxin‑α induces TRAF2 degradation and necroptotic death in AML

biomarkers.name: TRAF2 protein level
biomarkers.finding: TRAF2 is repressed in >70% of AML patient samples across subtypes

models.name: FLT3‑ITD driven AML (retroviral transplant) with TNF/LTα knockout

clinical_data.value: no leukemia development up to 300 days with LTα treatment

MOA_summary: Recombinant lymphotoxin‑α engages TNFR1 on AML cells, leading to depletion of the pro‑survival adaptor TRAF2, which shifts TNFR1 signaling toward necroptotic complex formation and cell death, while sparing normal hematopoietic progenitors that retain higher TRAF2 levels.
key_findings: Genetic deletion of LTα in FLT3‑ITD AML mice accelerates disease onset, indicating a tumor‑suppressive role for LTα.
Exogenous LTα induces rapid death of primary AML blasts, including high‑risk TP53‑mutated samples, and promotes differentiation (loss of CD34, gain of CD38).
LTα treatment eradicates leukemia in both syngeneic and immunodeficient PDX models without causing cytopenia or impairing normal hematopoiesis.
TRAF2 is markedly down‑regulated in AML patient samples across genetic subtypes, rendering leukemic cells uniquely sensitive to LTα‑mediated necroptosis.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity derives from low TRAF2 in AML versus normal cells; normal hematopoiesis retains TRAF2 and is resistant to LTα‑induced death.

combination_strategies.rationale: Potential additive cytotoxicity via distinct death pathways
combination_strategies.phenotype: Not yet tested

open_questions: Can LTα be engineered for improved pharmacokinetics and reduced immunogenicity?
Will chronic LTα exposure affect immune homeostasis?

talk_title: Integrating multi-omics and novel modalities to map EGFR TKI resistance in NSCLC

speaker.name: Daniel S.W. Tan
speaker.affiliation: University of California, San Francisco, Department of Oncology

targets.dependency_context: TKI‑treated NSCLC with high CD47 expression

biomarkers.name: EGFR ctDNA persistence
biomarkers.finding: Patients with persistent EGFR ctDNA after third‑generation TKI had shorter PFS (median 5.2 mo vs 9.8 mo) in retrospective cohort

biomarkers.name: cMET amplification
biomarkers.finding: Detected in 15‑20% of resistant biopsies; co‑targeting yielded partial responses in 4/9 evaluable patients

biomarkers.name: P53 co‑mutation
biomarkers.finding: Associated with median PFS 4.1 mo vs 7.6 mo in EGFR‑mutant cohort (HR 0.62, p=0.03)

biomarkers.name: Stromal TME signature (activated neutrophils, CAFs)
biomarkers.finding: High signature score correlated with 2‑year relapse rate 48% vs 22% (p=0.004)

models.name: NSCLC EGFR‑TKI resistance cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: EGFR mutant, pre‑ and post‑treatment biopsies, n=36

models.name: Persistent EGFR ctDNA + chemotherapy trial (North America)
models.genotype_or_key_features: EGFR mutant NSCLC with ctDNA persistence, randomized 1:1, n=112

clinical_data.metric: Patients with no known resistance mechanism

MOA_summary: Tan described how sequential EGFR TKIs select for on‑target (e.g., C797S) and off‑target alterations (MET amplification, lineage plasticity) and how multi‑omics (DNA, RNA, spatial transcriptomics, proteomics) can map these states. He showed that ctDNA persistence, MET amplification, and stromal TME signatures predict resistance, and that rational combinations (e.g., EGFR TKI + MET inhibitor, EGFR TKI + chemotherapy, anti‑CD47) can be matched to specific resistance phenotypes.
key_findings: EGFR TKIs achieve high initial ORR (60‑80%) but 50‑60% of patients on frontline third‑gen inhibitors lack any detectable resistance mechanism by NGS.
On‑target resistance mutations (e.g., C797S) occur in 10‑25% of resistant biopsies, while MET amplification is found in 15‑20% and predicts response to MET inhibitors (4/9 partial responses).
Persistent EGFR ctDNA after TKI therapy identifies a high‑risk subgroup with median PFS 5.2 mo vs 9.8 mo (p<0.01).
Spatial transcriptomics revealed CD47 up‑regulation in on‑treatment lesions, suggesting a combinatorial anti‑CD47 strategy.
Stromal‑rich TME signatures (activated neutrophils, CAFs) correlate with 2‑year relapse rates of 48% vs 22% (p=0.004), defining a poor‑risk population for upfront combination trials.
Retrospective analysis of RET inhibitor trials showed ORR >30% and median PFS 18.4 mo, yet phase III trials were terminated due to enrollment challenges.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Fourth‑generation EGFR TKIs produce deep ctDNA reductions but limited radiographic responses, indicating that target inhibition alone may not overcome lineage plasticity.
Lineage transition to neuroendocrine‑like phenotype observed in resistant single‑cell profiles, suggesting a non‑genetic resistance route.

combination_strategies.rationale: Add cytotoxic pressure to eradicate clones with persistent EGFR ctDNA after TKI
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved PFS in ctDNA‑positive subgroup (median 7.4 mo vs 5.2 mo)

combination_strategies.rationale: Target MET amplification as a bypass driver in EGFR‑TKI resistant disease
combination_strategies.phenotype: Partial responses in 44% of MET‑amplified patients

combination_strategies.rationale: Combine with osimertinib to suppress concurrent RET rearrangements
combination_strategies.phenotype: Objective responses observed in case series (n=3)

combination_strategies.rationale: Counteract CD47 up‑regulation identified by spatial transcriptomics in on‑treatment lesions
combination_strategies.phenotype: Preclinical synergy with EGFR TKI (tumor regression p<0.01)

open_questions: What is the optimal molecular threshold for defining MET amplification as a therapeutic target?
Can ctDNA persistence be validated as a surrogate endpoint for early efficacy in phase II trials?
How can real‑world molecular databases be leveraged to generate robust external controls for rare‑driver trials?

talk_title: Eco-evolutionary lens for understanding the tumor microenvironment

speaker.name: Ken Pienta
speaker.affiliation: Johns Hopkins University, Cancer Ecology Center

MOA_summary: Pienta presented an eco‑evolutionary framework in which tumors are viewed as ecosystems that undergo niche construction, creating steep fitness gradients (hypoxia, acidosis, immune pruning) that accelerate clonal selection and drive convergent lethal phenotypes such as metastasis and therapy resistance. He emphasized that cytokine‑driven “cancer swamp gas” (e.g., IL‑6, TNF‑α, MMPs) from the microenvironment underlies systemic lethal effects.
key_findings: Convergent evolution in cancer leads to two lethal phenotypes: metastasis and therapy resistance.
Ecological engineering by cancer cells (allogenic and autogenic) remodels the tumor microenvironment, accelerating clonal dominance (e.g., 73% reduction in barcode diversity in vivo vs. in vitro).
Steep fitness gradients from hypoxia, acidosis, and immune pruning create rapid clonal selection, observable across precancerous lesions to metastatic disease.
Cytokine overproduction (IL‑6, TNF‑α, MMPs) from stromal and immune cells constitutes a “miasma” that drives cachexia, thrombosis, and organ failure, accounting for mortality beyond tumor mass.
Integrating evolutionary ecology principles could enable new therapeutic timing and selective pressure strategies to disrupt the tumor ecosystem.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Niche construction creates feedback loops that increase clonal selection pressure, contributing to therapy resistance.
Cytokine‑mediated miasma illustrates systemic resistance mechanisms beyond cell‑intrinsic pathways.
open_questions: How can we quantitatively model ecological inheritance and its impact on mutation rates?
What therapeutic interventions can safely alter the selective landscape of the tumor microenvironment?

talk_title: AI‑enabled virtual patient modeling and tumor microenvironment analysis
MOA_summary: Poon described three AI‑derived capabilities—microscope, crystal ball, and wind tunnel—to decode tumor microenvironment complexity. He highlighted AI‑driven virtual trial simulation (TrialScope) that reproduces KEYNOTE‑10 outcomes, a pathology foundation model (GigaPath) that predicts EGFR mutation from H&E across 41 cancer types, a modality translator (GigaTime) that synthesizes spatial proteomics from routine slides, and a large‑scale virtual patient model (Curiosity) trained on 300 M patients and 100 B medical events that matches supervised baselines on 78 clinical tasks.
key_findings: TrialScope AI can simulate a virtual trial that reproduces the KEYNOTE‑10 pembrolizumab results, demonstrating feasibility of real‑world data‑driven trial emulation.
GigaPath transformer model scales to gigapixel whole‑slide images and learns a pan‑cancer signature across 41 tumor types, enabling EGFR mutation prediction directly from H&E with high accuracy.

GigaTime translates cheap H&E pathology into virtual multiplex immunofluorescence (MIF), generating over 300 000 virtual MIF maps from 14 000 patients and revealing spatial proteomic patterns linked to outcomes.
Curiosity, a 1‑billion‑parameter foundation model trained on 300 M patient journeys (100 B events), matches or exceeds 78 supervised clinical task models without task‑specific fine‑tuning.
AI can automate extraction of eligibility windows from unstructured clinical notes, reducing abstraction cost from hours to seconds and enabling 24/7 trial feasibility monitoring.
open_questions: How to quantify and communicate the precision and uncertainty of virtual trial outcomes to regulators and clinicians?
What methods can robustly control for confounding in observational real‑world data used to train virtual patient models?
How should privacy, re‑identification risk, and potential misuse of virtual patient predictions be governed?

talk_title: Single-cell clonal analysis of normal skin and cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma evolution

speaker.name: Hunter Shane
