Somatic mosaicism (e.g., ERCC2, ATRX) was detected in young patients with multiple primary tumors, underscoring the need for comprehensive genomic profiling.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: HER2 extracellular domain mutations in bladder cancer confer resistance to neratinib compared to kinase domain hotspots in breast cancer.
KRAS co‑mutations in HER2‑mutant colorectal cancer limit neratinib efficacy.

combination_strategies.rationale: enhance tumor kill in HER2‑high solid tumors
combination_strategies.phenotype: synergistic tumor regression in HER2‑high lung cancer (case report)

combination_strategies.rationale: potentially improve durability in NTRK‑fusion melanomas
combination_strategies.phenotype: not yet tested

open_questions: How can RNA‑based biomarkers be standardized for basket trial eligibility across institutions?
What is the prevalence and clinical impact of somatic mosaicism in young‑onset cancers?

talk_title: Birth cohort analysis of early-onset cancer incidence across multiple tumor types

speaker.name: Hyuna Sung
speaker.affiliation: American Cancer Society, Senior Principal Scientist

MOA_summary: Using age‑period‑cohort modeling of national cancer registry data, the speaker demonstrated generational shifts in incidence, showing that cohorts born after 1955 have ~50% higher risk of colorectal cancer and similar increases for ER‑positive breast and endometrial cancers, while other cancers show period effects; disparities by education and geography were highlighted.
key_findings: Over 9,000 people under age 50 are diagnosed with cancer worldwide each day, with >3,000 deaths.
People born in 1990 have ~1.5‑fold higher incidence of small intestinal, thyroid, kidney, and pancreatic cancers compared with those born in 1955.
Cohort rate ratio for colorectal cancer indicates a ~50% higher lifetime risk for those born in the 1980s versus the 1950s.
Early‑onset colorectal cancer mortality is higher among men with lower education, with the gap widening over 30 years.
In high‑income countries, early‑onset cancer rates rise while older‑adult rates fall; in Latin America and Asia, rates rise in both age groups.
Lung cancer incidence declines in younger male cohorts but plateaus or rises in younger female cohorts, reflecting tobacco‑control disparities.

talk_title: Metabolic dysfunction and early‑onset colorectal cancer: epidemiology to prevention

speaker.name: Andrew Chan
speaker.affiliation: Massachusetts General Hospital, Gastroenterology and Epidemiology

MOA_summary: Large prospective cohorts (NHS1, NHS2, HPFS) were leveraged to link obesity, early‑life adiposity, ultra‑processed food intake, and hyperinsulinemia to increased incidence of early‑onset colorectal adenomas and cancer, and a single‑arm tirzepatide trial is underway to test reversal of metabolic risk.
key_findings: Mid‑life obesity (BMI ≥30) confers a 2‑fold higher risk of early‑onset colorectal cancer compared with non‑obese individuals.
BMI at age 18 in the overweight range is associated with a 1.6‑fold increased risk of early‑onset colorectal cancer.
Maternal weight gain >14 kg during pregnancy is linked to a ~2‑fold higher colorectal cancer risk in offspring.
Highest quintile of ultra‑processed food intake is associated with a 1.5‑fold higher incidence of colorectal adenoma.
Predicted hyperinsulinemia (C‑peptide‑based lifestyle index) shows a 1.3‑fold increased early‑onset adenoma risk, especially in later birth cohorts.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target metabolic dysfunction and obesity to reduce adenoma recurrence
combination_strategies.phenotype: single‑arm trial assessing adenoma burden and epigenetic biomarkers

combination_strategies.rationale: Anti‑inflammatory prevention of colorectal neoplasia
combination_strategies.phenotype: well‑established risk reduction in younger adults

open_questions: Do early‑life exposures (e.g., in utero obesity) leave lasting epigenetic scars that predispose to cancer?
Will modulation of ultra‑processed food intake in childhood reduce adenoma incidence?

cited_papers: Du et al. 2024 Cancer Prev Res

talk_title: Early‑life mutagenesis by colibactin‑producing bacteria drives early‑onset colorectal cancer

speaker.name: Ludmil Alexandrov
speaker.affiliation: University of California, San Diego, Professor of Molecular Medicine

models.name: 1,000 colorectal cancers (11 countries)
models.genotype_or_key_features: including 102 early‑onset cases, MSI status, pks colonization data

MOA_summary: Whole‑genome sequencing of 1,000 colorectal cancers revealed enrichment of colibactin‑associated signatures (SBS88, ID18) in early‑onset cases; phylogenetic timing shows these mutations arise within the first five years of life, often generating early driver events such as APC mutations, implicating early‑life exposure to pks+ bacteria as a causal factor.
key_findings: Colibactin signatures (SBS88/ID18) are present in ~50% of patients <40 y versus ~17% of patients >70 y.
Early‑onset tumors show a three‑fold enrichment of colibactin signatures compared with late‑onset tumors.
Phylogenetic analysis of normal crypts indicates colibactin exposure occurs within the first 0‑5 years of life.
In >25% of colibactin‑positive early‑onset tumors, the APC driver mutation bears the colibactin signature.
pks colonization is not required to be detectable at tumor sampling, suggesting a past transient exposure.
open_questions: Has the prevalence of early‑life colibactin exposure increased over recent decades?
Can case‑control studies of normal pediatric colon tissue confirm higher mutational burden in future early‑onset cases?

talk_title: Postpartum breast cancer: involution biology and liver metastasis risk

speaker.name: Pepper Shedden
speaker.affiliation: Oregon Health & Sciences University, Professor of Cancer Biology

models.name: post‑weaning involution model
models.genotype_or_key_features: nulliparous vs. involuting mammary gland; liver involution

models.name: OHSU young‑onset breast cancer registry
models.genotype_or_key_features: ER‑positive, postpartum vs. nulliparous, metastasis outcomes

clinical_data.value: ≈30% of postpartum metastases vs. <10% in nulliparous

MOA_summary: Weaning‑induced mammary gland involution creates a wound‑healing, immunosuppressive microenvironment that promotes tumor cell shedding and metastasis; concurrent liver involution generates a pre‑metastatic niche that preferentially supports colonization of ER‑positive tumor cells, explaining the high liver metastasis rate in postpartum breast cancer.
key_findings: Women diagnosed with breast cancer within 5 y postpartum have a 2‑3‑fold worse overall survival than nulliparous peers, independent of ER status.
Postpartum tumors show a distinct gene expression signature matching mouse mammary involution (immune suppression, fibroblast activation, ECM deposition).
Postpartum breast cancers are enriched for liver metastases (≈30% of metastatic sites) compared with nulliparous cases.
Weaning‑induced liver involution in mice creates a fibroblast‑rich, immunosuppressive niche that enhances tumor cell engraftment after portal vein injection.
Human liver metastases from postpartum patients often display replacement growth, integrating into existing hepatic architecture without inducing new angiogenesis.

combination_strategies.rationale: Counteract involution‑associated immunosuppression
combination_strategies.phenotype: potentially restore CD8+ T‑cell activity in postpartum tumors

combination_strategies.rationale: Target fibroblast‑mediated ECM remodeling during involution
combination_strategies.phenotype: reduce metastatic niche formation

open_questions: Can pharmacologic modulation of mammary or liver involution pathways reduce postpartum metastasis?
What are the specific molecular drivers of the replacement growth pattern in liver metastases?

session_title: El Camino de la Pediatria: Finding Our Way Through the Chasm of Pediatric Oncology Drug Development
talk_title: Bridging Science and Market Access in Pediatric Oncology

speaker.name: Sam Blackman
speaker.affiliation: Google Ventures, Entrepreneur in Residence; Pediatric Neuro-Oncologist

models.name: FDA pediatric oncology approvals (2008‑2025)
models.genotype_or_key_features: all pediatric solid and hematologic malignancies

MOA_summary: Blackman presented a macro‑level analysis of pediatric oncology drug development, comparing the 6‑8% overall success rate of drug candidates to the 6‑8% completion rate of the Camino pilgrimage, and highlighted recent acceleration in FDA approvals driven by incentives, while emphasizing the need for faster trial activation and better market‑access pathways for agents such as the DRD2 antagonist dordivaprone in diffuse midline glioma.
key_findings: Pediatric oncology drug development success mirrors the 6‑8% completion rate of the Camino pilgrimage.
Exponential growth in FDA approvals over the past decade, with 8‑9 agents approved per year and 10‑14 new indications/label expansions annually.
Dordivaprone (ONC201) received FDA approval for progressive diffuse midline glioma in patients >1 year, based on a pooled analysis of 376 patients showing a 22% response rate.
Only two pediatric‑only EMA approvals and five FDA approvals were recorded for the 2025 cohort, underscoring persistent regulatory gaps.

session_title: El Camino de la Pediatria: Finding Our Way Through the Chasm of Pediatric Oncology Drug Development
talk_title: ADC Landscape and Emerging Small‑Molecule Opportunities in Pediatric Oncology

speaker.name: Beth Fox
speaker.affiliation: St. Jude Children’s Research Hospital, Senior Vice President for Clinical Research

targets.dependency_context: infant AML with CBF‑GLIS fusion, age ~2 y
cancer‑associated fibroblasts in pediatric solid tumors

biomarkers.name: HER2 IHC/cell‑surface expression
biomarkers.finding: 50% of screened osteosarcoma patients >12 y expressed HER2; 1/9 enrolled had prolonged stable disease

biomarkers.name: Folate receptor alpha expression
biomarkers.finding: High FRα in rare pediatric AML; 3/10 children treated with SUTRO‑002 achieved complete responses

clinical_data.metric: objective response rate in osteosarcoma trastuzumab deruxtecan

MOA_summary: Fox outlined the current ADC pipeline for children, highlighting successful CD30‑targeted brentuximab vedotin, the limited pediatric activity of folate‑receptor‑α ADCs (e.g., SUTRO‑002 achieving 30% CRs in a small AML cohort), and the challenges of HER2‑directed ADCs in osteosarcoma where low antigen expression limited efficacy, while also introducing emerging targets such as STEAP2 and B7‑H3 for future pediatric ADC development.
key_findings: Only 2 FDA pediatric‑only approvals (EMA 2, FDA 5) despite 712 registered ADC trials worldwide.
For solid‑tumor ADCs, no actively accruing pediatric trials were identified as of April 2024.
SUTRO‑002 treated 10 children with infant AML; 3 achieved complete responses, demonstrating feasibility of pediatric ADCs.
Trastuzumab deruxtecan screened 50 osteosarcoma patients, 9 enrolled; 1 DLT and 1 prolonged stable disease observed, insufficient to proceed to stage 2.
Emerging targets STEAP2 and B7‑H3 are being prioritized for future pediatric ADC programs.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Hepatotoxicity and myelosuppression observed with inotuzumab highlight the need for payload optimization for pediatric use.

combination_strategies.rationale: SUTRO‑002 showed synergy as a bridge to transplant in infant AML
combination_strategies.phenotype: 3/10 CRs when combined with chemotherapy backbone

open_questions: Can expanded‑access data be systematically leveraged to accelerate pediatric ADC approvals?
What regulatory pathways can support rapid pediatric trial activation for solid‑tumor ADCs?

session_title: El Camino de la Pediatria: Finding Our Way Through the Chasm of Pediatric Oncology Drug Development
talk_title: Precision Immunotherapy: CAR T Cells and Bispecific Antibodies in Pediatric Cancer

speaker.name: Ted Leach
speaker.affiliation: Children's Hospital of Philadelphia, Associate Professor of Pediatrics

targets.dependency_context: hepatoblastoma and other pediatric solid tumors

biomarkers.name: CD19 expression
biomarkers.finding: required for enrollment in blinatumomab and tisagenlecleucel trials

biomarkers.name: MRD negativity
biomarkers.finding: high rates observed in venetoclax + chemotherapy AML trial

clinical_data.metric: blinatumomab CR rate in phase 1/2 R/R ALL

MOA_summary: Leach reviewed the rapid clinical impact of CD19‑directed bispecific blinatumomab and CAR‑T (tisagenlecleucel) in pediatric B‑ALL, achieving up to 96% disease‑free survival when moved to frontline, and discussed next‑generation CAR‑T strategies (humanized, dual‑target CD19/CD22, armored constructs) and bispecifics (DLL3‑targeted tarlatamab) aimed at overcoming antigen escape and extending efficacy to solid tumors such as neuroblastoma and diffuse midline glioma.
key_findings: Blinatumomab achieved 27/70 CRs in relapsed/refractory pediatric ALL and improved DFS/OS in the randomized ALL 1331 trial.
Frontline blinatumomab (ALL 1731) raised 5‑year DFS to 96% versus 88% with chemotherapy alone.
Tisagenlecleucel produced an 81% remission rate and ~60% 3‑year OS in heavily pre‑treated pediatric B‑ALL.
Humanized CD19 CAR‑T showed 98% ORR in CAR‑naïve patients, suggesting reduced immunogenicity.
CD22‑CAR trials report 70‑75% ORR, supporting dual‑target strategies to prevent CD19 loss.
GD2‑CAR in neuroblastoma yielded a 66% ORR, with higher activity in low‑burden disease.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Antigen escape via loss of CD19 expression limits durability of CD19‑CAR T therapy.
Variable HER2 expression impacted efficacy of trastuzumab deruxtecan in osteosarcoma.

combination_strategies.rationale: Enhance CAR T cell expansion and reduce disease burden before infusion
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved remission rates and lower toxicity in early‑line settings

combination_strategies.rationale: Reduce hepatotoxicity and myelosuppression in pediatric patients
combination_strategies.phenotype: Preclinical safety improvement; phase 1 adult study ongoing

open_questions: How can academic CAR‑T manufacturing be standardized across institutions to ensure consistent efficacy?
What regulatory pathways will allow rapid adoption of bispecific antibodies without full commercial approval?

talk_title: Signaling-to-chromatin pathways and histone phosphorylation in lymphoma

speaker.name: Steven Josefowicz
speaker.affiliation: Weill Cornell Medicine, Department of Immunology

models.name: Myc‑GFP reporter mouse
models.genotype_or_key_features: GFP reporter activated after high‑quality GC B‑cell signaling

MOA_summary: Signal‑induced phosphorylation of histone H3 at serine 28 creates a transient domain that ejects the PRC2 complex, abolishing its allosteric activation and chromatin condensation activity, thereby converting repressive H3K27me3 domains into permissive transcriptional states in germinal‑center B cells and lymphoma.
key_findings: Serine 28 phosphorylation spreads across several hundred kilobase domains in stimulated macrophages and GC B cells, whereas serine 31 phosphorylation is confined to transcription units.
In ABC DLBCL, H3S28ph is constitutively high and overlaps with PRC2‑mediated H3K27me3 at the MYC locus, defining a ‘methyl‑phos switch’.
Biophysical assays show that H3S28ph‑containing nucleosome arrays are poor substrates for EZH2 and resist PRC2‑driven chromatin condensation.
Ex vivo GC B‑cell stimulation reveals progressive loss of H3K27me3 within H3S28ph domains, accompanied by increased chromatin interactivity and H3K36me2 gain.
Similar histone phosphorylation patterns are observed in T cells (TBET locus) and NK cells (IL15RA), and are constitutive in MYD88/CD79B mutant lymphoma PDX models.
open_questions: Does H3S28ph demarcate topologically associating domain (TAD) boundaries, and how does loss of PRC2 affect TAD architecture?
What molecular determinants decide whether a transient H3S28ph‑induced activation reverts to repression or becomes locked by NSD‑mediated H3K36me2?

talk_title: ELOVL1‑mediated lipid metabolism as a dependency in NRAS‑mutant IDH‑mutant AML

speaker.name: Ross Levine
speaker.affiliation: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Department of Medicine

biomarkers.name: NRAS mutation
biomarkers.finding: NRAS co‑mutation predicts resistance to IDH inhibitors and defines dependency on ELOVL1

biomarkers.name: IDH1/2 mutation
biomarkers.finding: IDH mutation defines the leukemia model but alone does not confer ELOVL1 dependency

models.name: IDH1‑NPM1‑NRAS GEM model
models.genotype_or_key_features: IDH1 R132H + NPM1c + oncogenic NRAS G12D

models.name: IDH‑mutant AML PDX (RAS WT or mutant)
Stromal co‑culture system for primary mouse stem cells
models.genotype_or_key_features: WT, Tet2, DNMT3A, IDH1/2 mutant stem cells

MOA_summary: ELOVL1 catalyzes very‑long‑chain fatty‑acid elongation, feeding sphingolipid/ceramide synthesis that sustains lipid‑raft integrity; in NRAS‑mutant IDH‑mutant AML, loss of ELOVL1 disrupts lipid rafts, attenuates NRAS‑driven MAPK signaling, and selectively impairs leukemia cell viability.
readouts: in vivo peripheral blood and bone marrow sequencing
key_findings: CRISPR screens of epigenetic and metabolic libraries identified ELOVL1 as a selective dependency in Tet/IDH mutant AML cells.
ELOVL1 knock‑out depletes NRAS‑mutant AML cells in vitro and in vivo (bone marrow and peripheral blood) but not WT or RAS‑WT AML.
Pharmacologic inhibition of ELOVL1 with a Vertex compound reproduces genetic dependency, causing selective depletion of triple‑mutant cells.
Catalytic‑dead ELOVL1 fails to rescue the phenotype, confirming requirement for enzymatic activity.
Disruption of sphingolipid biosynthesis (SPTLC1, KDSR) phenocopies ELOVL1 loss, whereas peroxisomal fatty‑acid pathways do not.
Lipid‑raft depletion (MBCD) selectively kills NRAS‑mutant AML, and ELOVL1 loss reduces raft abundance and downstream MAPK/PI3K signaling.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: NRAS co‑mutation confers resistance to IDH inhibitors; ELOVL1 dependency is exclusive to NRAS‑mutant context.

combination_strategies.rationale: ELOVL1 loss synergizes with IDH inhibition, potentially overcoming RAS‑mediated resistance
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced leukemia cell depletion in ex vivo cultures

combination_strategies.rationale: Directly mimics ELOVL1 loss by dismantling lipid rafts
combination_strategies.phenotype: selective killing of NRAS‑mutant AML cells

open_questions: What is the precise molecular link between sphingolipid/ceramide metabolism and NRAS membrane localization or myristoylation?
Can CNS‑sparing ELOVL1 inhibitors be developed with therapeutic windows suitable for AML patients?

talk_title: Epigenetic priming to enhance T‑cell directed therapies in lymphoma

biomarkers.name: HLA‑A/B/C expression
biomarkers.finding: YF2 treatment increased HLA class I mRNA and surface protein in lymphoma cell lines

biomarkers.name: MHC‑II (HLA‑DR) expression
biomarkers.finding: HDAC and EZH2 inhibition upregulated MHC‑II on B cells in mice and patient samples

biomarkers.name: CD20 surface density
biomarkers.finding: Belinostat + tazemetostat restored CD20 expression in bispecific‑resistant lymphoma cells

biomarkers.name: PD‑1 on T cells
biomarkers.finding: Combination therapy decreased PD‑1 expression on tumor‑infiltrating T cells

models.name: A20 lymphoma syngeneic model
models.genotype_or_key_features: immunocompetent BALB/c mice engrafted with A20 cells

models.name: CTEP study of belinostat + tazemetostat + mocetuzumab
tazemetostat priming of CAR‑T (various products)
models.genotype_or_key_features: 13 patients with diverse lymphomas, many TP53‑mutated

models.name: Lymphoma spheroid/organotypic culture
models.genotype_or_key_features: 3‑D B‑cell aggregates co‑cultured with healthy T cells

clinical_data.n: n=?? primary mediastinal large B‑cell lymphoma cohort
clinical_data.population: primary mediastinal large B‑cell lymphoma, checkpoint‑naïve

clinical_data.n: n=?? Hodgkin lymphoma PD‑1 pre‑treated responders

clinical_data.population: relapsed/refractory lymphoma patients receiving tazemetostat‑primed CAR‑T

MOA_summary: Epigenetic agents (HAT activator YF2, HDAC3 inhibitor, EZH2 inhibitor, DNMT inhibitor) remodel chromatin to up‑regulate antigen‑presentation genes (MHC‑I/II, CD20) and reverse exhaustion markers, thereby priming lymphoma cells for enhanced recognition and killing by checkpoint blockade, CAR‑T cells, and bispecific antibodies.
key_findings: YF2 increases HLA‑A/B/C and antigen‑processing gene expression, boosting MHC‑I/II surface levels in lymphoma cell lines and mouse B cells.
Combination of YF2 with anti‑PD‑L1 reduces tumor growth and prolongs survival in A20‑bearing mice, with increased tumor MHC‑I and decreased PD‑1 on infiltrating T cells.
HDAC3 inhibition plus PD‑L1 blockade yields 80% CR in primary mediastinal large B‑cell lymphoma and 57–71% CR in Hodgkin lymphoma, with durable responses.
Tazemetostat priming enhances CAR‑T cell infiltration, synapse formation, and cytokine production, achieving 100% ORR and 77% CR in a 13‑patient lymphoma cohort.

Belinostat + tazemetostat restores CD20 (including epitope‑specific variants) on bispecific‑resistant lymphoma cells, increases MHC expression, and drives T‑cell mediated killing in 3‑D spheroid models.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Loss of CD20 via transcriptional repression or epitope truncation underlies resistance to bispecific antibodies; epigenetic priming can partially reverse this.

combination_strategies.rationale: YF2‑mediated MHC up‑regulation enhances tumor antigen visibility to T cells
combination_strategies.phenotype: tumor growth inhibition and prolonged survival in mouse lymphoma model

combination_strategies.rationale: EZH2 inhibition lifts repression of MHC and CD20, improving CAR‑T cell engagement
combination_strategies.phenotype: 100% ORR, 77% CR in early‑phase CAR‑T trial

combination_strategies.rationale: HDAC inhibition restores CD20 expression and enhances bispecific antibody efficacy
combination_strategies.phenotype: re‑expression of CD20 and increased T‑cell mediated cytotoxicity in resistant lymphoma spheroids

combination_strategies.rationale: DNA demethylation synergizes with checkpoint blockade to increase response rates
combination_strategies.phenotype: higher CR rates in Hodgkin lymphoma compared with checkpoint inhibitor alone

open_questions: What transcription factors mediate the selective up‑regulation of HLA genes by YF2 (e.g., RFX5, CIITA)?
Can EZH2‑independent mechanisms also prime CAR‑T efficacy, and how durable are epigenetic changes after drug washout?
Will next‑generation EZH2 inhibitors with better safety profiles retain the priming effect observed with tazemetostat?

talk_title: Detecting and Modeling Genetic Epistasis in Cancer Using Conditional Selection

models.name: TCGA bulk sequencing
models.genotype_or_key_features: large pan‑cancer cohort, stratified by presence/absence of split gene

MOA_summary: Aranzo presented a statistical framework that uses synonymous mutations to build a neutral null model of mutation, compares expected vs observed non‑synonymous counts to estimate driver selection, and then quantifies conditional selection (epistasis) by stratifying tumors on a split gene and re‑estimating selection for a query gene, implemented in the R package cosalliance.
key_findings: Positive and negative epistasis are pervasive, with 25‑50% of driver mutations affected by positive epistasis across cancer types.
Epistasis networks are modular: genes within a module show synergistic interactions, while modules antagonize each other.
Module‑based mutational profiles predict patient survival better than individual gene mutations.
Early mutations in a module can forecast the eventual molecular subtype of a tumor.
Asymmetric epistatic relationships reveal logical ordering of mutations, e.g., TP53 enhances the effect of RB1 in glioblastoma but not vice versa.
open_questions: How do epistatic modules behave in longitudinal pre‑malignant lesions?
Can single‑cell data improve resolution of conditional selection estimates?

talk_title: Quantifying Epistasis and Evolutionary Trajectories in Cancer

models.name: TCGA COAD
models.genotype_or_key_features: APC, KRAS, BRAF mutation status per tumor

MOA_summary: Alfaro-Murillo described a continuous‑time Markov chain model that estimates substitution rates for all possible mutational pathways among a set of genes, fits these rates to observed genotype frequencies, and separates mutation from selection using neutral mutation rate estimates (e.g., NDS, SigMutCel) to derive selection coefficients and epistatic interactions.
key_findings: The model can infer four substitution rates for any gene pair, capturing order‑dependent effects.
In colorectal cancer, KRAS and BRAF show strong antagonistic epistasis, while APC and KRAS display weak or non‑significant interaction.
Selection coefficients derived after correcting for mutation rates reveal that many apparent co‑occurrences are driven by high mutation rates rather than true epistasis.
Statistical tests (likelihood ratio) can formally assess the presence of epistasis and compare epistatic effects across populations.
open_questions: How does incorporating copy‑number variation alter inferred substitution rates?
Can subclonal VAF information be integrated to improve order‑specific selection estimates?

talk_title: Tobacco Smoking and Somatic Selective Epistasis in Lung Adenocarcinoma

biomarkers.name: smoking status
biomarkers.finding: Stratifies selection coefficients; ever smokers show higher KRAS selection, never smokers higher EGFR selection

models.name: TCGA LUAD
models.genotype_or_key_features: ever vs never smokers, driver mutation status

clinical_data.value: 73% in never smokers vs 46% in >10 pack‑year smokers

MOA_summary: Jeff showed that smoking alters both mutation rates and selection coefficients of key lung adenocarcinoma drivers; after deconvolution, KRAS, KEAP1, STK11 and SDK11 have higher selection in ever smokers, while EGFR, PI3K and SMAD4 are stronger drivers in never smokers, and complex gene‑by‑gene‑by‑environment epistatic interactions reshape evolutionary trajectories.
key_findings: Smoking increases mutation rates genome‑wide but the magnitude varies by gene (e.g., ALK, GNAS show especially high rates).
After correcting for mutation rates, KRAS selection is significantly higher in ever smokers, while EGFR selection is negligible in smokers.
Pairwise epistasis is common in ever smokers; many driver mutations (e.g., KRAS, KEAP1) antagonize early genes like TP53, indicating early clonal emergence.
Higher‑order interactions (G×G×E) were identified, e.g., KEAP1 selection strongest in smokers without EGFR mutation, weaker in EGFR‑mutant smokers.
Clinical implications: EGFR TKIs achieve higher response rates in never smokers; co‑mutations (e.g., KRAS, KEAP1) predict poorer outcomes in EGFR‑mutant disease.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: KRAS and KEAP1 co‑mutations confer resistance to EGFR TKIs and worsen survival in KRAS‑mutant NSCLC.
Antagonistic epistasis between EGFR and KRAS/KEAP1 may reduce efficacy of EGFR‑targeted therapy when these alterations co‑occur.

combination_strategies.rationale: EGFR driver more selected in never smokers; co‑mutations may blunt response
combination_strategies.phenotype: Higher response rates in never smokers (73% vs 46% in smokers)

combination_strategies.rationale: KRAS and KEAP1 are strong drivers in ever smokers
combination_strategies.phenotype: Potential synthetic lethality; preclinical data show KEAP1 loss accelerates KRAS‑mutant tumor growth

open_questions: Can integrating proteomics or transcriptomics clarify convergent signaling downstream of distinct mutational modules?
How do age, sex, and cumulative pack‑years quantitatively modulate selection coefficients beyond binary smoking status?

cited_papers: Kira Glasmacher et al. 2024 (copy‑number selection poster)

talk_title: Foundation model of the tumor genome for drug response prediction and evolution analysis

speaker.name: Trey Ideker
speaker.affiliation: University of California, San Diego, Department of Computer Science and Engineering

models.name: Project GENIE
models.genotype_or_key_features: 468‑gene panel across 30,000 solid tumors (breast, lung, colorectal, etc.)

clinical_data.population: NSCLC patients treated with anti‑PD‑1/PD‑L1 therapy

MOA_summary: Ideker presented a self‑supervised foundation model (MutationProjector) that learns embeddings of tumor genomes by masking gene alterations and predicting them using graph‑attention networks informed by protein‑protein, TF‑binding, and synthetic‑lethal networks. The low‑dimensional embeddings capture tumor type and combinatorial mutation patterns, which can be used as features for downstream supervised models (e.g., random forest) to predict drug response, including immunotherapy efficacy.
key_findings: The foundation model organizes tumors by type and reveals key single‑gene and pairwise mutation interactions (e.g., RB1‑TP53, FGFR3‑CDKN2A) driving the embedding.
Embedding coordinates improve immunotherapy response prediction in NSCLC (AUC 0.78) versus traditional biomarkers.
Clonal evolution analysis shows a pseudo‑time trajectory correlating with increasing TP53, APC, and CDKN2A alterations across stages.
Scaling the model to 270,000 genomes further improves performance, indicating data‑limited learning.

talk_title: Aneuploidy burden as a predictive biomarker for colorectal cancer risk in inflammatory bowel disease

speaker.name: Trevor Graham
speaker.affiliation: Institute of Cancer Research, London, United Kingdom

biomarkers.name: Aneuploidy burden (copy‑number alteration count)
biomarkers.finding: High burden predicts progression to colorectal cancer with ~85% positive and negative predictive value in IBD patients

models.name: St. Mark's IBD surveillance cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: Whole‑genome sequencing of >100 biopsies per patient; copy‑number profiles

MOA_summary: Graham demonstrated that widespread copy‑number alterations (aneuploidy) arise in inflamed colonic epithelium of IBD patients, forming large clonal expansions that precede dysplasia. Quantifying the aneuploidy burden in dysplastic lesions or in morphologically normal biopsies predicts future colorectal cancer risk, providing a mechanistic link between chronic inflammation‑driven genomic instability and cancer evolution.
key_findings: Aneuploid clones can spread across the entire colon, covering meters of tissue, without visible dysplasia.
Copy‑number burden is significantly higher in lesions that later progress to cancer versus those that do not (p<0.001).
A high aneuploidy burden in low‑grade dysplasia predicts cancer development with ~85% PPV and NPV.
Aneuploidy in non‑dysplastic surveillance biopsies also predicts future cancer, suggesting a field‑effect biomarker.
Margin analysis after endoscopic resection shows that residual aneuploid clones correlate with subsequent cancer, indicating potential for molecular clearance monitoring.
open_questions: Which specific chromosome arms (e.g., loss of 17q) drive progression versus background inflammation?
Can chemoprevention agents (e.g., mesalazine) reduce aneuploidy burden and cancer risk?

talk_title: Evolutionary strategies to manage therapeutic resistance in cancer

models.name: conceptual frameworks and adaptive therapy trials

MOA_summary: The speaker outlined evolutionary concepts for reducing cancer evolvability (lowering mutation rate, limiting resources, reducing selection pressure) and for changing selective pressures (drug rotation, alternating opposing therapies, adaptive dosing). He highlighted adaptive therapy—dose modulation based on tumor burden—to maintain competition between sensitive and resistant clones, citing preclinical mouse work and a phase‑II adaptive trial in prostate cancer that doubled time to progression.
key_findings: Maximum tolerated dose accelerates selection for resistance; lower or adaptive dosing can prolong control.
Adaptive therapy in metastatic prostate cancer increased median time to progression from 14.3 to 33.5 months and suggested a ~7‑fold fitness cost of resistance.
Preclinical models show that adding benign selective pressures (multiple drugs) slows evolution of any single resistance.
Integrated pest management heuristics (minimum effective dose, monitoring, rotating modes of action) are proposed as a framework for cancer therapy.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Adaptive therapy assumes resistant clones carry a fitness cost; literature review shows only ~2/3 of experiments confirm this.

combination_strategies.with_agent: multiple benign selective pressures (e.g., non‑toxic agents)
combination_strategies.rationale: Create competition among clones to reduce speed of any single resistance
combination_strategies.phenotype: Slower tumor adaptation in yeast and mouse models

combination_strategies.rationale: Maintain sensitive clone competition while controlling tumor burden
combination_strategies.phenotype: Doubling of progression‑free time in prostate cancer trial

open_questions: How often does resistance carry a measurable fitness cost in human tumors?
What biomarkers can reliably indicate when to pause or reduce therapy in adaptive protocols?

cited_papers: Gatenby et al. 2022 Nat Rev Cancer

talk_title: Extracellular matrix regulation of metastatic dormancy via DDR1-STAT1 signaling

targets.dependency_context: collagen III matrix, dormant tumor cells
dormant head and neck and breast cancer cells

MOA_summary: Dormant disseminated tumor cells secrete collagen III, creating a pro‑quiescent ECM that signals through the collagen receptor DDR1 to activate STAT1, maintaining G0 arrest. Proline hydroxylase P4HA2 stabilizes this collagen matrix; its loss reduces hydroxylated collagens, remodels the ECM, activates mitochondria and LDH4A1‑driven metabolism, thereby awakening dormant cells.
key_findings: Dormant tumor cells produce collagen III‑rich ECM that induces nuclear CDK2 biosensor localization, indicating G0 arrest.
Collagen III up‑regulates DDR1 expression, activating STAT1 signaling to maintain dormancy.
P4HA2 is up‑regulated in dormant cells; knockdown restores proliferation and metastasis in lung colonization assays.
Loss of P4HA2 decreases proline‑hydroxylated collagens (III, I, V) in the tumor microenvironment.
P4HA2‑deficient ECM triggers mitochondrial enlargement, increased basal and maximal respiration, and higher ATP production.
LDH4A1 is up‑regulated in awakened cells, driving NADH production; its knockdown blocks awakening despite P4HA2 loss.
open_questions: What membrane receptor links collagen III‑derived signals to mitochondrial metabolic reprogramming?
Can pharmacologic inhibition of P4HA2 be therapeutically exploited to maintain dormancy?

talk_title: SPARC-mediated collagen regulation impacts immunity, autoimmunity and tumor progression

targets.dependency_context: fibroblast and tumor cells, collagen I/IV regulation

models.name: WT BM into SPARC KO host
NeuT x SPARC KO
models.genotype_or_key_features: SPARC expression in tumor vs stroma

MOA_summary: SPARC regulates collagen I/IV deposition, shaping ECM scaffolds that control immune cell localization and activation; loss of SPARC reduces collagen barriers, enhances NETosis, alters B‑cell maturation via CD24‑P‑selectin interactions, and promotes myeloid‑derived suppressor cell expansion, thereby influencing autoimmunity, AML aggressiveness, and breast cancer metastasis.
readouts: flow cytometry for CD24 and P‑selectin
key_findings: SPARC deficiency impairs germinal center formation and B‑cell maturation due to loss of collagen scaffolding and increased CD24‑P‑selectin‑mediated apoptosis.
In SPARC‑KO mice, neutrophil NETosis is enhanced, leading to increased IL‑6 and growth factor release that drives autoimmunity and CLL‑like disease.
High SPARC expression in breast cancer (ECM3 signature) correlates with collagen‑rich, immune‑cold tumors and increased metastasis.
SPARC‑high tumors produce more GM‑CSF, IL‑6, and CXCL12, recruiting myeloid‑derived suppressor cells; COX‑2 inhibition reduces these cytokines.
Knockdown of fatty‑acid desaturase 5 reduces SPARC secretion, decreasing myeloid suppression and metastasis in a SPARC‑high clone.

combination_strategies.rationale: reduces IL‑6 and GM‑CSF production in SPARC‑high tumors
combination_strategies.phenotype: decreased myeloid suppressor cell infiltration

open_questions: How does SPARC‑mediated collagen architecture specifically modulate neutrophil NETosis signaling?
Can therapeutic restoration of SPARC in the stroma reverse immune suppression in established tumors?

talk_title: CHIL1‑neutrophil axis drives ECM remodeling, myoepithelial loss and breast cancer metastasis

biomarkers.name: CK14
biomarkers.finding: Loss of CK14 staining indicates myoepithelial layer dissolution in CHIL1‑overexpressing tumors

MOA_summary: STAT3‑driven CHIL1 expression in mammary tumor cells recruits neutrophils, inducing NETosis and neutrophil degranulation; neutrophil elastase degrades laminin and collagen IV, dismantling the myoepithelial basement membrane, permitting tumor invasion and metastasis, while NETs create an immune‑excluded niche.
readouts: basement membrane component staining (laminin, collagen IV)
key_findings: CHIL1 overexpression accelerates lung metastasis in Middle T mice within six weeks.
CHIL1 knockout tumors retain an intact CK14+ myoepithelial layer and fail to metastasize.
Neutrophil depletion (anti‑Ly6G) preserves myoepithelial markers and prevents basement membrane degradation.
Pharmacologic inhibition of neutrophil elastase with Alvestat maintains CK14, calponin, and p63 expression and reduces circulating tumor cells.
CHIL1‑driven neutrophil recruitment leads to extensive laminin and collagen IV degradation, driving myoepithelial loss.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Neutrophil elastase inhibition blocks ECM degradation without affecting NETosis, indicating distinct neutrophil functions.

combination_strategies.rationale: prevent neutrophil‑mediated myoepithelial dissolution
combination_strategies.phenotype: retention of CK14 and reduced metastasis

combination_strategies.rationale: block elastase‑driven basement membrane degradation
combination_strategies.phenotype: preserved myoepithelial layer and lower circulating tumor cells

open_questions: Can CHIL1 inhibition synergize with checkpoint blockade to convert immune‑cold tumors to hot?
What is the relative contribution of NET‑associated versus granule‑released elastase in ECM remodeling?

cited_papers: Kim Marske lab neutrophil elastase knockout study

talk_title: Fibroblast heterogeneity and therapeutic strategies in pancreatic cancer

speaker.name: Stephanie Duggan
speaker.affiliation: Dana-Farber Cancer Institute and Harvard Medical School

models.name: KPC GEM model
models.genotype_or_key_features: spontaneous pancreatic tumor, KRAS G12D, TP53 loss

clinical_data.value: no benefit with pegylated hyaluronidase + FOLFIRINOX

MOA_summary: Stephanie Duggan reviewed fibroblast heterogeneity in pancreatic cancer, describing myCAF and iCAF subsets driven by TGF‑β and IL‑1β, and summarized failed clinical attempts to deplete fibroblasts or ECM (Smoothened inhibition, pegylated hyaluronidase) which paradoxically worsened survival, highlighting the need for more nuanced targeting.
key_findings: Smoothened inhibition combined with gemcitabine accelerated tumor progression in humans despite preclinical efficacy.
Pegylated hyaluronidase failed to improve overall survival and worsened outcomes when combined with FOLFIRINOX.
TGF‑β blockade depleted myCAFs but did not improve survival in pancreatic cancer trials.
IL‑1β blockade showed no benefit over chemotherapy in pancreatic cancer.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Depleting fibroblasts or ECM can increase tumor aggressiveness, suggesting fibroblast subsets have both tumor‑promoting and restraining roles.
open_questions: How can fibroblast subsets be selectively reprogrammed rather than globally depleted?
What are the precise mechanisms by which fibroblast depletion worsens outcomes?

talk_title: Stellate‑cell derived Kit ligand as a stromal tumor‑suppressive factor in pancreatic cancer

models.name: benign adjacent pancreas tissue fibroblast cultures

MOA_summary: Sherman demonstrated that Kit ligand (KITLG) is expressed by normal pancreatic stellate cells and lost during tumorigenesis; loss of stromal Kit ligand increases inflammation, leukocyte infiltration, and acinar‑to‑ductal metaplasia, thereby promoting tumor initiation, whereas its presence supports normal tissue architecture and restrains tumor progression.
key_findings: Kit ligand is highly expressed in normal pancreatic stellate cells but markedly reduced in pancreatic cancer stroma.
Conditional deletion of Kit ligand in mouse pancreas increases CD45+ leukocyte infiltration and perivascular mesenchymal cells.
Kit ligand loss exacerbates cerulein‑induced pancreatitis and promotes acinar‑to‑ductal metaplasia.
In implantable tumor models, stromal Kit ligand deficiency accelerates tumor growth.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: TGF‑β signaling down‑regulates Kit ligand expression, linking a known tumor‑promoting pathway to loss of a stromal tumor‑suppressive factor.
open_questions: Can downstream effectors of Kit ligand (e.g., basement membrane components) be therapeutically targeted to mimic its tumor‑suppressive role?
What cues in pre‑invasive lesions maintain Kit ligand expression, and can they be reinforced?

talk_title: Technical implementation and early results of MedGamma fine‑tuning with QLoRA across consortium sites

speaker.name: Umut Topaloglu
speaker.affiliation: National Cancer Institute, Clinical and Translational Informatics Branch

MOA_summary: Umut described the federation of MedGamma using QLoRA adapters, where each site fine‑tunes a frozen foundation model on local lung and breast cancer records, then shares low‑rank weight updates via NVIDIA FLARE to produce a more robust multimodal extractor.
readouts: structured data extraction from pathology reports
key_findings: Three of four sites completed local QLoRA fine‑tuning, showing improved extraction accuracy versus out‑of‑the‑box MedGamma (visualized as orange vs blue bars).
One site demonstrated a statistically significant increase in variable extraction performance after fine‑tuning; other sites reported even larger gains.
Federated aggregation of QLoRA weights is scheduled, with the hypothesis that exposure to four centers’ data will further boost model robustness.
open_questions: What is the optimal level of differential privacy that balances privacy guarantees with model accuracy across heterogeneous sites?

talk_title: Fine‑tuning MedGamma with QLoRA for trial‑matching in lung and breast cancer cohorts

speaker.name: Ghulam Rasool
speaker.affiliation: Moffitt Cancer Center, Department of Machine Learning

MOA_summary: Rasool reported that local QLoRA fine‑tuning of MedGamma on ~500 retrospective lung and breast cancer patients per site markedly improved the model’s ability to extract structured trial‑matching variables from pathology reports and clinical notes, and that a forthcoming federated run will test whether pooled updates further enhance performance.
key_findings: Local fine‑tuning at one site increased extraction performance from baseline (blue) to post‑fine‑tuning (orange).
Two additional sites reported even larger improvements after local QLoRA adaptation.
Federated aggregation of QLoRA weights is planned for the week following the conference.
open_questions: Will federated QLoRA updates yield additive gains beyond the best local fine‑tuned model?

talk_title: Federated deep‑learning model for overall survival prediction in glioblastoma using MRIs

models.name: Vision Transformer with Brain SegFounder encoder
models.genotype_or_key_features: pre‑trained on healthy brain MRIs and BRATS glioma data

MOA_summary: Khanna described a federated pipeline where a pre‑trained Vision Transformer extracts 768‑dimensional embeddings from pre‑operative MRI tumor ROIs, which are pooled and fed to an MLP to predict relative overall‑survival risk; the model was trained across public GBM datasets and a real‑world Moffitt cohort (n=104) using NVIDIA FLARE with FedAvg aggregation.
key_findings: Added 104 real‑world Moffitt GBM patients to three public datasets without centralizing data.
Leave‑one‑site‑out evaluation showed consistent Kaplan‑Meier separation across held‑out cohorts.
Time‑dependent 1‑year AUC and C‑index were comparable to prior centralized studies, though absolute performance was not yet sufficient for clinical decision‑making.
Future plans include incorporating an additional private NCI GBM cohort and expanding to radiology reports, other imaging modalities, and clinical markers.
open_questions: How to accurately compute Cox partial likelihood in a federated setting given incomplete at‑risk information across sites?
What is the impact of batch‑wise approximation on survival model bias across heterogeneous cohorts?

cited_papers: AI‑RANO review in The Lancet Oncology

talk_title: Neoadjuvant pembrolizumab stratified by tumor mutation burden and high-risk stage two, three deficient mismatch repair or MSI-high colorectal cancer: The NeoPRISM-CRC study

biomarkers.name: ctDNA clearance
biomarkers.finding: Super molecular responders (rapid/complete clearance) all achieved pCR; dynamic responders (reduction <100 ppm) had 80% pCR rate.

biomarkers.name: TCR clonal expansion
biomarkers.finding: Patients achieving pCR had more expanded large clones than non-responders.

biomarkers.name: post-operative ctDNA
biomarkers.finding: 100% specificity and 100% negative predictive value for relapse (median follow-up 33 months).

models.name: NeoPRISM-CRC
models.genotype_or_key_features: high-risk stage II or III MMRd colorectal cancers, n=32

MOA_summary: Neoadjuvant pembrolizumab was used in MMRd colorectal cancer to induce pathological complete response. The study integrated ultrasensitive ctDNA monitoring and TCR profiling to identify 'super molecular responders' who clear ctDNA rapidly, suggesting a potential for non-operative management.
key_findings: Nine weeks of neoadjuvant pembrolizumab achieved a 59% pCR rate in high-risk stage II/III MMRd CRC (n=31).
ctDNA was detected in 100% of patients at baseline, with 24% in the ultrasensitive range (<100 ppm).
100% of 'super molecular responders' (rapid clearance after 1 cycle) achieved pCR.
Post-operative ctDNA surveillance demonstrated 100% specificity and 100% negative predictive value for relapse over a median follow-up of 33 months.
Baseline TCR clonal expansion correlates with pCR, and a combined Bayesian model of TCR and ctDNA kinetics improves response prediction.
open_questions: Whether super molecular responders can be safely managed non-operatively.

talk_title: Early findings from MONITOR-BREAST: ctDNA dynamics during neoadjuvant therapy using an ultrasensitive MRD assay

biomarkers.name: post-neoadjuvant ctDNA
biomarkers.finding: 100% specificity for pCR; all 47 patients who achieved pCR were ctDNA negative.

biomarkers.name: sustained MRD negativity
biomarkers.finding: 63% pCR rate for sustained clearers vs 0% for persistently positive and 7% for intermittently positive.

models.name: MONITOR-BREAST
models.genotype_or_key_features: all breast cancer subtypes, early stages I-III, n=113 evaluable

MOA_summary: The study evaluated the association between dense, ultrasensitive ctDNA sampling and pathologic response in neoadjuvant breast cancer therapy. It found that sustained MRD negativity is a strong predictor of pCR, while persistent or intermittent positivity strongly predicts residual disease.
key_findings: 100% specificity: all 47 patients achieving pCR were ctDNA negative post-neoadjuvant therapy.
Sustained MRD negativity (76% of patients) resulted in a 63% pCR rate.
Persistent MRD positivity resulted in 0% pCR and 100% residual disease (all RCB2 or 3).
Frequent sampling identified 64% more patients at risk for residual disease compared to a single post-neoadjuvant time point.
Clearance timing varied by subtype: TNBC cleared within 45 days (90% of patients) vs HR+/HER2- which took 16 weeks (90% of patients).
open_questions: Whether a negative test in the presence of residual disease implies a favorable long-term outcome regardless of pCR.

talk_title: Phase 2 study of adjuvant PD-1 blockade with endocrine therapy in hormone receptor-positive and inflammatory breast cancer

biomarkers.name: ctDNA negativity at end of treatment
biomarkers.finding: 100% disease-free survival at 24 months.

biomarkers.name: ctDNA positivity
biomarkers.finding: ctDNA positivity preceded radiographic relapse by up to 15 months.

models.name: not specified
models.genotype_or_key_features: HR+ inflammatory breast cancer with residual disease after local therapy, n=31

MOA_summary: The study tested the combination of pembrolizumab and endocrine therapy in HR+ inflammatory breast cancer (IBC). The hypothesis was that targeting the immunosuppressive microenvironment of IBC would improve DFS, which was observed (71% vs 60% historical).
key_findings: Two-year DFS was 71%, an improvement over the historical benchmark of 60% for HR+ IBC.
ctDNA negativity at the end of treatment was associated with 100% disease-free survival at 24 months.
ctDNA positivity provided a lead time to clinical progression of up to 15 months.
Durable responders showed lower maximum variant allele frequency (VAF) in nodal tissue compared to non-responders.
Deletions in the immunoglobulin heavy locus (IGH) were found in durable responders, suggesting B-cell involvement in the response.

combination_strategies.rationale: Synergy between PD-1 blockade and endocrine therapy in high-risk HR+ disease
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved 2-year DFS (71% vs 60% historical)

open_questions: The specific role of B-cell clones in aiding T-cell effectors in IBC response.

talk_title: Molecular residual disease interception in local regionally advanced head and neck squamous cell carcinoma, the MERIDIAN phase two trial
tumor_types: head and neck squamous cell carcinoma

biomarkers.name: ctDNA (RADAR assay)
biomarkers.finding: Detection at 8-12 weeks post-therapy associated with recurrence; longitudinal tracking increased sensitivity to 73%.

biomarkers.name: HPV DNA
biomarkers.finding: Higher sensitivity than RADAR but lower specificity in oropharyngeal cancer patients.

biomarkers.name: PD-L1 CPS
biomarkers.finding: CPS >= 1 required for interception therapy.

models.name: MERIDIAN
models.genotype_or_key_features: locally advanced HNSCC, high risk of recurrence, n=42 evaluable for MRD

MOA_summary: The MERIDIAN trial tests the 'interception' hypothesis: treating MRD-positive HNSCC patients with the PD-1/TIGIT bispecific antibody rivocostimib to clear ctDNA and prevent clinical recurrence. One patient achieved persistent ctDNA clearance and remains disease-free 2 years later.
key_findings: A single patient treated with rivocostimib achieved persistent ctDNA clearance and remains disease-free 2 years post-therapy.
ctDNA detection at 8-12 weeks post-therapy had 50% sensitivity for recurrence, which improved to 73% with longitudinal tracking.
HPV DNA sequencing showed higher sensitivity but lower specificity compared to the RADAR assay in OPC patients.
Combining tumor-informed assays and HPV DNA increased sensitivity to 100% in OPC patients.
The study was paused due to lower-than-expected MRD prevalence (only 6 patients eligible for interception).
open_questions: Whether more sensitive assays (<100 ppm) can catch relapses that occur more than a year after the positive sample.

talk_title: Unlocking endocrine resistance, co-targeting KAT6 and ER with bifetostat and fulvestrant

biomarkers.name: H3K23 acetylation
biomarkers.finding: >=50% reduction achieved with bifetostat 1mg and 5mg doses.

biomarkers.name: ctDNA clearance
biomarkers.finding: Bifetostat 5mg + fulvestrant showed 26.5% overall clearance, 63% for ESR1, and 31% for PI3K pathway mutations.

biomarkers.name: ctDNA status at 9 weeks
biomarkers.finding: Median PFS was 21.9 months for ctDNA negative vs 5.3 months for ctDNA positive.

models.name: Phase 1 study
models.genotype_or_key_features: ER-positive, HER2-negative metastatic breast cancer, all prior CDK4/6 inhibitor use

MOA_summary: Bifetostat inhibits KAT6A/B, shifting the tumor transcriptome toward tumor suppressor programs (e.g., FOXP3, ING4) and away from cell cycle genes (TK1, CYCB1). Combining this with fulvestrant blocks both epigenetic and ER signaling, leading to robust clearance of ESR1 variants associated with endocrine resistance.
key_findings: Bifetostat 5mg + fulvestrant is the recommended dose for Phase 3 based on superior ctDNA clearance and gene expression changes.
Early ctDNA clearance (within 9 weeks) strongly correlates with clinical response (PR and SD).
Patients with undetectable ctDNA at 9 weeks had a significantly longer median PFS (21.9 vs 5.3 months).
The combination effectively clears ESR1 mutations (63% clearance rate), which are key drivers of endocrine resistance.

combination_strategies.rationale: Co-targeting epigenetic modifiers and ER to overcome endocrine resistance
combination_strategies.phenotype: Increased tumor suppressor expression and robust ESR1 ctDNA clearance

open_questions: Determinants of responder vs non-responder among patients who still showed >50% ctDNA drop but progressed.

talk_title: ctDNA Clearance as a Prognostic Biomarker in Extensive Stage Small Cell Lung Cancer: Insights from the LUMINOUS Phase 3B Study

biomarkers.name: ctDNA clearance
biomarkers.finding: Early sustained clearance associated with the most favorable OS and PFS; never-clearers had the poorest outcomes.

models.name: LUMINOUS
models.genotype_or_key_features: extensive stage small cell lung cancer, n=152

MOA_summary: The study used a tumor-agnostic methylation-based assay to monitor ctDNA in ES-SCLC patients treated with durvalumab and platinum-etoposide. The results show that the pattern of ctDNA clearance (sustained vs late vs never) is a powerful prognostic indicator of OS and PFS.
key_findings: Sustained ctDNA clearance was associated with the best outcomes (median PFS 19.3 months vs 6.2 months for non-clearers).
ctDNA re-emergence occurred 55 days before clinical progression in one representative case.
100% of sustained clearance patients were alive at 12 months, compared to 36% of never-clearers.
The methylation-based assay is viable for SCLC due to the high shedding nature of the tumor.
ctDNA dynamics aligned closely with longitudinal RECIST assessments.

combination_strategies.rationale: Standard of care combination with PD-L1 blockade
combination_strategies.phenotype: Median OS 16.4 months

open_questions: Need for prospective randomized trials to determine if ctDNA-guided therapy improves outcomes over standard care.

talk_title: Virtual patients with multimodal foundation models and agents

speaker.name: Charlotte Bonner
speaker.affiliation: EPFL, Computer Science and Life Science, AI Center, Cancer Research Institute

MOA_summary: Developed multimodal foundation models that jointly encode histopathology RGB images and spatial proteomics protein panels into a shared representation, enabling virtual patient profiling, in silico treatment forecasting, and AI‑derived biomarker discovery through early‑fusion transformer architectures and reinforcement‑learning‑based sequential acquisition (EchoK).
key_findings: Trained the first multimodal foundation model on 5,003 patients (4,605 publicly released) with paired H&E and spatial proteomics data across 8 cancer types.
Demonstrated that the model can virtually stain unmeasured proteins and predict optimal protein panels, achieving comparable performance to specialized single‑modality models.
Showed that model‑derived signatures stratify responders vs non‑responders in a triple‑negative breast cancer immunotherapy cohort better than existing clinical biomarkers.
Implemented EchoK, a reinforcement‑learning sequential acquisition system that selects a minimal set of proteomic markers (as few as 8) while preserving cell‑typing accuracy.

talk_title: Generative image models and visual question answering for disease dynamics

speaker.name: Serena Yeung Levy
speaker.affiliation: Stanford, Department of Biomedical Data Science

MOA_summary: Introduced CellFlux, a flow‑matching based generative framework that learns a continuous velocity field to transform distributions of control cell images into perturbed cell images, and described scaling of the model to >11 M images, joint imaging‑transcriptomics latent representations, and the MicroVQA benchmark for multimodal scientific visual question answering.
key_findings: CellFlux trained on 300 k images achieved higher image‑generation quality than prior baselines; scaling to 11.6 M images further improved performance.
Demonstrated that flow‑matching in latent space (CellFlux v2) enables realistic perturbation simulations and visualizes transformation trajectories.
Created MicroVQA, a benchmark of >1 000 expert‑curated microscopy questions covering higher‑order Bloom’s taxonomy, revealing gaps in current vision‑language models.
Showed that large multimodal LLMs (e.g., Gemini 3 Pro) achieve state‑of‑the‑art scores on MicroVQA but still struggle with fine‑grained visual reasoning.
open_questions: How to effectively model discrete biological events (e.g., apoptosis) within continuous flow‑matching frameworks?
Best strategies for joint training on limited matched multimodal (image‑transcriptomics) data combined with large unimodal datasets.

talk_title: Reliable medical AI agents that plan, evaluate, and verify reasoning

speaker.name: Michael Moore
speaker.affiliation: ETH Zurich, Assistant Professor of Medical AI

MOA_summary: Proposed a framework for medical AI agents that integrate structured knowledge atlases (Myriad QA pairs) and process‑reward verification models to ground and audit each reasoning step, enabling reliable multimodal decision‑making for tasks such as molecular tumor boards and 3D radiology analysis.
key_findings: Constructed Myriad, a 6 M QA pair knowledge base derived from peer‑reviewed literature, improving retrieval‑augmented generation (RAG) performance on MedExpertQA.
Developed a process‑reward model that checks individual reasoning steps of a language model against guidelines, reducing hallucinations across multiple downstream medical tasks.
Demonstrated an agent that performs step‑wise 3D chest CT analysis with traceable reasoning, achieving higher fidelity than baseline LLMs.
open_questions: How to scale structured knowledge representation while maintaining up‑to‑date clinical guidelines?
Methods for integrating multimodal inputs (imaging, labs, notes) into the agentic reasoning loop.

talk_title: Overview of the Cancer Research Data Commons infrastructure and usage statistics

speaker.name: Tania Davisson
speaker.affiliation: National Cancer Institute Center for Biomedical Informatics and Information Technology, Chief of Data Ecosystems Branch

MOA_summary: The CRDC provides a unified, cloud-based platform that aggregates genomic, proteomic, imaging, clinical, and other data types across seven specialized data commons, enabling standardized access, harmonized metadata, and scalable analysis without requiring data download.
key_findings: CRDC hosts 533 studies covering over 200,000 subjects and 17 petabytes of data.
Since inception, the cloud resource has performed over 1,600 compute years and offers more than 1,200 public analysis tools.
User engagement includes 100,000 monthly users and 47,000 PubMed citations referencing CRDC data.

talk_title: CCDI Data Ecosystem: resources, enrollment, and data integration

speaker.name: Subhashini Jagu
speaker.affiliation: National Cancer Institute Office of Data Sharing, Branch Chief

clinical_data.population: pediatric and AYA cancer patients on Project Every Child

MOA_summary: CCDI aggregates pediatric cancer clinical, genomic, and imaging data through a unified data model and APIs, enabling cohort building, cross‑resource queries, and rapid molecular characterization (results returned within 21 days) for over 9,000 enrolled patients.
key_findings: CCDI ecosystem currently includes 41 datasets, including Childhood Cancer Survival Study and Pediatric MATCH.
Project Every Child has enrolled >9,000 patients, delivering molecular characterization results within 21 days.
6,000 patients' molecular data from MCI have been released in cBioPortal, with integrated SNV and methylation widgets.
Over 4,000 pathology images are available in the Imaging Data Commons, with AI‑ready processing by DOE.

talk_title: ARPA‑H Biomedical Data Fabric Toolbox integration with CRDC

speaker.name: Erica Kim
speaker.affiliation: National Cancer Institute Center for Biomedical Informatics and IT; ARPA‑H Program Officer

clinical_data.population: real‑world use cases with Netrius tool

MOA_summary: The BDF Toolbox develops AI‑assisted tools (e.g., Netrius DataCord for metadata harmonization, Data Gatherer for literature‑linked dataset extraction, AI chatbots for data exploration) that integrate with CRDC APIs to automate data submission, curation, and discovery, reducing manual effort and improving data readiness for analysis.
key_findings: Netrius DataCord AI‑driven curation platform achieved ≥90% reduction in manual curation time in pilot deployments.
Data Gatherer combines rule‑based and LLM methods to extract dataset metadata from publications, enhancing discoverability.
BioInsight AI chatbot enables plain‑language queries across CRDC genomic, proteomic, and imaging resources.

talk_title: Obesity as a modifiable risk factor for cancer incidence and outcomes

models.name: International Agency for Research on Cancer meta‑analysis (≈1,000 studies)
Swedish Obese Subjects study (≈2,000 bariatric vs 2,000 controls, 25‑yr follow‑up)
Oxford Overview Group early‑stage breast cancer recurrence analysis (n≈200,000)
Look AHEAD lifestyle trial (n≈700 incident cancers, 11‑yr follow‑up)

clinical_data.metric: breast cancer risk per 5 kg/m² BMI increase
cancer‑specific mortality risk per 5 kg/m² BMI increase (breast cancer)
overall cancer incidence after bariatric surgery
obesity‑related cancer incidence after bariatric surgery
clinical_data.value: ≈50% lower breast cancer risk, ≈60% lower colorectal cancer risk

clinical_data.metric: weight loss in Breast Cancer Weight Loss Trial (intervention vs control)
percentage of participants achieving ≥5% weight loss

MOA_summary: Excess adiposity drives systemic metabolic dysregulation (hyperinsulinemia, hyperleptinemia, inflammation) that increases incidence and worsens outcomes across multiple malignancies; intentional weight loss via bariatric surgery or structured lifestyle programs reverses these metabolic perturbations and reduces cancer risk and recurrence, particularly in breast cancer, by lowering insulin, leptin, and inflammatory markers.
key_findings: Each 5 kg/m² increase in BMI raises post‑menopausal breast cancer risk by ~10% (IARC meta‑analysis).
Obesity at cancer diagnosis is associated with >20% higher breast cancer‑specific mortality across 36 studies.
Bariatric surgery reduces overall cancer incidence by ~40% and obesity‑related cancers (breast ~50% reduction, colorectal ~60% reduction).
In the Breast Cancer Weight Loss Trial, the lifestyle intervention achieved a mean 6% weight loss at 1 yr, with 50% of participants losing ≥5% body weight versus 12% in controls.
Weight loss lowered fasting insulin by 21% and reduced HOMA‑IR by 25% at 6 mo, indicating reversal of insulin resistance.
Metabolic improvements (insulin, leptin, CRP) were observed without yet having invasive disease‑free survival data, which are pending.
open_questions: Will randomized weight‑loss trials demonstrate a causal reduction in cancer incidence and mortality comparable to bariatric surgery?
Which metabolic biomarkers (e.g., insulin vs leptin) are most predictive of cancer risk reduction after weight loss?
Can body‑composition metrics (DEXA, bioimpedance) improve risk stratification beyond BMI?

cited_papers: International Agency for Research on Cancer monograph (2022)
Oxford Overview Group breast cancer recurrence analysis (2023)
Look AHEAD trial secondary cancer outcomes (2024)

talk_title: Preclinical mechanisms of GLP‑1/GIP dual agonist tirzepatide in obesity‑driven breast, colon and endometrial cancer

speaker.name: Steve Hursting
speaker.affiliation: Lineberger Comprehensive Cancer Center, UNC Chapel Hill

targets.dependency_context: obese mouse models (C57BL/6) with MC38, EO771, PY230 tumors

biomarkers.name: fasting insulin
biomarkers.finding: obesity‑induced 50% increase reversed to control levels by tirzepatide

biomarkers.name: leptin
biomarkers.finding: ≈20‑fold elevation in obese mice reduced to baseline by tirzepatide

biomarkers.name: CD8+ T‑cell activation markers (granzyme B, PD‑1, TIM‑3)
biomarkers.finding: obesity‑induced naive phenotype (TCF7, CCR7) restored to activated phenotype by tirzepatide

biomarkers.name: antigen presentation gene set score
biomarkers.finding: suppressed in obese tumors (18/18 gene sets) rescued by tirzepatide

models.name: LKB1/p53‑deficient endometrial cancer model
models.genotype_or_key_features: obese vs lean, tumor initiation and progression arms

MOA_summary: Tirzepatide induces ~20% body‑weight loss in diet‑induced obese mice, normalizing hyperglycemia, hyperinsulinemia and hyperleptinemia; this metabolic reprogramming restores CD8⁺ T‑cell activation and antigen‑presenting cell function, reverses obesity‑driven immunosuppression, and consequently reduces tumor growth in breast, colon and endometrial cancer models, an effect that is abolished by CD8⁺ T‑cell depletion, indicating a T‑cell‑dependent mechanism.
key_findings: Tirzepatide achieved ~20% weight loss in obese C57BL/6 mice, matching human clinical trial outcomes.

Obesity‑driven tumor growth in EO771, MC38 and PY230 models was reduced to lean‑control levels by tirzepatide; pair‑feeding reproduced most of the effect, indicating weight‑loss as the primary driver.
In the LKB1/p53‑deficient endometrial model, tirzepatide reduced tumor burden even in non‑obese mice, suggesting additional metabolic effects.

Obesity suppressed CD8⁺ T‑cell activation (naive markers TCF7, CCR7) and antigen‑presentation gene sets (18/18); tirzepatide fully restored activated phenotypes (granzyme B, PD‑1) and antigen‑presentation scores.
CD8⁺ T‑cell depletion abolished tirzepatide’s anti‑tumor effect, confirming T‑cell dependence.
Tirzepatide did not affect cancer cell proliferation in vitro, consistent with low GLP‑1R/GIPR expression in human cancer cell lines.
Gut microbiome analysis showed tirzepatide enriched butyrate‑producing bacteria, potentially contributing to reduced inflammation.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: No direct cytotoxic effect of tirzepatide on cancer cells observed across a wide concentration range.
Effectiveness appears largely mediated by systemic metabolic improvement rather than tumor‑intrinsic target engagement.

combination_strategies.rationale: positive control for weight‑loss–mediated anti‑tumor effects
combination_strategies.phenotype: near‑complete tumor regression, superior to tirzepatide alone

combination_strategies.rationale: isolate appetite‑suppression vs drug‑specific effects
combination_strategies.phenotype: tumor suppression comparable to tirzepatide, indicating weight‑loss as main driver

open_questions: Which specific metabolic factor (insulin, leptin, IGF‑1) is most critical for the immunologic rescue?
Can tirzepatide synergize with checkpoint inhibitors in obese tumor models?
Does the microbiome shift contribute causally to the restored anti‑tumor immunity?

cited_papers: Hursting et al. Nat Commun preprint 2024

talk_title: Real‑world evidence of GLP‑1 receptor agonists on cancer risk, incidence, and survivorship

models.name: Medicare 15% random sample (2016‑2020)
models.genotype_or_key_features: type 2 diabetes patients, n≈86,000 GLP‑1 vs SGLT2; n≈180,000 GLP‑1 vs DPP4

models.name: OneFlorida PCORnet EHR cohort (2014‑2024)
models.genotype_or_key_features: obesity patients with/without type 2 diabetes, n≈43,000 GLP‑1 users vs non‑users

models.name: Medicare cancer survivorship sub‑cohort (5% sample)
models.genotype_or_key_features: cancer survivors with type 2 diabetes, n≈2,500

clinical_data.metric: overall cancer incidence (GLP‑1 vs non‑users, EHR cohort)
kidney cancer incidence (GLP‑1 vs SGLT2, Medicare)
endometrial cancer incidence (GLP‑1 vs DPP4, Medicare)
overall survival among cancer survivors (GLP‑1 vs DPP4, Medicare)
breast cancer‑specific survival (GLP‑1 vs DPP4, Medicare)

MOA_summary: Observational analyses of large Medicare and EHR datasets suggest that GLP‑1 receptor agonist exposure is associated with modest reductions in overall and obesity‑related cancer incidence (notably endometrial, ovarian, meningioma) and improved survival among cancer survivors, while a potential increased risk of kidney cancer emerges in type 2 diabetes cohorts, highlighting drug‑specific effects that may be mediated by weight loss, metabolic improvement, and immune modulation observed in preclinical studies.
readouts: comparative effectiveness vs SGLT2 and DPP4
key_findings: GLP‑1 users had a 17% lower overall cancer incidence (HR 0.83) compared with matched non‑users in the OneFlorida cohort.
Significant risk reductions were observed for endometrial (HR 0.71), ovarian (HR 0.74) and meningioma (HR 0.68) cancers.
In Medicare type 2 diabetes patients, GLP‑1 vs SGLT2 was associated with a 22% increased kidney cancer risk (HR 1.22).
GLP‑1 vs DPP4 showed a borderline 18% increased endometrial cancer risk (HR 1.18) but was not statistically definitive.
Among cancer survivors with diabetes, GLP‑1 exposure improved overall survival (HR 0.78) and breast cancer‑specific survival (HR 0.71) versus DPP4.
Protective effects were consistent across age groups and most pronounced in non‑Hispanic White patients.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Potential increased kidney cancer risk in diabetic patients may reflect underlying CKD susceptibility amplified by GLP‑1 therapy.
open_questions: What biological mechanisms underlie the observed kidney cancer signal in diabetic cohorts?
Do newer GLP‑1 agents (semaglutide, tirzepatide) confer stronger protective effects than older agents?
How do GLP‑1 effects differ between primary prevention (risk) and secondary prevention (survivorship) settings?

talk_title: Real-world variant-level frequencies in NSCLC from liquid biopsy testing

models.name: IQ Lung Testing cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 350,000 liquid biopsy tests across US community settings

MOA_summary: Analyzed >350,000 liquid biopsy NGS and ddPCR tests to quantify variant-level prevalence of actionable NSCLC alterations, demonstrating population-level differences from trial-enriched cohorts and highlighting rapid, non‑invasive molecular profiling across diverse ethnic groups.
key_findings: Analyzed 350,000 NSCLC liquid biopsy tests from 2015‑2025, representing community practice across 20 self-reported ethnicities.
Technical success rate >90% for both NGS and ddPCR platforms.
KRAS G12C was the most frequent SNV; EGFR L858R and exon 19 deletions were also prevalent.
CCDC6‑RET was the most common fusion; ROS1 and ALK fusions were detected at expected frequencies.
MYC copy‑number gain was the most common CNV among the panel.
Observed variant prevalences were modestly lower than literature reports, reflecting real‑world, non‑referral‑biased populations.
open_questions: How do observed prevalence differences impact selection of targeted therapies in community settings?
Can longitudinal liquid biopsy monitoring improve outcomes compared with tissue genotyping?

talk_title: Histologic landscape and prognostic grading of lung cancer in never smokers

speaker.name: Trinh Tran
speaker.affiliation: National Cancer Institute, Integrative Tumor Epidemiology Branch

MOA_summary: Characterized histologic patterns of lung cancer in never smokers using the large XELOC Lung cohort and validated the IASLC grading system for non‑mucinous adenocarcinoma, then developed a deep‑learning model on whole‑slide images to predict five‑year overall survival, revealing ancestry‑linked risk patterns.
key_findings: In 844 never‑smoker lung cancers, adenocarcinoma and carcinoid were over‑represented, while squamous and SCLC were under‑represented compared with smoker cohorts.
Combined IASLC grades 1 + 2 showed better overall survival than grade 3 (HR 0.38 in all stages; HR 0.31 in stage I‑II).
Deep‑learning model improved AUC for five‑year OS when added to clinical variables and grading (integrative model AUC > clinical‑only model).
Model identified a subset of grade 3 tumors with low risk, enriched in East Asian ancestry.
Web‑based heat‑map tool enables pathologists to visualize high‑risk histologic regions.
open_questions: What histologic features drive the deep‑learning model’s risk predictions, especially in East Asian patients?
Can the model be prospectively validated in independent registries?

talk_title: Real-world outcomes of trastuzumab deruxtecan vs chemotherapy in HER2‑amplified mCRC

models.name: TriNetX US network cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: HER2‑amplified MSS mCRC, second‑line therapy, n≈1870 per arm after propensity matching

MOA_summary: Compared real‑world overall survival of HER2‑amplified microsatellite‑stable metastatic colorectal cancer patients receiving second‑line trastuzumab deruxtecan versus standard chemotherapy, showing a late survival advantage (5‑yr OS 58% vs 49%) despite similar 1‑yr OS and higher rates of ILD and heart failure.
key_findings: Propensity‑matched cohort of 1,870 patients per arm achieved covariate balance (SMD <0.1).
Early survival similar (1‑yr OS 83% vs 85%); long‑term survival diverged favoring trastuzumab deruxtecan (5‑yr OS 58% vs 49%).
Adjusted hazard ratio for death with chemotherapy vs T‑DXd = 1.14 (caution due to early curve crossing).
Higher incidence of all‑grade interstitial lung disease (12%) and heart failure (9%) in the T‑DXd group.
Hematologic toxicities comparable; fatigue and vomiting more frequent with T‑DXd, diarrhea more common with chemotherapy.
open_questions: Will prospective second‑line trials confirm the observed long‑term OS benefit?
What monitoring protocols best mitigate ILD risk in real‑world use?

talk_title: Cancer risk with ARB vs ACE inhibitor initiation in US veterans

models.name: VHA veteran cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 2.6 M veterans, 66 baseline confounders, ARB vs ACE initiators (10% vs 90%)

MOA_summary: Emulated a target trial using VHA data to compare cancer incidence and diagnosis‑free mortality between ARB and ACE inhibitor initiators, finding a 20‑25% lower cancer risk with ARBs without differences in overall mortality.
key_findings: Cohort of 2.6 M veterans (10% ARB, 90% ACE) followed up to 10 years.
ARB initiation associated with 20‑25% lower overall cancer risk (RR 0.75‑0.80).
No significant difference in diagnosis‑free mortality between groups.
Effect stronger in patients >70 years (≈30% risk reduction).
Consistent risk reduction across major solid‑tumor subtypes.
open_questions: What biological mechanisms underlie the observed cancer risk reduction with ARBs?
Can findings be replicated in non‑veteran, more gender‑balanced populations?

talk_title: Assisted reproductive technology and risk of female hormone‑related cancers

speaker.name: Emily Peiying Lin
speaker.affiliation: Taipei Medical University & Vanderbilt University Medical Center

MOA_summary: Nationwide propensity‑matched cohort study in Taiwan linking ART procedures to subsequent incidence of breast, ovarian, and endometrial cancers, revealing modest but statistically significant increased risks for breast (HR ≈ 1.12) and ovarian (HR ≈ 1.46) cancers, with no effect on endometrial cancer.
key_findings: Cohort of 105,000 ART users and 105,000 matched non‑ART controls (mean age 47).
Breast cancer events: 1,774 (ART) vs 1,581 (non‑ART).
Ovarian cancer events: 235 (ART) vs 161 (non‑ART).
Endometrial cancer events: 261 (ART) vs 252 (non‑ART).
Adjusted HRs: breast cancer 1.12, ovarian cancer 1.46, endometrial cancer 1.03 (not significant).
Risk persisted after excluding women with endometriosis or PCOS.
open_questions: Would inclusion of family history, BMI, and screening data modify the observed associations?
Is the risk dose‑dependent on number of ART cycles?

talk_title: Real‑world clinical genomics platform for under‑represented populations

speaker.name: Shubhankur Sood
speaker.affiliation: German Cancer Research Institute (DKFZ) & High STEM

models.name: India prospective clinico‑genomic cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: Target 10,000 cancer patients, WGS, imaging, histopathology

MOA_summary: Built a scalable, modular real‑world clinical genomics platform that integrates whole‑genome sequencing, transcriptomics, imaging, and AI‑driven decision support, initially linking German precision‑oncology infrastructure with a prospective Indian patient cohort to generate globally representative multi‑omics data.
key_findings: German WGS pipeline achieved >85% clinically actionable reporting across 20 tumor types.
Pilot Indian cohort demonstrated feasibility of generating WGS, imaging, and pathology data for under‑represented patients.
AI models trained on German data successfully applied to Indian cases for therapy recommendation and risk stratification.
open_questions: How to ensure data governance and patient ownership across jurisdictions?
What cost‑effective sequencing strategies can broaden access in low‑resource settings?

talk_title: Pan‑cancer landscape of alternative lengthening of telomeres via machine‑learning on clinical sequencing

models.name: MSK‑IMPACT cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: ≈78,000 tumors, targeted panel with off‑target reads

MOA_summary: Developed an ensemble classifier that leverages off‑target reads from MSK‑IMPACT panels to predict ALT activity, applying it to 78,000 tumors to map pan‑cancer ALT prevalence, uncover novel genetic associations (e.g., RB1, POT1) and tumor‑type specific prognostic impacts.
key_findings: Classifier achieved ROC‑AUC >0.85 on held‑out validation using C‑circle assay as ground truth.
ALT prevalence recapitulated known high‑ALT cancers (glioma, sarcoma, osteosarcoma) and identified estimates for >30 additional tumor types.
ATRX/DAXX truncations enriched for ALT but many ALT‑positive tumors lacked these alterations.
Missense mutations in RB1 and POT1 associated with ALT activity in wild‑type ATRX/DAXX tumors.
ALT positivity correlated with better OS in glioma but worse OS in sarcoma and osteosarcoma.
open_questions: Can the classifier be adapted to whole‑genome or other panel platforms?
Will prospective trials of ALT‑targeted agents use this model for patient selection?

session_title: From Risk Awareness to Informed Action Advancing Cancer Communication Across Prevention and Genomic Care
talk_title: Behavioral Insights for Cancer Prevention Messaging and Evaluation of Colorectal Cancer Screening Messages

models.name: NORC AmeriSpeak panel sample
models.genotype_or_key_features: Black and White American adults, age 45-74, eligible for colorectal cancer screening, n≈2000 for message ranking; n≈2000 for RCT (≈1000 per racial group)
