speaker.affiliation: University of Pittsburgh, Department of Immunology, UPMC Hillman Cancer Center

targets.dependency_context: exhausted CD8+ T cells in tumors

biomarkers.name: PD-1
biomarkers.finding: SLC25A1 KO T cells show reduced PD-1 expression in tumor

biomarkers.name: CD39
biomarkers.finding: SLC25A1 KO T cells have lower CD39 levels versus control

models.name: adoptive transfer model
models.genotype_or_key_features: antigen-specific CD8+ T cells, tumor-bearing mice

clinical_data.value: 2‑fold increase in IFN‑γ and IL‑2 per cell

MOA_summary: Delgoff showed that chronic TCR stimulation and hypoxia upregulate the mitochondrial citrate carrier SLC25A1, shunting citrate to the cytosol where it fuels de novo lipogenesis and protein acetylation, driving lipid droplet accumulation and hyperacetylation that enforce terminal T cell exhaustion; CRISPR knockout of SLC25A1 restores mitochondrial respiration, reduces lipid droplets, enhances polyfunctionality, and improves tumor control, synergizing with PD‑1 blockade.
key_findings: Chronic TCR stimulation plus hypoxia uniquely upregulates SLC25A1 in terminally exhausted CD8+ T cells.
SLC25A1 mediates export of mitochondrial citrate, leading to cytosolic acetyl‑CoA accumulation, lipid droplet formation, and widespread protein acetylation.
CRISPR knockout of SLC25A1 increases mitochondrial respiratory capacity, glycolysis, and reduces lipid droplet burden in exhausted T cells.
SLC25A1‑deficient T cells exhibit lower PD‑1, TIM‑3, and CD39 expression, retain IL‑2 production, and display superior polyfunctionality in tumors.
Adoptive transfer of SLC25A1‑KO T cells yields greater tumor regression and synergizes with anti‑PD‑1 therapy in mouse models.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: SLC25A1 expression persists despite PD‑1 blockade, suggesting a resistance mechanism independent of checkpoint inhibition.

combination_strategies.rationale: SLC25A1 KO T cells retain function and may respond better to checkpoint release.
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced tumor regression compared to either monotherapy

combination_strategies.rationale: Pharmacologic inhibition of SLC25A1 could mimic genetic knockout.
combination_strategies.phenotype: preliminary data show improved T cell metabolic fitness in vitro

open_questions: Does SLC25A1 inhibition affect PD‑L1 expression on tumor cells via increased IFN‑γ production?
What is the impact of SLC25A1 loss on myeloid cell populations within the TME?
Can selective SLC25A1 inhibitors be developed with acceptable safety for clinical use?

talk_title: Reprogramming NK cell metabolism for enhanced adoptive cell therapy

speaker.name: Dr Madar
speaker.affiliation: MD Anderson Cancer Center, Department of Immunology

targets.dependency_context: NK cells in AML and solid tumors
NK cells for solid tumor therapy

biomarkers.name: mTOR signaling
biomarkers.finding: CIS KO NK cells show increased phospho‑S6 compared with control

models.name: Phase 1/2 CD70 CAR NK cells (TGFBR2 KO)
models.genotype_or_key_features: TGFBR2 knockout, CD70 CAR, off‑the‑shelf product

models.name: Phase 1/2 CD70 CAR NK cells (TGFBR2 KO) for RCC
models.genotype_or_key_features: same product, renal cell carcinoma patients

MOA_summary: Madar described multiple metabolic engineering strategies for NK cell ACT, including CRISPR knockout of the cytokine‑inducible checkpoint CIS and TGF‑β receptor II to boost mTOR and glycolytic pathways, and overexpression of the adenoviral E4ORF1 protein to activate PI3K/AKT/mTOR and increase citrate‑derived biosynthesis, resulting in enhanced mitochondrial mass, oxidative phosphorylation, and cytotoxicity across solid and hematologic tumor models.
key_findings: NK cells cultured in lipid‑rich APEL media acquire higher mitochondrial mass, elongated morphology, and increased spare respiratory capacity versus standard media.
CIS knockout NK cells display upregulated mTOR signaling, glycolysis, and improved survival in aggressive Raji lymphoma mouse models.
TGFBR2‑KO CD70 CAR NK cells demonstrate superior anti‑AML activity in preclinical models and have entered a Phase 1/2 trial (NCT‑pending).
E4ORF1 overexpression in NK cells enhances glycolysis, OXPHOS, fatty‑acid oxidation, and glutaminolysis, leading to robust killing of multiple solid tumor lines even under nutrient starvation.
AMPK activity is required for the E4ORF1‑mediated metabolic boost; inhibition of AMPK abrogates the enhanced cytotoxic phenotype.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: TGF‑β signaling in the TME suppresses NK cell metabolism; TGFBR2 knockout overcomes this resistance.

combination_strategies.rationale: IL‑15 upregulates metabolic pathways; combined with CIS KO to further boost mTOR.
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced NK cell expansion and persistence in vivo

combination_strategies.rationale: Synergize with E4ORF1‑driven metabolic reprogramming.
combination_strategies.phenotype: further increased oxidative phosphorylation and tumor killing

open_questions: Which specific media components in APEL drive the observed metabolic reprogramming?
Can E4ORF1 overexpression be safely applied to CAR T cells without oncogenic transformation?
How does metabolic reprogramming affect NK cell receptor repertoire and ADCC function?

patent_or_ip_hints: CRISPR‑mediated TGFBR2 knockout NK cell IP filing 2023

talk_title: STK-12 IL-2 agonist plus pembrolizumab and chemotherapy in PD-L1 negative NSCLC

biomarkers.name: PD-L1 negative
biomarkers.finding: 89% of enrolled patients were PD-L1 negative

biomarkers.name: STK11/KEAP1 co-mutation
biomarkers.finding: ORR 50% in this subgroup, median PFS 5.5 months

models.name: Phase I STK-12 + pembrolizumab + carboplatin
models.genotype_or_key_features: Stage 4 non-squamous NSCLC, 89% PD-L1 negative, enriched STK11/KEAP1/SMARCA4 alterations

MOA_summary: STK-12 is a recombinant IL-2 partial agonist that selectively expands antigen-experienced CD8+ T cells while sparing NK and Treg cells, enhancing IFNγ and IP-10 production and clonal TCR expansion, thereby improving response rates when combined with pembrolizumab and chemotherapy in PD-L1 negative NSCLC.
key_findings: Overall response rate of 50% and disease control rate of 97% in a high‑risk PD‑L1 negative NSCLC cohort (n=39).
In STK11/KEAP1/SMARCA4 altered subgroup, ORR increased to 61% with 100% disease control.
STK‑12 induced robust expansion of 4‑1BB+ CD8 T cells and >10‑fold clonal expansion of pre‑existing TCR clones.
Minimal classic IL‑2 toxicities observed; most common AE was reversible maculopapular rash in ~50% of patients.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: STK‑12 did not expand NK cells or Tregs, preserving a favorable safety profile.
Efficacy appears limited in patients lacking pre‑existing antigen‑experienced T cells, suggesting dependence on recall responses.

combination_strategies.rationale: Checkpoint blockade provides antigen exposure while STK‑12 expands effector T cells.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression with ORR 50% vs historical 23‑32%

combination_strategies.rationale: Chemotherapy induces immunogenic cell death, increasing antigen availability for STK‑12‑expanded T cells.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Manageable safety profile, no DLTs observed

open_questions: Does STK‑12 generate de‑novo neoantigen responses or rely solely on pre‑existing clones?
What is the optimal dosing schedule to maximize T‑cell expansion while minimizing rash?

talk_title: Tertiary lymphoid structures predict response to durvalumab, trastuzumab, pertuzumab in HER2‑enriched early breast cancer

biomarkers.name: TLS-associated niche fraction
biomarkers.finding: Higher niche fraction correlated with PCR (pCR) after DTP therapy

biomarkers.name: TIL percentage
biomarkers.finding: Patients achieving PCR had higher baseline TILs and greater increase post‑treatment

MOA_summary: Durvalumab combined with HER2‑targeted antibodies induces formation of tertiary lymphoid structures (TLS) composed of Tfh‑like CD4+ cells, mature dendritic cells, and proliferating B cells; these TLS niches correlate with enhanced CD8+ T‑cell infiltration and higher pathologic complete response in HER2‑enriched breast cancer.
key_findings: TLS‑associated immune module (Tfh‑like CD4+, LAMB3+ mature DCs, cycling B cells) is enriched in pretreatment tumors of patients achieving PCR.
Spatial analysis shows co‑localization of these cell types within 20‑µm niches, defining TLS structures.
Higher TLS niche fraction predicts PCR (p<0.01) and correlates with increased intratumoral CD8+ T cells post‑treatment.
CellChat indicates CD4+ Tfh cells activate B cells via IL‑2, CD40, and ICAM pathways within TLS.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: High‑endothelial venules and specific dendritic subsets (XCR1+) were not fully characterized, suggesting heterogeneity in TLS composition.

combination_strategies.rationale: Checkpoint blockade releases PD‑1 inhibition, allowing TLS‑primed T cells to act.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic increase in PCR rate to 49% vs historical <30% with HER2‑targeted therapy alone

open_questions: Do post‑treatment biopsies show expansion of TLS structures, confirming dynamic induction?
Can specific TLS‑associated taxa be modulated to further improve response?

talk_title: Inhibiting nonsense‑mediated decay (SMG1) to enhance antitumor immunity

biomarkers.name: PD‑1+ CD8 T cells
biomarkers.finding: Increased frequency after SMG1 inhibition in patient‑derived tumor fragments

models.name: Various CRC and lung cancer lines

MOA_summary: SMG1 inhibition blocks nonsense‑mediated decay, stabilizing frameshift‑derived PTC transcripts that encode highly immunogenic neoantigens; these are presented on MHC‑I, leading to CD8+ T‑cell activation and tumor control, synergizing with anti‑PD‑1 therapy.
key_findings: SMG1 inhibition increased PD‑1+ CD8 T‑cell activation in ex vivo tumor fragments without affecting adjacent normal tissue.
In Lewis lung carcinoma, SMG1 inhibitor monotherapy matched anti‑PD‑1 efficacy; combination yielded superior tumor control.
Immunopeptidomics identified six neoantigen peptides up‑regulated only after NMD inhibition; four were immunogenic in vitro.
MHC‑I blockade abolished T‑cell activation induced by SMG1 inhibition, confirming antigen‑dependent mechanism.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: SMG1 inhibition showed minimal toxicity in normal tissue ex vivo and no significant weight loss in mice, suggesting tumor selectivity.

combination_strategies.rationale: Checkpoint blockade may unleash T cells primed by newly presented NMD‑derived neoantigens.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Superior tumor control versus either monotherapy in mouse model

open_questions: Will chronic SMG1 inhibition lead to autoimmunity due to presentation of self‑derived PTC peptides?
What is the optimal dosing schedule to maximize tumor antigen presentation while minimizing potential off‑target effects?

talk_title: High‑fiber dietary intervention improves immunotherapy outcomes in melanoma

biomarkers.name: Bacteroides spp.
biomarkers.finding: Enriched in high‑fiber arm and associated with response

biomarkers.name: Secondary/primary bile acid ratio
biomarkers.finding: Decreased ratio in high‑fiber arm correlates with improved outcomes

biomarkers.name: Classical monocytes (immunosuppressive cluster)
biomarkers.finding: Reduced frequency over time in high‑fiber arm

models.name: Randomized double‑blind high‑fiber vs control diet with ICB
models.genotype_or_key_features: Metastatic and neoadjuvant melanoma patients receiving standard checkpoint blockade

clinical_data.value: Numerically higher in high‑fiber arm (exact % not disclosed)

MOA_summary: A high‑fiber, whole‑food diet reshapes the gut microbiome toward response‑associated taxa, lowers secondary/primary bile acid ratios, reduces immunosuppressive classical monocytes, and enhances intratumoral CD8+ T‑cell and cytotoxic lymphocyte infiltration, thereby improving checkpoint inhibitor efficacy in melanoma.
key_findings: High‑fiber diet did not alter overall alpha/beta diversity but increased patient‑specific enrichment of known response‑associated taxa.
Serum bile acid profiling showed a significant decrease in secondary/primary bile acid ratio in the high‑fiber arm.
Classical monocyte immunosuppressive cluster decreased over time only in high‑fiber patients.
Digital spatial profiling revealed greater CD8+ T‑cell and cytotoxic lymphocyte density post‑intervention in responders receiving high‑fiber diet.

combination_strategies.rationale: Diet‑induced microbiome and metabolic changes prime the TME for ICB response.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Higher response rates and lower cutaneous toxicity

open_questions: Would isolated fermentable fiber supplements recapitulate the benefits of whole‑food high‑fiber diets?
What are the long‑term durability of immune benefits after cessation of the diet?

talk_title: IL‑17‑producing TH17 cells drive neurotoxicity in immune‑related adverse events of the CNS

biomarkers.name: POLR6+ CD4+ PD‑1+ Tfh‑like cells
biomarkers.finding: Higher burden correlates with increased neuroinflammation on PET and worse cognitive scores

biomarkers.name: IL‑17 in CSF
biomarkers.finding: Elevated in IRAE‑CNS and reduced after anti‑IL‑17 therapy

models.name: Multi‑center real‑world IRAE‑CNS cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: Various solid tumors treated with ICIs; excluded brain metastases

MOA_summary: A POLR6‑positive CD4+ PD‑1+ TH17‑like cell subset expands in the CSF of ICI‑treated patients, secreting IL‑17 and other inflammatory mediators that drive neuroinflammation; blockade of IL‑17A mitigates PET‑detected inflammation and improves neurologic outcomes.
key_findings: Identified a disease‑specific POLR6+ CD4+ PD‑1+ TH17‑like cell cluster producing IL‑17, MCP1, CCL11, granzyme B, etc.
Higher POLR6+ cell burden correlated with greater PET‑measured neuroinflammation and worse cognitive scores.
Clonal T‑cell expansions persisted up to 12 months, indicating ongoing antigen‑driven response, with NMDA‑R1 as a dominant neuroantigen.
Retrospective anti‑IL‑17A treatment was associated with reduced neuroinflammation and improved neurologic function without affecting cancer outcomes.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target downstream cytokine of pathogenic TH17 cells to reduce CNS toxicity.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced PET signal and improved cognition in treated patients

open_questions: What is the source of NMDA‑R1 antigen driving TH17 expansion?
Can prospective anti‑IL‑17A therapy prevent IRAE‑CNS when given prophylactically?

talk_title: NAT10‑MYC loop drives autophagy‑dependent MHC‑I downregulation and immunotherapy resistance in MSS colorectal cancer

biomarkers.name: NAT10 expression
biomarkers.finding: High NAT10 correlates with MSS status, low immune score, and poor survival

biomarkers.name: MHC‑I surface expression
biomarkers.finding: Increases after NAT10 knockout or inhibition

models.name: Syngeneic CRC tumor in immunocompetent mice
Fudan and TMDU CRC cohorts
models.genotype_or_key_features: Patient tumors with NAT10 expression data

MOA_summary: NAT10 acetylates RNA (ac4C) stabilizing autophagy genes (ATG3, ATG5, Beclin‑1), leading to increased autophagic flux that degrades MHC‑I molecules; a MYC‑NAT10 positive feedback loop sustains this program, causing immune evasion in MSS CRC, which can be reversed by NAT10 inhibition to restore MHC‑I and sensitize tumors to anti‑PD‑1 therapy.
key_findings: NAT10 is overexpressed in MSS CRC and predicts worse overall survival.
NAT10 knockout increases CD8+ T‑cell infiltration and tumor regression in mice.
Combination of NAT10 inhibition with anti‑PD‑1 yields complete tumor regression in 80% of mice.
MYC transcriptionally up‑regulates NAT10; NAT10‑mediated ac4C stabilizes MYC mRNA, forming a feed‑forward loop.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: NAT10 inhibition may affect normal cells due to global RNA modification, but preclinical data show limited toxicity.

combination_strategies.rationale: Restored MHC‑I expression after NAT10 inhibition enables effective checkpoint blockade.
combination_strategies.phenotype: 80% complete regression in mouse model

open_questions: Will NAT10 inhibition synergize with other immunotherapies (e.g., CTLA‑4 blockade) in MSS CRC?
What biomarkers best predict response to NAT10‑targeted therapy in patients?

talk_title: Versican proteolysis restores tumor matrix signaling to reverse checkpoint resistance

biomarkers.name: VCAN proteolysis level
biomarkers.finding: High VCAN proteolysis correlates with increased CD8+ T‑cell infiltration and better response to pembrolizumab in colorectal cancer trial

MOA_summary: Proteolytic cleavage of versican generates bioactive fragments that promote formation of immunotriads (CD4+ Tfh‑like cells, CD8+ T cells, dendritic cells) within the tumor stroma, enhancing CD8+ T‑cell infiltration and synergizing with checkpoint blockade to overcome immune exclusion.
key_findings: Versican overexpression in MC38 tumors induced immunotriad formation and cured >70% of mice with anti‑PD‑1 alone.
In VCAN‑high LSC tumors, versican plus dual checkpoint blockade (PD‑1 + CTLA‑4) was required for tumor control.
Exogenous versican delivered via LNP mRNA or recombinant protein showed monotherapy activity in VCAN‑low models and synergized with ICB.
Efficacy depended on cDC1 dendritic cells and CD8+ T cells; depletion abrogated therapeutic benefit.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Potential concern that matrix remodeling could facilitate tumor cell dissemination; however, versican fragments appear to act primarily on immune cell recruitment rather than tumor invasion.

combination_strategies.rationale: Versican‑induced TLS‑like structures prime T cells for checkpoint blockade.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Monotherapy effect in VCAN‑low tumors; enhanced cure rates with PD‑1 alone

combination_strategies.rationale: VCAN‑high tumors require broader checkpoint inhibition to overcome deep immune exclusion.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression in LSC model

open_questions: Which tumor types beyond CRC and lung cancer exhibit VCAN proteolysis deficiency and could benefit from versican therapy?
What is the optimal dosing schedule for versican protein versus mRNA delivery to maximize immune activation while minimizing potential pro‑metastatic effects?

talk_title: Implementation planning for lung cancer screening in HIV care settings

models.name: electronic health record data of people living with HIV
models.genotype_or_key_features: approximately 7,000 HIV+ patients with lung cancer, demographic and socioeconomic variables

MOA_summary: Dr. Islam described a pre-implementation research package (R01) that uses multilevel epidemiologic, causal inference, and qualitative methods to characterize LDCT lung cancer screening uptake, barriers, and patient decision-making among people with HIV, with the goal of informing tailored implementation strategies.
key_findings: Among ~7,000 HIV+ lung cancer patients, 75% were diagnosed at metastatic stage.
People with HIV had a 15% increased risk of metastatic lung cancer diagnosis compared to non‑HIV patients.
Socioeconomic factors (ZIP‑code education, income, care location) predicted higher probability of metastatic diagnosis.
The R01 aims to (1) describe LDCT screening patterns, (2) estimate direct/indirect effects of social determinants, and (3) explore patient decision‑making via qualitative interviews.

talk_title: Participatory approaches to prioritize colorectal cancer screening for patients with diabetes in FQHCs

speaker.name: Dina Leo O'Malley
speaker.affiliation: Rutgers Robert Wood Johnson Medical School, Associate Professor

models.name: Federally qualified health centers (FQHC) patient population
models.genotype_or_key_features: patients with diabetes, age 45‑75, screening eligibility

MOA_summary: Dr. O'Malley used implementation mapping with a multi‑stakeholder planning group to identify barriers and select four prioritized strategies (champion identification, low‑literacy educational materials, quality monitoring systems, and implementation meetings) to improve colorectal cancer screening among diabetic patients in safety‑net clinics.
key_findings: Implementation planning group generated 36 potential strategies, refined to top five themes (patient education/communication, clinical flow).
Top four selected strategies: identify/prep champions; develop low‑literacy educational materials; organize quality monitoring (UDS metrics); conduct implementation meetings.
Pilot clinic A achieved 77% screening among diabetic patients (higher among uncontrolled diabetes), using champion‑driven Cologuard outreach.
Pilot clinic B, with lower baseline screening (28%), improved to 60% using adapted postcards, QR‑code videos, and staff training.
Uncontrolled diabetes patients comprised the largest unscreened subgroup in both clinics.

talk_title: Sustaining and scaling HPV vaccination implementation in safety‑net settings

speaker.name: Jennifer Tsui
speaker.affiliation: University of Southern California, Professor, Department of Population and Public Health Sciences

models.name: FQHC clinics in Southern California (Ultimed, AltaMed, Chinatown Service Center, Riverside San Bernardino IHS)
models.genotype_or_key_features: adolescent patients ages 9‑12, diverse ethnic groups, safety‑net settings

MOA_summary: Dr. Tsui presented a multi‑year R37/R01 project that implemented audit‑and‑feedback, provider communication training (Announcement Approach), and co‑designed staff workflow tools to increase HPV vaccine initiation, demonstrated sustained provider practices and identified contextual factors for scaling to other FQHC systems.
readouts: surveys of clinic staff and providers
key_findings: After 12‑month implementation, HPV vaccine initiation increased significantly at three pilot clinics, driven by higher uptake at ages 9‑10.
18‑month follow‑up showed sustained provider discussion of vaccination at age 9 (88% of staff) and increased confidence addressing parental hesitancy (69%).
CSAT scores indicated staff found the strategies easy to use, integrable into workflow, and clinically meaningful (median 5‑7/7).
Turnover of clinic staff remained a major barrier to sustainment; half of staff changed within 24 months.
Scale‑out to AltaMed, Chinatown Service Center, and Riverside San Bernardino IHS required adaptation of training modality (in‑person, virtual, hybrid) and cultural tailoring of materials.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: High staff turnover limits long‑term maintenance of implementation gains.

combination_strategies.rationale: provide data-driven performance feedback to motivate improvement
combination_strategies.phenotype: increased HPV vaccine initiation rates

combination_strategies.rationale: standardize provider recommendation to improve parental acceptance
combination_strategies.phenotype: higher provider confidence and sustained discussion at age 9

combination_strategies.rationale: embed vaccination prompts and education into routine practice
combination_strategies.phenotype: integration into EMR and sustained use across sites

talk_title: The Role of pks+ E. coli in Intestinal Stem Cell Plasticity and Early Onset of Colorectal Cancer

biomarkers.name: SBS88 mutation signature
biomarkers.finding: Detected in early-onset colorectal cancers and enriched in tumors from patients <50 years

biomarkers.name: pks+ E. coli colonization
biomarkers.finding: Higher prevalence in early-onset colorectal cancer patients

models.name: inverted polarity human colon organoid
models.genotype_or_key_features: exposed apical surface to pks+ E. coli

MOA_summary: pks+ E. coli produces colibactin, a genotoxin that creates AT-rich DNA lesions (SBS88) and mutates APC splice sites in young patients; colibactin also binds AT-rich motifs, displacing CDX2 and repressing differentiation programs, thereby promoting a shift of epithelial cells toward an LGR5+ intestinal stem cell state, enhancing regenerative capacity and tumor initiation in the young colon.
key_findings: Developed a targeted-exome assay to detect the colibactin SBS88 signature in >80,000 clinical tumors, finding enrichment in colorectal, esophageal, bladder, and head/neck cancers.
Colibactin signature-positive tumors occur in significantly younger patients and are more often located in the rectosigmoid region.
Identified a specific APC splice-site mutation occurring in the AT-rich colibactin context, enriched in young-onset colorectal cancers, suggesting colibactin-driven initiation.
Inverted-polarity organoid model exposed to pks+ E. coli recapitulated colibactin mutational signatures and induced upregulation of LGR5 and ASCL2 stem cell markers.
pks+ E. coli exposure increased functional stem cell capacity (organoid formation) and proliferation compared to Δpks controls.
Thermal proteomics and ATAC-seq revealed colibactin-induced repression of AT-rich transcription factor motifs, notably CDX2, leading to loss of differentiation and promotion of stemness.
Young colon organoids showed greater regenerative response to colibactin-induced injury than aged organoids, linking age-dependent stem cell capacity to early-onset tumorigenesis.
open_questions: Does inhibition of colibactin production or colonization by pks+ E. coli prevent early-onset colorectal cancer in high-risk populations?
Can therapeutic restoration of CDX2 activity counteract colibactin-induced stemness and tumor initiation?
What host factors (diet, microbiome composition) modulate susceptibility to colibactin-mediated mutagenesis?

talk_title: Towards New Cancer Medicines with Degraders of Chromatin
MOA_summary: Danette presented a framework that quantifies degrader kinetics by measuring degradation rate versus Dmax, defining an inflection point (the "rate cliff") that predicts complete target loss across multiple chromatin regulators; fast degraders with high rate efficiency translate to robust in vivo pharmacodynamics independent of DC50.
key_findings: A universal rate‑versus‑Dmax relationship was observed across diverse degraders (BRD9, EP300, CBP) regardless of target or cell line.
The rate inflection point (0.7–1.8) predicts ≥85–90% Dmax; compounds below this "cliff" show target recovery.
AUC of rate‑versus‑concentration curves distinguishes compounds with identical DC50 but up to five‑fold differences in maximal degradation rate.
Faster degraders exhibit higher ubiquitination levels and form more stable ternary complexes with slower koff rates measured by SPR.
In vivo PK/PD mapping of CBP degraders demonstrates that dosing at Cmax and trough samples the maximal degradation rate, leading to robust target loss in mouse models.

talk_title: Designing Effective Degrader Therapeutics: What Early Clinical Experience Has Taught Us

targets.dependency_context: relapsed CLL with BTK resistance mutations

biomarkers.name: BTK resistance mutations
biomarkers.finding: Degrader retained activity against all tested BTK resistance mutations, unlike inhibitors which showed gaps in coverage

models.name: Phase 1 A/B BTK degrader trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: Relapsed CLL patients, many with prior BTK inhibitor exposure, BTK resistance mutations, subset with CNS disease

clinical_data.population: heavily pretreated CLL, including BTK‑inhibitor failures

clinical_data.value: No increase in AE rate at 600 mg vs all doses

MOA_summary: Bexipridil is a BTK degrader that induces proteasomal removal of BTK, achieving sub‑stoichiometric target clearance; this eliminates residual kinase activity and overcomes a broad spectrum of BTK resistance mutations, providing deeper and more durable responses than BTK inhibitors.
key_findings: 83% overall response rate in a heavily pre‑treated CLL population, including patients who progressed on the approved BTK inhibitor pirtobrutinib.
Median duration of response reached 20 months, exceeding the ~12‑month DOR reported for the best current inhibitor.
Degrader retained activity against all known BTK resistance mutations, whereas inhibitors showed gaps in coverage.
Higher dose (600 mg) improved progression‑free survival without increasing adverse‑event frequency, indicating a wide therapeutic index.
Comprehensive proteomics showed BTK as one of the top‑down‑regulated proteins with minimal off‑target degradation.
No QT prolongation observed in healthy volunteers or patients, differentiating this degrader from earlier molecules.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: A novel BTK A428D mutation abolishes binding of both inhibitors and degraders, representing a potential resistance mechanism.
open_questions: What therapeutic strategies can overcome the emergent A428D BTK mutation that blocks degrader binding?

talk_title: First‑in‑Class Dual AR/ARV7 Molecular Glue Degraders

targets.dependency_context: CRPC cells expressing AR splice variant V7

biomarkers.name: ARV7 expression
biomarkers.finding: High ARV7 levels predict resistance to enzalutamide; compound 15 degrades ARV7 and restores sensitivity

models.name: LNCaP 95
models.genotype_or_key_features: AR ligand‑binding mutation + ARV7 expression, enzalutamide‑resistant

models.name: 22RV1
models.genotype_or_key_features: Endogenous ARV7 and full‑length AR, CRPC xenograft source

models.name: not specified
models.genotype_or_key_features: patient‑derived CLL samples used for proteomics (for context only)

MOA_summary: Compounds 15 and 64 act as molecular glues that bind the N‑terminal domain of AR/ARV7 and recruit a Cullin‑RING ligase complex (DDB1‑containing) to induce proteasomal degradation of both isoforms, thereby suppressing AR‑driven transcription and overcoming enzalutamide resistance.
key_findings: High‑throughput screen of 170,000 compounds identified 13 hits; lead chemotype 7907 degraded both AR and ARV7 via proteasome.
Medicinal chemistry optimization yielded analogs 15 and 64 with sub‑micromolar EC50s and rapid (≤4 h) degradation of both proteins.
Compound 15 suppressed AR‑driven transcription in 22RV1 cells, outperforming enzalutamide and ARV110 in RNA‑seq AR‑score analysis.
In vivo, oral dosing of compounds 15 and 64 markedly inhibited growth of ARV7‑positive 22RV1 xenografts, achieving greater tumor regression than enzalutamide or ARV110.
Mechanistic studies mapped activity to the AR N‑terminal TAL1 and AF1 subdomains; degradation required Cullin‑RING ligase activity (blocked by MLN4924) and was partially reversed by DDB1 knockout.
Global proteomics showed AR and ARV7 among the top‑down‑regulated proteins with minimal off‑target effects; compounds were well tolerated with no weight loss.
open_questions: What is the precise E3 ligase complex (beyond DDB1) mediating degradation?
Can off‑target degradation be further minimized in primary prostate cells?

talk_title: Role of Sex and Ancestry in Inflammaging, Lymphomagenesis and Its Precision Therapy

speaker.name: Ari Melnick
speaker.affiliation: Jose Carreras Leukemia Research Center, Barcelona

biomarkers.name: TLR7 expression
biomarkers.finding: higher in female BN2 DLBCLs and correlates with IRAK inhibitor sensitivity

biomarkers.name: X chromosome hypomethylation
biomarkers.finding: female BN2 DLBCLs show reduced X methylation and increased TLR7 expression

biomarkers.name: Senescence-associated GLB1 staining
biomarkers.finding: strongly increased in African ancestry DLBCLs and associated with worse survival

models.name: SPEN truncation + Notch2 gain-of-function
models.genotype_or_key_features: SPEN haploinsufficient, Notch2 truncation, female mice

models.name: DLBCL patient cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: ~4,500 sequenced patients, ancestry and sex annotations

clinical_data.population: BN2 DLBCL with SPEN+Notch2 double mutation

clinical_data.metric: hazard ratio for high vs low GDF15

MOA_summary: Sex- and ancestry-specific mutations (SPEN, Notch2, ATM, MGA, SETD2) reprogram B-cell differentiation toward aged, auto-reactive AIBCs, driving aggressive DLBCL phenotypes; female BN2 DLBCLs exhibit X-chromosome instability and heightened TLR7 signaling, rendering them sensitive to IRAK inhibition, while African ancestry DLBCLs display senescence-associated secretory phenotypes linked to SETD2 loss, suggesting precision therapeutic windows.
key_findings: SPEN truncation co-occurs with Notch2 truncation in BN2 DLBCLs and predicts inferior survival in a 4,500-patient cohort.
Female BN2 DLBCL mice develop X‑chromosome hypomethylation, increased TLR7 expression, and are more sensitive to IRAK inhibitors than males.
African ancestry DLBCLs harbor frequent ATM, MGA, and SETD2 mutations (>50%) and show elevated senescence (GLB1) and exhausted CD4 T‑cell infiltrates.
SETD2‑heterozygous DLBCL cells are hypersensitive to a clinical SETD2 inhibitor, offering a potential ancestry‑specific therapy.
IRAK inhibition reduces tumor growth in female BN2 DLBCL xenografts, supporting a sex‑specific precision therapy approach.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Female BN2 DLBCLs display selective IRAK inhibitor sensitivity due to X‑linked TLR7 overexpression.
SETD2‑mutant African ancestry DLBCLs are resistant to standard therapies but vulnerable to SETD2 inhibition.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target TLR7‑driven signaling in female BN2 DLBCLs with X‑instability
combination_strategies.phenotype: significant tumor regression in female mice

combination_strategies.rationale: Exploit synthetic lethality in SETD2‑heterozygous African ancestry DLBCLs
combination_strategies.phenotype: tumor shrinkage in xenograft models

open_questions: What molecular mechanisms link X‑chromosome hypomethylation to TLR7 overexpression in female BN2 DLBCLs?
Can GDF15 modulation improve outcomes in the described DLBCL subtypes?
Are there additional sex‑specific vulnerabilities beyond IRAK signaling?

talk_title: Age-Associated Molecular Landscapes and Therapeutic Responses in Breast Cancer

speaker.name: Sandra McAllister
speaker.affiliation: Brigham Hospital and Harvard Medical School

biomarkers.name: EMT gene signature
biomarkers.finding: enriched in older triple‑negative breast cancer tumors and mouse models

biomarkers.name: HLA‑E / A2Q1 expression
biomarkers.finding: higher in aged tumors, associated with immune exhaustion

models.name: ELEVATE study
models.genotype_or_key_features: women >70 years, longitudinal tissue and blood collection, n≈1500

models.name: NET1 transplantable TNBC model
models.genotype_or_key_features: young (8‑10 wk) vs aged (12‑18 mo) mice

models.name: NET1 barcoded clones
models.genotype_or_key_features: single‑cell derived populations with molecular barcodes

clinical_data.value: significant tumor regression in young mice, no significant benefit in aged mice
clinical_data.population: young vs aged mice with NET1 tumors

clinical_data.value: extended in aged mice with panobinostat + anti‑PD‑L1

MOA_summary: Aging reprograms breast cancer transcriptomes: older ER‑positive tumors show reduced immune and metabolic pathways, while older triple‑negative tumors acquire an inflamed, EMT‑driven phenotype; age‑selected subclones (e.g., barcode 25) become resistant to paclitaxel, but are sensitized to HDAC inhibition combined with checkpoint blockade, revealing age‑specific therapeutic vulnerabilities.
key_findings: Older breast cancer patients exhibit distinct molecular landscapes: ER+ tumors lose immune/metabolic activity, while TNBCs gain an inflamed EMT phenotype.
In mouse models, aged mice show slower NET1 tumor growth but reduced response to paclitaxel + anti‑PD‑L1, despite higher CD8 infiltration and Treg expansion.
Barcode 25 subclone is enriched by age and paclitaxel, displays intrinsic paclitaxel resistance, and can be eliminated by HDAC inhibition.
Panobinostat combined with anti‑PD‑L1 dramatically extends survival in aged mice, whereas the same combination is less effective in young mice without chemotherapy.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Barcode 25 subclone confers paclitaxel resistance and limits combination efficacy in aged mice.
HDAC inhibitors selectively target the age‑enriched resistant clone.

combination_strategies.rationale: standard chemotherapy for TNBC
combination_strategies.phenotype: robust tumor regression in young mice, minimal benefit in aged mice

combination_strategies.rationale: immune checkpoint blockade
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced response in young mice when combined with chemo; limited effect in aged mice

combination_strategies.rationale: eliminate age‑selected paclitaxel‑resistant clone
combination_strategies.phenotype: significant survival benefit in aged mice when combined with anti‑PD‑L1

open_questions: What mechanisms drive the EMT and immune exhaustion programs uniquely in aged TNBC?
Can selective HDAC isoform inhibitors improve efficacy while reducing toxicity in older patients?
How does the middle‑aged cohort (40‑60 yr) molecularly differ from young and old groups?

talk_title: Targeting GDF15‑Mediated Immunosuppression to Overcome Age‑Driven Immunotherapy Resistance in Pancreatic Cancer

biomarkers.name: circulating GDF15 level
biomarkers.finding: higher in PDAC patients vs healthy controls and predicts poorer OS

biomarkers.name: CD8 T‑cell infiltration
biomarkers.finding: increased after GDF15 neutralization in aged mice

models.name: orthotopic KPC PDAC model
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS^G12D; TP53 loss, young (8‑10 wk) vs aged (12‑18 mo) C57BL/6

models.name: GVAX vaccine trial PDAC patients
models.genotype_or_key_features: GDF15 high vs low, survival analysis

clinical_data.population: aged KPC mice treated with anti‑GDF15
high GDF15 PDAC patients in GVAX trial
aged mice vaccinated with KRAS peptide + anti‑GDF15

MOA_summary: Aging elevates systemic and tumor‑derived GDF15, which suppresses CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration and impairs vaccine‑induced neoantigen responses in PDAC; neutralizing GDF15 restores T‑cell priming and synergizes with KRAS peptide vaccination and checkpoint blockade to achieve tumor regression in aged mouse models.
key_findings: Aged mice develop larger orthotopic KPC tumors and exhibit markedly reduced CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration compared with young mice.
Circulating GDF15 is low in young mice but rises sharply in aged mice and further increases after tumor implantation.
Genetic or antibody‑mediated GDF15 neutralization halves tumor size in aged mice and restores CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration; effect is lost after T‑cell depletion.
GDF15 neutralization enhances KRAS mutant peptide vaccine‑induced IFN‑γ T‑cell responses in aged mice.
In a PDAC GVAX trial, high tumor GDF15 expression correlates with ~2‑fold worse overall survival, independent of age.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: GDF15‑mediated immunosuppression is age‑specific; young mice show minimal response to GDF15 blockade.
Tumor‑intrinsic KRAS vaccine efficacy is limited in aged hosts unless GDF15 is neutralized.

combination_strategies.rationale: release T‑cell inhibition in the GDF15‑high TME
combination_strategies.phenotype: modest tumor control alone; enhanced when combined with GDF15 neutralization

combination_strategies.rationale: prime neoantigen‑specific T cells
combination_strategies.phenotype: significant increase in IFN‑γ ELISpot responses and tumor regression when combined with GDF15 blockade

open_questions: What is the precise receptor and downstream signaling pathway by which GDF15 suppresses CD8⁺ T‑cell trafficking in PDAC?
Can GDF15 neutralization improve responses to standard chemotherapy or other immunotherapies in human PDAC trials?
Are there biomarkers beyond circulating GDF15 that predict benefit from GDF15‑targeted therapy?

talk_title: Evasion of Hepatic ILC1 Immunosurveillance Drives Breast Cancer Liver Metastasis

targets.dependency_context: regulates DR5 expression in breast cancer cells

models.name: high-fat diet steatotic liver model
models.genotype_or_key_features: fed high-fat diet 4 months, IV injection of breast cancer cells

MOA_summary: Hepatic ILC1s mediate immune surveillance of breast cancer liver metastasis via TRAIL-DR5 signaling; loss of DR5 or depletion of ILC1s enhances liver tumor burden, while GLP-1 agonist restores ILC1 numbers and reduces metastasis.
key_findings: High-fat diet mice develop increased breast cancer liver metastasis compared to control diet.
RAG2 gamma knockout mice show increased liver and lung tumor burden, implicating innate lymphocytes in surveillance.
Selective depletion of conventional NK cells increases lung but not liver metastasis, indicating liver ILC1s are essential for hepatic surveillance.
ILC1s mediate tumor killing via TRAIL binding to DR5 on cancer cells; DR5 loss increases liver metastasis.
GLP-1 receptor agonist restores hepatic ILC1 numbers in steatotic mice and reduces liver tumor burden.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: DR5 loss in tumor cells enables escape from ILC1-mediated killing.

combination_strategies.rationale: Restores hepatic ILC1 numbers reduced by steatosis
combination_strategies.phenotype: Decreased liver metastasis in high-fat diet mice

open_questions: What upstream signals regulate ILC1 activation in the liver microenvironment?
Can GLP-1 agonist synergize with checkpoint blockade in metastatic breast cancer?

talk_title: Neutrophil Extracellular Trap Inhibition Mitigates Tumor Necrosis and Metastasis in Colorectal Cancer

speaker.name: Sepideh Gholamin
speaker.affiliation: Cold Spring Harbor Laboratory / Surgical Oncology

biomarkers.name: citH3
biomarkers.finding: Reduced citH3 staining in tumors treated with disulfiram

models.name: AKPS organoid model
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS, APC, TP53, SMAD4 mutations; MSS colorectal cancer

MOA_summary: NETs accumulate in necrotic regions of colorectal tumors, driving hypoxia, EMT, and metastasis; pharmacologic (disulfiram) or genetic (PAD4 KO) inhibition of NET formation reduces necrosis, EMT signaling, and liver metastases, and enhances chemotherapy efficacy.
key_findings: PAD4 knockout mice show decreased tumor necrosis and fewer liver metastases compared to wild-type.
Disulfiram treatment reduces NETs, necrosis, and liver metastasis burden in AKPS organoid mice.
NET inhibition combined with FOLFOX chemotherapy further decreases liver metastasis compared to chemotherapy alone.
Necrotic tumor regions exhibit higher EMT markers (vimentin, SNAI1) and inflammatory pathways.
Mice with NET inhibition display increased neutrophil activation markers (citH3) reduction.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Disulfiram is a non-specific aldehyde dehydrogenase inhibitor, raising potential off-target concerns.

combination_strategies.rationale: NET inhibition may improve drug delivery to necrotic tumors
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic reduction of liver metastases

open_questions: What upstream signals trigger NET release in colorectal tumors?
Can NET inhibition improve response to immunotherapy in CRC?

talk_title: Metabolic Reuse of Tumor-Derived Signals Coordinates Dendritic Cell Function
MOA_summary: Tumor-derived polyamines enhance dendritic cell uptake via RAC1 activation, while tumor-derived cholesterol fuels dendritic cell maturation through NPC1-mediated transport and lipid nanodomain formation; AXL deletion amplifies cholesterol accumulation, boosting maturation and antitumor immunity.
key_findings: ODC1-deficient tumor debris is less efficiently taken up by dendritic cells in vitro and in vivo.
Blocking polyamine transport (AMTX) impairs dendritic cell uptake of tumor debris.
Polyamine deficiency reduces RAC1 expression and activation in dendritic cells.
Inhibition of NPC1 or statin treatment diminishes CCR7 and other maturation markers on dendritic cells.
AXL knockout dendritic cells accumulate more cholesterol, lipid nanodomains, and display heightened maturation and improved tumor control.

combination_strategies.rationale: Enhances cholesterol-driven maturation of dendritic cells
combination_strategies.phenotype: Increased CCR7 expression and antitumor response

open_questions: Can pharmacologic modulation of polyamine transport improve dendritic cell vaccines in patients?
What is the impact of systemic statin use on dendritic cell function in cancer patients?

talk_title: Spatial Rewiring of IL-1β Network Limits Therapeutic Efficiency in Advanced Non‑Squamous Carcinoma

biomarkers.name: IL1β plasma level
biomarkers.finding: Increased after anti‑IL1β treatment in mice and humans

models.name: Keratitis‑induced lung adenocarcinoma
models.genotype_or_key_features: virus‑induced tumor, pre‑cancer vs advanced treatment windows

models.name: MNU carcinogen‑induced lung adenocarcinoma
models.genotype_or_key_features: chemical induction, pre‑cancer vs advanced treatment

MOA_summary: Anti‑IL1β therapy is effective when administered at pre‑cancer stages by disconnecting IL1β‑IL1R1 signaling in epithelial cells; in advanced disease the network shifts to fibroblast‑centric signaling, limiting efficacy despite increased IL1β levels.
key_findings: IL1β pathway activity rises from normal to pre‑cancer to invasive adenocarcinoma in both human and mouse samples.
Anti‑IL1β antibody suppresses tumor growth when given at pre‑cancer stage but not at advanced stage in two mouse models.
Anti‑IL1β treatment paradoxically increases IL1β plasma levels in mice and humans.
Spatial analysis shows pre‑cancer blockade reduces IL1R1‑positive epithelial cells within the IL1β network, whereas advanced stage blockade reduces IL1R1‑positive fibroblasts.
Location‑aware cell‑cell interaction analysis reveals that IL1β‑IL1R1 contacts are disrupted in pre‑cancer but persist in advanced disease.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Advanced tumors retain fibroblast‑driven IL1β signaling, providing a resistance mechanism to IL1β blockade.

combination_strategies.rationale: Disrupt fibroblast‑centric IL1β network in advanced disease
combination_strategies.phenotype: Potentially restores anti‑IL1β efficacy

open_questions: Which fibroblast subtypes sustain IL1β signaling in advanced NSCLC?
Can simultaneous targeting of IL1β and fibroblast pathways improve outcomes in late‑stage patients?

talk_title: Gut Commensal Dysbiosis Modulates Lung Microenvironment to Promote Breast Tumor Metastasis via Mast Cell‑Dependent Axis

targets.dependency_context: mast cell secretion in dysbiotic lungs

biomarkers.name: Mast cell count
biomarkers.finding: Higher mast cell density correlates with reduced survival in dysbiotic mice

biomarkers.name: p‑STAT3
biomarkers.finding: Increased in lungs of dysbiotic mice

models.name: antibiotic‑induced dysbiosis model
models.genotype_or_key_features: broad‑spectrum antibiotics followed by pathogenic recolonization

models.name: tail‑vein injection of HR‑positive breast cancer cells
orthotopic mammary tumor model with pre‑metastatic lung analysis
models.genotype_or_key_features: single‑cell RNA‑seq of lung immune cells

MOA_summary: Gut dysbiosis elevates mast cell activation in the lung, leading to IL‑6 secretion that activates STAT3 in fibroblasts and endothelial cells, promoting ECM stiffening, angiogenesis, and M2 macrophage polarization, thereby creating a permissive niche for breast cancer lung metastasis.
key_findings: Antibiotic‑induced gut dysbiosis accelerates death of mice after tail‑vein breast cancer cell injection.
Lung mast cell density inversely correlates with survival in dysbiotic mice.
Ketotifen (mast cell stabilizer) improves survival of dysbiotic mice, implicating mast cells in metastasis promotion.
Single‑cell RNA‑seq reveals dysbiotic lung mast cells upregulate IL‑6 and degranulation genes.
Fibroblasts and endothelial cells in dysbiotic lungs show STAT3‑driven fibrotic and angiogenic transcriptional programs.
Macrophages shift toward M2 phenotype with increased CCL2 and MMP‑related genes in dysbiotic lungs.

combination_strategies.rationale: Stabilize mast cells to block IL‑6/STAT3 axis
combination_strategies.phenotype: Extended survival in dysbiotic mice

open_questions: Do primary bile acids mediate the gut‑lung signaling that activates mast cells?
Does lung microbiome alteration contribute independently to the pro‑metastatic niche?

talk_title: Mild Interferon‑γ Signaling Regulates Immune Suppression and Fibrotic TME in Pancreatic Cancer Liver Metastasis

biomarkers.name: CD80
biomarkers.finding: Reduced on TAMs in IFNγR2 myeloid KO mice

biomarkers.name: p‑STAT1
biomarkers.finding: Decreased in bone‑marrow derived macrophages from KO mice

models.name: portal vein injection liver metastasis model
models.genotype_or_key_features: pancreatic cancer cells injected into portal vein

MOA_summary: Mild IFN‑γ signaling through IFNγR2 on macrophages sustains antigen‑presentation (CD80/CD86/MHC‑II) and limits immunosuppressive markers (ARG1, CD206, SPP1); loss of this signaling drives SPP1‑high macrophages, fibrosis, and T‑cell dysfunction in pancreatic cancer liver metastasis.
key_findings: Systemic IFN‑γ neutralization increases tumor number and burden in liver metastasis model.
Myeloid‑specific IFNγR2 KO mice exhibit larger tumor burden and higher TAM numbers.
KO TAMs show decreased CD80/CD86/MHC‑II and increased ARG1/CD206/SPP1 expression.
T‑cell proliferation (Ki67) and cytokine production (IFN‑γ, TNF‑α) are reduced in KO mice.
Bulk RNA‑seq reveals down‑regulation of IFN‑γ response and up‑regulation of fibrosis pathways in KO tumors.
Single‑cell RNA‑seq identifies expansion of SPP1‑high macrophages with ECM and TGF‑β signatures in KO.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Loss of IFN‑γ signaling shifts macrophages toward a pro‑fibrotic, immunosuppressive phenotype.

combination_strategies.rationale: Counteract fibrotic remodeling caused by IFNγR2 loss
combination_strategies.phenotype: Potentially restore antitumor immunity

open_questions: Do resident Kupffer cells versus monocyte‑derived macrophages differ in IFN‑γ responsiveness?
Can exogenous low‑dose IFN‑γ rescue the fibrotic phenotype without promoting tumor immune evasion?

talk_title: Targeting Chronic Inflammation in Cancer and Fibrosis with Engineered uPAR‑CAR T Cells

targets.dependency_context: tumor cells, myeloid cells, CAFs across solid tumors

biomarkers.name: uPAR expression
biomarkers.finding: High on tumor cells, CAFs, and myeloid cells in multiple cancers

MOA_summary: uPAR‑CAR T cells recognize and eliminate uPAR‑positive tumor cells, cancer‑associated fibroblasts, and immunosuppressive myeloid cells, thereby dismantling fibrotic stroma, enhancing T‑cell infiltration, and preventing antigen escape.
key_findings: uPAR is co‑expressed on tumor cells, CAFs, and myeloid suppressor cells in >10 solid tumor types.
Single dose of uPAR‑CAR T yields durable tumor regression and protects against rechallenge in multiple xenograft models.
Cisplatin treatment up‑regulates uPAR on ovarian cancer cells, sensitizing them to uPAR‑CAR T killing.
uPAR‑CAR T reduces collagen deposition and fibrosis, leading to increased CAR T cell infiltration.
In humanized mice, uPAR‑CAR T shows antitumor activity without significant depletion of myeloid or granulocyte compartments.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Potential on‑target off‑tumor effects on monocytes due to uPAR expression.

combination_strategies.rationale: Chemotherapy induces uPAR expression, enhancing CAR T targeting
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved survival and tumor infiltration

open_questions: Can uPAR‑CAR T be combined with checkpoint blockade to further boost antitumor immunity?
What is the long‑term impact on normal monocyte populations and infection risk?

session_title: Innovations in Translating Social and Structural Determinants of Health Across Population, Community, and Basic Cancer Research
talk_title: Novel Spatiotemporal Approaches for Built Environment Physical Disorder Measurement

speaker.name: Jesse Placek
speaker.affiliation: Division of Cancer Prevention and Control, Ohio State University College of Medicine

models.name: Women's Circle of Health Follow-Up Study
models.genotype_or_key_features: Black women with breast cancer, urban New Jersey, n not specified

MOA_summary: Placek described a workflow that audits Google Street View images to quantify neighborhood physical disorder, creates a composite disinvestment score with high inter‑rater reliability, fits a spatial semivariogram and applies kriging to interpolate exposure values for individual cohort members, and links these exposures to breast cancer survival outcomes, revealing that higher disinvestment predicts shorter survival for stage 2/3 disease.
key_findings: Audited 23,000 street‑view locations with standardized protocol achieving high kappa agreement, demonstrating reliable measurement of physical disorder.
Internal consistency analysis (Cronbach's alpha) justified collapsing multiple disorder items into a single composite disinvestment score.
Spatial kriging based on the fitted semivariogram provided continuous disinvestment estimates for cohort participants lacking exact address‑level data.
In the breast cancer cohort, each unit increase in the disinvestment score was associated with reduced survival time for stage 2 and stage 3 patients in accelerated failure‑time models.
The methodology enables rapid, reproducible, and scalable exposure assessment for built‑environment research across large urban areas.
open_questions: How can temporal variation in street‑view imagery be incorporated to capture changes in neighborhood disorder over the life‑course?
What is the optimal spatial scale (parcel vs. census tract) for linking disinvestment scores to individual health outcomes?

session_title: Innovations in Translating Social and Structural Determinants of Health Across Population, Community, and Basic Cancer Research
talk_title: Integrating Social Determinants of Health with Cancer Registry Data to Identify Geographic Drivers of Lung and Prostate Cancer

speaker.name: Shannon Lynch
speaker.affiliation: Cancer Prevention and Control Program, Fox Chase Cancer Center; Co‑Director, Center for Biostatistics and Epidemiology, Temple University

models.name: Pennsylvania State Cancer Registry
models.genotype_or_key_features: All incident lung cancer cases, n≈62,000, diagnosed 20XX‑20XX

models.name: Prostate Cancer Aggressiveness Cohort (PA State Registry linked to Census)
models.genotype_or_key_features: ≈80,000 prostate cancer cases, 900 Black aggressive, 6,500 White aggressive

clinical_data.metric: association with old housing (proxy for asbestos)

clinical_data.population: Black men with aggressive prostate cancer
White men with aggressive prostate cancer

MOA_summary: Lynch integrated publicly available SSDOH datasets (census, EPA, zoning, etc.) with state cancer registry records, used SaTScan to detect census‑tract clusters of excess lung cancer incidence, and applied multivariable logistic regression to identify smoking, air pollution, traffic density, and old housing as independent drivers; similar neighborhood‑wide association studies linked specific SSDOH variables (e.g., public‑transport use, housing quality) to aggressive prostate cancer, demonstrating that macro‑level social and environmental exposures can be quantified and associated with cancer incidence and outcomes.
key_findings: Identified multiple census‑tract clusters in Pennsylvania with significantly higher lung cancer incidence after age, sex, and race adjustment.

In multivariate models, smoking prevalence, ambient air‑pollution levels, traffic density, and proportion of old housing (asbestos proxy) were each significantly associated with cluster status (p<0.05).
Philadelphia‑specific analysis revealed high‑mortality neighborhoods with greater tobacco outlet density and elevated environmental exposures.

Neighborhood‑wide association study of >14,000 Census variables pinpointed five SSDOH factors (e.g., public‑transport use, housing disinvestment) linked to aggressive prostate cancer in Black men and 17 factors in White men.
Combining three top SSDOH variables into an exposure score markedly improved prostate cancer risk prediction beyond PSA alone (probability ≈30% vs 8% for high vs low exposure scores).
Community‑outreach pilots were launched in identified high‑burden areas, including smoking‑cessation programs and air‑quality monitoring collaborations.
open_questions: How can longitudinal residential histories be incorporated to capture cumulative SSDOH exposure over the cancer latency period?
What is the optimal spatial resolution (ZIP code vs. census tract vs. block group) for linking SSDOH variables to molecular biomarkers such as epigenetic age acceleration?

talk_title: Preclinical development of EFTX G12V, a first‑in‑class EGFR‑directed KRAS G12V selective siRNA

targets.dependency_context: KRAS G12V mutant NSCLC and colon cancer

MOA_summary: EFTX G12V is an EGFR‑targeted, chemically modified siRNA that selectively silences KRAS G12V while sparing wild‑type KRAS, achieving high tumor delivery, sustained KRAS knockdown, downstream ERK inhibition, and tumor regression in KRAS G12V‑driven lung and colon models, including immunocompetent settings with CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration.
key_findings: EGFR‑directed siRNA achieved ~90% tumor cell uptake after 2 h in xenograft mice.
Selective KRAS G12V silencing at RNA and protein levels with no effect on wild‑type KRAS.
Single‑agent EFTX G12V induced significant tumor growth control and complete responses in KRAS G12V lung (H727) and colon models.
Sustained tumor tissue accumulation and KRAS protein knockdown observed up to 96 h after dosing.
Immunocompetent MPK195 model showed comparable anti‑tumor efficacy and robust CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity for KRAS G12V over wild‑type KRAS minimizes on‑target toxicity in normal tissues.
Kidney shows highest off‑target delivery but no KRAS inhibition observed.

combination_strategies.rationale: EGFR ligand is non‑mitogenic; combining may enhance KRAS knockdown efficacy.
combination_strategies.phenotype: synergistic tumor regression in colon cancer models

open_questions: Can the EGFR‑targeted siRNA platform be adapted for other KRAS mutants (e.g., G12C, G12D)?
What is the therapeutic window in larger animal models and humans?

talk_title: Targeting ORC1A to sensitize KRAS‑mutant colorectal cancer to KRAS inhibitors

speaker.name: Dr. Zhaoqing Liu
speaker.affiliation: University of Southern California, Lin Jiang Lab

biomarkers.name: MCL1 protein level
biomarkers.finding: Combination of KRAS inhibitor and ORC1A inhibitor markedly reduces MCL1 levels, correlating with apoptosis

MOA_summary: ORC1A inhibition by olaparib synergizes with KRAS G12C inhibitors by suppressing MCL1 stability via GSK3β activation, leading to proteasomal degradation of MCL1 and caspase‑dependent apoptosis in KRAS‑mutant colorectal cancer models.
key_findings: ORC1A is up‑regulated in KRAS‑mutant CRC and induced after KRAS inhibitor treatment.
Combining olaparib with KRAS inhibitors reduces KRAS inhibitor IC50 by ~10‑fold in SLIC14‑63 cells.
Synergy is driven by marked down‑regulation of MCL1 via GSK3β‑mediated proteasomal degradation.
MCL1 knockdown or pharmacologic inhibition phenocopies ORC1A inhibition, confirming MCL1 as functional driver.
Dual inhibition leads to robust tumor regression in KRAS G12C CRC PDX models without overt toxicity.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: RESTORATION of MCL1 levels by proteasome inhibition rescues viability, confirming on‑target mechanism.
Potential for resistance via MCL1 stabilization.

combination_strategies.rationale: KRAS‑mutant CRC shows compensatory EGFR signaling; combining may further enhance efficacy.
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced tumor growth inhibition in vitro

open_questions: Will ORC1A inhibition sensitize KRAS G12D or G13D CRC models similarly?
Can the combination be tolerated in vivo with standard chemotherapy?

talk_title: The Kinase Library: a motif‑based platform to infer kinase activity from phosphoproteomics

speaker.name: Tom Yaron‑Barrier
speaker.affiliation: Dana‑Farber Cancer Institute / Boston Children’s Hospital (co‑founder of Maride Therapeutics)

MOA_summary: The Kinase Library uses combinatorial peptide motifs to define kinase substrate specificity across the human kinome; by scoring phosphosite sequences from quantitative phosphoproteomics, it infers kinase activity changes in any perturbation, enabling mechanistic insights and biomarker discovery without prior interaction data.
key_findings: Motif‑based scoring accurately identified BRAF/ERK activity dynamics after BRAF inhibitor treatment in melanoma cells.
DNA‑damage kinases (ATM/ATR) were temporally resolved after UV exposure, matching known kinetics.
The platform can predict upstream kinases for >95% of phosphosites, vastly expanding annotation beyond PhosphoSitePlus.
Open‑source Python package and web interface released for community use.
open_questions: How to integrate quantitative phosphosite intensity with motif scores for improved activity inference?
Can the library predict off‑target kinase inhibition of novel inhibitors?

talk_title: CHRNA3‑B4 targeted CAR‑T cells for neuroblastoma and neuroendocrine tumors

biomarkers.name: CHRNA3/B4 co‑expression
biomarkers.finding: High in neuroblastoma tumors and PDXs, low in normal tissues

models.name: ongoing DISCO CAR T trial (NCT not disclosed)

clinical_data.value: significant regression in 453X and 44X PDX models
extended survival in DISCO CAR‑treated mice

MOA_summary: DISCO binders recognize the heteropentameric CHRNA3‑CHRNB4 complex on neuroblastoma cells; engineered second‑generation CD28‑CAR T cells bearing these binders selectively activate and kill double‑positive tumor cells, leading to tumor regression and survival benefit in neuroblastoma and retinoblastoma models.
key_findings: ImmunoTar pipeline identified CHRNA3/B4 as a neuroblastoma‑specific surface complex.
DISCO binders specifically bind cells co‑expressing CHRNA3 and CHRNB4, not single‑positive or negative cells.
DISCO CAR T cells mediate potent in‑vitro killing of CHRNA3/B4‑positive neuroblastoma lines.
Single and double‑dose DISCO CAR T treatment induced tumor regression and prolonged survival in neuroblastoma PDX models.
Intravitreal DISCO CAR T delivery eradicated retinoblastoma tumors in mice, preserving ocular integrity.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Antigen loss observed in low‑expressing tumor variants; double dosing improved control.
No off‑target activity detected on normal tissues with low CHRNA3/B4 expression.

combination_strategies.rationale: Enhance neuroblastoma differentiation and potentially increase CHRNA3/B4 expression.
combination_strategies.phenotype: improved CAR T efficacy in preclinical models

open_questions: Will CHRNA3/B4 expression be stable in patients after CAR T pressure?
Can DISCO CAR be combined with checkpoint blockade for synergistic effect?

talk_title: REST‑driven Notch activation confers chemoresistance and immunotherapy sensitivity in small‑cell lung cancer

biomarkers.name: MHC‑I expression
biomarkers.finding: REST overexpression ↑ MHC‑I and CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration

clinical_data.value: minimal in REST‑overexpressing PDX vs robust in control
significant tumor control in REST‑overexpressing syngeneic tumors

MOA_summary: REST overexpression suppresses neuroendocrine differentiation, activates Notch signaling, and up‑regulates MHC‑I antigen presentation, leading to chemoresistance but heightened sensitivity to PD‑1 blockade via increased CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration.
key_findings: CRISPRa screen identified REST as a top chemoresistance hit in SCLC PDX.
REST overexpression in PDX abrogates cisplatin/topotecan‑induced tumor regression.
REST drives Notch activation (↑ NICD1/3, Hes1) which mediates resistance; gamma‑secretase inhibition restores chemosensitivity.
REST‑high tumors display increased MHC‑I, CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration, and respond to anti‑PD‑1 therapy.
Single‑cell analysis revealed a REST‑associated tumor cell cluster enriched for Notch signaling and CX3CL1 expression.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: REST expression defines a chemoresistant, immunogenic SCLC subset.
Gamma‑secretase inhibition reverses chemoresistance but not yet tested with immunotherapy.

combination_strategies.rationale: Block REST‑induced Notch signaling to restore chemosensitivity.
combination_strategies.phenotype: restores cisplatin/topotecan efficacy in REST‑overexpressing PDX

combination_strategies.rationale: REST‑driven MHC‑I up‑regulation sensitizes tumors to immune checkpoint blockade.
combination_strategies.phenotype: significant tumor control in REST‑overexpressing syngeneic model

open_questions: Will combined Notch inhibition and PD‑1 blockade provide additive benefit?
What proportion of patient SCLC tumors exhibit REST‑high phenotype?

talk_title: Age‑associated ferroptosis vulnerability in melanoma via WNT5A‑driven phenotype switching

speaker.name: Marilo Ramos Recha
speaker.affiliation: Johns Hopkins School of Public Health

biomarkers.name: 4‑HNE (lipid peroxidation product)
biomarkers.finding: Higher levels in aged skin and aged melanoma tumors

biomarkers.name: WNT5A expression
biomarkers.finding: Correlates with ferroptosis sensitivity and 4‑HNE levels

clinical_data.value: significant reduction in 24‑month‑old mice treated with IKE
effect of IKE abolished by lipid‑peroxidation inhibitor

MOA_summary: Aged dermal fibroblasts secrete factors that induce melanoma phenotype switching (↓MITF, ↑WNT5A), leading to down‑regulation of GPX4 and other ferroptosis defenses; this creates a lipid‑peroxidation‑rich environment that renders aged melanoma cells hypersensitive to ferroptosis inducers such as IKE or RSL3.
key_findings: Aged skin fibroblast conditioned media sensitizes melanoma cells to GPX4 inhibition.
WNT5A expression inversely correlates with MITF and directly with GPX4 down‑regulation.
Aged mice (24 mo) exhibit higher tumor lipid peroxidation and respond to IKE with tumor growth suppression.
Lipid‑peroxidation inhibitor rescues IKE efficacy, confirming ferroptosis as the mechanism.
WNT5A spatially co‑localizes with 4‑HNE in both mouse and human melanoma samples.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Normal aged tissues may have increased ferroptosis susceptibility; tumor‑specific WNT5A‑driven GPX4 loss provides therapeutic window.

combination_strategies.rationale: Ferroptosis may enhance antigen release and improve anti‑PD‑1 efficacy.
combination_strategies.phenotype: under investigation

open_questions: Will systemic ferroptosis induction cause toxicity in aged patients?
Can WNT5A inhibition reverse ferroptosis sensitivity?

talk_title: Theranostics and Precision Imaging for Prostate Cancer

speaker.name: Martin Pomper
speaker.affiliation: University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center, Department of Radiology

models.name: PSMA PET imaging
models.genotype_or_key_features: high PSMA expression in prostate lesions

MOA_summary: Pomper described small-molecule PSMA-targeted radioligands labeled with fluorine-18 for PET imaging and with beta or alpha-emitting radionuclides for therapy; internalization of the ligand provides prolonged tumor residence and DNA damage via emitted particles, enabling both precise diagnosis and targeted radiotherapy.
key_findings: Fluorine-18 labeled PSMA small molecule provides high-contrast PET images of tiny prostate lesions, including bone marrow metastases.
PSMA-617 radioligand therapy yields PSA 50% declines superior to cabazitaxel with milder side effects.
Alpha-emitting astatine-labeled PSMA agents enable intracellular targeting and potential precision dosimetry.
FAP-targeted radiopharmaceutical detected pancreatic cancer lesions missed by CT/MR, suggesting utility for hard-to-image tumors.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Heterogeneous PSMA expression can lead to residual disease; combination with stereotactic radiation (LUNAR trial) aims to address residual clones.

combination_strategies.rationale: Debulk residual disease after radioligand therapy
combination_strategies.phenotype: incremental progression-free survival

combination_strategies.rationale: Potential synergistic immune activation after tumor debulking
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced response rates in early studies

open_questions: How to optimize patient dosing and precision dosimetry for alpha emitters?
What strategies will overcome tumor heterogeneity and PSMA-negative clones?

talk_title: The Revolution of Antibody Drug Conjugates From Early Concept to Next Generation

biomarkers.name: FRα expression
biomarkers.finding: higher FRα correlates with increased ORR in ZW291 phase 1 (61% ORR)

MOA_summary: Colombo presented next‑generation ADCs that balance payload potency, linker stability, and antibody affinity; examples include a moderate‑potency Top1 payload conjugated to a high‑internalizing FRα antibody (ZW291) and a pan‑RAS inhibitor payload designed for ADC delivery, achieving tumor‑specific drug exposure and bystander killing while minimizing normal‑tissue toxicity.
key_findings: ZW291 achieved >50% ORR in FRα‑positive patients despite using a non‑maximally potent Top1 payload.
Dual‑binding (avidity) approach enabled selective killing of cells with >10,000 receptors/cell, improving therapeutic window.
Pan‑RAS ADC showed tumor regression at 1 mg/kg in CDX models with sustained target inhibition for 14 days, while sparing normal tissue.

Linker stability critically influences ADC PK: moderate‑stable linkers can still yield long half‑life (e.g., T‑DXd), whereas unstable linkers produce rapid payload release (e.g., sacituzumab govitecan).
Bispecific ADCs (e.g., EGFR/HER3) demonstrate activity independent of single‑target expression, suggesting broader applicability.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Target downregulation (e.g., HER2 after T‑DXd) can reduce efficacy; bispecific formats aim to mitigate this.
Efflux pump overexpression (ABCC1, Pgp) correlates with reduced ADC benefit in some trials.

combination_strategies.rationale: Orthogonal mechanisms may enhance tumor kill
combination_strategies.phenotype: synergistic tumor regression in preclinical models

combination_strategies.rationale: Overcome immunosuppressive TME
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced antitumor activity in mouse models

open_questions: What is the optimal balance of payload potency vs. linker stability for different tumor types?
Can truly tumor‑specific cleavable linkers be engineered to further improve safety?

talk_title: Protein Engineering Driving T‑Cell Engager Innovation

speaker.name: Angela Coxon
speaker.affiliation: Amgen, Vice President of Research and Development

biomarkers.name: DLL3 expression
biomarkers.finding: 80‑90% of SCLC samples high; negligible normal tissue expression

biomarkers.name: STIP1 expression
biomarkers.finding: high in >90% prostate tumors, low in normal tissues

MOA_summary: Coxon described engineered bispecific T‑cell engagers that bind CD3 and tumor antigens (DLL3, STIP1, CDH3/Mesothelin) with a silent Fc for half‑life extension; avidity‑based dual binding (zalyuritamic) and logic‑gated dual‑targeting (AMG 305) improve selectivity and therapeutic window, while combination with PD‑L1 blockade or IL‑2 muteins aims to overcome the immunosuppressive tumor microenvironment.
key_findings: Talatamab achieved median OS 13.6 mo vs 8.3 mo chemotherapy in SCLC (Phase 3).
Frontline talatamab + atezolizumab increased median OS to 25.3 mo, maintaining manageable safety.
Zalyuritamic’s bivalent STIP1 binding yields activity only on cells with >10 k receptors/cell, sparing normal tissue.
AMG 305 shows potent killing of CDH3/mesothelin double‑positive cells with minimal toxicity in preclinical models.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Off‑tumor CD3 engagement can cause cytokine release syndrome; silent Fc reduces FcγR binding.
Target heterogeneity addressed by dual‑target logic gating to limit activity on single‑positive normal cells.

combination_strategies.rationale: Talatamab upregulates PD‑L1; blockade may enhance T‑cell activity
combination_strategies.phenotype: OS improvement to 25.3 mo in frontline SCLC

combination_strategies.rationale: Orthogonal cytotoxic mechanisms
combination_strategies.phenotype: preclinical synergistic tumor regression

combination_strategies.rationale: Boost T‑cell proliferation in immunosuppressive TME
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced antitumor activity in mouse models

open_questions: Can dual‑target logic gating be generalized to other antigen pairs?
What is the optimal CD3 affinity to balance potency and cytokine toxicity?

talk_title: TIL Therapy of Solid Cancers: Features and Clinical Activity

speaker.name: John Hannan
speaker.affiliation: Netherlands Cancer Institute, Center for Cell Therapy

biomarkers.name: CD25 expression on tumor‑reactive CD8 TILs
biomarkers.finding: Higher CD25+ tumor‑reactive TILs correlate with response and expansion

models.name: Dropout screen library
models.genotype_or_key_features: autologous B‑cell antigen library with tumor neoantigens

MOA_summary: Hannan detailed autologous tumor‑infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) therapy: surgical tumor harvest, ex‑vivo expansion with high‑dose IL‑2, lymphodepleting chemotherapy, and reinfusion; single‑cell and TCR dropout analyses identified CD25‑high, tumor‑reactive CD8 “Goldilocks” cells as key predictors of clinical response.
key_findings: TIL therapy improved PFS and ORR versus ipilimumab in PD‑1‑refractory melanoma (HR significant).
Responders harbored abundant CD25+ tumor‑reactive CD8 TILs with IL‑2/STAT5 signaling signatures.
Dropout screens revealed responders recognized more neoantigens and tumor‑associated antigens, while non‑responders showed viral antigen bystander activity.
In real‑world data (n≈100), anti‑PD‑1 pretreated patients achieved 45% ORR, comparable to trial results; prior ipilimumab+nivolumab patients had lower ORR (30%).
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Non‑responders lacked sufficient tumor‑reactive CD8 clones or had highly dysfunctional phenotypes, limiting expansion.
High LDH and prior combination checkpoint therapy associated with poorer outcomes.

combination_strategies.rationale: Talatamab upregulates PD‑L1; checkpoint blockade may synergize
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced OS in frontline SCLC

combination_strategies.rationale: Combine orthogonal cytotoxic mechanisms with TIL activity
combination_strategies.phenotype: preclinical synergistic tumor regression

open_questions: Can engineered TILs (e.g., TCR‑modified) overcome the lack of tumor‑reactive clones in non‑responders?
What biomarkers best predict durable expansion of tumor‑reactive TILs post‑infusion?

talk_title: Digital pathology models AEON and Paladin for tumor subtyping and molecular alteration inference

speaker.name: Kevin Boehm
speaker.affiliation: Computational Oncology, Radiation Oncology, Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center

MOA_summary: AEON uses a knowledge graph–derived representation to infer 124 detailed tumor subtypes directly from H&E whole‑slide images, while Paladin conditions on each subtype to predict the presence of specific molecular alterations, demonstrating that digital pathology can capture phenotype‑genotype relationships for a subset of alterations (~4%).
key_findings: AEON achieved an average one‑vs‑rest AUROC of 0.996 across 124 tumor subtypes in the internal MSK test set.
Multimodal integration of AEON with the GDENS genomic classifier improved performance in both primary and metastatic validation cohorts.
Paladin correctly identified 9 of 11 ovarian granulosa cell tumors and outperformed DeepHistology on most molecular alteration predictions.
Only ~4% of examined molecular alterations showed a strong phenotype‑genotype signal on H&E, confirming limited but actionable predictive value.
Digital pathology aided variant‑of‑unknown‑significance annotation, low‑purity sample inference, and phenocopy detection.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Most molecular alterations lacked a detectable histologic phenotype, limiting the utility of H&E‑based prediction for many targets.

combination_strategies.rationale: Combine histologic and genomic information to improve tumor subtype and alteration inference.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Higher AUROC and accuracy in external validation.

open_questions: Can allele‑specific effects of mutations be resolved by the current models?
How can the approach be extended to rare tumor subtypes with limited image data?

talk_title: Multi‑omic model for glioblastoma recurrence prediction centered on histopathology

models.name: Xenium (RNA) / Codex (protein)
models.genotype_or_key_features: single‑cell resolution spatial transcriptomics and proteomics

models.name: 19 GBM patients (total resection)
models.genotype_or_key_features: short, intermediate, long recurrence groups; 4 received PD‑1 inhibitor

MOA_summary: A pathology‑foundation model (UniHeal) is fine‑tuned to predict GBM recurrence time from H&E tiles, using MRI radiomics and clinical covariates as inputs, while spatial omics (Xenium, Codex) provide explainability by linking predictive tiles to gene‑program and immune‑cell signatures.
key_findings: Preliminary cohort of 19 GBM patients shows that short‑recurrence tumors exhibit disrupted tissue architecture and necrosis, whereas long‑recurrence tumors retain normal segmentation.
UniHeal embeddings cluster tiles into biologically meaningful groups (e.g., necrotic vs. vascular regions).
Spatial alignment of Xenium and Codex data reveals that longer recurrence is associated with enhanced immune activity, while short recurrence shows immunosuppressive signatures.
A K‑means spatial domain analysis identified an oligodendrocyte‑enriched niche (cluster 8) with increased immune engagement in long‑recurrence tumors.
open_questions: Will the model generalize to larger, multi‑institutional GBM cohorts?
How robust is the approach to varying adjuvant therapies (e.g., PD‑1 inhibition)?

talk_title: Unified single‑cell and spatial atlas of prostate cancer progression

models.name: Pan‑PCa atlas
models.genotype_or_key_features: 700k cells from 19 sites, epithelial, immune, stromal states

MOA_summary: Integration of >700 k single‑cell profiles across localized, metastatic, and neuroendocrine prostate cancer defines epithelial, immune, and stromal state trajectories, revealing SP1‑macrophage and mCAF‑centered immunosuppressive niches that drive progression and correlate with poor survival.
key_findings: SP1‑macrophage expansion is associated with lymph‑node and bone metastasis and predicts worse overall survival.
mCAF enrichment co‑localizes with SP1‑macrophage in a stromal niche (CN7) linked to poor outcomes.
Anti‑SP1 treatment in bone‑metastasis mouse models reduced tumor burden, pain, and prolonged survival.
NEPC emergence aligns with specific epithelial lineage‑positive states and distinct transcription factor programs.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: SP1‑macrophage mediates TGF‑β–driven immunosuppression, representing a selective vulnerability.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target the immunosuppressive macrophage niche to relieve TGF‑β–mediated suppression.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced tumor burden and pain, extended survival in bone metastasis mouse model.

open_questions: Can SP1‑targeted therapy be safely combined with checkpoint inhibition?
What are the mechanisms underlying SP1‑macrophage recruitment to bone metastases?

talk_title: Deep learning profiling of regulatory somatic variants across clonal cancer evolution

models.name: SAIE framework
models.genotype_or_key_features: 22,000 chromatin profiles condensed into 40 sequence classes
