MOA_summary: A sequence‑based deep learning model (SAIE) predicts the regulatory impact of somatic variants by comparing reference and mutant genome embeddings across 40 learned sequence classes, enabling patient‑level regulatory disruption scores that correlate with progression‑free interval independent of mutational burden.
key_findings: High regulatory disruption scores are associated with significantly worse progression‑free survival across TCGA pan‑cancer cohorts.
Severity of regulatory impact, not the number of regulatory variants, drives the prognostic signal.
Clonal versus subclonal disruption severity provides complementary prognostic information.
Six patient clusters based on disruption profiles show distinct pathway enrichments (e.g., immune vs. cell‑cycle).
open_questions: How do regulatory disruption scores integrate with coding‑mutation drivers for therapy selection?
Can the approach be adapted to rare pediatric cancers with limited variant data?

talk_title: Spatial multi‑omics integration to guide glioblastoma surgery and engineered macrophage therapy

speaker.name: Chibawanye Ene
speaker.affiliation: Department of Neurosurgery, MD Anderson Cancer Center

MOA_summary: Integrated spatial transcriptomics, proteomics, and mass‑spec imaging of glioblastoma core and edge identifies mesenchymal‑like and AC‑like cell states linked to TGF‑β–driven immunosuppression; engineered macrophages lacking TGF‑β receptors are designed to resist this suppression and improve therapeutic efficacy.
key_findings: Mesenchymal‑like cell states are enriched in hypoxic tumor cores, while AC‑like cells produce TGF‑β2 that activates tumor‑associated macrophages.
SP1‑macrophage and mCAF co‑localization defines an immunosuppressive niche associated with poor survival.
MasSpec Pen can detect metabolic signatures of proliferating (Ki‑67+) tumor cells intra‑operatively, potentially guiding resection margins.
CRISPR‑KO of TGF‑β receptors in engineered macrophages reduces tumor burden and pain in a glioblastoma bone‑metastasis mouse model.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: TGF‑β signaling from AC‑like cells creates a selective immunosuppressive pressure on macrophages.

combination_strategies.rationale: Render cell therapy resistant to tumor‑derived TGF‑β immunosuppression.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced tumor control and survival in mouse models.

open_questions: Will intra‑operative metabolomic signatures reliably predict residual aggressive cell states across patients?
How will engineered macrophages perform in the immunosuppressive brain microenvironment in humans?

talk_title: ADT Norm: non‑parametric batch correction for single‑cell proteomics across technologies

speaker.name: Ye Zhang
speaker.affiliation: Pharmacology and Computational Biology, MD Anderson Cancer Center

models.name: ADT Norm
models.genotype_or_key_features: non‑parametric peak alignment for ADT counts

MOA_summary: ADT Norm identifies negative and positive peaks in antibody‑derived tag (ADT) distributions using functional data analysis and aligns these landmarks across batches, removing technical variation while preserving biological cell‑type signals for downstream integration of CITE‑seq, CyTOF, and other single‑cell proteomics platforms.
key_findings: ADT Norm aligns background and signal peaks across heterogeneous CITE‑seq batches, achieving superior batch mixing and cell‑type separation compared with SeR normalization.
The method quantifies antibody staining quality, flagging markers with poor signal‑to‑background separation.
An AI‑agent interface enables natural‑language driven parameter tuning for large‑scale multi‑technology integration.
open_questions: How does ADT Norm perform on highly multiplexed spatial proteomics (e.g., CODEX) where signal bleed is prominent?
Can the AI‑agent be extended to automatically select optimal markers for downstream analyses?

talk_title: Foundation‑model‑driven MHC‑II peptide binding prediction with data‑efficient graph neural network

models.name: AlphaFold3 + ESM2 + GNN (trained on PepSeq)
models.genotype_or_key_features: structure embeddings + protein language model embeddings

MOA_summary: AlphaFold3 generates structural representations of peptide‑MHC‑II complexes, ESM2 provides sequence embeddings, and a graph neural network trained on low‑volume PepSeq binding data predicts peptide affinity, achieving state‑of‑the‑art performance with only ~2% of traditional training data.
readouts: AUC on IEDB CD4 epitope benchmark
key_findings: The GNN model trained on 2% of the data matches or exceeds NetMHC‑II Pan performance on DRB1 alleles.
Zero‑shot AlphaFold3 scoring (Fisher score) provides moderate predictive power, but the GNN substantially improves AUC.
Orientation errors in AlphaFold3 predictions for HLA‑DP alleles reduce performance, suggesting a need for orientation‑aware modeling.
Training NetMHC‑II Pan on the same limited dataset yields a 10% lower AUC, confirming the data efficiency of the new approach.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: AlphaFold3 sometimes predicts reverse peptide orientation for HLA‑DP, leading to reduced binding prediction accuracy.

combination_strategies.rationale: Combine complementary predictions to improve overall AUC.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Slight performance boost over either model alone.

open_questions: Can orientation‑corrected AlphaFold3 models further improve predictions for HLA‑DP alleles?
How will the model perform on rare HLA alleles with minimal PepSeq data?

talk_title: Spatial metabolomics and microdevice delivery to map intratumoral drug responses

biomarkers.name: GSSG/GSH ratio
biomarkers.finding: Elevated GSSG/GSH ratio in cell‑death zones indicating oxidative stress

biomarkers.name: Uric acid
biomarkers.finding: Enriched in cell‑death zones; potential danger signal

biomarkers.name: Glutamine / glutamate levels
biomarkers.finding: Spatially distinct between stromal and tumor regions, suggesting metabolic crosstalk

MOA_summary: Implantable microdevices release drugs locally; MALDI imaging visualizes spatial drug distribution and metabolic changes. In KRAS‑mutant tumors, inhibition of macropinocytosis with EIPA reduces intracellular amino acids, confirming nutrient acquisition via protein uptake. Metabolomic signatures (purine catabolites, lipid profiles) correlate with immune cell neighborhoods, enabling prediction of immune states from metabolites alone.
key_findings: EIPA delivery via microdevice decreased tumor amino‑acid levels in KRAS‑mutant xenografts, confirming macropinocytosis‑driven nutrient uptake.
DQ‑BSA fluorescence showed lysosomal processing of extracellular protein in KRAS‑mutant but not KRAS‑wildtype cells.
Spatial metabolomics identified two macrophage metabolic states: purine‑rich (pro‑inflammatory) versus lipid‑rich (pro‑tumor).
Logistic regression on metabolite data predicted immune neighborhoods with ROC AUC >0.85, suggesting metabolites can serve as surrogate immune biomarkers.
Microdevice can co‑deliver diverse agents (small molecules, base editors, antisense oligos, ADCs) with release kinetics tuned by PEG molecular weight.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Macropinocytosis inhibition selectively affected KRAS‑mutant cells, indicating a metabolic vulnerability not present in KRAS‑wildtype tumors.
open_questions: What temporal dynamics govern the shift from purine‑driven to lipid‑driven macrophage states during treatment?
Can stabilized isotope tracing be integrated with the microdevice to quantify metabolic fluxes in vivo?

talk_title: Implantable microdevice platform for intratumoral pharmacodynamics and precision treatment selection

biomarkers.name: Cleaved caspase‑3
biomarkers.finding: Spatial gradient of apoptosis correlates with local drug concentration

biomarkers.name: PCNA
biomarkers.finding: Reduced proliferation in high‑dose zones of DNA‑damaging agents

biomarkers.name: Interferon response genes
biomarkers.finding: Up‑regulated in high‑efficacy temozolomide zones

biomarkers.name: EGFR pathway activity
biomarkers.finding: Down‑regulated after local osimertinib delivery

biomarkers.name: mTOR signaling
biomarkers.finding: Suppressed in everolimus‑treated zones

models.name: Microdevice GBM study
models.genotype_or_key_features: Patients with primary or recurrent GBM, n≈12

models.name: CTCL microdevice study
models.genotype_or_key_features: Patients with cutaneous T‑cell lymphoma, multiple agents

clinical_data.population: GBM patients receiving temozolomide or lomustine

MOA_summary: The grain‑of‑rice sized implantable microdevice contains ~20 reservoirs delivering micro‑doses of drugs directly into the tumor for 3–5 days. Spatial gradients of drug concentration are measured by MALDI‑MS or fluorescence, while parallel readouts (IHC, spatial transcriptomics, CyTOF) capture pharmacodynamic effects on tumor cells, immune subsets, and signaling pathways, enabling rapid prediction of systemic response and mechanistic biomarker discovery.
readouts: IHC (cleaved caspase‑3, PCNA, DNA damage markers)
key_findings: Implantable microdevices were placed in >350 patients across 13 trials with no serious adverse events.
Local drug concentration gradients correlated with apoptosis (cleaved caspase‑3) and proliferation inhibition (PCNA) in GBM xenografts.
Spatial transcriptomics confirmed on‑target pathway inhibition for osimertinib (EGFR), lapatinib (HER2/EGFR), everolimus (mTOR), and abemaciclib (cell‑cycle).
In a pilot GBM cohort, microdevice‑derived biomarkers predicted clinical temozolomide response in 5/5 patients, outperforming MGMT methylation status.
Interferon response programs were strongly associated with high‑efficacy temozolomide zones, suggesting a role for innate immunity in alkylator sensitivity.
Combination testing on the device (e.g., palbociclib + anti‑CD4) identified synergistic immune remodeling that translated to superior efficacy in preclinical models.
The platform enables rapid, patient‑specific drug selection and early human PD data for investigational agents, accelerating IND‑enabling studies.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Device insertion causes limited local inflammation confined to 3–4 cell layers, distinguishable from drug‑induced effects.
Variable drug diffusion can create concentration‑dependent resistance zones; precise polymer formulation mitigates this.

combination_strategies.rationale: Palbociclib induced protumorigenic macrophages; CD4 blockade reprograms macrophages to enhance efficacy
combination_strategies.phenotype: Superior tumor regression in mouse models compared to palbociclib alone

combination_strategies.rationale: Panobinostat increased antigen‑presenting neutrophils; CSF1R blockade targets pro‑tumor macrophages
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced anti‑tumor immunity in xenograft studies

combination_strategies.rationale: Order of release may affect synergy; microdevice allows testing of priming vs. concurrent exposure
combination_strategies.phenotype: Variable efficacy depending on sequence; data pending

open_questions: How do microdevice‑induced spatial drug gradients compare to systemic pharmacokinetics in heterogeneous tumors?
Can the platform be adapted for real‑time monitoring of immune checkpoint inhibitor activity in lymph nodes?
What is the minimal drug dose required locally to achieve systemic‑equivalent efficacy while avoiding toxicity?

talk_title: Granulocyte reprogramming in gastric metaplasia and phosphodiesterase inhibition as neoadjuvant therapy

biomarkers.name: SLFN12L-positive granulocytic MDSC
biomarkers.finding: frequency increased from normal mucosa to intestinal metaplasia to tumor; decreased after 2 weeks of tadalafil

biomarkers.name: ARG1 expression
biomarkers.finding: detectable arginase‑1 signature in human granulocytic MDSCs

biomarkers.name: exhausted CD8 T cells (PD‑1 high)
biomarkers.finding: high in pretreatment tumors; reduced after tadalafil + FLOT

models.name: Helicobacter felis infection Glee1‑LacZ reporter
models.genotype_or_key_features: wild‑type vs Glee1 knockout; metaplasia at 6 months

models.name: IL1B‑Tet‑On gastrin‑CreERT2 + MNU carcinogen
models.genotype_or_key_features: IL1B overexpression + nitrate carcinogen; tumor penetrance 45‑90%

models.name: Stage I‑III gastric cancer neoadjuvant window trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: 14 patients receiving tadalafil 20 mg daily for 2 weeks before FLOT

MOA_summary: Helicobacter infection induces type I interferon production by plasmacytoid dendritic cells, which together with Hedgehog signaling up‑regulates SLFN4 in granulocytic neutrophils, converting them into suppressive MDSCs that consume L‑arginine via arginase‑1 and maintain low cyclic GMP through PDE6 activity. Inhibition of PDE6 with tadalafil or sildenafil raises intracellular cGMP, activates PKG, and triggers apoptosis of these MDSCs, restoring CD8 T‑cell function and enhancing response to standard FLOT chemotherapy.
key_findings: Glee1 knockout mice retain normal gastric architecture despite Helicobacter infection, implicating Glee1‑positive myeloid cells in metaplasia.
SLFN4 expression marks granulocytic MDSCs; knockdown of SLFN4 increases apoptosis and reduces T‑cell suppression in vitro.
IL1B overexpression plus MNU yields gastric tumors in 45‑90% of mice; sildenafil treatment reduces tumor size and SLFN4‑positive cell infiltration.
In a human neoadjuvant window trial (n≈14), 2 weeks of tadalafil 20 mg daily decreased SLFN12L‑positive granulocytic MDSC frequency in tumor biopsies.
Subsequent FLOT chemotherapy after tadalafil further eliminated MDSCs and exhausted T‑cells while expanding cytotoxic CD8 T‑cells.
Bulk RNA‑seq of sorted SLFN4‑positive cells identified strong up‑regulation of PDE6, providing a mechanistic link to cyclic GMP metabolism.

combination_strategies.rationale: Standard neoadjuvant regimen; MDSC depletion by tadalafil may enhance chemotherapy‑induced tumor cell kill and immune activation.
combination_strategies.phenotype: complete loss of granulocytic MDSCs and exhausted T cells; increase in CD8 cytotoxic T cells in post‑treatment biopsies

open_questions: Can cyclic GMP phosphodiesterase inhibition prevent progression from intestinal metaplasia to invasive gastric cancer in a true preventive setting?
What is the relative contribution of PDE5 versus PDE6 inhibition to MDSC apoptosis, and can selective PDE6 inhibitors improve therapeutic index?
Does SLFN4 expression in T cells have functional relevance, and how does it affect overall immune remodeling?

talk_title: Mechanistic insights into KRAS activation and novel direct KRAS inhibitors

speaker.name: Frank McCormick
speaker.affiliation: Frederick National Laboratory for Cancer Research

MOA_summary: McCormick described a covalent inhibitor that targets a histidine-95 residue unique to KRAS, allowing binding to the GTP‑bound state and shifting the switch‑one region to an inactive state‑one conformation. He also presented a PI3K‑alpha “breaker” that blocks the Ras‑PI3K interaction, increasing affinity from ~20 µM to ~6 nM and preventing downstream signaling.
key_findings: SHP2 phosphatase activity is dispensable for RAS activation, indicating a non‑catalytic role in SOS‑mediated signaling.
A covalent inhibitor targeting KRAS histidine‑95 can engage both GDP‑ and GTP‑bound KRAS, overcoming the limitation of off‑state inhibitors.
Binding of the inhibitor to the switch‑two pocket forces switch‑one into an unproductive state‑one conformation, blocking effector interactions.
The PI3K‑alpha breaker increases KRAS‑PI3K binding affinity to low‑nanomolar range, enabling selective disruption of KRAS‑driven PI3K signaling.
Structural studies reveal distinct conformations of RAF activation complexes that may be exploitable for next‑generation RAF inhibitors.

talk_title: Clinical activity of the KRAS G12D inhibitor 3034 combined with EGFR blockade

biomarkers.name: KRAS G12D ctDNA
biomarkers.finding: ≥90% reduction in ctDNA levels observed in majority of patients across tumor types

biomarkers.name: CA19-9
biomarkers.finding: >90% decline in CA19-9 concurrent with radiographic response in pancreatic cancer patient

models.name: QTX-3034 study
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS G12D mutant colorectal, pancreatic, endometrial cancers; some with MTAP loss

clinical_data.population: KRAS G12D mutant across CRC, PDAC, endometrial

clinical_data.value: partial responses observed; durable responses noted
comparable between monotherapy and combination over 6 months
majority of mice showed regression; in patient, deep partial response sustained >9 months
clinical_data.n: preclinical mouse n=??; clinical patient case n=1
clinical_data.population: HPAC pancreatic xenograft; patient with KRAS G12D PDAC

clinical_data.value: 300 days until radiographic PD after combination therapy
clinical_data.population: KRAS G12D PDAC with MTAP loss

MOA_summary: Pitt presented the allosteric KRAS inhibitor 3034, which binds the switch‑two pocket and forces switch‑one into an inactive conformation, thereby blocking effector signaling of both G12D and G12V mutants. When combined with EGFR inhibition (cetuximab), the regimen suppresses wild‑type KRAS amplification and upstream RTK feedback, leading to deep tumor regressions and >90% ctDNA clearance in KRAS‑mutant colorectal, pancreatic, and endometrial cancers.
readouts: tumor volume measurements in xenograft models
key_findings: 3034 monotherapy slowed tumor growth in KRAS G12D HPAC xenografts but did not induce regression.
Combination of 3034 with cetuximab produced tumor regressions in the majority of mice and deep partial responses in patients.
≥90% reduction in KRAS G12D ctDNA correlated with radiographic responses across colorectal, pancreatic, and endometrial cohorts.
No increase in cetuximab‑related rash or Grade ≥3 adverse events observed with the combination.
Acquired resistance manifested as KRAS wild‑type amplification after ~300 days of therapy, highlighting a limitation of the current inhibitor’s coverage of wild‑type KRAS.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Acquired KRAS wild‑type amplification led to disease progression despite continued KRAS G12D inhibition.

combination_strategies.rationale: EGFR blockade drives KRAS into inactive state, enhancing allosteric inhibitor engagement and preventing feedback activation.
combination_strategies.phenotype: synergistic tumor regression in preclinical models and objective responses in patients

combination_strategies.rationale: Early‑line combination may increase depth of response in KRAS‑mutant colorectal and pancreatic cancers.
combination_strategies.phenotype: planned evaluation in first‑ and second‑line settings

combination_strategies.rationale: Broad RTK blockade to further suppress upstream signaling and enhance KRAS inhibition.
combination_strategies.phenotype: preclinical synergy across KRAS mutant models

open_questions: How can wild‑type KRAS amplification be effectively targeted alongside mutant KRAS inhibition?
What is the optimal dosing strategy to achieve EC90 coverage across all KRAS alleles while maintaining tolerability?

talk_title: Preclinical and early clinical evaluation of a pan‑KRAS on/off state inhibitor (3034)

biomarkers.name: ctDNA
biomarkers.finding: early ctDNA suppression correlated with tumor response in dose‑escalation patients

MOA_summary: The oral pan‑KRAS inhibitor 3034 binds both the inactive and active states of KRAS, suppressing downstream MAPK signaling and shifting the tumor microenvironment toward immune activation while reducing immunosuppressive signals, leading to tumor growth inhibition and shrinkage that can be enhanced by EGFR blockade.
key_findings: Endometrioid endometrial cancer patients showed robust and durable responses despite co‑occurring PI3K pathway alterations.
Combination of 3034 with cetuximab prevented outgrowth observed with monotherapy in preclinical models.
Preclinical mouse studies demonstrated tumor shrinkage at low oral doses (10‑30 mg) with reduced gastrointestinal adverse events compared with earlier KRAS inhibitors.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Monotherapy led to outgrowth over time, suggesting adaptive resistance mechanisms.
Preliminary data indicate possible KRAS allele amplification as a resistance mechanism in patients.

combination_strategies.rationale: Upstream EGFR blockade suppresses compensatory MAPK re‑activation seen with KRAS inhibition alone.
combination_strategies.phenotype: prevents tumor outgrowth and enhances shrinkage in xenograft models

combination_strategies.rationale: Concurrent inhibition of upstream SHP2 could more completely shut down RAS signaling.
combination_strategies.phenotype: potentially higher efficacy but increased toxicity observed in early studies

open_questions: What specific genotypic features (e.g., PI3K mutation status) drive the observed responses in endometrial cancer?
Does PI3K wild‑type versus mutant status affect early ctDNA suppression and durability of response?

talk_title: Clinical development of multi‑selective KRAS tricomplex inhibitors (Deruxon Rasib) and combination strategies

targets.dependency_context: KRAS mutant solid tumors (NSCLC, PDAC, CRC)

biomarkers.name: Disease Control Rate
biomarkers.finding: Overall DCR 95% in Resolute‑302 trial (n not specified)

biomarkers.name: Progression‑Free Survival
biomarkers.finding: Lower bound of 95% CI for PFS exceeded median PFS of standard second‑line chemotherapy

biomarkers.name: Overall Survival
biomarkers.finding: Lower bound of 95% CI for OS exceeded median OS of standard second‑line chemotherapy

models.name: Resolute‑302 (Phase III)
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS mutant solid tumors, includes G12, G13, Q61 variants

clinical_data.value: lower 95% CI > median PFS of standard care
lower 95% CI > median OS of standard care

MOA_summary: Deruxon Rasib forms a tricomplex with cyclophilin A and KRAS, sterically blocking effector binding and stimulating GTP hydrolysis, thereby shutting down MAPK and PI3K signaling across multiple KRAS mutants; this dual mechanism yields tumor regression and induces immune activation without significant liver toxicity.
readouts: in vivo tumor xenograft growth curves
key_findings: Resolute‑302 met its primary and secondary endpoints with a disease control rate of 95% in KRAS mutant patients.
PFS and OS curves showed lower confidence‑interval bounds exceeding those of standard second‑line chemotherapy, supporting a survival benefit.
Preclinical models demonstrated durable tumor regression and prevention of relapse when Deruxon Rasib was combined with covalent KRAS G12C inhibitor (Aleron) or G12D inhibitor (Zoldon).
Combination of Deruxon Rasib with gemcitabine/nab‑paclitaxel improved durability of response in pancreatic cancer models, informing the frontline RESOLUTE‑303 trial design.
Resistance mechanisms identified include amplification of the mutant KRAS allele (≈60% of resistant cases) and non‑genomic adaptations (~40%).
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Amplification of mutant KRAS allele observed in ~60% of resistant patient samples.
Non‑genomic resistance mechanisms (e.g., pathway re‑activation) account for the remaining resistant cases.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target both on‑state (covalent) and off‑state (tricomplex) KRAS pools to prevent adaptive resistance.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression in resistant KRAS G12C models

combination_strategies.rationale: Dual inhibition of distinct KRAS mutant alleles to block compensatory signaling.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Durable tumor control in KRAS G12D allograft models

combination_strategies.rationale: Combine RAS pathway blockade with cytotoxic chemotherapy to target complementary tumor cell states in pancreatic cancer.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved response durability versus chemotherapy alone in PDAC models

combination_strategies.rationale: Simultaneously trap KRAS in GDP state while Deruxon Rasib promotes GTP hydrolysis, covering both conformations.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Strong synergistic antitumor activity in pan‑KRAS‑amplified resistant models

open_questions: What are the specific non‑genomic mechanisms driving resistance in the 40% of patients without KRAS amplification?
Can cyclophilin A expression levels predict response or resistance to tricomplex inhibitors?

patent_or_ip_hints: US Patent Application for KRAS tricomplex inhibitors (filed 2022)

talk_title: KEAP1-NRF2 mediated resistance to KRAS inhibition and glutamine metabolism targeting

targets.dependency_context: KRAS G12D inhibitor treated PDAC and NSCLC models

MOA_summary: Loss of KEAP1 stabilizes NRF2, reprogramming transcription and glutamine metabolism to confer resistance to KRAS G12D and pan‑RAS inhibitors; co‑inhibition of glutamine metabolism with DRP104 restores sensitivity in vitro and in vivo.
key_findings: CRISPR screens identified KEAP1 knockout as a top driver of resistance to KRAS G12D (MRTX1133) and pan‑RAS (RMC7977) inhibitors.
KEAP1 KO cells show NRF2 and NQO1 up‑regulation and reduced sensitivity to KRAS inhibitors in both pancreatic and NSCLC models.
A glutamine antagonist (DRP104) combined with KRAS inhibitors suppresses tumor growth in three resistant in vivo models.
Patient and PDX datasets reveal higher expression of a KEAP1‑deficiency gene signature in KRAS inhibitor‑resistant cases.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: KEAP1 loss drives resistance via NRF2‑dependent transcriptional program distinct from RAS, MYC, TEAD pathways.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target glutamine metabolism up‑regulated by KEAP1 loss to overcome KRAS inhibitor resistance
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor growth inhibition in PDAC PDX, lung allograft, and KRAS‑mutant models

talk_title: Selective ULK1/2 inhibitor 2088 synergizes with KRAS pathway blockade
MOA_summary: The ULK1/2 inhibitor 2088 blocks autophagy initiation, enhancing the efficacy of KRAS pathway inhibitors (MEK or KRAS G12C inhibitors) by preventing drug‑tolerant persister cell emergence without clonal selection.
key_findings: 2088 shows higher cellular affinity than existing ULK1 inhibitors in NanoBRET assays.
Combination of 2088 with trametinib (MEK inhibitor) or sotorasib (KRAS G12C inhibitor) yields strong synergy in NSCLC H2030 cells.
In vivo, 2088 plus sotorasib significantly improves tumor growth inhibition compared to monotherapy in Calu‑1 xenografts.
DNA barcode tracking indicates the combination delays clonal outgrowth and reduces drug‑tolerant persister populations.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: ULK1 inhibition overcomes adaptive autophagy‑mediated resistance; no specific resistance to 2088 observed.

combination_strategies.rationale: Block autophagy that supports survival of KRAS‑inhibited cells
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced tumor regression in xenograft models

combination_strategies.rationale: Target parallel MAPK signaling while suppressing autophagy
combination_strategies.phenotype: High synergy in vitro

talk_title: Modeling acquired resistance to pan‑KRAS inhibitor RMC‑6236 in pancreatic cancer
MOA_summary: KRAS Y64 mutations disrupt binding of the pan‑KRAS molecular glue RMC‑6236, conferring resistance; resistance can be overcome by MEK inhibition, suggesting downstream MAPK blockade as a viable combination.
key_findings: Y64D mutation abolishes RMC‑6236 binding to KRAS and prevents tri‑complex formation with RAF.
KRAS Y64D engineered cell lines show >50‑fold increase in IC50 to RMC‑6236 compared with wild‑type.
In xenografts, Y64D tumors exhibit markedly reduced response to RMC‑6236 at both low and high doses.
MEK inhibitor restores sensitivity in Y64D mutant models, indicating downstream MAPK blockade can bypass resistance.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Resistance driven by a single point mutation (Y64) that blocks molecular glue engagement; not due to KRAS amplification.

combination_strategies.rationale: Bypass KRAS‑mediated resistance caused by Y64 mutation
combination_strategies.phenotype: Restored tumor growth inhibition in Y64D models

talk_title: Combining RAS inhibitors with ribonucleotide reductase inhibitor BBI 825 to prevent ecDNA‑driven resistance

biomarkers.name: KRAS amplification (ecDNA)
biomarkers.finding: High baseline ecDNA correlates with rapid resistance emergence

models.name: RW7213
models.genotype_or_key_features: MSS CRC, ecDNA+, KRAS amplification prone

models.name: CLR142
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS G12C CRC, acquired KRAS amplification at progression

MOA_summary: Inhibition of ribonucleotide reductase with BBI 825 limits nucleotide pools, suppressing ecDNA‑driven KRAS amplification and delaying or preventing resistance to KRAS inhibitors in colorectal cancer models.
key_findings: Combination of BBI 825 with adagrasib or sotorasib completely prevented resistance emergence in ecDNA‑positive RW7213 cells.
BBI 825 suppressed KRAS amplification in resistant subclones and reduced MEK amplification in deraxurib‑treated cells.
In RW7213 xenografts, BBI 825 plus adagrasib markedly delayed tumor growth and lowered KRAS amplification frequency.
In CLR142 PDX, addition of BBI 825 enhanced efficacy of sotorasib plus cetuximab and reduced c‑MYC expression.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Resistance driven by ecDNA‑mediated oncogene amplification; BBI 825 selectively impairs this process.

combination_strategies.rationale: Prevent KRAS amplification–driven resistance in ecDNA‑positive CRC
combination_strategies.phenotype: No resistant clones detected in vitro

combination_strategies.rationale: Same as above; block nucleotide supply for ecDNA replication
combination_strategies.phenotype: Significant delay of resistance in RW7213 xenografts

talk_title: JAK‑STAT pathway inhibition sensitizes KRAS G12D mutant pancreatic cancer to KRAS inhibitors

speaker.name: Yi Zhao
speaker.affiliation: Ruijin Hospital, Shanghai Jiao Tong University School of Medicine

biomarkers.name: phospho‑STAT3 (Y705)
biomarkers.finding: Higher baseline p‑STAT3 correlates with resistance to KRAS G12D inhibitor

models.name: MRT1133‑resistant PDAC line
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS G12D, acquired resistance, high p‑STAT3

clinical_data.population: advanced PDAC patients receiving HRS‑4642 + gemcitabine + pardusol

MOA_summary: KRKRAS G12D inhibition induces acute STAT3 activation via IL‑6 feedback; STAT3 inhibition restores sensitivity and synergizes with KRAS inhibitors in resistant pancreatic cancer models.
key_findings: Acquired KRAS G12D inhibitor‑resistant PDAC cells display up‑regulated JAK1/STAT3 signaling.
An oral STAT3 inhibitor selectively kills KRAS‑resistant cells and synergizes with KRAS G12D inhibitor in vitro.
Combination of KRAS inhibitor and STAT3 inhibitor leads to durable tumor regressions in KPC CDX models, overcoming relapse seen with KRAS inhibitor alone.
JAK1 inhibitor tofacitinib also blocks phospho‑STAT3 and enhances KRAS inhibitor efficacy.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: STAT3 activation acts as both acute and chronic resistance mechanism to KRAS inhibition.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target STAT3 activation that mediates resistance
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression in murine PDAC models

combination_strategies.rationale: Block upstream JAK1 to suppress STAT3 phosphorylation
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced cell viability reduction in combination

talk_title: VEGFR2 blockade overcomes KRAS G12D inhibitor resistance driven by PI3Kγ activation

biomarkers.name: phospho‑VEGFR2
biomarkers.finding: Elevated in KRAS inhibitor‑resistant models

MOA_summary: Acquired resistance to KRAS G12D inhibition activates a KRAS‑PI3Kγ‑AKT‑SP1 axis that drives VEGF‑A secretion and autocrine VEGFR2 signaling; VEGFR2 blockade with DC101 restores sensitivity.
key_findings: Resistant PDOs show increased VEGF‑A expression and phospho‑VEGFR2 activation.
CRISPR‑mediated VEGFR2 knockout or DC101 treatment sensitizes resistant cells to MRTX1133.
PI3Kγ inhibition reduces VEGF‑A/VEGFR2 signaling and re‑sensitizes resistant models.
Combination of MRTX1133 with DC101 markedly suppresses tumor growth in multiple resistant xenograft models.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Resistance mediated by a KRAS‑PI3Kγ‑SP1‑VEGF‑VEGFR2 autocrine loop; targeting VEGFR2 overcomes this specific pathway.

combination_strategies.rationale: Block autocrine VEGF‑VEGFR2 signaling sustaining resistance
combination_strategies.phenotype: Significant tumor growth inhibition in resistant xenografts

combination_strategies.rationale: Disrupt upstream driver of VEGF‑A production
combination_strategies.phenotype: Decreased VEGF‑A secretion and restored KRAS inhibitor sensitivity

talk_title: KRAS G12C inhibition induces chromosomal instability creating a vulnerability to Aurora kinase A inhibition

targets.dependency_context: KRAS G12C inhibitor‑induced CIN high NSCLC lines

biomarkers.name: micronuclei formation
biomarkers.finding: Higher induction correlates with synergy between KRAS G12C and AURKA inhibition

MOA_summary: KRAS G12C inhibition induces DNA damage and micronuclei formation in a subset of NSCLC cells, activating ATR/ATM pathways that stabilize cyclin B1; Aurora kinase A inhibition forces mitotic catastrophe in these cells.
key_findings: KRAS G12C inhibitor LY3499446 induces variable DNA damage and micronuclei across NSCLC lines.
Aurora kinase A inhibitor LSN3321213 shows the strongest synergy with KRAS G12C inhibition, especially in micronuclei‑high cells.
Combination treatment leads to prolonged G2/M arrest and mitotic catastrophe, markedly reducing colony formation.
In vivo, low‑dose AURKA inhibition combined with KRAS G12C inhibitor produces tumor regression and extends relapse‑free survival.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Synthetic vulnerability limited to KRAS‑inhibitor‑induced chromosomal instability; not observed in CIN‑low lines.

combination_strategies.rationale: Exploit mitotic dependency created by KRAS‑inhibitor‑induced CIN
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression at nanomolar AURKA inhibitor doses

talk_title: TAP2 downregulation drives immune evasion and can be therapeutically reversed

speaker.name: Kurt Sch"alper
speaker.affiliation: University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center, Department of Oncology

targets.dependency_context: lung cancer cells (KRAS or EGFR mutant)
acquired resistance to dual checkpoint blockade
myeloid-derived suppressor cells in lung tumor microenvironment
lung and pancreatic tumors with immune exclusion phenotype

biomarkers.name: TAP2 low expression
biomarkers.finding: Patients with low TAP2 had a hazard ratio of 1.8 for death after immunotherapy (n≈819, retrospective cohort)

biomarkers.name: IL4 RNA in tumor microenvironment
biomarkers.finding: IL4 RNA colocalized with CD11b+ myeloid cells in TAP2-low tumor regions

biomarkers.name: SOX1 upregulation
biomarkers.finding: TAP2 knockdown increased SOX1 protein levels; overexpression of TAP2 reduced SOX1

models.name: 819 treatment‑naïve lung tumors
models.genotype_or_key_features: baseline specimens, diverse histology, prior to any therapy

models.name: KRAS‑mutant and EGFR‑mutant lung cancer lines
primary human lung tumors (n≈250) for spatial transcriptomics
Tumor‑Associated Primary Explants (TAPEs)
models.genotype_or_key_features: patient‑derived lung tumors cultured up to 10 days

clinical_data.population: low TAP2 lung cancer patients treated with immunotherapy

MOA_summary: Downregulation of TAP2 in lung cancer cells reduces antigen presentation modestly (10‑15% peptide loss) but more critically impairs interferon‑γ signaling via upregulation of the inhibitor SOX1 and suppression of chemokines (CXCL10, CCL5, CXCL11), leading to T‑cell exclusion. IL‑4 produced by tumor‑associated myeloid cells epigenetically silences TAP2, an effect reversible by IL‑4R blockade or epigenetic drugs (HDAC inhibitors, taxanes). Restoring TAP2 re‑sensitizes cells to cytokine signaling and tumor‑antigen specific killing. Separately, secreted factors such as PLA2G10 act as active T‑cell excluders, limiting lymphocyte migration and promoting tumor growth; blocking these molecules restores T‑cell infiltration.
key_findings: 24% of treatment‑naïve lung cancers show cancer‑cell selective TAP2 downregulation; only 6% show TAP1 loss.
Low TAP2 expression predicts an 80% increased risk of death (HR 1.8) after immunotherapy, with no effect on chemotherapy outcomes.
TAP2 knockout reduces tumor‑antigen specific CD8 T‑cell killing more than TAP1 loss; re‑expression of TAP2 rescues killing.
TAP2 loss leads to a 10‑15% reduction in total HLA‑bound peptides but causes a >50% decrease in interferon‑γ‑induced gene expression (e.g., IDO1, PD‑L1) via SOX1 up‑regulation.
IL‑4 secreted by CD11b+ myeloid cells epigenetically closes the TAP2 promoter, an effect not reversed by exogenous IFN‑γ/TNF‑α.
HDAC inhibitors (vorinostat), taxanes, and dasatinib up‑regulate TAP2 without increasing PD‑L1, restoring antigen presentation and T‑cell killing in vitro.
Secreted phospholipase PLA2G10 acts as a T‑cell excluder; overexpression in mouse models accelerates tumor growth and reduces CD8 infiltration, while anti‑PLA2G10 antibody restores T‑cell entry.
Spatial analysis of 250 lung cancers identifies ~20% with a dominant immune‑excluded phenotype, which correlates with poorer immunotherapy survival.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: TAP2 loss confers resistance specifically to checkpoint blockade, not to chemotherapy.
IL‑4–mediated epigenetic silencing of TAP2 is not overcome by IFN‑γ/TNF‑α stimulation, indicating a durable resistance mechanism.

combination_strategies.rationale: Blocks IL‑4–driven epigenetic silencing of TAP2, restoring antigen presentation and enhancing PD‑1 blockade efficacy
combination_strategies.phenotype: In autologous co‑culture, combined IL‑4R blockade + pembrolizumab increased CD8 activation (CD25+) and cancer cell killing (Annexin V+ EpCAM+)

combination_strategies.rationale: Epigenetically re‑expresses TAP2 without up‑regulating PD‑L1
combination_strategies.phenotype: Increased TAP2 protein, surface peptide load, and tumor‑antigen specific T‑cell killing in vitro

combination_strategies.rationale: Induces TAP2 expression and inflammatory signaling
combination_strategies.phenotype: Up‑regulation of TAP2 and enhanced T‑cell mediated cytotoxicity

combination_strategies.rationale: Neutralizes secreted PLA2G10 to relieve active T‑cell exclusion
combination_strategies.phenotype: In mouse KPC and MC38 models, antibody treatment increased CD8 infiltration and synergized with anti‑PD‑1

open_questions: What upstream signals drive IL‑4 production by myeloid cells in TAP2‑low tumors?
Can plasma IL‑4 levels reliably predict TAP2 epigenetic silencing across cancer types?
What are the precise molecular mechanisms by which PLA2G10 impairs T‑cell migration?
Will TAP2‑restoring epigenetic therapies improve clinical outcomes when combined with checkpoint inhibitors in a prospective trial?

talk_title: Chromatin redistribution of Rb reveals estrogen‑response activation by CDK4/6 inhibition

models.name: Patients treated with CDK4/6 inhibitor monotherapy

clinical_data.n: n=3 cell lines, n=2 PDX, n=patients (paired)

MOA_summary: CDK4/6 inhibition dephosphorylates Rb, causing widespread chromatin binding; beyond canonical E2F repression, Rb is recruited to active enhancers of estrogen‑responsive genes, enhancing their expression (including cyclin D1), which can blunt cell‑cycle arrest unless blocked by endocrine therapy.
key_findings: CDK4/6 inhibition increases chromatin‑bound Rb at >10,000 sites, 75% at promoters and many at active enhancers.
Rb binding at non‑promoter enhancers is associated with increased H3K27ac and up‑regulation of nearby estrogen‑response genes.
CRISPR disruption of Rb‑bound enhancers attenuates estrogen‑response gene induction and cyclin D1 protein up‑regulation.
Combining CDK4/6 inhibitors with endocrine therapy yields deeper suppression of cell‑cycle genes and prevents cyclin D1‑mediated resistance.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Rb‑driven estrogen response may limit monotherapy efficacy and contribute to resistance in ESR1‑mutant tumors.

combination_strategies.rationale: Blocks Rb‑induced estrogen‑response program that counteracts cell‑cycle arrest
combination_strategies.phenotype: Deeper suppression of E2F targets and reduced cyclin D1 levels

open_questions: Which E2F family members are recruited by Rb at non‑cell‑cycle sites?
Can selective Rb mutants separate E2F repression from estrogen‑response activation?

talk_title: Programmable RNA sensors for cancer‑specific fusion targeting
MOA_summary: ADAR‑mediated RNA editing sensors are programmed to bind cancer‑specific fusion breakpoints; upon editing a stop‑codon in the guide, translation of a downstream cytotoxic payload (e.g., iCaspase9) is enabled, selectively killing fusion‑positive cells.
key_findings: Linear and gap RNA sensors selectively activate reporter expression in cells harboring the target fusion RNA.
In MCF7 cells with an endogenous ARFGAP2‑SOX2 fusion, gap sensors drive mNeon fluorescence with >10‑fold specificity over fusion‑negative MCF10A.
Fusion‑targeted sensors coupled to iCaspase9 reduce MCF7 viability to ~80% versus ~100% for non‑targeting controls.
High‑throughput sensor library screening begins to define design rules for effective RNA‑sensor constructs.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Variable sensor performance suggests sequence‑context dependent efficacy; loss or mutation of the fusion breakpoint could confer resistance.
open_questions: What are the optimal payloads (immune‑stimulating, secreted factors) for in‑vivo efficacy?
How can delivery be achieved at scale (LNP, viral nanoparticles) to reach tumor cells in patients?

talk_title: Systems design challenges and technology opportunities for clinical trial enrollment

speaker.name: Laura Mangravite
speaker.affiliation: Digitalis Commons (nonprofit sister of a venture firm)

MOA_summary: Mangravite described the clinical trial enrollment problem as a two‑sided optimization issue, outlining public and private data‑centric solutions, the $2 billion private investment landscape, and the need for many‑to‑many trial‑patient visibility, buyer‑driven optimization, and vertical integration of platform technologies.
key_findings: Only ~7% of cancer patients enroll in trials, whereas ~8% would be needed to fill open slots.
Approximately 50% of trials fail to meet enrollment targets.
Public data sources (e.g., ClinicalTrials.gov, TEFCA) provide trial listings but lack granular inclusion/exclusion criteria.
Private sector investment in trial‑matching technologies exceeds $2 billion, roughly matching public investment.
Current solutions are fragmented (within‑system vs. cross‑system) and often expose patients to only a subset of available trials.
Future focus should shift toward study design that aligns trials with patient care needs, leveraging AI for faster, smaller trials.

talk_title: Clinical trial matching, nudges, and AI: practical experiences and future directions
MOA_summary: Schrag presented the evolution of clinical trial matching nudges at MSK—from first‑generation email alerts to second‑generation progression‑timed nudges (which showed modest, non‑significant accrual gains)—and outlined a vision for third‑generation AI‑driven, EHR‑integrated, multi‑modal workflows that combine eligibility prescreening with immediate patient‑facing actions such as video consent and scheduling.
key_findings: MSK’s first‑generation nudge system sends daily email alerts about potential trial matches based on genomic profiling.
A randomized trial of progression‑timed nudges showed a small, non‑significant increase in accrual (a few percentage points).
Clinician time and health‑system money were identified as the top barriers to trial accrual.
Patient trust, rather than travel logistics, was highlighted as the primary barrier to enrollment.
Telehealth, video‑informed consent, and home‑based monitoring were proposed as operational solutions to reduce patient burden.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: AI prescreening cannot replace dynamic eligibility checks (e.g., pregnancy test on day of enrollment).
Overly strict matching criteria risk excluding eligible patients; overly lax criteria increase clinician workload.

talk_title: Serial Plasma Comprehensive Genomic Profiling Detects Therapy Resistance and Guides Management of Metastatic Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer

speaker.name: William Bowers
speaker.affiliation: Johns Hopkins University, Sidney Kimmel Comprehensive Cancer Center

biomarkers.name: ctDNA detectability (mutation count)
biomarkers.finding: Undetectable ctDNA associated with significantly better overall survival

biomarkers.name: Actionable alteration on plasma
biomarkers.finding: Patients with actionable alteration who received genotype‑matched therapy had significantly improved overall survival

clinical_data.metric: unique clinical utility of plasma genotyping

MOA_summary: Serial plasma comprehensive genomic profiling (PCGP) using Guardant 360 detects actionable and resistance alterations (e.g., EGFR, MET, EGFR T790M, C797S, BRAF) in metastatic NSCLC, enabling genotype‑matched therapy selection and demonstrating that ctDNA detectability correlates with overall survival.
key_findings: 37% of NSCLC patients had an actionable alteration on first plasma PCGP.
In treatment‑naive patients, 36% harbored FDA‑approved genotype‑matched alterations.
22% of treatment‑naive patients started genotype‑matched therapy within 30 days of plasma result.
Undetectable ctDNA was associated with significantly longer overall survival versus detectable ctDNA.
Patients receiving genotype‑matched therapy based on plasma findings had significantly improved overall survival.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: MET amplification and secondary EGFR alterations were the most common resistance mechanisms to osimertinib.
Resistance landscapes differed between first/second‑generation EGFR TKIs (T790M‑driven) and osimertinib (MET, C797S).
open_questions: How does plasma PCGP performance compare with tissue NGS in low‑tumor‑burden settings?
Can earlier serial sampling improve detection of emerging resistance?

talk_title: Feasibility of Plasma ctDNA for Detecting Brain Metastasis Specific Alterations in Lung Cancer

speaker.name: Dr. Stabile
speaker.affiliation: University of Pittsburgh Hillman Cancer Center

biomarkers.name: MET amplification in plasma ctDNA
biomarkers.finding: Detected more frequently in patients with brain metastases than without

models.name: retrospective metastatic NSCLC ctDNA cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 28 patients with paired pre/post‑craniotomy plasma and brain tissue

clinical_data.metric: MET amplification detection in plasma at progression
clinical_data.population: patients with progression after EGFR TKI

clinical_data.metric: MET amplification in brain metastasis tissue
MET amplification detection in matched plasma (complete sets)
clinical_data.population: patients with paired tissue and plasma

clinical_data.metric: MET amplification detection in all plasma (including partial sets)

MOA_summary: Plasma ctDNA profiling detects MET amplifications, which are enriched in lung cancer brain metastases; radiation may enhance ctDNA shedding, improving detection of brain‑specific alterations.
key_findings: MET amplification is significantly more common in lung cancer brain metastases (16.4%) than in primary tumors (3.7%).
Plasma ctDNA detects MET amplifications in ~10% of patients at progression, but concordance with tissue is limited.
In paired pre‑ and post‑craniotomy samples, only 1 of 2 tissue MET amplifications were captured in plasma.
Radiation therapy appears to increase ctDNA shedding, leading to higher VAF/CNV signals post‑radiation.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Technical challenges capture MET amplification by ctDNA, especially with low tumor burden or intrathoracic disease.
open_questions: Can assay sensitivity be improved to reliably detect brain‑specific MET amplifications?
What is the optimal timing of plasma collection relative to radiotherapy for maximal ctDNA yield?

talk_title: AXL mRNA Overexpression in CTCs as a New Potential Liquid Biopsy Biomarker in Non-Small Cell Lung Cancer

biomarkers.name: AXL mRNA in CTCs
biomarkers.finding: Detected in ~13% baseline, 16% after first osimertinib cycle, 9% during treatment, 12% at progression

biomarkers.name: AXL mRNA in CTCs during immunotherapy
biomarkers.finding: Low baseline; increased after anti‑PD‑1 in a few patients

models.name: NSCLC patient CTC cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: Group A: osimertinib-treated (multiple time points); Group B: immunotherapy-treated

clinical_data.metric: AXL overexpression after first osimertinib cycle

MOA_summary: AXL mRNA overexpression in size‑enriched circulating tumor cells (CTCs) is detected by RT‑qPCR and rises after osimertinib exposure, suggesting a role in EMT‑mediated resistance and providing a potential biomarker for selecting AXL‑targeted therapies.
readouts: Droplet digital PCR for EGFR mutations
key_findings: AXL mRNA is overexpressed in CTCs of ~13% of NSCLC patients at baseline and increases after the first osimertinib cycle.
AXL overexpression persists in a subset of patients at disease progression, indicating a possible resistance mechanism.
During anti‑PD‑1 therapy, AXL overexpression emerges in a few patients, suggesting adaptive resistance.
CTC‑derived AXL expression provides complementary information to ctDNA and tissue analyses.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: AXL overexpression is linked to EMT and resistance to EGFR TKIs and immune checkpoint inhibitors.

combination_strategies.rationale: Counteract EMT‑driven resistance mediated by AXL
combination_strategies.phenotype: Potential restoration of osimertinib sensitivity

open_questions: What is the therapeutic threshold of AXL mRNA expression in CTCs for initiating AXL‑targeted therapy?
How does CTC‑derived AXL expression correlate with tissue AXL protein levels?

talk_title: Circulating Tumor Cell‑Informed Pipeline for Rapid Personalization of Synergistic Cytokine‑Armed CAR‑NK Cell Therapy in Pancreatic Ductal Adenocarcinoma

models.name: BxPC3 (MUC4+), AsPC1 (mesothelin+)
models.genotype_or_key_features: PDAC lines used for killing assays

MOA_summary: CTC enrichment via a microfluidic Labyrinth device identifies mesothelin or MUC4 expression, guiding selection of cytokine‑armed CAR‑NK cells (IL‑12/IL‑15) that synergistically enhance NK activation, granzyme B release, and long‑term tumor killing in PDAC models.
readouts: Granzyme B and IFN‑γ flow cytometry
key_findings: 87% of PDAC patients had CTCs expressing at least one CAR‑NK target (mesothelin or MUC4).
MUC4‑positive CTCs were enriched in metastatic patients.
IL‑12 and IL‑15 armed CAR‑NK cells achieved near‑complete tumor eradication in 2D killing assays and eliminated spheroids by day 12.
Synergistic cytokine‑armed CAR‑NK cells provided superior tumor suppression and survival benefit in mouse models compared with conventional CAR‑NK.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Target expression heterogeneity (exclusive mesothelin vs. MUC4) underscores need for personalized CAR‑NK selection.

combination_strategies.rationale: Enhance NK activation and proliferation for synergistic anti‑tumor effect
combination_strategies.phenotype: Increased granzyme B, IFN‑γ, and sustained killing in vitro and in vivo

open_questions: How will CTC target expression evolve during therapy and affect CAR‑NK efficacy?
Can this pipeline be adapted for other solid tumors with distinct CTC antigens?

talk_title: ctDNA Clearance Dynamics in MSI‑High Colorectal Cancer Treated with Immune Checkpoint Inhibitors

biomarkers.name: ctDNA (Signatera tumor‑informed assay)
biomarkers.finding: Early ctDNA negativity after ICI start associated with markedly improved OS (HR 4.7, 4‑yr OS 95% vs 81%)

biomarkers.name: ctDNA clearance (any time)
biomarkers.finding: Patients who cleared ctDNA at any point had 10‑fold OS advantage (HR 10, 4‑yr OS 95.5% vs 66.1%)

clinical_data.metric: OS hazard ratio for early ctDNA negative vs positive
OS hazard ratio for any ctDNA clearance vs persistent positivity

MOA_summary: Longitudinal tumor‑informed ctDNA monitoring (Signatera) in MSI‑high metastatic colorectal cancer reveals that early and sustained ctDNA clearance after immune checkpoint inhibitor initiation predicts markedly improved overall survival.
key_findings: 73% of MSI‑high mCRC patients were ctDNA positive before ICI therapy.
77% achieved ctDNA clearance at least once after ICI start, median time 96 days.
Early clearance (<4 months) occurred in 44% and correlated with 4‑year OS of 95% vs 81% for ctDNA‑positive patients.
Any clearance conferred a 10‑fold OS advantage (95.5% vs 66.1% 4‑yr OS).
Sustained clearance (≥6 months) was observed in 63% of patients.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Persistent ctDNA positivity identified a high‑risk group with markedly inferior survival.
open_questions: Do ctDNA dynamics differ between pembrolizumab monotherapy and ipilimumab/nivolumab combination?
Can ctDNA monitoring guide optimal duration of ICI therapy in MSI‑high mCRC?

talk_title: Genomic Signatures in Red Blood Cell DNA‑Enabled Early Non‑Invasive Detection of Colorectal Neoplasia

biomarkers.name: RBC‑DNA genomic signature
biomarkers.assay: Low‑coverage whole‑genome sequencing of RBC DNA
biomarkers.finding: AUC 0.95 for advanced colorectal neoplasia; 85% sensitivity for advanced adenomas, 95% for CRC at 90% specificity

clinical_data.metric: AUC for advanced colorectal neoplasia (internal test)
Advanced adenoma sensitivity in external cohorts

clinical_data.value: Higher sensitivity for advanced adenomas and Stage 1 CRC

MOA_summary: Red blood cell‑associated DNA fragments, released during erythropoiesis under tumor‑induced genotoxic stress, carry distinct genomic coverage patterns that differ between colorectal cancer patients and healthy controls, enabling a sequencing‑based classifier to detect advanced neoplasia with high accuracy.
readouts: Low‑coverage whole‑genome sequencing of RBC DNA
key_findings: RBC DNA fragments are shorter (avg 4.3 kb) than genomic DNA but longer than cfDNA.
Distinct genome‑wide coverage patterns differentiate CRC patients from healthy donors.
A binary classifier achieved AUC 0.95, 85% sensitivity for advanced adenomas and 95% for CRC at 90% specificity.
Performance remained robust across external validation cohorts and was not confounded by blood counts or lifestyle factors.
In a prospective head‑to‑head comparison, RBC‑DNA testing outperformed FIT for detecting advanced adenomas and Stage 1 CRC.
open_questions: What are the specific genomic regions driving the RBC‑DNA signal and their biological relevance?
Can RBC‑DNA signatures be extended to other cancer types or pan‑cancer early detection?

talk_title: CUPIA 2 collaborative multi-agent LLM for cancer of unknown primary diagnosis

models.name: CUPIA 2 LLM framework
models.genotype_or_key_features: NGS mutation profiles across 19 cancer types

MOA_summary: CUPIA 2 uses a multi-agent large language model ensemble to ingest NGS variant data and generate tissue-of-origin predictions with rationale derived from a 100,000-patient Smart Data database, achieving ~77% top‑1 accuracy internally and ~62% on independent MSK data.
key_findings: CUPIA 2 achieved 77% top‑1 accuracy and 93% top‑3 accuracy across 19 cancer types in internal validation.
On independent MSK cohort (12,000 samples) top‑1 accuracy was 62% and top‑3 accuracy 82%.
Model provided rationale linking co‑mutations (e.g., KRAS G12D + TP53 R175H) to predicted tissue origins.
Chairman LLM overwrote 24% of ensemble predictions during training.
open_questions: How to improve performance on under‑represented cancer types such as small‑cell lung cancer and endometrial cancer?
Can multimodal inputs (RNA‑seq, imaging) be incorporated to boost accuracy?

talk_title: Deep learning model predicts palbociclib response in ER+ HER2‑ negative advanced breast cancer

speaker.name: Akshat Singhal
speaker.affiliation: City of Hope / UC San Diego

targets.dependency_context: ER+ HER2‑ negative advanced breast cancer

models.name: PalboVNN
models.genotype_or_key_features: trained on 900 cell lines, 718 gene panel

models.name: UCSD cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 139 patients, NGS within ±180 days of palbociclib start

MOA_summary: A visible neural network maps genomic alterations to hierarchical protein complexes, enabling prediction of palbociclib sensitivity; the model stratifies patients into sensitive (HR 0.38, median PFS +14 mo) and resistant groups, with specificity for CDK4/6 inhibition versus endocrine therapy alone.
key_findings: PalboVNN stratified UCSD patients (n=36) into responders vs non‑responders with HR 0.38 and median PFS benefit of 14 months.
In the larger MSK cohort (n=474) the model retained significance (HR 0.56) with an 18‑month median PFS advantage.
Model performance was specific to palbociclib‑containing regimens and did not stratify letrozole‑only patients.
Interpretation identified 18 protein assemblies (11 shared) driving predictions, including DNA integrity and AKT signaling.
A prospective phase‑II trial has been launched based on these findings.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: ROS1 mutation was a dominant driver of predicted resistance in a non‑responding patient.
open_questions: Can the VNN framework be generalized to other CDK4/6 inhibitors (ribociclib, abemaciclib) with comparable performance?
How will inclusion of clinical covariates (ancestry, comorbidities) affect model accuracy?

talk_title: AI‑derived H&E biomarkers predict survival under immunotherapy vs chemotherapy in NEPTUNE NSCLC trial

biomarkers.name: AI‑derived fibroblast density
biomarkers.finding: high fibroblast density associated with worse OS in both IO and chemo arms

biomarkers.name: AI‑derived immune cell density
biomarkers.finding: high immune density predicted better OS in IO arm but not in chemo arm

models.name: NEPTUNE phase III NSCLC
models.genotype_or_key_features: 468 H&E images (426 after QC), IO vs chemo arms

clinical_data.value: significant separation by median split of immune phenotype in IO arm

MOA_summary: PathAI's off‑the‑shelf AI model segments tumor, immune and fibroblast compartments on H&E slides, quantifies densities and spatial relationships, and these features improve survival stratification beyond PD‑L1 and TMB, especially predicting benefit from immunotherapy.
key_findings: Fibroblast density was a negative prognostic marker in both IO and chemo arms (high density → worse OS).
Immune cell density showed a predictive interaction: high density correlated with improved OS only in the immunotherapy arm.
Multivariate Cox model adding H&E features to PD‑L1/TMB achieved significant separation (p<0.05) whereas clinical features alone did not.
At 18‑month follow‑up, high‑risk group defined by H&E features had 21% event rate, supporting enrichment potential.
open_questions: Will prospective validation confirm the added predictive value of H&E‑derived biomarkers?
Can these AI features be integrated with other omics (e.g., RNA‑seq) for further improvement?

talk_title: Deep learning predicts metabolic pathway activity from routine H&E slides

models.name: ResNet34‑decoder metabolic pathway predictor
models.genotype_or_key_features: Tile‑level H&E images labeled with binary pathway activity from GSMMs

MOA_summary: A ResNet34 encoder‑decoder network is trained on 256 × 256 H&E tiles paired with binary metabolic pathway activity derived from patient‑specific genome‑scale metabolic models (GSMMs) built from TCGA transcriptomics, enabling prediction of pathway enrichment directly from morphology.
key_findings: Across 21 cancer types, 87% of 450 pathway‑cancer models achieved AUC > 0.5.
9% of models (top 10 pathways) reached AUC 0.65–0.85, notably nucleotide metabolism (AUC 0.85 in TGCT) and nuclear transport (AUC 0.79 in COAD).
Metabolic pathways with high predictability clustered in nucleotide synthesis, nuclear/mitochondrial transport, and fatty‑acid β‑oxidation.
Demonstrated feasibility of using H&E morphology to infer functional metabolic states, suggesting utility for trial enrichment.
open_questions: Will external validation on independent cohorts confirm the high‑performing pathways?
Can predicted metabolic activity be linked to response to pathway‑targeted therapies?

talk_title: Longitudinal fusion of low‑dose CT improves lung cancer risk prediction

speaker.name: Hanieh Ajami
speaker.affiliation: Moffitt Cancer Center / University of South Florida

models.name: Hybrid, Bi‑fusion, Tri‑fusion architectures
models.genotype_or_key_features: Sybil CNN features from up to three LDCT time points

MOA_summary: Multiple low‑dose CT scans are processed by the Sybil model to extract encoded and attention features; these are fused via vision and temporal transformers (Hybrid) or concatenated (Bi/ Tri‑fusion) to produce six‑year risk scores, leveraging temporal disease progression for improved discrimination.
key_findings: Hybrid architecture achieved near‑perfect AUC (0.99) at year 1 and maintained 0.94 at year 6 on NLST test set.
Hybrid model outperformed Bi‑fusion and Tri‑fusion across all horizons and also generalized to external Moffitt cohort after fine‑tuning.
Statistical tests confirmed significant performance differences (p < 0.05) between hybrid and other models.
Model handles missing time points via attention masking, making it applicable to real‑world screening data.
open_questions: How will incorporation of clinical/demographic risk factors affect long‑term prediction?
Can federated learning across institutions improve robustness to variable scan intervals?

talk_title: Multimodal deep learning predicts gastric cancer response to first‑line anti‑PD‑1 therapy

biomarkers.name: CRP multimodal model prediction
biomarkers.finding: predicted partial response associated with longer PFS (HR 0.45) and OS (HR 0.48) across training, validation, and two external cohorts

models.name: CRT multimodal model
models.genotype_or_key_features: CT radiomics + H&E pathomics (CLIME + CNN‑Transformer) + clinical features

MOA_summary: A self‑attention fusion network combines CT‑derived radiomic features, H&E‑derived graph‑based pathomic embeddings, and clinical variables to predict first‑line chemo‑immunotherapy response; high predicted PR status correlates with increased tumor fraction, up‑regulated immune‑activating pathways, and improved survival.
key_findings: CRT model achieved AUC 0.97 for response prediction in training set and validated across two external cohorts.
Predicted PR patients had significantly longer PFS and OS (HR ≈ 0.45–0.48) after multivariate adjustment for age, sex, PD‑L1 CPS.
Pathway analysis showed enrichment of immune‑activating signatures (alpha/gamma response) in predicted PR group versus immune‑inhibitory pathways (IL‑10, NF‑κB) in non‑PR group.
Model outperformed PD‑L1 CPS and unimodal radiomics or pathomics models in precision‑recall and confusion matrix metrics.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: High fibroblast density (from pathomics) was linked to non‑PR status and immune‑inhibitory gene expression.
open_questions: Will prospective multicenter trials confirm the CRT model’s predictive power for treatment selection?
Can the model be adapted for other immunotherapy regimens or combination therapies?

company_or_sponsor: Yangtze River Delta Guochi Intelligent Medical Technology Co.

talk_title: Lipid-associated macrophages as therapeutic targets and CAR‑monocyte strategy

speaker.name: Jennifer Guerrero
speaker.affiliation: Brigham and Women's Hospital, Department of Oncology

targets.dependency_context: CSF1R‑positive lipid‑associated macrophages after PARP inhibition
Up‑regulated on lipid‑associated macrophages after olaparib
Induced on macrophages by PARP inhibition

biomarkers.name: CD68
biomarkers.finding: ≈50% of breast tumor cells were CD68‑positive macrophages; high density correlated with worse relapse‑free survival

biomarkers.name: TREM2
biomarkers.finding: High TREM2 expression on TAMs associated with poor clinical outcome

biomarkers.name: CSF1R
biomarkers.finding: CSF1R up‑regulated on lipid‑associated macrophages after olaparib treatment

biomarkers.name: CD36
biomarkers.finding: Olaparib increased CD36 mean fluorescence intensity on murine bone‑marrow‑derived macrophages

models.name: TALAZO trial (talazaparib ± avelumab)
models.genotype_or_key_features: Advanced BRCA‑mutated breast cancer, pre‑ and on‑treatment biopsies

MOA_summary: PARP inhibition induces cGAS‑STING activation and CD8 T‑cell recruitment but simultaneously expands lipid‑associated, CSF1R‑ and TREM2‑positive macrophages that suppress immunity; anti‑CSF1R synergizes with PARP inhibition to double survival in mice, and a TREM2‑directed CAR‑monocyte platform is designed to phagocytose and eliminate these suppressive macrophages while secreting pro‑inflammatory cytokines.
key_findings: Olaparib treatment of BRCA‑deficient mice increased tumor‑infiltrating macrophages expressing PD‑L1, CSF1R, CD36 and TREM2.
Anti‑CSF1R combined with olaparib doubled median overall survival from ~100 to ~200 days in the mouse model, an effect dependent on CD8 T cells.
TREM2‑CAR monocytes specifically phagocytosed TREM2‑positive target cells in vitro, whereas irrelevant CARs did not.
In vivo, a single IV dose of TREM2‑CAR monocytes increased intratumoral CD8 T cells and reduced tumor burden by ~50% in a breast cancer model, further enhanced by anti‑PD‑1.
The AXLAP phase 1b trial (axcitilimab + olaparib) is >50% enrolled in metastatic HER2‑negative, BRCA1/PALB2‑mutated breast cancer, enabling correlative studies of macrophage modulation.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: PARP + PD‑L1 showed no benefit in a prior trial, likely due to expansion of lipid‑associated macrophages.
CAR‑monocytes avoid the poor tumor penetration seen with CAR‑T cells because monocytes naturally home to hypoxic, macrophage‑rich niches.

combination_strategies.rationale: PARP inhibition induces immunogenic DNA damage but also expands suppressive macrophages; CSF1R blockade mitigates the latter.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Survival doubled, increased CD8 T‑cell infiltration

combination_strategies.rationale: CSF1R blockade alone leaves residual immunosuppression; checkpoint inhibition further activates T cells.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Additional tumor regression in mouse model

combination_strategies.rationale: Direct elimination of TREM2‑positive suppressive macrophages and cytokine release to boost T‑cell activity.
combination_strategies.phenotype: 50% tumor burden reduction; synergy with anti‑PD‑1

open_questions: Can CAR‑monocytes be further engineered with cytokine payloads to enhance durability of anti‑tumor immunity?
What is the optimal sequencing of PARP inhibition, CSF1R blockade, and CAR‑monocyte therapy in patients?

talk_title: Redefining myeloid states in glioma with CNMF and organoid platforms

speaker.name: Tyler Miller
speaker.affiliation: Case Western Reserve University School of Medicine, Department of Neurology

biomarkers.name: IL‑1β
biomarkers.finding: IL‑1β expression defines a systemic inflammatory program in monocytes infiltrating glioma organoids

biomarkers.name: IL‑4
biomarkers.finding: IL‑4 signaling program is highly active in monocyte‑derived macrophages across glioma samples

models.name: glioma GBO organoids
models.genotype_or_key_features: Intact tumor microenvironment, retains microglia, macrophages, T cells for 2‑8 weeks

models.name: CSF samples from CAR‑T trial (44 patients)
models.genotype_or_key_features: CSF myeloid cells profiled pre‑ and post‑CAR‑T infusion

MOA_summary: Consensus non‑negative matrix factorization (CNMF) decomposes glioma single‑cell RNA‑seq into identity and activity programs, revealing four dominant myeloid activity programs (systemic inflammatory, microglial inflammatory, dexamethasone‑driven immunosuppressive, hypoxia‑driven scavenger); the scavenger program predicts poor response to checkpoint blockade and CAR‑T therapy, while the microglial inflammatory program correlates with response.
key_findings: CNMF identified 14 myeloid programs across glioma, with four activity programs accounting for >90% of cells.
The scavenger immunosuppressive program (hypoxia‑linked) was enriched in non‑responders to checkpoint blockade and CAR‑T therapy.
Microglial inflammatory program was enriched in responders and associated with improved overall survival.
CAR‑T infusion induced a dose‑dependent surge of the complement immunosuppressive program in CSF myeloid cells across all patients.
Baseline high scavenger program levels in CSF predicted worse overall survival after CAR‑T treatment.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Dexamethasone treatment drives a distinct immunosuppressive myeloid program that may blunt immunotherapy efficacy.

combination_strategies.rationale: Reduce pyroptosis‑mediated IL‑1β release that fuels the scavenger program.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Decreased scavenger program expression in organoid co‑culture

combination_strategies.rationale: Target IL‑4‑driven immunosuppressive polarization observed in glioma macrophages.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Proposed to shift macrophages toward inflammatory program

open_questions: Can selective inhibition of the dexamethasone‑driven program improve CAR‑T efficacy without compromising steroid benefits?
What upstream signals trigger the hypoxia‑linked scavenger program in brain metastases?

talk_title: Clinical targeting of immunosuppressive macrophages: lessons from IL‑4, IL‑1 and checkpoint combos

targets.dependency_context: IL‑4/IL‑13 signaling in monocyte‑derived macrophages of NSCLC
IL‑1α/β signaling in age‑related tumor promotion
CSF1R‑positive TAMs in BRCA‑mutated breast cancer
Do‑not‑eat‑me signal on lymphoma and AML cells

biomarkers.name: PD‑L1
biomarkers.finding: Baseline PD‑L1 expression used to select patients for first‑line immunotherapy in NSCLC

biomarkers.name: TREM2
biomarkers.finding: CCR2/5 inhibition reduced intratumoral TREM2⁺ macrophages in a neoadjuvant lung cancer cohort

models.name: Anakinra IL‑1 blockade trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: Advanced solid tumors progressing on PD‑1 blockade

clinical_data.value: morphine use reduced from 8×/day to 0 within weeks

clinical_data.population: NSCLC progressing on PD‑1 therapy receiving dupilumab

MOA_summary: IL‑4Rα blockade with dupilumab interrupts tumor‑derived type II cytokine signaling that imprints immunosuppressive IL‑4‑responsive macrophages in the bone marrow, restoring anti‑tumor immunity in a subset of PD‑1‑refractory NSCLC patients; combined IL‑1α/β inhibition (anakinra) further disrupts inflammasome‑driven myeloid suppression, enhancing checkpoint efficacy in preclinical models of lung, colorectal and pancreatic cancer.
key_findings: Dupilumab added to ongoing PD‑1 blockade induced remission and complete pain relief in a patient with progressive NSCLC.
Approximately one‑third of PD‑1‑refractory NSCLC patients achieved disease control after three dupilumab doses, allowing continuation of checkpoint therapy.
IL‑4 neutralization reduced emergency myelopoiesis and tumor‑associated macrophage accumulation in KRAS‑mutant mouse lung cancer.
Combined IL‑4Rα and PD‑1 blockade synergistically reduced tumor burden in KP1 mouse model compared with either monotherapy.
Dual IL‑1α/β blockade (anakinra) suppressed age‑related inflammasome activation and tumor growth in old mouse models of colorectal and pancreatic cancer.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: CCR2/5 inhibition depleted TREM2⁺ macrophages but also unexpectedly reduced peripheral lymphocytes, potentially limiting efficacy.
CD47 antibodies caused hemolysis due to CD47 expression on red blood cells, leading to halted development.

combination_strategies.rationale: IL‑4 blockade reprograms suppressive macrophages, enabling T‑cell mediated checkpoint response.
combination_strategies.phenotype: 33% disease control in refractory NSCLC; synergistic tumor shrinkage in mouse models

combination_strategies.rationale: Concurrent inhibition of IL‑1α/β reduces inflammasome‑driven myeloid suppression and may augment IL‑4 blockade.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Significant tumor growth inhibition in aged mouse models of CRC and PDA

combination_strategies.rationale: Block tolerogenic AXL signaling in TAMs to prevent immunosuppressive reprogramming after therapy‑induced cell death.
combination_strategies.phenotype: No OS benefit in phase 2 lung cancer trial when combined with PD‑1 blockade

open_questions: What biomarkers predict which NSCLC patients will respond to IL‑4Rα blockade combined with PD‑1 inhibition?
Can simultaneous targeting of IL‑4 and IL‑1 pathways overcome resistance in tumors with mixed myeloid programs?

talk_title: Engineering next‑generation CAR T cells: membrane, signaling, and armoring strategies

speaker.name: Remus
speaker.affiliation: Senior Scientific Director, Miltenyi Biotec; Adjunct, Johns Hopkins School of Public Health

models.name: syngeneic and xenograft tumor models
models.genotype_or_key_features: CAR T cell infusion, tumor burden monitoring

MOA_summary: Remus described engineering of CAR T cells by optimizing membrane residence, hinge, transmembrane, and intracellular signaling domains, and by adding armoring payloads (e.g., cytokine secretion, PD‑1/FAS/CD200 re‑programming) to improve persistence, reduce exhaustion, and modulate the tumor microenvironment.
key_findings: Kinase‑inhibitor “metronomic rest” improves CAR T memory phenotype (Ramos et al.).
Self‑clustering of Cilta‑cel‑like CARs drives cytokine release and neurotoxicity in vitro, which can be mitigated by selecting non‑clustering VHH binders.
Bispecific/tandem CARs (e.g., CD19/CD20) show variable efficacy depending on transmembrane and co‑stimulatory domain configuration.
Armoring with IL‑18 or anti‑PD‑1 scFv linked to IL‑12 improves tumor control in murine models.
TGF‑β during manufacturing preserves central‑memory phenotype and enhances in vivo persistence of CAR T cells.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Antigen loss (CD19 splice variants, CD22 down‑regulation) identified as a major resistance mechanism.
CAR self‑association can cause cytokine‑independent proliferation, raising safety concerns.

combination_strategies.rationale: Enhance CAR T memory phenotype during expansion
combination_strategies.phenotype: Increased central‑memory markers, improved in vivo persistence

combination_strategies.rationale: Reprogram tumor‑associated macrophages (M2 → M1)
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced CD206+ macrophages, enhanced tumor control

combination_strategies.with_agent: Bispecific antibodies secreted by CAR T
combination_strategies.rationale: Target antigen‑negative escape variants
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved killing of CD19‑negative relapses in mouse models

open_questions: What is the optimal balance of CAR clustering to avoid tonic signaling‑induced exhaustion while preserving potency?
Can in vivo CAR T manufacturing achieve comparable memory phenotypes without ex vivo cytokine conditioning?

talk_title: Innate bridge cell engineering: gamma‑delta T cells for cancer therapy

speaker.name: Trent Spencer
speaker.affiliation: Professor, Emory University; Director, Marcus Center for Cellular Therapies

targets.dependency_context: stress antigen recognition on tumor cells

models.name: xenograft neuroblastoma/osteosarcoma models
models.genotype_or_key_features: human gamma‑delta T cell adoptive transfer

MOA_summary: Spencer outlined the use of ex vivo expanded Vδ1 and Vδ2 gamma‑delta T cells, which recognize phosphoantigens (via butyrophilin‑mediated TCR signaling), stress ligands (NKG2D), and mediate ADCC (CD16), and described strategies to enhance memory phenotype (TGF‑β during expansion) and to combine with chemotherapy, zoledronate, and dinutuximab for synergistic tumor killing.
readouts: flow cytometry for CD16, NKG2D, memory markers
in vivo tumor growth inhibition in mouse xenografts
key_findings: Allogeneic gamma‑delta T cells can be expanded from donors without causing graft‑versus‑host disease.
Donor fitness (exercise, age) correlates with expansion potential and CD16 expression.
TGF‑β during manufacturing preserves central‑memory phenotype and markedly improves in vivo persistence and osteosarcoma killing.
Triple combination (temozolomide + zoledronate + dinutuximab) with gamma‑delta T cells yields superior neuroblastoma killing in preclinical models.
BiTE or CAR expression in gamma‑delta T cells achieves comparable cytotoxicity to conventional alpha‑beta CAR T cells.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Gamma‑delta T cells lack HLA restriction, reducing risk of tumor immune evasion via HLA loss.
CD16 down‑regulation during TGF‑β expansion may limit ADCC, requiring genetic replacement.

combination_strategies.rationale: Induce tumor stress antigens to enhance gamma‑delta T cell recognition
combination_strategies.phenotype: Increased tumor cell killing in neuroblastoma models

combination_strategies.rationale: Elevate intracellular phosphoantigens for Vδ2 TCR activation
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced activation and cytotoxicity of Vδ2 cells

combination_strategies.rationale: Provide ADCC target for CD16‑expressing gamma‑delta cells
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic killing of neuroblastoma in vitro and in vivo

combination_strategies.rationale: Redirect gamma‑delta cells to tumor‑specific antigens (e.g., PTK7)
combination_strategies.phenotype: Targeted killing of PTK7‑positive neuroblastoma and T‑ALL

open_questions: What is the optimal donor selection algorithm to maximize expansion and functional potency?
Can large‑scale bioreactor processes achieve consistent Vδ1/Vδ2 ratios for allogeneic products?

patent_or_ip_hints: Gamma‑delta T cell expansion protocol patents

talk_title: In vivo lentiviral vector CAR delivery platform (VivoVEC) and RACR armoring

models.name: NSG MHC‑KO with Nalm6 CD19+ tumor
models.genotype_or_key_features: in vivo lentiviral delivery, CAR T generation

models.name: cynomolgus macaque
models.genotype_or_key_features: IV VivoVEC dosing, anti‑CD20 CAR T kinetics, B‑cell depletion

MOA_summary: Larsen presented VivoVEC, a surface‑engineered lentiviral vector displaying a multi‑domain fusion (anti‑CD3 scFv, CD80, CD58) that binds T cells with high avidity, delivers a CAR transgene and a rapamycin‑activated cytokine receptor (RACR) that provides selective IL‑2/IL‑15‑like signaling upon rapamycin administration, enabling in vivo generation, expansion, and functional persistence of CAR T cells without lymphodepletion.
readouts: in situ hybridization for vector copy
key_findings: Multi‑domain fusion particles achieve rapid (≤10 min) high‑avidity binding to human T cells in vitro.
Co‑stimulatory‑enhanced particles generate polyfunctional CAR T cells with superior serial killing versus anti‑CD3‑only particles.
In NSG MHC‑KO humanized mice, particles encoding anti‑CD19 CAR produce durable tumor control at low vector doses.
In cynomolgus macaques, a single IV dose yields ~40% CAR‑positive T cells, peaks at days 7‑10, and induces rapid, complete B‑cell depletion.
Transient low‑grade CRS observed; no severe infusion reactions despite high in vivo transduction.
RACR activation with rapamycin markedly expands CAR T cells and improves tumor clearance in low‑antigen (CD22‑low) models.
