resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Anti‑CAR immune response (against Lu16 scFv and hinge) caused B‑cell recovery in macaques, indicating potential immunogenicity of murine scFv components.
No off‑target transduction of endothelial or hepatocyte cells detected at therapeutic doses.

combination_strategies.rationale: Activate RACR to provide selective IL‑2/IL‑15 signaling to in vivo engineered CAR T cells
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced CAR T expansion and tumor clearance in CD22‑low models

combination_strategies.rationale: Potentially increase vector access to T cells in heavily pre‑treated patients
combination_strategies.phenotype: Not yet tested; discussed as future option

open_questions: What is the optimal balance of anti‑CD3 affinity to achieve efficient transduction without excessive activation?
Can the RACR system be safely cycled on/off in patients to modulate CAR T persistence?

talk_title: Spatial omics workflow and challenges for neuroimmune characterization
MOA_summary: Described the development and benchmarking of cell segmentation tools and hierarchical phenotyping pipelines for spatial transcriptomics and proteomics, emphasizing the importance of accurate nucleus and membrane segmentation to correctly assign immune phenotypes in tumor sections.
key_findings: Developed Circle Cell Segmentation Across Scales (CCSAS) tool that outperforms existing segmentation methods qualitatively and quantitatively.
Implemented a ranked hierarchy pixel‑by‑pixel classification approach for immune phenotyping that improves cell type assignment compared with simple dimensionality reduction.
Demonstrated that accurate segmentation dramatically impacts downstream spatial analyses such as pairwise cell interaction and cellular neighborhood quantification.
open_questions: How can segmentation algorithms be further optimized for heterogeneous tumor architectures and nerve‑rich regions?
What are the best practices for integrating multi‑modal spatial data (RNA, protein, metabolomics) in a single analysis pipeline?

talk_title: Spatial transcriptomics of perineural invasion in pancreatic cancer and PDGFD‑PDGFRB functional validation

speaker.name: Will Wang
speaker.affiliation: Massachusetts General Hospital / Harvard Medical School

targets.dependency_context: PDAC cancer cells adjacent to nerves
neurons, Schwann cells, PDAC cells near nerves

biomarkers.name: PDGFD protein
biomarkers.finding: Enriched in cancer cells proximal to nerves (p<0.01)

models.name: DRG‑Matrigel organoid co‑culture
models.genotype_or_key_features: tdTomato‑labeled PDAC organoids with CRISPR‑activated PDGFD or PDGFRB

clinical_data.value: significantly increased in PDGFD‑overexpressing orthotopic tumors

clinical_data.metric: neurite arborization length per cell body area

MOA_summary: Spatial transcriptomics identified PDGFD enrichment in cancer cells adjacent to nerves; functional assays showed PDGFD‑PDGFRB signaling promotes perineural invasion by enhancing cancer cell invasiveness, neurite outgrowth, and Schwann‑cell mediated tumor migration, which can be blocked by PDGFRB inhibition.
key_findings: A bulk RNA‑derived classifier predicts perineural invasion and uncovered missed PNI cases on histology.
Spatial profiling revealed PDGFD‑PDGFRB as the only ligand‑receptor pair overlapping between sequencing‑based (GeoMx) and imaging‑based (SMI) datasets.
CRISPR activation of PDGFD in PDAC organoids increased 3D neural invasion index, which was dose‑dependently blocked by the PDGFRB inhibitor SU16f.
Recombinant PDGFD stimulates neurite outgrowth from DRG neurons; this effect is abolished by PDGFRB inhibition.
In vivo, PDGFD overexpression augments orthotopic tumor nerve invasion, while PDGFRB inhibition reduces sciatic nerve invasion.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: PDGFRB inhibition selectively blocks PDGFD‑driven invasion without affecting baseline tumor growth in control lines.

combination_strategies.rationale: Block PDGFD‑PDGFRB signaling axis that drives perineural invasion.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Dose‑dependent reduction of nerve invasion in 3D assay and in vivo models.

open_questions: Will PDGFRB inhibition improve response to immunotherapy in PNI‑positive pancreatic cancer?
What are the downstream signaling pathways in neurons and Schwann cells mediating PDGFD effects?

talk_title: Integrating spatial neuroimmune signatures with bulk RNA to predict immunotherapy resistance

biomarkers.name: nerve injury signature (e.g., ATF3, C-JUN)
biomarkers.finding: Enriched in non‑responders to anti‑PD‑1 across multiple cohorts (p<0.01)

biomarkers.name: neurodegeneration GO terms
biomarkers.finding: High scores correlate with worse overall survival in TCGA head‑and‑neck cohort

models.name: Neoadjuvant anti‑PD‑1 trial (resectable skin cancer)
models.genotype_or_key_features: pre‑ and post‑treatment bulk RNA, spatial transcriptomics, proteomics

clinical_data.value: HR 0.56 for low nerve‑injury signature vs high

MOA_summary: Bulk RNA signatures of nerve injury and neurodegeneration are enriched in tumors that fail anti‑PD‑1 therapy; spatial profiling links these signatures to immunosuppressive microenvironments (e.g., FoxP3+ Tregs, myeloid cells) around injured nerves, suggesting nerve‑mediated resistance mechanisms.
readouts: IHC for NFH and immune markers
key_findings: Half of anti‑PD‑1 non‑responders exhibited histologic perineural invasion, whereas responders had significantly lower rates.
Bulk RNA‑seq of non‑responders showed enrichment of nerve injury pathways (ATF3, C‑JUN) and neurodegeneration GO terms.
Applying neurodegeneration signatures to TCGA head‑and‑neck cohort stratified patients by survival, with high‑signature tumors having worse outcomes.
Spatial profiling demonstrated co‑localization of nerve injury markers with immunosuppressive cells (FoxP3+ Tregs, myeloid cells) but not with effective anti‑tumor immune signatures.
NanoString GeoMx analysis confirmed that nerve‑adjacent regions in non‑responders express higher checkpoint molecules (PD‑L1, CTLA‑4) compared with responders.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Nerve injury signatures correlate specifically with immunosuppressive phenotypes, suggesting a selective resistance mechanism rather than a global tumor aggressiveness effect.

combination_strategies.rationale: Targeting nerve‑mediated immunosuppression may sensitize tumors to checkpoint inhibition.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Potential reduction of Treg enrichment around nerves and improved response rates (hypothetical).

combination_strategies.rationale: Block PDGFD‑PDGFRB axis implicated in nerve‑driven invasion and immunosuppression.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced perineural invasion and decreased immunosuppressive niche in preclinical models.

open_questions: Can nerve‑injury signatures be used prospectively to stratify patients for combination neuro‑targeted therapies with immunotherapy?
What are the optimal biomarkers (RNA vs protein) for detecting nerve‑mediated immunosuppression in routine clinical samples?

talk_title: Targeting Telomeres in Cancer and Aging

speaker.name: Maria Blasco
speaker.affiliation: CNIO (Spanish National Cancer Research Centre), Department of Molecular Oncology

targets.dependency_context: mouse models of aging and fibrosis
POT1 knock‑in mouse models of pulmonary and renal fibrosis
lung cancer and glioblastoma mouse models

models.name: ultra‑long telomere chimeric mice
models.genotype_or_key_features: ES cells with ultra‑long telomeres, GFP‑labeled

models.name: AAV‑TERT gene therapy mice
models.genotype_or_key_features: treated at 1 yr or 2 yr of age

models.name: POT1 knock‑in mouse
models.genotype_or_key_features: human POT1 mutation, short telomeres, fibrosis phenotype

models.name: chemical carcinogenesis model (DMBA/TPA)
models.genotype_or_key_features: assesses papilloma formation in TRF1 mutants

models.name: K‑RAS‑driven lung cancer model (CNIO/Barbacid)
models.genotype_or_key_features: K‑RAS activation with/without telomerase gene therapy

MOA_summary: Blasco's work demonstrates that extending telomere length via TERT gene therapy or ultra‑long telomere chimeric mice prolongs lifespan and reduces cancer incidence in mice, while telomere shortening drives fibrosis in lung and kidney. Mutations in the shelterin component POT1 act either as dominant‑negative (causing short telomeres and fibrosis) or gain‑of‑function (producing abnormally long but fragile telomeres that predispose to cancer). Inhibition of the shelterin protein TRF1, whose stability is regulated by PI3K/Akt‑mediated phosphorylation, impairs tumor growth in lung cancer and glioblastoma mouse models without overt toxicity, establishing TRF1 as a druggable telomere‑protective target.
key_findings: Mice engineered to carry ultra‑long telomeres live significantly longer and develop 40% fewer spontaneous tumors compared with wild‑type controls.
AAV‑mediated, non‑integrative TERT gene therapy administered at 1 yr or 2 yr of age extends median lifespan by ~15% and markedly reduces progression of bleomycin‑induced pulmonary fibrosis.

POT1 knock‑in mice recapitulate human telomere‑biology disease phenotypes: short telomeres, multi‑telomeric FISH signals, reduced litter size, intestinal and testicular atrophy, and accelerated lung and kidney fibrosis.
Genetic or pharmacologic inhibition of TRF1 reduces tumor burden by ~50% in KRAS‑driven lung cancer and glioblastoma mouse models without increasing normal tissue toxicity.

TRF1 stability is controlled by Akt‑mediated phosphorylation at threonine 330; phospho‑dead (T330A) mice exhibit shorter telomeres, increased DNA damage, and higher papilloma formation after chemical carcinogenesis, whereas phospho‑mimetic (T330E) mice maintain longer telomeres but display rapid telomere attrition in adult tissues.
Screening of FDA‑approved drugs identified PI3K/Akt and RAS pathway inhibitors as indirect TRF1 destabilizers, linking extracellular signaling to telomere protection.
open_questions: What molecular mechanisms determine the species‑specific rate of telomere shortening and its link to DNA‑repair efficiency?
How does TRF1 support pluripotency and adult stem‑cell function, and can pathways that rescue TRF1 loss be therapeutically exploited?
Will long‑telomere, POT1‑mutant cancers be selectively vulnerable to agents that exacerbate telomere fragility?
What are the long‑term safety implications of systemic telomerase gene therapy in humans, especially regarding potential tumor promotion?

talk_title: Dendritic cell mitochondrial metabolism regulates anti-tumor immunity
MOA_summary: Qi identified that cDC1 cells in tumors exist in two mitochondrial states; OPA1 drives mitochondrial fusion and high oxidative phosphorylation, enabling superior T cell priming. Loss of OPA1 impairs mitochondrial cristae, reduces oxidative phosphorylation, and diminishes anti‑tumor immunity. NRF1 acts downstream of OPA1 to regulate electron transport chain genes, and its overexpression partially rescues OPA1 deficiency.
readouts: flow cytometry for T cell proliferation
key_findings: cDC1 cells display mitochondrial high and low populations across multiple tumor models (B16, lung, osteosarcoma, pancreatic).
Mitochondrial‑high cDC1 have higher oxidative phosphorylation, ATP production, and superior ability to prime CD8 T cells in vitro and in vivo.
Tumor progression shifts cDC1 toward the mitochondrial‑low, dysfunctional state, reducing T cell priming capacity.
Adoptive transfer of mitochondrial‑high cDC1 slows tumor growth and synergizes with anti‑PD‑L1, anti‑CTLA‑4, and anti‑PD‑1 therapies, leading to durable immunity upon rechallenge.

OPA1 is up‑regulated in tumor‑infiltrating cDC1; its conditional knockout impairs mitochondrial cristae, oxidative phosphorylation, and anti‑tumor immunity, leading to uncontrolled tumor growth in multiple models.

NRF1 expression is down‑regulated in OPA1‑deficient cDC1; NRF1 knockout phenocopies OPA1 loss, while NRF1 overexpression rescues mitochondrial function and T cell priming, dependent on intact electron transport chain.

combination_strategies.rationale: Checkpoint blockade may amplify T cell responses primed by metabolically fit cDC1
combination_strategies.phenotype: Complete tumor growth blockade in combination with mitochondrial‑high cDC1 adoptive transfer

combination_strategies.rationale: Dual checkpoint inhibition enhances T cell activation alongside improved DC priming
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor control in mouse models

open_questions: What upstream tumor microenvironment signals repress OPA1 expression in cDC1?
Can in vivo metabolic re‑programming of cDC1 (e.g., via micropinocytosis modulation) replace adoptive transfer?
Do other DC subsets (e.g., cDC2) exhibit similar mitochondrial heterogeneity and therapeutic potential?

talk_title: BACH1 drives tumor‑intrinsic immune evasion in triple‑negative breast cancer

biomarkers.name: BACH1 activity signature (HRO genes)
biomarkers.finding: High BACH1 signature predicts non‑response to pembrolizumab + chemo and correlates with low TILs in I‑SPY and other TNBC cohorts

clinical_data.value: significantly lower in high BACH1 tumors
clinical_data.population: high‑risk TNBC patients receiving pembrolizumab + chemo

MOA_summary: BACH1 acts as a transcriptional repressor that silences type I interferon genes (e.g., IFI21) in TNBC cells; loss of BACH1 derepresses interferon signaling, increases CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration and activation, reduces lung metastasis, and improves response to checkpoint blockade, whereas BACH1 overexpression promotes immune‑evasive, hypoxia‑driven cell states.
key_findings: A transition cell state (20‑30% of tumor cells) links proliferative, invasive, and hypoxic clusters and predicts T‑cell exclusion in TNBC.
BACH1 is identified as a top regulator of this transition state; knockout reduces the transition population, overexpression expands it.
High BACH1 activity signature correlates with poor response to pembrolizumab + chemotherapy and low TILs in I‑SPY and other TNBC cohorts.
BACH1 knockout in mouse TNBC models increases CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration, activation, and reduces lung metastasis; phenotype rescued by BACH1 re‑expression.
Mechanistically, BACH1 directly binds and represses IFI21 (type I IFN) locus; loss of BACH1 elevates type I IFN signaling, enhancing anti‑tumor immunity.
Combination of BACH1 loss with anti‑CTLA‑4 yields superior tumor control compared with either alone, indicating synergy with checkpoint blockade.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Long‑term BACH1 knockout tumors eventually resume growth, suggesting compensatory pathways emerge.
BACH1‑deficient tumors show early T‑cell activation but later develop increased Treg/CTLA‑4‑mediated resistance.

combination_strategies.rationale: BACH1 loss increases T‑cell infiltration but may induce adaptive Treg/CTLA‑4 resistance; blockade may sustain response
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced tumor control in BACH1‑KO mice

combination_strategies.rationale: BACH1 binds heme; pharmacologic heme may destabilize BACH1 and mimic knockout
combination_strategies.phenotype: Increased CD8⁺ T‑cell activation in early experiments

open_questions: Which additional stressors (e.g., low glucose, iron deprivation) modulate BACH1 activity in human TNBC?
Can pharmacologic BACH1 inhibitors be developed with acceptable toxicity given its developmental roles?
What are the downstream effectors of BACH1‑mediated immune evasion beyond type I IFN repression?

talk_title: Temporal dynamics of PD‑1 blockade reveal drug holidays and CD22 as a co‑inhibitory target

biomarkers.name: Ly108 (TCF‑1) positive progenitor exhausted T cells
biomarkers.finding: Preserved by drug holiday, depleted by continuous PD‑1 blockade

biomarkers.name: CD63⁺ CD45RA⁻ intermediate exhausted T cells
biomarkers.finding: Higher frequencies after drug holiday or CD22 blockade

models.name: LCMV chronic infection
models.genotype_or_key_features: PD‑1 blockade short‑term vs long‑term vs drug holiday

clinical_data.value: single peak after initial PD‑1 blockade, similar magnitude after drug holiday re‑treatment
significant tumor growth delay with PD‑1×CD22 bispecific vs anti‑PD‑1 alone

MOA_summary: Continuous PD‑1 blockade chronically enhances TCR signaling in progenitor exhausted T cells, eroding their capacity to generate intermediate effector‑like exhausted cells and leading to functional decline. A drug holiday restores this capacity, allowing a second wave of reinvigoration. Long‑term PD‑1 loss induces up‑regulation of CD22 on progenitor exhausted cells, providing an additional negative feedback; blocking CD22 (or using a PD‑1×CD22 bispecific) restores intermediate exhausted cell expansion and improves viral control and tumor suppression.
key_findings: Short‑term PD‑1 blockade yields a transient T cell reinvigoration peak; continuous blockade does not improve long‑term outcomes compared with short‑term treatment.
A drug holiday (2‑week treatment, 2‑month washout, re‑treatment) produces a second robust reinvigoration, leading to better viral control in chronic LCMV infection.
Continuous PD‑1 blockade depletes the intermediate exhausted T cell population (CD63⁺) while preserving terminally exhausted cells.
Progenitor exhausted T cells exposed to long‑term PD‑1 blockade up‑regulate CD22, a B‑cell inhibitory receptor that dampens TCR signaling.
Anti‑CD22 antibody or PD‑1×CD22 bispecific restores intermediate exhausted cell expansion and improves disease control in both viral and tumor models.
Bispecific therapy outperforms either monotherapy in the AT3‑OVA tumor model, increasing T cell numbers, proliferation, and cytokine production.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Chronic PD‑1 loss leads to adaptive resistance via CD22 up‑regulation on Tpex cells.
Long‑term PD‑1 blockade skews differentiation toward terminal exhaustion, reducing therapeutic efficacy.

combination_strategies.rationale: Counteracts adaptive up‑regulation of CD22 after chronic PD‑1 inhibition
combination_strategies.phenotype: Restores intermediate exhausted T cell pool and improves viral/tumor control

combination_strategies.rationale: Allows progenitor exhausted cells to reset signaling equilibrium before re‑treatment
combination_strategies.phenotype: Second wave of T cell reinvigoration with improved functional outcomes

open_questions: What is the optimal timing and duration of PD‑1 drug holidays in patients?
How prevalent is CD22 expression on human progenitor exhausted T cells across cancer types?
Can CD22 ligands (sialic acids) be modulated to enhance the effect of CD22 blockade?

talk_title: Mechanisms of resistance to direct KRAS inhibitors and combinatorial strategies

biomarkers.name: neoantigen-specific T cell response
biomarkers.finding: Patients with detectable neoantigen-specific T cells had significantly longer relapse‑free survival (p<0.01)

models.name: G12C/G12D mixed KRAS tumor model
models.genotype_or_key_features: Mixture of KRAS G12C and G12D cells labeled with BFP and GFP

MOA_summary: Allele‑specific KRAS G12C covalent inhibitors selectively bind mutant KRAS, sparing wild‑type KRAS and T cells, while checkpoint blockade releases PD‑1 mediated inhibition, together enabling immune recognition of shared neoantigens and leading to durable tumor regressions.
readouts: flow cytometry for neoantigen‑specific T cells
key_findings: KRAS G12C covalent inhibitors achieve ~80% disease control in KRAS G12C NSCLC patients but relapses are common.
In mouse models, a minor resistant G12D subpopulation expands under G12C inhibition, limiting response.
Combining a KRAS G12C inhibitor with anti‑PD‑1 therapy increases complete response rate to ~45% versus 7% with inhibitor alone.
Mice achieving complete responses reject rechallenge with 100% G12D tumors, indicating immune memory.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Intrinsic resistance due to co‑occurring KEAP1, SMARCA4, CDKN2A alterations predicts inferior outcomes.
Acquired cis‑mutations at KRAS residues 95, 96, 68 prevent inhibitor binding.

combination_strategies.rationale: Release T‑cell inhibition to exploit neoantigen release after KRAS‑mediated tumor cell killing
combination_strategies.phenotype: Complete responses in ~45% of mice versus 7% with KRAS inhibitor alone

combination_strategies.rationale: Vertical inhibition of MAPK pathway to prevent pathway re‑activation
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved tumor growth inhibition in KRAS G12C xenografts

open_questions: What is the optimal sequencing of KRAS inhibitor and checkpoint blockade in patients?
Can biomarkers such as early neoantigen‑specific T‑cell expansion predict durable responses?

talk_title: Intrinsic mechanisms of resistance to KRAS inhibitors and rational combination strategies

models.name: KRAS G12D SOS1 inhibitor study
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS G12D xenografts treated with SOS1 inhibitor

models.name: KRAS G12C + MEK inhibitor study
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS G12C tumors treated with adagrasib + MEK inhibitor

MOA_summary: Allele‑specific KRAS G12C inhibitors bind the mutant cysteine pocket, while upstream SOS1 inhibition or downstream MEK inhibition provides vertical blockade of MAPK signaling, overcoming intrinsic resistance mechanisms such as KEAP1 or SMARCA4 co‑alterations.
key_findings: Combination of KRAS G12C inhibitor with SOS1 inhibitor yields superior tumor regression in KRAS G12D mouse models compared with monotherapy.
KRAS G12C plus MEK inhibitor produces the greatest tumor shrinkage among tested combinations.
Preclinical data support ongoing clinical trials testing KRAS inhibitor + SOS1 or MEK inhibitor combinations.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Intrinsic resistance linked to KEAP1, SMARCA4, CDKN2A co‑mutations reduces efficacy of KRAS monotherapy.

combination_strategies.rationale: Block upstream RAS activation to enhance KRAS inhibitor efficacy
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced tumor regression in KRAS G12D xenografts

combination_strategies.rationale: Vertical inhibition of MAPK pathway to prevent feedback re‑activation
combination_strategies.phenotype: Superior tumor shrinkage versus KRAS inhibitor alone

open_questions: Which biomarker panel best predicts benefit from KRAS inhibitor + SOS1 or MEK combinations?
Can adaptive resistance mechanisms be pre‑emptively targeted in early‑line therapy?

talk_title: Hippo‑YAP/TAZ pathway as an adaptive resistance mechanism and therapeutic target
tumor_types: head and neck squamous cell carcinoma

biomarkers.name: RND3 (RhoE) expression
biomarkers.finding: MEK inhibition reduces RND3 leading to rapid YAP activation within 24 h

MOA_summary: Inhibition of MAPK signaling triggers rapid down‑regulation of RND3, relieving suppression of RhoA and activating FAK‑YAP/TAZ signaling, which drives adaptive resistance; combined FAK inhibition and TEAD disruption synergize with KRAS or MEK inhibitors to suppress tumor growth and restore immune infiltration.
readouts: flow cytometry for CD8 T cells
key_findings: MEK inhibition leads to loss of RND3 within 24 h, causing rapid YAP target gene up‑regulation.
FAK inhibitor defactinib combined with a TEAD inhibitor produces tumor regressions in uveal melanoma models where each agent alone is cytostatic.
In mouse models, the combination restores CD8+ T‑cell infiltration and improves survival compared with monotherapy.
Hippo‑YAP activation also induces chemokines (CXCL1, CXCL5, CXCL8) that recruit immunosuppressive myeloid cells.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Adaptive resistance via Hippo‑YAP activation occurs across KRAS, BRAF, and GNAQ‑driven tumors.
FAK inhibition does not markedly affect normal immune cell function at therapeutic doses.

combination_strategies.rationale: Block FAK‑mediated YAP activation that arises after MAPK inhibition
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression in uveal melanoma PDX

combination_strategies.rationale: Directly inhibit YAP/TAZ transcriptional output downstream of FAK
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced tumor shrinkage and increased CD8+ T‑cell infiltration

combination_strategies.rationale: Combine KRAS blockade with Hippo pathway inhibition to prevent adaptive YAP activation
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved tumor control in KRAS G12C mouse models

open_questions: What biomarkers can predict which patients will develop Hippo‑YAP mediated adaptive resistance?
Can TEAD inhibitors be safely combined with checkpoint blockade in humans?

talk_title: HA-based biomaterial models reveal ROS and hyaluronidase driven glioblastoma invasion

speaker.name: Dr. Sanjay Kumar
speaker.affiliation: University of California, Berkeley, Department of Bioengineering

targets.dependency_context: GBM cells interacting with hyaluronic acid

models.name: HA invasion device
models.genotype_or_key_features: millions of GBM cells loaded, core vs invasive rim analysis

MOA_summary: Glioblastoma cells invade hyaluronic acid-rich brain matrix via CD44 engagement; leader cells secrete HYAL2 to locally degrade HA, creating paths for follower cells, and ROS signaling via the transsulfuration pathway supports invasion, which can be limited by targeting this pathway.
key_findings: HA-based 3D invasion device enables separation of invasive rim and core GBM cells for comparative analysis.
Invasive GBM cells show enrichment of ROS and transsulfuration pathway genes, matching patient tumor data.
Leader GBM cells deposit a trail of HYAL2, degrading HA and facilitating faster follower cell invasion.
Inhibition of hyaluronidase activity or genetic targeting of the transsulfuration pathway reduces GBM invasion in vitro.
open_questions: What upstream signals trigger HYAL2 secretion by leader cells?
Can targeting the transsulfuration pathway synergize with standard GBM therapies?

talk_title: Stiffness-driven Twist nuclear translocation via G3BP2 and TYK2 in triple‑negative breast cancer

speaker.name: Dr. Valerie Weaver
speaker.affiliation: Harvard Medical School, Department of Cell Biology

biomarkers.name: E-cadherin
biomarkers.finding: Loss of membrane E-cadherin correlates with Twist nuclear translocation on stiff matrices

biomarkers.name: p‑TYK2 membrane localization
biomarkers.finding: Membrane TYK2 present in soft ECM, diffused cytosolic in stiff ECM

models.name: TNBC patient‑derived xenograft
models.genotype_or_key_features: treated with TYK2 inhibitor in vivo

models.name: human breast cancer tissue microarray
models.genotype_or_key_features: normal vs invasive carcinoma TYK2 localization

clinical_data.population: TNBC organoids on soft matrix with TYK2 inhibitor
PDX‑bearing mice treated with TYK2 inhibitor

MOA_summary: In stiff extracellular matrices, TYK2 dissociates from interferon‑R1 and becomes cytosolic, allowing LIN kinase to phosphorylate Twist1 on a specific tyrosine, releasing Twist from G3BP2 anchoring and driving its nuclear translocation, which triggers EMT and invasion; TYK2 inhibition mimics stiffness loss, promoting invasion even on soft matrices via the same Twist pathway.
key_findings: Stiff ECM induces nuclear translocation of Twist1 without changing its protein level.
G3BP2 binds Twist1 in soft ECM, preventing nuclear entry; stiffness‑dependent phosphorylation by LIN releases Twist.
TYK2 membrane localization is lost on stiff matrices, acting as a metastasis suppressor; TYK2 inhibition drives invasion on soft matrices.
TYK2 deletion or inhibition increases lung dissemination of TNBC PDX without affecting primary tumor size.
Twist deletion abrogates the invasive phenotype induced by TYK2 loss, confirming epistatic relationship.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: TYK2 inhibition unexpectedly promotes invasion, suggesting potential resistance mechanism if used clinically for other indications.

combination_strategies.rationale: Block Twist phosphorylation downstream of stiffness
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced Twist nuclear translocation and invasion on stiff matrices

combination_strategies.rationale: Investigate paradoxical pro‑invasive effect and potential combination with EMT blockers
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic increase in invasion when combined with TGF‑β

open_questions: What upstream mechanosensor triggers TYK2 membrane dissociation on stiff ECM?
Does TYK2 have similar metastasis‑suppressive roles in other epithelial cancers?

talk_title: One Gene at a Time: Metabolic Adaptations Driving Metastatic Growth in Pancreatic Cancer
MOA_summary: The lab identified metastasis‑specific metabolic genes (GSTT1, SLC30A4, MAT2A) that are up‑regulated in PDAC liver and lung metastases and are required and sufficient for metastatic colonization; GSTT1 drives extracellular matrix remodeling via fibronectin glutathionylation, SLC30A4 mediates serine uptake addiction, and MAT2A translocates to the nucleus to produce SAM for chromatin methylation in metastatic cells.
key_findings: GSTT1 is absent in primary PDAC but highly expressed in liver and lung metastases and its knockout abolishes metastatic outgrowth while primary tumor growth is unaffected
GSTT1 overexpression induces ectopic metastases in multiple organs and promotes fibronectin secretion via glutathione conjugation

SLC30A4 is specifically up‑regulated in metastatic lesions, and its loss impairs 3D colony formation and metastatic colonization; overexpression increases serine uptake and rescues growth in serine‑depleted conditions
MAT2A shifts from cytoplasmic to nuclear localization in metastatic cells, supporting nuclear SAM production for histone methylation; nuclear MAT2A is required for metastatic growth
open_questions: What transcription factors drive the coordinated up‑regulation of GSTT1, SLC30A4 and MAT2A in disseminated tumor cells?
Can pharmacologic inhibition of GSTT1 or SLC30A4 antibodies prevent metastatic colonization in vivo?

talk_title: Targeting IMPDH2 and Ferroptosis to Suppress Metastatic Pancreatic Cancer

speaker.name: Dr. Pankaj Singh
speaker.affiliation: University of Oklahoma Health Sciences Center

models.name: PDAC metastasis mouse splenic injection model

MOA_summary: IMPDH2 is overexpressed in PDAC metastases and drives purine biosynthesis; chronic mycophenolic acid (MPA) exposure induces resistance via up‑regulation of glutathione metabolism (SLC7A11, GPX4); co‑targeting ferroptosis (RSL3) or cystine uptake (sulfasalazine) restores MPA sensitivity and markedly reduces metastatic burden in mouse models.
key_findings: IMPDH2 expression is high in primary and metastatic PDAC and correlates with poor patient survival
Long‑term MPA exposure selects for PDAC cells with increased glutathione metabolism (up‑regulation of SLC7A11, GPX4) and a 20‑fold increase in IC50
Sulfasalazine sensitizes MPA‑resistant cells to MPA in vitro and modestly reduces tumor burden in vivo
Combination of MPA with the ferroptosis inducer RSL3 enhances cell killing and decreases liver metastasis size in mouse splenic injection models
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Acquired MPA resistance is mediated by up‑regulation of cystine‑glutathione axis, conferring cross‑resistance to oxidative stress

combination_strategies.rationale: Inhibit cystine uptake to deplete glutathione and overcome MPA resistance
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced sensitivity of resistant PDAC cells to MPA; modest tumor reduction in vivo

combination_strategies.rationale: Induce ferroptosis by inhibiting GPX4, synergizing with purine synthesis inhibition
combination_strategies.phenotype: greater cell death across MPA‑sensitive and resistant lines; reduced metastatic nodules in mice

open_questions: Will combining MPA with KRAS inhibitors improve efficacy in metastatic PDAC?
Can dietary serine or methionine restriction augment the anti‑metastatic effect of IMPDH2 inhibition?

talk_title: NUDT5‑PPAT Interaction Controls Purine Biosynthesis Switch and Thiopurine Sensitivity

clinical_data.comparator: NUDT5 KO or PPAT‑binding mutant xenografts

MOA_summary: NUDT5 binds PPAT via its R70 residue, promoting PPAT tetramerization and suppressing de novo purine synthesis; loss of NUDT5 or disruption of the NUDT5‑PPAT interaction maintains PPAT in an active dimeric state, rendering cells resistant to thiopurines and de novo pathway inhibitors.
key_findings: NUDT5 deficiency confers resistance to 6‑thioguanine and to de novo pathway inhibitors (methotrexate, leflunomide)
NUDT5 directly interacts with PPAT; the R70A mutation abolishes binding without affecting catalytic activity
Wild‑type NUDT5 (or catalytic dead) induces PPAT tetramerization and reduces its enzymatic activity by ~80% in vitro
In xenografts, tumors expressing NUDT5‑binding‑defective PPAT remain resistant to 6‑TG, whereas WT NUDT5 restores drug sensitivity
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: NUDT5‑mediated PPAT tetramerization provides a novel mechanism of metabolic drug resistance
open_questions: What cellular signal triggers NUDT5‑PPAT binding during purine abundance?
Does NUDT5 regulate purinosome dynamics in vivo and can this be therapeutically exploited?

talk_title: Targetable Metabolite Increases Neuronal Hyperexcitability in Glioma Microenvironment

speaker.name: Khalil Abdullah
speaker.affiliation: University of Pittsburgh Medical Center, Translational Neuro-Oncology

biomarkers.name: GAA (guanosine acetate)
biomarkers.finding: Elevated in high‑grade glioma vs low‑grade and normal brain with high true positive rates

biomarkers.name: c‑Fos activation
biomarkers.finding: Reduced peritumoral c‑Fos in AGAT knockout mice

MOA_summary: Elevated guanosine acetate (GAA) accumulates in glioma due to AGAT/GAMT imbalance, acts on GABA_A receptors in tumor‑infiltrated brain where KCC2 is downregulated, increasing neuronal hyperexcitability and tumor growth; AGAT knockout reduces GAA secretion and prolongs survival without affecting in‑vitro cell fitness.
key_findings: GAA is the most overabundant metabolite in high‑grade glioma compared to normal brain (563 metabolites screened).
AGAT expression correlates with glioma grade; AGAT knockout mice show increased survival despite unchanged tumor growth in flank.
GAA induces neuronal hyperexcitability via GABA_A receptors; effect is blocked by gabazine and KCC2 agonist CLP257.
Dietary ornithine supplementation with arginine restriction lowers tumor GAA and extends survival, mimicking AGAT knockout.
A surgical window trial will enroll 12 patients to test ornithine supplementation and arginine‑restricted diet pre‑operatively.

combination_strategies.rationale: Mimics metabolic effect of AGAT knockout, reducing GAA production
combination_strategies.phenotype: Survival benefit comparable to genetic AGAT knockout in mice

open_questions: Will dietary modulation of GAA translate to clinical benefit in glioma patients?
What is the impact of GAA reduction on seizure frequency and overall neurologic outcomes?

talk_title: Targeting Mitochondrial Complex I Overcomes Adaptive Response to Chemotherapy in Advanced Pancreatic Cancer

speaker.name: Sergio Attanasio
speaker.affiliation: MD Anderson Cancer Center, Viale Lab

targets.dependency_context: PDAC cells with chemotherapy‑induced OXPHOS upregulation

biomarkers.name: DRP1 phosphorylation
biomarkers.finding: Increased phospho‑DRP1 after gemcitabine, blocked by IM‑156

biomarkers.name: Oxidative stress markers
biomarkers.finding: Elevated in gemcitabine+IM‑156 combination; partially rescued by tocopherol

models.name: PDAC clones (sensitive vs resistant)
models.genotype_or_key_features: Barcoded clones with gemcitabine resistance phenotype

models.name: Phase 1/2 MD Anderson trial of Lixumustat + gemcitabine/nab‑paclitaxel

MOA_summary: Chemotherapy induces mitochondrial fission and OXPHOS up‑regulation in PDAC clones; IM‑156, a potent Complex I inhibitor, blocks this adaptive respiration, increases oxidative stress, impairs clonogenic regrowth, and eradicates relapse‑associated clones when combined with gemcitabine/nab‑paclitaxel.
key_findings: Clonal barcoding identified ~12% of PDAC clones driving gemcitabine relapse.
Chemotherapy‑responsive clones upregulate OXPHOS and mitochondrial fission (phospho‑DRP1).
IM‑156 more potent than metformin/phenformin, inhibits OCR at 10 µM without lactic acidosis.
Gemcitabine + IM‑156 prolongs regrowth delay, increases apoptosis, and reduces relapse‑clone frequency in mice.
Phase 1/2 trial (n=18) showed 50% partial responses, 80% disease control, median PFS 6 mo, OS 18 mo, with acceptable safety at 400 mg PO daily.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Potential resistance may emerge via alternative ETC bypass or metabolic rewiring; ongoing studies to combine with KRAS inhibitors.

combination_strategies.rationale: Chemotherapy induces OXPHOS dependence; Complex I inhibition blocks adaptive respiration
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved tumor control and survival in PDAC models

open_questions: What mechanisms underlie eventual resistance to Complex I inhibition after prolonged treatment?
Can biomarkers (e.g., phospho‑DRP1) predict patients who will benefit from IM‑156?

talk_title: Estrogen Therapy Alleviates Cancer‑Associated Cachexia in Mouse Models

targets.dependency_context: male cachectic mice bearing KPL or B16 tumors

biomarkers.name: Body weight / fat mass
biomarkers.finding: E2 treatment maintained body weight and fat in cachectic male mice

biomarkers.name: Muscle mass
biomarkers.finding: Preserved in E2‑treated male mice compared to vehicle

biomarkers.name: Serum catabolic metabolites
biomarkers.finding: E2 normalized elevated circulating fatty acids and amino‑acid catabolites in cachectic males

MOA_summary: LKB1 loss drives HIF‑2α‑mediated cytokine secretion and severe cachexia; HIF‑2α inhibition (rezotibib) rescues fat and muscle but worsens anemia, which can be corrected with EPO. Estrogen (E2) restores mitochondrial TCA activity, suppresses glycolysis, and reverses systemic catabolism, preserving body composition in male cachectic mice.
key_findings: KPL mice develop rapid weight loss, fat and muscle wasting, and anemia despite tumor size similar to KP controls.
HIF‑2α inhibition with rezotibib restores body weight, fat, and muscle but induces anemia due to reduced EPO.
E2 pellet treatment in cachectic male mice maintains body weight, fat, and muscle, normalizing serum catabolic metabolites.
Estrogen reprograms peripheral tissues: upregulates OXPHOS/TCA, downregulates proteolysis pathways in muscle and adipose.
Ovariectomy abolishes female protection from cachexia, indicating ovarian hormone dependence.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: HIF‑2α inhibition alone fails to address anemia, requiring adjunct EPO therapy.

combination_strategies.rationale: Counteract anemia caused by HIF‑2α blockade
combination_strategies.phenotype: Restores hemoglobin while preserving cachexia rescue

combination_strategies.rationale: Broadly reverses metabolic catabolism independent of HIF‑2α
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved body composition and serum metabolite profile

open_questions: Does estrogen therapy benefit cachexia in human cancer patients, and how does hormone receptor status affect response?
Can combined HIF‑2α inhibition and estrogen therapy provide additive benefits without anemia?

talk_title: Ketogenic Diet Sensitizes Pancreatic Cancer to Metabolic Therapies

speaker.name: Omid Hajihassani
speaker.affiliation: Case Western Reserve University, Surgery Fellow

biomarkers.name: Tumor glutamine levels
biomarkers.finding: ≈4‑fold increase in tumor glutamine under ketogenic diet

biomarkers.name: Serum glutamine
biomarkers.finding: Elevated with ketogenic diet; liver‑derived

MOA_summary: Ketogenic diet forces glycolytic PDAC cells to rely on TCA cycle and induces hepatic glutamine overproduction; tumors upregulate glutamine transporters and enzymes, becoming glutamine‑addicted; concurrent inhibition of glutamine metabolism (CB‑839 or DON) synergizes with the diet to suppress tumor growth.
readouts: stable_isotope_tracing (U‑13C glucose, ketone bodies, capric acid)
key_findings: Ketogenic diet slows tumor growth and extends mouse survival but does not eradicate tumors.
Tumors on ketogenic diet show increased TCA cycle metabolites and a 4‑fold rise in intratumoral glutamine.
Isotope tracing shows tumor does not synthesize glutamine de novo; liver produces excess glutamine under low‑protein ketogenic diet.
Glutamine transporter and utilization enzymes are upregulated in ketogenic‑diet tumors.
Combination of ketogenic diet with glutaminase inhibitor CB‑839 or DON yields greater tumor suppression than either alone.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Ketogenic diet alone leads to metabolic adaptation; tumors become glutamine‑dependent, suggesting a potential resistance pathway if glutamine is not co‑targeted.

combination_strategies.rationale: Block glutamine utilization that becomes essential under ketogenic diet
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced anti‑tumor efficacy in PDAC mice

combination_strategies.rationale: Inhibit broader glutamine‑dependent pathways
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor growth inhibition with ketogenic diet

open_questions: How does ketogenic diet affect immune cell function within the PDAC microenvironment?
Can patient stratification biomarkers (e.g., baseline glutamine transporter expression) predict response to diet‑plus‑glutamine inhibition?

cited_papers: Vander Heiden et al. 2022 Nat Metab

talk_title: Metabolic Changes Associated with Senescence in CAFs Regulate Fibrosis in Pancreatic Cancer

speaker.name: Devashi Sen
speaker.affiliation: Washington University School of Medicine, DeNardo Lab

biomarkers.name: P16 (CDKN2A)
biomarkers.finding: Marks senescent myofibroblasts in PDAC tumors

biomarkers.name: Alpha‑ketoglutarate (αKG)
biomarkers.finding: Accumulated in senescent CAFs, supporting prolyl/lysyl hydroxylase activity

models.name: pancreatic fibroblasts induced senescent by bleomycin

MOA_summary: Senescent myofibroblasts upregulate mitochondrial αKG production via IDH2/3, providing substrate for collagen prolyl/lysyl hydroxylases, leading to thicker collagen fibers and enhanced fibrosis in PDAC; targeting IDH2/3 may blunt this pro‑fibrotic program.
key_findings: Senescent CAFs (P16+ α‑SMA+) increase tumor collagen deposition and fiber thickness.
Senescent CAFs display higher mitochondrial content but lower per‑mitochondrion membrane potential.
Metabolomics shows accumulation of glycolytic intermediates and early TCA metabolites (citrate, αKG) with reduced downstream TCA metabolites.
IDH2 and IDH3 expression is upregulated in senescent CAFs, suggesting enhanced αKG production for collagen hydroxylation.
Depletion of senescent cells (Ink‑Tag model) reduces collagen abundance in PDAC tumors.

combination_strategies.rationale: Block αKG supply for collagen hydroxylation in senescent CAFs
combination_strategies.phenotype: Predicted reduction in fibrosis

open_questions: Will inhibition of IDH2/3 in senescent CAFs affect tumor growth or immune infiltration?
Are there systemic effects of targeting αKG production in fibroblasts?

talk_title: Targeting Metabolism in BRCA‑Deficient Tumors via PKM2 Activation

biomarkers.name: PKM1/PKM2 isoform ratio
biomarkers.finding: BRCA2 loss shifts expression toward PKM1 over PKM2

biomarkers.name: γH2AX
biomarkers.finding: Increased after PKM2 activation in BRCA2‑KO cells

MOA_summary: BRCA2 loss induces PKM1 overexpression, creating a high pyruvate kinase state; pharmacologic activation of PKM2 (mitapivat) further drives pyruvate flux, causing mitochondrial oxidative stress, impaired mitophagy, and selective death of BRCA‑deficient cells.
key_findings: BRCA2 loss upregulates PKM1 (constitutively active) while PKM2 remains unchanged.
PKM2 activator (dasatinib or mitapivat) selectively kills BRCA2‑KO cells and increases γH2AX.
Overexpression of PKM1 in WT cells sensitizes them to PKM2 activation, confirming PKM1’s role.
In vivo, mitapivat (400 mg PO daily) delays growth of BRCA2‑KO tumors without weight loss or organ toxicity.
PKM activation reduces glucose oxidation in TCA cycle, downregulates ETC complexes, and impairs PINK1‑mediated mitophagy, leading to mitochondrial superoxide accumulation.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: BRCA‑deficient cells may develop resistance via alternative ATP‑generating pathways; combination with PARP inhibitors shows additive effects.

combination_strategies.rationale: Potential additive cytotoxicity in BRCA‑deficient cells
combination_strategies.phenotype: Additive in vitro; in vivo studies planned

open_questions: What transcriptional or splicing mechanisms drive PKM1 upregulation after BRCA loss?
Can PKM1/PKM2 isoform ratio serve as a biomarker to select patients for PKM activation therapy?

talk_title: Stromal microenvironment signatures predict bone metastasis in prostate cancer

speaker.name: Massimo Loda
speaker.affiliation: Weill Cornell Medical Center, Department of Pathology, New York, USA

biomarkers.name: periostin expression
biomarkers.finding: higher periostin in stromal fibroblasts correlates with neuroendocrine differentiation and bone metastasis

models.name: TMPRSS2-ERG, PTEN knockout, MYC high, N-Myc overexpression prostate models
models.genotype_or_key_features: various oncogenic alterations driving progressive aggressiveness

MOA_summary: Loda's group identified a bone‑like stromal gene signature in primary prostate tumors, driven by periostin‑rich cancer‑associated fibroblasts that arise via epithelial‑to‑stromal signaling (loss of AR motifs, gain of AP1 motifs) and promote neuroendocrine differentiation and bone organotropism.
key_findings: A stromal osteomimicry signature, including periostin, distinguishes high‑grade prostate tumors that metastasize to bone.
Periostin is enriched in neuroendocrine prostate cancer stroma and colocalizes with αVβ3/αVβ5 integrin signaling.
Mouse models recapitulate human stromal changes; fibroblast clusters acquire AP1 motifs and lose AR/GR motifs.
Co‑culture of periostin‑expressing fibroblasts or recombinant periostin induces neuroendocrine differentiation (FOXA2) in organoids.
Spatial analysis shows reduced distance between periostin‑positive fibroblasts and neuroendocrine cells in bone metastases versus primary tumors.
open_questions: Can periostin inhibition prevent neuroendocrine transdifferentiation and bone metastasis in vivo?
What are the distinct ECM compositions produced by periostin‑high versus periostin‑low fibroblast clusters?

talk_title: Metabolic dependencies drive organ‑specific metastasis in breast cancer

speaker.name: George Poulogiannis
speaker.affiliation: Institute of Cancer Research, Division of Cell and Molecular Biology, London, UK

biomarkers.name: glutamine uptake
biomarkers.finding: bone‑metastatic cells show higher glutamine uptake and increased M+4 labeling of TCA intermediates

models.name: TRAP neuroendocrine prostate model (used for stromal comparison)

MOA_summary: Poulogiannis demonstrated that organ‑specific metabolic environments create unique dependencies: brain metastases rely on de novo fatty‑acid synthesis (FASN), lung metastases on pyruvate carboxylase, and bone metastases on glutaminolysis (GLS1), making these enzymes tractable therapeutic targets.
key_findings: Metabolic gene signatures predict overall metastatic potential with 86% accuracy; metabolic‑only features improve prediction.
Organ‑specific dependencies identified: FASN in brain, PC in lung, GLS1 in bone metastases.
Bone‑metastatic clones exhibit heightened glutamine uptake, increased GLS1 activity, and sensitivity to GLS1 inhibitor CBA39.
Depleting serine/glycine reduces metastasis in bone, kidney, ovary but not in brain, liver, lung.
Integrated MetaboForm platform infers tumor metabolomes from transcriptomics, revealing distinct metabolic landscapes across metastatic sites.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: GLS1 inhibition shows specificity for bone‑derived clones; transporter inhibition (SLC1A5, SLC38A12) ineffective due to compensatory uptake.

combination_strategies.rationale: lung metastatic cells depend on serine‑derived α‑KG to sustain mTOR signaling
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced sensitivity of lung clones to rapamycin when pyruvate carboxylase is inhibited

open_questions: Can metabolic dependencies be exploited clinically to prevent organ‑specific colonization?
What determines the heterogeneity of metabolic phenotypes within a single patient’s metastatic lesions?

talk_title: CTC heterogeneity and TMPO as a driver of metastasis in prostate cancer

speaker.name: Cory Barret Shen
speaker.affiliation: Columbia University, Department of Medicine and Molecular Pharmacology and Systems Biology, New York, USA

biomarkers.name: TMPO expression
biomarkers.finding: high TMPO in castration‑resistant prostate CTCs correlates with increased CTC count and bone metastasis

models.name: Prostate cancer GEM models with lineage‑traceable fluorescent markers
CTC‑derived organoids (iOrganoids)
models.genotype_or_key_features: derived from single circulating tumor cells, retain primary tumor phenotype

MOA_summary: Shen showed that TMPO (LAP2) sustains a hypoxia‑driven transcriptional program in large CTC‑derived organoids, promoting survival in circulation and bone colonization; TMPO knockdown shifts cells toward oxidative phosphorylation, reduces organoid size, CTC numbers, and bone metastasis without affecting primary tumor growth.
key_findings: CTC burden correlates tightly (R>0.9) with metastatic score in GEM mice.
Single CTCs can be cultured into organoids; organoid size segregates into hypoxic (large) vs. ox‑phos (small) phenotypes.
TMPO knockdown does not affect primary tumor growth but reduces circulating CTCs and bone metastasis in both subcutaneous and intracardiac models.
TMPO activity correlates with hypoxia signatures and organoid size; loss of TMPO shifts metabolism toward oxidative phosphorylation.
Human CRPC CTCs show high nuclear TMPO co‑expressed with lamin, supporting clinical relevance.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: TMPO inhibition selectively impairs hypoxia‑adapted CTCs while sparing ox‑phos clones.

combination_strategies.rationale: bone‑metastatic CTCs rely on glutamine; TMPO loss reduces hypoxia‑driven glutamine dependence
combination_strategies.phenotype: additive reduction of bone metastasis in mouse models

open_questions: Does TMPO regulate dormancy versus active dissemination in CTCs?
What upstream signals control TMPO expression in circulating cells?

talk_title: Colibactin-producing pks+ E. coli adhesion drives CRC in mouse models

speaker.name: Lars Verbeke
speaker.affiliation: Ghent University and VIB, Flemish Institute for Biotechnology

targets.dependency_context: pks+ E. coli colonizing intestinal epithelium

biomarkers.name: γ-H2AX
biomarkers.finding: Increased γ-H2AX-positive cells in colon tissue of mice colonized with wild‑type 11G5 but not with clbQ‑deficient strain

models.name: Zap2 (ZEB2) intestinal epithelial transgenic
models.genotype_or_key_features: ZEB2 overexpression in IECs, germ‑free or colonized with 11G5 or Nissle 1917

MOA_summary: pks+ E. coli adheres to intestinal epithelium via high‑affinity FimH/FimLH adhesins, positioning the unstable colibactin toxin in close proximity to host DNA where it cross‑links AT‑rich sequences, inducing a SBS88/ID18 mutational signature and driving colorectal tumorigenesis; pharmacologic blockade of FimH (Sibofimlock) reduces bacterial adhesion, colibactin‑induced DNA damage, and tumor development in germ‑free‑derived Zap2 mice.
key_findings: Only mice colonized with the CRC isolate 11G5 (pks+, clbQ+) showed severe CRC phenotypes, whereas Nissle 1917 did not despite both being pks+.
Deletion of clbQ abolished the tumor‑promoting effect of 11G5, confirming colibactin dependence.
FimH or FimLH loss in 11G5 eliminated epithelial binding, γ‑H2AX induction, and CRC exacerbation.
High‑affinity 11G5 FimH variants restored binding and genotoxicity when expressed in Nissle, demonstrating variant‑specific adhesion strength.
Sibofimlock, a small‑molecule FimH inhibitor, dose‑dependently reduced bacterial adhesion and colibactin‑driven tumor development in vivo.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: E. coli Nissle 1917, although pks+, fails to promote CRC due to poor epithelial adhesion, highlighting adhesion as a selectivity determinant.

combination_strategies.rationale: Inhibit bacterial adhesion to prevent colibactin delivery to host DNA
combination_strategies.phenotype: Dose‑dependent reduction in epithelial binding and CRC development

open_questions: What cellular receptor or transport mechanism mediates colibactin entry into the nucleus after epithelial adhesion?
Do other Enterobacteriaceae employ similar adhesin variants to modulate colibactin toxicity?

talk_title: Sulfur amino‑acid diet, Mucispirillum schaedleri and NKT‑XCL1‑CDC1 axis in colorectal cancer

speaker.name: Wendy Garrett
speaker.affiliation: Harvard Chan School of Public Health

targets.dependency_context: High sulfur amino‑acid diet in mice
NKT cell production in response to M. schaedleri phospholipids
Activated by XCL1 in high‑diet mice

biomarkers.name: CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration
biomarkers.finding: Increased CD8⁺ T cells and granzyme B in tumors of mice on high sulfur amino‑acid diet

biomarkers.name: XCL1 levels
biomarkers.finding: Elevated XCL1 produced by NKT cells; neutralization abolishes diet‑induced anti‑tumor effect

models.name: Cdx2‑Cre;Apc^fl/fl (ApcMin) intestinal tumor model
models.genotype_or_key_features: Conditional Apc loss in colon epithelium; used with high vs low sulfur amino‑acid diets

models.name: MC38 flank tumor model
models.genotype_or_key_features: Mismatch‑repair‑deficient colon carcinoma; used to test diet effects

models.name: Apc/Kras/Trp53 organoids (orthotopic injection)
models.genotype_or_key_features: Orthotopic colon tumor model for diet studies

models.name: Peripheral blood mononuclear cells
models.genotype_or_key_features: NKT cell activation by M. schaedleri conditioned media

MOA_summary: A high sulfur‑amino‑acid diet thickens the colonic inner mucus layer, creating a niche for Mucispirillum schaedleri; this bacterium releases phospholipids that activate NKT cells to secrete XCL1, which in turn recruits and activates Batf3‑dependent CDC1 dendritic cells, enhancing CD8⁺ T‑cell cytotoxicity and reducing adenocarcinoma burden in Apc‑driven mouse models.
readouts: flow cytometry for CD8⁺ T cells and granzyme B
key_findings: High sulfur amino‑acid diet increased inner mucus thickness without increasing goblet cell numbers.
M. schaedleri bloomed in high‑diet mice across three tumor models (MC38, ApcMin, orthotopic organoids).
Diet‑induced tumor protection was lost in Batf3‑KO mice lacking CDC1 dendritic cells.
Neutralizing XCL1 abolished the anti‑tumor benefit of the high diet, implicating NKT‑derived XCL1 as essential.
Conditioned media from M. schaedleri stimulated NKT cells to produce XCL1 in an MR1‑independent manner.
open_questions: What are the specific phospholipid species from M. schaedleri that trigger NKT cell activation?
Can dietary modulation of sulfur amino acids be safely translated to human CRC prevention or therapy?

talk_title: Intratumoral Fusobacterium nucleatum drives epithelial quiescence and therapy resistance

speaker.name: Susan Bowman
speaker.affiliation: University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center

targets.dependency_context: Enriched in CRC tumors, especially clade 2 (F. animalis)
Up‑regulated in epithelial cells upon Fusobacterium exposure

biomarkers.name: Ki‑67
biomarkers.finding: Reduced Ki‑67 staining in high‑Fusobacterium density tumor regions

biomarkers.name: Cell‑cycle phase (G0/G1 arrest)
biomarkers.finding: Dose‑dependent increase in G0/G1 fraction of CRC spheroids at MOI ≥10

biomarkers.name: 5‑FU pathological response
biomarkers.finding: Patients with high Fusobacterium burden showed lower major pathological response to neoadjuvant 5‑FU

models.name: CRC resection specimens
models.genotype_or_key_features: Spatial mapping of Fusobacterium by RNA‑scope and 10x Visium

models.name: CRC cancer cell spheroids
models.genotype_or_key_features: Co‑culture with clinical Fusobacterium isolate; neutrophil recruitment assays

models.name: Fusobacterium‑colonized ApcMin mice
models.genotype_or_key_features: Oral gavage of F. nucleatum; tumor multiplicity assessment

models.name: InvadeSeq / COSMIC spatial transcriptomics
models.genotype_or_key_features: Single‑cell resolution of bacterial‑infected vs uninfected niches

MOA_summary: Intratumoral Fusobacterium nucleatum localizes predominantly extracellularly, where its phospholipid components trigger epithelial MAPK activation and CXCL8 secretion, leading to a dose‑dependent G0/G1 cell‑cycle arrest (quiescence) without increasing proliferation; this quiescent state reduces 5‑FU efficacy and creates an immunosuppressive microenvironment characterized by myeloid infiltration and checkpoint up‑regulation.
readouts: tumor weight and number in mice
key_findings: Fusobacterium signals are mainly extracellular; bacterial load correlates with increased inter‑cellular spacing in tumor tissue.
High bacterial density regions show reduced Ki‑67 and a shift toward G0/G1 arrest in cancer cells (MOI ≥10).
In vitro, Fusobacterium exposure induces MAPK (p‑p38, FOSL) activation and CXCL8 secretion in a dose‑dependent manner.
Patients with high intratumoral Fusobacterium have lower major pathological response to neoadjuvant 5‑FU therapy.
Clade 2 (F. animalis sensu stricto) dominates CRC tumors and possesses acid‑resistance (GAD) and ethanolamine utilization genes, suggesting selective fitness.
Spatial profiling reveals Fusobacterium‑rich niches enriched for myeloid cells (ARG1, CD66b) and immune checkpoints (PD‑1, CTLA‑4, LAG‑3).
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Quiescent G0/G1 state confers resistance to S‑phase‑targeting chemotherapies such as 5‑FU.
Extracellular bacterial load disrupts epithelial cell‑cell adhesion (loss of E‑cadherin, EpCAM) creating a protected niche.

combination_strategies.rationale: Antibiotic targeting anaerobic Fusobacterium to reduce bacterial burden and restore chemotherapy sensitivity
combination_strategies.phenotype: Ongoing clinical trial (MD Anderson) evaluating metronidazole plus standard neoadjuvant therapy

open_questions: What specific bacterial metabolites mediate MAPK activation and cell‑cycle arrest?
Does intracellular versus extracellular Fusobacterium have distinct effects on tumor immunity?
Can targeting the quiescent niche reverse 5‑FU resistance in patients?

talk_title: Neuroinflammatory memory drives gastric tumorigenesis via vagal sensory neurons and ILC2

targets.dependency_context: IL-13 signaling in vagal sensory neurons

models.name: high-dose tamoxifen gastric injury model
models.genotype_or_key_features: wild-type C57BL/6, gastric parietal cell loss

MOA_summary: Vagal sensory neurons retain an inflammatory memory after gastric injury or H. pylori eradication, mediated by IL-13 signaling that epigenetically programs neurons to release CGRP, which together with ILC2-derived IL-5 amplifies epithelial metaplasia and promotes orthotopic gastric tumor progression.
key_findings: Prior gastric injury or H. pylori eradication creates a lasting inflammatory memory in vagal sensory neurons lasting >6 months.
Memory-encoded neurons require IL-13 signaling; IL-13 receptor knockout abolishes the memory phenotype.
ILC2-derived IL-5 induces CGRP release from sensory neurons, enhancing epithelial progenitor activation.
Selective vagal denervation or ablation of memory-encoded neurons eliminates accelerated regeneration and tumor promotion.
Transplantation of memory-encoded neurons into Trpv1-deficient recipients restores the tumor-promoting memory.
open_questions: Can pharmacologic IL-13 blockade prevent neuronal inflammatory memory and reduce gastric cancer risk?
What are the epigenetic modifiers responsible for IL-13 imprinting in sensory neurons?

talk_title: ILC2-driven T cell exhaustion in MASLD-associated hepatocellular carcinoma

targets.dependency_context: CXCL9 produced by LSEC recruits CXCR3+ T cells
Granzyme K expression in exhausted CD8 T cells drives IL-1β release

biomarkers.name: CXCL9
biomarkers.finding: CXCL9 elevated in serum and LSEC of HFD mice; CXCR3 blockade reduced tumor incidence

biomarkers.name: Granzyme K
biomarkers.finding: Granzyme K high in TPEX/TEc exhausted CD8 T cells in steatotic liver

models.name: neonatal DMBA + high-fat diet HCC model
MASLD patient plasma and liver samples

MOA_summary: High‑fat diet induces gut gram‑positive bacterial metabolites that activate hepatic ILC2 via TLR2, leading to CXCL9 secretion by LSEC and recruitment of CXCR3+ CD8 T cells that become exhausted through granzyme K‑mediated IL‑1β release, driving MASLD‑associated HCC progression.
key_findings: HFD increases gram‑positive bacteria‑derived deoxycholic acid and LTA, activating hepatic ILC2 via TLR2.
ILC2 produce IL‑5 and IL‑13, promoting CXCL9 expression in LSEC, which recruits CXCR3+ T cells.
CXCR3 blockade or CXCL9 knockout markedly reduces HCC development in HFD mice.
Exhausted CD8 T cells in steatotic liver express high Granzyme K, which drives IL‑1β release and tumor promotion.
Granzyme K knockout mice show reduced HCC incidence; overexpression accelerates tumor growth.

combination_strategies.rationale: Block CXCL9‑CXCR3 axis to prevent T cell recruitment and exhaustion
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced liver tumor incidence in HFD mice

open_questions: Can targeting Granzyme K therapeutically reverse T cell exhaustion in MASLD‑HCC?
What is the relative contribution of IL‑5 versus IL‑13 in ILC2‑driven tumor promotion?

talk_title: Stress‑induced remodeling of enteric glia promotes colorectal tumor initiation

targets.dependency_context: Glucocorticoid activation in enteric glia under restraint stress

models.name: pre‑tumor organoids from ApcMin mice
models.genotype_or_key_features: Apc mutation, treated with stressed glia conditioned medium

MOA_summary: Chronic restraint stress elevates glucocorticoids, activating enteric glial cells that secrete factors promoting ROS, DNA damage and hyperproliferation of intestinal epithelial stem cells, thereby accelerating tumor initiation in ApcMin mice; glial‑derived conditioned medium upregulates tumorigenic genes in organoids.
key_findings: Physical restraint stress raises corticosterone levels in mice.
Stressed enteric glia increase ROS and γH2AX DNA damage in gut epithelium.
Glial depletion (Plp1‑CreERT2/DTR) markedly reduces stress‑induced tumor burden in ApcMin mice.
Conditioned medium from glucocorticoid‑treated glia enhances outgrowth and tumorigenic gene expression in pre‑tumor organoids.
Stress‑induced glial activation correlates with early‑stage glial infiltration in mouse polyps, which diminishes in advanced tumors.
open_questions: Which specific glial‑derived factors mediate ROS and DNA damage in epithelial cells?
Do distinct enteric glial subtypes differentially respond to stress?

talk_title: Chronic sleep deprivation reshapes microbiota and impairs 5‑FU efficacy in colorectal cancer

biomarkers.name: BMAL1
biomarkers.finding: BMAL1 expression decreased in tumors of sleep‑deprived mice

biomarkers.name: IL‑6
biomarkers.finding: IL‑6 increased in SD tumors despite 5‑FU treatment

models.name: chronic sleep deprivation + MC38 colorectal tumor model
FMT recipient model
models.genotype_or_key_features: antibiotic‑treated C57BL/6 male receiving SD or control stool

MOA_summary: Repeated nocturnal bar disturbance induces circadian disruption (↓BMAL1, ↑Cry1/2) and dysbiosis, leading to reduced intratumoral CD4⁺/CD8⁺ T cells, lower IFN‑γ, and impaired 5‑FU response; fecal microbiota from sleep‑deprived donors recapitulates these effects in recipient mice.
key_findings: Sleep‑deprived mice develop larger MC38 tumors and higher tumor weight than controls.
Tumors from SD mice show decreased BMAL1 and increased Cry1/2 expression.
SD reduces intratumoral CD4⁺, CD8⁺, IFN‑γ⁺ T cells and NK cell granzyme A frequency.
5‑FU efficacy is blunted in SD mice, with higher tumor growth despite chemotherapy.
FMT from SD donors transfers dysbiosis and chemoresistance to antibiotic‑treated recipients.

combination_strategies.rationale: Assess whether sleep‑deprivation‑induced microbiota impairs standard chemotherapy
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced tumor control and T cell infiltration in SD mice

open_questions: Does stress hormone elevation (cortisol) contribute independently of circadian disruption?
Can probiotic or circadian‑restoring interventions rescue 5‑FU sensitivity?

talk_title: Obesity‑induced pre‑adipocyte IGF‑1 signaling drives ovarian cancer metastasis to omentum

targets.dependency_context: IGF‑1 secreted by pre‑adipocytes enhances ovarian cancer AKT and NF‑κB activation

models.name: obesity‑induced ovarian cancer metastasis model
models.genotype_or_key_features: high‑fat diet‑induced obesity, ovarian cancer cell injection

models.name: pre‑adipocyte‑cancer co‑culture organoids
models.genotype_or_key_features: GFP‑labeled ovarian cancer cells with normal or high‑BMI pre‑adipocytes

MOA_summary: Pre‑adipocytes from high‑BMI donors secrete higher IGF‑1 and reduced IGFBPs, activating AKT and NF‑κB (p65) in ovarian cancer cells, promoting SOX2‑driven tumor initiation and fibronectin‑mediated extracellular matrix remodeling, thereby enhancing omental colonization and decreasing survival.
key_findings: Co‑injection of high‑BMI pre‑adipocytes accelerates early tumor growth and reduces mouse survival compared to normal‑BMI pre‑adipocytes.
Both normal and high‑BMI pre‑adipocytes increase IGF‑1 secretion; high‑BMI cells have lower IGFBP levels, increasing bioavailable IGF‑1.
IGF‑1 stimulation activates AKT and NF‑κB (p65) in ovarian cancer cells under both nutrient‑replete and -deprived conditions.
High‑BMI pre‑adipocyte co‑culture upregulates ECM genes (fibronectin) and SOX2 expression in cancer cells.
Obese patient omental tissue shows elevated IGF‑1 and activated IGF‑1R signaling.

combination_strategies.rationale: Block obesity‑driven IGF‑1 signaling to reduce metastatic colonization
combination_strategies.phenotype: Predicted reduction in AKT/NF‑κB activation and tumor growth

open_questions: Does targeting IGFBPs restore sensitivity to IGF‑1 blockade in high‑BMI pre‑adipocyte contexts?
What is the contribution of other adipokines (e.g., IL‑6, MCP‑1) to the observed phenotype?

talk_title: Hybrid EMT tumor cells and TIMO monocytes drive immunosuppression in head and neck cancer
tumor_types: head and neck squamous cell carcinoma

biomarkers.name: ITGB4
biomarkers.finding: ITGB4 high in hybrid EMT tumor cells; correlates with proximity to TIMO monocytes

models.name: patient‑derived tumor organoids co‑cultured with PBMC monocytes

MOA_summary: A tumor‑enriched monocyte subset (TIMO) co‑localizes with hybrid EMT cancer cells; reciprocal signaling enhances EMT gene expression and monocyte immunosuppressive programs, leading to reduced T cell activation and resistance to cisplatin and ICB.
key_findings: TIMO monocytes are enriched in tumors and associate with poor survival across HNSCC cohorts.
Hybrid EMT tumor cells (high ITGB4) are spatially adjacent to TIMO monocytes.
Co‑culture of TIMO monocytes with EMT‑high organoids increases EMT gene expression and monocyte immunosuppressive markers.
EMT‑high organoids recruit more monocytes than EMT‑low counterparts.
Presence of TIMO monocytes diminishes CD69 and granzyme B expression in co‑cultured T cells.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target metabolic vulnerability of TIMO monocytes to overcome immunosuppression
combination_strategies.phenotype: Proposed future strategy

open_questions: What specific metabolic pathways sustain TIMO monocyte immunosuppressive function?
Can targeting ITGB4‑mediated adhesion disrupt the TIMO‑EMT crosstalk?

talk_title: IL‑1β‑high mature neutrophils mediate ICB resistance in prostate cancer bone metastasis

targets.dependency_context: IL‑1β produced by mature neutrophils suppresses CD8 T cell activity in bone metastases

biomarkers.name: IL1B mature neutrophil signature
biomarkers.finding: High signature scores correlate with reduced overall survival in bone metastatic prostate cancer patients

models.name: bone biopsies from metastatic prostate cancer
subcutaneous prostate cancer model with neutrophil transfer
models.genotype_or_key_features: receives IL‑1β‑high neutrophils from bone lesions

MOA_summary: Bone‑metastatic prostate cancer harbors a mature neutrophil subset expressing high IL‑1β that suppresses CD8⁺ T cell proliferation; IL‑1R blockade restores checkpoint inhibitor efficacy, indicating IL‑1 signaling as a therapeutic target for ICB‑resistant bone disease.
readouts: flow cytometry of T cell proliferation
key_findings: IL‑1β‑high mature neutrophils are enriched in prostate cancer bone metastases but scarce in soft‑tissue sites.
These neutrophils suppress CD8⁺ T cell proliferation in vitro.
Adoptive transfer of bone‑derived neutrophils confers ICB resistance to subcutaneous tumors.
IL‑1R antagonist combined with anti‑PD‑L1 markedly reduces tumor growth in intra‑osseous models, whereas monotherapies are ineffective.
High neutrophil IL‑1β signature predicts poorer survival and increased immune‑suppressive gene expression in patient cohorts.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: IL‑1β‑high neutrophils specifically mediate resistance in bone, not in primary or soft‑tissue lesions.

combination_strategies.rationale: Blockade of IL‑1 signaling may relieve neutrophil‑mediated suppression and enhance ICB
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression in bone metastasis model

open_questions: What upstream signals drive IL‑1β upregulation in bone‑resident neutrophils?
Can targeting neutrophil recruitment (e.g., CXCR2) complement IL‑1R blockade?

talk_title: The Genomic Landscape of Likely Human Pre-cancers
MOA_summary: Comprehensive genomic profiling of ~1,500 human pre-cancer lesions across 33 tissue types identified high heterogeneity in mutational burden, copy-number alterations, and driver gene alterations, and a random forest model using TMB, ploidy, and genome aberration predicts malignant potential.
key_findings: Pre-cancer samples exhibited coding mutation counts ranging from <10 to >3,000 across tissue types.
43% of pre-cancers lacked detectable driver gene alterations compared with 11% in TCGA cancers.
Tumor mutational burden, ploidy, and percent genome aberrant were the top three features distinguishing cancer-like from precancer-like samples.
In Barrett's esophagus, the cancer-like score correlated with progression to invasive cancer.
open_questions: How to collect longitudinal clinical outcome data for indolent pre-cancers that never progress?
What is the role of germline predisposition in pre-cancer genomic signatures?

talk_title: Mechanisms of Tumor Dormancy Through Immune Niche Formation and Its Impact on Immune Therapeutic Relapse

models.name: Tgp‑myeloid knockout mouse with inducible GFP tumor cells
models.genotype_or_key_features: TGF‑βR2 knockout in myeloid cells, disseminated tumor cells

MOA_summary: Dormant tumor cells up‑regulate CD200, which engages CD200R on NK cells within a monocyte‑neutrophil‑NK cell niche, suppressing NK‑mediated killing; CD200 knockdown reduces dormant lesions, and anti‑CD200 therapy eradicates residual dormant cells after chemotherapy, preventing relapse.
key_findings: Dormant lesions (1‑8 cells) were enriched for monocytes, neutrophils, dendritic cells, and NK cells, but lacked T cells.
CD200 expression was high in dormant tumor cells and mediated resistance to NK‑cell killing.
CD200 knockdown reduced dormant lesion burden without affecting proliferative lesions.
Anti‑CD200 antibody combined with cisplatin and anti‑PD‑1 eliminated residual dormant cells and prevented tumor regrowth after therapy withdrawal.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Dormant cells evade immune clearance via CD200‑mediated inhibition of NK cells.

combination_strategies.rationale: Chemotherapy reduces proliferative tumor bulk, leaving dormant cells.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Residual dormant cells persist; anti‑CD200 needed for eradication.

combination_strategies.rationale: Checkpoint blockade enhances T‑cell activity; combined with anti‑CD200 targets both T‑cell and NK‑cell pathways.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Complete elimination of dormant lesions and prevention of relapse.

open_questions: Can CD200 blockade improve outcomes in patients with minimal residual disease after standard therapy?
What is the relevance of CD200‑mediated dormancy in other cancer types?

talk_title: Ligandable PNT Domain Establishes ERG as a Druggable Oncogenic Driver in Prostate Cancer

speaker.name: Dr. Wang
speaker.affiliation: University of Michigan / Shanghai Institute of Chemistry

models.name: ERG+ mouse organoid, ERG+ PDX‑derived organoid

MOA_summary: Pbite‑1 binds the PNT domain of ERG with ~28 µM affinity, stabilizing the protein and disrupting PNT‑mediated protein‑protein interactions, leading to down‑regulation of ERG target genes, induction of apoptosis, and inhibition of invasion in ERG‑positive prostate cancer models.
key_findings: Pbite‑1 stabilizes ERG PNT domain in vitro (DSF) and in cells (CETSA) with KD ~28 µM.
Biotinylated Pbite‑1 pulls down full‑length ERG but not PNT‑deleted fragments, confirming domain specificity.
Pbite‑1 selectively reduces viability and induces apoptosis in ERG‑positive cell lines and organoids, sparing ERG‑negative controls.
Treatment suppresses ERG‑regulated transcriptional programs (MYC, AR, etc.) and inhibits invasion in ERG‑overexpressing models.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Pbite‑1 shows modest potency (micromolar) and requires further optimization for clinical translation.
open_questions: Can higher‑affinity PNT binders achieve therapeutic efficacy in vivo?
Will targeting the PNT domain affect other ETS family members and cause off‑target effects?

talk_title: Novel Brain‑Penetrant BRAF Inhibitor HSK42360 for BRAF‑Mutant Glioma

biomarkers.name: BRAF V600E mutation
biomarkers.finding: Patients harboring V600E were enrolled

MOA_summary: HSK42360 induces an external rotation of the αC‑helix of BRAF, preventing RAF dimerization and thus avoiding paradoxical MAPK activation; it penetrates the brain (Kp,uu = 1.1) and shows potent inhibition of class I‑III BRAF mutants in vitro and tumor regression in intracranial glioma models.
key_findings: HSK42360 does not cause paradoxical MAPK activation in HCT116 cells.
In vitro IC50s against class II (G469A) and class III (G466V) BRAF mutants ranged from 7.6 nM to 101 nM, outperforming dabrafenib.
Brain penetration Kp,uu = 1.1 exceeds the 0.3 threshold for CNS drugs.
In intracranial A375 CDX mice, HSK42360 significantly prolonged survival.
In a clinical cohort (n=23), ORR was 42.1% (high‑grade) and 50% (low‑grade) with durable responses and reduced BRAF‑inhibitor toxicities.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: No paradoxical activation observed; activity retained in NRAS‑mutant resistant models.

combination_strategies.rationale: Potential synergistic MAPK pathway blockade.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced tumor regression in class II/III BRAF models.

combination_strategies.rationale: Overcome resistance in CRC models with EGFR activation.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Tumor control in cetuximab‑plus‑vemurafenib resistant CRC PDX.

open_questions: Will HSK42360 demonstrate efficacy in BRAF‑inhibitor resistant glioma patients?
Can broader tumor types with BRAF mutations benefit from this brain‑penetrant inhibitor?

talk_title: Active RAS Inhibition Intercepts Pancreas Cancer in Mice

models.name: KPC mouse (KRAS G12D + TP53 mutation)
models.genotype_or_key_features: Develops PanIN at 1‑2 mo, tumors at 3‑4 mo

MOA_summary: RAS inhibitors induce apoptosis of KRAS‑mutant PanIN lesions, reducing their number and delaying tumor onset; intermittent metronomic dosing further prolongs latency, demonstrating that early interception of pre‑malignant KRAS‑driven lesions can extend survival independent of adaptive T‑cell immunity.
key_findings: 10‑day RAS inhibition reduced PanIN lesions from ~400 to significantly fewer in 9‑week‑old KPC mice.
RAS inhibition increased cleaved caspase‑3 in PanIN cells, indicating apoptosis without transdifferentiation.
Four weeks of RAS inhibition delayed tumor onset and improved overall survival in KPC mice.
T‑cell depletion did not affect the interception benefit, suggesting a T‑cell‑independent mechanism.
Metronomic weekly RAS inhibition (1 week on/1 week off) delayed tumor onset by >200 days and extended survival by >1 year.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Interception efficacy appears independent of immune surveillance.
open_questions: How to translate PanIN interception to high‑risk human populations?
Will long‑term intermittent RAS inhibition be safe in humans?

talk_title: Phase 1/2 Study of Deruxnaratib with Gemcitabine and Nab‑Paclitaxel in First‑Line Metastatic Pancreatic Cancer

biomarkers.name: KRAS mutant allele fraction (ctDNA)
biomarkers.finding: 96% of patients achieved ≥50% reduction; 61% cleared ctDNA

models.name: GI102 sub‑protocol C (dose level 1B)
models.genotype_or_key_features: 40 pts, KRAS G12D/V/R, first‑line metastatic PDAC

MOA_summary: Deruxnaratib binds cyclophilin A and forms a complex that sterically blocks effector binding to GTP‑bound KRAS (codons 12,13,61 and WT), suppressing MAPK signaling; combined with gemcitabine/nab‑paclitaxel, it yields high response rates and deep ctDNA reductions in KRAS‑mutant PDAC.
key_findings: Confirmed ORR of 58% and DCR of 90% in 40 first‑line PDAC patients receiving deruxnaratib + gemcitabine/nab‑paclitaxel.
Six‑month landmark PFS and OS were 84% and 90%, respectively, exceeding historical chemotherapy benchmarks.
96% of evaluable patients achieved ≥50% reduction in KRAS ctDNA; 61% cleared detectable KRAS mutation.
Grade ≥3 rash occurred in ~10‑15% of patients; overall safety profile was manageable with dose reductions.

combination_strategies.rationale: Standard chemotherapy backbone to enhance tumor kill while KRAS inhibition blocks MAPK signaling.
combination_strategies.phenotype: High ORR and durable disease control in KRAS‑mutant PDAC.

open_questions: Will maintenance deruxnaratib after chemotherapy improve long‑term outcomes?
How does efficacy differ among KRAS alleles (G12D vs G12V vs G12R)?

talk_title: Gapless But Not Global: Ancestry-Enriched Functional Sequence Missing from Human Reference Genomes

models.name: not specified
models.genotype_or_key_features: African ancestry-enriched contigs from asthma cohort (n≈910)

MOA_summary: The study aligns African ancestry-enriched contigs (~300 Mb) to gapless T2T-CHM13 and HPRC pangenome assemblies, revealing that many unmapped sequences map to centromeric, HLA, and CpG island regions, contain predicted coding and regulatory elements, and are transcriptionally active in normal and cancer RNA‑seq datasets, highlighting reference bias that can miss clinically relevant variants.
key_findings: 40% of African ancestry-enriched contigs map nearly perfectly to T2T‑CHM13; ~80% map reasonably well.
94% of mapped contigs overlap centromeric or satellite repeat regions, with notable enrichment in the HLA locus.
Gene set enrichment shows over‑representation of MHC complex proteins and neuronal signaling genes.
92% of contigs align to at least one HPRC assembly, with significant enrichment to African and admixed American references.
