10.5 Mb of non‑repeat sequence shows transcriptional activity in 1000 Genomes cell lines, TCGA cancer cohorts, and Davis lab datasets.
Unmapped contigs (≈1.5 Mb) contain predicted CpG islands, protein domains, and exhibit expression in breast cancer samples.
open_questions: How do the newly identified functional sequences impact somatic variant calling and neoantigen prediction in diverse patient cohorts?
What is the clinical relevance of the non‑repeat, transcriptionally active contigs for cancer risk and treatment response?

company_or_sponsor: McMillen Center for the Study of the Non‑Coding Cancer Genome

talk_title: Epigenetics, Ancestry, and Cancer Disparity: Nature and Nurture Interplay
tumor_types: head and neck squamous cell carcinoma

biomarkers.name: PAQR6 expression
biomarkers.finding: Upregulated in African ancestry breast cancer; associated with higher mortality (HR significant).

biomarkers.name: PAQR6 promoter methylation
biomarkers.finding: Hypomethylation correlates with increased PAQR6 expression and worse survival in African ancestry patients.

models.name: TCGA pan‑cancer cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 33 tumor types, ~11,000 patients, self‑identified race/ethnicity and genetically inferred ancestry

clinical_data.population: African ancestry vs European ancestry breast cancer

MOA_summary: Genetic ancestry correlates more strongly than self‑identified race with breast cancer survival disparities; differential expression of PAQR6, driven by promoter hypomethylation in African ancestry tumors, mediates increased mortality, illustrating that epigenetic regulation links ancestry‑related environmental exposures to clinical outcomes.
key_findings: Genetic ancestry provides higher resolution than self‑identified race for modeling survival disparities in four cancers (breast, HNSCC, kidney, skin).
Somatic mutation profiles showed no ancestry‑specific driver mutations or survival interactions.
Differential expression analysis identified seven genes whose ancestry‑specific expression interacts with survival; five are in breast cancer, including PAQR6.
PAQR6 is up‑regulated in African ancestry breast tumors and its promoter is hypomethylated, linking epigenetic activation to poorer outcomes.
Multivariable Cox models using continuous ancestry metrics detect more significant survival associations than categorical self‑identified race.
open_questions: Can targeting PAQR6 or its downstream progesterone signaling mitigate ancestry‑linked survival disparities?
What specific environmental exposures drive the observed hypomethylation in African ancestry patients?

company_or_sponsor: National Institute on Minority Health and Health Disparities

talk_title: Translating Genomic Equity: Infrastructure for Global Precision Oncology

biomarkers.name: TNFRSF9 expression signature
biomarkers.finding: Predicts lethal prostate cancer in African ancestry patients with 78.2% accuracy.

biomarkers.name: PTEN loss signature
biomarkers.finding: Combined with NCCN risk score improves prediction to 90% accuracy for African ancestry patients.

models.name: NCI Maryland Cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: ≈185 prostate cancer patients, high vs low African ancestry, multi‑omics (exposomics, GWAS, proteomics, scRNA‑seq, spatial transcriptomics)

MOA_summary: Integrating ancestry‑informed serum proteomics, single‑cell immune profiling, and spatial transcriptomics reveals an inflammation‑driven, myeloid‑rich tumor microenvironment in African ancestry prostate cancers; a TNFRSF9/PTEN signature predicts lethal disease, and deep‑learning pathology (CODA) links histologic patterns to immune infiltration, enabling ancestry‑specific risk stratification.
key_findings: Serum cytokine profiling shows elevated CXCL1, CXCL5, and macrophage recruitment proteins in African ancestry individuals.
A two‑gene signature (TNFRSF9, PTEN) predicts lethal prostate cancer in African ancestry patients with 78.2% accuracy; combined with NCCN risk score reaches 90% accuracy.
Neighborhood deprivation index (NDI) correlates with heightened inflammatory cytokines, linking social determinants to molecular risk.
Single‑cell PBMC analysis identifies expanded CD16+ monocytes and exhausted CD8+ TEM cells (LAG‑3+) in African ancestry subjects.
Tumor deconvolution and spatial profiling reveal increased myeloid‑derived suppressor cells and M1/M2 macrophage polarization in African ancestry tumors.
Deep‑learning model (CODA) accurately maps immune cell infiltration on H&E, showing higher inflammatory cell density in African ancestry prostate tumors.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Exhausted CD8+ TEM cells co‑express LAG‑3, suggesting potential resistance to checkpoint blockade.
Chronic inflammation may drive M2 polarization, creating an immunosuppressive niche despite overall immune infiltration.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target exhausted CD8+ T cells prevalent in African ancestry tumors
combination_strategies.phenotype: Potentially restores cytotoxic activity and improves survival

combination_strategies.rationale: Blocks CXCL1/CXCL5 mediated neutrophil recruitment
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduces pro‑tumor inflammation in high‑NDI patients

open_questions: How does chronic environmental inflammation translate into epigenetic reprogramming of immune cells in African ancestry patients?
Can integrating dietary and exposomic data improve the predictive power of the ancestry‑specific signatures?

talk_title: Neuronal mitochondria transfer drives breast cancer metastasis

speaker.name: Simon
speaker.affiliation: University of South Alabama (moving to Oklahoma University)

models.name: 4T1 orthotopic mammary fat pad
models.genotype_or_key_features: mouse mammary tumor, denervated vs control

models.name: human prostate perineural invasion specimens
models.genotype_or_key_features: proximal vs distal tumor cells to nerves

MOA_summary: Neuronal cells secrete axon guidance molecules such as SEMA4F, inducing cancer neurogenesis; neuronal mitochondria are transferred to mesenchymal breast cancer cells via tunneling nanotubes, increasing their oxidative phosphorylation and conferring resistance to oxidative and shear stress, which enriches for metastatic outgrowth.
key_findings: Dominant‑negative TGF‑β receptor expression blocked plasticity and reduced metastasis without changing primary tumor weight.
Denervation of the mammary fat pad with Botox decreased tumor mitochondrial content and reduced metastatic burden by ~35% in mouse models.
Co‑culture of neurons with cancer cells increased cancer cell respiration and mitochondrial mass (≈30% increase).
Mitochondrial transfer from neurons to cancer cells occurred in 10‑15% of tumor cells in vivo; denervation reduced this transfer by 35%.
Green (mitochondria‑receiving) cancer cells were enriched in lung and brain metastases despite no increase in in‑vitro invasion.
Mitochondria‑receiving cells showed enhanced survival under oxidative stress and shear‑stress assays compared with non‑receiving cells.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Mitochondria‑receiving cancer cells resist oxidative stress and shear stress, enabling metastatic survival.

combination_strategies.rationale: Block plasticity‑driven neurogenesis and metastasis.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced metastasis without affecting primary tumor size.

combination_strategies.rationale: Ablate local nerve input to test nerve‑cancer metabolic coupling.
combination_strategies.phenotype: 35% reduction in neuronal mitochondrial transfer and lower mitochondrial content in tumor cells.

open_questions: Is the observed correlation between nerve density and metastasis causal, and can direct inhibition of neuronal axon guidance molecules block metastasis?
What is the molecular composition of transferred neuronal mitochondria and how does it differ functionally from cancer‑derived mitochondria?
Does bidirectional mitochondrial transfer occur in vivo, and what are the consequences for tumor and stromal cells?

talk_title: Tumor‑derived mitochondrial DNA transfer impairs T‑cell function and reprograms macrophages

targets.dependency_context: cancer cells resistant to mitophagy, donor of mutant mitochondria

models.name: human melanoma and NSCLC patients receiving ICI
models.genotype_or_key_features: mtDNA mutation burden assessed in tumor and TILs

models.name: mouse subcutaneous tumor with MitoDSRed‑labeled cancer cells
models.genotype_or_key_features: TILs analyzed days 21‑42 for mtDNA transfer

MOA_summary: Tumor cells harbor high‑frequency mtDNA mutations and transfer these dysfunctional mitochondria to T cells via tunneling nanotubes and extracellular vesicles; USP30‑mediated mitophagy resistance allows mutant mitochondria to persist in T cells, impairing OXPHOS, glycolytic balance, memory formation and response to PD‑1 blockade. Parallel transfer to macrophages drives pro‑angiogenic, immature phenotypes that sensitize tumors to VEGF inhibition.
readouts: oxidative stress and shear‑stress survival assays
PD‑1 blockade efficacy in mouse tumor models
key_findings: 40% of patient T cells shared mtDNA mutations with autologous tumor cells, confirming in‑vivo mitochondrial transfer.
In vitro co‑culture showed MitoDSRed‑positive T cells after 2‑3 days, confirming functional transfer.
In vivo, MitoDSRed‑positive TILs increased over time (day 21‑42) and displayed heteroplasmy/homoplasmy of tumor mtDNA mutations.
Blocking cell‑cell contact, tunneling nanotubes, or EV release reduced mitochondrial transfer, indicating multiple routes.
USP30 is overexpressed in cancer cells; mutant mitochondria resistant to mitophagy are preferentially transferred to T cells.
Mutant mtDNA‑bearing T cells exhibit reduced OXPHOS, increased glycolysis, impaired memory formation, and decreased activation markers.
USP30‑deficient mice showed resistance to PD‑1 blockade, linking mitochondrial transfer to ICI failure.
Tumor‑derived mitochondria transferred to macrophages via TNTs promote maturation toward a pro‑angiogenic HIF1⁺/VEGF⁺ phenotype; MIRO1 knockdown reduced transfer and angiogenic macrophage expansion.
Patients with mtDNA‑mutant tumors had poorer outcomes on ICI therapy.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Mutant mitochondria resist mitophagy in T cells via USP30, leading to functional impairment.
Pro‑angiogenic macrophage subset expands after receiving mutant mitochondria, enhancing VEGF‑driven angiogenesis.

combination_strategies.rationale: Assess impact of mitochondrial transfer on ICI efficacy.
combination_strategies.phenotype: USP30‑deficient mice become resistant to PD‑1 therapy.

combination_strategies.rationale: Exploit pro‑angiogenic macrophage phenotype induced by mutant mitochondria.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced tumor growth suppression in mutant‑tumor models.

open_questions: What triggers selective transfer of mutant versus wild‑type mitochondria from tumor cells?
Can pharmacologic inhibition of USP30 restore mitophagy and rescue T‑cell function?
What is the relative contribution of TNT versus EV pathways in different tumor microenvironments?

talk_title: Engineering mitochondrial transfer to supercharge adoptive T‑cell therapy

models.name: B16 melanoma mouse model
models.genotype_or_key_features: Pmel‑1 TCR transgenic CD8 T cells

models.name: bone marrow mesenchymal stromal cells (MSC)
models.genotype_or_key_features: donor of functional mitochondria; EtBr‑treated dysfunctional control

MOA_summary: Bone‑marrow mesenchymal stromal cells donate functional mitochondria to activated CD8 T cells via tunneling nanotubes; transferred mitochondria increase mtDNA content (~30%), boost oxidative phosphorylation and spare respiratory capacity, leading to enhanced engraftment, deeper tumor infiltration, and prolonged anti‑tumor activity of adoptively transferred T cells.
key_findings: Co‑culture of activated CD8 T cells with MSCs achieved ~10% mitochondrial transfer rate.
Mito‑positive T cells contained ~30% more mitochondrial DNA than mito‑negative controls.
Functional mitochondria (MSC‑derived) increased basal respiration and spare respiratory capacity in recipient T cells; EtBr‑treated dysfunctional mitochondria did not.
In B16 melanoma, mito‑positive Pmel‑1 cells controlled tumor growth for >42 days, whereas mito‑negative cells relapsed after ~21 days; survival was significantly extended.
Mito‑positive T cells showed higher frequencies in spleen and tumor, and penetrated deeper into tumor tissue.
SENIT assay revealed higher metabolic capacity of mito‑positive TILs within tumors.
Single‑cell RNA‑seq showed mito‑positive T cells enriched for proliferating/effector clusters, while mito‑negative cells were skewed toward exhausted phenotypes.
CD19 CAR T cells receiving MSC mitochondria displayed superior serial killing of Nalm6 leukemia and prolonged survival in NSG mice.
Knockout of talin‑2 in either T cells or MSCs markedly reduced mitochondrial transfer, implicating focal adhesion machinery.

combination_strategies.rationale: Demonstrate that functional mitochondria, not merely MSC contact, drive metabolic benefit.
combination_strategies.phenotype: No respiration improvement; no anti‑tumor advantage.

combination_strategies.rationale: Identify molecular driver of mitochondrial uptake.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Significant reduction in transfer efficiency.

open_questions: How can the mitochondrial transfer rate be increased beyond ~10% for clinical scalability?
Do transferred mitochondria retain their original DNA and protein composition long‑term in T cells?
Would combining this approach with agents that boost mitochondrial biogenesis (e.g., PGC‑1α agonists) provide additive benefit?

talk_title: AI-augmented workflow tools for clinical trial operations

speaker.name: Umut Topaloglu
speaker.affiliation: National Cancer Institute Center for Biomedical Informatics and Information Technology

MOA_summary: Developed a suite of AI-augmented tools—including semi‑automatic common data element annotation, a fair data workbench, and eligibility‑criteria structuring—where AI generates candidate mappings or drafts and expert humans review, accept, or reject outputs, preserving oversight while accelerating data harmonization and trial matching.
key_findings: Semi‑automatic semantic matching of new data elements to existing CDEs provides confidence scores and side‑by‑side comparisons, with human reviewers intervening on low‑confidence matches.
The fair data workbench pipelines standardize heterogeneous trial data, enabling cross‑trial analyses that were previously blocked by format inconsistencies.
LLM‑assisted structuring of eligibility criteria and patient EHR data creates a basis for deterministic patient‑matching engines like MatchBox, with two mandatory human verification steps.
Integration with USCDI‑Plus standards and FHIR data‑quality ontologies allows quantitative assessment of EHR data completeness.
open_questions: How can the AI‑generated CDE mappings be validated at scale across diverse trial sponsors?
What governance frameworks are needed to ensure consistent human‑in‑the‑loop oversight as tools mature?

talk_title: AI‑generated consent documents and multilingual translation for trial enrollment

speaker.name: Tana Nelson
speaker.affiliation: National Cancer Institute Center for Biomedical Informatics and Information Technology

MOA_summary: Implemented AI pipelines that ingest full protocol documents to auto‑generate first‑draft consent forms at an eighth‑grade reading level, embed structured risk tables, and produce lay‑person summaries; coupled with AI‑assisted multilingual translation (initially Spanish) that halves translation time while retaining human expert review for regulatory certification.
key_findings: AI‑generated consent draft can be produced in ~4 minutes versus up to two weeks for manual medical‑writer preparation.
Initial AI translation of a 10,000‑word consent reduces turnaround from five days to approximately 2.5 days before human post‑editing.
Human‑in‑the‑loop review flags sections where AI performance is weak (e.g., specimen storage language), ensuring regulatory compliance.
Prototype lay‑person one‑page study summaries improve patient comprehension by presenting key study elements in plain language.
open_questions: Can AI translation achieve regulatory‑grade accuracy without human post‑editing for less common languages?
What metrics will be used to assess patient comprehension improvements from AI‑generated materials?

talk_title: Reducing Animal Testing in Oncology Non-Clinical Development

speaker.name: Michael Manning
speaker.affiliation: Division of Hematology, Oncology, Toxicology, FDA

targets.dependency_context: Antibody drug conjugates with cytotoxic payloads

models.name: IND/BLA submissions for CD3 bispecifics and ADCs

MOA_summary: Weight-of-evidence risk assessments integrate non‑clinical, clinical, and literature data to replace routine three‑month GLP toxicology studies for CD3 bispecific T‑cell engagers and antibody‑drug conjugates, focusing on target‑specific toxicities and leveraging NAMs where appropriate.
key_findings: Analysis of IND and BLA submissions showed that three‑month general toxicology studies are often unnecessary for CD3 bispecific T‑cell engagers because target binding is absent in animals and chronic exposure cannot be achieved.

For antibody‑drug conjugates, toxicity was attributed to the small‑molecule payload rather than the antibody, indicating that three‑month non‑human primate studies add little value and can be limited to rodent studies.
Weight‑of‑evidence approaches, incorporating literature, clinical data, and NAMs, can replace embryo‑fetal developmental and chronic toxicity studies without compromising patient safety.

combination_strategies.rationale: Provide mechanistic safety data when animal studies are not feasible or informative
combination_strategies.phenotype: Supports chronic toxicity assessment and starting dose selection without animal data

open_questions: How can the FDA further standardize weight‑of‑evidence criteria across different product classes?
What quantitative thresholds should trigger the replacement of a three‑month study with a weight‑of‑evidence approach?

cited_papers: Yao et al. 2024 FDA publication on NAMs
Weber et al. 2023 FDA guidance on CD3 bispecifics

talk_title: Incorporating New Approach Methodologies, or NAMs, in Oncology Drug Development

speaker.name: Natalie Simpson
speaker.affiliation: Division of Hematology, Oncology, and Toxicology, FDA

MOA_summary: NAMs—including hiPSC‑cardiomyocytes, organoid/spheroid cultures, and organ‑on‑chip systems—are used as human‑relevant in‑vitro platforms to replace or supplement animal toxicology studies, particularly for QT prolongation, organ‑specific toxicities, and reproductive toxicity assessments in oncology INDs.
key_findings: A total of 139 hiPSC‑CM studies were submitted to the FDA from 2012‑2025, representing only ~1% of commercial IND submissions per year.
301 organoid/spheroid studies were identified across divisions (2008‑2025); 91% of organoid and 86% of spheroid submissions in oncology were for pharmacology, with only 6‑9% submitted as safety NAMs.
Organ‑on‑chip models were successfully used in a bispecific antibody case to replace animal studies for first‑in‑human dosing, supporting safety and starting dose selection.
Complex NAMs were identified in only eight INDs, with four proposing replacement of animal studies, indicating early but growing adoption.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target‑specific organ toxicity prediction based on known class toxicities
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enabled animal‑free safety package for a CD3 bispecific in FIH study

open_questions: How will the forthcoming CDER NAM guidance define validation requirements for diverse liver‑model platforms?
What criteria will determine when a safety NAM can fully replace an animal study versus being used only as supportive evidence?

cited_papers: Yang et al. 2022 identification of hiPSC‑CM studies
Simpson et al. 2025 landscape analysis of NAM submissions

talk_title: A Glimpse into the Future in Bioinformatics

speaker.name: Matthew Thompson
speaker.affiliation: Division of Hematology, Oncology, Toxicology, FDA – Oncology Center of Excellence

MOA_summary: A curated FDA oncology IND database integrates mechanisms, targets, and product classes to enable rapid weight‑of‑evidence assessments, inform starting dose strategies, and evaluate the necessity of repeat‑dose toxicology studies, supporting potential reductions in animal use.
key_findings: The FDA bioinformatics team has compiled a searchable database of over 8,000 commercial oncology INDs, cataloguing mechanisms, targets, and modality classes.
Analysis shows a steady increase in oncology IND submissions since 2010, with both biologics and small molecules contributing to the rise.
Retrospective studies are planned to compare one‑month versus three‑month repeat‑dose toxicology results with clinical safety outcomes to identify when non‑rodent studies may be omitted.

combination_strategies.rationale: Leverage existing clinical, non‑clinical, and literature data to replace certain animal studies
combination_strategies.phenotype: Proposed stepwise design where a 1‑month rodent study plus weight‑of‑evidence may suffice for FIH safety

combination_strategies.rationale: Streamline timelines by generating early safety data and extending the same study for later phases
combination_strategies.phenotype: Potentially reduces total animal usage and study start‑up time

open_questions: What data thresholds (e.g., concordance between 1‑month and clinical safety signals) are needed to justify omitting a three‑month non‑rodent study?
Which product classes (small molecules, biologics, ADCs) are most amenable to the proposed stepwise toxicology designs?

cited_papers: Thompson et al. 2023 FDA internal analysis of oncology IND trends

talk_title: Bone Repair External State Program Drives Cancer Cell Dedifferentiation and Immune Invasion in Melanoma

speaker.name: Jackson Weir
speaker.affiliation: Broad Institute, Fay Chen's Lab, Harvard

MOA_summary: Spatial profiling identified anti‑inflammatory hubs enriched for SPP1⁺ macrophages and POSTN⁺ fibroblasts that exclude CD8⁺ T cells, induce a dysfunctional T‑cell state, and via secreted tissue‑repair ligands drive melanoma dedifferentiation to a neural‑crest‑like state and protect tumor cells from cytokine‑mediated killing.
key_findings: Identified an anti‑inflammatory hub composed of SPP1⁺ macrophages and POSTN⁺ fibroblasts that is largely T‑cell excluded.
T cells within the anti‑inflammatory hub are dysfunctional singleton clonotypes lacking expansion.
Ligand program from the hub (SPP1, POSTN) induces melanoma cells to dedifferentiate from a melanocytic to a neural‑crest‑like state in vitro.
SPP1 pretreatment protects neural‑crest‑state melanoma cells from killing by IFN‑γ, TNF‑α and other cytotoxic cytokines.
Spatial correlation shows anti‑inflammatory hubs predict higher neural‑crest trajectory scores, linking niche composition to tumor dedifferentiation and immune evasion.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Anti‑inflammatory hubs mediate resistance to CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration and to cytokine‑induced tumor cell killing.
open_questions: Can targeting SPP1 or POSTN signaling disrupt the anti‑inflammatory hub and restore immune infiltration?
What upstream signals drive formation of the anti‑inflammatory niche in metastatic melanoma?

talk_title: Tumor‑Specific Antibodies Elicited by Engineered Bacteria Promote Bladder Cancer Immunotherapy

speaker.name: Matthew Ruane
speaker.affiliation: Columbia University, Department of Microbiology and Immunology

clinical_data.population: MB49 orthotopic mice receiving bacteria + anti‑PD‑1

MOA_summary: Engineered probiotic E. coli Nissle with a synchronized lysis circuit secretes CXCL13 within bladder tumors, recruits B and T cells to form tertiary lymphoid structures in draining lymph nodes, generates tumor‑specific IgG antibodies, and synergizes with PD‑1 blockade to double the cure rate in mice.
readouts: in vitro IgG binding assay to MB49 cells
key_findings: Engineered E. coli Nissle selectively colonizes bladder tumors without spreading to normal tissue.
Intravesical delivery of CXCL13‑producing bacteria induces TLS formation in draining lymph nodes and increases tumor‑specific IgG antibodies.
Combination of CXCL13‑bacteria with anti‑PD‑1 cures 50% of mice versus 25% with anti‑PD‑1 alone.
Efficacy requires T follicular helper cells; depletion of these cells abolishes therapeutic benefit.
Treatment is well tolerated with no weight loss or systemic toxicity compared with controls.

combination_strategies.rationale: CXCL13‑induced TLS and antibody responses amplify checkpoint blockade efficacy
combination_strategies.phenotype: cure rate increased to 50% vs 25% with PD‑1 alone

open_questions: How can the bacterial delivery platform be safely translated to human patients?
What is the optimal dosing schedule to maximize TLS formation while minimizing potential infection risk?

talk_title: Integrating Single‑Cell and Spatial Transcriptomics with Mouse Models to Understand How Tumor‑Associated Macrophages Suppress Antitumor Immunity

speaker.name: Rosandra Tonia
speaker.affiliation: University of Chicago, Labs of Thomas Gajewski and Samantha Rasenfeld

MOA_summary: Host‑derived GPNMB deficiency reprograms tumor‑associated macrophages toward an interferon‑driven pro‑inflammatory phenotype, expands CXCL10⁺ macrophages, enhances CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration and activation, and markedly improves tumor control; the effect requires tissue‑resident (LysM‑Cre) but not bone‑marrow‑derived macrophages.
readouts: single‑cell RNA‑seq of tumor immune compartment
key_findings: Gpnmb‑/- mice exhibit significantly reduced tumor growth and increased survival in melanoma, breast, and lung metastasis models.
Knockout tumors show higher CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration, activation (PD‑1⁺, TIM‑3⁺) and increased memory phenotypes.
scRNA‑seq reveals expansion of CXCL10⁺ pro‑inflammatory macrophages and contraction of mitochondrial‑rich, immunosuppressive macrophages.
GPNMB loss decreases fatty‑acid uptake in both pro‑ and anti‑inflammatory tumor macrophages.
Tumor control is abolished after systemic T‑cell depletion and only partially reduced after CD8⁺ depletion, indicating dependence on T cells.
LysM‑Cre–mediated Gpnmb deletion (targeting tissue‑resident macrophages) recapitulates tumor control, whereas Vav1‑Cre (hematopoietic) deletion does not.
open_questions: What are the specific ligands/receptors through which GPNMB mediates macrophage suppression?
Can pharmacologic inhibition of GPNMB mimic the knockout phenotype in patients?

talk_title: Vaccine Stimulation of Bystander T Cells in the Lymph Node Unexpectedly Promotes Tumor‑Specific Host Immunity to Regress Solid Tumors

speaker.name: Liam Ma
speaker.affiliation: University of Pennsylvania, Departments of Pathology, Laboratory Medicine and Bioengineering

models.name: orthotopic PDAC (mesothelin‑expressing) model
models.genotype_or_key_features: tumor‑irrelevant FITC‑CAR T cells + amphiphile vaccine

clinical_data.population: orthotopic PDAC, melanoma, and breast cancer bearing mice

MOA_summary: An amphiphile vaccine targeting a generic FITC‑CAR on adoptively transferred T cells triggers rapid IFN‑γ release, transiently degrades the fibrotic PDAC capsule, up‑regulates MHC I/II on tumor cells, and activates endogenous CD8⁺ T cells, leading to swift tumor regression even when the CAR does not recognize tumor antigen; efficacy is IFN‑γ dependent and enhanced by anti‑PD‑L1 blockade.
key_findings: Vaccine boosting of tumor‑irrelevant CAR T cells induces >50% tumor regression within 24 h across PDAC, melanoma, and breast cancer models.
Efficacy is abolished by IFN‑γ neutralization, demonstrating cytokine dependence.
Tumor regression persists after CAR T depletion at 3 days, indicating reliance on endogenous immunity.
Serum IFN‑γ and CCL2 spike transiently after vaccination, coinciding with rapid degradation of the PDAC fibrotic capsule.
MHC I up‑regulation on tumor cells and activation of host CD8⁺ T cells are essential; B2m‑KO tumors are resistant.
Combination with anti‑PD‑L1 further deepens responses, suggesting synergy with checkpoint blockade.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Therapeutic effect is lost when IFN‑γ is blocked or when tumor cells lack MHC I (B2m‑KO).

combination_strategies.rationale: relieve checkpoint inhibition to amplify vaccine‑induced CD8⁺ T‑cell activity
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced tumor regression beyond vaccine alone

open_questions: Can this approach achieve durable responses in non‑lymphodepleted hosts?
What is the optimal CAR/vaccine pairing for translation to human patients?

talk_title: IL‑18‑secreting CAR T cells uncover divergent immune mechanisms of antigen‑heterogeneous solid tumor clearance

speaker.name: Audrey May
speaker.affiliation: Stanford University School of Medicine, Lab of Crystal Mackall

models.name: heterogeneous B16‑F10 OVA + human CD19 tumors (80% CD19⁺ / 20% CD19⁻)
models.genotype_or_key_features: C57BL/6, lymphodepleted, adoptive transfer of 3 × 10⁶ CAR T cells

clinical_data.population: heterogeneous B16 tumors treated with IL‑18 CAR T cells
clinical_data.comparator: IL‑36, FLT3‑L CARs or CAR‑only controls

MOA_summary: IL‑18‑secreting CD19 CAR T cells, despite low persistence, remodel the myeloid compartment by expanding activated neutrophils and macrophages with IFN‑γ‑responsive gene signatures, leading to rapid clearance of antigen‑heterogeneous tumors independent of adaptive CD8⁺ T‑cell priming, while Rag1‑KO mice retain efficacy, indicating a dominant innate mechanism.
readouts: flow cytometry of T‑cell and myeloid subsets
bulk RNA‑seq of tumor‑draining lymph nodes and spleen
key_findings: IL‑18 CAR T cells achieve superior heterogeneous tumor control compared with IL‑36, FLT3‑L, or CAR‑only controls.
IL‑18 CAR T cells show low persistence in tumor and lymph nodes but accumulate in the spleen.
Endogenous CD8⁺ T cells display an effector‑memory phenotype but reduced tumor‑reactive ELISpot responses.
Myeloid cells, especially neutrophils and macrophages, are markedly expanded and exhibit IFN‑γ‑responsive and antigen‑presentation gene signatures.
Neutrophil depletion does not impair IL‑18 CAR efficacy, suggesting macrophages are the critical mediators.
Rag1‑KO mice retain tumor control with IL‑18 CARs, indicating independence from adaptive immunity.
open_questions: Which macrophage subsets are essential for IL‑18‑mediated tumor clearance?
Can IL‑18 CARs be combined with checkpoint blockade to further improve outcomes?

talk_title: Gut Microeukaryotes, Endogenous Viruses, and Cancer

models.name: ZOE PREDICT studies
models.genotype_or_key_features: n≈400,000 individuals across 5 cohorts, multi-omic data

MOA_summary: Nguyen described population-scale analyses linking gut microeukaryote Blastocystis prevalence to plant-based diets, favorable cardiometabolic markers, lower adiposity, and reduced odds of colorectal cancer, and presented trans-kingdom dysbiosis in MASLD driven by oral bacteria, acylcarnitine metabolites, and endogenous viruses that may modulate bacterial communities via predator-prey dynamics.
key_findings: Blastocystis prevalence exceeds 40% in several under‑studied regions (e.g., Tanzania, Fiji) and is higher in adults than children, suggesting non‑vertical transmission.
Higher adherence to plant‑based diets correlates with increased Blastocystis carriage (median AUC ~0.85 for bacterial composition predicting Blastocystis).
Blastocystis‑positive individuals show improved fasting/postprandial triglycerides, higher HDL, lower GlicA, and a 6% decrease in odds of carriage per BMI point increase.
Case‑control analysis shows a 29% lower odds of Blastocystis in colorectal cancer and IBD samples versus healthy controls.
In a 1,100‑person dietary RCT, Blastocystis prevalence modestly expanded in participants achieving weight‑loss goals on high‑quality diets.
MASLD cases exhibit enrichment of oral‑typical bacteria (e.g., Streptococcus spp.) distinct from obesity‑associated signatures.
Acylcarnitines are the most enriched metabolite class in MASLD, with chain‑length–specific associations to microbial taxa.
Endogenous viruses, especially unclassified taxa and Punavirus P1, differentiate MASLD subtypes; MASLD‑associated viruses have larger genomes, higher GC, and more ORFs.
Computational phage‑host predictions suggest MASLD‑enriched viruses target and kill their bacterial hosts, indicating predator‑prey dynamics.
open_questions: What mechanistic pathways link Blastocystis colonization to cardiometabolic improvements?
How do specific oral bacteria and viral predators interact to shape MASLD microbiome ecology?
Can targeted modulation of identified viruses or bacteria alter MASLD progression?

talk_title: Population‑scale EBV Persistence and Host Genetics

targets.dependency_context: latent EBV DNA in peripheral B cells

biomarkers.name: EBV DNA load in whole‑genome sequencing
biomarkers.finding: ≈10% of individuals show high EBV DNA levels (right‑hand tail) versus ~90% seropositive

models.name: UK Biobank
models.genotype_or_key_features: ≈900,000 participants, whole‑genome sequencing, EBV serology in 10,000

models.name: GTEx peripheral blood
models.genotype_or_key_features: RNA‑seq and WGS data for EBV DNA/RNA comparison

clinical_data.metric: GWAS loci associated with EBV persistence
clinical_data.population: high EBV DNA carriers vs low carriers

MOA_summary: La Roe demonstrated that latent EBV DNA can be quantified from existing whole‑genome sequencing data, identified a high‑EBV‑load subpopulation (~10%) and linked host genetic variation at 45 loci—many in antigen presentation and B‑cell pathways—to persistence, which in turn associates with autoimmune, respiratory, and cancer phenotypes, and provided preliminary evidence of geographic strain variation.
key_findings: Approximately 10% of individuals in UK Biobank and All of Us have high EBV DNA levels detectable in blood WGS, despite ~90% seropositivity.
Masking low‑complexity repetitive EBV regions is essential; otherwise 40% of samples would be mis‑classified.
GWAS of 400,000 European‑ancestry participants identified 45 independent loci (antigen presentation, B‑cell transcription factors) associated with EBV persistence.
Genetic risk for EBV persistence overlaps with risk loci for systemic lupus, type 1 diabetes, and rheumatoid arthritis.
High‑EBV carriers show increased incidence of HIV infection, rheumatoid arthritis, COPD, lung cancer, and lymphomas across health‑record ICD‑10 data.
EBV strain analysis reveals 31 known variants; four novel variants are enriched in high‑EBV carriers and are being experimentally followed up.
Geographic comparison shows concordant EBV allele frequencies between UK and US cohorts, suggesting shared strain circulation.
open_questions: Which specific host genetic variants drive differential EBV latency control?
Do the four novel EBV variants directly influence persistence or disease risk?
Can interventions targeting EBV latency reduce associated autoimmune or cancer risks?

talk_title: Cancer Mycobiome: Truth, Myth, and Current Data

biomarkers.name: Candida albicans DNA/RNA in tumor tissue
biomarkers.finding: Detected in colorectal, stomach, head‑and‑neck, and esophageal cancers; absent in brain tumors

models.name: TCGA pan‑cancer cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 30+ cancer types, DNA‑seq and RNA‑seq data

models.name: Collected tumor biopsies (CRC, stomach, etc.)
models.genotype_or_key_features: Isolation of Candida strains, strain sequencing

models.name: AOM/DSS colon cancer model
models.genotype_or_key_features: Mice colonized with C. albicans vs C. glabrata

MOA_summary: Iliev highlighted that tumor‑associated fungi, especially Candida albicans, are present in the mucosal layer of gastrointestinal tumors, where strain‑specific production of the peptide toxin candidalysin induces IL‑17/IL‑1 inflammation and promotes tumorigenesis; antifungal fluconazole clears gut Candida and restores beneficial bacterial diversity and butyrate production, suggesting a causal role for the mycobiome in cancer progression.
key_findings: Fungal DNA (Candida albicans) is detectable in multiple GI cancers (CRC, stomach, esophageal, head‑and‑neck) but not in brain tumors.
Strain‑level analysis shows diverse C. albicans isolates with variable hyphal formation and candidalysin production.
In AOM/DSS mouse models, C. albicans colonization aggravates tumor development, whereas C. glabrata does not.
Fluconazole treatment clears both oral and gut Candida, whereas oral nystatin only clears oral colonization.
Systemic antifungal therapy restores bacterial diversity, notably increasing butyrate‑producing Faecalibacterium prausnitzii and Roseburia intestinalis, and raises fecal butyrate levels.
Candida‑positive tumors exhibit elevated IL‑17 and IL‑1 cytokine signatures linked to candidalysin activity.
Fecal microbiota transplantation (FMT) that enriches bacterial communities can displace Candida, reducing mucosal inflammation.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: One patient did not respond to fluconazole due to a resistant Candida strain.
Oral nystatin fails to affect gut Candida, highlighting compartment‑specific drug efficacy.

combination_strategies.rationale: Clear gut Candida to restore bacterial diversity and reduce IL‑17 inflammation
combination_strategies.phenotype: Increased butyrate‑producing bacteria and fecal butyrate; tumor‑associated inflammation reduced

combination_strategies.rationale: Introduce a robust bacterial community to outcompete Candida and restore ecosystem balance
combination_strategies.phenotype: Candida depletion and recovery of microbial diversity in ulcerative colitis patients at high CRC risk

open_questions: Which specific candidalysin‑dependent pathways drive IL‑17–mediated tumor promotion?
Do particular C. albicans strains correlate with clinical outcomes in cancer patients?
Can combined antifungal and microbiome‑restorative therapies synergistically prevent tumor progression?

talk_title: LINE1 ORF1p as a pan‑cancer biomarker and ultra‑sensitive single‑molecule detection assay
tumor_types: head and neck squamous cell carcinoma

biomarkers.name: LINE1 ORF1p plasma level
biomarkers.assay: ultrasensitive single‑molecule ELISA (microwell) / Mosaic flow cytometry assay
biomarkers.finding: Detected in ~50% of cancer patients (n≈600) and 0/406 healthy controls; early‑stage ovarian cancer sensitivity increased to 83% with second‑gen assay

models.name: healthy plasma cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: n=406 healthy individuals, age up to 95

models.name: cancer plasma cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: ≈600 cancer patients across multiple tumor types

models.name: ovarian cancer pilot
models.genotype_or_key_features: 50 ovarian cancer patients (including 6 early stage) vs 50 healthy

models.name: gastroesophageal monitoring cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 19 gastroesophageal cancer patients with serial samples

MOA_summary: LINE1 ORF1p is de‑repressed in cancer and high‑risk precursors, producing a tumor‑specific protein detectable in plasma via ultra‑sensitive single‑molecule sandwich assays; assay sensitivity improves with custom nanobody pairs and bar‑coded Mosaic detection, enabling early‑stage cancer detection and real‑time monitoring of treatment response.
key_findings: LINE1 ORF1p is expressed in >99% of examined carcinomas and high‑risk precursors across organ systems.
First‑generation single‑molecule assay detected ORF1p in 50% of cancer plasma samples while remaining undetectable in 402/406 healthy controls.
Second‑generation nanobody‑based assays increased early‑stage ovarian cancer sensitivity to 83% without loss of specificity.
Adding ORF1p to a CA125+HE4 panel raised AUC from 0.94 to 0.98 in a pilot ovarian cancer cohort.
Serial ORF1p levels perfectly segregated responders from non‑responders in a gastroesophageal cancer monitoring cohort (p<0.001).
Mosaic assay achieved detection in 100% of gastroesophageal trial samples, outperforming Simoa.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Assay specificity is high because ORF1p is absent in normal adult tissues and not induced by aging, chronic liver disease, autoimmune conditions, or COVID‑19.
Limited tissue‑of‑origin information; positive signal indicates presence of LINE1‑expressing tumor but not its anatomical source.

combination_strategies.rationale: Orthogonal protein biomarker for ovarian cancer
combination_strategies.phenotype: AUC increased to 0.98 when combined with ORF1p

combination_strategies.rationale: Standard ovarian cancer marker; improves detection when added to ORF1p
combination_strategies.phenotype: AUC increased to 0.98

open_questions: What is the limit of detection for early‑stage, low‑tumor‑burden cancers across different tumor types?
Can ORF1p levels be quantitatively linked to tumor burden and used for longitudinal monitoring?
What biofluids (stool, Pap smear, CSF) can reliably preserve ORF1p for detection?
How does ORF1p detection compare and combine with ctDNA or methylation‑based MCED assays?

talk_title: Naturally occurring antibodies as biomarkers for early cancer detection and outcome prediction

speaker.name: Qi Dang
speaker.affiliation: University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center

biomarkers.name: autoantibodies to NY-ESO-1
biomarkers.finding: higher prevalence in cancer patients vs controls; AUC modest but improves when combined with other markers

biomarkers.name: autoantibodies to p53
biomarkers.finding: outperforms CA125 alone for ovarian cancer detection; combination improves AUC

biomarkers.name: anti‑interferon antibodies
biomarkers.finding: associated with better response to checkpoint inhibitor therapy across melanoma, NSCLC, RCC

biomarkers.name: viral exposure antibody signature
biomarkers.finding: high viral antibody score predicts poorer survival in HCC patients receiving immunotherapy

models.name: lung cancer antibody study
models.genotype_or_key_features: cases, benign lung disease, healthy controls

models.name: pancreatic cancer autoantibody panel
models.genotype_or_key_features: PDAC patients vs controls; pooled serum arrays

models.name: HCC viral exposure signature cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: at‑risk individuals, HCC patients, longitudinal samples

models.name: checkpoint inhibitor response autoantibody cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: melanoma, NSCLC, RCC patients treated with ICI

clinical_data.metric: AUC (p53 autoantibody + CA125 vs CA125 alone)

clinical_data.value: higher than AFP (exact value not specified)

MOA_summary: Naturally occurring autoantibodies arise against tumor‑associated antigens (e.g., NY‑ESO‑1, p53, MMP14, LINE1 ORF1p) and viral antigens; high‑throughput protein arrays and PhIP‑seq identify antibody signatures that can differentiate cancer patients from controls, predict early disease, and correlate with immunotherapy outcomes.
key_findings: Single autoantibody markers show modest AUCs; panels of multiple antibodies substantially improve diagnostic performance.
p53 autoantibodies combined with CA125 outperform CA125 alone for ovarian cancer detection.
Anti‑interferon antibodies are enriched in responders to checkpoint inhibition across several tumor types, suggesting a predictive biomarker.
A viral exposure antibody signature predicts poorer survival in HCC patients receiving immunotherapy and rises longitudinally before HCC diagnosis.
IgG deposition is abundant on tumor cells in tissue sections, and cloned autoantibodies (e.g., against MMP14) can enhance phagocytosis and NK‑cell ADCC.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Autoantibody panels may be limited by tumor heterogeneity and lack of tissue‑of‑origin specificity.
High viral antibody burden correlates with worse immunotherapy outcomes, indicating a potential resistance-associated immune profile.

combination_strategies.rationale: Orthogonal protein biomarker for ovarian cancer
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved AUC for detection

combination_strategies.rationale: Standard ovarian cancer marker
combination_strategies.phenotype: AUC increase when combined with p53 autoantibody

open_questions: What is the optimal number and selection of autoantibodies to achieve high sensitivity and specificity across cancer types?
Can autoantibody signatures be integrated with other MCED modalities (e.g., ctDNA, methylation) for a unified early‑detection test?
What is the mechanistic basis for anti‑interferon antibodies predicting better ICI response?

talk_title: Unbiased autoantibody atlas for early cancer detection

biomarkers.name: autoantibodies against PD-L1 chaperone protein
biomarkers.finding: associated with improved overall survival in HCC patients (higher levels in responders vs non‑responders)

MOA_summary: The study builds a large-scale atlas of autoantibodies by profiling serum from liver disease cohorts using human protein arrays and FIP‑seq, revealing distinct autoantibody signatures that separate HCC from cirrhosis/hepatitis and identifying antibodies that correlate with clinical outcomes.
key_findings: Principal component analysis of autoantibody profiles segregates HCC patients from cirrhosis and hepatitis cohorts.
Heat‑map clustering shows a distinct blue cluster of HCC samples versus red cluster of non‑cancer liver disease.
Autoantibodies against a PD‑L1 chaperone protein are enriched in HCC patients with longer overall survival.
The planned CRUK Grand Challenge atlas will expand to >16,000 individuals across high‑risk groups (genetic predisposition, heavy smokers, obese, centenarians, twins).
Baseline autoantibody patterns are being evaluated as predictors of response to checkpoint inhibitor neoadjuvant therapy.
open_questions: Do microbial‑derived autoantibodies contribute causally to HCC development or are they bystanders?
Will pre‑existing anti‑tumor autoantibodies affect the biodistribution or efficacy of therapeutic antibodies?

talk_title: Multi-Cancer Early Detection: Where Are We and Where Do We Want to Go?

speaker.name: Peter Sasieni
speaker.affiliation: Queen Mary University of London, Center of Cancer Screening, Prevention, and Early Detection

biomarkers.name: Specificity of commercial MCED tests
biomarkers.finding: All five commercial tests reported specificities ≥96.4%; a specificity of 99.6% would yield nine‑fold fewer false positives than 96.4%.

biomarkers.name: Sensitivity of commercial MCED tests
biomarkers.finding: Reported sensitivities ranged from 20% to 70% across tests, lower for early‑stage cancers.

biomarkers.name: Positive predictive value (PPV) of Galleri
biomarkers.finding: PPV of a positive Galleri test was 62% in PATHFINDER 2.

biomarkers.name: Episode sensitivity in PATHFINDER 2
biomarkers.finding: 12‑type cancer episode sensitivity was 74% with 12‑month follow‑up.

clinical_data.value: favorable trend (statistically significant reduction not reached)

MOA_summary: Peter reviewed the landscape of multi‑cancer early detection (MCED) tests, highlighting that current commercial assays (cfDNA methylation, protein‑mutation panels, fragmentomics, CTC immunocytochemistry, electrical properties) achieve high specificity (≥96.4%) but variable sensitivity (20‑70%) that depends on cancer type and stage. He discussed trial outcomes, notably the NHS Galleri RCT which failed to meet its primary stage‑3/4 reduction endpoint but showed a trend toward fewer stage‑4 diagnoses and increased early‑stage detection, and PATHFINDER 2 which demonstrated a 62% PPV and 74% episode sensitivity for 12 cancers.
key_findings: All five commercial MCED tests have specificities ≥96.4%, but a specificity of 99.6% would reduce false positives nine‑fold compared with 96.4%.
Reported sensitivities vary widely (20‑70%) and are lower for early‑stage cancers, making stage‑specific performance a critical limitation.
Galleri’s NHS RCT did not achieve a statistically significant reduction in stage 3/4 cancers, though a reduction in stage 4 disease was observed.
PATHFINDER 2 reported a PPV of 62% and an episode sensitivity of 74% for 12 targeted cancers in an asymptomatic cohort.
Overall detection rate of cancers with current MCED tests is approximately 1 in 200 screened, with >50% of positive tests leading to a cancer diagnosis.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Sensitivity is highly dependent on cancer type and stage; early‑stage tumors shed little cfDNA, limiting detection.
High specificity is essential to avoid excessive false‑positive work‑ups, especially given the low prevalence of cancer in screening populations.
open_questions: How can diagnostic performance metrics be standardized across heterogeneous MCED platforms to enable fair comparisons?
What is the optimal specificity‑sensitivity trade‑off for population‑wide screening versus high‑risk cohorts?
Can stage‑specific sensitivity be reliably measured in prospective screening trials, and how does it translate to mortality benefit?

talk_title: Navigating from Hype to Hope in Multi-Cancer Early Detection

speaker.name: Ruth Etzioni
speaker.affiliation: Fred Hutchinson Cancer Center, Public Health Science Division, Rosalind and Harold Ray Brown Chair

biomarkers.name: Positive predictive value vs sensitivity
biomarkers.finding: PPV reflects proportion of true cancers among positives; sensitivity reflects proportion of cancers detected. Misinterpretation can lead to over‑optimistic claims.

biomarkers.name: Stage‑specific sensitivity
biomarkers.finding: Early‑stage sensitivity is difficult to measure; most available data are overall or late‑stage weighted.

MOA_summary: Ruth dissected the conceptual gap between diagnostic performance and clinical benefit, emphasizing that PPV and sensitivity are often conflated, that early‑stage sensitivity is hard to ascertain, and that down‑staging (stage 3/4 reduction) may not reliably predict mortality benefit without robust modeling of sojourn times and incidence patterns.
key_findings: PPV and sensitivity are distinct metrics; confusion between them fuels hype around MCED performance.
Early‑stage sensitivity cannot be directly measured in most diagnostic studies and must be inferred from stage‑specific incidence data.
Late‑stage (stage 3/4) reduction, as used in the Galleri trial, does not consistently correlate with mortality benefit across cancer types.
Modeling of sojourn times shows that cancers with very short early‑stage windows (e.g., ovarian) may not benefit from high‑sensitivity tests.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: High sensitivity alone may not translate to down‑staging if the early‑stage window is brief.
Variability in stage definitions (clinical vs pathological) complicates interpretation of down‑staging outcomes.
open_questions: What are the most reliable surrogate endpoints for mortality in MCED trials?
How can we standardize early‑stage sensitivity measurement across studies?
What modeling assumptions most critically affect projected mortality benefits from stage‑reduction data?

cited_papers: Etzioni et al. 2024 Nat Rev Cancer
Ruth Etzioni workshop summary (forthcoming ACS report)

talk_title: NCI Director's Address: Funding, Policy, and Future Priorities
MOA_summary: Dr. Lattai outlined the NCI's funding landscape, highlighting a 34% decline in US cancer death rates since 1991, a $128 M budget increase to $7.3 B for FY2026, and a shift to multi‑year and Select Pay funding mechanisms that maintain scientific merit while providing flexibility. He emphasized new priorities such as functional precision medicine, cancer vaccine trials, prevention and screening initiatives, and efforts to accelerate clinical trial activation.
key_findings: US cancer death rates have fallen 34% since 1991, reflecting improved screening, prevention, and therapies.
NCI FY2026 budget increased by $128 M to a total of $7.3 B, representing a record level of extramural funding.
Approximately 200 new competing and 1,500 non‑competing extramural grants have been processed for FY2026.
Projected R01 success rate for FY2026 is ~11%, with a target of ~120 Early Stage Investigator awards.
NCI will fund three new therapeutic cancer vaccine trials over the next three years through a public‑private partnership with FNIH.

talk_title: Stepping Stones program overview and resource support

speaker.name: Morgan O'Hare
speaker.affiliation: National Cancer Institute, Developmental Therapeutics Program

MOA_summary: The Stepping Stones program provides gap‑filling, IND‑enabling services (toxicology, PK, formulation, synthetic route optimization, ADME/DMPK) for NCI‑funded small‑molecule projects, aiming to de‑risk leads and improve eligibility for subsequent funding such as NExT, SBIR, or venture capital.
key_findings: Six rounds of the Stepping Stones program have been completed, supporting 17 distinct projects across various malignancies and a chemotherapy‑induced peripheral neuropathy indication.

Supported activities have included formulation work (e.g., salt screens, oral suspensions), synthetic route scale‑up, mouse and rat PK studies, ADME/DMPK profiling (metabolic stability, CYP, HERG), and biomarker assay development.
Several investigators who received Stepping Stones support subsequently secured SBIR/STTR funding, while others refined chemistry or advanced compounds toward startup formation.

talk_title: Ceramide-based colorectal cancer risk score and DES1 inhibitor studies

speaker.name: Mary Playdon
speaker.affiliation: University of Utah, Huntsman Cancer Institute

targets.dependency_context: obesity mouse models and colorectal cancer risk

biomarkers.name: Ceramide-based colorectal cancer risk score
biomarkers.finding: validated in prospective cohorts and linked to colorectal cancer incidence

models.name: not specified
models.genotype_or_key_features: prospective cohort with BMI and metabolic profiling

MOA_summary: The consortium developed a ceramide-based risk score for colorectal cancer by integrating prospective cohort lipidomics with genetic analyses of ceramide pathway genes. In parallel, DES1 knockout and pharmacologic inhibition of DES1 in obese mouse models reduced ceramide production and delayed colorectal tumor development, supporting a causal role for ceramides in obesity-driven colorectal carcinogenesis.
key_findings: A ceramide-based risk score predicts colorectal cancer risk in prospective human cohorts.
DES1 knockout mice exhibit lower tissue ceramide levels and reduced colorectal tumor burden under high‑fat diet conditions.
A first‑in‑class DES1 small‑molecule inhibitor lowers ceramide production and recapitulates the protective phenotype of the knockout in obese mouse models.

talk_title: AFABP as circulating biomarker and immunotherapy target in high‑risk breast cancer

targets.dependency_context: high‑fat diet‑induced lipid dysregulation in breast cancer

biomarkers.name: Circulating AFABP
biomarkers.finding: elevated levels correlate with high‑risk breast cancer in patient plasma samples

MOA_summary: AFABP (FABP4) mediates fatty‑acid transport and signaling in adipose tissue; its overexpression in obesity creates a lipid‑rich microenvironment that promotes breast tumor growth. Anti‑AFABP antibodies block this signaling, reducing tumor progression in both FABP4‑deficient mice and human breast cancer PDX models, suggesting AFABP as a circulating biomarker and immunotherapy target.
key_findings: Circulating AFABP levels are significantly higher in women with high‑risk breast cancer compared with controls.
Anti‑AFABP antibodies demonstrate therapeutic efficacy, reducing tumor volume by ~45% in PDX models.
Conditional knockout of FABP4 in mice attenuates high‑fat diet‑induced breast tumor promotion.

talk_title: Visceral fat‑induced inflammation inflammatype for colorectal and liver cancer risk

biomarkers.name: Inflammatype (visceral fat inflammation signature)
biomarkers.finding: signature derived from DEXA‑measured visceral fat cohorts correlates with increased incidence of colorectal and liver cancer

models.name: not specified
models.genotype_or_key_features: participants with DEXA visceral adiposity measurements, plasma inflammatory profiling

MOA_summary: Using cohorts with direct visceral adiposity measurements (DEXA) and comprehensive plasma inflammatory profiling, the team identified a multi‑protein “inflammatype” that reflects visceral fat‑driven inflammation. This signature is associated with higher risk of colorectal and liver cancers, providing a blood‑based biomarker to capture obesity‑related inflammatory pathways beyond BMI.
key_findings: An inflammatype derived from visceral fat‑associated proteins predicts colorectal cancer risk (hazard ratio ~1.6 in top quartile).
The same signature is linked to increased liver cancer incidence, independent of BMI.
Visceral fat measured by DEXA correlates more strongly with the inflammatype than BMI (r=0.48 vs 0.22).

talk_title: Biospecimen science funding opportunities and Moonshot Biobank overview

speaker.name: Lokesh Agrawal
speaker.affiliation: Acting Chief, Biorepositories and Biospecimen Research Branch, NCI

models.name: Cancer Moonshot Biobank
models.genotype_or_key_features: pan‑cancer cohort of ~1,300 patients with longitudinal blood and tissue samples; includes solid and hematologic malignancies

clinical_data.population: U01 mechanism grants across cancer types

clinical_data.n: n=1,020 subjects with longitudinal blood or tissue

MOA_summary: Dr. Agrawal described the NCI biospecimen science infrastructure that standardizes pre‑analytical processes, provides curated specimen banks (NCCN, CHTN, Moonshot), and funds multidisciplinary U01 projects to generate reproducible, clinically annotated biospecimens for assay validation across solid and hematologic cancers.
key_findings: The U01 biospecimen science program has funded 24 grants covering a broad range of cancer types.
From 2022‑2024, the CHTN network served 618 unique researchers, distributing 117 specimens and supporting 122 publications.
The Cancer Moonshot Biobank enrolled ~1,300 patients, with 1,020 having longitudinal biospecimens, including ~300 with multiple blood draws and ~150 with paired tissue collections.
Diversity metrics of the Moonshot cohort show 18% Black/African American and 8% Hispanic participants, exceeding typical US trial representation.

talk_title: Acetylcholine signaling by T and B cells modulates tumor and liver regeneration

models.name: partial hepatectomy model
models.genotype_or_key_features: T cell-specific Chat knockout, B cell-specific Chat knockout

MOA_summary: T cell-derived acetylcholine promotes vascular dilation and immune cell trafficking, while B cell-derived acetylcholine is essential for liver regeneration after partial hepatectomy; loss of acetylcholine in T cells reduces tumor growth and alters immune phenotypes.
key_findings: B cell-specific deletion of Chat leads to lethality after 70% partial hepatectomy, indicating essential role in liver regeneration
T cell-specific Chat deletion reduces tumor growth and alters vascular density in mouse models
Serum choline levels correlate with patient prognosis across multiple cancer types
open_questions: How can acetylcholine signaling be targeted in an organ‑specific manner without affecting systemic cholinergic functions?

talk_title: Profiling and targeting innervating peripheral neurons in pancreatic cancer

models.name: SYNERGY trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: locally advanced PDAC, denervation + nab‑paclitaxel + PD‑1 inhibitor

MOA_summary: Fast‑Blue retrograde tracing identifies pancreas‑innervating sympathetic and sensory neurons; denervation with 6‑OHDA or nab‑paclitaxel reduces neuronal density, reprograms CAFs to a pro‑inflammatory state, enhances CD8 T‑cell infiltration, and synergizes with PD‑1 blockade to suppress tumor growth.
key_findings: Fast‑Blue tracing reveals identical numbers of neurons innervating normal pancreas and PDAC, but PDAC shows massive axonal sprouting
Denervation with 6‑OHDA (sympathetic) or nab‑paclitaxel (sensory) individually slows tumor growth; combined denervation nearly abolishes tumor progression in mouse models
Denervation converts immunosuppressive CAFs to pro‑inflammatory phenotype and increases CD8 T‑cell infiltration
Preclinical data support a phase I/II trial (SYNERGY) combining celiac ganglion radiotherapy, nab‑paclitaxel/gemcitabine, and PD‑1 inhibition
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Potential off‑target effects of 6‑OHDA on non‑neuronal catecholaminergic cells

combination_strategies.rationale: Chemotherapy induces peripheral neuropathy that reduces sensory innervation
combination_strategies.phenotype: synergistic tumor growth inhibition when combined with sympathetic denervation

combination_strategies.rationale: Denervation reprograms CAFs and restores T‑cell infiltration, sensitizing tumors to checkpoint blockade
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced anti‑tumor immunity and tumor regression in mouse PDAC

open_questions: What are the long‑term effects of celiac ganglion radiotherapy on autonomic function?
Can specific neuronal subpopulations be targeted without systemic neuropathy?

talk_title: Nociceptor neurons drive CD8 T‑cell exhaustion via CGRP in melanoma

targets.dependency_context: nociceptor‑derived CGRP acting on RAMP1+ CD8 T cells
CD8 T cells in melanoma microenvironment

biomarkers.name: RAMP1 expression
biomarkers.finding: High tumor RAMP1 correlates with poorer overall survival in melanoma patients

models.name: B16‑F10 melanoma model
models.genotype_or_key_features: TRPV1‑Cre DTR for nociceptor ablation; RAMP1‑KO CD8 T cells

models.name: melanoma patient cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: TCGA and single‑cell datasets with RAMP1 expression

MOA_summary: Melanoma activates nociceptor neurons to release CGRP, which binds RAMP1 on CD8 T cells, driving an exhausted phenotype (↑PD‑1, LAG‑3, TIM‑3, ↓IFN‑γ); genetic or pharmacologic blockade of CGRP‑RAMP1 signaling restores T‑cell function and reduces tumor growth.
readouts: RNA‑seq of neurons and T cells
key_findings: Ablation of TRPV1+ nociceptors reduces melanoma growth and increases IFN‑γ+ CD8 T cells in tumors
Co‑culture of DRG neurons with melanoma up‑regulates CGRP expression in neurons
Exogenous CGRP induces PD‑1, LAG‑3, TIM‑3 up‑regulation and IFN‑γ loss in CD8 T cells; effect absent in RAMP1‑KO T cells
RAMP1‑deficient CD8 T cells show reduced exhaustion and slower tumor growth when transferred into melanoma‑bearing mice
High RAMP1 expression in melanoma patients associates with worse survival and reduced response to checkpoint blockade
Retrospective analysis of migraine patients on CGRP antagonists suggests improved outcomes in melanoma with lung metastasis
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: CGRP signaling appears selective for a subset of Nav1.8+ nociceptors (~25% CGRP+)
Potential off‑target effects of systemic CGRP blockade on vascular tone

combination_strategies.rationale: Denervation or CGRP blockade restores T‑cell function, potentially synergizing with checkpoint blockade
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced tumor regression in mouse melanoma

combination_strategies.rationale: Pharmacologic inhibition of CGRP‑RAMP1 axis to prevent T‑cell exhaustion
combination_strategies.phenotype: reduced tumor growth and improved survival in mouse models

open_questions: What upstream tumor factors induce CGRP secretion from nociceptors?
Can CGRP antagonists be safely combined with existing immunotherapies in patients?

talk_title: Engineering biparatopic antibodies for FGFR2 fusion targeting and ADC development

speaker.name: Bill Sellers
speaker.affiliation: Red Ridge Bio (academic affiliation not specified)

models.name: KPC mouse tumor model (FGFR2‑selective small molecule control)

MOA_summary: Biparatopic antibodies BC and BD simultaneously engage two distinct epitopes on the extracellular domain of FGFR2 fusions, increasing avidity, promoting receptor internalization and lysosomal degradation, and retaining activity against kinase‑domain resistance mutations; when conjugated to ADC payloads they induce tumor regression in FGFR2‑amplified models.
key_findings: Biparatopic antibodies BC and BD showed >2‑fold higher solution affinity and avidity compared with parental antibodies.
BC and BD blocked colony formation of FGFR2‑driven BaF3 cells more effectively than parental antibodies.
In FGFR2‑OPTN PDX, BC and BD inhibited tumor growth (~20% regression) whereas small‑molecule FGFR2 inhibitor achieved ~20% regression, demonstrating comparable efficacy.
Tumors treated with BC/BD showed near‑complete loss of surface FGFR2, indicating receptor down‑regulation via internalization.
BC and BD retained internalization and anti‑proliferative activity against FGFR2 kinase‑domain resistance mutants.
ADC conjugates of BC (MMAF, MMAE, deruxtecan) induced tumor regression across breast, colon, and gastric models, with deruxtecan showing the most consistent bystander effect.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: BC and BD internalize and retain activity despite FGFR2 kinase‑domain resistance mutations, suggesting a mechanism independent of kinase inhibition.

combination_strategies.rationale: Potential to overcome resistance by targeting both extracellular degradation and intracellular kinase activity.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor growth inhibition in FGFR2‑driven models.

open_questions: Can biparatopic antibodies be further optimized to achieve tumor regression comparable to small‑molecule inhibitors in all FGFR2‑amplified indications?
What is the clinical relevance of the rapid CDC activity observed in vitro?

talk_title: Zenitatamab (ZAN) bi‑paratopic HER2 antibody: unique CDC activity and clinical benefit

speaker.name: Nina Weiser
speaker.affiliation: Designworks (executive director of multispecific antibody therapeutics)

biomarkers.name: HER2 IHC 3+
biomarkers.finding: Patients must be HER2 IHC 3+ to receive ZAN

models.name: Phase III HORIZON‑GEA101
models.genotype_or_key_features: Advanced HER2‑positive gastric cancer, HER2 IHC 3+

MOA_summary: Zenitatamab binds HER2 ECD2 and ECD4 on adjacent HER2 molecules (trans bi‑paratopic binding), clustering receptors into large caps that trigger rapid complement activation (CDC) and enhanced internalization, leading to superior tumor cell killing compared with the trastuzumab + pertuzumab combination.
readouts: in vivo tumor regression in PDX models
key_findings: Zenitatamab shows 1.5‑fold higher cell‑surface saturation than trastuzumab or pertuzumab alone, indicating trans bi‑paratopic binding.
Super‑resolution microscopy revealed larger HER2 clusters (caps) with ZAN versus microclusters with the antibody combination.
ZAN mediates potent CDC, achieving near‑100% cytotoxicity within 15 min, whereas trastuzumab + pertuzumab show no CDC.
In HER2‑positive biliary tract PDX, ZAN induced complete tumor regression at doses equal or lower than the trastuzumab + pertuzumab combination.
Phase III HORIZON‑GEA101 demonstrated significant PFS (12.4 mo vs 8.0 mo) and a trend toward OS benefit (24.4 mo vs 19.2 mo) for ZAN + chemo, independent of PD‑L1 status.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: ZAN’s CDC activity is rapid and antigen‑dependent, providing a mechanism not achievable by the parental antibody combination.

combination_strategies.rationale: Enhance anti‑tumor efficacy in HER2‑positive solid tumors.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved PFS and OS in gastric cancer trial.

combination_strategies.rationale: Potential immunologic synergy; benefit observed independent of PD‑L1 status.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Further increased PFS (12.4 mo vs 8.1 mo) and OS (26.4 mo vs 19.2 mo) in triplet arm.

open_questions: What is the mechanistic basis for the observed benefit in PD‑L1‑negative patients when combined with PD‑1 blockade?
Can alternative formats (e.g., dual scFv) improve growth‑inhibitory activity without inducing agonism?

talk_title: Two‑plus‑one bispecific platform for EMPP3‑targeted CD3 T‑cell engagers and CD28 costimulation

biomarkers.name: EMPP3 membrane positivity
biomarkers.finding: Patients with any membrane positivity were enrolled; two best responders had 0% IHC but responded, suggesting possible bystander effect

models.name: Phase I EMPP3‑CD3 2+1 study
models.genotype_or_key_features: Refractory clear cell RCC, 100% prior checkpoint inhibitor and VEGF TKI

clinical_data.value: Common AE due to basophil/mast cell depletion

MOA_summary: A 2+1 bispecific format places two EMPP3‑binding VHHs in tandem to achieve avidity‑driven selectivity for high‑density EMPP3 tumors, while a monovalent CD3 arm provides T‑cell activation; co‑targeting CD28 with a separate bispecific further amplifies T‑cell signaling, overcoming exhaustion and enhancing tumor killing.
readouts: Flow cytometry for T‑cell activation markers
key_findings: The 2+1 EMPP3‑CD3 bispecific shows potent cytotoxicity against EMPP3‑high RCC cells while sparing low‑expressing normal cells, demonstrating an avidity‑driven therapeutic window.
In the Phase I study (n=20), 5 patients achieved partial responses and >50% tumor reduction was observed in >50% of patients despite heavy pretreatment.
CRS occurred in 70% of patients but was mostly grade 1‑2; grade 3 occurred in <5% and no grade 4 events were reported.
Two best responders lacked detectable EMPP3 by IHC, suggesting possible bystander killing or assay sensitivity issues.
Combination of EMPP3‑CD3 with EMPP3‑CD28 bispecific restored T‑cell activity in chronic stimulation assays, indicating potential to overcome T‑cell exhaustion.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Avidity‑based selectivity allows discrimination between high‑density tumor antigen and low‑density normal tissue, reducing off‑target cytotoxicity.

combination_strategies.rationale: Provide CD28 costimulatory signal (signal 2) to augment CD3‑mediated T‑cell activation and prevent exhaustion.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Restores tumor killing in chronic T‑cell stimulation assays and improves in vivo efficacy.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target orthogonal pathway in RCC to enhance overall response.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Not yet tested clinically.

open_questions: Will EMPP3 expression alone be sufficient as a predictive biomarker across tumor types?
Can the 2+1 format be generalized to other low‑density antigens without loss of selectivity?
What is the optimal sequencing of CD3 and CD28 bispecifics in patients?

talk_title: Development of NEO811, a molecular glue degrader of ARNT (HIF-1β) for clear cell renal cell carcinoma

biomarkers.name: VEGF expression
biomarkers.finding: NEO811 potently suppresses VEGF expression compared with belzutifan

biomarkers.name: Anemia (RBC count)
biomarkers.finding: Anemia observed as on-target effect, manageable with erythropoietin

clinical_data.value: robust single‑agent regression at low oral doses

MOA_summary: NEO811 is a cereblon‑recruiting molecular glue that induces ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation of the transcription factor ARNT, disrupting the obligate HIF‑α/ARNT dimer and shutting down pan‑HIF transcriptional programs in VHL‑deficient ccRCC, leading to tumor regression even in models resistant to HIF‑2α inhibitors.
key_findings: NEO811 degrades ARNT with >log‑unit selectivity over other NEO substrates in whole‑cell proteomics.
Oral NEO811 induces potent tumor regression in ccRCC xenografts at low doses with no body‑weight loss.
In HIF‑2α G323E mutant and HIF‑2α‑low PDX models, NEO811 retains activity whereas belzutifan loses efficacy.
Systemic ARNT degradation in mice is well tolerated, indicating a potentially safe therapeutic window.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: ARNT is traditionally undruggable; NEO811 achieves degradation via a novel induced degron requiring cereblon.
Potential resistance via mutations that disrupt ternary complex formation not yet observed.

combination_strategies.with_agent: Standard of care HIF‑2α inhibitors (e.g., belzutifan)
combination_strategies.rationale: NEO811 retains activity in HIF‑2α inhibitor‑resistant tumors, offering complementary pathway blockade.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Additive tumor regression in resistant models

open_questions: Will systemic ARNT degradation cause on‑target toxicities in normal tissues beyond anemia?
What is the optimal dosing schedule to maintain target suppression while minimizing hematologic effects?

cited_papers: Nobel Prize work on VHL/HIF pathway

talk_title: AZD8359, a CD8‑biased T‑cell engager targeting STEAP2 for metastatic castration‑resistant prostate cancer

targets.dependency_context: highly expressed on prostate adenocarcinoma cells

biomarkers.name: STEAP2 expression
biomarkers.finding: Virtually all metastatic castration‑resistant prostate lesions (>95%) express STEAP2 homogeneously

biomarkers.name: Cytokine release (CRS)
biomarkers.finding: AZD8359 shows a wider therapeutic index with reduced cytokine release versus conventional TCEs in humanized mice

models.name: intratubal bone metastasis model
models.genotype_or_key_features: STEAP2‑positive prostate tumor in bone niche

clinical_data.value: complete tumor eradication in intratubal model
significantly lower cytokine levels at efficacious doses

MOA_summary: AZD8359 is a bivalent bispecific antibody that binds STEAP2 on prostate tumor cells and a CD8‑binding arm to preferentially engage CD8+ T cells, delivering cytotoxic signals while limiting CD4‑driven cytokine release, thereby achieving potent tumor regression with a wider therapeutic index.
key_findings: STEAP2 is highly and homogeneously expressed in >95% of metastatic castration‑resistant prostate cancer lesions with minimal normal tissue expression.
AZD8359 mediates STEAP2‑dependent tumor cell killing in vitro; killing is abolished in STEAP2‑KO cells.
CD8‑biased format increases potency compared with a non‑CD8‑guided TCE while reducing CD4 activation and cytokine release.
In humanized mouse flank and bone‑metastasis models, AZD8359 achieves complete tumor clearance with no activity from isotype control.
Therapeutic index (cytotoxic vs cytokine release) is markedly expanded versus conventional 2+1 TCEs both in vitro and in vivo.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity relies on STEAP2 expression; tumors lacking STEAP2 are not targeted.
Potential resistance via down‑regulation of STEAP2 not yet observed.

combination_strategies.with_agent: Standard of care androgen deprivation therapy
combination_strategies.rationale: Potential synergistic anti‑tumor activity in prostate cancer
combination_strategies.phenotype: not yet tested

combination_strategies.rationale: Validate CD8 guidance across formats
combination_strategies.phenotype: similar efficacy and reduced cytokine release

open_questions: Will CD8 bias affect long‑term T‑cell memory and durability of response?
Can STEAP2 expression be used as a predictive biomarker across diverse patient populations?

talk_title: TNG961, an oral HBS1L molecular glue degrader for FOCAD‑deleted cancers

biomarkers.name: HBS1L protein level
biomarkers.finding: Dose‑dependent degradation of HBS1L in tumor tissue

biomarkers.name: Pelo destabilization
biomarkers.finding: Secondary loss of PELO observed following HBS1L degradation

biomarkers.name: IFRD1 expression
biomarkers.finding: Induction of IFRD1 only in FOCAD‑negative cells treated with TNG961

clinical_data.value: complete regressions in pancreatic and NSCLC models
all tumors regressed, 6/8 complete regressions with sub‑therapeutic TNG961 + PRMT5 inhibitor
dose‑proportional, 20‑fold above predicted human efficacious dose

MOA_summary: TNG961 is a cereblon‑recruiting molecular glue that induces selective proteasomal degradation of HBS1L, a key component of the HBS1L‑PELO ribosome rescue complex; loss of HBS1L in FOCAD‑deficient tumors triggers ER stress and the unfolded protein response, leading to selective tumor cell death.
key_findings: TNG961 degrades HBS1L with picomolar potency while sparing GSPT1 and other NEO substrates.
In FOCAD‑negative isogenic cell lines, TNG961 shows >100‑fold selectivity for loss of viability compared with FOCAD‑positive counterparts.
Oral dosing leads to dose‑proportional exposure and robust HBS1L degradation in tumor PD tissues.
Complete tumor regressions observed in multiple FOCAD‑deleted CDX/PDX models of pancreatic cancer and NSCLC.
Non‑overlapping sensitivity with MTAP‑cooperative PRMT5 inhibitors; sequential or combined therapy yields enhanced tumor control.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity driven by synthetic lethality of HBS1L loss in FOCAD‑deleted context; other cell lines show minimal sensitivity.
Potential resistance via alternative ribosome rescue pathways not yet explored.

combination_strategies.rationale: Non‑overlapping mechanisms in MTAP‑co‑deleted tumors
combination_strategies.phenotype: sequential treatment leads to tumor regression after PRMT5 resistance

combination_strategies.rationale: Synergistic tumor regression in FOCAD‑/MTAP‑deleted NSCLC
combination_strategies.phenotype: all tumors regressed, 6/8 complete regressions

combination_strategies.rationale: Broad safety margin supports combination
combination_strategies.phenotype: proposed

open_questions: What biomarkers beyond FOCAD loss predict response to HBS1L degradation?
Will long‑term HBS1L degradation affect normal tissues reliant on ribosome rescue?

talk_title: EPI-326, a bispecific EGFR degrader (EpiTac) targeting integrin β6 for EGFR‑driven solid tumors

targets.dependency_context: co‑expressed with EGFR on tumor cells

biomarkers.name: EGFR protein level
biomarkers.finding: ≥90% reduction in tumor EGFR 72 h after a single dose, lasting >7 days

biomarkers.name: Phospho‑EGFR
biomarkers.finding: Greater suppression than blocking antibodies

biomarkers.name: Phospho‑HER3 and c‑Jun
biomarkers.finding: Significant down‑regulation indicating loss of EGFR scaffolding activity

models.name: EGFR mutant NSCLC lines (ex19del, L858R, T790M, C797S)

clinical_data.value: complete responses in mutant EGFR NSCLC models after 3 doses
204 mg/kg every 2 weeks for 3 doses, no EGFR‑related toxicities

MOA_summary: EPI-326 is a bispecific IgG1 antibody that binds EGFR on tumor cells and integrin β6 as a degrading receptor; the ternary complex drives lysosomal internalization and degradation of EGFR, removing both its kinase activity and scaffolding functions while sparing normal tissues lacking integrin β6, resulting in durable tumor regressions.
key_findings: Integrin β6 is highly expressed and co‑localized with EGFR in NSCLC, head‑and‑neck, and CRC, but limited in normal skin and colon.
EPI-326 degrades EGFR across wild‑type, activating, and resistance mutations, achieving complete tumor regressions where blocking antibodies only induce stasis.
Single dose yields >90% EGFR loss lasting >7 days, indicating catalytic degradation.
Preclinical safety in cynomolgus monkeys shows no EGFR‑related toxicities up to 204 mg/kg, supporting a wide therapeutic index.
Combination with osimertinib (NSCLC) or KRAS inhibitor (CRC) yields deeper and more durable responses.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity depends on tumor‑restricted integrin β6 expression; normal tissues lacking β6 are spared.
Potential resistance via loss of integrin β6 expression not yet observed.

combination_strategies.rationale: Combine degradation with kinase inhibition for frontline NSCLC
combination_strategies.phenotype: deeper and longer tumor control

combination_strategies.rationale: Target KRAS‑mutant CRC with EGFR degradation to prevent bypass signaling
