combination_strategies.phenotype: complete responses in PDX models

open_questions: Will integrin β6 expression be sufficient in all EGFR‑driven patient subsets to achieve tumor‑restricted degradation?
What is the long‑term impact of sustained EGFR loss on normal epithelial turnover in tissues with low β6 expression?

talk_title: First-in-class B7-H3 targeted sialidase for boosting innate and adaptive anti-tumor immunity

biomarkers.name: CD163+ M2 macrophage density
biomarkers.finding: Reduced by 80-90% (up to 100% in some biopsies) after treatment

biomarkers.name: Hypersialylation score
biomarkers.finding: Two‑thirds of platinum‑resistant ovarian samples scored high; used for patient selection

models.name: A375 human melanoma in humanized NCG mouse
paired tumor biopsies from first‑gen sialidase trial

MOA_summary: The next‑generation B7‑H3‑targeted sialidase binds tumor cells via a B7‑H3 antibody arm and delivers a high‑Km human sialidase that removes surface sialic acids, exposing neoantigens and reducing CD163+ M2 macrophages, thereby enhancing both innate and adaptive immune responses. The targeting arm adds Fc‑mediated ADCC/ADCP and prolongs desialylation to >7 days, overcoming the short‑lived effect of the first‑gen construct.
readouts: Flow cytometry for immune cell activation
Tumor growth curves in humanized mice
key_findings: Targeted sialidase achieved markedly deeper and more durable tumor desialylation than untargeted enzyme (PNA signal sustained for up to 7 days).
In a humanized A375 xenograft, targeted sialidase outperformed PD‑1 blockade in tumor control.
Paired patient biopsies showed 80‑90% reduction of CD163+ macrophages after treatment.
First‑gen molecule demonstrated safety and proof‑of‑mechanism but only modest clinical activity due to transient desialylation.
B7‑H3 and hypersialylation co‑occur in >50% of ovarian, lung, and prostate cancers, supporting patient selection.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Desialylation on tumors re‑accumulates within 2‑3 days, necessitating enhanced durability.

combination_strategies.rationale: Targeted sialidase acts on a distinct immune evasion axis and may synergize with checkpoint blockade.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Potential additive tumor regression in preclinical models.

open_questions: What is the optimal dosing schedule to maintain desialylation below the immunosuppressive threshold?
Can the platform be adapted to other tumor‑associated antigens beyond B7‑H3?

talk_title: Discovery of ECI‑830, a potent and selective CDK2 inhibitor for HR‑positive breast cancer and CCNE1‑amplified tumors

targets.dependency_context: Cyclin‑E1 amplified or HR‑positive breast cancer cells

biomarkers.name: phospho‑Rb (Ser807/811)
biomarkers.finding: ECI‑830 suppresses p‑Rb with IC50 ≈ 100 nM in cyclin‑E1 amplified cells.

biomarkers.name: E2F target gene expression
biomarkers.finding: Combination with ribociclib further reduces E2F signatures compared with ribociclib alone.

models.name: MCF7 subcutaneous tumor model
models.genotype_or_key_features: ER+, treated with oral BID ECI‑830 ± ribociclib

MOA_summary: ECI‑830 is a type‑I ATP‑competitive inhibitor that binds the CDK2 ATP pocket, exploiting unique lower‑hinge residues (His84, Gln85) to achieve >100‑fold selectivity over CDK1. Inhibition of CDK2 blocks cyclin‑E1‑driven Rb phosphorylation, leading to deeper G1 arrest and enhanced suppression of E2F‑driven transcription, especially when combined with the CDK4/6 inhibitor ribociclib.
key_findings: ECI‑830 shows >100‑fold selectivity for CDK2 over CDK1 and other CDKs.
In cyclin‑E1 amplified models, single‑agent ECI‑830 produces dose‑dependent tumor growth inhibition with corresponding p‑Rb suppression.
Combination of ECI‑830 with ribociclib yields greater tumor regression than ribociclib alone in multiple HR‑positive breast cancer xenografts.
RNA‑seq confirms that the combination more profoundly down‑regulates E2F target pathways than ribociclib monotherapy.
Steady‑state unbound plasma concentrations above the phospho‑Rb AC50 are required for efficacy.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity achieved by engaging CDK2‑specific lower‑hinge residues, minimizing CDK1 off‑target activity.

combination_strategies.rationale: Dual inhibition of CDK4/6 and CDK2 provides deeper cell‑cycle blockade in HR‑positive breast cancer.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced tumor growth inhibition and deeper p‑Rb suppression in xenograft models.

open_questions: Will CDK2 inhibition overcome clinical resistance mechanisms observed with CDK4/6 inhibitors in patients?
What is the therapeutic window of ECI‑830 in humans given CDK1 sparing selectivity?

talk_title: TRI‑611, a CNS‑penetrant ALK molecular glue degrader for ALK‑fusion positive NSCLC

targets.dependency_context: ALK‑fusion positive NSCLC, including TKI‑resistant mutants

biomarkers.name: EML4‑ALK protein level
biomarkers.finding: >90% degradation in tumor tissue after oral dosing.

biomarkers.name: Tumor regression
biomarkers.finding: Dose‑dependent tumor regression in subcutaneous and intracranial xenograft models.

models.name: BAF‑3 engineered with ALK TKI‑resistance mutations
models.genotype_or_key_features: Various known and predicted ALK resistance mutations

models.name: EML4‑ALK subcutaneous NSCLC xenograft
models.genotype_or_key_features: ALK fusion positive, sensitive and lorlatinib‑resistant variants

MOA_summary: TRI‑611 is a cereblon‑recruiting molecular glue that binds a distal site on the ALK kinase domain, inducing a ternary complex with CRBN and a novel helical degron on ALK. This leads to poly‑ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation of EML4‑ALK, including all tested TKI‑resistance mutations, while sparing other kinases and cereblon neosubstrates.
readouts: Immunohistochemistry for ALK in brain tumors
key_findings: TRI‑611 induces selective degradation of EML4‑ALK with >90% loss in tumor tissue after oral dosing.
Degradation is cereblon‑dependent; CRBN knockout abrogates activity.
Potent anti‑tumor activity against a panel of ALK TKI‑resistance mutations, including double and triple mutants.
In lorlatinib‑resistant xenografts, TRI‑611 alone drives tumor regression, whereas lorlatinib is ineffective.
Oral administration achieves brain concentrations comparable to lorlatinib and regresses intracranial ALK‑positive tumors.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: TRI‑611 retains activity against all tested ALK kinase domain resistance mutations due to distal binding site.

combination_strategies.rationale: Orthogonal mechanisms (degradation vs kinase inhibition) may produce synergistic tumor regression.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Sub‑optimal doses of each agent alone show modest inhibition; combination yields robust tumor regression in both subcutaneous and intracranial models.

open_questions: Will CNS exposure and degradation efficacy be maintained in patients with brain metastases?
What is the safety profile of chronic cereblon recruitment in humans?

talk_title: Discovery and development of a CD30 dual‑payload ADC combining MMAE and TOP1 inhibitor

biomarkers.name: CD30 expression level
biomarkers.finding: High expression (>10,000 copies/cell) correlates with ADC internalization.

biomarkers.name: P‑gp (MDR1) expression
biomarkers.finding: P‑gp overexpression abrogates activity of MMAE‑only ADC but not the dual‑payload ADC.

models.name: ALCL line engineered to overexpress P‑gp
Intracranial ALK‑positive NSCLC model (for brain penetration test)

MOA_summary: The ADC uses a site‑specific non‑natural amino acid to attach two MMAE molecules (DAR2) and native cysteines to attach eight topoisomerase‑I inhibitor molecules (DAR8). CD30 binding triggers internalization; the MMAE component kills microtubules while the TOP1 inhibitor induces DNA damage, providing complementary mechanisms that overcome P‑gp‑mediated resistance and enable robust tumor regression at sub‑optimal doses.
readouts: In‑vitro cytotoxicity (IC50) in sensitive and P‑gp‑overexpressing lines
Tumor volume measurements in xenograft models
key_findings: Dual‑payload ADC achieves tumor regression in both parental and P‑gp‑overexpressing ALCL models, whereas MMAE‑only ADC fails in the resistant line.
At a sub‑optimal dose (0.6 mg/kg), the dual‑payload ADC induces robust tumor regression, while single‑payload ADCs show minimal effect.
Rat tolerability study shows MTD of 60 mg/kg for the dual‑payload ADC, substantially higher than brentuximab vedotin (10 mg/kg).
Pharmacokinetics reveal a longer half‑life and stable linker‑payload integrity compared with conventional stochastic conjugates.
Preliminary non‑human primate data (not disclosed) indicate favorable safety at clinically relevant exposures.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Dual payload overcomes P‑gp mediated efflux that limits MMAE activity.

combination_strategies.rationale: Potential additive cytotoxicity in CD30‑positive lymphomas.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Not yet tested; planned for future studies.

open_questions: Will the high MTD translate to an improved therapeutic index in humans?
Can the non‑natural amino acid platform be adapted to other antibody‑payload combinations?

talk_title: First-in-class oral macrocycle Cyclin AB RXL inhibitor CID-078

biomarkers.name: RB loss (biallelic)
biomarkers.finding: RB loss correlates with increased sensitivity to CID-078 in cell line screens

biomarkers.name: E2F target gene expression
biomarkers.finding: High E2F signature predicts sensitivity to CID-078

clinical_data.population: solid tumor patients with RB loss or E2F high

MOA_summary: CID-078 is an oral macrocyclic inhibitor that binds the hydrophobic RXL pocket of cyclin A and cyclin B, preventing their interaction with E2F and Mit1, respectively. This sustains E2F activity and forces cyclin B‑Cdk complexes into premature mitosis, causing replication stress, mitotic catastrophe and apoptosis preferentially in RB‑deficient or E2F‑high tumor cells.
readouts: in vivo tumor regression in PDX models
key_findings: CID-078 achieves sub‑nanomolar potency against cyclin A/B in biochemical assays.
Oral bioavailability improved from 2% to 25% in preclinical species.
RB loss or biallelic RB1 inactivation correlates with >2‑fold increased sensitivity in cell line screens.
E2F‑high transcriptional signature predicts sensitivity across a broad panel of cancer cell lines.
In vivo, CID-078 induces tumor regression in RB‑deficient PDX models, with responses correlating with E2F expression.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity for cyclin A/B over cyclin E is driven by subtle differences in the hydrophobic pocket residues.
Some RB‑wildtype tumors still respond, indicating additional unknown drivers of E2F activation.

combination_strategies.rationale: CID-078 acts downstream of CDK4/6 and may overcome resistance due to RB loss.
combination_strategies.phenotype: expected synergistic tumor regression in CDK4/6‑resistant models

open_questions: What additional molecular determinants beyond RB loss drive sensitivity to CID-078?
Can biomarkers of E2F activity be standardized for patient selection?

talk_title: Oral BRD9 degrader AMX883 for AML

targets.dependency_context: AML blasts with high BRD9 expression

biomarkers.name: BRD9 protein level
biomarkers.finding: AMX883 induces rapid degradation of BRD9 in AML cell lines

MOA_summary: AMX883 is an oral, covalent reversible PROTAC that binds BRD9 and recruits the E3 ligase DCAF16 via a cyanoacrylamide warhead, forming a ternary complex that leads to ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation of BRD9, thereby releasing the differentiation block in AML blasts and inducing myeloid differentiation and apoptosis.
key_findings: AMX883 degrades BRD9 with DC90 of 0.3 nM and high selectivity over other bromodomain proteins.
BRD9 degradation induces myeloid differentiation markers (CD11B, CD86) in AML cell lines.
In MV411 xenografts, AMX883 achieves sustained BRD9 degradation beyond plasma PK.
In two AML PDX models, AMX883 monotherapy leads to complete tumor regression and significant survival benefit versus venetoclax or vehicle.
Combination of AMX883 with venetoclax prevents emergence of venetoclax resistance and shows synergistic tumor control in vivo.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: BRD9 is the sole degraded protein; no off‑target degradation observed.
Resistance to venetoclax (MCL1 up‑regulation) is not observed when combined with AMX883.

combination_strategies.rationale: BRD9 degradation may prevent venetoclax resistance mechanisms (MCL1 up‑regulation).
combination_strategies.phenotype: synergistic tumor regression and prevention of resistance in MV411 model

open_questions: Will BRD9 degradation affect normal hematopoiesis in patients?
What is the safety margin for BRD9 inhibition in humans?

talk_title: ENPP3‑targeted ADC JNJ‑2175 for solid tumors

speaker.name: Smruthi Vijayaraghavan
speaker.affiliation: Johnson & Johnson, Director and Head of Solid Tumor Oncology Discovery

biomarkers.name: ENPP3 IHC positivity
biomarkers.finding: 93% of clear cell RCC, 78% papillary RCC, ~45‑50% of CRC, LUAD, endometrial cancers are ENPP3 positive

clinical_data.population: ENPP3‑positive NSCLC and RCC PDX models

MOA_summary: JNJ‑2175 is a site‑specifically conjugated ADC comprising a high‑affinity ENPP3 antibody linked via a branched linker to the AFHPA auristatin‑derived payload; upon internalization in ENPP3‑positive tumor cells, the payload is metabolized to a non‑bystander auristatin, delivering potent microtubule inhibition while controlled bystander activity is achieved through the initial cell‑permeable intermediate.
readouts: in vivo tumor growth inhibition in PDX models
key_findings: JNJ‑2175 binds dose‑dependently to ENPP3‑positive cells with no off‑target binding to ENPP1/2.
Rapid internalization observed in ENPP3‑positive cell lines.
Potent cytotoxicity against ENPP3‑positive lines; no activity in ENPP3‑negative or knockout cells.
Co‑culture assays demonstrate bystander killing of ENPP3‑negative cells only when mixed with ENPP3‑positive cells.
In three ENPP3‑positive PDX models (NSCLC, RCC), JNJ‑2175 produces dose‑dependent tumor regressions with complete responses.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity confirmed by lack of binding to ENPP1/2 and ENPP3‑negative cells.
No observed toxicity in non‑human primate IND‑enabling studies.
open_questions: Will the controlled bystander effect translate to improved efficacy in heterogeneous tumors?
What is the therapeutic window for ENPP3‑positive normal tissues with apical restriction?

talk_title: Dual Vbeta6/Vbeta10 T‑cell agonist IPN‑1203

speaker.name: Stefan Pleiss
speaker.affiliation: Ipsen Biopharmaceuticals, Senior Director and Head of Translational Science Leads in Oncology

targets.dependency_context: Vbeta6/Vbeta10 expressing T cells (~11% of TILs)

biomarkers.name: Vbeta6+ T cell frequency
biomarkers.finding: 19‑fold expansion at 0.25 mg/kg in monkeys

biomarkers.name: CD8 central memory phenotype (CD27+CD45RA‑CCR7+)
biomarkers.finding: Preferential expansion of central memory‑like CD8 T cells

MOA_summary: IPN‑1203 is a trispecific bispecific construct that simultaneously binds Vbeta6 and Vbeta10 TCR domains on a subset of T cells and delivers a wild‑type IL‑15/IL‑15Rα fusion, providing potent, selective activation and proliferation of these T cells with enhanced STAT5 signaling, leading to central‑memory differentiation, reduced exhaustion, and durable antitumor immunity.
key_findings: IPN‑1203 binds selectively to ~6% of peripheral T cells (Vbeta6/Vbeta10) with high affinity (1.9 nM).
Rapid STAT5 activation via IL‑15 component without direct TCR phosphorylation.
Five‑day PBMC cultures show >50‑fold expansion of Vbeta‑positive T cells (EC50 ~50 pM).
In mouse surrogate model, IPN‑1203 induces complete tumor regressions dependent on CD8 and Vbeta13 T cells.
Single‑cell RNA‑seq reveals >5‑fold increase in central memory CD8 T cells and >50% reduction in exhausted T cells.
In cynomolgus monkeys, a single 0.25 mg/kg dose yields a 19‑fold expansion of Vbeta6+ T cells with minimal cytokine release.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity limited to Vbeta6/Vbeta10 subsets avoids broad T‑cell activation and cytokine release.
No NK cell expansion observed despite IL‑15 component, indicating T‑cell‑biased activation.

combination_strategies.with_agent: Standard AML or solid tumor therapies
combination_strategies.rationale: Selective T‑cell activation may synergize with checkpoint blockade or chemotherapy.
combination_strategies.phenotype: proposed enhanced antitumor immunity

open_questions: Will selective Vbeta activation be sufficient in tumors with low Vbeta6/Vbeta10 infiltration?
Long‑term safety of chronic IL‑15 signaling in humans remains to be defined.

talk_title: Safety and efficacy of elacrasib (D-3S001) in KRAS G12C mutant NSCLC

biomarkers.name: KRAS G12C ctDNA allele fraction
biomarkers.finding: 87% of evaluable patients achieved >50% clearance; 73% achieved 100% clearance

biomarkers.name: SK11 or KEAP1 alteration
biomarkers.finding: ORR 76% without alteration vs 55% with alteration

models.name: Phase I/II elacrasib study
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS G12C mutant NSCLC, both inhibitor‑naïve and refractory

MOA_summary: Elacrasib is a CNS‑penetrant, GDP‑bound KRAS G12C covalent inhibitor that achieves sustained target engagement, depleting active KRAS even under growth‑factor stimulation, leading to tumor shrinkage and durable responses in both G12C‑inhibitor‑naïve and refractory NSCLC.
key_findings: Elacrasib achieved an overall ORR of 58.8% in KRAS G12C NSCLC (phase I/II).
In G12C‑inhibitor‑naïve patients, ORR was 53.6% with a median PFS of 9.4 months.
In G12C‑inhibitor‑refractory patients, ORR was 32.3% with a median PFS of 8.1 months.
Patients with KRAS amplification (n=5) showed an ORR of 60%.
Rapid and deep ctDNA KRAS G12C allele fraction clearance correlated with radiologic response.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: KRAS amplification observed as a resistance mechanism; elacrasib showed activity despite amplification.
SK11/KEAP1 co‑mutations reduced ORR but efficacy remained notable.

combination_strategies.rationale: potential synergistic immune activation in frontline setting
combination_strategies.phenotype: planned pivotal trial of monotherapy plus anti‑PD‑1

open_questions: Will earlier use of elacrasib overcome primary resistance mechanisms seen with earlier G12C inhibitors?
What is the optimal sequencing with immunotherapy in KRAS G12C NSCLC?

talk_title: Contextualizing KRAS G12C inhibitor landscape and future directions
MOA_summary: The speaker reviewed the mechanisms of KRAS G12C off‑inhibitors (covalent binding to GDP‑bound KRAS) and emerging on‑state tri‑complex inhibitors, discussing potency, resistance mechanisms, and toxicity profiles.
key_findings: Sotorasib demonstrated a 28.1% ORR in CodeBreaK‑200 but did not improve OS.
Divorasib showed a 53.4% ORR and median PFS of 13.1 months in early‑phase data.
Autoimmune hepatitis is a class‑specific toxicity, especially after prior PD‑1 therapy.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Resistance mechanisms include KRAS amplification, alternative KRAS variants, and bypass pathway activation.

combination_strategies.rationale: potential synergistic immune activation
combination_strategies.phenotype: response rate 87% in PD‑L1 high patients with calderacib + pembrolizumab

combination_strategies.rationale: target downstream MAPK reactivation
combination_strategies.phenotype: modest activity observed

open_questions: Do newer on‑state KRAS inhibitors overcome resistance seen with off‑state agents?
How can autoimmune hepatitis be mitigated in combination regimens?

talk_title: Preliminary safety and activity of zoldonrasib (RMC‑9805) in KRAS G12D NSCLC

speaker.name: Jonathan Rees
speaker.affiliation: University of California Davis Comprehensive Cancer Center

biomarkers.name: KRAS G12D ctDNA VAF
biomarkers.finding: 87% of evaluable patients achieved >50% clearance; 73% achieved 100% clearance

models.name: RMC-9805-001 Phase I
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS G12D mutant NSCLC, post‑IO and platinum

clinical_data.population: KRAS G12D NSCLC, prior IO+platinum, no docetaxel

MOA_summary: Zoldonrasib is an oral mutant‑selective covalent inhibitor that forms a cyclophilin A‑mediated tri‑complex with the GTP‑bound KRAS G12D, sterically blocking RAF binding and shutting down downstream MAPK signaling, leading to tumor regressions.
key_findings: Confirmed ORR of 52% in KRAS G12D NSCLC patients previously treated with IO and platinum.
Disease control rate reached 93% with median time to response of 1.4 months.
Median PFS was 11.1 months; 12‑month OS rate 73% with median follow‑up 13.1 months.
High ctDNA clearance: 87% >50% reduction, 73% complete clearance.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: No grade 4/5 treatment‑related AEs; safety profile favorable.

combination_strategies.rationale: potential to overcome resistance and broaden RAS coverage
combination_strategies.phenotype: planned combination studies

combination_strategies.rationale: standard post‑IO backbone
combination_strategies.phenotype: planned combination

open_questions: Does ctDNA persistence predict earlier progression?
How does smoking status affect response to zoldonrasib?

talk_title: Discussion of KRAS G12C and G12D inhibitor landscape
MOA_summary: The discussant highlighted molecular heterogeneity of KRAS G12D across tumor types, the impact of co‑mutations and smoking status on immunogenicity, and the rationale for combining KRAS G12D inhibitors with immunotherapy or other targeted agents.
key_findings: KRAS G12D NSCLC is enriched in never‑smokers and shows lower PD‑L1 expression.
Co‑mutations (STK11, KEAP1) may influence response to KRAS inhibitors.
Preclinical models suggest KRAS G12D inhibition can increase T‑cell infiltration and synergize with checkpoint blockade.

combination_strategies.rationale: KRAS G12D inhibition may enhance tumor immunogenicity
combination_strategies.phenotype: potential synergy in preclinical models

open_questions: Do never‑smoker vs smoker subgroups differ in response to KRAS G12D inhibitors?
Can ctDNA dynamics be used to guide escalation of combination therapy?

talk_title: Phase I trial of WEE1 inhibitor zetaracib + PKMYT1 inhibitor lonresorib in CCNE1/FBXW7/PPP2R1A altered tumors

biomarkers.name: CCNE1 amplification
biomarkers.finding: 56% of patients; enriched response in ovarian cohort

biomarkers.name: FBXW7 mutation
biomarkers.finding: 39% of patients; responses observed

models.name: Phase I dose‑escalation of zetaracib + lonresorib
models.genotype_or_key_features: advanced solid tumors with CCNE1 amp, FBXW7 or PPP2R1A alterations

MOA_summary: Zetaracib inhibits WEE1, preventing CDK1 Y15 phosphorylation, while lonresorib blocks PKMYT1‑mediated CDK1 T14 phosphorylation; concurrent loss of both checkpoints forces replication‑stressed tumor cells into premature mitosis, causing mitotic catastrophe.
key_findings: Overall response rate of 37.5% in heavily pre‑treated ovarian cancer, rising to 50% at RP2D.
Disease control rate of 68.5% across all tumor types.
High ctDNA molecular response (47% overall, 67% in ovarian cohort).
Combination was well tolerated with mainly grade 1‑2 nausea, fatigue, anemia.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: No significant myelosuppression observed despite cell‑cycle targeting.

combination_strategies.rationale: reduce low‑grade toxicities while maintaining efficacy
combination_strategies.phenotype: maintained tumor regressions

open_questions: Will intermittent dosing improve tolerability without compromising efficacy?
Can biomarkers beyond CCNE1/FBXW7 refine patient selection?

talk_title: Discussion of synthetic lethal and replication stress strategies
MOA_summary: The discussant contextualized the WEE1/PKMYT1 combination within broader synthetic lethal approaches targeting replication stress, emphasizing biomarker challenges and the need for tolerable regimens.
key_findings: Emphasized importance of RB loss detection (IHC) for cyclin AB inhibitor selection.
Highlighted that low‑grade toxicities may be more clinically relevant than grade 3/4 events.
open_questions: How to best define RB dysfunction for patient selection?
What is the optimal sequencing of cell‑cycle inhibitors with chemotherapy or ADCs?

talk_title: Phase I evaluation of CID‑078, a cyclin A/B RxL inhibitor, in advanced solid tumors

speaker.name: Afshin Delati
speaker.affiliation: University Hospital Seidman Cancer Center, Case Western Reserve University

biomarkers.name: RB1 loss
biomarkers.finding: 57% of enrolled patients had RB1 alterations

models.name: CID-078 Phase I dose escalation
models.genotype_or_key_features: advanced solid tumors, RB1 or CDKN2A loss

MOA_summary: CID‑078 is a macrocyclic oral agent that binds the RxL motif on cyclin A and B, blocking cyclin‑E2F and cyclin‑MYT1 interactions, causing unchecked CDK activity, DNA damage accumulation, spindle checkpoint activation and mitotic catastrophe in RB‑deficient tumors.
key_findings: Linear PK from 40‑960 mg BID with ~3 h half‑life and no accumulation.
Grade 3 ALT elevations were rare (2 patients) and reversible.
Confirmed tumor responses observed in RB‑loss osteosarcoma and neuroendocrine tumor.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity appears linked to RB pathway loss; normal cells tolerate cyclin A/E2F blockade.

combination_strategies.rationale: non‑overlapping toxicity profile
combination_strategies.phenotype: potential additive efficacy

open_questions: What is the optimal biomarker (RB IHC vs genomic) for patient selection?
Will higher doses improve efficacy without new toxicities?

talk_title: Cyclin AB inhibition: the next mile in cell cycle therapy
MOA_summary: The discussant reviewed the mechanistic rationale for targeting the cyclin A/B RxL interface, emphasizing synthetic lethality in RB‑deficient, E2F‑high tumors and the need for biomarker‑driven patient selection.
key_findings: CID‑078 blocks cyclin‑A/E2F and cyclin‑B/MYT1 interactions, leading to mitotic catastrophe.
RB loss can be detected by IHC, offering a practical selection method.

combination_strategies.rationale: CID‑078 lacks hematologic toxicity, enabling safe combos
combination_strategies.phenotype: potential synergistic efficacy

open_questions: What pharmacodynamic markers will confirm target engagement?
Which tumor types will benefit most from cyclin AB inhibition?

talk_title: Neural representation learning for pan‑cancer HRD prediction and platinum response
MOA_summary: Pitt presented HR‑Difuse, a multi‑view neural representation learning framework that encodes SBS, indel, SV, and copy‑number features from whole‑genome sequencing, fuses them via self‑attention, and uses contrastive learning to predict homologous recombination deficiency (HRD) and platinum response, outperforming HRDetect and random‑forest‑based Cord across pan‑cancer cohorts.
key_findings: HR‑Difuse achieved higher AUROC and AUPRC than HRDetect and Cord for HRD prediction in TCGA and PEACOG datasets.
The model retained predictive power for somatic HRD events lacking high microhomology‑mediated deletions, where existing tools performed poorly.
Transfer‑learning fine‑tuning of the HRD model on platinum‑response labels yielded an F1 score of ~0.75, surpassing baseline HR‑Difuse and other classifiers.
Contrastive learning of latent encodings improved cross‑view feature integration compared with pure NMF‑based signatures.

talk_title: Dimensional structural chromosome instability and the JAVEL mass‑balance method
MOA_summary: Imielinski described JAVEL, a mass‑balance‑principle algorithm that jointly infers copy‑number segments and rearrangement junctions from whole‑genome sequencing, leveraging stoichiometric constraints to produce coherent structural chromosome instability profiles across cancer types.
key_findings: JAVEL integrates copy‑number and structural‑variant data into a unified representation based on segment neighbor balance.
The method enables detection of complex events such as chromothripsis, chromoplexy, and retrotransposition with improved resolution over separate CNV or SV callers.
Application to TCGA whole‑genome data reveals pervasive multi‑scale chromosomal instability signatures across tumor types.

talk_title: Graph-based genome analysis reveals structural chromosomal instability patterns
MOA_summary: The presenter introduced JAVEL, a graph‑based copy‑number and breakpoint detection tool, and demonstrated how genome graphs can be mined for structural chromosomal instability (SIN) patterns such as chromoplexy, chromothripsis, ecDNA, BFB cycles and a novel class called Typhonis. Specific graph motifs correlate with DNA repair deficiencies (e.g., BRCA1 loss) and with oncogene amplification contexts (e.g., HER2 HSR vs ecDNA).
key_findings: JAVEL outperforms prior methods in breakpoint precision and recall on TCGA genomes.
Identified a new structural scar highly specific for BRCA1 deficiency, present in >90% of BRCA1‑deficient tumors and rare elsewhere.
Discovered Typhonis, a distinct HSR amplification pattern affecting ~3% of cancers and enriched for MYC, CCND1, ERBB2 amplifications.
Found that glioblastoma is dominated by ecDNA amplifications of EGFR/PDGFRA, whereas most other cancers show HSR‑type amplifications.
HSR HER2 amplifications predict lower likelihood of resistance to HER2‑targeted ADCs compared with ecDNA‑based HER2 amplifications.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: HSR‑mediated HER2 amplifications may be less prone to resistance than ecDNA‑mediated amplifications.
open_questions: What molecular mechanisms drive conversion of ecDNA to HSR versus retention as ecDNA?
Can long‑read or Hi‑C sequencing reliably distinguish reintegrated ecDNA from primary HSR events?

talk_title: Comprehensive analysis of >8,000 TCGA whole‑genome sequences reveals new coding, non‑coding and structural drivers
MOA_summary: Using dual cloud pipelines, the team generated a high‑quality variant call set from >8,000 TCGA whole‑genome samples, uncovering sequencing artifacts (vapor‑trail T>G/A>C), correcting coverage gaps, and identifying novel coding and non‑coding driver mutations, structural variation drivers, and HRD signatures across cancer types.
key_findings: Identified a novel NovaSeq‑specific sequencing artifact (T>G/A>C “vapor trail”) and created a filter that removed up to 15% of false SNVs in affected tumor types.
PCR‑free whole‑genome data recovered >90% of missed TERT promoter mutations and uncovered new hotspots in GC‑rich regions of VHL, ARID1A and other tumor suppressors.
Detected 200 million SNVs, 1.7 million DNVs, 56 million indels and 1.3 million SVs across the cohort.
Discovered new non‑coding drivers (e.g., recurrent promoter indels in albumin, surfactant genes, P63, GATA3, XBP1, ESR1 regulatory region amplifications).
SVelfi analysis revealed known SV drivers (TMPRSS2‑ERG) and novel recurrent SVs such as SLC30A4 translocations driving ETV1 overexpression in prostate cancer.
HRD signatures were present in 7% of all tumors, including 3.3% outside ovarian and breast cancers, with some cases lacking known BRCA1/2 alterations.
Approximately 10% of samples harbored pathogenic germline variants; a novel intronic BRCA1 splice variant showed LOH and reduced expression, indicating functional HRD.
open_questions: What is the functional impact of the newly identified non‑coding driver mutations on tumor biology?
How do the novel SV drivers (e.g., SLC30A4‑ETV1) influence therapeutic vulnerabilities?
Can the vapor‑trail filter be generalized to other sequencing platforms?

talk_title: Spatial metabolomics of pancreas and breast cancer reveals cell-type specific lipid and nutrient utilization

speaker.name: Josh Rabinowitz
speaker.affiliation: Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Department of Chemistry

models.name: human breast cancer and pancreatic cancer tissue sections
models.genotype_or_key_features: hormone receptor positive HER2 negative breast cancer; pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma

MOA_summary: Using MALDI imaging mass spectrometry, Rabinowitz mapped lipid species that demarcate epithelial cancer cells versus fibroblasts and immune cells, and showed spatial depletion of glucose and enrichment of ATP/ADP in cancer and immune compartments, highlighting metabolic heterogeneity and suggesting ketogenic diet–induced glucose restriction could stress tumor cells while supporting T cell ketone utilization.
readouts: metabolite imaging (glucose, glucose‑phosphate, ATP, ADP, sialic acid)
key_findings: Distinct phosphatidylethanolamine lipids (PE 18:1/18:1 vs PE with saturated/omega‑6 tail) specifically mark breast cancer epithelium versus fibroblasts.
Glucose is depleted and ATP/ADP is enriched in cancer cells and immune infiltrates across breast and pancreatic tumors.
Lactate tracing in mouse tumors shows universal lactate utilization across tumor cell types, contradicting prior reports of Treg‑specific lactate use.
CD8+ T cells preferentially utilize 3‑hydroxybutyrate, suggesting ketogenic diet may boost antitumor immunity.
A small phase I/II trial of a supervised ketogenic diet combined with standard chemotherapy in metastatic pancreatic cancer showed ~3‑month improvement in progression‑free and overall survival.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Technical artifact: glucose‑phosphate signal can arise from phospholipid fragmentation, not true glycolytic intermediate.

combination_strategies.rationale: Enhance metabolic stress by combining glucose restriction with PI3K pathway blockade.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Profound slowing of pancreatic tumor growth in mice.

combination_strategies.rationale: Combine ketogenic diet–induced glucose restriction with standard chemotherapy.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Positive tumor growth inhibition in mouse models and modest survival benefit in early clinical trial.

open_questions: Can higher‑resolution MALDI imaging reliably detect low‑molecular‑weight metabolites like lactate and 3‑hydroxybutyrate?
What is the mechanistic link between sialic acid accumulation and immune exclusion in pancreatic tumors?

talk_title: Mitochondrial NADK2 supports proline synthesis, protein lipoylation, and mitochondrial translation

speaker.name: Gerta Hoxha
speaker.affiliation: UT Southwestern, Department of Molecular Biology

MOA_summary: NADK2 generates mitochondrial NADPH, which fuels proline biosynthesis and mitochondrial fatty‑acid synthesis; the latter supplies acyl‑carrier protein lipoylation required for PDH and other dehydrogenase complexes, supporting TCA flux, oxidative phosphorylation, and mitochondrial translation, thereby promoting tumor cell proliferation.
readouts: mass spectrometry of ACP‑bound acyl chains
key_findings: NADK2 loss impairs lung cancer cell proliferation in vitro and tumor growth in vivo.
Supplementation with non‑essential amino acids, especially proline, fully rescues proliferation of NADK2‑deficient cells.
NADK2 knockout abolishes de novo proline synthesis, linking mitochondrial NADPH to proline biosynthesis.
Mitochondrial NADK2 is required for protein lipoylation of PDH, OGDH, GCS, and BCKDH complexes; kinase‑dead NADK2 fails to restore lipoylation.
Restoring mitochondrial NADPH via cytosolic NADK targeted to mitochondria rescues lipoylation, TCA cycle intermediates, and oxidative respiration.
NADK2 supports mitochondrial fatty‑acid synthesis, as shown by reduced ACP‑bound acyl chains in NADK2‑deficient cells.
Loss of NADK2 diminishes mitochondrial translation and ETC complex assembly, leading to decreased OCR.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: NADK2 amplification correlates with KRAS/TP53 mutant lung adenocarcinoma, suggesting a selective dependency.

combination_strategies.rationale: Restore pyruvate oxidation downstream of NADK2 loss.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Partial rescue of proliferation in NADK2‑deficient cells.

combination_strategies.rationale: Bypass the need for NADK2‑dependent proline synthesis.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Complete rescue of growth in vitro.

open_questions: How does mitochondrial fatty‑acid synthesis mechanistically regulate protein lipoylation?
What is the impact of NADK2 inhibition on immune cell metabolism within the tumor microenvironment?
Can NADK2 amplification serve as a predictive biomarker for response to agents targeting proline metabolism or lipoylation pathways?

patent_or_ip_hints: US20250012345A1 – methods targeting mitochondrial NADK2 for cancer therapy

talk_title: Methodological advances reveal tissue‑specific nutrient environments and folate‑limited nucleotide metabolism in cancer

speaker.name: Matt Vander Heiden
speaker.affiliation: University of California, San Diego, Department of Medicine

targets.dependency_context: B‑ALL cells in mouse plasma‑like media

biomarkers.name: plasma folate concentration
biomarkers.finding: Folates in the 30‑115 nM range limit nucleotide synthesis and constrain B‑ALL proliferation

models.name: human B‑ALL bone marrow plasma samples
models.genotype_or_key_features: diagnosed B‑cell ALL, varied folate levels

models.name: B‑ALL cell lines
models.genotype_or_key_features: wild‑type, salvage enzyme knockout, de novo synthesis knockout

models.name: Phase I/II ketogenic diet + chemotherapy in metastatic pancreatic cancer
models.genotype_or_key_features: 16 patients per arm, supervised carbohydrate‑restricted diet

clinical_data.population: metastatic pancreatic cancer, first‑line triple chemotherapy

MOA_summary: Vander Heiden’s group quantified tissue‑specific nutrient milieus, revealing that folate scarcity in plasma‑like media limits de novo nucleotide synthesis in B‑ALL, making cells dependent on salvage; dietary folate titration directly modulates leukemia growth, linking systemic nutrient availability to replication stress and tumor progression.
readouts: mass spectrometry–based metabolomics of tissues and plasma
principal component analysis of nutrient profiles
CRISPR knockout of nucleotide synthesis vs salvage enzymes
cell proliferation assays in RPMI vs mouse plasma‑like media
glucose tolerance tests with labeled starch vs free glucose in mice
key_findings: Each mouse tissue exhibits a distinct nutrient composition; blood differs markedly from standard culture media.
Bone‑marrow plasma from ALL patients and mice is low in folates (~30 nM), limiting de novo nucleotide synthesis.
B‑ALL cells grown in mouse plasma‑like media become dependent on nucleotide salvage; removal of exogenous nucleotides impairs growth.
Folates are the primary driver of this phenotype; raising folate levels to physiological (~30 nM) restores proliferation even without salvage.
In vivo folate manipulation in mice alters B‑ALL tumor burden, confirming folate limitation as a growth constraint.
Low folate induces replication stress, which can reprogram gene expression and may affect therapeutic response.
A ketogenic diet in pancreatic cancer patients modestly improves PFS and OS, likely via glucose restriction and increased ketone utilization by T cells.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Folates in standard RPMI are supra‑physiological, potentially masking nucleotide‑limited phenotypes.

combination_strategies.rationale: Rescue nucleotide synthesis in folate‑limited microenvironments.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Restores B‑ALL proliferation in plasma‑like media.

combination_strategies.rationale: Reduce glucose availability to tumor cells while providing ketone bodies for T cells.
combination_strategies.phenotype: ≈3‑month PFS and OS benefit in metastatic pancreatic cancer.

open_questions: How does systemic folate limitation interact with antifolate chemotherapy in solid tumors?
Can precise dietary modulation of tissue nutrients be leveraged to sensitize tumors to metabolic inhibitors?
What are the long‑term effects of chronic ketogenic diet on tumor evolution and immune surveillance?

cited_papers: Keene Abbott et al. 2025 Cell Metabolism

talk_title: Extracellular Vesicles and Particles, Their Biogenesis and Function
MOA_summary: AF6 activation drives the formation of tumor microvesicles (TMVs) by recruiting Exportin‑5, pre‑microRNAs and MT1‑MMP to actin‑myosin contractile sites via a VAMP3‑CD9 complex, resulting in protease‑laden vesicles that promote matrix degradation, invasion and intercellular transfer of oncogenic RNAs and DNA.
key_findings: AF6‑activated tumor cells shed large microvesicles (TMVs) enriched for AF6, VAMP3, CD9 and MT1‑MMP.
TMVs display proteolytic activity on gelatin matrices and promote endothelial tube formation with distinct phenotypes (elongated vs sprouting) depending on tumor origin.
AF6 activation increases pre‑microRNA loading into TMVs via an Exportin‑5‑GRP1 scaffold that preferentially binds GTP‑AF6.
Knockdown of GRP1 reduces pre‑microRNA content in TMVs, demonstrating its scaffolding role.
TMV‑delivered miR‑21 suppresses SMAD7 in recipient fibroblasts; this effect is reversed by miR‑21 or GRP1 knockdown.
TMVs contain double‑stranded DNA packaged through cGAS‑RAN‑GTP‑AF6 endosomal trafficking; DNA removal diminishes TMV‑mediated signaling.
open_questions: What determines selective cargo packaging (DNA‑rich vs protein‑rich) into distinct TMV subpopulations?
Are there unique regulators that distinguish biogenesis of specific large EV subtypes beyond actin‑myosin contraction?

talk_title: Biology and Translation of Large Oncosomes as Cancer Biomarkers

speaker.name: Dolores Di Visio
speaker.affiliation: Los Angeles and Sapienza University, Rome

models.name: aggressive prostate cancer line
models.genotype_or_key_features: large oncosome production, diaphanous‑related formin 3 deficiency

models.name: 4T1.2 orthotopic breast cancer
models.genotype_or_key_features: spontaneous bone metastasis, high large oncosome secretion

models.name: plasma from metastatic prostate cancer patients

MOA_summary: Large oncosomes released by highly aggressive cancer cells carry abundant mitochondrial RNA/proteins, kinases and DNA, which upon uptake by bone‑marrow stromal cells trigger interferon‑driven inflammatory programs, neutrophil recruitment and myeloid‑derived suppressor cell expansion, thereby fostering metastatic niche formation via STING‑dependent signaling.
key_findings: Mice injected with large oncosomes after primary tumor resection develop significantly more bone metastases than those receiving small EVs.
Large oncosomes induce a strong interferon‑stimulated gene signature in bone‑marrow MSCs, driving neutrophil chemotaxis and MDSC‑like polarization (arginase‑1, IL‑10 up‑regulation).
STING inhibition abolishes large‑oncosome‑mediated NF‑κB/IFN reporter activation and blocks induction of IRF3/7 in vivo.
Large oncosomes are enriched for mitochondrial transcripts and proteins; this signature is conserved across cancer types and patient plasma.
Proteomic profiling identified a set of bona‑fide large‑oncosome surface markers (e.g., VDAC1, RP2) enabling magnetic‑bead capture from plasma.
Longitudinal plasma analysis distinguishes castration‑sensitive from castration‑resistant prostate cancer and identifies progression‑associated protein panels.
Septin‑2 knockdown selectively suppresses large oncosome release and prevents the immunosuppressive microenvironment in a breast cancer model.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Large oncosomes display heterogeneous cargo; the extent of mitochondrial enrichment may vary by tumor type, influencing their metastatic potency.

combination_strategies.rationale: Block interferon‑driven niche conditioning by large oncosomes
combination_strategies.phenotype: Complete abrogation of NF‑κB/IFN reporter activation in vitro and reduced metastasis in vivo

combination_strategies.with_agent: magnetic bead capture using VDAC1/RP2 antibodies
combination_strategies.rationale: Enrich large oncosomes from plasma for biomarker analysis
combination_strategies.phenotype: High purity isolation enabling sensitive ddPCR detection of mitochondrial transcripts

open_questions: What molecular mechanisms dictate selective loading of mitochondrial components into large oncosomes?
Can targeted inhibition of large‑oncosome biogenesis (e.g., Septin‑2) be translated into a therapeutic strategy without affecting normal EV pathways?

talk_title: Activated T‑Cell Extracellular Vesicles Deliver DNA to Reprogram Immunity and Tumors

targets.dependency_context: mediates T‑cell EV uptake by dendritic cells and tumor cells
packaged in activated T‑cell EVs to facilitate nuclear entry of EV DNA

models.name: KPC pancreatic cancer orthotopic model
models.genotype_or_key_features: highly metastatic, low baseline T‑cell infiltration

models.name: activated mouse T‑cells (anti‑CD3/CD28 + IL‑2)
models.genotype_or_key_features: high EV‑DNA and granzyme B content

MOA_summary: Activated T‑cell extracellular vesicles (ATEVs) are enriched for extracellular DNA and granzyme B; LFA‑1 mediates their uptake by lymph‑node dendritic cells and tumor cells, where DNA reaches the nucleus, triggers antigen‑presentation gene transcription, and reprograms the tumor microenvironment toward an immune‑active state, synergizing with anti‑PD‑1 therapy to suppress tumor growth.
readouts: fluorescent lipophilic dye labeling of EVs
digital droplet PCR for mitochondrial transcripts
key_findings: Activated T‑cell EVs contain ~80 % surface DNA and 20 % lumen DNA, markedly higher than non‑activated EVs.
LFA‑1 on ATEVs drives preferential uptake by dendritic cells in lymph nodes and by tumor cells in pancreatic and GBM models.
DNA‑depleted ATEVs (DNase‑treated) fail to induce antigen‑presentation gene signatures in recipient lymph‑node cells.
Single‑cell RNA‑seq shows up‑regulation of MHC‑I/II, TAP1/2 and other antigen‑processing genes only after DNA‑competent ATEV exposure.
Combination of ATEVs with anti‑PD‑1 completely arrests tumor growth in KPC pancreatic and GBM mouse models for >30 days, whereas either agent alone is less effective.
Granzyme B packaged in ATEVs facilitates nuclear membrane disruption, allowing EV DNA to access euchromatin and drive transcription without genomic integration.
Short‑term ATEV treatment induces transient immune activation without overt autoimmunity; antigen‑presentation genes return to baseline after ~7 days.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: DNA removal (DNase treatment) abolishes immunogenic reprogramming, indicating DNA is the critical active component.
Blocking LFA‑1 with antibodies prevents EV uptake and downstream immune activation.

combination_strategies.rationale: ATEV‑induced antigen presentation sensitizes tumors to checkpoint blockade
combination_strategies.phenotype: Complete tumor growth arrest in pancreatic and GBM mouse models

combination_strategies.rationale: Dissect contribution of cytosolic DNA sensing versus nuclear transcription
combination_strategies.phenotype: Minor reduction in non‑activated EV‑mediated signaling; primary effect remains DNA‑driven nuclear transcription

open_questions: What are the precise molecular determinants governing DNA packaging into activated T‑cell EVs?
Can the ATEV platform be scaled and standardized for clinical-grade manufacturing?
What is the long‑term safety profile of repeated ATEV administration regarding potential autoimmunity?

talk_title: Integrin αvβ6-targeted theranostic peptide and lutetium therapy in pancreatic and other cancers

speaker.name: Julie Sutcliffe
speaker.affiliation: University of California, Davis; Founder and CEO of Luminance Biosciences

targets.dependency_context: overexpressed in many solid tumors including pancreatic cancer
nanomolar affinity, internalized in αvβ6‑positive tumors

biomarkers.name: CA19-9
biomarkers.finding: CA19-9 levels dropped in patient after single Lu‑177 dose

models.name: Phase I αvβ6 theranostic trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: patients with locally advanced/metastatic pancreatic cancer, progressed on prior chemotherapy

MOA_summary: The αvβ6‑targeting peptide (A20FMDV2) is radiolabeled with F‑18 for PET imaging and later re‑engineered with a DOTA chelator and albumin‑binding moiety to create a Ga‑68 diagnostic and Lu‑177 therapeutic pair that binds with nanomolar affinity, is rapidly internalized, and delivers tumor‑specific radiation while being cleared renally.
key_findings: Developed a first‑in‑human αvβ6 theranostic pair (Ga‑68 diagnostic / Lu‑177 therapeutic) after a decade of peptide optimization.
Phase I dose escalation reached 200 mCi with no dose‑limiting toxicities and acceptable renal clearance.
Imaging detected lesions missed by standard FDG‑PET, including small brain metastases, demonstrating high tumor‑to‑background contrast.
A single Lu‑177 dose produced a 40% SUV reduction and CA19‑9 decline in a heavily pre‑treated pancreatic cancer patient without tumor growth over 90 days.
The agent showed consistent tumor retention over days on SPECT, delivering 15–40 Gy per lesion at the top dose.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Renal clearance is the dose‑limiting organ; amino‑acid co‑infusion used to protect kidneys.
Variable GI activity observed across patients, but therapeutic Lu‑177 showed minimal GI binding.

combination_strategies.rationale: Potential radiosensitization of DNA damage induced by Lu‑177
combination_strategies.phenotype: preclinical synergy reported in manuscript under review

open_questions: Can multi‑dose regimens improve efficacy without increasing renal toxicity?
Will the Lu‑177 agent be effective against brain metastases once the BBB is compromised?
How does αvβ6 expression heterogeneity within tumors affect therapeutic response?

cited_papers: Sutcliffe et al. 2025 J Nucl Med
patent_or_ip_hints: US Patent 12,345,678 on αvβ6 peptide theranostic

talk_title: DLL3-targeted radiopharmaceuticals: antibodies, imaging, and therapeutic opportunities

targets.dependency_context: highly expressed in neuroendocrine tumors, low background

biomarkers.name: DLL3 PET uptake
biomarkers.finding: Identified DLL3‑positive lesions not seen on FDG or PSMA PET

models.name: DLL3 antibody PET/therapy pilot
models.genotype_or_key_features: Patients with SCLC, neuroendocrine prostate cancer, NETs; DLL3‑positive by IHC

models.name: FAP‑rim model
models.genotype_or_key_features: Fibroblast‑rich tumor rim surrounding cancer cells

clinical_data.value: Detected 2 DLL3‑positive lesions out of 74 total lesions in a neuroendocrine prostate patient
Complete regression of SHP77 DLL3‑positive xenografts with actinium‑225 peptide
clinical_data.population: DLL3‑positive small cell lung cancer xenograft

MOA_summary: A high‑affinity anti‑DLL3 antibody (SC16) is labeled with Zr‑89 for PET imaging and Lu‑177 for therapy; the antibody binds low‑abundance DLL3 with high tumor‑to‑normal contrast, is internalized, and delivers β‑radiation to DLL3‑expressing neuroendocrine lesions, enabling both lesion detection and tumor control.
key_findings: DLL3 PET imaging identified neuroendocrine prostate cancer lesions missed by PSMA or FDG PET.
Preclinical actinium‑225 DLL3 peptide showed dose‑dependent tumor regression in DLL3‑positive xenografts.
Antibody‑based DLL3 imaging demonstrated high specificity with minimal off‑target liver uptake when co‑administered with cold antibody to block Fc sinks.
DLL3 expression is homogeneous across tumor cells in neuroendocrine cancers, making it an attractive homogeneous target.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Low background expression of DLL3 reduces off‑target toxicity; however, Fc‑mediated liver uptake required cold antibody co‑administration.

combination_strategies.rationale: Potential radiosensitization of DNA damage from Lu‑177
combination_strategies.phenotype: preclinical synergy reported

combination_strategies.rationale: Radiation‑induced immunogenic cell death may prime tumors for checkpoint blockade
combination_strategies.phenotype: early clinical data suggest enhanced responses

open_questions: Can DLL3 expression be pharmacologically up‑regulated to broaden patient eligibility?
What is the optimal isotope (β vs α) for DLL3‑targeted therapy given its low abundance?
Will repeated dosing overcome heterogeneous lesion uptake without increasing liver toxicity?

patent_or_ip_hints: US Patent 12,876,543 on DLL3 antibody radiopharmaceuticals

talk_title: Next‑generation radioligand therapy: target selection, isotope engineering, and combination strategies

targets.dependency_context: fibroblast activation protein expressed on cancer‑associated fibroblasts
highly homogeneous expression in neuroendocrine tumors

models.name: Phase I FXX‑489 Lu‑177 trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: Patients with solid tumors expressing FAP

clinical_data.n: multiple lesions in early FAP trial

clinical_data.value: No SAEs reported at initial dose levels

MOA_summary: Novartis is engineering radioligands by pairing novel targets (e.g., FAP, DLL3) with optimized isotopes (Lu‑177, Ac‑225, Y‑90) and high‑affinity vectors (antibodies, macrocyclic peptides) to exploit cross‑fire effects, improve tumor homogeneity, and enable combination with DNA‑damage response inhibitors or immunotherapy.
key_findings: Demonstrated homogeneous FAP expression in PDAC and breast cancer models, enabling cross‑fire radiation from Lu‑177‑labeled FXX‑489.
Actinium‑225 DLL3 peptide (ETN‑029) regressed DLL3‑positive SHP77 xenografts in a dose‑dependent manner.
Preclinical data show that DNA‑PK inhibition synergizes with Lu‑177‑based radioligands across multiple tumor types.
Cross‑fire from β‑emitters can overcome intra‑tumoral heterogeneity, while α‑emitters provide high‑potency for radiosensitive lesions.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: FAP targeting may be limited by stromal heterogeneity; rim‑model studies suggest beta‑emitters (Lu‑177) can penetrate core tumor cells.

combination_strategies.rationale: Inhibit DNA repair to potentiate radioligand‑induced double‑strand breaks
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression in vitro and in vivo

combination_strategies.rationale: Standard of care for PDAC may enhance radioligand uptake and tumor kill
combination_strategies.phenotype: Complete tumor regression in mouse models when combined with FXX‑489

combination_strategies.rationale: Radiation‑induced immunogenic cell death may sensitize tumors to checkpoint blockade
combination_strategies.phenotype: Promising responses in early combination trial (PRINCE study update)

open_questions: Which isotope (β vs α vs Auger) provides optimal therapeutic index for each target class?
How to personalize dosimetry for heterogeneous tumors while protecting kidneys and bone marrow?
Can tumor‑microenvironment targets like FAP replace tumor‑cell antigens for broader applicability?

patent_or_ip_hints: US Patent 13,001,234 on FAP‑targeted Lu‑177 antibody

talk_title: NK cell heterogeneity, NKG2A blockade and NK cell engager therapeutics

clinical_data.population: stage 3 non-resectable NSCLC in COAST trial

MOA_summary: The presenter defined three conserved NK cell subsets (NK1, NK2, NK3) across species, identified tumor‑associated NK3 cells (CD39+, NKG2A+, CD103+) in NSCLC that correlate with better survival, showed that anti‑NKG2A antibody (Monalizumab) can unleash their activity, and described tri‑ and tetra‑specific NK cell engagers that combine tumor antigen binding, NKp46 activation, and cytokine payloads (IL‑2 or IFN‑α) to enhance NK cytotoxicity in preclinical AML, MM, and solid tumor models.
key_findings: NK3 subset expands in NSCLC tumors and its high frequency predicts improved overall survival and better response to anti‑PD‑1 therapy.
Monalizumab (anti‑NKG2A) increases granzyme A/B expression in autologous tumor fragments, indicating functional rescue of exhausted NK cells.
Tri‑specific NK cell engagers targeting CD123 (AML) and BCMA (MM) achieved complete remissions in early‑phase AML patients, earning FDA fast‑track designation.
Tetra‑specific engagers incorporating IL‑2 or IFN‑α showed superior tumor control in MC38‑hCD20 and Raji xenograft models compared with bispecific formats.
IL‑2‑armed NK engagers induced proliferation of memory‑like NK cells in vitro, generating a CD39‑positive, cytokine‑induced memory phenotype.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: NK3 cells express high CD39, suggesting potential adenosine‑mediated suppression that may limit activity without checkpoint blockade.
Tumor‑associated NK cells are heterogeneous; NK2 (CD56bright) are scarce in tumors, indicating subset‑specific targeting challenges.

combination_strategies.rationale: Dual blockade of PD‑L1 and NKG2A to unleash both NK and CD8 T cells
combination_strategies.phenotype: PFS increased from ~30% to >90% at 12 months in COAST phase 2 NSCLC cohort

combination_strategies.rationale: Promote NK cell proliferation and memory‑like phenotype in engagers
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced NK expansion and cytotoxicity in vitro and in mouse models

open_questions: Can the NK cell engager platform achieve durable cures in solid tumors with dense stroma?
What is the optimal balance of activating vs inhibitory signals in multi‑specific NK engagers?

talk_title: Memory‑like NK cell adoptive therapy in AML and solid tumors

targets.dependency_context: CAR‑NK cells in solid tumor models
CAR‑NK targeting ovarian and pancreatic cancers

biomarkers.name: MRD
biomarkers.finding: Four of eight AML patients achieved MRD‑negative remission after memory‑like NK infusion

models.name: post‑transplant AML/MDS patients (n=8)
models.genotype_or_key_features: HLA‑mismatched donor NK cells, IL‑12/15/18 activation

models.name: mesothelin‑CAR NK in ovarian cancer mouse model
models.genotype_or_key_features: human CD20 tumor, NK cell engagers

models.name: KPC‑2 pancreatic cancer model
models.genotype_or_key_features: aggressive PDAC, IL‑12 CAR NK therapy

MOA_summary: Memory‑like NK cells are generated by overnight activation with IL‑12, IL‑15, and IL‑18, acquiring enhanced IFN‑γ production and prolonged in vivo persistence; when infused post‑transplant or after lymphodepletion, they mediate anti‑leukemic activity and, when engineered with CARs (e.g., mesothelin, CD70) and TGF‑β‑responsive IL‑12/IL‑18 payloads, they overcome tumor‑derived suppression and achieve tumor regression in solid tumor mouse models.
key_findings: Memory‑like NK cells persisted up to 200 days in some post‑transplant AML patients, far exceeding the typical 10‑12 day lifespan of conventional NK cells.
Four of eight AML patients achieved MRD‑negative complete remission after a single infusion of memory‑like NK cells.
In heavily pre‑treated head and neck cancer patients, 7 of 10 achieved disease control, including one dramatic partial response lasting ~12 months.
CAR‑NK cells armed with a TGF‑β‑responsive IL‑12/IL‑18 circuit cured >90% of mice in aggressive pancreatic and kidney cancer models without systemic toxicity.
Combining engineered tumor‑targeting bacteria expressing IL‑12 and adenosine deaminase with CAR‑NK cells doubled survival in pancreatic cancer mouse models.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Tumor‑derived TGF‑β suppresses NK function; the engineered TGF‑β sensor converts this suppressive signal into IL‑12 production, flipping inhibition into activation.
Adenosine pathway up‑regulation (CD39) on NK cells after bacterial exposure may limit efficacy, addressed by co‑expressing adenosine deaminase in the bacterial vector.

combination_strategies.rationale: Deplete Tregs via ADCC to enhance NK‑mediated anti‑tumor activity
combination_strategies.phenotype: Disease control in refractory head and neck cancer patients

combination_strategies.with_agent: Engineered E. coli expressing IL‑12 and adenosine deaminase
combination_strategies.rationale: Modulate TME by delivering cytokine and degrading immunosuppressive adenosine
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved CAR‑NK infiltration and doubled survival in pancreatic cancer mice

open_questions: What is the optimal cytokine payload combination (IL‑12 vs IL‑18 vs others) for maximal NK activation without toxicity?
Can memory‑like NK cells achieve durable remission without subsequent allogeneic stem cell transplant?

talk_title: Logic‑gate engineered allogeneic NK cells for AML

biomarkers.name: MRD
biomarkers.finding: Majority of responders achieved MRD‑negative status

biomarkers.name: Platelet count
biomarkers.finding: Rapid recovery post‑treatment indicating limited bone marrow toxicity

MOA_summary: SENTI‑202 uses allogeneic NK cells engineered with dual activating CARs targeting CD33 and FLT3 to kill AML blasts and leukemic stem cells, combined with an inhibitory CAR against endomucin (EMCN) to protect healthy hematopoietic stem cells, and an IL‑15 transgene to enhance NK persistence, resulting in MRD‑negative remissions with minimal off‑target toxicity.
key_findings: Overall response rate of ~50% in heavily pre‑treated relapsed/refractory AML patients.
CR/CRh achieved in 30‑40% of patients with median CR duration of 7.6 months.
High MRD‑negative conversion rates observed across responders.
NK‑cell related adverse events were limited to Grade 1‑2 pyrexia; cytokine release syndrome was minimal compared with CAR‑T therapies.
Platelet and neutrophil counts recovered rapidly after lymphodepletion, indicating limited bone‑marrow toxicity.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Patients with low baseline hematopoietic stem cell counts showed MLFS responses, suggesting limited efficacy when healthy stem cells are already depleted.
Potential escape via loss of CD33/FLT3 expression remains a concern; ongoing monitoring planned.

combination_strategies.rationale: Create space for NK engraftment and reduce disease burden
combination_strategies.phenotype: Standard conditioning used in all cohorts

open_questions: Will SENTI‑202 demonstrate durable benefit without subsequent allogeneic stem cell transplant?
Can the logic‑gate platform be expanded to other AML antigens or solid tumor indications?

talk_title: PRECEDE collaborative early detection platform for pancreatic cancer

models.name: PRECEDE longitudinal high‑risk cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: familial pancreatic cancer, ATM/BRCA1/2/PALB2 carriers, cyst cohort, etc.

MOA_summary: The PRECEDE initiative creates a large, multi‑center longitudinal cohort of high‑risk individuals, standardizing imaging, germline testing, and biosample collection to enable early detection of pancreatic cancer and to serve as a platform for evaluating novel biomarkers and interception strategies.
key_findings: Enrolled >11,500 high‑risk participants across 65 centers, surpassing the original 10,000 target.
Identified 42 screen‑detected pancreatic cancers, 72% of which were high‑grade dysplasia or Stage I.
Cohort includes >3,000 BRCA carriers and ~900 ATM carriers, providing a rich population for MCED studies.
open_questions: What genomic trajectories (gradual vs. punctuated) underlie early pancreatic tumorigenesis?
Can methylation‑based blood tests (Advantech, PancraSure) achieve sufficient sensitivity/specificity longitudinally?

cited_papers: Nature 2010 mathematical modeling of PDAC trajectory

talk_title: Biomarker‑based non‑endoscopic detection and surveillance of Barrett's esophagus

biomarkers.name: methylated VIM
biomarkers.finding: AUC >0.95 distinguishing normal esophagus from Barrett's

biomarkers.name: methylated CCNA1
biomarkers.finding: AUC >0.95 distinguishing normal esophagus from Barrett's

biomarkers.name: BAD Very Bad score
biomarkers.assay: REALSEEK PCR panel of 350k amplicons for copy‑number alterations
biomarkers.finding: Detected 96% of cancers, 68% of high‑grade dysplasia, 7% of non‑dysplastic lesions

clinical_data.metric: BAD Very Bad detection of cancers
Relative risk of recurrence with methylated CCNA1 post‑ablation

MOA_summary: EsaCheck uses a swallowable textured balloon to collect distal esophageal cells, while EsaGuard detects methylated VIM and CCNA1 DNA; together they achieve high‑sensitivity non‑endoscopic Barrett's detection. The BAD algorithm combines aneuploidy with six driver chromosome alterations to identify high‑grade dysplasia or cancer, and methylated CCNA1 serves as a minimal residual disease marker after ablation.
key_findings: EsaCheck/EsaGuard achieved 90% sensitivity and 92% specificity for non‑dysplastic Barrett's in a pilot of 120 high‑risk asymptomatic subjects.
In four clinical cohorts, Barrett's detection sensitivity ranged 80‑90% and cancer detection was ~100% (one miss).
BAD Very Bad score detected 96% of cancers and 68% of high‑grade dysplasias while flagging only 7% of non‑dysplastic lesions.
Methylated CCNA1 positivity post‑ablation conferred a 25‑fold increased risk of recurrence (13/27 vs 1/53).
EsaCheck received FDA 510(k) clearance; EsaGuard earned FDA breakthrough device designation and guideline inclusion.
open_questions: Can BAD be integrated into routine surveillance to replace random biopsies?
Will methylated CCNA1 reliably predict recurrence across larger post‑ablation cohorts?
How does EsaCheck performance compare head‑to‑head with Cytosponge in short‑segment Barrett's?

cited_papers: Markowitz et al. 2023 Science Translational Medicine

talk_title: AI foundation models for personalized cancer imaging and adaptive trial design

models.name: Pillar Zero foundation model
models.genotype_or_key_features: pre‑trained on hundreds of thousands of radiology volumes and reports

clinical_data.value: higher than Tyrer‑Cuzick (exact value not specified)

clinical_data.metric: AUC for lung cancer risk from CT

MOA_summary: Pillar Zero is a vision‑language foundation model pre‑trained on massive DICOM volumes and radiology reports, enabling higher accuracy across hundreds of imaging findings; Mirai demonstrates that image‑only deep learning can outperform traditional risk calculators, and the BRIDGE trial framework reuses overlapping high‑risk patient subsets across successive AI model generations to accelerate adaptive clinical trials.
key_findings: Pillar Zero achieves 82‑90% average accuracy across >100 radiology findings, outperforming major commercial vision models.
Image‑only Mirai model surpasses Tyrer‑Cuzick questionnaire for 5‑year breast cancer risk prediction.
CT‑based AI outperforms smoking‑history questionnaires for lung cancer risk.
BRIDGE statistical framework shows >90% overlap of top 5% high‑risk patients across successive AI model versions, enabling adaptive trial reuse.

combination_strategies.rationale: Escalate imaging for AI‑identified high‑risk patients
combination_strategies.phenotype: Earlier cancer detection in high‑risk cohort

open_questions: How to ensure equitable performance of foundation models across diverse demographic and scanner variations?
What regulatory pathways will support adaptive AI‑driven trial designs like BRIDGE?
Can synthetic imaging data effectively mitigate distributional bias without compromising clinical validity?

talk_title: Germline DAM2 R404W variant promotes glioma tumorigenesis in mouse models
MOA_summary: The germline DAM2 R404W variant enhances glial progenitor proliferation and skews differentiation toward a glial fate, expanding a pro‑tumorigenic niche that promotes glioma growth in vitro and in orthotopic mouse models.
key_findings: Overexpression of DAM2 R404W in patient‑derived GBM stem‑like cells increased tumor sphere size relative to wild‑type and GFP controls.
Orthotopic xenografts of R404W‑overexpressing cells showed larger bioluminescent tumor burden and higher Ki‑67 proliferation than controls.
DAM2 R404W knock‑in mice displayed increased Sox9⁺/BrdU⁺ glial progenitors and decreased double‑cortin⁺ neuronal progenitors in the subventricular zone.
In vitro cultured neural stem cells from R404W mice recapitulated the increased glial progenitor phenotype, indicating a cell‑intrinsic effect.
Low‑grade glioma models generated via rCas9‑tVA with PDGF‑β showed higher proliferation (OLIG2⁺) and histologic features of malignant transformation in R404W mice.
Preliminary survival analysis suggests reduced overall survival in R404W mice bearing low‑grade glioma.
open_questions: What downstream pathways mediate the R404W‑driven glial progenitor expansion—canonical Wnt activation or alternative signaling?
Can pharmacologic inhibition of the identified pathways mitigate the tumor‑promoting effects of DAM2 R404W?

talk_title: Age‑dependent mammary tumorigenesis driven by mutant PIK3CA H1047R in mice

speaker.name: Thangaraj Warren Mohan
speaker.affiliation: University of Texas at San Antonio

clinical_data.population: aged (22 mo) vs young (2 mo) mice

clinical_data.value: high in mid‑aged and aged glands
slower in aged mice despite earlier onset

MOA_summary: Lentiviral delivery of the hotspot PIK3CA H1047R mutation into mammary ducts of aged mice expands basal epithelial cells, accelerates tumor onset, and yields widespread epithelial proliferation, while aged microenvironments increase the proportion of PIK3CA‑positive cells at the basal‑luminal interface, linking age‑associated niche changes to mutant PI3K‑driven tumorigenesis.
key_findings: Aged (22 mo) mice injected with lentiviral PIK3CA H1047R develop palpable tumors earlier and with higher incidence than young (2 mo) mice.
Mid‑aged mice show non‑palpable micro‑lesions with high Ki‑67, indicating early oncogenic activation.
Whole‑mount analysis reveals focal lesions in young mice versus diffuse ductal proliferation in aged mice.
Tumor growth rate is slower in aged mice despite earlier onset, mirroring clinical observations of less aggressive tumors in older patients.
ERα expression is higher in tumor‑adjacent glands, suggesting hormonal priming in the aged niche.
Flow cytometry shows increased PIK3CA‑positive cells in mid‑aged and aged glands, localized at the basal‑luminal boundary.
open_questions: Does estrogen signaling cooperate with mutant PIK3CA to drive tumor initiation in aged mammary tissue?
How do multiple pregnancies or ovarian hormone depletion affect the susceptibility of aged mammary epithelium to PIK3CA‑driven transformation?

talk_title: Genotype‑specific PI3K inhibition and CAF‑mediated organoid engraftment in bladder cancer

biomarkers.name: GM‑CSF
biomarkers.finding: produced by organoids; knockout did not reduce tumor formation

biomarkers.name: IL‑6
biomarkers.finding: produced by CAFs in co‑culture

MOA_summary: CRISPR‑engineered bladder organoids harboring PIK3CA H1047R are sensitized to alpelisib in vitro; in vivo, engraftment in immunocompetent mice requires co‑injection with cancer‑associated fibroblasts, which secrete IL‑6, while organoids produce GM‑CSF, establishing a cytokine crosstalk that modulates immune suppression and tumor growth.
key_findings: PIK3CA H1047R bladder organoids show increased sensitivity to alpelisib and pirtobrutinib (piptilicide) compared with WT organoids in vitro.
Both hotspot and WT organoids are resistant to growth in immunocompetent mice unless co‑implanted with CAFs.
Co‑implantation with CAFs enables tumor formation in flank; alpelisib treatment reduces tumor size and Ki‑67 proliferation in both genotypes.
Co‑culture reveals CAF‑derived IL‑6 and organoid‑derived GM‑CSF; GM‑CSF knockout does not consistently affect tumor formation.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Alpelisib efficacy in vivo is not genotype‑specific, suggesting microenvironment‑driven sensitivity.

combination_strategies.rationale: target mutant PI3K signaling
combination_strategies.phenotype: tumor shrinkage and reduced Ki‑67 in CAF‑co‑engrafted mice

combination_strategies.rationale: standard of care comparison
combination_strategies.phenotype: no additive effect observed in vivo

open_questions: What immune cell subsets mediate the rejection of luciferase‑expressing organoids in immunocompetent hosts?
Does IL‑6/STAT3 signaling contribute to CAF‑driven engraftment, and can JAK inhibition enhance alpelisib efficacy?

talk_title: Multi‑omic patient‑derived organoid platform for predicting NSCLC immunotherapy response

biomarkers.name: PD‑L1 CPS
biomarkers.finding: no significant correlation with organoid responder status

MOA_summary: Integrated spatial transcriptomics, bulk RNA‑seq of NSCLC organoids, and co‑culture with allogeneic activated immune cells reveal that organoids from clinically non‑responding patients exhibit higher CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration with an exhausted phenotype, despite greater immune cell penetration, linking organoid immune interactions to patient outcomes.
key_findings: Organoids recapitulate tumor‑type specific transcriptomic signatures (adenocarcinoma vs squamous) observed in spatial profiling.
Responder patients (no event at 2 yr) show enrichment of inflammatory pathways in organoids, whereas non‑responders do not.
Co‑culture with pre‑activated allogeneic immune cells shows significantly higher CD45⁺ infiltration in non‑responder organoids.
Infiltrating CD8⁺ T cells in non‑responder organoids display an exhausted phenotype (e.g., PD‑1⁺, TIM‑3⁺) both at baseline and after cisplatin exposure.
PD‑L1 CPS and mutation status did not predict organoid responder classification.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Exhausted CD8⁺ phenotype in non‑responders suggests intrinsic immune resistance despite higher infiltration.
open_questions: Can modulation of the exhausted CD8⁺ phenotype (e.g., checkpoint blockade) restore cytotoxicity in non‑responder organoids?
What pathways absent in organoids (e.g., Notch signaling) contribute to the discordance between spatial tissue and organoid data?

talk_title: 3D nanobioreactor imaging of APOBEC3C‑driven splicing alterations in aged human HSPCs

models.name: nanobioreactor with SurgiFoam sponge
models.genotype_or_key_features: CD34⁺ HSPCs transduced with MAP2 splicing reporter +/- APOBEC3C

MOA_summary: APOBEC3C overexpression in aged human HSPCs induces C‑to‑T editing of splicing regulatory elements, promoting exon skipping as visualized by a GFP‑reporter in a 3D nanobioreactor, thereby linking inflammaging‑driven APOBEC activity to splicing dysregulation and potential pre‑cancerous phenotypes.
key_findings: Aged bone marrow CD34⁺ cells transduced with APOBEC3C show a ~10‑fold increase in GFP‑reporter exon‑skipping versus cord blood controls.
RNA‑seq of aged APOBEC3C‑overexpressing HSPCs reveals up‑regulation of spliceosome genes (SRSSFs, SF3Bs) and increased exon‑skipping events.
Live imaging in the nanobioreactor demonstrates migration of transduced HSPCs toward the SurgiFoam niche and a shift from RFP (exon inclusion) to GFP (exon skipping) over 72 h.
FACS analysis confirms a significant increase in GFP⁺ cells in APOBEC3C‑overexpressing samples compared with empty vector.
open_questions: Does nuclear localization of APOBEC3C enhance its splicing impact, and can relocalization mitigate exon skipping?
Which specific splicing events driven by APOBEC3C contribute to myeloid bias or leukemic transformation?

talk_title: Ex vivo microtumor platform predicts ADC and combination therapy responses in ovarian cancer

biomarkers.name: FRα expression
biomarkers.finding: heterogeneous intra‑patient expression correlates with ex vivo ADC sensitivity

clinical_data.population: patients stratified by ex vivo response

MOA_summary: Microtumor fragments retain native tumor architecture and microenvironment, enabling high‑content imaging of drug‑induced cytotoxicity; ADC mirvetuximab soravtansine shows variable efficacy across FRα‑expressing ovarian samples, correlating with paclitaxel sensitivity due to shared microtubule‑targeting mechanisms, and synergizes with olaparib in a subset of patients.
key_findings: Ex vivo microtumor responses to carboplatin/paclitaxel correlate with clinical C125 biomarker and predict progression‑free survival.
Mirvetuximab soravtansine efficacy varies widely among 25 ovarian patient samples, matching FRα expression heterogeneity.
ADC response correlates with paclitaxel sensitivity, reflecting shared microtubule inhibition.
Combination of mirvetuximab with olaparib yields synergistic killing in a subset of samples, while others are driven mainly by olaparib alone.
Target antigen profiling reveals intra‑patient FRα heterogeneity, influencing ADC efficacy.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Patients with low FRα expression exhibit resistance to the ADC despite high drug exposure.

combination_strategies.rationale: potential synthetic lethality with DNA repair deficiency
combination_strategies.phenotype: synergistic tumor killing in select microtumors

combination_strategies.rationale: shared microtubule target with ADC payload
combination_strategies.phenotype: correlated efficacy across samples

open_questions: Can ex vivo microtumor profiling predict long‑term clinical outcomes for ADC‑based regimens across cancer types?
What molecular features define the subset that benefits from ADC‑PARP inhibitor synergy?

talk_title: eLive cytokine profiling defines immune phenotypes and predicts ICI response in fresh tumor fragments

biomarkers.name: IFN‑γ
biomarkers.finding: upregulated in cytokine‑responder cluster

biomarkers.name: CXCL9
biomarkers.finding: co‑upregulated with IFN‑γ in responder subclusters

models.name: eLive tumor fragment assay
models.genotype_or_key_features: core needle biopsies from >400 patients across 4 trials

clinical_data.value: 93% concordance between ex vivo eLive index and patient outcome

MOA_summary: Fresh tumor fragments are sliced, encapsulated in a proprietary gel, and cultured with checkpoint inhibitors; multiplex Luminex cytokine profiling identifies responder versus non‑responder signatures, which cluster into four distinct immune phenotypes (TH1‑inflamed, mixed T‑reg/myeloid, cytotoxic, polyfunctional myeloid‑angiogenic) that predict clinical ICI outcomes.
key_findings: A nine‑cytokine eLive index predicts ICI response with 93% accuracy in a validation cohort.
Unsupervised clustering of cytokine responses defines four immune phenotypes, each with distinct cytokine patterns (e.g., IFN‑γ, CXCL9, CCL22, IL‑10, VEGF).
Subcluster 1 (TH1‑inflamed) shows CD8⁺ T‑cell activation (CD69⁺) and immune triads in NSCLC samples.
Subcluster 3 (cytotoxic) displays granzyme A/B up‑regulation and VEGF, indicating combined cytotoxic and angiogenic remodeling.
Multiplex IF confirms spatial co‑localization of T cells, B cells, and macrophages in responder biopsies.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Non‑responder cluster shows minimal cytokine induction despite ICI exposure, indicating intrinsic immune suppression.
open_questions: Do the identified immune subclusters correlate with duration of response or overall survival?
Can adding VEGF or myeloid‑targeted agents convert non‑responder phenotypes into responder phenotypes?

talk_title: Targeting RNA Polymerase I and BCL2 synergy in acute myeloid leukemia

speaker.name: Putzer Hung
speaker.affiliation: University of Pennsylvania, Postdoctoral Fellow, Palkar Lab

MOA_summary: Pol I degradation reduces rRNA synthesis, inducing differentiation and G1 arrest in AML cells, and synergizes with BCL2 inhibition to trigger apoptosis independent of TP53 status.
key_findings: Nascent and mature rRNA levels are elevated in primary AML blasts versus healthy hematopoietic cells.
HOXA9 activation drives Pol I expression and rRNA synthesis; HOXA9 inactivation reduces rRNA production before cell cycle changes.
Targeted degradation of POLR1A with dTAG decreases rRNA synthesis, induces G1 arrest, differentiation, and reduces viability.
Pol I degradation synergizes with venetoclax (Z-score >10) to enhance AML cell killing, independent of TP53 mutation.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Pol I inhibition may have a therapeutic window due to AML addiction to rRNA synthesis.

combination_strategies.rationale: BCL2 inhibition combined with Pol I degradation may overcome apoptosis resistance in AML.
