combination_strategies.phenotype: synergistic lethal effect in vitro, left-shifted dose-response curves

open_questions: What is the therapeutic window for Pol I inhibition in vivo?
Can Pol I degradation overcome venetoclax resistance in patient-derived xenografts?

talk_title: LncRNA CYP4A22-AS1/YBX1/PK1 axis drives radiation resistance in cervical adenocarcinoma

speaker.name: Dr. Zhao
speaker.affiliation: University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center

biomarkers.name: CYP4A22-AS1 expression
biomarkers.finding: high expression correlates with shorter overall and disease-free survival in cervical adenocarcinoma patients

biomarkers.name: PK1 protein level
biomarkers.finding: high PK1 predicts poorer disease-free and progression-free survival

MOA_summary: CYP4A22-AS1 binds YBX1, enhancing transcription of PK1, which drives glycolysis and confers radiation resistance; depletion of the lncRNA or PK1 sensitizes cells to radiation-induced DNA damage and apoptosis.
key_findings: CYP4A22-AS1 is upregulated in cervical adenocarcinoma and predicts poor survival.
Knockdown of CYP4A22-AS1 increases radiation-induced apoptosis and DNA damage markers.
CYP4A22-AS1 regulates glycolysis via upregulation of PK1 through YBX1 interaction.
PK1 depletion reverses glycolytic enhancement and radiosensitizes CYP4A22-AS1 overexpressing cells.
In vivo, CYP4A22-AS1 overexpression accelerates tumor growth in mouse models.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: CYP4A22-AS1 mediates resistance to radiation by metabolic reprogramming.
open_questions: Does the CYP4A22-AS1/YBX1/PK1 axis operate in other tumor types?
Can pharmacologic inhibition of PK1 sensitize tumors to radiotherapy?

talk_title: Ultrasmall lactoferrin nanoassemblies delivering miR-196b antagomir and COP TF II inhibitor for RCC CAF remodeling

speaker.name: Ahmed Ziyadeh
speaker.affiliation: Amherst College (Zoughbi Lab, Harvard Medical School)

MOA_summary: An ultra‑small lactoferrin‑stabilized nanoassembly co‑delivers a miR‑196b antagomir and the COP TF II inhibitor CIA1 to RCC cancer‑associated fibroblasts, suppressing activation markers, ECM production, and secreted cytokines, thereby reducing CAF‑driven tumor growth and enhancing immune infiltration.
key_findings: Nanoassembly size ~6.3 nm enables renal filtration and accumulation in tumor kidney.
Both miR‑196b antagomir and CIA1 independently down‑regulate CAF activation (α‑SMA) and ECM markers (collagen I, LOX).
Conditioned media from treated CAFs reduces RCC cell apoptosis resistance, EMT markers, and migration.
In orthotopic RENCA mice, combined nanoassembly markedly reduces tumor volume and weight without systemic toxicity.
Treatment increases intratumoral CD8⁺ T cells, dendritic cells, and NK cells while decreasing Tregs and suppressive myeloid cells.

combination_strategies.rationale: CAF remodeling may enhance immune infiltration and response to checkpoint blockade
combination_strategies.phenotype: greater tumor reduction when combined with immunotherapy versus immunotherapy alone

open_questions: Can targeting PDGFRβ improve nanoassembly tumor specificity?
What is the optimal dosing schedule for sustained CAF reprogramming?

talk_title: Mitochondrial tRNA‑Arg fragments modulate breast cancer metastasis

models.name: MINX (mitochondrial nuclear exchange) mice
models.genotype_or_key_features: Mismatched nuclear and mitochondrial genomes (e.g., C57BL/6 nuclear, FVB mitochondria)

models.name: PyMT breast cancer model
models.genotype_or_key_features: MMTV‑PyMT transgene on various mitochondrial backgrounds

MOA_summary: Strain‑specific SNPs in mitochondrial tRNA^Arg alter its secondary structure, influencing cleavage into ~50‑nt fragments; these fragments correlate with primary tumor latency and pulmonary metastasis, suggesting a non‑coding RNA signaling role in metastatic progression.
key_findings: Mitochondrial SNPs in tRNA^Arg D‑loop differ among mouse strains and associate with metastatic burden.
Northern blots reveal 40‑50 nt tRNA^Arg fragments whose abundance varies with mitochondrial genotype.
Enzymatic end‑repair and demethylation enable detection of these fragments by small‑RNA‑seq.
MINX mice with mismatched mitochondria show altered primary tumor latency and number of lung metastases.
Fragment production is higher in strains with higher metastatic propensity, implicating a functional role.
open_questions: What endonuclease generates the mt‑tRNA^Arg fragments?
Do the fragments act in stromal cells or tumor cells to promote metastasis?

talk_title: Urinary extracellular vesicle microRNA panel for early detection and prognosis of lung cancer

biomarkers.name: Urinary EV miRNA signature (11 miRNAs, final 3‑miRNA panel)
biomarkers.finding: AUC 0.94 for cancer vs control; early‑stage sensitivity 88% and specificity 87%

biomarkers.name: Three‑miRNA prognostic panel
biomarkers.finding: Hazard ratio 8.3 for recurrence‑free survival; 3‑year AUC 0.80

models.name: 278 lung cancer patients + 213 non‑cancer controls
models.genotype_or_key_features: Stage 0‑I NSCLC predominant, urine EV miRNA sequencing

models.name: 111 surgically resected lung cancer patients (stage I‑II)
models.genotype_or_key_features: Follow‑up up to 4 years for recurrence

MOA_summary: Urinary extracellular vesicles enriched for tumor‑derived miRNAs provide a non‑invasive source of cancer‑specific signatures; a machine‑learning model using selected miRNAs discriminates early‑stage lung cancer from controls and stratifies recurrence risk after surgery.
key_findings: Urinary EV isolation increases miRNA read proportion fivefold.
A 11‑miRNA feature set yields AUC 0.94 for cancer detection; performance holds in age‑matched subset.
Three‑miRNA prognostic panel (selected from 11) predicts recurrence‑free survival with HR 8.3 and 3‑year AUC 0.80.
Age and other clinical covariates do not confound the detection score.
open_questions: Will the miRNA panel retain performance in broader, multi‑ethnic cohorts?
Can longitudinal urine sampling monitor treatment response?

talk_title: Multimodal miR‑15a conjugates overcome olaparib resistance in epithelial ovarian cancer

models.name: panel of ovarian cancer lines (various BRCA/P53 status)
metastatic ovarian cancer model (parental and olaparib‑resistant SCOP3 cells)

MOA_summary: A chemically modified miR‑15a mimic incorporates 5‑FU, gemcitabine, and methotrexate, delivering tumor‑suppressive miRNA activity plus chemotherapeutic payloads; it down‑regulates DNA repair/checkpoint proteins (CHEK1, E1) and induces DNA damage, forcing cells into mitotic catastrophe and synergizing with olaparib.
key_findings: Modified miR‑15a reduces viability of ovarian cancer cells at low nanomolar concentrations without delivery vehicle.
The construct retains potency in olaparib‑resistant cells, showing similar IC50 values.
RNA‑seq reveals up‑regulation of DNA damage pathways and down‑regulation of WNT, mTOR, AMPK signaling.
Western blots confirm down‑regulation of CHEK1 and E1, and activation of CDK1/CDC25A.
Bliss analysis demonstrates strong synergy between the modified miR‑15a and olaparib.
In vivo, treatment (3.75 mg/kg) markedly suppresses metastatic tumor growth in both parental and resistant models with no observable toxicity.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Modified miR‑15a overcomes olaparib resistance regardless of BRCA or TP53 status.

combination_strategies.rationale: Combined DNA damage from PARP inhibition with checkpoint abrogation by miR‑15a targets CHEK1/E1
combination_strategies.phenotype: synergistic tumor cell killing (Bliss score >10)

open_questions: What is the pharmacokinetic profile of the modified miR‑15a in vivo?
Can the approach be translated to other PARP‑resistant tumor types?

talk_title: Therapy‑induced senescent cells promote early dissemination via JUP‑mediated clustering

targets.dependency_context: senescent TNBC cells promote clustering of parental cells

models.name: NSG immunodeficient mice
models.genotype_or_key_features: orthotopic and tail‑vein injection of 231 cells

MOA_summary: Therapy‑induced senescent breast cancer cells acquire a JUP‑dependent adhesive phenotype that forms multicellular clusters, enhancing early lung dissemination and, in immunocompetent hosts, promoting metastatic outgrowth through immune modulation.
key_findings: Doxorubicin or fractionated irradiation induces dose‑dependent senescence in TNBC xenografts (X‑gal staining, beta‑gal activity).
Co‑injection of senescent cells with parental 231 cells does not affect primary tumor size but increases lung metastases (p<0.01).
Senescent cells accelerate early dissemination: 3‑day tail‑vein experiments show higher lung luciferase signal versus parental cells alone.
3D imaging reveals larger tumor cell clusters and extravasation of senescent cells themselves in the lung.
RNA‑seq identifies up‑regulation of adhesion pathways; JUP (plakoglobin) is highly expressed in senescent cells.
JUP knockout abolishes senescent‑cell‑mediated adhesion, cluster formation, and early dissemination (p<0.001).
In immunocompetent mice, senescent cells further enhance metastatic outgrowth beyond early dissemination, suggesting an immune‑modulatory role.
open_questions: Which immune cell subsets mediate the enhanced metastatic outgrowth observed in immunocompetent mice?
Does the JUP‑dependent clustering mechanism operate in metastatic sites other than lung?

talk_title: Extracellular matrix stiffness regulates TYK2‑mediated EMT and breast cancer metastasis

targets.dependency_context: low ECM stiffness breast epithelial cells

models.name: orthotopic mammary fat pad model
models.genotype_or_key_features: TAK2 knockdown or inhibitor‑treated breast cancer cells

models.name: PDX breast tumor
models.genotype_or_key_features: human breast cancer organoids in 3D hydrogel

MOA_summary: Reduced matrix stiffness maintains TYK2 activity at the cell membrane via IFN‑α1 binding; loss of TYK2 at high stiffness releases EPHA2 and Nyn activity, leading to Twist1 phosphorylation, nuclear translocation, and EMT‑driven invasion and lung dissemination.
key_findings: Normal breast tissue stiffness ~100‑200 Pa; invasive tumors reach 5‑6 kPa (30‑fold increase).
TAK2 knockdown or pharmacologic inhibition in 3D hydrogel (≤150 Pa) increases cell invasion and mesenchymal marker expression (vimentin, N‑cadherin).
TAK2 phosphorylation is high at low stiffness and drops sharply with increased matrix rigidity.
TAK2 inhibition (Tucabasertib) phenocopies knockdown, enhancing EMT and lung dissemination in orthotopic mouse models.
TAK2 interacts with IFN‑α1 and EPHA2 at low stiffness; high stiffness disrupts these interactions, activating Nyn‑mediated Twist1 phosphorylation.
Patient‑derived organoids recapitulate stiffness‑dependent invasion; TAK2 inhibition drives EMT markers and Twist1 nuclear localization.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: TAK2 activity is selectively lost in high‑stiffness environments, suggesting a context‑dependent therapeutic window.

combination_strategies.rationale: Blocks stiffness‑induced EMT by preserving TYK2 activity
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduces invasion and lung metastasis in low‑stiffness 3D cultures

open_questions: What upstream signals regulate IFN‑α1 expression in the tumor microenvironment?
Can pharmacologic activation of TYK2 counteract stiffness‑driven EMT in vivo?

talk_title: Spatial profiling reveals SPP1‑positive macrophages as central architects of colorectal brain metastasis

targets.dependency_context: macrophages in CRC brain metastasis niche

biomarkers.name: SPP1
biomarkers.finding: High SPP1 expression in macrophages correlates with increased tumor‑fibroblast interaction and poorer prognosis in brain mets

models.name: archival FFPE CRC brain metastasis tissue

MOA_summary: Macrophage‑derived SPP1 engages integrin/CD44 receptors on neighboring tumor and stromal cells, driving collagen‑rich ECM remodeling, immunosuppression, and enhanced tumor cell invasion in the colorectal brain metastasis niche.
key_findings: SPP1‑high macrophages are present in >80 % of CRC brain metastasis samples and co‑localize with tumor cells and fibroblasts.
Spatial neighborhood analysis identifies two macrophage‑enriched niches; one directly contacts tumor cells, the other contacts stromal cells.
SPP1 knockout in macrophages reduces expression of lipid‑metabolism genes (e.g., SQLE) and diminishes tumor cell invasion in co‑culture.
Macrophage SPP1 loss decreases tumor cell expression of metastasis‑related genes (e.g., S100 family, VIM).
Organ‑on‑a‑chip assays show a ~40 % reduction in macrophage migration speed when SPP1 is deleted.
Analysis of paired primary CRC and brain mets shows higher predicted SPP1‑integrin/CD44 interactions in brain lesions (p<0.01).

combination_strategies.rationale: Block autocrine/paracrine SPP1 signaling to reduce ECM remodeling and tumor invasion
combination_strategies.phenotype: Predicted decrease in macrophage‑tumor adhesion and invasion

open_questions: Do SPP1‑positive macrophages also modulate the blood‑CSF barrier and facilitate tumor cell entry?
Is there a therapeutic window for targeting SPP1 without impairing normal bone‑marrow macrophage function?

talk_title: KPC1 ubiquitin ligase suppresses melanoma mesenchymal transition by degrading ZEB1

targets.dependency_context: melanoma cells, low KPC1 leads to high ZEB1

clinical_data.value: HR 0.45 for high KPC1 vs low KPC1

MOA_summary: KPC1 ubiquitinates ZEB1, targeting it for proteasomal degradation; loss of KPC1 stabilizes ZEB1, driving EMT, N‑cadherin up‑regulation and poor melanoma prognosis.
key_findings: Low KPC1 expression correlates with high ZEB1 and CDH2 (N‑cadherin) in metastatic melanoma samples (p<0.001).
KPC1 knockout in melanoma cells increases ZEB1 protein levels without changing mRNA, indicating post‑translational regulation.
Cycloheximide chase shows prolonged ZEB1 half‑life in KPC1‑deficient cells.
Co‑IP demonstrates direct binding of KPC1 to ZEB1.
High KPC1 / low ZEB1 cohort exhibits significantly better overall survival (HR 0.45, 95% CI 0.30‑0.68).
MicroRNA‑155 mediates KPC1 down‑regulation in melanoma.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: KPC1 loss does not affect p65 NF‑κB subunit, indicating selective impact on the p50 pathway.

combination_strategies.rationale: Restore KPC1 expression to suppress ZEB1‑driven EMT
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced ZEB1 levels and mesenchymal markers in vitro

open_questions: Can pharmacologic activation of KPC1 be achieved in patients?
Does KPC1 loss affect immune checkpoint therapy response in melanoma?

talk_title: Jagged1 expression in breast cancer cells drives lymph node metastasis via Notch4‑mediated endothelial remodeling

targets.dependency_context: breast cancer cells with high Jagged1

biomarkers.name: Jagged1
biomarkers.assay: IHC on primary tumor and lymph node sections
biomarkers.finding: Higher Jagged1 intensity in lymph node metastases vs primary tumors (p<0.01) and correlates with increased relapse risk

models.name: orthotopic mammary fat pad model
models.genotype_or_key_features: RFP‑labeled tumor cells, lymphatic dye tracing

clinical_data.value: HR 2.1 for Jagged1‑positive lymph node cells

MOA_summary: Tumor‑cell Jagged1 engages Notch4 on lymphatic endothelial cells in a non‑canonical manner, disrupting VE‑cadherin junctions, increasing endothelial permeability, and facilitating tumor cell trans‑endothelial migration and colonization of lymph nodes.
readouts: In vivo lymph node imaging (RFP, dextran)
key_findings: Jagged1‑positive breast cancer cells show >3‑fold increase in lymphovascular invasion compared with Jagged1‑negative controls (p<0.001).
Jagged1 overexpression leads to larger lymph node tumor burden (RFP‑PanCK area) and earlier colonization.
Coating lymphatic endothelial cells with Jagged1 protein disrupts VE‑cadherin junctions and creates intercellular gaps.
Notch4 neutralizing antibody blocks Jagged1‑induced endothelial gap formation and reduces transendothelial migration (≈45 % reduction).
VEGF‑R3 soluble trap reduces lymph node metastasis but does not affect primary tumor growth.
High Jagged1 expression in lymph node metastases predicts shorter relapse‑free survival (HR 2.1).
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Jagged1‑driven effects are gamma‑secretase‑independent, indicating resistance to canonical Notch inhibitors.

combination_strategies.rationale: Block lymphangiogenic VEGF signaling synergistic with Notch4 blockade
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced lymph node colonization without affecting primary tumor size

combination_strategies.rationale: Inhibit non‑canonical Jagged1‑Notch4 signaling on lymphatic endothelium
combination_strategies.phenotype: Decreased endothelial gap formation and tumor cell migration

open_questions: Does Jagged1 also modulate immune cell recruitment within the lymph node microenvironment?
Can combined Notch4 and VEGF‑R3 blockade prevent distant organ metastasis originating from lymph nodes?

talk_title: Allogeneic CAR Cells: Are They the New Black?

models.name: hospital exemption CD19 CAR‑T cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 19 pediatric patients, high‑risk B‑ALL, prior transplant or refractory disease

models.name: GD2 CAR‑T neuroblastoma update
models.genotype_or_key_features: 54 patients (35 trial, 19 exemption), low tumor burden

clinical_data.population: pediatric B‑ALL treated with allogeneic CD19 CAR‑T
neuroblastoma patients with low tumor burden

MOA_summary: Allogeneic CAR‑T cells engineered to lack TCR and HLA molecules (or co‑knockdown CD52) avoid GvHD and host rejection, allowing robust expansion and MRD‑negative complete remissions across B‑ALL, T‑ALL, AML and neuroblastoma; CD7‑CAR‑T utilizes a protein‑expression blocker to target CD7 without fratricide, and GD2‑CAR‑T shows superior outcomes when administered early with low tumor burden.
key_findings: Allogeneic CD19 CAR‑T in 19 high‑risk pediatric B‑ALL patients achieved 100% MRD‑negative CR with a 3‑year PFS of 42% and no grade 3‑4 CRS/ICANS.
Only two cases of acute GvHD occurred (both grade 2, resolved with steroids/JAK1/2 inhibitor) despite use of unmanipulated donor cells.
CD7 CAR‑T with a protein‑expression blocker induced complete MRD‑negative remissions in two pediatric patients, one remaining disease‑free at 2 years post‑allograft.
GD2 CAR‑T in neuroblastoma (n=35) yielded >50% 5‑year EFS in low‑burden disease, with better outcomes when cells were collected at diagnosis.
Pre‑clinical data showed that knocking down TCR/HLA and CD52 together markedly improves expansion and persistence of allogeneic CAR‑T cells.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Two patients relapsed with CD19‑negative blasts, highlighting antigen‑loss escape.
CD7 CAR‑T efficacy limited by low CD3+ cell counts or high blast contamination preventing product generation.
open_questions: What is the optimal lymphodepletion regimen to balance safety and CAR‑T expansion in allogeneic settings?
Can broader antigen targeting (e.g., dual CARs) further reduce relapse due to antigen loss?

talk_title: Allogeneic CAR NK Cell Platforms and Clinical Data

biomarkers.name: LDH
biomarkers.finding: normalization within 2 weeks post‑infusion in a CD70 CAR‑NK patient

biomarkers.name: ctDNA
biomarkers.finding: undetectable after CAR‑NK therapy in a refractory DLBCL patient

models.name: AFM13 + CAR‑NK in Hodgkin lymphoma
models.genotype_or_key_features: 42 heavily pre‑treated pts, median 7 prior lines

clinical_data.n: n=8 (CD70 CAR‑NK patients with HLA‑A3 mismatch)

MOA_summary: Cord‑blood derived allogeneic NK cells are engineered with an IL‑15 armoured CD70‑CAR (or CD5‑CAR) and a safety switch; they expand robustly in patients, persist for months, and mediate tumor cell killing directly and via ADCC, while a bispecific engager (AFM13) redirects NK cells to CD30‑positive Hodgkin lymphoma, achieving >90% ORR.
key_findings: Cord‑blood derived CD70 CAR‑NK cells from optimal units (frozen ≤24 h, nucleated RBC ≈ 80 M) yielded 70% 1‑year PFS and 95% 1‑year OS, whereas delayed‑freeze units performed poorly.
In a 42‑patient Hodgkin lymphoma study combining AFM13 with CAR‑NK, overall response rate exceeded 90% with two‑thirds achieving CR, despite lack of bridging therapy.
CAR‑NK cells persisted up to 6 months in peripheral blood, with early clonal expansion (clone 0) followed by emergence of a memory‑like clone (clone 4) linked to long‑term persistence.
CD5 CAR‑NK therapy in T‑PLL achieved CR in 3 of 4 heavily pre‑treated patients, with donor NK cells tracking to disease clearance.
Manufacturing from a single cord‑blood unit can produce 250 patient doses at <$600 per dose, enabling off‑the‑shelf scalability.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Patients with cord‑blood units frozen after 24 h showed markedly inferior outcomes, indicating product quality as a critical variable.
Limited NK‑cell lifespan (1‑2 weeks) without cytokine support may restrict durability in non‑engineered NK approaches.

combination_strategies.rationale: Redirects NK cells via CD16 to CD30‑positive Hodgkin lymphoma cells
combination_strategies.phenotype: ORR >90%, CR 66% in heavily pre‑treated patients

combination_strategies.rationale: Plurilink NK cells express high‑affinity CD16 to mediate ADCC
combination_strategies.phenotype: potent tumor eradication in vitro

open_questions: How does the source of NK cells (cord blood vs peripheral blood vs iPSC) affect long‑term persistence and anti‑tumor immunity?
Can IL‑21 armoring further improve NK‑cell metabolism and durability compared with IL‑15?

talk_title: Allogeneic CAR T Strategies to Overcome Rejection and Enhance Efficacy

biomarkers.name: MRD
biomarkers.finding: Patients with MRD‑positive disease selected for ALO‑501A trial

biomarkers.name: ctDNA reduction
biomarkers.finding: Median 98% ctDNA reduction at day 45 in CAR‑T arm vs 26% increase in observation arm

models.name: LO316 CD70 CAR‑T RCC trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: Renal cell carcinoma, high CD70 expression

clinical_data.population: renal cell carcinoma patients with high CD70

MOA_summary: Allogeneic CAR‑T cells are engineered to eliminate TCR (prevent GvHD) and CD52 (allow anti‑CD52 lymphodepletion), or to express HLA‑E and knock‑out β2‑microglobulin (stealth), enabling robust expansion after standard lymphodepletion; CD70 is leveraged both as a tumor antigen and as a target to deplete alloreactive host T cells, improving persistence and efficacy in RCC and lymphoma.
key_findings: In the MRD‑guided ALO‑501A trial, 7 of 8 patients cleared MRD after a single dose of allogeneic CD19 CAR‑T with standard flu‑cyclophosphamide lymphodepletion, without CRS/ICANS.
CD70‑targeted allogeneic CAR‑T (LO316) achieved a 31% ORR in CD70‑high RCC, with durable responses >12 months in most responders.
Gene‑editing approaches (TCR knockout, CD52 knockout, HLA‑E insertion) enable allogeneic CAR‑T expansion comparable to autologous products despite HLA‑agnostic lymphodepletion.
Selective depletion of alloreactive host T cells via CD70 targeting (the “dagger” approach) enhances CAR‑T persistence and reduces rejection.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: CD70 expression on activated host T cells can cause fratricide; a cis‑masking antibody strategy is used to mitigate this.
Patients receiving anti‑CD52 lymphodepletion showed longer CAR‑T persistence than those without.

combination_strategies.rationale: Selective depletion of host lymphocytes to prevent rejection of CD52‑knockout CAR‑T
combination_strategies.phenotype: Extended CAR‑T persistence up to 2‑3 months

combination_strategies.rationale: Create space for CAR‑T expansion
combination_strategies.phenotype: Robust in vivo expansion (Cmax comparable to autologous products)

open_questions: Will dual CD19/CD70 CAR‑T maintain expansion and safety comparable to single‑antigen CARs?
What is the optimal lymphodepletion intensity for MRD‑only settings to balance safety and efficacy?

talk_title: SOX2 as a master regulator of classical squamous cell carcinoma
MOA_summary: SOX2 amplification in classical SCC drives a transcriptional program by maintaining chromatin accessibility at enhancers, forming enhancer‑promoter loops that upregulate ferroptosis suppressors, thereby conferring resistance to ferroptosis.
key_findings: Classical SCC shows focal 3q amplification of SOX2 and a selective dependency on SOX2 for proliferation.
SOX2 knockdown reduces chromatin accessibility at thousands of sites and downregulates the classical SCC gene signature.
SOX2 overexpression restores accessibility and upregulates ferroptosis suppressor genes (GPX2, GSR, AKR1C3, SLC25A10).
SOX2‑deficient classical SCC cells exhibit increased lipid peroxidation and apoptosis upon ferroptosis inducer ML162 treatment.
open_questions: What upstream signals drive SOX2 amplification in the classical subtype?
Can pharmacologic inhibition of SOX2 or its downstream ferroptosis suppressors sensitize tumors to ferroptosis inducers?

talk_title: FOXP2B as a driver of neuroendocrine transformation in lung cancer

speaker.name: Amin Sabit
speaker.affiliation: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Charlie Rudin Lab

targets.dependency_context: EGFR mutant LUAD with p53/Rb loss

MOA_summary: FOXP2B overexpression in EGFR‑mutant LUAD with p53/Rb loss suppresses EGFR signaling and induces neuroendocrine markers, an effect amplified by REST inhibition, suggesting FOXP2B drives neuroendocrine transdifferentiation.
key_findings: FOXP2B is highly up‑regulated in transformed small‑cell samples compared with de novo small‑cell.
FOXP2B overexpression reduces total and phospho‑EGFR levels and induces neuroendocrine markers (ASCL1, synaptophysin, chromogranin) in p53/Rb‑deficient LUAD cells.
Combined FOXP2B overexpression and REST inhibition further enhances neuroendocrine marker expression.
FOXP2B directly binds the EGFR locus, suggesting transcriptional repression of EGFR.

combination_strategies.rationale: REST suppression synergizes with FOXP2B to induce neuroendocrine program
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced neuroendocrine marker expression

open_questions: What upstream signals induce FOXP2B expression during transformation?
Can FOXP2B inhibition prevent or reverse neuroendocrine transdifferentiation in vivo?

talk_title: MBD4 regulates FOXA1 lineage‑specific enhancers to promote prostate tumorigenesis
MOA_summary: MBD4 binds FOXA1‑motif enhancers in AR‑positive prostate cells, restricting FOXA1 pioneer activity; loss of MBD4 expands FOXA1 enhancer occupancy, reprogramming AR signaling toward an oncogenic program and altering lineage specification.
key_findings: CRISPR screen identified MBD4 as a top gene whose loss rescues growth suppression caused by AR‑ARE activation.
MBD4 knockout in LNCaP cells expands FOXA1‑motif enhancer accessibility and increases AR and H3K27ac signals at these sites.
Overexpression of MBD4 reduces FOXA1 enhancer sites, while knockout in normal prostate organoids drives luminal differentiation (CK8/18 up, CHOP2 down).
MBD4 interacts with HDAC1/2, suggesting a deacetylase‑mediated mechanism restricting FOXA1 activity.
open_questions: Does pharmacologic inhibition of MBD4 phenocopy the genetic knockout in prostate cancer models?
How does the MBD4‑HDAC interaction mechanistically suppress FOXA1 pioneer function?

talk_title: First‑in‑class oral small molecule DPX8743 dissolves MYC condensates

biomarkers.name: MYC protein level
biomarkers.finding: Higher MYC expression correlates with increased DPX8743 sensitivity

MOA_summary: DPX8743 selectively dissolves MYC‑containing transcriptional condensates in MYC‑high cancer cells, leading to down‑regulation of MYC target genes, G1 cell‑cycle arrest, and apoptosis, while sparing global transcriptional condensates.
key_findings: DPX8743 dissolves MYC condensates in MYC‑high tumor cells but not in normal cells with basal MYC levels.
Condensate dissolution correlates with G1 arrest, reduced MYC target expression, and tumor regression in MYC‑amplified CDX/PDX models.
Compound activity is potentiated by higher MYC protein levels and MYC genetic dependency; MYC knockdown desensitizes cells.
Seven‑day rat toxicology shows no weight loss or major organ toxicity except reversible thymus atrophy, indicating a therapeutic index >4×.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity achieved by phenotypic condensate screening; pan‑transcription inhibitors lack this specificity.
open_questions: What are the precise upstream protein targets of DPX8743 that mediate condensate dissolution?
Can DPX8743 be combined with standard MYC‑dependent therapies to enhance efficacy?

talk_title: NSD3 as a co‑factor stabilizing BRD4‑NUT condensates in NUT carcinoma
MOA_summary: NSD3 deposits H3K36me2 within BRD4‑NUT mega‑domains, stabilizing BRD4‑NUT occupancy and nuclear condensate intensity, thereby maintaining long‑range chromatin contacts and MYC‑driven transcriptional programs in NUT carcinoma.
key_findings: NSD3 interacts with BRD4‑NUT and deposits H3K36me2 at mega‑domains.
NSD3 knockdown reduces H3K36me2, diminishes BRD4‑NUT chromatin binding, and lowers condensate intensity while increasing condensate number.
Hi‑C reveals ~2‑fold loss of long‑range contacts between mega‑domains upon NSD3 depletion.
Genes within mega‑domains, especially MYC targets, are down‑regulated, leading to modest growth inhibition and differentiation markers up‑regulation.

combination_strategies.rationale: Dual targeting may overcome toxicity of BRD4 inhibitors alone
combination_strategies.phenotype: potential synergistic reduction of BRD4‑NUT activity

open_questions: Will pharmacologic NSD3 inhibition recapitulate genetic knockdown effects in vivo?
Are other NSD family members compensatory in NUT carcinoma?

talk_title: Extrachromosomal DNA generates oncogenic fusion transcripts such as PVT1‑MYC

biomarkers.name: PVT1‑MYC fusion RNA
biomarkers.finding: Detected in 72% of ecDNA‑positive cancers, improves ecDNA detection accuracy to 95%

MOA_summary: ecDNA harbors high‑frequency structural rearrangements that generate fusion transcripts; the PVT1‑MYC fusion places PVT1 as a 5′ UTR, enhancing MYC mRNA stability and translation, leading to amplified MYC target activation and a proliferative advantage.
readouts: TCGA and CCLE whole‑genome & RNA‑seq analysis
key_findings: ecDNA exhibits the highest DNA and RNA fusion frequencies across cancers.
PVT1‑MYC is the most prevalent fusion on ecDNA, with >98% of fusions placing PVT1 at the 5′ end.
PVT1‑MYC transcripts show greater RNA stability and drive six‑fold stronger MYC‑target activation than canonical MYC.
In MYC‑depleted cells, PVT1‑MYC rescues viability more efficiently than canonical MYC.
open_questions: Can PVT1‑MYC fusion peptides serve as neo‑antigens for immunotherapy?
What mechanisms regulate the initial formation of ecDNA‑driven fusions?

talk_title: VNTR2‑1 antisense transcript regulates DNA replication via METTL3‑dependent translation

speaker.name: Brennan Papenberg
speaker.affiliation: National Cancer Institute, Division of Cancer Epidemiology and Genetics

MOA_summary: The VNTR2‑1 repeat in TERT intron 2 encodes an antisense transcript with an open reading frame whose length scales with repeat number; METTL3‑mediated m6A modification enhances its translation, driving DNA replication gene programs and promoting cell proliferation.
key_findings: VNTR2‑1 antisense transcript is expressed and contains multiple DRACH motifs for METTL3‑dependent m6A modification.
M6A‑intact reporter shows higher luciferase activity; METTL3 inhibition reduces this activity.
Flag‑tagged VNTR2‑1AS construct produces protein, confirming translational competence.
Overexpression of VNTR2‑1AS up‑regulates DNA replication and cell‑cycle pathways; knockout reduces these pathways and slows proliferation.
Translation‑deficient start‑codon mutant fails to enhance proliferation, indicating translation‑dependent function.
open_questions: Does VNTR2‑1AS affect TERT expression or telomere biology?
Can targeting METTL3 or the VNTR2‑1AS peptide provide therapeutic benefit in VNTR‑expanded cancers?

talk_title: AACR Progress and the GLITZ Clinical Trial

biomarkers.name: multi-biomarker informed treatment approach
biomarkers.finding: Assessing feasibility to spare gastroesophageal cancer patients from life-altering surgery.

MOA_summary: The speaker introduced the GLITZ clinical trial, which utilizes a multi-biomarker informed treatment approach to determine if patients with gastroesophageal cancer can safely avoid surgery. The trial's IND application has been approved by the FDA and Health Canada.
key_findings: The GLITZ Clinical Trial IND application was approved by the US FDA and Health Canada to assess multi-biomarker informed treatment in gastroesophageal cancer.
Cancer mortality has decreased by nearly 35% since the peak in the 1990s.
The number of cancer survivors in the US has increased by approximately 6.6 million over the last 15 years.
Over 20 new anticancer therapeutics were FDA approved between July 2024 and June 2025.

talk_title: Targeting Programmed Cell Death in Leukemia
MOA_summary: The speaker described the translational journey from basic protein chemistry to the clinical application of targeting programmed cell death pathways. This work culminated in the development and use of venetoclax for the treatment of AML and CLL.
key_findings: Targeting programmed cell death pathways is an effective strategy for treating chronic lymphocytic leukemia and acute myelogenous leukemia.
Venetoclax was successfully translated from laboratory research into a standard of care for AML patients.

talk_title: How Cancer Cells Escape Targeted Therapy by Changing Identity

speaker.name: Dr. Charles L. Sawyers
speaker.affiliation: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Chair of Human Oncology and Pathogenesis Program

models.name: RB/TP53/PTEN floxed mouse
models.genotype_or_key_features: prostate‑specific Cre‑mediated deletion of RB with PTEN and/or TP53

models.name: prostate organoid
models.genotype_or_key_features: RB loss, TP53 loss, MYC overexpression

MOA_summary: Loss of RB1 primes prostate adenocarcinoma cells for lineage plasticity; extracellular matrix engagement via integrin‑β1 restrains YAP/TAZ‑TEAD activity, while Notch signaling also suppresses neuroendocrine transition. Disruption of matrix signaling, inhibition of LATS kinase, or pharmacologic blockade of YAP/TEAD or Notch releases this brake, inducing ASCL1 expression and a neuroendocrine phenotype that can be reversed pharmacologically. FOXA1 relocalization and TEAD binding rewire chromatin, enabling MYC paralog‑TIP60 co‑activator dependence in the neuroendocrine state.
key_findings: RB loss combined with PTEN or TP53 loss drives adenocarcinoma-to‑neuroendocrine transition in mouse prostate models.
Matrix withdrawal or β1‑integrin deletion induces ASCL1 expression within 7 days in organoids.
LATS inhibition restores YAP activity and blocks ASCL1 up‑regulation, demonstrating reversibility of lineage transition.
Combined TEAD and Notch inhibition drives >90% of organoid cells to a neuroendocrine state.
FOXA1 relocalization to TEAD‑bound enhancers is required for transition; FOXA1 knockout prevents neuroendocrine differentiation.
Neuroendocrine cells become dependent on L‑MYC/TIP60 partnership, revealing a potential therapeutic vulnerability.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Lineage plasticity creates resistance to androgen‑targeted therapy by bypassing AR dependence.
Neuroendocrine transition may render tumors resistant to AR‑directed agents but creates new antigenic targets (e.g., DLL3).

combination_strategies.rationale: Release brake on YAP/TAZ to force neuroendocrine transition for targeted DLL3 CAR/T‑cell therapy.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Accelerated ASCL1 expression and neuroendocrine phenotype

combination_strategies.rationale: Synergize with TEAD inhibition to achieve near‑complete neuroendocrine conversion.
combination_strategies.phenotype: ≈100% ASCL1‑positive cells in organoids

open_questions: Can pharmacologic reversal of neuroendocrine state improve response to AR‑targeted therapies?
What are the optimal timing and sequencing of TEAD/Notch inhibition with immunotherapies such as DLL3‑CAR T cells?

talk_title: Beyond Blood: The Evolution of CAR T Therapy for Solid Malignancies

speaker.name: Dr. Carl H. June
speaker.affiliation: University of Pennsylvania, Perelman School of Medicine, Director of Parker Institute for Cancer Immunotherapy

biomarkers.name: NK cell infiltration
biomarkers.finding: Higher baseline NK cell proportion correlated with longer PFS in glioblastoma CAR T trial

biomarkers.name: Treg density
biomarkers.finding: Higher Treg levels associated with poorer response and shorter PFS

MOA_summary: Engineered autologous T cells expressing chimeric antigen receptors target tumor‑associated antigens (e.g., CD19, mesothelin, claudin‑18.2, EGFRvIII/IL‑13Rα2). Armoring CARs with cytokines (IL‑18) or delivering CAR constructs via lipid nanoparticles enhances persistence and stromal targeting (anti‑FAP). Dual‑target CARs and regional delivery (Ommaya reservoir) improve trafficking and efficacy in solid tumors such as glioblastoma, while NK cell infiltration and low Treg density correlate with better outcomes.
key_findings: IL‑18‑armored CAR T cells achieved 75% complete response in CAR‑refractory lymphoma with no increase in CRS.
Dual‑target EGFRvIII/IL‑13Rα2 CAR T delivered via Ommaya achieved 2 PRs and ~70% disease control in 18 glioblastoma patients.
Higher intratumoral NK cell levels predicted longer PFS, whereas higher Treg levels predicted resistance in glioblastoma CAR T trials.
Sequential anti‑FAP followed by anti‑mesothelin CAR T in KPC mice markedly improved survival compared with either monotherapy.
Lipid‑nanoparticle‑delivered anti‑FAP CAR RNA achieved superior tumor control versus retroviral CAR T in pancreatic cancer models.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: CAR T cells show limited infiltration and rapid exhaustion in dense pancreatic stroma.
Neuroendocrine lineage plasticity in prostate cancer creates heterogeneous antigen expression, reducing efficacy of DLL3‑CAR T.

combination_strategies.rationale: Reduce CRS while enhancing CAR T expansion and activity
combination_strategies.phenotype: 75% CR in refractory lymphoma

combination_strategies.rationale: Deplete fibroblasts to improve CAR T trafficking into pancreatic tumors
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced tumor control and survival in KPC mice

combination_strategies.rationale: Synergize with TEAD inhibition to drive neuroendocrine transition (preclinical context)
combination_strategies.phenotype: ≈100% ASCL1+ cells

open_questions: What are the optimal biomarkers to predict CAR T persistence in solid tumors?
Can transient stromal depletion combined with CAR T lead to durable responses in patients?

cited_papers: Rork et al. 2023 J Clin Oncol

talk_title: Chemical Rewiring of Biological Circuits for Cancer Therapy: Targeted Protein Degradation and Beyond

speaker.name: Dr. Georg E. Winter
speaker.affiliation: CeMM Research Center for Molecular Medicine; Aithera Research Institute for Biomedical AI, Austrian Academy of Sciences

MOA_summary: PROTACs and molecular glues induce proximity between an E3 ligase (e.g., CRBN, VHL, FBXO3, DCAF16) and a target protein, leading to ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation. Novel mechanisms include intermolecular bivalent gluing (IBG‑1 bridges two bromodomains of RAD4 to recruit DCAF16) and inhibitor‑induced supercharging of native degradation pathways (kinase inhibitors promote native E3 ligase recognition). SERDs such as fulvestrant act by inducing SUMOylation of ERα, which then triggers RNF4/RNF111‑mediated ubiquitination; however, degradation is dispensable for anti‑estrogenic activity, while SUMOylation is essential for transcriptional repression.
key_findings: Identified UBE2M as a global regulator of >200 E3 ligases, enabling phenotypic discovery of molecular glues.
Discovered DHTC3, a molecular glue that recruits FBXO3 to degrade BET4 via a chemically derived exit vector.
Revealed IBG‑1 acts via intermolecular bivalent gluing, bridging two RAD4 bromodomains and recruiting DCAF16 despite low affinity.
Showed that many kinase inhibitors cause “supercharging” of native degradation, leading to target destabilization without a dedicated E3 ligase.
Demonstrated that fulvestrant induces SUMOylation of ERα, which is required for transcriptional repression, while degradation by RNF4/RNF111 is dispensable.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Molecular glues can target previously undruggable proteins by reshaping ligase surfaces.
Kinase inhibitor‑induced degradation may be context‑dependent, limiting broad applicability.
open_questions: Can SUMOylation‑inducing small molecules be generalized to other oncogenic transcription factors?
What determines the selectivity of inhibitor‑induced supercharging across the kinome?

talk_title: Rethinking Cancer Diagnosis and Treatment with AI from Molecular Mechanisms to Clinical Management

speaker.name: Dr. Regina Barzilay
speaker.affiliation: MIT, School of Engineering Distinguished Professor for AI and Health, Jameel Clinic

models.name: Mammography cohort (1.3 M exams)
models.genotype_or_key_features: linked outcomes for breast cancer risk

clinical_data.n: n=45% of US women (dense breast notification)

MOA_summary: Deep learning models (e.g., SPARK, Mirai) integrate routine pathology slides or mammograms with spatial transcriptomics to predict molecular pathway activity and long‑term clinical outcomes. The models learn patch‑level annotations of ~40 molecular programs from paired imaging‑omics data, enabling risk stratification, early detection, and mechanistic insight into tumor biology.
key_findings: SPARK predicts 40 molecular programs from routine pathology slides with AUC ~0.90 and Pearson ~0.74.
Adding program-level information to image features improves prediction of disease‑free survival and treatment response across multiple cancer types.
Mirai mammography model achieves a 5‑year c‑index of 0.80, outperforming density (c‑index 0.50) and questionnaire‑based risk models (c‑index 0.63).
High‑risk patients identified by Mirai correspond to risk levels exceeding those of BRCA1 carriers.
Prospective trials (UMass, UCSF, Israel, MiPEBS) are underway to evaluate AI‑driven risk‑adapted screening.
open_questions: How can AI‑derived molecular program maps be prospectively validated as predictive biomarkers for therapy selection?
What regulatory frameworks are needed to integrate AI risk models into standard screening guidelines?

talk_title: Mechanistic analysis of CTLA-4 and PD-1 checkpoint inhibitor colitis in human patients

targets.dependency_context: dual checkpoint blockade patients with colitis
dual checkpoint blockade patients with colitis

models.name: colitis biopsy cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 24 patients receiving dual CTLA-4/PD-1 blockade, biopsied within days of symptom onset

MOA_summary: CTLA-4 and PD-1 blockade induce colitis by reactivating resident memory CD8 T cells and expanding Th1/Th17 CD4 effector populations; regulatory T cells are not depleted in human colitis, and S1PR1+ Tregs expand uniquely in CTLA-4 colitis, suggesting distinct mechanistic pathways for each checkpoint.
key_findings: Both CTLA-4 and PD-1 colitis show expansion of resident memory CD8 T cells and Th1/Th17 CD4 effector cells.
Regulatory T cells are broadly expanded rather than depleted in human colitis biopsies.
CTLA-4 colitis uniquely expands S1PR1+ Treg populations not seen in PD-1 colitis.
Clonotypic expansion is observed in effector cells, with resident memory clones dominating in healthy controls and effector clones dominating in colitis.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: No depletion of Tregs observed with CTLA-4 blockade in human colitis, contrary to mouse models.
S1PR1+ Tregs may represent a compensatory but insufficient regulatory response.
open_questions: What is the functional role of the expanded S1PR1+ Treg population in CTLA-4 colitis?
Can peripheral blood TCR signatures predict imminent colitis before tissue symptoms appear?

talk_title: Wild microbiota–driven CTLA-4 blockade colitis model and Fc‑dependent toxicity separation

targets.dependency_context: combined with CTLA‑4 blockade in mouse tumor models

models.name: wild‑microbiota colonized C57BL/6 mice
models.genotype_or_key_features: germ‑free or antibiotic‑treated mice gavaged with wild‑caught mouse microbiota; CD4‑dependent colitis after anti‑CTLA‑4

models.name: M38 tumor model
models.genotype_or_key_features: CTLA‑4 antibody with Fc vs Fc‑null (H11) extender; tumor growth inhibition assessed

models.name: C26 tumor model
models.genotype_or_key_features: Combination CTLA‑4 + PD‑1 blockade; tumor suppression with or without colitis

MOA_summary: Gavage of wild mouse microbiota into antibiotic‑treated mice creates a microbiota‑dependent, CD4‑Th1–driven colitis upon CTLA‑4 blockade; Fc‑effector function of the anti‑CTLA‑4 antibody is required for colitis but not for anti‑tumor efficacy, allowing separation of toxicity from therapeutic benefit.
key_findings: Wild‑derived microbiota, but not standard SPF microbiota, enables severe colitis after anti‑CTLA‑4 treatment.
Colitis is CD4‑Th1 dependent; depletion of CD4 T cells or neutralization of IFN‑γ abolishes disease.
Peripheral Treg depletion (Helios‑negative FoxP3+ cells) occurs with CTLA‑4 antibody; thymic Tregs remain.
Fc‑null CTLA‑4 antibody (H11 extender) retains anti‑tumor activity while eliminating colitis, demonstrating Fc‑dependence of toxicity.
Clonotypic analysis reveals oligoclonal expansion of Th1 cells recognizing microbial antigens in colitic mice.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Fc‑mediated depletion of peripheral Tregs drives colitis; Fc‑null antibodies avoid this effect.
Microbiota composition dictates susceptibility to checkpoint‑induced colitis.

combination_strategies.rationale: Neutralize Th1 cytokine driving colitis
combination_strategies.phenotype: Complete abrogation of colitis despite CTLA‑4 blockade

combination_strategies.rationale: Separate anti‑tumor efficacy from Fc‑dependent toxicity
combination_strategies.phenotype: Tumor growth inhibition retained; colitis absent

open_questions: Which specific microbial antigens are recognized by the oligoclonal Th1 repertoire?
Can the Fc‑null strategy be translated to human CTLA‑4 antibodies without loss of efficacy in diverse tumor types?
What is the role of the residual peripheral Tregs in tumor control versus toxicity?

cited_papers: Nunez et al. 2024 Immune checkpoint blockade induce colitis is microbiota dependent

talk_title: Bench to bedside in epithelioid hemangioendothelioma (EHE)

biomarkers.name: CAMTA1 IHC
biomarkers.finding: Specific nuclear staining identifies TAZ-CAMTA1 fusion positive EHE

MOA_summary: The TAZ-CAMTA1 fusion drives EHE by constitutively activating the Hippo pathway effector TAZ, leading to nuclear TEAD transcriptional programs. Trametinib inhibits MAPK signaling downstream of the fusion, reducing colony formation in vitro and tumor growth in mouse models. CDK9 inhibition (dinaciclib) disrupts transcriptional elongation complexes essential for fusion-driven transcription, showing synergistic tumor regression when combined with MEK inhibition.
key_findings: TAZ-CAMTA1 fusion activates MAPK pathway; MEK inhibitor trametinib shows IC50 <10 nM in colony assays.
Phase 2 trial of trametinib in unresectable/metastatic EHE yielded 20 responders among 24 patients but did not meet primary endpoint for FDA approval.
Genetically engineered TAZ-CAMTA1 homozygous mice develop histologically identical EHE tumors, providing a faithful preclinical model.
CDK9 inhibitors (dinaciclib, CGP) induce apoptosis in EHE cell lines and dramatically reduce lung tumor burden in xenograft models.
Combination of MEK inhibition with TEAD inhibition (MGH CP1) shows synergistic growth suppression in vitro.
NMR‑based fragment screening identified small‑molecule hits targeting TEAD interface pockets, achieving sub‑micromolar affinity after optimization.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Trametinib toxicity limited treatment duration in some patients.
CDK9 inhibition appears selective for EHE cells due to dependence on transcriptional elongation complex.

combination_strategies.rationale: Dual blockade of Hippo downstream transcriptional output enhances anti‑tumor effect.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic inhibition of colony formation in EHE cell lines

combination_strategies.rationale: Target MAPK activation downstream of TAZ‑CAMTA1 fusion.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Significant tumor regression in mouse xenografts

open_questions: What is the precise functional contribution of the CAMTA1 portion of the fusion to oncogenesis?
Can TEAD inhibitors be optimized for clinical use in EHE patients?
What mechanisms underlie trametinib resistance in EHE?

cited_papers: Rubin et al. 2024 Clinical Cancer Research

talk_title: Project Cure CRC Integrated Adaptive Clinical Trial Platform

speaker.name: Kim Newcomer
speaker.affiliation: Colorectal Cancer Alliance, Senior Director of Research Programs

clinical_data.population: Project Cure CRC past two years

MOA_summary: The Alliance has built an integrated ecosystem (BlueHQ, Blue Lake, adaptive trial) that links patient-reported data, genomic profiling, and real-world outcomes to accelerate colorectal cancer drug development, with patients shaping trial windows and governance.
key_findings: Received 525 research proposals in the past cycle.
Allocated $15 M to 34 projects across 150 investigators.
Engaged 20 industry partners in the adaptive trial platform.
Goal to increase 5‑year metastatic CRC survival from 13% to 35% within five years.

talk_title: Lineberger Excellence in Advocacy Program Impact Metrics

speaker.name: Lourdes Baiz Conde-Carbonatti
speaker.affiliation: USC Norris Cancer Center, Associate Director of Community Outreach and Engagement

MOA_summary: LEAP consolidates multiple patient/community advisory structures into a single program that tracks engagement metrics, provides training, matches advocates to research projects, and integrates patient voices into grant review and protocol development at a comprehensive cancer center.
key_findings: Advocate pool composition: 83% female, 17% male.
Processed 86 matching requests with 250 advocate responses.
Reviewed 328 pilot grants with participation of 36 individual advocates.
Implemented systematic evaluation surveys with >90% positive scores on respect and impact.
