speaker.name: Annie Ellis
speaker.affiliation: Ovarian Cancer Research Alliance, Survivor Advocate

clinical_data.population: Matchmakers trainees surveyed on advocate impact

MOA_summary: The Matchmakers team integrates patient advocates into a preclinical T‑cell receptor engineering program, providing monthly meetings, co‑created educational deliverables, and bidirectional feedback that improves scientific relevance and trainee communication skills.
key_findings: 70+ patient advocates contributed to 21 preclinical immunotherapy teams.
Advocate involvement increased trainee awareness of patient relevance from 0% to ~50% in surveys.
Co‑created lay summaries, glossaries, and videos improved communication of complex TCR science.
Advocates reported enhanced understanding of basic research, while trainees noted better non‑scientist communication.

talk_title: Spatial single-cell analysis of supratentorial ependymoma subtypes

models.name: 60+ fresh/frozen supratentorial ependymoma tumors
models.genotype_or_key_features: YAP1 fusion, CEFTA/RELA fusions, various subclusters

models.name: neurosphere and laminin cultures
models.genotype_or_key_features: stem cell media, co-culture with astrocytes/neurons

MOA_summary: Single-cell RNA‑seq and 10x Xenium spatial transcriptomics were used to map developmental cell states across supratentorial ependymoma subtypes, revealing that YAP1‑driven tumors are differentiated ependymal‑like, while CEFTA/RELA tumors retain neuroepithelial and embryonic‑like stem states; spatial organization correlates with a mesenchymal‑hypoxia cell state, and in vitro/in vivo models show that differentiation requires microenvironmental cues.
key_findings: Assembled a cohort of >60 supratentorial ependymoma samples covering all molecular subclusters.
YAP1‑fusion tumors project to later developmental stages and consist almost exclusively of differentiated ependymal‑like cells.
CEFTA/RELA‑fusion tumors contain a mixture of neuroepithelial‑like stem cells, embryonic‑like cells, and differentiated ependymal/neuronal states.
Spatial transcriptomics identified two global tumor architectures (organized vs disorganized) driven by the presence of a mesenchymal‑hypoxia cell state.
In vitro cultures grown as PDX or neuron/astrocyte co‑cultures recapitulate the full differentiation hierarchy, whereas neurosphere‑only cultures enrich for stem‑like cells.

In vivo two‑photon imaging showed distinct morphologies and migration patterns: stem‑like cells are slow/proliferative, neuronal‑like cells display saltatory migration, and ependymal‑like cells are stationary with dynamic projections.
open_questions: What molecular mechanisms drive the transition from organized to disorganized spatial architecture over time?
Do specific cell states confer resistance to radiation or future targeted therapies?

talk_title: Targeting ACOD1‑itaconate axis to suppress ZFTA‑RELA fusion in ependymoma

models.name: mouse neuronal stem cells expressing ZFTA/RELA
IOE immune‑intact mouse model of ZFTA/RELA ependymoma

MOA_summary: ZFTA/RELA fusion upregulates ACOD1, leading to increased production of itaconate; itaconate acts as an oncometabolite that inhibits KDM5 histone demethylases, raising H3K4me3 at the fusion promoter and sustaining fusion expression. Pharmacologic or genetic inhibition of ACOD1 (dimethyl itaconate, citraconate analogs) or upstream glutamine metabolism (GHU‑083) reduces itaconate levels, lowers H3K4me3, down‑regulates fusion mRNA and protein, and prolongs survival in mouse models.
key_findings: ZFTA/RELA fusion drives strong up‑regulation of ACOD1 and accumulation of itaconate in both mouse and human ependymoma cells.
shRNA knockdown of the fusion or ACOD1 reduces cell viability and ACOD1 expression.
ACOD1 whole‑body or tumor‑cell specific knockout in mice dramatically improves survival (median age 25 days → >50 % long‑term survivors).
Dimethyl itaconate treatment lowers fusion mRNA and protein levels in vitro and in vivo; rescue with exogenous itaconate restores fusion expression.
Itaconate inhibits KDM5 demethylases (IC50 ~470 µM), increasing H3K4me3; KDM5 inhibition phenocopies itaconate’s effect on fusion expression.
Glutamine antagonist GHU‑083 reduces itaconate production, lowers fusion levels, and extends survival in IOE mouse models.
Pharmacologic ACOD1 inhibition prevents spinal metastasis in treated mice (0/7 vs 7/7 in controls).
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Partial survival benefit in ACOD1 knockout mice suggests residual ACOD1 allele may sustain tumor in ~50 % of animals.

combination_strategies.rationale: Both ACOD1 inhibition and KDM5 inhibition increase H3K4me3, driving fusion downregulation
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic reduction of fusion protein levels in vitro

combination_strategies.rationale: Blocks upstream substrate for itaconate synthesis
combination_strategies.phenotype: Survival benefit comparable to ACOD1 knockout in mouse models

open_questions: Can selective ACOD1 inhibitors with better brain penetration be developed for clinical use?
What is the impact of ACOD1 inhibition on normal immune cells and macrophage function in patients?

talk_title: Anti‑GM‑CSF therapy reverses immunotolerance in pediatric brain tumors

targets.dependency_context: tumor‑derived CSF in mouse models of ependymoma, CPC, medulloblastoma

biomarkers.name: CSF GM‑CSF concentration
biomarkers.finding: Reduced after Lenzilumab treatment in all three tumor models

models.name: preclinical mouse models (ZFTA‑RELA ependymoma, CPC, group 3 medulloblastoma)

clinical_data.population: mouse models of ependymoma, CPC, medulloblastoma

MOA_summary: Tumor‑derived GM‑CSF in the CSF drives expansion of MHC‑II⁺ hematopoietic stem cells in calvarial bone marrow, which differentiate into immunosuppressive myeloid cells and promote T‑reg development; intrathecal or intravenous Lenzilumab blocks GM‑CSF, reducing stem‑cell proliferation, myeloid suppression, and restoring CD8⁺ T‑cell anti‑tumor activity, leading to tumor regression in multiple pediatric brain tumor models.
key_findings: CSF of tumor‑bearing mice contains high GM‑CSF levels, absent in tumor‑free controls.
GM‑CSF promotes expansion of CD34⁺MHC‑II⁺ hematopoietic stem cells in calvarial marrow, which migrate to dura and tumor.
These stem cells differentiate into immunosuppressive myeloid cells; CD4⁺ T cells skew toward FoxP3⁺ Tregs.
Intrathecal Lenzilumab lowers CSF GM‑CSF, reduces skull HSC proliferation, decreases tumor‑infiltrating myeloid cells, and increases CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration.
Tumor regression observed in ZFTA‑RELA ependymoma, choroid plexus carcinoma, and group 3 medulloblastoma models; effect replicated with intravenous dosing.
Combination of Lenzilumab with checkpoint inhibition prolongs remission beyond monotherapy.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Tumors eventually relapse with CD8⁺ T‑cell exhaustion, suggesting need for checkpoint blockade.

combination_strategies.rationale: Prevent CD8⁺ T‑cell exhaustion after GM‑CSF blockade
combination_strategies.phenotype: Prolonged remission in mouse models

open_questions: Will anti‑GM‑CSF therapy be effective in adult glioma patients with similar HSC expansion?
What are the specific antigens presented by the MHC‑II⁺ HSCs in the tumor context?

talk_title: Dysregulated AKT Signaling Reprograms Osteosarcoma to Drive Selective Reliance on EP300

biomarkers.name: PTEN loss
biomarkers.finding: All JQUAD1-sensitive osteosarcoma lines harbored PTEN loss or AKT amplification

biomarkers.name: AKT activation signature
biomarkers.finding: High AKT signaling correlated with JQUAD1 sensitivity across 800 cell lines

models.name: low-passage osteosarcoma PDX-derived cell lines
models.genotype_or_key_features: PTEN loss or AKT amplification in sensitive lines

MOA_summary: JQUAD1 is a cereblon-recruiting PROTAC that selectively degrades EP300, sparing CBP, leading to loss of enhancer acetylation and transcriptional reprogramming in AKT‑activated osteosarcoma. Co‑inhibition of AKT with MK‑2206 or capivasertib reduces translation rates, enhancing EP300 degradation and synergistic tumor growth inhibition.
key_findings: JQUAD1 selectively degrades EP300 with minimal effect on CBP in vitro.
AKT‑dysregulated osteosarcoma lines (PTEN loss or AKT amplification) are uniquely sensitive to JQUAD1.
Combining JQUAD1 with AKT inhibitors (MK‑2206 or capivasertib) yields synergistic cell death in ~10‑15% of 800 screened cell lines.
Synergy is linked to reduced translational capacity in sensitive lines, as shown by puromycin incorporation assays.
In orthotopic osteosarcoma xenografts, the JQUAD1 + MK‑2206 combination markedly suppresses tumor growth and prolongs survival compared to either agent alone.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Resistance associated with high basal translational rates and lack of AKT activation.
GPC2 downregulation not observed; resistance linked to MDSC infiltration in later studies (other talks).

combination_strategies.rationale: AKT inhibition lowers translation, enhancing EP300 degradation by JQUAD1
combination_strategies.phenotype: synergistic tumor regression in osteosarcoma xenografts

combination_strategies.rationale: Same as MK-2206, FDA‑approved AKT inhibitor
combination_strategies.phenotype: greater synergy than MK-2206 in cell line screens

open_questions: Can EP300 degradation be achieved safely in pediatric patients?
What biomarkers predict durable response beyond AKT activation?

talk_title: Cell‑free DNA Profiling in Relapsed/Refractory Neuroblastoma

biomarkers.name: MYCN amplification
biomarkers.finding: Associated with poorer outcome in BEACON cohort

biomarkers.name: cfDNA quantity
biomarkers.finding: Patients with cfDNA above cohort cutoff had worse survival

clinical_data.metric: response rate with dinutuximab beta backbone

MOA_summary: Low‑coverage whole‑genome and high‑coverage exome sequencing of plasma cfDNA enables detection of tumor‑specific copy‑number alterations and SNVs, allowing longitudinal monitoring of clonal dynamics, emergence of resistance mutations (e.g., ALK, FGFR1), and prognostic assessment via cfDNA quantity in relapsed/refractory neuroblastoma.
key_findings: cfDNA quantity at enrollment predicts poorer survival independent of disease burden.
Relapsed neuroblastoma shows enrichment of ALK and RAS/MAPK pathway mutations, including FGFR1.
Longitudinal cfDNA dynamics correlate with treatment response: decrease during response, re‑increase at progression.
Clonal hematopoiesis events (e.g., JAK3) were detected in cfDNA, highlighting need for careful interpretation.
Predicted gene expression from cfDNA fragment patterns identifies activation of oncogenes (e.g., ALK, TERT) at relapse.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Emergence of subclonal RAS/MAPK mutations during therapy suggests adaptive resistance.
open_questions: Can cfDNA‑guided early intervention improve outcomes?
What is the functional impact of identified subclonal mutations?

talk_title: Spatial TCR Profiling Reveals Barriers to T Cell Engagement in High‑Risk Neuroblastoma

speaker.name: January Zhang
speaker.affiliation: Princess Margaret Cancer Centre, University of Toronto

biomarkers.name: LAG3 expression
biomarkers.finding: Higher in T cells located in neural blast‑rich domains

models.name: AMBL1531 (131I‑MIBG) trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: high‑risk neuroblastoma, paired diagnostic and resection FFPE

MOA_summary: Custom Xenium spatial transcriptomics probes track specific TCR clonotypes in situ, revealing that gamma‑delta T cells preferentially infiltrate neural‑blast rich tumor domains and adopt an exhausted phenotype (LAG3 high), while alpha‑beta clones show variable localization, linking spatial context to T‑cell functional states in high‑risk neuroblastoma.
key_findings: Gamma‑delta T cells are the most spatially privileged population, with ~45% of nearest neighbors being neural blast cells.
T cells in neural‑blast rich domains express exhaustion marker LAG3, whereas those in immune‑rich domains express activation markers CD4 and FOS.
Cross‑patient gamma‑delta clone 2 is detected in tumor, blood, and cfDNA across multiple patients and time points, often adjacent to tumor cells.
Alpha‑beta TCR pairing can be inferred by co‑detection of alpha and beta probes within the same cell, confirming clonotype identity in situ.
Predicted antigen specificity for clone 2 includes NY‑ESO‑1, present in ~23% of neuroblastoma tumors.
open_questions: Do gamma‑delta clones exert functional anti‑tumor activity or are they bystanders?
Can targeting NY‑ESO‑1 improve T‑cell mediated clearance in neuroblastoma?

talk_title: Co‑targeting GPC2 and B7H3 with Bicistronic CAR T Cells in Neuroblastoma and Medulloblastoma

clinical_data.value: complete responses in majority of mice
higher peripheral expansion with bicistronic CARs

MOA_summary: Bicistronic CAR T cells co‑express GPC2‑specific and B7H3‑specific CARs in a single vector, enabling simultaneous targeting of two tumor antigens; this prevents antigen escape (GPC2 down‑regulation) and improves T‑cell proliferation, cytokine secretion, and tumor infiltration, leading to durable tumor control in neuroblastoma and medulloblastoma PDX models.
key_findings: Bicistronic GPC2/B7H3 CAR T cells outperform single‑antigen CARs and co‑transduced mixtures in vitro and in vivo.
Dual CARs achieve complete tumor regressions and long‑term survival in neuroblastoma and medulloblastoma PDX models.
B7H3 expression remains stable under CAR pressure, while GPC2 is frequently down‑regulated, supporting the need for dual targeting.
Mice cured with dual CARs retain persisting memory T cells in bone marrow, conferring protection against rechallenge.
Relapse after dual CAR therapy is associated with increased MDSC infiltration.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: GPC2 down‑regulation observed across all treatment groups.
MDSC infiltration linked to relapse in a subset of dual‑CAR treated mice.

combination_strategies.rationale: MDSCs may suppress CAR T efficacy in relapsed tumors
combination_strategies.phenotype: potentially improved durability

open_questions: Will bicistronic CARs retain efficacy in immune‑competent models?
What mechanisms drive GPC2 down‑regulation under CAR pressure?

cited_papers: Dotti et al. bicistronic CAR study

talk_title: Surface‑Localized Nuclear Envelope Proteins as Targets in MYC‑Driven Group 3 Medulloblastoma

biomarkers.name: LBR expression
biomarkers.finding: Higher LBR correlates with MYC amplification and recurrence

MOA_summary: Single‑domain camelid antibodies against LBR and TMPO are engineered into second‑generation CARs; these antigens are aberrantly presented on the plasma membrane of MYC‑driven Group 3 medulloblastoma due to nuclear envelope fragmentation, enabling CAR‑T mediated cytotoxicity against a proliferative tumor subpopulation.
readouts: in vivo tumor clearance in mouse xenografts
RNA‑seq of LBR+ vs LBR‑ sorted cells
key_findings: LBR and TMPO are mislocalized to the cell surface specifically in MYC‑driven Group 3 medulloblastoma, not in normal neural cells.
LBR+ tumor cells display high proliferation signatures (G2/M enrichment).
LBR‑CAR and TMPO‑CAR T cells achieve potent killing in vitro at low E:T ratios.
Bispecific CAR combining LBR and TMPO binders yields superior tumor remission and durability in vivo.
Therapy‑adapted mouse models show enrichment of LBR expression after standard chemoradiotherapy, indicating a post‑treatment target.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Low antigen density of individual targets necessitates bispecific approach.
open_questions: Can LBR/TMPO CARs be combined with checkpoint blockade to overcome T‑cell exhaustion?
Is the surface localization stable over long‑term treatment?

talk_title: Neuronal Activity‑Regulated Mechanisms Promote Growth of Diffuse Hemispheric Glioma H3‑G34 Mutant
MOA_summary: Neuronal activity, via both glutamatergic and GABAergic circuits, enhances proliferation of H3‑G34 glioma cells through synaptic integration and release of activity‑regulated secreted factors; proteomic analysis identified N‑cadherin and neurofascin as novel paracrine mediators that promote tumor growth and migration.
key_findings: Electron microscopy shows ~10% neuron‑to‑glioma synapses and glioma‑to‑glioma synapses in G34 xenografts.
Glioma cells exhibit evoked EPSCs and spontaneous activity, indicating functional integration.
Pharmacologic blockade of neuronal activity (TTX, NBQX) abolishes neuron‑induced proliferation boost.
Optogenetic activation of either glutamatergic or GABAergic neurons increases tumor proliferation in vivo.
Proteomics of activity‑stimulated conditioned media identifies N‑cadherin and neurofascin as upregulated secreted factors.
Recombinant N‑cadherin promotes proliferation; neurofascin enhances glioma migration.

combination_strategies.rationale: Block glutamatergic activity‑driven proliferation
combination_strategies.phenotype: potential reduction of activity‑dependent growth

combination_strategies.rationale: Modulate GABAergic circuit influence on tumor
combination_strategies.phenotype: potential attenuation of activity‑driven proliferation

open_questions: What receptors mediate N‑cadherin and neurofascin signaling on G34 cells?
Can targeting these adhesion molecules improve survival in vivo?

talk_title: Targeting MIB and REM4 with REM422 in Pediatric T‑ALL

biomarkers.name: MIB mRNA expression
biomarkers.finding: Higher MIB levels observed in ALL PDXs versus solid tumors

clinical_data.population: pediatric ALL PDXs treated with REM422

clinical_data.value: 4/20 B‑ALL responses; 7/10 T‑ALL responses (4 CR, 2 MCR)

MOA_summary: REM422 is an oral small‑molecule that promotes inclusion of a poison exon in MIB pre‑mRNA, triggering nonsense‑mediated decay and loss of MIB protein, thereby inhibiting NOTCH pathway signaling essential for T‑ALL survival.
key_findings: REM422 induces rapid (2‑24 h) depletion of MIB protein in ALL PDX cells at nanomolar concentrations.
In vivo, daily oral REM422 (5 mg/kg) is well tolerated and extends EFS across diverse ALL subtypes.
T‑ALL PDXs show greater benefit (median +33 days, T/C = 3.6) than B‑ALL.
Seven of ten T‑ALL models achieve complete or maintained complete responses, including durable remissions beyond treatment cessation.
MIB expression alone does not fully predict response, indicating additional determinants of sensitivity.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Some T‑ALL PDXs with high MIB expression did not respond, suggesting alternative survival pathways.
open_questions: What biomarkers beyond MIB expression predict REM422 sensitivity?
Will combining REM422 with NOTCH inhibitors enhance efficacy?

talk_title: Development and impact of prophylactic HPV virus‑like particle vaccines

biomarkers.name: HPV16 DNA persistence
biomarkers.finding: 41 cases in placebo, 0 cases in vaccine arm (p<0.001)

biomarkers.name: Neutralizing antibody titer
biomarkers.finding: >98% of subjects seroconverted after a single dose; titers remained within 2.5% change per year over 16 years

biomarkers.name: HPV‑related cancer incidence
biomarkers.finding: ≈90% reduction in cervical precancer in vaccinated cohorts; 1.4 million future deaths averted globally

models.name: rabbit papillomavirus model
models.genotype_or_key_features: HPV L1 VLP immunization, challenge with rabbit PV

models.name: bovine papillomavirus model
models.genotype_or_key_features: HPV L1 VLP immunization, challenge with bovine PV

models.name: ESCUDO trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: Single‑dose vs two‑dose Gardasil 9 or Cervarix in Costa Rica, n≈2,000

MOA_summary: HPV L1 virus‑like particles self‑assemble into 360‑subunit VLPs that present repetitive neutralizing epitopes, inducing durable, high‑titer antibodies that persist for >15 years and provide sterilizing immunity by blocking the slow, basement‑membrane‑mediated entry of HPV into wounded epithelium.
key_findings: A monovalent HPV16 VLP trial showed 0 persistent infections in the vaccine arm versus 41 in placebo, demonstrating sterilizing immunity.
Single‑dose HPV vaccination achieved non‑inferior efficacy (97%–98%) compared with two doses in the ESCUDO trial, supporting dose de‑escalation.
Antibody titers after a single dose remain flat over 16 years, declining <2.5% per year, indicating long‑lived plasma cell maintenance.
Real‑world data from Denmark show ~90% protection against cervical cancer precursors in women vaccinated ≤16 y.
Global vaccine uptake has risen from 72% to 78% (≥1 dose) and 75% to 80% (up‑to‑date) among US adolescents, contributing to an estimated 1.4 million future deaths averted.
open_questions: What is the minimal antibody titer required for long‑term protection across diverse HPV genotypes?
Can single‑dose schedules be safely extended to older age groups or immunocompromised populations?

patent_or_ip_hints: NIH patents on HPV VLP technology

talk_title: Therapeutic applications of HPV VLPs and VLP‑IR700 conjugates

targets.dependency_context: EMT‑associated HSPG modifications on cancer cells

biomarkers.name: HSPG modification pattern
biomarkers.finding: Strong correlation between EMT/TGF‑β signature and VLP binding across 129 cell lines

biomarkers.name: Tumor response (time to referral)
biomarkers.assay: clinical observation in ocular melanoma trial
biomarkers.finding: Therapeutic dose increased median time to standard‑of‑care referral vs sub‑therapeutic dose (exact values not disclosed)

models.name: Panel of 129 cancer cell lines
models.genotype_or_key_features: Diverse tissue origins; EMT status assessed

models.name: Phase I/II ocular melanoma VLP‑IR700 trial
Phase I/II bladder cancer VLP‑IR700 intravesical trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: Local administration via cystoscope, window‑of‑opportunity resection

clinical_data.metric: Time to referral to standard of care (ocular melanoma)
clinical_data.value: significantly longer with therapeutic dose vs sub‑therapeutic dose

clinical_data.metric: Tumor growth arrest (ocular melanoma therapeutic dose)
clinical_data.value: tumors stopped growing during treatment period

clinical_data.metric: Complete pathological response (bladder cancer window trial)
clinical_data.value: 0 residual tumor in resected specimens after VLP‑IR700 treatment

MOA_summary: HPV VLPs bind preferentially to cancer cells displaying EMT‑associated, modified heparan‑sulfate proteoglycans; conjugation with the infrared‑activatable dye IR700 enables light‑triggered membrane disruption and necrotic cell death, while simultaneously releasing neoantigens that prime secondary T‑cell responses, creating a dual‑mode therapeutic platform.
key_findings: HPV VLPs bind >90% of a 129‑cell‑line panel, with binding strongly correlated to EMT/TGF‑β transcriptional programs.
In a Phase I/II ocular melanoma trial, the therapeutic VLP‑IR700 regimen markedly prolonged time to standard‑of‑care referral compared with sub‑therapeutic dosing.
Therapeutic dosing halted tumor growth in ocular melanoma patients, suggesting disease‑modifying activity.
A bladder cancer window‑of‑opportunity study showed complete pathological response after intravesical VLP‑IR700 plus light, indicating potent local tumor ablation.
The VLP‑IR700 platform leverages dual specificity (tumor binding + light activation) to achieve on‑target cytotoxicity with minimal off‑target effects.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Binding is absent on hematopoietic cells lacking the specific HSPG modifications, providing intrinsic tumor selectivity.

combination_strategies.rationale: VLP‑IR700 can delay progression, allowing eye‑preserving management
combination_strategies.phenotype: Extended time to referral and tumor stabilization

combination_strategies.rationale: Localized activation of IR700 after VLP binding in bladder
combination_strategies.phenotype: Complete tumor eradication in window trial

open_questions: What is the optimal light dose and delivery method for deep‑seated tumors?
Can the VLP‑IR700 platform be combined with checkpoint inhibition to amplify secondary T‑cell responses?

patent_or_ip_hints: NIH patents on HPV VLP technology; IP on VLP‑IR700 conjugate with Ora Biosciences

talk_title: AI-augmented drug discovery across biologics, small molecules, and multimodal biomarkers

biomarkers.name: membrane-to-cytoplasm optical density difference
biomarkers.finding: correlates with internalization rate of datupizumab duoxetecane and predicts response in Trop‑2 ADC trials

models.name: AstraZeneca‑Tempus‑Pathos multimodal model
models.genotype_or_key_features: longitudinal EHR, labs, H&E, genomics, transcriptomics

MOA_summary: AstraZeneca embeds AI models to predict protein structures for de novo mini‑protein binders (17% hit rate, 28 nM affinity) and to guide small‑molecule optimization (potency, clearance, exposure) for an intractable target, while computational pathology AI quantifies membrane‑cytoplasm optical density to predict ADC internalization, and multimodal foundation models integrate EHR, imaging, and omics to improve patient selection and trial design.
key_findings: AI‑designed mini‑protein library yielded 45 binders (17% success) with highest affinity of 28 nM in just one month, a three‑fold speedup over traditional methods.

Reinvent AI tool accelerated small‑molecule lead optimization for a confidential target, reducing the design cycle from ~8 months to ~4 months while achieving target‑level potency, clearance, exposure, and tumor growth inhibition.

Computational pathology model identified membrane‑to‑cytoplasm optical density difference as a surrogate for internalization of the TROP2 ADC datupizumab duoxetecane, now entering prospective validation in a phase‑III trial.

A multimodal foundation model built with Tempus and Pathos integrates longitudinal EHR, labs, histology, and genomics to predict control‑arm performance and patient response, aiming to improve phase‑III success rates.
open_questions: How can AI‑driven computational pathology be scaled across diverse ADC platforms beyond TROP2?
What are the optimal data governance frameworks to share multimodal clinical data while preserving patient privacy?

talk_title: Chromatin remodeling complexes as AI‑guided therapeutic targets

speaker.name: Sigal Kadish
speaker.affiliation: Harvard University, Professor of Molecular Biology; Co‑founder, Foghorn Therapeutics

targets.dependency_context: various solid tumors with ARID1A loss
BAF complex subunits across >1,000 cancer cell lines

models.name: pan‑cancer dependency screen
models.genotype_or_key_features: 1,000+ cancer cell lines, BAF complex knockouts

MOA_summary: AI‑driven AlphaFold‑based modeling identified a novel immunoglobulin‑like SWIFT domain within BAF chromatin remodelers that mediates transcription‑factor interactions; hydrogen‑deuterium exchange validated TF‑SWIFT binding sites, and CRISPR dependency screens revealed synthetic lethalities (e.g., SMARCA2 dependence in SMARCA4‑mutant lung cancer), establishing these interfaces as emerging therapeutic targets.
key_findings: AI‑predicted SWIFT domain structures correctly identified three PU.1 TAD binding motifs, validated by pull‑down and HDX experiments.
Fitness correlation across >1,000 cancer cell lines highlighted SWIFT‑containing paralogs (SMARCD1‑3) as top codependencies, suggesting selective vulnerability.
Histone modification profiling showed that specific nucleosome acetylation patterns dramatically modulate BAF complex remodeling activity, revealing allele‑specific inhibition opportunities.
Synthetic‑lethal relationship between SMARCA2 and SMARCA4 mutations was confirmed, positioning SMARCA2 as a high‑value target in SMARCA4‑mutant lung cancer.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Paralog redundancy within BAF complexes may confer resistance; targeting SWIFT domain offers paralog‑specific selectivity.

combination_strategies.rationale: BAF complex loss induces replication stress, sensitizing cells to ATR blockade
combination_strategies.phenotype: synergistic tumor regression in SMARCA4‑mutant xenografts

open_questions: Can AI‑designed small molecules achieve sufficient selectivity for SWIFT‑mediated PPIs without off‑target chromatin effects?
What is the therapeutic window for targeting intrinsically disordered regions of BAF subunits?

talk_title: AI‑driven de novo antibody design with atomic precision

models.name: internal high‑resolution folding model (AlphaFold‑derived)
models.genotype_or_key_features: atomic‑level predictions for antibodies and antigens

models.name: epitope selection agent
models.genotype_or_key_features: guides design to avoid off‑target binding

clinical_data.value: KRAS G12V mutant binding, no WT binding

MOA_summary: Chai Discovery uses diffusion‑based generative models and high‑resolution folding predictions to design full‑length antibodies (including VHH) that directly target defined epitopes on GPCRs, peptide‑MHC complexes, and cytokine interfaces, achieving nanomolar affinities and functional agonism/antagonism without library screening, while agentic AI enforces developability constraints and off‑target avoidance.
key_findings: Design of antibodies against >50 diverse targets succeeded with ≤20 designs per target, yielding binders for ~50% of targets (≈10‑fold improvement over prior 0.1% rates).
Atomic‑resolution predictions matched cryo‑EM structures (global RMSD <2 Å, CDR loop RMSD <1 Å) for five test cases, confirming near‑perfect epitope modeling.
GPCR VHH agonist for CCR8 achieved functional signaling after testing only 10 designs, with 1 agonist hit, demonstrating feasibility of de novo agonist design.
Peptide‑MHC antibody selectively bound KRAS G12V peptide‑MHC complex while sparing WT KRAS and alternative MHCs, illustrating precise epitope discrimination.
Developability metrics (expression, stability, viscosity, polyreactivity) of AI‑generated antibodies matched or exceeded those of clinically approved mAbs on first‑pass designs.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Model failures traced to missing constraints (e.g., membrane or glycan clashes), highlighting need for explicit negative selection prompts.

combination_strategies.rationale: GPCR agonist antibodies may enhance T‑cell infiltration when combined with checkpoint blockade
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced tumor regression in murine models

open_questions: How can AI agents be further trained to predict and avoid immunogenic epitopes during antibody design?
What is the upper limit of design‑to‑experiment cycles before diminishing returns in hit rate?

talk_title: Adolescent adherence to Planetary Health Dietary Index and breast cancer risk

speaker.name: Phuong Anh Le
speaker.affiliation: Harvard Chan School of Public Health

biomarkers.name: ER-negative status
biomarkers.finding: Adolescent high PHDI associated with >30% reduced risk of ER-negative breast cancer (HR ~0.70)

models.name: Nurses' Health Study II
models.genotype_or_key_features: 47,000 women, recalled adolescent diet, prospective follow-up 1998-2019

clinical_data.metric: hazard ratio for ER-negative breast cancer

MOA_summary: Higher adherence to the EAT-Lancet planetary health diet during adolescence is linked to a lower incidence of ER-negative breast cancer, particularly among premenopausal women with lower BMI, suggesting a protective effect of plant-forward dietary patterns on hormone‑negative tumor development.
key_findings: Adolescent high PHDI reduced overall invasive breast cancer risk by >30% for ER-negative tumors.
Association remained significant after adjustment for weight change since age 18.
Effect was strongest in premenopausal women and those with BMI <21 kg/m2 at age 18.
Whole fruits were protective; added saturated and trans fats increased risk.
open_questions: Would consistent adherence to the diet from adolescence through adulthood further reduce risk?
Can intermediate biomarkers (e.g., mammographic density) clarify the causal pathway?

talk_title: Cardiorespiratory multimorbidity and lung cancer risk in the UK Biobank

speaker.name: Wang Peng
speaker.affiliation: Tianjin Medical University Cancer Institute and Hospital

models.name: UK Biobank
models.genotype_or_key_features: Middle-aged adults, median follow-up 13.7 years, free of cancer/CRD/CVD at baseline

clinical_data.metric: hazard ratio for lung cancer (cardiorespiratory multimorbidity)
clinical_data.population: participants with both CRD and CVD

clinical_data.metric: hazard ratio for transition from CRM to lung cancer
risk reduction with healthy lifestyle (CRM to lung cancer)

MOA_summary: Co-occurrence of chronic respiratory disease and cardiovascular disease markedly increases lung cancer incidence, with a hazard ratio of 4.11 versus healthy individuals, and healthy lifestyle factors (non‑smoking, diet) attenuate progression risk by up to 57%.
key_findings: Cardiorespiratory multimorbidity (CRM) confers the highest lung cancer risk (HR 4.11) compared with single disease states.
22% of CRD patients progressed to CRM versus 4.9% of CVD patients.
CRM shows the shortest transition time to lung cancer among disease states.
Healthy lifestyle reduces progression from single disease to CRM by 34‑54% and from CRM to lung cancer by 57%.
open_questions: How to account for reverse causality between health status and lifestyle adoption?
What are optimal thresholds for lifestyle scores to maximize risk reduction?

talk_title: Generational immigration status and gastric cancer incidence among Japanese descendants

models.name: Multi-Ethnic Cohort Study
models.genotype_or_key_features: 94,000 participants of Japanese ancestry and NHW, median follow-up ~20 years, 716 gastric cancer cases

clinical_data.metric: hazard ratio for gastric cancer (first generation vs NHW)
hazard ratio for gastric cancer (third generation vs NHW)

MOA_summary: Risk of gastric cancer remains elevated across generations of Japanese immigrants in the US, decreasing from a five‑fold increase in first‑generation to a 2.5‑fold increase in third‑generation, indicating persistent ancestry‑related susceptibility beyond environmental assimilation.
key_findings: First‑generation Japanese immigrants have ~5× higher gastric cancer risk than NHW.
Risk declines across generations but remains ~2.5× higher in third generation.
Pattern is strongest for non‑cardia gastric cancer.
No effect modification by family history, education, BMI, smoking, or sodium intake.
open_questions: Would targeted gastric cancer screening be cost‑effective for high‑risk immigrant groups?
How does H. pylori prevalence across generations influence residual risk?

talk_title: PRIME: Parsimonious EHR risk model for pancreatic cancer

speaker.name: Lucas Mavromatis
speaker.affiliation: NYU Grossman School of Medicine, Division of Precision Medicine

models.name: OptumLabs Data Warehouse
models.genotype_or_key_features: 4.9M training, 31 health systems validation, adults ≥40 y, BMI measured 2016‑2018

clinical_data.metric: absolute 3‑year PDAC risk for top 1% PRIME

clinical_data.value: 5.8% of all PDAC cases within 3 years

MOA_summary: An interpretable elastic‑net derived EHR model (PRIME) using 19 routine clinical variables predicts pancreatic cancer risk with AUC ≈0.75, identifying a high‑risk 1% subgroup with a 0.61% three‑year absolute risk, comparable to germline mutation carriers.
key_findings: PRIME achieved AUC 0.76 (24 mo) and 0.75 (36 mo) in US validation health systems.
Top 1% risk group has 0.61% 3‑year absolute PDAC risk (1 in 128) and captures 5.8% of incident cases.
Model performance modestly declines in UK Biobank (AUC 0.71) but remains well‑calibrated.
High‑risk individuals have similar prevalence of known PDAC pathogenic germline variants as the general population, indicating capture of sporadic risk.

combination_strategies.rationale: Sequential case‑finding: define high‑risk then apply blood biomarker
combination_strategies.phenotype: Positive predictive value 5.9%, LR 47

combination_strategies.rationale: Alternative blood biomarker with higher specificity
combination_strategies.phenotype: Higher PPV but fewer cancers identified

open_questions: What absolute risk threshold optimally balances detection yield and resource use?
How will prospective implementation affect patient uptake and downstream imaging burden?

talk_title: Basal metabolic rate and survival in colorectal cancer (COLO-CARE)

speaker.name: Sheetal Hardikar
speaker.affiliation: University of Utah, Huntsman Cancer Institute

models.name: COLO-CARE study
models.genotype_or_key_features: 3,760 CRC patients, US and Germany, all stages, BMR calculated from weight/height/age/sex

clinical_data.metric: hazard ratio for overall survival (highest BMR quartile vs lowest)
hazard ratio for CRC‑specific survival (highest BMR quartile vs lowest)
overall survival benefit in younger men (<50 y)

MOA_summary: Higher predicted basal metabolic rate, reflecting greater physiological reserve, is associated with improved overall and colorectal‑cancer‑specific survival, especially pronounced in younger male patients.
key_findings: Patients in the highest BMR quartile had 23% lower risk of overall death (HR 0.77).
Highest BMR quartile showed 36% lower risk of CRC‑specific death (HR 0.64).
Benefit was strongest in men under 50, with ~70% better survival.
BMR correlated positively with BMI and was higher in men than women.
open_questions: Can serial BMR measurements track cachexia development and guide interventions?
How does BMR integrate with imaging‑derived body composition metrics?

talk_title: Telehealth utilization disparities among older cancer patients

models.name: Medicare fee‑for‑service claims (2019‑2021)
models.genotype_or_key_features: 1.1 M cancer patients, 6 cancer types, ~70 M oncology claims

clinical_data.metric: overall telehealth utilization within first year of diagnosis
telehealth use by race (Black vs NHW)

MOA_summary: Older cancer patients exhibit marked disparities in telehealth adoption, with lower uptake among Black, low‑income, and non‑English‑speaking groups, and provider/system factors (e.g., academic setting, female providers) influencing delivery.
readouts: E&M CPT codes with telehealth modifiers
key_findings: 14.1% of older cancer patients used telehealth at least once in the first year post‑diagnosis.
Utilization peaked in 2020 (21.2%) then declined to 16.2% in 2021.
Black patients had the lowest utilization (11.2%); Asian/Pacific Islanders highest (17.9%).
Provider type (APPs, female clinicians) and academic facilities increased telehealth delivery.
open_questions: What specific barriers (digital literacy, broadband access) drive lower use in vulnerable groups?
How can provider‑level interventions improve sustained telehealth engagement?

talk_title: CONFLUENCE: Multi‑ancestry GWAS of breast cancer

models.name: CONFLUENCE consortium
models.genotype_or_key_features: ≈227,000 breast cancer cases, 1.7 M controls, 5 ancestries, 45 M variants

clinical_data.metric: number of independent risk loci identified
AUC improvement of CONFLUENCE PRS vs 313‑variant PRS (UK Biobank EUR)

MOA_summary: A massive multi‑ancestry GWAS (CONFLUENCE) uncovered 545 independent breast cancer risk loci, including 273 novel signals, and a 544‑variant PRS derived from these loci outperforms the standard 313‑variant score across European, African, and admixed American populations.
key_findings: CONFLUENCE includes >2 M participants, tripling effective sample size of prior multi‑ancestry studies.
Identified 545 independent loci, of which 273 are novel.
A 544‑variant PRS improves AUC by 2‑3% across EUR, AFR, and AMR cohorts versus the 313‑variant PRS.
Resource will be publicly released with summary statistics and data access platform.
open_questions: How to calibrate PRS thresholds across ancestries for clinical risk stratification?
Can integration with lifestyle and clinical factors enhance predictive performance?

talk_title: Plasma proteomic signature of persistent smoking effects and cancer risk

biomarkers.name: PSPSE proteomic signature score
biomarkers.finding: Higher score associated with increased risk of lung cancer (HR ~1.5) and improved PLCOm2012 model AUC, especially in long-term quitters

MOA_summary: The PSPSE proteomic signature captures persistent protein alterations after smoking cessation, reflecting cumulative exposure and biological memory, and its elevated levels predict higher incidence of smoking-related cancers, particularly lung cancer, independent of self-reported smoking metrics.
key_findings: Identified 18 proteins forming a PSPSE score that distinguishes current, former, and never smokers.
Higher PSPSE score significantly associated with increased lung cancer risk (HR ~1.5) in UK Biobank former smokers.
PSPSE score improves PLCOm2012 lung cancer risk prediction, especially in quitters >20 years.
Signature validated in an independent Asian cohort (Shanghai Men's Health Study) with consistent risk association.
open_questions: What are the functional roles of the 18 signature proteins in carcinogenesis?
Can the signature predict response to lung cancer screening or chemoprevention?

talk_title: Adult height genetics and fatal prostate cancer risk

speaker.name: Anqi Wang
speaker.affiliation: Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health

biomarkers.name: Height polygenic risk score
biomarkers.finding: PRS associated with fatal prostate cancer; residual height after adjusting for PRS shows no association

models.name: Health Professionals Follow-up Study
models.genotype_or_key_features: 51,000 men, DNA and tumor tissue available

clinical_data.metric: HR for fatal prostate cancer per SD increase in height PRS

MOA_summary: Genetically determined adult height, captured by a polygenic risk score, drives the observed association with fatal prostate cancer, likely via growth‑related pathways such as androgen signaling, rather than early‑life nutrition.
key_findings: Height PRS strongly associated with fatal prostate cancer (HR 1.30 per SD).
Residual height after accounting for PRS shows no association, indicating genetics drive the risk.
Androgen response pathway upregulated in tumors of taller men, linking height to prostate cancer biology.
open_questions: Can height PRS improve risk stratification beyond PSA screening?
What specific genetic variants within the PRS mediate androgen pathway activation?

talk_title: Copy number alteration subtypes in high‑grade serous ovarian cancer and racial survival disparities

models.name: ACES/NCSCS/NHS
models.genotype_or_key_features: 638 HSOC tumors with matched blood, 37% Black

clinical_data.metric: Hazard ratio for death (C1 loss‑dominant vs C2 CNA‑quiet)
Hazard ratio for death (Black vs White within gain‑dominant C3)

MOA_summary: Unsupervised clustering of genome‑wide copy‑number profiles from methylation arrays defines three HSOC subtypes (loss‑dominant, CNA‑quiet, gain‑dominant); CNA‑quiet tumors show higher immune infiltration and better survival, while Black patients in the gain‑dominant subtype experience higher mortality, suggesting ancestry‑linked biological contributors to disparity.
key_findings: Identified three CNA‑based clusters: loss‑dominant (44%), CNA‑quiet (40%), gain‑dominant (16%).
CNA‑quiet tumors have significantly higher immune cell infiltration and improved overall survival.
Within the gain‑dominant cluster, Black women have a 60% higher hazard of death compared to White women.
open_questions: Do specific copy‑number events drive the immune‑cold phenotype in gain‑dominant tumors?
Can targeted therapies reverse the survival disadvantage in Black patients with gain‑dominant HSOC?

talk_title: Spatial context of T‑cell infiltration and exhaustion predicts ovarian cancer survival in Black women

biomarkers.name: Cytotoxic T‑cell enrichment (tumor vs stroma)
biomarkers.finding: Tumor‑dominant enrichment associated with better overall survival (HR 0.68)

biomarkers.name: TIM‑3+ exhausted CD8+ T cells in tumor
biomarkers.finding: Presence associated with improved survival in tumors with ≥2% T‑cell infiltration

clinical_data.metric: HR for death per unit increase in tumor‑dominant cytotoxic gradient
HR for death with tumor TIM‑3+ exhaustion (≥2% T cells)

MOA_summary: Multiplex immunofluorescence of whole‑section ovarian tumors reveals that cytotoxic CD8+ T cells confer survival benefit when enriched in the tumor compartment versus stroma, and tumor‑localized TIM‑3+ exhausted T cells paradoxically mark better outcomes, indicating spatially resolved immune functionality drives prognosis in Black women.
key_findings: Tumor‑dominant cytotoxic T‑cell enrichment predicts improved survival (HR 0.68).
Stroma‑dominant cytotoxic enrichment predicts worse survival.
Tumor TIM‑3+ exhaustion associated with better survival among tumors with ≥2% T cells.
open_questions: Would checkpoint blockade efficacy differ by the identified spatial immunotypes?
Can spatial profiling be integrated into routine pathology for risk stratification?

talk_title: Pathways enriched in primary breast tumors with disseminated tumor cells

biomarkers.name: Wnt pathway mutations
biomarkers.finding: Associated with EMT signature and lower proliferation in DTC‑positive tumors

biomarkers.name: PI3K/AKT/mTOR mutations
biomarkers.finding: Linked to increased proliferative pathway expression and reduced apoptosis

biomarkers.name: APOBEC mutational signature activity
biomarkers.finding: ~2‑fold higher in DTC‑positive patients

models.name: Prospective breast cancer DTC cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 148 patients with RNA‑seq, WGS, bone‑marrow DTC assessment

MOA_summary: In early‑stage hormone‑receptor‑positive breast cancer, presence of disseminated tumor cells correlates with somatic mutations in Wnt, PI3K/AKT/mTOR, and NRF2 pathways; Wnt mutations drive EMT and a stem‑like migratory phenotype, while PI3K alterations promote proliferation and resistance, together facilitating dormancy and later metastasis.
key_findings: DTC‑positive tumors enriched for mutations in Wnt, PI3K/AKT/mTOR, and NRF2 pathways.
Wnt‑mutated DTC‑positive tumors show increased EMT and decreased proliferation signatures.
PI3K‑mutated DTC‑positive tumors display upregulated proliferative pathways and reduced apoptosis.
APOBEC mutagenesis activity nearly doubled in DTC‑positive patients.
open_questions: Can targeting Wnt or PI3K pathways eradicate DTCs and prevent recurrence?
What is the temporal relationship between DTC emergence and pathway activation?

talk_title: Colorectal cancer polygenic risk across birth cohorts

speaker.name: Xinyu Wang
speaker.affiliation: Harvard Chan School of Public Health

biomarkers.name: Colorectal cancer PRS (204 SNPs)
biomarkers.finding: Association with CRC risk strengthens in more recent birth cohorts (trend p<0.001)

clinical_data.metric: OR per SD increase in PRS

MOA_summary: A colorectal cancer polygenic risk score shows progressively stronger association with disease risk in later birth cohorts, suggesting gene‑environment interactions where modern lifestyle exposures amplify genetic susceptibility, particularly for distal colon and rectal cancers.
key_findings: PRS distribution unchanged across birth cohorts, but effect size increases in recent cohorts.
Stronger PRS‑CRC association observed in individuals <55 years, unscreened, without family history, and for distal colon/rectal cancers.
Innate immune system pathways enriched among SNPs showing birth‑cohort interaction.
open_questions: Which specific environmental factors (e.g., diet, obesity) drive the observed gene‑environment interaction?
Can integrating PRS with lifestyle scores improve early‑onset CRC screening?

talk_title: Functional characterization of STARD13 as a BRCA2 modifier in breast cancer

models.name: HMLE BRCA2 6174delT heterozygous
models.genotype_or_key_features: same mutation in mammary epithelial cells

MOA_summary: A non‑coding variant rs3742324 within an enhancer region regulates STARD13 expression; loss of STARD13 in BRCA2‑mutant breast epithelial cells amplifies EMT‑related genes (e.g., MMP9) and proliferative pathways, suggesting STARD13 acts as a genetic modifier of BRCA2‑associated breast cancer risk.
key_findings: rs3742324 region shows allele‑specific enhancer activity and regulates STARD13 expression.
CRISPR deletion of the enhancer reduces STARD13 transcript levels.
STARD13 knockdown leads to greater downregulation of LATAS2 in BRCA2‑mutant cells.
RNA‑seq reveals upregulation of EMT and extracellular matrix genes (e.g., MMP9) when STARD13 is reduced in BRCA2‑mutant background.
open_questions: Does STARD13 modulation affect sensitivity to PARP inhibitors in BRCA2‑mutant tumors?
Are there therapeutic strategies to restore STARD13 activity?

session_title: Advances in Hemologic Malignancies: Population Sciences for Hematological Malignancies
talk_title: Building infrastructure for population genomics research at Roswell Park
MOA_summary: Ken Onel described the creation of a universal consent program, a large biorepository of serial blood and bone marrow samples, and an integrated clinical-genomic data commons (Insight) to enable population‑scale genetic studies in hematologic malignancies, linking germline and tumor sequencing with detailed exposure and demographic data.
key_findings: Implemented universal consent with >80% accrual across >50,000 patients.
Biorepository contains 1.3 million biospecimens, including 220,000 bone marrow/blood samples from heme malignancy patients.
Insight tool identified 523 AML patients with germline testing versus 1,195 without, revealing ancestry‑linked testing disparities.
open_questions: How will e‑consent impact consent rates across diverse populations?
What strategies will ensure equitable access to germline testing for under‑represented groups?

session_title: Advances in Hemologic Malignancies: Population Sciences for Hematological Malignancies
talk_title: Genetic ancestry and outcome disparities in acute myeloid leukemia

biomarkers.name: self‑reported Black race
biomarkers.finding: 12‑year shorter median OS in patients <30 y (p<0.001)

biomarkers.name: core binding factor abnormality
biomarkers.finding: 40% of Black patients harbor this yet have inferior survival

models.name: FLT3 background with CRISPR‑engineered PHIP mutation

clinical_data.metric: relapse rate for NPM1‑mutated Black patients
relapse rate with CH in African‑American AML

MOA_summary: Eisfeldt presented data showing that genetic ancestry profoundly modifies AML biology: African ancestry patients exhibit distinct mutation frequencies (e.g., lower NPM1, higher PHIP, novel Rho GTPase mutations) and altered epigenetic landscapes, leading to poorer survival even when harboring traditionally favorable lesions such as NPM1.
key_findings: Black AML patients have ~12‑year shorter median OS compared with White patients, especially pronounced in <30 y age group.
NPM1 mutation, usually favorable, predicts adverse outcome in African‑American patients (near‑universal relapse).
PHIP mutations occur in ~7% of African‑American AML and confer aggressive disease in mouse models.
Rho GTPase family mutations are present in 12% of African‑American/A Hispanic AML, a novel finding.
Clonal hematopoiesis is present in 15% of African‑American AML and associates with 80% relapse rate.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: NPM1 mutation loses its favorable prognostic effect in African ancestry, suggesting ancestry‑specific resistance mechanisms.
CH in African‑American patients predicts high relapse despite being traditionally low‑risk.
open_questions: What molecular mechanisms underlie the ancestry‑specific reversal of NPM1 prognostic impact?
Can targeting PHIP‑driven pathways improve outcomes in high‑risk ancestry groups?

talk_title: Overview of Pediatric Extrapolation in Oncology Drug Development

speaker.name: Lauren Price
speaker.affiliation: FDA, Clinical Pharmacology, Solid Tumor Division

MOA_summary: Pediatric extrapolation leverages similarity in disease biology, drug pharmacology, and response between adult (or older pediatric) reference populations and younger pediatric patients to support safe and efficient drug development without repeating full efficacy trials.
key_findings: More than 50% of new pediatric oncology indications (2013‑2024) used pediatric extrapolation.
ICH E11A (2024) formalizes guidance, including allowance for safety extrapolation when supported by data.
Three‑step framework: concept development, plan creation, and plan execution with iterative updates.
Early FDA engagement (early advice meetings, pediatric study plans) is encouraged to reduce uncertainty.

talk_title: Model‑Informed Drug Development (MIDD) to Support Pediatric Extrapolation

speaker.name: Jiang Liu
speaker.affiliation: FDA, Deputy Division Director of Pharmacometrics/Quantitative Clinical Pharmacology

MOA_summary: MIDD integrates PK/PD, PBPK, population PK, and QSP models to bridge adult and pediatric data, enabling exposure matching, dose optimization, and trial design for pediatric oncology drugs.
key_findings: MIDD methods (population PK, PBPK, QSP) can predict pediatric exposure and support dose selection without extensive pediatric trials.
Examples: gadolinium‑based contrast agents, RILACE, itolizumab, rituximab—all received pediatric indications down to neonates via MIDD.
Renal maturation modeling shows GFR normalizes after ~2 years, informing GBCA dosing in infants.
FDA recommends early, iterative MIDD discussions (type D, MIDD‑focused meetings) to reduce uncertainty.

talk_title: Pediatric Extrapolation for Lutathera in Adolescents with SSTR‑Positive GEP‑NETs

speaker.name: Nicole Dresner
speaker.affiliation: FDA, Deputy Director, Division of Oncology 2

biomarkers.name: SSTR expression
biomarkers.finding: Present in >90% of adolescent GEP‑NETs, supporting target relevance

models.name: NETTER‑P
models.genotype_or_key_features: Adolescents with SSTR‑positive GEP‑NETs or PPGLs, n=9 (4 GEP‑NET)

MOA_summary: Lutathera delivers beta‑emitting 177Lu to SSTR‑expressing neuroendocrine tumor cells, causing DNA damage and tumor regression; adult NETTER‑1 showed a 79% reduction in progression risk (HR 0.21) and 13% ORR, supporting extrapolation to adolescents with similar SSTR expression.
key_findings: Adult NETTER‑1 trial demonstrated PFS benefit (HR 0.21) and ORR 13% for Lutathera in SSTR‑positive GEP‑NETs.
NETTER‑P enrolled 9 adolescents; safety profile matched adults (lymphopenia, neutropenia).
Organ dosimetry (kidney, bone marrow) in adolescents was comparable to adults, though predicted high‑dose kidney probability exceeded 20% at the 95th percentile.
FDA concluded extrapolation appropriate despite limited pediatric efficacy data, issuing PMRs for long‑term renal and hematologic safety.
Lutathera received FDA approval for adolescents with SSTR‑positive GEP‑NETs in 2024, marking a successful pediatric extrapolation case.

talk_title: Pediatric Extrapolation for Blinatumomab in B‑ALL

speaker.name: Mansi Dalal
speaker.affiliation: FDA, Clinical Reviewer, Division of Hemomalignancies 1

biomarkers.name: CD19 expression
biomarkers.finding: Uniformly present in adult and pediatric B‑ALL blasts

biomarkers.name: MRD negativity
biomarkers.finding: 81% of adult BLAST patients achieved MRD‑negative status after one cycle

models.name: MT103205
models.genotype_or_key_features: Children <18 y, relapsed/refractory Ph‑negative B‑ALL, n≈30

MOA_summary: Blinatumomab is a CD19‑directed CD3 bispecific T‑cell engager that forms an immunologic synapse, activating T‑cells to lyse CD19‑positive B‑ALL blasts; PK exposure matching between adults and children supports similar efficacy and safety extrapolation.
key_findings: Adult BLAST study showed 81% MRD‑negative conversion, providing a robust efficacy signal for extrapolation.
Pediatric dose‑finding study (MT103205) demonstrated tolerability and similar cytokine release syndrome rates to adults.

Meta‑analysis of three randomized trials (adult E1910, pediatric 201/20215, AALL 1331) plus BLIM‑FANT showed consistent survival benefit across ages, supporting extrapolation to pediatric consolidation therapy.
FDA granted age‑agnostic indications for blinatumomab in MRD‑positive B‑ALL and in consolidation, relying on adult efficacy and pediatric safety/PK data.
Post‑marketing requirements include long‑term safety follow‑up for neurotoxicity and cytokine release syndrome.

combination_strategies.rationale: Intercalating blinatumomab improves survival when combined with multi‑phase chemotherapy
combination_strategies.phenotype: Consistent OS hazard ratio benefit across adult and pediatric trials

talk_title: African Cancer Atlas (TACA) project and early genomic findings

models.name: TACA cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: paired germline and somatic whole genomes from African ancestry cancer patients

MOA_summary: The TACA initiative generates paired whole-genome sequencing of African cancer patients to uncover ancestry-specific mutational signatures, driver alterations such as TP53 enrichment, and novel candidate drivers like LEPR and KRTAP10-6, aiming to improve cancer biology understanding and drug discovery for African and global populations.
key_findings: Phase 1 of TACA funded to generate 3,000 genomes from 1,500 African cancer patients across 7 countries.
Median sequencing coverage achieved 41X for germline and 121X for somatic samples, comparable to reference datasets.
Tumor mutational burden in African breast cancer cohort comparable to TCGA, suggesting eligibility for checkpoint blockade.
Enrichment of TP53 mutations and novel driver candidates (e.g., LEPR in TNBC, KRTAP10-6 in luminal) relative to European datasets.
Geographic subtype distribution observed: higher triple‑negative breast cancer prevalence in West Africa versus luminal B in East Africa.

talk_title: Proteomics studies in African cancer cohorts and the African ClinMap multi‑omic platform

speaker.name: Irechen
speaker.affiliation: Council for Scientific and Industrial Research (CSIR), South Africa

models.name: Southern African proteomics cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 150+ patient tissue samples across multiple cancer types

MOA_summary: Proteomic profiling of African tumor specimens reveals ECM remodeling, altered lipid metabolism, and distinct protein signatures (e.g., decreased CD36 in breast cancer, high LGR1 in pancreatic cancer) that differentiate tumor from normal tissue and suggest ancestry‑specific therapeutic vulnerabilities.
key_findings: Breast cancer proteomics (n=28) identified >2,000 proteins with a five‑fold decrease in CD36 compared to US cohorts.
Machine‑learning classifier achieved AUC 0.96 distinguishing tumor vs normal tissue using proteomic data.
Pancreatic cancer plasma proteomics showed ECM remodeling and inflammatory mediators; LGR1 achieved AUC 0.94 for cancer vs benign.
Prostate cancer tissue proteomics (n>150) uncovered a five‑protein panel linked to neurogenous metastasis.
Gallbladder cancer proteomics identified nine key proteins associated with ECM and smooth muscle contraction loss.

talk_title: Ancestry‑driven genomic and transcriptomic landscape of aggressive endometrial cancer

speaker.name: Nyasha Chambwe
speaker.affiliation: Northwell Health / New York Genome Center, New York, USA

targets.dependency_context: copy number high endometrial cancer, African ancestry
African ancestry endometrial cancer, copy number high

biomarkers.name: CD8 T cell infiltration
biomarkers.finding: Higher African ancestry coefficient associated with lower CD8 T cell infiltration across tumors

models.name: NYC Endometrial Cancer Ancestry Cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 71 patients, enriched for Black women, high‑grade non‑endometrioid subtypes, paired tumor/normal WGS and RNA‑seq

MOA_summary: Whole‑genome and transcriptomic profiling of African‑ancestry endometrial cancers reveals ancestry‑specific driver alterations (e.g., enriched MECOM short‑isoform amplifications) and reduced CD8 T‑cell infiltration, indicating distinct molecular and immune landscapes that may affect therapeutic response.
key_findings: African ancestry patients showed lower CD8 T‑cell infiltration after adjusting for tumor purity and subtype.
MECOM short‑isoform focal amplifications were present in ~35% of African ancestry tumors versus ~10% in European ancestry, linked to poorer progression‑free survival in TCGA.
FBXW7 mutations enriched in African ancestry copy‑number‑high endometrial cancers.
Overall fewer clinically actionable mutations (OncoKB) in African ancestry tumors compared with European ancestry.
Ancestry‑specific expression of PLXNA3 and other genes correlated with admixture proportion.
open_questions: Functional impact of MECOM short‑isoform amplification on tumor biology and therapy response.
Mechanisms underlying reduced CD8 infiltration in high‑African‑ancestry tumors.

talk_title: Biomarker‑guided precision prevention and chemoprevention of HCC

speaker.name: Yujin Hosoda
speaker.affiliation: University of Texas Southwestern Medical Center, Director of Liver Tumor Translational Research

biomarkers.name: Prognostic Liver Signature (PLS)
biomarkers.finding: High PLS risk score predicts higher 5‑year HCC incidence; risk score reduced in ~80% of antiviral‑cured patients

biomarkers.name: Phospho‑EGFR
biomarkers.finding: Suppressed at 25 mg erlotinib (one‑sixth therapeutic dose) in window trial liver biopsies

biomarkers.name: Risk signature modulation
biomarkers.finding: Significant down‑modulation observed at ≥50 mg erlotinib with no grade ≥3 adverse events

models.name: diet‑induced fibrosis‑cirrhosis‑HCC model
models.genotype_or_key_features: progressive fibrosis → cirrhosis → early HCC; high‑risk gene expression signature

models.name: Texas prospective cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: n>2000 cirrhotic patients, longitudinal PLS validation

models.name: Phase 1 window trial (cirrhosis patients undergoing liver resection)
models.genotype_or_key_features: dose escalation erlotinib 25‑75 mg, paired pre‑/post‑treatment biopsies

clinical_data.population: cirrhotic patients undergoing elective liver surgery

clinical_data.value: significant reduction at 50 mg and 75 mg
mild rash at 75 mg, resolved after drug stop
dose‑dependent decrease, significant for small nodules only

MOA_summary: Hosoda demonstrated that a liver‑tissue risk signature (PLS) can be modulated by targeting EGFR signaling in myofibroblasts with low‑dose erlotinib, leading to dose‑dependent suppression of phospho‑EGFR, reduction of early HCC nodules, and improvement of the risk signature; similar modulation is observed with lipophilic statins, bariatric surgery, and GLP‑1 agonists, supporting the signature as a surrogate endpoint for chemoprevention trials.
key_findings: Risk‑stratified HCC screening using the PLS risk signature improves cost‑effectiveness and predicts incident HCC in >2,000 US patients and a 1,300‑patient NIH cohort.
Low‑dose erlotinib (25‑75 mg) suppresses phospho‑EGFR in cirrhotic liver tissue, with risk‑signature improvement at ≥50 mg and no grade ≥3 toxicity.
Erlotinib reduces early HCC nodules in a rat fibrosis model in a dose‑dependent manner, but has minimal effect on larger established tumors due to poor tumor cell uptake.
Lipophilic statins (atorvastatin) and bariatric surgery improve the PLS risk signature, correlating with lower long‑term HCC incidence.
The EGFR‑driven myofibroblast signature can serve as a surrogate endpoint for future chemoprevention trials (ECHO‑B, CATCH‑B, TOSS).
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Erlotinib fails to shrink larger HCC nodules because the drug is poorly taken up by tumor cells, limiting activity to early, myofibroblast‑driven stages.
Only ~80% of antiviral‑cured patients show risk‑signature reduction, indicating heterogeneity in chemoprevention response.

combination_strategies.rationale: Potential synergy by combining EGFR inhibition in myofibroblasts with checkpoint blockade to enhance T‑cell infiltration
combination_strategies.phenotype: proposed enhanced early‑stage tumor control without added toxicity

open_questions: What molecular features determine why only a subset of patients achieve risk‑signature modulation with antiviral or statin therapy?
Can the PLS signature predict response to other targeted agents beyond EGFR inhibition?

talk_title: Personalized care in advanced HCC: gamma‑delta T cells, liver decompensation AI model, and genomics‑driven targeted therapy

speaker.name: Manon Allaire
speaker.affiliation: La Pitié‑Salpêtrière Hospital, Paris, France; Inserm Centre de Recherche des Cordeliers

biomarkers.name: Gamma‑delta T cell signature
biomarkers.finding: Higher baseline gamma‑delta T cell levels associated with disease control at 6 months and significantly longer PFS/OS in atezolizumab‑bevacizumab‑treated HCC (n=31)

biomarkers.name: Interferon‑γ level
biomarkers.finding: Baseline serum IFN‑γ higher in responders versus non‑responders in the same cohort

biomarkers.name: Portal hypertension (CT‑derived HepatoSAJ model)
biomarkers.finding: Model predicts high risk of liver decompensation (AUC ≈ 0.84) and associated 30% 1‑year decompensation rate, correlating with median OS 6 months vs 24 months without decompensation

models.name: Prospective French atezo‑bev cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: n=31 advanced HCC patients, blood RNA‑seq and cytokine profiling

models.name: Multicentric French real‑world cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: n=180 advanced HCC, median OS 23 months, 50% portal hypertension

models.name: 500‑patient CT‑AI cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: Baseline CT used to train HepatoSAJ model for decompensation risk

models.name: Molecular tumor board cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: n=103 sequenced liver tumors, 65% with targetable alteration

clinical_data.value: significantly higher in high gamma‑delta T cell group
longer in high gamma‑delta T cell signature (median not specified)
median 23 months in real‑world French cohort (n=180)
30% at 1 year in AI‑predicted high‑risk group
32% of treated patients achieved disease control (median PFS 12 months vs 2 months)

MOA_summary: Allaire identified a blood‑based gamma‑delta T cell signature that predicts response to atezolizumab‑bevacizumab in HCC, demonstrated that CT‑derived AI modeling (HepatoSAJ) can stratify patients at high risk of portal‑hypertension‑driven decompensation, and showed that genomics‑driven molecular tumor boards enable targeted therapy for ~65% of liver cancer patients, yielding disease control in one‑third of treated cases.
key_findings: High baseline gamma‑delta T cell levels in blood correlate with disease control and longer PFS/OS in atezo‑bev‑treated HCC.
Interferon‑γ is elevated at baseline in responders, supporting a Th1‑type immune milieu.
The HepatoSAJ CT‑AI model predicts liver decompensation risk (AUC ≈ 0.84) better than conventional clinical scores.
Portal hypertension identified by AI is a stronger predictor of mortality than tumor‑related factors in real‑world HCC cohorts.
Genomic profiling revealed targetable alterations in 65% of liver cancer patients; genotype‑matched therapy achieved disease control in 32% with a median PFS of 12 months.

combination_strategies.rationale: Gamma‑delta T cells produce IFN‑γ; combining with checkpoint blockade may amplify Th1 response
combination_strategies.phenotype: proposed improved response rates in atezo‑bev refractory patients

combination_strategies.rationale: Reduce portal hypertension‑driven decompensation in AI‑high‑risk patients
combination_strategies.phenotype: potentially lower 1‑year decompensation from 30% to <15%

open_questions: Does the gamma‑delta T cell signature reflect tumor‑infiltrating cells or systemic immunity, and can it be modulated therapeutically?
Can the HepatoSAJ model be integrated with longitudinal lab data to further improve decompensation prediction?
What are the mechanisms of resistance in patients with targetable alterations who still progress on matched therapy?

cited_papers: Zucman‑Rossi et al. Nat Rev Cancer 2023

talk_title: Therapy-associated clonal hematopoiesis and risk of secondary hematologic malignancies in cancer survivors

speaker.name: Ms. Berneo
speaker.affiliation: National Cancer Institute, Division of Cancer Epidemiology and Genetics

biomarkers.name: CHIP
biomarkers.finding: CHIP prevalence increased from 4.5% in non‑cancer to 7.5% in treated cancer survivors (≈50% relative increase)

biomarkers.name: MCA
biomarkers.finding: MCA prevalence rose from 3% in non‑cancer to 4% in solid tumor survivors (≈15% relative increase)

models.name: UK Biobank
models.genotype_or_key_features: 485,000 participants, 23,000 with prevalent solid tumor, ~4,000 with chemo/radiation exposure

clinical_data.population: cancer survivors with chemotherapy or radiation
cancer survivors with chemotherapy or radiation

clinical_data.metric: Hazard ratio for secondary hematologic malignancy with any MCA
Hazard ratio for secondary hematologic malignancy with CHIP

MOA_summary: Therapy (chemo/radiation) promotes expansion of clonal hematopoiesis, especially DNA‑damage‑response CHIP mutations and copy‑neutral loss‑of‑heterozygosity MCAs, which in turn increase the risk of secondary hematologic malignancies in cancer survivors.
key_findings: CHIP prevalence rises from 4.5% in cancer‑free individuals to 7.5% in treated cancer survivors (≈50% relative increase).
MCA prevalence rises from 3% to 4% in treated survivors (≈19% relative increase).
Therapy‑associated MCAs are distinct from those linked to secondary hematologic malignancy risk.
Presence of any autosomal MCA confers a hazard ratio of ~4.8 for secondary hematologic malignancy.
CHIP confers a hazard ratio of ~2.8 for secondary hematologic malignancy, with TP53 and PPM1D mutations linking therapy and malignancy risk.
open_questions: Will sex‑specific CH events further refine risk stratification?
How do ancestry and comorbidities modify therapy‑associated CH dynamics?

talk_title: Somatic APC mosaicism and colibactin‑producing bacteria in unexplained colorectal adenomatous polyposis

biomarkers.name: colibactin signature
biomarkers.finding: Detected in 40% of patients; associated with APC mutations in polyps

biomarkers.name: E. coli colibactin‑producing strain
biomarkers.finding: Detected in majority of polyps; concordant with genomic colibactin motif

models.name: Unexplained adenomatous polyposis cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 125 patients, 60 analyzed for APC mosaicism, 1/3 mosaic

clinical_data.metric: Median age at diagnosis (mosaic vs non‑mosaic)

MOA_summary: Somatic APC mosaicism arising early in embryogenesis drives polyposis in one‑third of unexplained cases, while colibactin‑producing E. coli induces a mutational signature that co‑occurs with APC mutations, together contributing to adenoma burden and earlier disease onset.
key_findings: APC mosaicism was identified in ~33% of tested unexplained polyposis patients.
Mosaic carriers presented at a younger median age (45 y) and had higher adenoma counts than non‑mosaic patients.
Colibactin‑associated mutational signatures were present in 40% of patients and often co‑occurred with APC mutations within the same lesion.
Blood detection of low‑frequency mosaic APC variants was possible in two cases, confirming systemic mosaicism.
Both mosaic APC and colibactin exposure represent distinct etiologic pathways for unexplained adenomatous polyposis.
open_questions: What is the relative contribution of bacterial colibactin versus APC mosaicism to cancer risk?
Can systemic screening for low‑frequency APC mosaicism improve early detection?

talk_title: Intrinsic and extrinsic determinants of racial disparities in breast cancer biology

biomarkers.name: COL1A1 collagen density
biomarkers.finding: Higher tumoral collagen in Black women tumors

biomarkers.name: CD4/CD8 T‑cell ratio
biomarkers.finding: Elevated CD4 and reduced CD8 in Black women; further increased with high poverty burden

models.genotype_or_key_features: ≈60 archival tumors, oversampled Black women, annotated socioeconomic data

models.name: HCC1500, MDA‑175, MCF7, T47D
models.genotype_or_key_features: HR‑positive lines derived from Black or White patients

clinical_data.value: higher in Black women; further increased with high poverty
increased with collagen density for Black‑derived cells (p<0.05)
clinical_data.population: Black vs White HR‑positive cell lines

MOA_summary: Black women’s breast tumors exhibit higher collagen deposition and a CD4‑dominant, immunosuppressive T‑cell milieu; poverty amplifies the CD4 signature, and collagen‑rich environments enhance invasive potential of Black‑derived tumor cells, linking intrinsic ancestry‑related biology with extrinsic socioeconomic stressors.
key_findings: Tumors from Black women show significantly increased collagen content compared with White women.
CD4 T‑cell infiltration is enriched and CD8 depleted in Black women’s tumors; poverty burden further raises CD4 dominance.
Black‑derived HR‑positive cell lines require collagen‑containing matrix for 3D growth, unlike White‑derived lines.
HCC1500 xenografts (Black origin) form palpable tumors with radial collagen alignment; MDA‑175 (White) do not.
In silico invasion modeling predicts collagen density boosts invasion only for Black‑derived cells.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: White‑derived cell lines fail to thrive in collagen‑rich matrices, indicating selective microenvironmental dependence.
open_questions: Does targeting collagen remodeling improve outcomes specifically in Black patients?
Can socioeconomic interventions modify the tumor immune microenvironment?

talk_title: Microdysplastic cytomorphologic signatures in normal breast tissue predict future cancer risk

biomarkers.name: Epithelial microdysplastic signature
biomarkers.finding: Associated with ≥2‑fold increased breast cancer risk (HR≈2.1)

biomarkers.name: Stromal microdysplastic signature
biomarkers.finding: Associated with ≥2‑fold increased risk; strongest for ER‑negative cancers

biomarkers.name: Mixed epithelial‑stromal signature
biomarkers.finding: ≥2‑fold risk increase, independent of age

Risk for ER‑negative cancer with stromal signature
Risk in benign disease cohort with any signature

MOA_summary: Quantitative single‑cell analysis of H&E slides identifies epithelial nuclear enlargement and stromal inflammation signatures that reflect early oncogenic and immune alterations, conferring a two‑fold or greater future breast cancer risk independent of age and histologic atypia.
readouts: nuclear size, perimeter, chromasia, density metrics
key_findings: Four microdysplastic signatures (absent, epithelial, stromal, mixed) were defined based on nuclear and stromal metrics.
Presence of any signature conferred >2‑fold increased breast cancer risk after multivariable adjustment.
Stromal signature strongly predicted ER‑negative cancers, especially in stroma‑rich tissue.
Signatures were predictive across age groups (<50 and ≥50) and in benign disease cohorts.
RNA‑seq of signature‑positive tissues showed immune and wound‑repair pathways activation.
open_questions: Can integration of mammographic density improve predictive performance?
Will prospective application of the signatures guide personalized surveillance?

talk_title: Deep plasma proteomics for early‑stage breast cancer detection in dense breasts

biomarkers.name: Proteomic classifier score
biomarkers.finding: AUC 0.96 overall; 90.2% sensitivity in high‑density breasts at 90.4% specificity

models.name: Case‑control study
models.genotype_or_key_features: 335 women (116 early‑stage breast cancer, 219 healthy); 70% with density data

MOA_summary: A mass‑spectrometry‑based deep proteomic platform quantifies ~7,000 plasma proteins; a machine‑learning classifier distinguishes early‑stage breast cancer, achieving high sensitivity especially in women with dense breast tissue where conventional imaging is limited.
key_findings: Deep proteomics identified ~7,000 proteins per sample in both cancer and healthy cohorts.
Classifier achieved AUC 0.96, 86.2% sensitivity and 90.4% specificity overall.
In women with high breast density, sensitivity rose to 90.2% at fixed 90.4% specificity.
Stage‑0, I, II cancers detected with sensitivities of 84.6%, 88%, and 97% respectively.
Prospective pilot (n≈300) showed 89% sensitivity and 93% NPV, supporting clinical utility.

combination_strategies.rationale: Proteomic test can triage women who would benefit most from MRI
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved detection while reducing unnecessary MRIs

open_questions: Will assay performance be consistent across diverse racial/ethnic groups?
How will positive proteomic results impact downstream diagnostic work‑up and cost?

talk_title: Multi‑analyte liquid biopsy for early detection of pancreatic cancer in high‑risk individuals

biomarkers.name: 5‑hmC signature
biomarkers.finding: Distinguishes pancreatic cancer from non‑cancer with high specificity

biomarkers.name: cfDNA fragment size
biomarkers.finding: Shorter fragments enriched in cancer samples

biomarkers.name: Adjusted CA19‑9
biomarkers.finding: Improves tumor‑specific signal when combined with 5‑hmC and fragment size

MOA_summary: A multi‑analyte liquid biopsy combines 5‑hmC epigenomic profiling, cfDNA fragment‑size analysis, and genotype‑adjusted CA19‑9 to generate a machine‑learning classifier that discriminates pancreatic cancer from non‑cancer high‑risk individuals with >80% sensitivity and >97% specificity.
key_findings: Training set achieved 80.5% overall sensitivity and 97.5% specificity for pancreatic cancer detection.
Independent validation (n=1,445) confirmed 82.6% sensitivity and 97.5% specificity.
Performance remained consistent across three additional high‑risk validation cohorts.
Specificity loss across 11 non‑pancreatic cancers was negligible (<0.05%).
Pathway analysis of selected features recapitulated cancer hallmarks, confirming biological relevance.

combination_strategies.rationale: Combine blood‑based risk score with imaging to improve early detection
combination_strategies.phenotype: Higher PPV and earlier stage identification

open_questions: Can the assay detect pre‑diagnostic cancer in longitudinal samples?
What is performance in patients with IPMN or other premalignant pancreatic lesions?

talk_title: Risk-based breast cancer screening: evidence, models, and implementation challenges

speaker.name: Graham Colditz
speaker.affiliation: Washington University School of Medicine, St. Louis

MOA_summary: Colditz presented a framework for risk-based breast cancer screening that stratifies women by absolute five‑year risk using calibrated models (e.g., Gail, Tyrer‑Cuzick) and assigns screening frequency accordingly, aiming to increase mortality reduction while decreasing false‑positive workups.
key_findings: Risk‑based screening could improve breast cancer deaths averted while reducing false‑positive workups by up to 25% (CISNET analysis).
No consensus exists on a universal five‑year risk cut‑point for initiating annual screening; proposed cut points: >3% for annual + supplemental imaging, 1.67–3% for annual, <1.67% for biennial.
Implementation challenges include lack of consistent risk thresholds across trials, guideline variability, and need for model‑agnostic frameworks.
open_questions: How can a universally accepted absolute risk threshold be defined for clinical implementation?
What strategies will ensure equitable adoption of risk‑based screening across diverse populations?

cited_papers: JNCI systematic review on image‑based risk models

talk_title: Development and validation of blood‑based biomarkers to guide mammography interval

biomarkers.name: REG4 protein
