biomarkers.finding: validated in CHS cohort (p<0.05) with specificity ~95% and sensitivity ~20‑35%

biomarkers.name: JAK2 protein
biomarkers.finding: validated in CHS cohort (p<0.05) with similar performance

models.name: Women's Health Initiative (WHI)
models.genotype_or_key_features: women 50‑69, HR‑positive breast cancer cases, preclinical plasma

models.name: Cardiovascular Health Study (CHS)
models.genotype_or_key_features: women >65, HR‑positive breast cancer cases, preclinical plasma

MOA_summary: Lee described a PROBE‑compliant pipeline that used pre‑clinical plasma from WHI and CHS cohorts to discover protein and auto‑antibody markers (e.g., REG4, JAK2) associated with future hormone‑receptor‑positive breast cancer, then combined them into a four‑marker panel achieving 50% sensitivity at 95% specificity to guide individualized mammography recall intervals.
key_findings: From 3,000 screened proteins and 3,000 auto‑antibodies, 6 protein and 5 auto‑antibody markers met pre‑specified statistical criteria.
A four‑marker panel (including REG4 and JAK2) achieved 50% sensitivity at 95% specificity, exceeding the minimal performance needed to inform six‑month recall.
Validated markers in an independent CHS cohort (p<0.05 for 6 proteins, 5 auto‑antibodies).
Developed clinical‑grade MSD assays for top markers and plan prospective validation in the MD Anderson MERIT cohort (n≈250 cancers).

combination_strategies.rationale: Integrate imaging biomarkers to improve risk prediction beyond blood markers alone
combination_strategies.phenotype: potentially higher AUC and better interval risk stratification

open_questions: Will the four‑marker blood panel retain performance in diverse racial/ethnic populations?
What is the optimal decision rule for combining blood markers with imaging and clinical data?

cited_papers: Pepe et al. 2008 J Natl Cancer Inst (PROBE design)

talk_title: Precision screening and surveillance strategies for colorectal cancer

speaker.name: Mingyang Song
speaker.affiliation: Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health

models.name: Nurses' Health Study / Nurses' Health Study II / Health Professionals Follow‑Up Study
models.genotype_or_key_features: prospective cohort, detailed lifestyle, diet, genetics, CRC outcomes

clinical_data.metric: age to reach benchmark 10‑year CRC risk
clinical_data.value: 44 years for highest‑risk, 56 years for lowest‑risk
clinical_data.population: high‑risk vs low‑risk individuals in NHS/HPFS cohorts

clinical_data.value: persistent risk reduction up to 20 years (RR <1)
clinical_data.population: participants with negative colonoscopy vs no colonoscopy

clinical_data.value: 64% received surveillance; 21% adhered to recommended interval
clinical_data.population: MGB colonoscopy cohort with high‑risk polyps

clinical_data.metric: polygenic risk score association with post‑colonoscopy CRC
clinical_data.population: colonoscoped individuals in NHS/HPFS and UK Biobank

clinical_data.metric: adenoma detection rate (ADR) impact on post‑colonoscopy CRC
clinical_data.value: 3‑fold higher 10‑year CRC incidence for lowest ADR quartile vs highest

MOA_summary: Song presented epidemiologic and genetic risk models that estimate individual 10‑year colorectal cancer risk, showing that high‑risk individuals could start colonoscopy up to six years earlier and low‑risk individuals could delay up to six years; post‑colonoscopy risk varies by polyps, colonoscopy quality (ADR), and polygenic risk scores, supporting personalized surveillance intervals.
key_findings: High‑risk individuals (top risk score) reach the 10‑year CRC risk benchmark at age 44, suggesting screening 6 years earlier than standard age 50.
Low‑risk individuals (bottom risk score) reach the benchmark at age 56, indicating possible delay of screening by 6 years.
Negative colonoscopy confers sustained risk reduction for up to 20 years, but risk varies widely by underlying risk score, allowing interval extension up to 25 years for very low‑risk patients.
Only 64% of patients with high‑risk polyps receive recommended surveillance, and only 21% adhere to the guideline‑specified interval.
Polygenic risk scores add modest predictive value (AUC increase ~0.02) for post‑colonoscopy CRC and polyp recurrence.
Physician adenoma detection rate strongly predicts post‑colonoscopy CRC risk, with a three‑fold difference between lowest and highest ADR quartiles.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Current risk tools have modest AUC (~0.63), limiting cost‑effectiveness of risk‑based screening.
Implementation barriers include algorithm complexity, provider/patient acceptance, and potential to exacerbate disparities.

combination_strategies.rationale: Improve ADR and detect missed lesions, reducing interval cancers
combination_strategies.phenotype: higher ADR associated with lower post‑colonoscopy CRC incidence

combination_strategies.rationale: Capture life‑course exposures not reflected in questionnaire data
combination_strategies.phenotype: potentially improve risk prediction beyond PRS and epidemiologic factors

open_questions: How can risk models achieve higher discrimination (AUC >0.75) to be cost‑effective?
What is the optimal way to integrate PRS, lifestyle, and colonoscopy quality into a single actionable risk score?
How to ensure equitable access to precision colonoscopy recommendations across socioeconomic groups?

cited_papers: Lansdorp‑Vogelaar et al. cost‑effectiveness of risk‑based CRC screening 2023
CISNET breast cancer risk‑based screening analysis 2024

talk_title: Mapping the Molecular Landscape of Head and Neck Cancer to Inform Pan‑Tumor Targeting

speaker.name: Lillian L. Siu
speaker.affiliation: Princess Margaret Cancer Centre, University of Toronto, Senior Medical Oncologist, Director of the Phase 1 Clinical Trials Program

biomarkers.name: ctDNA MRD
biomarkers.finding: Positive ctDNA after definitive therapy predicted relapse with lead time 100–245 days; MRD‑negative patients had markedly better relapse‑free survival (HR significant).

biomarkers.name: HPV DNA in plasma
biomarkers.finding: HPV‑positive ctDNA identified early relapse and stratified patients similarly to mutation‑based MRD.

biomarkers.name: PD‑L1 expression
biomarkers.finding: Required for enrollment on bispecific arm; all randomized patients were PD‑L1 positive.

biomarkers.name: TCR repertoire richness
biomarkers.finding: Patients with decreasing ctDNA showed increased TCR diversity; rising ctDNA correlated with reduced repertoire richness.

models.name: MERIDIAN
models.genotype_or_key_features: locally advanced HNSCC (oral cavity, oropharynx, larynx, hypopharynx), stage III‑IV, HPV‑positive or high‑risk HPV‑negative, PD‑L1 positive, no macroscopic disease

models.name: PREMERIDIAN feasibility cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: ≈17 patients with curative‑intent surgery/chemoradiation, serial plasma sampling

clinical_data.metric: Relapse‑free survival hazard ratio (MRD‑positive vs MRD‑negative)

MOA_summary: Dr. Siu demonstrated that ultrasensitive tumor‑informed ctDNA (RADAR) and HPV‑DNA assays can detect molecular residual disease months before radiographic recurrence in head‑and‑neck cancer. Randomization of MRD‑positive patients to a PD‑1‑targeting bispecific antibody (revagostumab) resulted in durable disease‑free survival, supporting the concept of early immunotherapy interception based on real‑time MRD status.
key_findings: Feasibility study (PREMERIDIAN) showed 100% blood sample capture in 17 patients and identified ctDNA lead times of 100–245 days before clinical recurrence.

In MERIDIAN, only 5/42 evaluable patients were ctDNA‑positive at early post‑treatment time points, and most of those already had macroscopic disease, highlighting the need for radiologic confirmation of MRD.
MRD‑negative patients (≈35) had excellent relapse‑free outcomes (30/35 alive without recurrence, median follow‑up >22 months).
The single patient randomized to revagostumab remained disease‑free for >22 months, whereas the observation patient progressed, providing proof‑of‑concept for MRD‑guided immunotherapy interception.
Longitudinal ctDNA sampling (multiple post‑treatment windows) increased detection sensitivity compared with single landmark testing.
Early ctDNA kinetics (day 8) after PD‑1 blockade in advanced disease correlated with later response, and decreasing ctDNA was associated with increased peripheral TCR diversity.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: MRD detection sensitivity varied by assay type; tumor‑informed assays outperformed tumor‑agnostic panels.
Some ctDNA‑positive results coincided with overt radiologic disease, indicating false‑positive MRD if imaging is not integrated.
Limited assay sensitivity in head‑and‑neck cancer compared with other sites (e.g., colorectal) may restrict broader applicability.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target residual disease with potent immune activation before macroscopic relapse.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Disease‑free for >22 months in MRD‑positive patient vs progression in observation.

combination_strategies.rationale: Combine tumor‑associated antigen targeting with checkpoint blockade to overcome immune resistance in advanced HNSCC.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Planned study; no efficacy data yet.

open_questions: What is the optimal timing and number of ctDNA sampling points to maximize MRD detection without over‑testing?
Can newer ultrasensitive assays increase the proportion of true MRD‑positive patients eligible for interception?
How generalizable are head‑and‑neck MRD kinetics and bispecific immunotherapy benefits to other tumor types?

cited_papers: Signatera assay validation studies (clinical cancer research)
Siu et al. 2022 Cancer Cell perspective on adaptive trial design

talk_title: Risk for Pediatric Cancer in Newborn Screening

models.name: Michigan newborn blood spot cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 2000 children with linked tumor outcomes, 6.8% pathogenic variants

models.name: NCARE prospective newborn screening trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: target 10,000 newborns, focus on RB1, WT1, DICER1

clinical_data.metric: cancer death reduction for predisposition carriers
estimated cancer risk for positive blood spot

MOA_summary: Newborn screening using targeted NGS panels identifies pathogenic variants in cancer predisposition genes (e.g., RB1, TP53) from dried blood spots, enabling early surveillance and treatment to reduce mortality and treatment-related toxicity in high‑risk children.
key_findings: Modeling predicts newborn cancer‑risk screening could reduce deaths before age 20 by 8% and by up to 54% in carriers of predisposition syndromes.

Retrospective analysis of 2,000 Michigan newborn spots found 6.8% carried pathogenic variants, with 5/5 RET carriers developing medullary thyroid cancer and RB1 carriers developing retinoblastoma or pinealoblastoma.
Estimated incidence of cancer among newborns with a positive screen is ~1 in 30,000, comparable to other rare screened conditions.
Prospective NCARE trial will randomize 10,000 mothers to focused vs. broad panels to assess feasibility, acceptability, and downstream clinical impact.
open_questions: What proportion of newborns with a positive genetic screen will never develop cancer (penetrance uncertainty)?
How to balance panel breadth with cost and false‑positive rates in a public health setting?

talk_title: Therapeutic Precision Cancer Prevention: The Power of Canaries

models.name: CAP2 (Lynch syndrome aspirin trial)
models.genotype_or_key_features: MSH2/MLH1 carriers, 4‑year aspirin, follow‑up to 10 years

models.name: CAP3 dose‑finding aspirin trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: 1,547 Lynch syndrome carriers, 100 mg, 300 mg, 600 mg aspirin arms

clinical_data.metric: hazard ratio for all Lynch‑syndrome cancers (CAP3 vs historic placebo)
incidence reduction for upper GI cancers (CAP2)
serious adverse events in 100 mg aspirin arm

MOA_summary: Low‑dose aspirin reduces cancer incidence in Lynch syndrome by inhibiting platelet‑derived thromboxane A2, thereby enhancing anti‑tumor T‑cell responses; dose‑finding trials (CAP3) show the smallest dose (100 mg) yields the fewest cancers with minimal toxicity.
key_findings: CAP3 trial (1,547 participants) demonstrated the lowest cancer incidence in the 100 mg aspirin arm, with 30% of participants experiencing the fewest cancers after 8 years.
Serious adverse events were absent in the 100 mg group, indicating a favorable safety profile compared with higher doses.
Combined analysis of CAP2 and CAP3 yields HR 0.67 versus historic placebo, confirming a protective effect of aspirin across Lynch‑syndrome cancer sites.
Guideline update in the UK will recommend ~81 mg aspirin starting five years before the first colonoscopy for Lynch carriers.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Aspirin efficacy appears dose‑dependent with lower doses outperforming higher doses, suggesting a narrow therapeutic window.
Obesity may require dose doubling, indicating metabolic factors influence aspirin’s preventive effect.

combination_strategies.rationale: Aspirin inactivates platelet COX‑1, reducing thromboxane A2‑mediated T‑cell inhibition, synergizing with vaccine‑induced immunity
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced tumor‑specific T‑cell responses and reduced cancer incidence

open_questions: What is the optimal aspirin dose for obese Lynch carriers?
Can aspirin be combined with emerging MSI‑plus assay‑guided surveillance to further lower cancer risk?

talk_title: Vaccines for Cancer Prevention and Intervention

biomarkers.name: type‑1 T‑cell response
biomarkers.finding: 100% of NAUS209 recipients generated CD8+ T‑cell responses to at least one frameshift antigen

biomarkers.name: antibody titers against HER2
biomarkers.finding: Significant increase in HER2‑specific IgG in >70% of DCIS vaccine recipients

models.name: NAUS209 Lynch syndrome vaccine
models.genotype_or_key_features: 45 Lynch syndrome patients with high‑grade dysplasia, phase I

models.name: TgMMTV‑PyM obesity‑breast cancer model
models.genotype_or_key_features: Obese mice, Advac vaccine vs sham

clinical_data.metric: immune response rate to frameshift antigens
pathologic complete response in HER2‑positive DCIS

MOA_summary: Vaccines deliver tumor‑associated antigens (e.g., HER2, MUC1, frameshift neoantigens) via dendritic cell loading or viral vectors to elicit robust type‑1 CD4⁺/CD8⁺ T‑cell and antibody responses that can eradicate pre‑invasive lesions (interception) or provide prophylactic immunity in high‑risk individuals such as Lynch syndrome carriers.
key_findings: HER2/neu dendritic‑cell vaccination induced type‑1 T‑cell and antibody responses and led to loss of HER2 expression in 7/11 evaluable DCIS patients.
Pathologic complete responses were observed in ~30% of HER2‑positive, ER‑negative DCIS patients receiving the vaccine.
NAUS209 (viral‑vector frameshift vaccine) was safe (8% grade 3 AEs) and immunogenic, with 100% of 45 Lynch syndrome participants generating T‑cell responses, persisting in 85% at 12 months.

Adipocyte‑directed Advac vaccine reduced visceral CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration, restored regulatory T‑cells, improved insulin sensitivity, and prevented tumor development in 60% of obese breast‑cancer‑prone mice.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: HER2 loss after vaccination suggests antigen escape as a potential resistance mechanism.
Obesity‑driven vaccine targets metabolic antigens, addressing a novel selective vulnerability.

combination_strategies.rationale: Aspirin may enhance vaccine‑induced immunity by inhibiting platelet‑derived immunosuppression
combination_strategies.phenotype: synergistic tumor regression in preclinical models

open_questions: Will prophylactic vaccination in Lynch syndrome reduce cancer incidence long‑term?
What is the optimal dosing schedule for anti‑inflammatory vaccines in obese populations?

talk_title: AI in Cancer Diagnosis: Promise, Performance, and the Human‑AI Factor

models.name: PRISM mammography AI trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: 400,000 screening exams, randomization to AI‑assisted vs standard reading

models.name: Pathology AI variability study
models.genotype_or_key_features: 240 cases, 187 pathologists, repeat reads after 6‑month washout

clinical_data.metric: recall rate with AI in mammography (PRISM)
pathologist concordance with expert consensus (skin biopsy)

MOA_summary: AI models process imaging (mammography, pathology slides) to generate diagnostic predictions; variability studies reveal human inter‑observer inconsistency, while AI can improve reproducibility but may introduce automation bias, highlighting the need for rigorous evaluation and human‑in‑the‑loop designs.
key_findings: In a large mammography cohort, AI assistance was associated with a statistically significant increase in recall rate, suggesting potential over‑diagnosis.
Only 10% of 36 pathologists agreed with expert consensus on a challenging skin biopsy, illustrating high diagnostic variability.
Repeated reads by the same pathologists after six months showed poor intra‑observer reproducibility, underscoring the need for decision support.
Human‑AI interaction studies (PRISM) are enrolling 400,000 exams to assess real‑world impact of AI cue timing and presentation.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Automation bias may cause clinicians to over‑rely on AI outputs, increasing false‑positive recalls.
De‑skilling risk: reduced independent diagnostic skill with prolonged AI use.

combination_strategies.rationale: Combining AI predictions with expert oversight may mitigate automation bias and improve reproducibility
combination_strategies.phenotype: maintained accuracy with reduced recall rates in pilot studies

open_questions: What is the optimal timing and format of AI cues to minimize automation bias?
How can AI training datasets be diversified to avoid racial and demographic bias?

talk_title: STING agonists amplify BH3 mimetic activity via p53- and cGAS-STING-dependent apoptosis loops

biomarkers.name: p53 status
biomarkers.finding: p53 loss correlates with reduced amplification loop and resistance to BH3 mimetics

biomarkers.name: STING expression
biomarkers.finding: High STING levels predict sensitivity to STING agonist killing in AML samples

models.name: Rag knockout mice
models.genotype_or_key_features: immune deficient, engrafted with Cas9-expressing lymphoma

clinical_data.value: 50% of mice survived with dual treatment
clinical_data.comparator: STING agonist alone or MCL-1 inhibitor alone

clinical_data.population: Cas9 transgenic mice with p53 knockout lymphoma
human AML PDX treated with venetoclax + STING agonist

MOA_summary: STING agonists activate cGAS-STING signaling leading to IRF3-dependent transcription of BH3-only proteins (BIM, NOXA, PUMA) and p53 activation, creating a feed‑forward loop that amplifies apoptosis; combined with BH3 mimetics (venetoclax or MCL‑1 inhibitors) this overcomes resistance by releasing BIM from anti‑apoptotic proteins, resulting in enhanced tumor cell killing in vitro and in mouse models.
key_findings: STING agonists induce p53‑independent IRF3 activation and upregulation of BIM, NOXA, and PUMA in lymphoma and leukemia cells.
Loss of Bax/Bak abolishes STING‑agonist‑mediated apoptosis, confirming dependence on the intrinsic pathway.
Combination of STING agonist with BH3 mimetics (MCL‑1 inhibitor or venetoclax) yields synergistic killing in vitro and improves survival in immune‑deficient and immune‑competent mouse models.
High STING expression in AML patient samples predicts sensitivity to STING agonist monotherapy and to the combination with venetoclax.
In vivo, dual treatment leads to ~50% survival benefit in Rag‑KO mice and extends median survival three‑fold in Cas9‑transgenic mice.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: p53 loss reduces the amplification loop, conferring resistance to BH3 mimetics.
BCL2 point mutations can abrogate venetoclax binding while preserving BH3‑only protein interaction.

combination_strategies.rationale: STING agonist upregulates BH3‑only proteins, increasing dependence on anti‑apoptotic proteins that are blocked by BH3 mimetics.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor cell killing and improved mouse survival

open_questions: How can STING expression be increased in cancer cells with low baseline STING levels?
What are the precise molecular mechanisms linking mitochondrial outer membrane permeabilization to p53 activation?

talk_title: Mitochondrial priming, BCL2 family dependencies and resistance mechanisms in multiple myeloma

biomarkers.name: BIM (BCL2L11) expression
biomarkers.finding: Higher BIM levels correlate with increased mitochondrial priming and venetoclax sensitivity

biomarkers.name: IL6 levels
biomarkers.finding: Stromal IL6 induces resistance to BCL2 inhibition by shifting BIM binding to MCL1

biomarkers.name: BIM phosphorylation (Ser94)
biomarkers.finding: Phosphorylation at Ser94 reduces sensitivity to MCL1 inhibitor AZD5991

biomarkers.name: t(11;14) translocation
biomarkers.finding: Patients with t(11;14) show higher ex‑vivo venetoclax sensitivity (≈40% response vs 6% without)

models.name: KMS18
models.genotype_or_key_features: BCL2‑bound BIM, shifts to MCL1 upon venetoclax

models.name: venetoclax trial cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: n≈70, includes t(11;14) and non‑t(11;14) patients

clinical_data.value: 40% in t(11;14) patients vs 6% in non‑t(11;14)
clinical_data.population: multiple myeloma patients treated with venetoclax

clinical_data.value: significantly longer in venetoclax‑sensitive ex‑vivo group

MOA_summary: Myeloma cells exhibit high mitochondrial priming driven by BIM up‑regulation; venetoclax releases BIM from BCL2 but BIM can be re‑sequestered by MCL1, especially after IL6‑mediated signaling or BIM phosphorylation, leading to resistance. Combination with dexamethasone or IL6 blockade restores priming and sensitizes cells, while t(11;14) myeloma shows intrinsic BCL2 dependence and higher venetoclax response.
key_findings: BIM is up‑regulated in myeloma and drives mitochondrial priming, making cells vulnerable to BH3 mimetics.
Venetoclax treatment releases BIM from BCL2, which then binds MCL1, causing resistance in BCL2‑dependent lines.
Dexamethasone increases total BIM and restores venetoclax sensitivity across resistant myeloma samples.
Stromal IL6 shifts BIM binding to MCL1, conferring resistance; IL6 neutralization or Tocilizumab pretreatment re‑sensitizes cells to venetoclax.
BIM phosphorylation at Ser94 (and Ser87) reduces sensitivity to MCL1 inhibition, indicating a post‑translational resistance mechanism.
t(11;14) myeloma shows higher venetoclax sensitivity linked to a B‑cell‑like differentiation state rather than BCL2 expression levels.
Acquired BCL2 point mutations (e.g., G101V) after therapy diminish venetoclax binding and drive clinical resistance.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: IL6‑mediated signaling induces rapid, post‑translational adaptation increasing MCL1 and shifting BIM binding, leading to venetoclax resistance.
BIM phosphorylation (Ser94) creates resistance to MCL1 inhibitors.
Acquired BCL2 mutations (G101V) impair venetoclax efficacy while preserving BIM binding.

combination_strategies.rationale: Increases BIM transcription, enhancing priming and venetoclax efficacy
combination_strategies.phenotype: Conversion of venetoclax‑resistant cells to sensitive; higher ORR in early phase trial

combination_strategies.rationale: Blocks stromal IL6 signaling that shifts BIM to MCL1
combination_strategies.phenotype: Restores venetoclax sensitivity in vitro and in patient‑derived samples

combination_strategies.rationale: Dual inhibition of BCL2 and MCL1 overcomes BIM redistribution
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic killing in cell lines; resistance observed with BIM phosphorylation

open_questions: What kinases mediate BIM Ser94 phosphorylation and can they be targeted to prevent resistance?
How does Ras activation confer venetoclax resistance independent of MCL1 up‑regulation?

talk_title: Domain Alteration Chimeras (DALTACs) reprogram AR-p300 enhancer complexes in prostate cancer

biomarkers.name: N‑terminal H2B acetylation
biomarkers.finding: Markedly elevated in malignant prostate epithelium versus benign and reduced after DALTAC treatment

MOA_summary: The DALTAC heterobifunctional molecule simultaneously binds the androgen receptor ligand‑binding domain and the p300 bromodomain, inducing a ternary complex that repositions p300 from its native chromatin sites to AR‑bound enhancers. This relocalization extinguishes N‑terminal H2B acetylation, suppresses AR‑target gene transcription, and creates neomorphic complexes that impair transcriptional elongation, leading to selective cytotoxicity in AR‑positive prostate cancer cells.
key_findings: DALTAC induced ternary complex formation between AR and p300 confirmed by proximity ligation assay and IP‑mass spec.
In AR‑positive 22RV1 cells, 10 nM DALTAC caused >90% reduction in crystal violet staining, whereas p300 inhibitor or AR inhibitor alone showed minimal effect.

Across a panel of prostate cancer cell lines, AR‑positive lines displayed nanomolar IC50s (median ~5 nM) while AR‑negative and normal cell lines had IC50s >10 µM, demonstrating >2000‑fold selectivity.
In castrated VCAP xenografts, oral DALTAC modestly inhibited tumor growth; intraperitoneal dosing achieved tumor regression in all treated mice with no regrowth after drug withdrawal.
Histone H2B N‑terminal acetylation, a pharmacodynamic biomarker, was abolished in tumors treated with DALTAC, correlating with loss of AR‑target gene expression.
Toxicology showed no significant weight loss, CBC, or chemistry abnormalities, indicating a favorable safety profile in mice.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: DALTAC activity is exquisitely dependent on co‑expression of AR and p300; AR‑negative prostate cancer and neuroblastoma cells are resistant.
Potential resistance mechanisms include down‑regulation of AR or p300 expression and emergence of AR splice variants lacking the ligand‑binding domain.
open_questions: Will DALTAC retain activity in enzalutamide‑resistant patient‑derived organoids?
What are the long‑term effects of sequestering p300 on non‑AR transcriptional programs?
Can normal androgen‑dependent tissues tolerate AR‑p300 gluing without functional impairment?

cited_papers: Crews et al. 2019 Nat Chem Biol

talk_title: Phase I/II trial of heterobifunctional BRD4‑AR degrader (RIPTag) in heavily pre‑treated CRPC

models.name: Phase I/II BRD4‑AR RIPTag trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: Heavily pre‑treated CRPC, prior taxanes, AR‑targeted therapy, some with AR splice variants

clinical_data.value: radiographic regressions observed in liver and prostate lesions

MOA_summary: The heterobifunctional RIPTag molecule simultaneously binds BRD4 bromodomain and the AR ligand‑binding domain, creating a non‑degradative ternary complex that locks AR and BRD4 together, disrupting AR transcriptional programs without classic AR antagonism, leading to PSA reductions and radiographic tumor regressions in heavily pre‑treated CRPC.
key_findings: No dose‑limiting toxicities observed; MTD not reached up to the highest tested dose (~1000 Da molecule).
Patients experienced rapid, dose‑dependent increases in Cmax and AUC with steady‑state achieved within a few days.
Significant PSA declines (≥50%) were seen in a subset of heavily pre‑treated CRPC patients, including those with AR splice variants.
Radiographic regressions were documented in liver metastases and primary prostate lesions, indicating drug penetration into sanctuary sites.
Common adverse events were mild nausea, anemia, and fatigue; overall tolerability was favorable.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Efficacy appears higher in patients with AR mutations or splice variants; the drug does not rely on AR degradation.
open_questions: Will the RIPTag maintain activity in castration‑sensitive metastatic disease?
What biomarkers predict response beyond AR mutation status?

talk_title: DLL3‑targeted T‑cell engager gocatumig in neuroendocrine prostate cancer

biomarkers.name: DLL3 expression (IHC)
biomarkers.finding: High DLL3 expression in 86% of NEPC clusters; response rates did not correlate strongly with expression level

biomarkers.name: ASCL1 expression
biomarkers.finding: Strong correlation with DLL3; ASCL1 knock‑out reduces DLL3 levels

models.name: Phase I/II gocatumig pan‑neuroendocrine carcinoma trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: Heavily pre‑treated NEPC patients, mixed histology, prior therapies

models.name: DLL3‑positive NEPC PDX with human T cells

clinical_data.value: ≥90% decline in 50% of patients; ≥99% decline in 30% of patients
Reduced DLL3 protein levels in post‑progression biopsies
clinical_data.population: Patients with initial response then progression

MOA_summary: Gocatumig is a bispecific T‑cell engager that binds DLL3 on neuroendocrine prostate cancer cells and CD3 on T cells, with an albumin‑binding domain to improve half‑life. Engagement redirects cytotoxic T cells to DLL3‑expressing tumor cells, inducing tumor regression; a by‑stander effect may contribute to activity against DLL3‑negative cells within heterogeneous tumors.
readouts: flow cytometry of CD3+ T cells
key_findings: Phase I/II trial showed an overall response rate of ~50% and disease control rate of 72% in heavily pre‑treated NEPC patients.
Deep PSA reductions were observed: 50% of patients achieved ≥90% PSA decline, and 30% achieved ≥99% decline.
Response did not strongly correlate with baseline DLL3 IHC intensity, indicating possible by‑stander killing or heterogeneous antigen expression.
Acquired resistance was associated with loss of DLL3 protein in post‑treatment biopsies, suggesting antigen loss as a resistance mechanism.
Pre‑clinical PDX models demonstrated potent tumor regression and increased CD3+ T‑cell infiltration after gocatumig treatment.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Loss of DLL3 expression in progressing lesions suggests antigen‑escape as a resistance pathway.
Heterogeneity of DLL3 within and between tumors may limit efficacy of single‑target approaches.

combination_strategies.rationale: PARP inhibition depletes myeloid suppressor cells, potentially enhancing T‑cell engager activity
combination_strategies.phenotype: Pre‑clinical data show myeloid clearance and improved tumor control

combination_strategies.rationale: Combining checkpoint inhibition may overcome T‑cell exhaustion in the tumor microenvironment
combination_strategies.phenotype: Ongoing early‑phase combination trial

open_questions: What molecular features distinguish DLL3‑high responders from non‑responders despite similar antigen levels?
Can dual‑targeting (e.g., DLL3 + B7‑H3) improve durability and prevent antigen‑loss resistance?
How does prior PSMA‑targeted radioligand therapy affect DLL3 expression and gocatumig efficacy?

cited_papers: Ku et al. 2023 Nat Med (DLL3 in NEPC)
Donad et al. 2022 Cancer Res (gocatumig preclinical)

talk_title: Immunological considerations and B7H3-targeted radioligand therapy mechanisms

targets.dependency_context: 4IG isoform overexpressed in prostate, breast, colon, lung, gastric cancers

models.name: CT26 (murine colorectal carcinoma transduced with human 4IG B7H3)
BetaBART Phase 1/2
models.genotype_or_key_features: first‑in‑human enrollment, dose 100 µCi Y‑90 MIL‑33B

clinical_data.population: IR‑resistant CT26 tumors in immunocompetent mice

MOA_summary: BetaBART is a humanized anti‑B7H3 antibody with 20 pM affinity for the tumor‑specific 4‑Ig isoform; when labeled with Y‑90 it delivers beta radiation that induces DNA damage, immunogenic cell death and a CD8‑dependent in situ vaccination, leading to long‑term tumor control and immune memory in murine models.
key_findings: MIL‑33B (Zr‑89) specifically localizes to 4IG‑expressing tumors in vivo, with loss of signal in B7H3‑KO or excess cold antibody controls.
A single 100 µCi Y‑90‑MIL‑33B injection yields 56% long‑term survival in IR‑resistant CT26 tumors, whereas controls die.
CD8 T‑cell depletion abolishes the survival benefit, indicating CD8‑mediated adaptive immunity is essential.
Treated mice develop immune memory, rejecting rechallenge with the same tumor cells at >100 days post‑treatment.
Cold (non‑radiolabeled) MIL‑33B antibody at high dose shows no anti‑tumor effect, confirming radiation drives the response.
Small‑molecule NM600 requires checkpoint blockade to achieve long‑term survivors, highlighting modality‑specific immune priming.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Human 4IG isoform is absent in standard mouse models, requiring transduction; circulating soluble 2IG isoform may act as a decoy sink for the antibody.

combination_strategies.rationale: Radioligand‑induced immunogenic cell death may synergize with checkpoint blockade to enhance CD8 T‑cell activity.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Increased long‑term survival and immune memory compared with radioligand alone.

combination_strategies.rationale: Combining beta or alpha emitting NM600 with checkpoint blockade overcomes lack of single‑agent efficacy.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Long‑term survivors observed only in combination arms; actinium‑225 more effective in immune‑resistant melanoma.

open_questions: Can the 4IG‑specific antibody be engineered to avoid the soluble 2IG decoy and improve tumor delivery?
What is the optimal isotope (beta vs alpha) and dosing schedule to maximize immune priming while minimizing lymphocyte toxicity?

talk_title: Granzyme‑B targeted PET imaging to monitor immune activation during PSMA‑RLT plus pembrolizumab

targets.dependency_context: activated CD8 T‑cells releasing granzyme B in tumor microenvironment

biomarkers.name: Granzyme B PET signal
biomarkers.finding: Lesions with increased SUV correlated with PSA declines and later tumor response in mCRPC patients

models.name: Phase 1 PSMA‑617 + pembrolizumab with granzyme PET
models.genotype_or_key_features: metastatic castration‑resistant prostate cancer, schedule 1/2 dosing

models.name: Syngeneic tumor models (e.g., MC38, CT26) with checkpoint blockade

clinical_data.value: increase in SUV of 8 bone lesions (April scan) and further intensification on August scan
clinical_data.population: same patient, lesions confirmed as cancer by PSMA PET

clinical_data.value: new lesions became PET‑avid on second scan, matching PSA decline

MOA_summary: A Cu‑64‑labeled GRIP peptide is proteolytically activated by granzyme B released from cytotoxic T cells; the activated probe inserts into nearby membranes, retaining signal on PET and enabling non‑invasive visualization of immune‑mediated tumor killing during PSMA‑targeted radioligand therapy combined with pembrolizumab.
readouts: flow cytometry of granzyme B expression
key_findings: Granzyme‑B PET signal appears in bone lesions after sub‑ablative PSMA‑617 (Pluvicto) plus pembrolizumab, coinciding with PSA reductions.
Serial granzyme PET scans show increased SUV in existing lesions and emergence of new PET‑avid lesions, reflecting evolving immune activation.
Granzyme PET uptake is absent in control mice lacking granzyme B, confirming probe specificity.
Probe uptake correlates with peripheral blood granzyme B induction and with clinical response, suggesting a pharmacodynamic biomarker for RLT‑IO combinations.
No significant off‑target uptake observed in most patients; occasional uptake in sarcoma of unclear relevance.

combination_strategies.rationale: Sub‑ablative radioligand may reprogram tumor microenvironment to enable granzyme‑B release from CD8 T cells.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced granzyme PET signal and PSA decline when administered before or concurrent with pembrolizumab.

combination_strategies.rationale: Checkpoint blockade amplifies CD8 T‑cell activation, increasing granzyme B release detectable by the probe.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Higher response rates in schedule 1/2 cohorts versus schedule 3 (pembrolizumab first).

open_questions: What is the optimal timing of granzyme‑B PET relative to radioligand dosing to capture peak immune activation?
Can granzyme‑B PET predict responders early enough to guide treatment adaptation?

talk_title: Immunostimulatory radioligand therapy to enhance checkpoint blockade and CAR‑T efficacy

targets.dependency_context: expressed on multiple solid and liquid tumors; also on lymphocytes
high B7‑H3 expression in neuroblastoma models
multiple tumor types including B78 melanoma, 4T1 breast cancer

models.name: B16 melanoma
models.genotype_or_key_features: GP100 antigen for CAR T studies

models.name: SK‑N‑AS neuroblastoma (NSG)
models.genotype_or_key_features: adrenergic vs mesenchymal subtypes, VLA4 expression

MOA_summary: Low‑dose targeted radionuclide therapy (e.g., NM600 or Cu‑67‑LLP‑2A) delivers localized radiation that modulates the tumor microenvironment, increases pro‑inflammatory cytokines and CD8 infiltration, and can serve as a lymphodepleting conditioning regimen for checkpoint blockade or CAR‑T cells, while sparing systemic lymphocytes when using small‑molecule agents.
readouts: flow cytometry for CD8, CD4, exhaustion markers (TIM3, LAG3, PD‑1)
key_findings: NM600 at 50–100 µCi (2.5–5 Gy) combined with checkpoint blockade yields ~50% complete responses in B78 melanoma, whereas either monotherapy is ineffective.
In 4T1 metastatic breast cancer, low‑dose NM600 alone controls primary tumor but not micrometastases; combination with checkpoint blockade controls both primary and metastatic disease.
Whole‑body 2.5 Gy radiation abrogates the synergy, indicating systemic lymphocyte depletion impairs efficacy, whereas tumor‑restricted dosing preserves benefit.
Cu‑67‑LLP‑2A enhances lymphodepletion, improving engraftment and persistence of adoptively transferred T cells (Flucy conditioning) and boosting CAR‑T efficacy in B16‑PMEL and neuroblastoma models.
Copper‑67 conditioning reduces exhaustion markers (TIM3, LAG3, PD‑1) on CAR‑T cells and shifts tumor‑associated macrophages from M2 to a less suppressive phenotype.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Full‑length antibody radioligands (e.g., GD2‑Ab) cause systemic lymphodepletion and lack synergy with checkpoint blockade, highlighting the importance of preserving peripheral immune cells.

combination_strategies.rationale: Radiation‑induced immunogenic cell death primes tumors for checkpoint‑mediated T‑cell activation.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression and increased cytokine milieu; loss of effect when CD8 cells depleted.

combination_strategies.rationale: Cu‑67‑LLP‑2A provides lymphodepleting conditioning and tumor microenvironment remodeling to enhance CAR‑T expansion and reduce exhaustion.
combination_strategies.phenotype: 5/7 mice achieve complete response versus 0/7 with CAR‑T alone; increased CD8/T‑cell memory markers.

combination_strategies.rationale: Combining Cu‑67‑LLP‑2A with standard chemotherapy conditioning further improves donor T‑cell engraftment.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Higher donor T‑cell chimerism and tumor control.

open_questions: What is the optimal radiation dose and timing to maximize immune priming while minimizing systemic lymphocyte loss?
Can VLA4‑targeted radionuclide therapy be safely translated to humans for CAR‑T conditioning without excessive off‑target toxicity?

talk_title: Clinical application of radiopharmaceutical theranostics in neuroendocrine tumors and prostate cancer

speaker.name: Dr. Jonathan McConathy
speaker.affiliation: University of Alabama, Department of Radiology

biomarkers.name: SSTR2 PET uptake (DOTATATE SUV)
biomarkers.finding: Higher tumor SUV correlated with improved progression‑free survival in NETTER‑1

biomarkers.name: PSMA PET SUVmean >10
biomarkers.finding: Patients with SUVmean >10 had higher PSA response rates in PSMA‑targeted trials

biomarkers.name: Serum PSA
biomarkers.finding: Median PSA decline >50% observed after three cycles of PSMA‑617 in responsive patients

models.name: NETTER‑1
models.genotype_or_key_features: Grade 1‑2 GEP‑NETs progressed on somatostatin analog

models.name: VISION
models.genotype_or_key_features: Metastatic castration‑resistant prostate cancer after ARPI and taxane

clinical_data.value: significant improvement vs high‑dose somatostatin analog

MOA_summary: Radiolabeled somatostatin analogs (DOTATATE) and PSMA‑targeted ligands (PSMA‑617) bind cell‑surface receptors on neuroendocrine tumors or prostate cancer cells, are internalized, and deliver beta‑particle radiation (Lu‑177) that induces DNA double‑strand breaks, leading to tumor cell death while allowing PET imaging of target expression for patient selection and dosimetry.
key_findings: DOTATATE therapy (NETTER‑1) provided a marked progression‑free survival benefit compared with high‑dose octreotide in Grade 1‑2 GEP‑NETs.
PSMA‑617 therapy (VISION) improved overall survival by 4 months versus standard of care in mCRPC after ARPI and taxane.
Higher baseline PSMA PET SUVmean (>10) predicted greater PSA response and longer PFS in PSMA‑targeted trials.
Patients with high circulating tumor DNA or PTEN loss had poorer outcomes on PSMA therapy, suggesting genomic biomarkers of resistance.
Combination of PSMA‑targeted radioligand therapy with PARP inhibitors or immune checkpoint blockade is feasible and shows early safety signals.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Patients with low PSMA uptake or high FDG‑avid disease showed limited response to PSMA‑617.
PTEN alterations were associated with worse PFS on PSMA therapy, indicating a potential resistance mechanism.

combination_strategies.rationale: Inhibit DNA repair to potentiate radiation‑induced DNA damage
combination_strategies.phenotype: Early phase trials report acceptable safety and suggest enhanced tumor control

combination_strategies.rationale: Radiation may increase neoantigen release and immune activation
combination_strategies.phenotype: Preliminary data show synergistic tumor regression in a subset of patients

combination_strategies.rationale: Increase dose to bulky or resistant lesions while systemic radioligand treats micrometastases
combination_strategies.phenotype: Palliative pain relief and improved local control reported in case series

open_questions: What is the optimal sequencing of beta‑ versus alpha‑emitting PSMA agents?
Can early on‑treatment imaging biomarkers (e.g., new lesions on interim PET) reliably predict long‑term survival?
How should dosing intervals be personalized based on hematologic recovery versus tumor dosimetry?

talk_title: Overview of RAS inhibitor landscape, resistance mechanisms and combination strategies
MOA_summary: KRAS G12C inhibitors achieve modest response rates (~40%) with limited duration (<10 months) due to primary and acquired resistance driven by MAPK pathway reactivation, secondary KRAS mutations, and non‑genetic transcriptional programs such as TEAD, MYC, and ERK activation.
key_findings: 73 RAS inhibitors have entered clinical evaluation with ~150 actively recruiting trials as of 2026.
Median ORR for G12C inhibitors across trials is ~40% with median DOR <10 months.
Acquired resistance mechanisms are split roughly 50% genomic (e.g., MAPK pathway reactivating mutations) and 50% non‑genomic (transcriptional reprogramming).
Pan‑RAS inhibitors (e.g., daraxasib) show activity without reactivation of RAS alleles but face toxicity challenges.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Resistance often involves secondary mutations that reactivate MAPK signaling.
Non‑genetic mechanisms include TEAD, MYC, and ERK transcriptional programs.

combination_strategies.rationale: EGFR blockade synergizes with KRAS inhibition in colorectal cancer to overcome upstream signaling reactivation.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved response rates leading to FDA approval for KRAS‑mutant CRC

open_questions: How to best sequence or combine pan‑RAS inhibitors with EGFR or other RTK inhibitors to minimize toxicity?
Can transient ERK inhibition be timed to avoid on‑target toxicities while preventing rebound signaling?

talk_title: BMAL2 as a KRAS‑linked hypoxia driver in pancreatic cancer

speaker.name: Alvaro (post‑doc, Ken Olive lab)
speaker.affiliation: University of Texas Southwestern / Ken Olive Lab

biomarkers.name: BMAL2 activity score
biomarkers.finding: Higher BMAL2 activity correlates with KRAS activity and poor survival in PDA

models.name: laser capture microdissection cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 200+ PDA and precursor samples, epithelial compartment

models.name: patient‑derived organoids
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS mutant, BMAL2 KO or OE

MOA_summary: BMAL2 functions as a KRAS‑responsive transcription factor that links KRAS signaling to hypoxia responses; it promotes EMT, cell proliferation, and glycolytic metabolism via interaction with HIF‑1α/2α, and its loss delays tumor growth in orthotopic PDA models.
key_findings: Regulatory network analysis identified BMAL2 as the top KRAS‑associated master regulator across >200 PDA samples.
BMAL2 activity decreases after treatment with KRAS inhibitor RNC7977 in vitro and in KPC mouse tumors.
BMAL2 knockout reduces proliferation, EMT markers, and glycolysis; overexpression induces mesenchymal morphology and increases proliferation.
BMAL2 physically interacts with HIF‑1α under hypoxia, suggesting a complex that drives hypoxia‑responsive gene expression.
Orthotopic BMAL2‑KO tumors show prolonged mouse survival compared with controls.

combination_strategies.rationale: BMAL2 activity is KRAS‑dependent; combined inhibition may yield additive anti‑tumor effects.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Predicted synergistic reduction of proliferation and EMT

combination_strategies.rationale: AXL is a BMAL2 target; dual targeting may enhance response.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Potential additive tumor regression

open_questions: Does BMAL2 contribute to acquired resistance to KRAS inhibitors in patients?
Can BMAL2 inhibition sensitize tumors to existing KRAS‑targeted therapies?

talk_title: Rewiring RTK signaling under RAS inhibition in pancreatic cancer

models.name: PDAC PDX panel
models.genotype_or_key_features: various KRAS mutations, basal‑like or classical subtypes

MOA_summary: RAS inhibition induces dynamic up‑regulation of RTKs (EGFR, HER2, ERBB3) in pancreatic cancer, altering receptor internalization and creating windows for ADC delivery; stromal fibroblasts secrete neuregulin to activate ERBB3, and combined RTK blockade enhances tumor regression.
key_findings: RAS inhibition increases HER2 and ERBB3 protein levels in a subset of PDAC models, while EGFR remains stable.
Combining RAS inhibitors with HER2‑targeted ADC (T‑DXd) yields synergistic tumor regression in vitro and in PDX models.
Neuregulin‑1 secreted by cancer‑associated fibroblasts drives ERBB3 activation; anti‑neuregulin antibody plus RAS inhibitor improves survival in murine PDAC.
FRODO assay shows that RAS inhibition accelerates HER2 internalization, enhancing ADC payload delivery.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: RTK up‑regulation may represent an adaptive resistance mechanism to RAS inhibition.

combination_strategies.rationale: EGFR remains a driver in KRAS G12‑mutant PDAC; concurrent inhibition prevents MAPK reactivation.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved tumor control in PDX models

combination_strategies.rationale: Blocks fibroblast‑derived ERBB3 activation that sustains growth under RAS inhibition.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Extended survival in KPC mice

combination_strategies.rationale: RAS inhibition up‑regulates HER2, increasing ADC internalization and cytotoxicity.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression in organoids and PDX

open_questions: How does stromal composition influence RTK dynamics and ADC efficacy?
Can sequential short‑course EGFR inhibition be used to prime ADC internalization while minimizing toxicity?

talk_title: KRAS-targeted therapy Phase III trial results in pancreatic cancer

speaker.name: Julie Fleshman
speaker.affiliation: President and CEO, Pancreatic Cancer Action Network (PanCAN)

models.name: Phase III KRAS inhibitor trial (Revolution Medicines)

MOA_summary: A KRAS-directed small‑molecule inhibitor was evaluated in a Phase III trial for pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, demonstrating a favorable safety profile and evidence of clinical activity that supports KRAS as a therapeutic target in this disease.
key_findings: Revolution Medicines reported positive Phase III trial results for a KRAS‑targeted therapy in pancreatic cancer.
The trial represents a major step forward for KRAS‑directed treatment strategies in pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma.

talk_title: Implementation gaps and early data from the TUBAWISP-2 salpingectomy trial

speaker.name: Rebecca Stone
speaker.affiliation: Johns Hopkins University, Department of Obstetrics and Gynecology

biomarkers.name: BRCA1 pathogenic variant
biomarkers.finding: 56% of BRCA1 carriers delay risk‑reducing surgery beyond age 40

biomarkers.name: BRCA2 pathogenic variant
biomarkers.finding: ≈33% of BRCA2 carriers delay risk‑reducing surgery beyond age 45

biomarkers.name: STIC presence at risk‑reducing surgery
biomarkers.finding: ≈30% 10‑year risk of peritoneal high‑grade serous cancer when STIC is present

models.name: TUBAWISP-2
models.genotype_or_key_features: high‑risk women with germline BRCA1/2 or other pathogenic variants

clinical_data.population: women with STIC at risk‑reducing surgery

clinical_data.metric: hazard ratio for serous ovarian cancer with opportunistic salpingectomy (BC, Canada)
adoption rate of opportunistic salpingectomy at inpatient hysterectomy (US claims data)
adoption rate of salpingectomy as alternative to tubal ligation (outpatient)

MOA_summary: Stone outlined the epidemiologic and health‑policy data supporting salpingectomy as a primary ovarian cancer prevention strategy, presented early enrollment data from the TUBAWISP-2 preference trial comparing standard risk‑reducing salpingo‑oophorectomy to early salpingectomy with delayed oophorectomy, and highlighted implementation barriers (coding, coverage, disparities) that limit adoption despite a 0.22 hazard ratio for serous ovarian cancer.
key_findings: 56% of BRCA1 carriers and ~33% of BRCA2 carriers delay risk‑reducing surgery beyond recommended ages, increasing lifetime serous cancer risk.
Presence of STIC at risk‑reducing surgery confers ~30% 10‑year risk of peritoneal high‑grade serous carcinoma.
Opportunistic salpingectomy reduces serous ovarian cancer risk by ~80% (HR 0.22) in the BC, Canada population‑based cohort.
US adoption of opportunistic salpingectomy at inpatient hysterectomy has plateaued at ~60% since 2018, with lower uptake in outpatient settings (11‑14%).
Racial and insurance disparities exist: Black women and Medicaid recipients are less likely to receive salpingectomy during tubal ligation or postpartum sterilization.
open_questions: How can health‑policy and reimbursement reforms accelerate universal adoption of opportunistic salpingectomy?
What are the optimal surveillance or interception strategies for women with residual STIC after salpingectomy?

talk_title: OVASEEK: hyperspectral and fluorescence imaging platform for whole fallopian tube screening

speaker.name: Angela Belcher
speaker.affiliation: Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Department of Biological Engineering

models.name: fallopian tube specimens
models.genotype_or_key_features: 86 tubes from risk‑reducing salpingectomy patients, including BRCA1/2 carriers

MOA_summary: Belcher described the OVASEEK system that combines near‑infrared hyperspectral imaging, >1000 nm fluorescent nanoparticle labeling, and machine‑learning segmentation to scan entire excised fallopian tubes in ~15 minutes, identify regions with atypical optical signatures, and predict BRCA mutation status and nanoparticle binding with >90% accuracy, enabling whole‑organ detection of STIC and pre‑cancer lesions.
readouts: optical biometrics (tube length, weight, water/lipid content)
key_findings: Imaged 86 fallopian tubes; initial prototype required 8 hours per tube, later optimized toward 15‑minute workflow.
Machine‑learning model predicts presence of gold‑nanoparticle binding sites with 86% accuracy based on hyperspectral data alone.
BRCA1/2 mutation status can be inferred from optical spectra with 90% accuracy at the whole‑tube level and 95% at the cellular level.
Detected a previously missed STIC in a colon‑surgery patient by highlighting a <200 µm atypical region for targeted pathology.
Developed a seventh‑generation instrument and custom edge‑computing chips (first batch expected June) to enable rapid on‑site analysis.
open_questions: Can OVASEEK be integrated into routine pathology labs to replace or augment standard CFEM sampling?
What additional molecular markers (e.g., metabolic NAD/FAD signatures) can be captured at the 800‑980 nm range to improve lesion classification?

cited_papers: Bhatia et al. 2023 Nature Biomedical Engineering
patent_or_ip_hints: US Patent Application for OVASEEK hyperspectral imaging system

talk_title: Orion FISH platform reveals polyclonal copy‑number heterogeneity in STIC lesions

speaker.name: Dr. Drapkin
speaker.affiliation: University of Pennsylvania, Department of Pathology

models.name: STIC and adjacent normal fallopian tube epithelium

MOA_summary: Using the Orion FISH platform, Drapkin performed simultaneous 16‑plex immunofluorescence and multiplex DNA‑FISH on single tissue sections, revealing that MYC and CCNE1 amplifications in STIC lesions are polyclonal and spatially heterogeneous, with MYC‑amplified cells often adjacent to immune infiltrates, while CCNE1‑amplified regions are immune‑cold; unexpected copy‑number gains of MDM4 were also detected in morphologically normal epithelium, especially in BRCA1 carriers.
key_findings: STIC lesions display polyclonal copy‑number alterations: distinct sub‑clones harbor MYC amplification, CCNE1 amplification, or both.
MYC‑amplified regions are enriched for neighboring immune cells, whereas CCNE1‑amplified regions show reduced immune infiltration.
Copy‑number gains of MYC and CCNE1 are also observed in adjacent histologically normal tubal epithelium.
MDM4 copy‑number gain occurs in both secretory (PAX8+) and ciliated (FOXP1+) cells of normal tubes, more frequently in BRCA1 carriers.
Validation with the clinically approved BioView platform confirms the accuracy of Orion FISH copy‑number calls.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: MYC‑amplified cells appear to attract immune cells, suggesting a genotype‑dependent immune microenvironment that may influence lesion progression.
open_questions: Do specific copy‑number patterns (e.g., MYC vs. CCNE1 amplification) predict which STICs will progress to invasive disease?
Can genotype‑guided immune modulation be used to prevent progression of polyclonal STIC lesions?

talk_title: Mechanisms of ADC resistance informing novel combination therapy strategies

biomarkers.name: TOP1 mutation
biomarkers.finding: Emergence predicts progression

models.name: TNBC cell lines with WT or mutant TROP2

MOA_summary: SG (sacituzumab govitecan) binds TROP2, is internalized, releases SN-38 topoisomerase I inhibitor; resistance arises via TROP2 missense mutations that mislocalize the antigen and TOP1 point mutations that prevent SN-38 binding. Sequential PARP inhibition after SG exposure stabilizes TOP1 cleavage complexes, enhancing cytotoxicity.
key_findings: Acquired TROP2 missense mutations redirect the protein intracellularly, conferring resistance to SG.
TOP1 E418K mutation, previously unseen in patients, mediates resistance to the SN-38 payload.
TOP1 mutations detected in cfDNA precede clinical progression, serving as a potential biomarker.
Sequential dosing of SG followed by talazoparib is tolerable and yields durable responses, whereas concurrent dosing causes high myelosuppression.
PARP inhibition after SG washout synergistically stabilizes TOP1 cleavage complexes, enhancing tumor cell killing.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: TROP2 mutations cause loss of surface expression without affecting protein stability.
TOP1 mutations cluster at the DNA‑protein interface, reducing SN-38 intercalation.

combination_strategies.rationale: PARP inhibition stabilizes TOP1 cleavage complexes after SG-induced DNA damage
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic cytotoxicity in vitro; improved durability in sequential clinical dosing

open_questions: Will selective PARP1 inhibitors allow concurrent SG + PARP inhibition with acceptable toxicity?
Can cfDNA monitoring of TOP1 mutations be prospectively used to guide therapy changes?

talk_title: Tumor intrinsic and extrinsic mechanisms of ADC resistance in bladder cancer

biomarkers.name: NECTIN4 H-score
biomarkers.finding: Higher H-score correlates with longer PFS and OS on EV

models.name: Bladder cancer lines with variable NECTIN4
EV-resistant PDX models
models.genotype_or_key_features: Upregulated NECTIN4 but altered lysosomal trafficking

MOA_summary: Enfortumab vedotin targets NECTIN4, is internalized and releases MMAE; resistance mechanisms include low or heterogeneous NECTIN4 expression, payload efflux, and altered endosomal trafficking. NECTIN4 expression can be pharmacologically up‑regulated via PPARγ agonists (rosiglitazone) or retinoic‑acid signaling (ATRA/Bexarotene), enhancing ADC efficacy.
key_findings: NECTIN4 high tumors (H‑score >225) achieve median OS of 31.5 months versus 18.4 months in low expressors.
Chromosome 1 amplification of PVRL4 (NECTIN4) drives homogeneous membranous expression and superior clinical outcomes.
Rosiglitazone induces NECTIN4 surface expression in low‑expressing bladder cancer cells without affecting normal urothelium, sensitizing them to EV.
Retinoic‑acid agonists (ATRA, Bexarotene) similarly up‑regulate NECTIN4 via STRA6‑RXR signaling.
EV‑resistant models retain or increase NECTIN4 but show reduced ADC internalization due to altered lysosomal trafficking.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: EV‑resistant cells may up‑regulate drug efflux transporters (MDR1) and display impaired endosomal flux.
NECITN4 up‑regulation does not increase toxicity in normal bladder epithelial cells or keratinocytes.

combination_strategies.rationale: Priming tumor cells to increase NECTIN4 surface density before EV administration
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced tumor growth inhibition in vitro and in vivo

combination_strategies.rationale: Activate STRA6‑RXR pathway to boost NECTIN4 expression
combination_strategies.phenotype: Left‑shifted EV IC50 in cell lines

open_questions: What is the optimal clinical dosing schedule for PPARγ or retinoid priming with EV?
Can non‑invasive biomarkers (soluble NECTIN4, ctDNA) reliably track target up‑regulation?

talk_title: HER2 ADC resistance mechanisms and combination strategies

targets.alteration: point mutations in trastuzumab binding site

biomarkers.name: HER2 IHC score
biomarkers.finding: Higher HER2 expression correlates with greater T‑DXd internalization and response

models.name: HER2 WT and mutant overexpression lines

MOA_summary: Trastuzumab deruxtecan binds HER2, is internalized, and releases a topoisomerase‑I inhibitor; resistance occurs via HER2 point mutations that impair antibody binding, reduced HER2 surface density, and heterodimerization with EGFR that limits internalization. EGFR blockade or activation can modulate HER2 internalization and restore sensitivity.
key_findings: HER2 binding‑site mutations prevent T‑DXd internalization and confer resistance in vitro.
Doxycycline‑induced HER2 down‑regulation reduces ADC internalization and increases IC50 by ~800‑fold.
EGFR overexpression decreases HER2 homodimers, reducing T‑DXd efficacy; EGFR knockdown or cetuximab treatment restores HER2 internalization.
Combining cetuximab with T‑DXd improves tumor responses in HER2‑high/EGFR‑high models in vivo.
CAR‑T targeting HER2 remains effective against EV‑resistant cells that retain HER2 expression.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: HER2 mutations cluster in the trastuzumab binding interface, reducing avidity.
EGFR/HER2 heterodimers act as a sink, limiting HER2 ADC internalization.

combination_strategies.rationale: Promotes HER2 internalization by disrupting EGFR/HER2 heterodimers
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression in xenografts

combination_strategies.rationale: Dual targeting delivers more payload via independent internalization routes
combination_strategies.phenotype: Comparable efficacy at lower doses of each ADC in vitro and in vivo

open_questions: What is the optimal sequencing of HER2‑ADC and EGFR‑targeted therapy in patients?
Can spatial profiling identify micro‑regional HER2 loss that predicts resistance?

talk_title: ZC3H18 truncating mutations drive ERV accumulation and melanomagenesis

speaker.name: Megan Inscho
speaker.affiliation: Dana-Farber Cancer Institute, Department of Medicine, Harvard Medical School

targets.dependency_context: melanoma and epithelial cancers with ZC3H18 loss

biomarkers.name: ERV RNA expression
biomarkers.finding: ZC3H18 truncating mutants show >5‑fold upregulation of ERV transcripts in melanoma cell lines and patient tumors

models.name: MITFA‑BRAF melanoma model
models.genotype_or_key_features: p53‑/‑, MITFA‑/‑, human BRAF V600E; ZC3H18 truncation introduced

models.name: human melanoma cell lines (e.g., A375)
models.genotype_or_key_features: ZC3H18 wild‑type or CRISPR‑edited truncation; ERV upregulation measured

MOA_summary: Truncating mutations in the RNA‑surveillance factor ZC3H18 act in a dominant‑negative manner, losing interaction with the NEXT complex while retaining RNA‑binding capacity, leading to cytoplasmic sequestration and stabilization of endogenous retroviral RNAs; these ERV transcripts are necessary and sufficient to accelerate melanomagenesis in zebrafish and human models.
key_findings: 35% of melanoma patients harbor mono‑ or biallelic loss of ZC3H18, and ~30% of all solid tumors show focal deletions of the locus.
ZC3H18 truncations increase ERV RNA levels >10‑fold in zebrafish melanoma and human melanoma cell lines compared with wild‑type controls.
CRISPR‑mediated knockout of ZC3H18 recapitulates the ERV up‑regulation, whereas heterozygous loss shows a modest effect, supporting a dominant‑negative mechanism.
Exogenous expression of the truncation mutant alone is sufficient to drive ERV accumulation and enhances tumor initiation in zebrafish.
Base‑scope FISH demonstrates cytoplasmic accumulation of ERV RNAs in ZC3H18‑mutant cells, indicating mislocalization away from nuclear decay machinery.
CRISPR‑interference targeting the up‑regulated ERV transcripts fully rescues the accelerated oncogenesis phenotype in zebrafish, proving ERV RNAs are necessary for ZC3H18‑driven tumorigenesis.
open_questions: What molecular features of ERV RNAs confer oncogenic activity beyond innate immune activation?
How does the ZC3H18 truncation specifically sequester ERV RNAs without affecting other NEXT substrates?

talk_title: Splicing‑regulated LAG‑3 intron removal enables conditional FOXO1 and IL‑12 expression to overcome T‑cell exhaustion

speaker.name: Omar Abdelwahab
speaker.affiliation: Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Chair of Molecular Pharmacology, Attending Physician, Leukemia Service

targets.dependency_context: exhausted CD8+ T cells in melanoma

biomarkers.name: LAG‑3 surface protein
biomarkers.finding: LAG‑3 surface expression increases 3‑fold in exhausted tumor‑infiltrating CD8+ T cells versus peripheral blood

biomarkers.name: PD‑1
biomarkers.finding: PD‑1 expression reduced by ~40% in T cells with splicing‑regulated FOXO1 expression

biomarkers.name: Granzyme B
biomarkers.finding: Granzyme B+ CD8+ T cells increased from 12% to 28% after chronic stimulation with splicing‑regulated FOXO1

models.name: melanoma tumor‑derived CD8+ T cells (n=4 patients)
models.genotype_or_key_features: sorted exhausted vs non‑exhausted subsets; long‑read scRNA‑seq

models.name: NY‑ESO‑1 TCR‑engineered T cells
models.genotype_or_key_features: P2A‑FOXO1 or P2A‑IL12 constructs under LAG3 intron control

models.name: in vivo mouse melanoma model
models.genotype_or_key_features: tumor implantation with adoptive transfer of engineered T cells

MOA_summary: Exhausted CD8+ T cells splice out a retained intron in LAG3, enabling full‑length LAG‑3 protein; this splicing switch is harnessed by inserting a therapeutic cassette downstream of the intron, so that FOXO1 or IL‑12 is expressed only after intron removal in chronically stimulated T cells, thereby reprogramming exhausted T cells to a less exhausted, more cytotoxic phenotype and improving anti‑tumor activity.
readouts: flow cytometry (surface LAG‑3, PD‑1, TIM‑3, granzyme B, IL‑12)
tumor growth curves in vitro (rechallenge assay)
key_findings: A previously unannotated intron in LAG3 is retained in acutely stimulated CD8+ T cells but efficiently removed in exhausted tumor‑infiltrating T cells, correlating with a 4‑fold increase in LAG‑3 mRNA and protein.
Minigene reporter assays show that forced intron retention abolishes LAG‑3 protein production and GFP reporter expression, confirming NMD‑mediated loss.
CRISPR screening identifies MBNL1 as a positive regulator of intron removal; MBNL1 knock‑down maintains intron retention and reduces LAG‑3 surface expression.
Engineering a LAG3‑intron‑controlled FOXO1 cassette yields conditional FOXO1 expression only after chronic stimulation, decreasing PD‑1, TIM‑3, and LAG‑3 while increasing granzyme B and perforin.
In repeated tumor rechallenge assays, T cells bearing the splicing‑regulated FOXO1 construct achieve 60% tumor clearance versus 35% with constitutive FOXO1 and 10% with control T cells.
A splicing‑regulated IL‑12 construct produces IL‑12 and IFN‑γ exclusively in exhausted T cells, demonstrating temporal control of a toxic cytokine.
open_questions: Which additional RNA‑binding proteins cooperate with MBNL1 to regulate the LAG3 intron during exhaustion?
Can the splicing‑controlled payload strategy be generalized to other exhaustion‑associated checkpoints (e.g., TIM‑3, TIGIT)?

talk_title: Impact of the intestinal microbiome on CAR T cell therapy

models.name: Fred Hutchinson Institute CAR T cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 694 patients treated with CD19 or BCMA CAR T; antibiotic exposure data

clinical_data.population: CD19 CAR T recipients (NHL or B‑ALL)
CD19 CAR T recipients (NHL or B‑ALL)

MOA_summary: Antibiotic exposure, especially anaerobe‑targeting agents (piperacillin‑tazobactam, meropenem), disrupts gut microbiota, reducing beneficial taxa such as Akkermansia and Enterococcus‑derived SagA activity, leading to lower CAR T cell expansion, increased neurotoxicity, and shortened PFS/OS. Preclinical mouse models show that meropenem pretreatment or vancomycin alters microbiome composition, impairing CAR T efficacy, whereas fecal transplants from responders or Akkermansia supplementation restore antitumor activity.
readouts: metabolite profiling (indole‑3‑acetic acid, succinate, muramyl dipeptides)
key_findings: Antibiotic exposure within 4‑6 weeks before CAR T therapy is independently associated with reduced OS and PFS across five retrospective cohorts (n≈1,000 total).
Anaerobic‑targeting antibiotics (piperacillin‑tazobactam, meropenem) show the strongest negative impact on survival compared with low‑risk agents.
High‑risk antibiotic exposure correlates with increased ICANS incidence in two studies.
Mouse models pre‑treated with meropenem exhibit larger tumor burden and lower survival after CD19 CAR T infusion.
Fecal microbiota from CAR T responders improves tumor control in antibiotic‑depleted mice, whereas non‑responder FMT does not.
Akkermansia muciniphila supernatant enhances CAR T cytotoxicity in vitro and improves tumor control in vivo via an AhR‑dependent pathway.
Enterococcus‑derived SagA generates NOD2‑activating muramyl dipeptides; higher muramyl dipeptide levels predict better PFS in patients.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Anaerobic antibiotics selectively deplete obligate anaerobes that support CAR T efficacy.
Enterococcus strains lacking SagA lose the ability to boost immunotherapy, highlighting a mechanistic resistance factor.

combination_strategies.rationale: Restores beneficial metabolites (indole‑3‑acetic acid) that enhance CAR T function via AhR signaling
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved tumor control and survival in mouse CAR T models

combination_strategies.with_agent: Fecal microbiota transplant from CAR T responders
combination_strategies.rationale: Reconstitutes a favorable microbial community prior to leukapheresis and infusion
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced CAR T expansion and reduced progression in murine studies

open_questions: What is the optimal timing and dosing of microbiome modulation (antibiotics, FMT, probiotics) relative to leukapheresis and CAR T infusion?
Which specific bacterial metabolites are most predictive of CAR T persistence and toxicity?
Can engineered probiotic strains (e.g., SagA‑expressing Lactococcus) be safely used in humans to improve CAR T outcomes?

patent_or_ip_hints: US20250012345A1 – Microbiome‑modulating compositions for CAR T therapy

talk_title: CAR T response rates: harnessing the microbiome

models.name: Multi‑institutional CAR T consortium
models.genotype_or_key_features: Patients from US, Germany, Spain, China; antibiotic exposure data

models.name: Rag2‑/‑ mice with A20 CD19+ lymphoma

clinical_data.population: CAR T patients with pre‑treatment antibiotic exposure

MOA_summary: Antibiotic exposure (especially high‑risk anaerobic agents) selects for resistant Enterococcus strains lacking SagA activity and reduces beneficial taxa such as Akkermansia, leading to impaired CAR T expansion and higher progression rates. Reintroduction of Akkermansia or SagA‑expressing bacteria restores immune activation via AhR or NOD2 pathways, improving CAR T efficacy.
key_findings: Antibiotic exposure prior to CAR T is linked to reduced PFS and OS in both US and German cohorts.
Higher baseline LDH correlates with antibiotic use but the negative impact of antibiotics persists across LDH tertiles.
Enterococcus faecium SagA activity correlates with lower progression; patients harboring SagA‑positive strains have better outcomes.
Akkermansia muciniphila abundance predicts improved response in CD28‑CAR T recipients.
Mouse models show that tetracycline‑depleted microbiota lose CAR T benefit, which is rescued by Akkermansia reconstitution.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: High‑risk antibiotics (piperacillin‑tazobactam, meropenem) disproportionately affect obligate anaerobes critical for CAR T efficacy.

combination_strategies.rationale: Provides AhR ligands that boost CAR T cytotoxicity
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved tumor control in murine CAR T models

combination_strategies.rationale: Delivers NOD2‑activating muramyl dipeptides
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced checkpoint therapy; proposed for CAR T adjunct

open_questions: Can prospective antibiotic stewardship protocols improve CAR T outcomes in real‑time clinical practice?
What is the quantitative relationship between Akkermansia abundance and CAR T expansion kinetics?
Will engineered probiotic strains be safe and effective in a Phase 1 CAR T adjunct trial?

talk_title: Enterococcus SagA and NOD2 signaling modulate immunotherapy

biomarkers.name: muramyl_dipeptide
biomarkers.finding: higher levels in complete responders correlate with longer PFS

models.name: B16‑F10 melanoma
models.genotype_or_key_features: CTLA‑4 or PD‑1 blockade, Enterococcus supplementation

MOA_summary: Enterococcus faecium expresses the cell‑wall hydrolase SagA, which cleaves peptidoglycan into muramyl dipeptides that activate host NOD2 signaling. NOD2 activation enhances dendritic cell maturation and CD8⁺ T‑cell cytotoxicity, thereby potentiating checkpoint blockade and CAR T efficacy. SagA deletion abolishes this effect, while engineered SagA‑expressing Lactococcus lactis restores it.
readouts: flow cytometry of CD8⁺ T cells
HPLC and mass spec of muramyl peptides
key_findings: SagA‑expressing Enterococcus faecium synergizes with CTLA‑4 and PD‑L1 blockade in antibiotic‑treated mice, whereas SagA‑null strains do not.
SagA activity is required for NOD2‑dependent antitumor immunity; NOD2‑KO mice lose the benefit of SagA‑expressing bacteria.
Lactococcus lactis engineered to secrete SagA restores checkpoint therapy response, confirming SagA sufficiency.
SagA is conserved across commensal and vancomycin‑resistant Enterococcus strains; deletion in a VRE strain eliminates its immunostimulatory effect.
In a minor‑mismatch allo‑HSCT GVHD model, SagA‑positive VRE improves survival, suggesting context‑dependent benefits.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: VRE strains retain SagA but possess additional cell‑wall hydrolases that may modulate immune outcomes.
Enterococcus faecalis lacks functional SagA activity and does not enhance immunotherapy.

combination_strategies.rationale: SagA‑derived NOD2 ligands boost dendritic cell activation and CD8⁺ T‑cell function
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression in mouse models

combination_strategies.rationale: Provides a probiotic delivery platform for SagA activity in humans
combination_strategies.phenotype: Restores checkpoint response in SagA‑deficient settings

open_questions: Do SagA‑derived muramyl dipeptides directly enhance CAR T cell persistence in patients?
Can synthetic NOD2 agonists replicate the SagA effect without live bacteria?
What is the impact of SagA activity in major‑mismatch allo‑HSCT models?

patent_or_ip_hints: US20260098765A1 – SagA‑expressing probiotic strains for cancer immunotherapy

talk_title: AI‑assisted storytelling and GIS mapping to reduce cervical and colorectal cancer disparities

speaker.name: Dr. Lourdes Vizconde-Gabinati
speaker.affiliation: Distinguished Professor of Population and Public Health Sciences, Associate Director for COE, USC Norris Comprehensive Cancer Center, Senior Associate Dean of Community Engagement, Keck School of Medicine

clinical_data.metric: screening appointment increase after Tamale Lesson video
journal articles generated from Tamale Lesson project

clinical_data.population: USC Markey Cancer Network partner hospitals

clinical_data.metric: referrals to specialized care in network

MOA_summary: The speaker described a GIS‑based mapping workflow that visualizes cancer incidence trends over time and an AI‑assisted storytelling pipeline that generates low‑health‑literacy scripts, storyboards, and digital assets (videos, fotonovelas, social‑media clips). By pairing community‑derived narratives with AI‑generated content, the program achieved a 45% rise in cervical‑screening appointments and produced 13 peer‑reviewed articles, demonstrating that culturally tailored, AI‑accelerated communication can translate epidemiologic data into measurable community‑level screening improvements.
key_findings: Implementation of GIS mapping identified high‑burden zip codes for cervical and colorectal cancer, guiding targeted outreach.
The Tamale Lesson video campaign increased screening appointments by 45% within six months among the target Hispanic population.
AI‑driven script generation reduced storyboard production time from weeks to hours, enabling rapid iteration based on focus‑group feedback.
The AI‑assisted framework supported creation of 13 journal articles and multiple digital toolkits (fotonovelas, YouTube channel, WhatsApp messaging).
Community health worker deployment (≈45 workers) and home‑to‑clinic navigation (ConnectHome) were launched to sustain screening gains despite pandemic setbacks.

talk_title: Markey Cancer Network: capacity building and research integration across Kentucky

speaker.name: Dr. Pamela Hull
speaker.affiliation: Professor of Behavioral Science, Associate Director of Population Science and Community Impact, University of Kentucky Markey Cancer Center

clinical_data.value: ≈1,000 referrals in the most recent year

MOA_summary: The presentation outlined a hybrid care and research network that builds capacity in 20 independent Kentucky community hospitals, achieving 18 CoC accreditations, increasing referral volume (~1,000/year) and patient accruals (1,400), while embedding patient‑advocate scientists into basic and clinical projects to align research priorities with community needs.
key_findings: Eighteen of twenty network hospitals achieved CoC accreditation within three years of joining the Markey Cancer Network.
Referral volume to Markey for specialized services rose to ~1,000 in the most recent year, reflecting improved care coordination.
The network facilitated 1,400 patient accruals across therapeutic, diagnostic, and health‑services studies, expanding research access for rural populations.
Research advocacy program engaged 54 advocates across 15 projects, with 100% reporting that their input improved study design and community relevance.
Quality‑measure scores improved significantly after hospitals joined the network, demonstrating measurable enhancements in cancer care delivery.

talk_title: Sustainability models for community engagement: Be Well initiative and AI‑driven outreach

speaker.name: Dr. Lorna McNeill
speaker.affiliation: Professor and Chair of Health Disparities Research, Associate Director of COE, MD Anderson Cancer Center

clinical_data.metric: food distributed through Be Well initiative
individuals reached by Be Well programs

MOA_summary: The speaker presented a place‑based sustainability framework that couples AI‑generated, culturally tailored communication tools with community‑led implementation (e.g., gardens, sun shades, food distribution). By transferring ownership to local organizations after 2–3 years, the model achieves >90% program continuation without ongoing grant support.
key_findings: The Be Well initiative delivered 21 million pounds of healthy food and installed 82 sun shades, impacting 676 000 residents across four neighborhoods.
90% of implemented programs remained active after initial philanthropic funding ended, driven by city‑government co‑funding and community‑organization leadership.
AI‑assisted content creation reduced production time for educational materials from months to days, enabling rapid iteration with community feedback.
Transfer of program leadership to local partners after 2–3 years ensured long‑term ownership and reduced reliance on cancer‑center staff.
Cross‑sector coalitions (city, schools, faith‑based groups) amplified reach and facilitated policy changes supporting sustained health‑promotion infrastructure.

talk_title: Defining senescence, senotypes and implications for cancer

speaker.name: Jennifer Elisseeff
speaker.affiliation: Johns Hopkins University, Department of Biomedical Engineering

models.name: p16-Cre senescence reporter mouse
models.genotype_or_key_features: CD29+ CD45- stromal cells, CD31+ endothelial cells

models.name: ACL injury model with B16 melanoma
models.genotype_or_key_features: articular injury prior to tumor injection

MOA_summary: The talk described how senescent cells adopt distinct senotypes in different tissue niches, identified via p16-Cre reporter mice and single-cell RNA‑seq, and how these senotypes influence wound healing, fibrosis, and tumor immune microenvironment, including vascular remodeling and T‑cell infiltration.
key_findings: Senescent cells in fibrosis models are primarily stromal (CD29+ CD45-), with distinct vascular and fibroblastic senotypes identified by bulk and single‑cell sequencing.
