Arthritic (ACL) injury before B16 tumor implantation reduces tumor growth and markedly increases response to checkpoint blockade, associated with increased CD8+ T‑cell infiltration and enhanced vascular P‑selectin expression.

Genetic ablation of p16‑positive senescent cells (Incakat model) abolishes the vascular and T‑cell infiltration phenotype and eliminates the immunotherapy benefit, indicating senescent cells are necessary for the observed anti‑tumor effect.
open_questions: What specific SASP components from senescent pericytes and endothelial cells drive the enhanced T‑cell trafficking?
How do different senotypes temporally evolve during wound healing versus chronic disease?

talk_title: Redox‑based pan‑senolytic Senlytic and its preclinical cancer prevention potential

speaker.name: Clemens Schmidt
speaker.affiliation: Charité – Universitätsmedizin Berlin, Max Delbrück Center for Molecular Medicine

targets.dependency_context: senescent cells with high NADH/low GSH

models.name: p53‑proficient lymphoma TIS model
models.genotype_or_key_features: p53 WT lymphoma cells treated with chemotherapy

clinical_data.value: median survival increased from 30 to 45 days
clinical_data.population: p53‑proficient lymphoma‑bearing mice receiving chemotherapy + Senlytic

MOA_summary: Senlytic is a redox‑activated pro‑drug that exploits the elevated NADH/low GSH ratio and labile iron in senescent cells to generate hydroxyl radicals via Fenton chemistry, inducing ferroptosis and apoptosis selectively in p53‑competent senescent cells across multiple senescence inducers.
key_findings: Senlytic shows >10‑fold senolytic index over proliferating cells across replicative, oncogene‑induced, therapy‑induced, and virus‑induced senescence models.
In vivo, Senlytic clears p16‑positive senescent cells in aged mouse lungs, reducing collagen cuff formation without measurable toxicity.
Senlytic administered after chemotherapy eliminates therapy‑induced senescent lymphoma cells, extending mouse survival, but is ineffective in p53‑deficient tumors.
In a BRAF‑nevus mouse model, Senlytic reduces nevi and delays melanoma onset, demonstrating cancer‑preventive potential by targeting OIS lesions.
Senlytic prevents NMU‑induced RAS‑driven T‑cell lymphomagenesis in wild‑type mice, indicating that removal of incipient senescent lesions blocks tumor formation.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Senlytic requires functional p53 to induce senescence; p53‑null cells are resistant.
Senolytic activity is abolished by iron chelation (desferrioxamine) or overexpression of catalase, confirming dependence on iron‑mediated ROS.

combination_strategies.rationale: Chemotherapy induces senescence; Senlytic then eliminates senescent tumor cells to prevent relapse.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Extended survival and reduced tumor regrowth in lymphoma model

combination_strategies.rationale: BRAF‑driven nevi become senescent; Senlytic clears these lesions to prevent progression to melanoma.
combination_strategies.phenotype: 80% reduction in pigmented lesions, delayed melanoma onset

open_questions: What is the optimal dosing interval for Senlytic in humans to balance clearance of detrimental senescent cells while preserving beneficial acute senescence?
Can Senlytic be combined with immune checkpoint blockade to enhance anti‑tumor immunity in senescence‑rich tumors?

talk_title: Foundation models in pathology: data requirements, performance and robustness

speaker.name: Jeroen van der Laak
speaker.affiliation: Radboud University Medical Center, Nijmegen, Netherlands, Department of Computational Pathology

biomarkers.name: KRAS mutation
biomarkers.finding: F1 score 0.85 for KRAS mutation prediction from H&E slides

MOA_summary: Foundation models are trained with self‑supervised learning on large, diverse pathology image collections to produce high‑dimensional embeddings that capture generic visual features; these embeddings enable few‑shot downstream tasks such as cancer grading, mutation prediction, and survival modeling with reduced data requirements.
key_findings: Training on 12,000 prostate biopsies and 5,600 pathologist reviews yielded a quadratic weighted Kappa of up to 0.93, matching expert performance.
Foundation models achieved comparable performance with as little as 1% of the training data, whereas task‑specific models required >30% of data to reach similar accuracy.
Generalizability of foundation models was superior on out‑of‑distribution cohorts but depended on the diversity of the pre‑training data.
Foundation models consumed 35 times more energy than task‑specific models, highlighting a trade‑off between data efficiency and computational cost.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Performance degrades on demographic subgroups under‑represented in the training set (e.g., Asian patients in breast cancer subtype classification).
Bias in training data leads to reduced accuracy on minority groups, but foundation models like CTranspath mitigate this bias.

combination_strategies.rationale: Combining image embeddings with textual pathology reports provides contextual information that improves robustness and performance.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved classification accuracy and robustness with multimodal embeddings versus image‑only models.

open_questions: How can we further reduce the energy footprint of large foundation models without sacrificing data efficiency?
What is the optimal balance between model size and data diversity for maximal generalizability?

talk_title: Benchmarking foundation models for survival prediction and the SURFER prognostic model

speaker.name: Jana Lipkova
speaker.affiliation: University of California, Irvine, Departments of Pathology and Biomedical Engineering

biomarkers.name: KRAS mutation
biomarkers.finding: F1 score 0.85 for KRAS mutation prediction

models.name: TCGA and NIH‑PLCO cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: ≈15,000 whole slide images across 13 cancer types

models.name: PATOS benchmark dataset
models.genotype_or_key_features: 13 cancer types, internal and external validation splits

MOA_summary: SURFER extends a vision‑language foundation model (Titan) with SoftCLIP, a weighted contrastive loss that incorporates patient survival times into the embedding space, enabling the model to learn prognostic features directly from whole‑slide images and associated outcome text.
key_findings: Patch‑level foundation models outperform slide‑level models for survival prediction across 13 cancer types.
Multimodal models (image + text) do not significantly improve survival prediction over unimodal models when prognostic information is absent from the text.
The SURFER model, trained with SoftCLIP on ~15,000 slides, yields a clear gradient of risk groups in UMAP space and improves downstream survival C‑index compared with the baseline Titan model.
Pan‑cancer training slightly outperforms cancer‑specific models, demonstrating feasibility of a single prognostic model for diverse tumor types.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Interpretability heatmaps differ markedly between foundation models, indicating model‑specific attention patterns that may affect biomarker discovery.

combination_strategies.rationale: Embedding survival information guides the contrastive learning to cluster patients with similar outcomes.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved risk stratification and higher C‑index in downstream survival models.

open_questions: Can additional modalities such as treatment response or molecular subtypes further enhance prognostic foundation models?
What is the optimal balance between patch‑level and slide‑level representations for different clinical endpoints?

talk_title: Clinical applications of pathology foundation models and future AI agents

speaker.name: Jakob Kater
speaker.affiliation: Technical University of Dresden, Germany, Professor of Clinical Artificial Intelligence; Senior Physician, Medical Oncology, University Hospital Dresden

biomarkers.name: EGFR mutation
biomarkers.finding: Reduced rapid molecular testing by 43% while maintaining clinical performance in a prospective lung cancer screening study

models.name: EGFR screening trial (Memorial Sloan Kettering)
models.genotype_or_key_features: Prospective evaluation of AI‑based EGFR prediction from pathology slides

clinical_data.population: lung cancer patients undergoing EGFR screening

MOA_summary: Foundation models generate generic image embeddings that can be fine‑tuned for specific clinical tasks; when combined with large language model agents, they enable multimodal reasoning across pathology, radiology, genomics and clinical records to support tumor board decision‑making and trial matching.
key_findings: AI models trained on H&E slides can predict EGFR mutation status, allowing a 43% reduction in rapid molecular testing without loss of diagnostic accuracy.
Foundation models accelerate development of downstream tasks (e.g., molecular alteration inference, survival prediction) by requiring far fewer labeled examples.
Multimodal agents that integrate foundation model embeddings with language models can replicate tumor board reasoning and achieve performance comparable to human oncologists in benchmark studies.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Current AI agents lack robust regulatory guardrails and human‑oversight mechanisms, posing safety and compliance challenges.

combination_strategies.rationale: Leverage LLM reasoning and tool‑use capabilities to synthesize pathology, radiology, genomics and clinical data for treatment recommendations.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Agent performance comparable to human oncologists in simulated tumor board cases.

open_questions: How can regulatory frameworks be adapted to certify autonomous AI agents for clinical decision support?
What infrastructure investments are required to achieve widespread digital pathology adoption necessary for foundation model deployment?

talk_title: New insights into SHP2 signaling to RAS and its catalytic domain functions

speaker.name: Ben
speaker.affiliation: University of Connecticut (collaborations with Wolfgang Petty, Rebecca Page, Jeroen den Hertog)

targets.dependency_context: EGF, FGF, PDGF signaling in fibroblasts and HEK cells

models.name: shp2 knockout
models.genotype_or_key_features: cardiac edema, rescued by C/D mutant

MOA_summary: Catalytic cysteine mutations in SHP2 (C459D) retain signaling to RAS/ERK despite being phosphatase-dead, acting as an adapter that directly binds the DH/PH domain of SOS1 to promote sustained Ras activation, while phosphatase activity serves as a negative feedback.
readouts: Western blot for ERK and AKT phosphorylation
key_findings: RMZ-4550 allosteric SHP2 inhibitor blocks EGF-induced RAS activation in 3T3 cells, rescued by SHP2 TMQL mutant expression.
Catalytic cysteine-to-aspartate (C459D) mutant restores RAS activation and sustains ERK/AKT signaling despite being >20,000‑fold phosphatase dead.
C459D mice are viable with mild phenotype, whereas C459E is embryonic lethal, indicating functional divergence of catalytic mutants.
Zebrafish shp2 C/D mutant rescues cardiac defects, confirming functional signaling across species.
Structural studies reveal direct binding of SHP2 catalytic domain to SOS1 DH/PH domain; mutations disrupting this interface abrogate signaling while preserving phosphatase activity.
Super‑resolution microscopy shows increased SOS1 membrane clustering in C/D mutant cells, correlating with enhanced Ras activation.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Phosphatase activity of SHP2 is not required for Ras/ERK activation, overturning 20‑year dogma.
Cysteine mutants display selective signaling for PDGF/FGF but not EGF.

combination_strategies.rationale: Block SHP2 catalytic activity while preserving adapter function to dissect signaling.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Complete inhibition of RAS activation in wild‑type cells; mutant SHP2 rescues signaling.

combination_strategies.rationale: Disrupt SHP2‑SOS1 adapter interaction identified as critical for signaling.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Predicted loss of Ras activation despite SHP2 presence.

open_questions: What is the identity of the physiological substrate(s) dephosphorylated by SHP2 that mediate its negative feedback role?
How does SHP2 catalytic activity regulate checkpoint receptor signaling in T cells?

talk_title: Omega‑6 fatty acids activate mTORC1 via FABP5 and promote aggressive triple‑negative breast cancer

targets.dependency_context: high in TNBC, binds omega‑6 to recruit Raptor

biomarkers.name: FABP5 serum level
biomarkers.finding: Elevated in TNBC patients and in mice fed omega‑6 diet

biomarkers.name: p‑S6 (mTOR activity)
biomarkers.finding: High in omega‑6‑fed xenografts, low in omega‑3‑fed controls

models.name: diet‑only (no tumor)
models.genotype_or_key_features: omega‑6 diet elevates serum FABP5, IL‑6, TOR signaling in liver/pancreas

clinical_data.value: significantly faster in omega‑6 diet vs omega‑3
elevated in omega‑6 fed mice and TNBC patients

MOA_summary: Omega‑6 fatty acids bind FABP5, inducing a conformational change that enables FABP5 to dock Raptor and assemble active mTORC1, driving proliferation of FABP5‑high triple‑negative breast cancer; omega‑3 promotes FABP5 degradation and induces microbiome‑derived lithocholic acid, which activates AMPK and suppresses mTOR signaling.
key_findings: FABP5 directly binds omega‑6 fatty acids, exposing a docking site for Raptor and activating mTORC1 in TNBC cells.
FABP5 knockdown abolishes omega‑6‑induced mTOR activation and proliferation in TNBC cell lines.
TNBC PDX tumors and patient serum show elevated FABP5; omega‑6‑fed mice exhibit higher tumor FABP5 and mTOR signaling than omega‑3‑fed controls.
Visceral adipocyte‑derived extracellular vesicle FABP5 is taken up via macropinocytosis, sensitizing otherwise FABP5‑low cells to omega‑6‑driven mTOR activation.
Omega‑3 fatty acids accelerate FABP5 proteasomal degradation (half‑life 2‑4 h vs 24 h with omega‑6).
Omega‑3‑rich diet increases microbiome‑derived lithocholic acid, which activates AMPK, suppresses mTORC1, and reduces tumor growth.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Effect is selective for FABP5‑high TNBC; HR+ tumors are resistant unless FABP5 is overexpressed.
Obesity‑derived FABP5 may broaden susceptibility to omega‑6.

combination_strategies.rationale: Block the adaptor function to prevent mTORC1 activation.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced tumor growth in omega‑6 diet mice.

combination_strategies.rationale: Mimic lithocholic acid‑mediated mTOR suppression.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic reduction of TOR signaling in omega‑3 context.

open_questions: What is the precise source of circulating FABP5 in tumor‑free mice on omega‑6 diet?
Can individual omega‑6 fatty acids (linoleic vs arachidonic) differentially drive FABP5‑Raptor interaction?

talk_title: Structural and regulatory mechanisms of BRAF and CRAF activation and dimerization

targets.dependency_context: requires dimerization with CRAF for class II/III mutants
forms BC heterodimers upon RAS activation

MOA_summary: BRAF and CRAF are held in an auto‑inhibited state by 14‑3‑3 and the cysteine‑rich domain; oncogenic mutations or RAS‑GTP binding destabilize this interface, allowing the CRD to release, BC heterodimer formation, and activation of MEK/ERK. Distinct N‑terminal acidic regions confer KRAS selectivity for BRAF, while CRAF relies more on 14‑3‑3 interactions.
key_findings: BRAF possesses a constitutive acidic N‑terminal segment that drives selective binding to KRAS polybasic region.
Disruption of the BC dimer interface abolishes EGF‑stimulated kinase activity of both BRAF and CRAF.
Class II/III BRAF mutants require BC heterodimerization for MAPK signaling, explaining paradoxical activation by first‑generation RAF inhibitors.
Cryo‑EM of auto‑inhibited BRAF shows the cysteine‑rich domain cradled by 14‑3‑3, occluding the dimer interface; mutations in this region release the inhibition.
In vitro KRAS‑GTP nanodisc assays demonstrate concentration‑dependent activation of BRAF that is dimer‑dependent and enhanced by membrane context.
C‑RAF auto‑inhibited complexes lack CRD‑14‑3‑3 contacts seen in BRAF, indicating distinct regulatory mechanisms.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: BC dimerization underlies paradoxical MAPK activation and resistance to early RAF inhibitors.
Acidic N‑terminal segment of BRAF confers KRAS selectivity, not seen in CRAF.

combination_strategies.rationale: Prevent BC dimerization and downstream MEK activation.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Suppressed KRAS‑dependent BRAF activation in vitro.

combination_strategies.rationale: Block KRAS binding to BRAF RBD, maintaining auto‑inhibited state.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced BRAF activation in nanodisc assays.

open_questions: How do specific disease‑associated CRD mutations in CRAF alter 14‑3‑3 dependence?
Can small molecules be designed to stabilize the auto‑inhibited CRD‑14‑3‑3 interface?

cited_papers: Eck et al. Cryo‑EM of BRAF

talk_title: Autocrine LIF signaling drives myeloid immunosuppression and plasticity in ALK‑mutant lung adenocarcinoma

biomarkers.name: phospho‑STAT3 (Y705)
biomarkers.finding: Elevated in ALK‑mutant tumors and human LUAD samples with LKB1 loss

biomarkers.name: ARG1 expression in interstitial macrophages
biomarkers.finding: High in LIF‑driven tumors; reduced after LIF deletion or antibody treatment

models.name: TCGA LUAD
models.genotype_or_key_features: ALK‑mutant, LKB1 loss, high LIF expression

models.name: KP GEM (KRAS G12D + TP53 loss + Cas9)
LIF or LIFR knockout KP tumors

clinical_data.value: significant reduction with LIF or LIFR deletion and with anti‑LIF antibody

MOA_summary: Loss of LKB1 in ALK‑mutant lung adenocarcinoma upregulates leukemia inhibitory factor (LIF), which acts in an autocrine manner via LIFR/STAT3 to maintain dedifferentiated, SOX17‑high tumor cell states that secrete chemokines (CCL2, CXCL1/5/7) recruiting immunosuppressive ARG1⁺ interstitial macrophages and cyclic‑high neutrophils, thereby suppressing T‑cell function; blockade of LIF signaling genetically or with an anti‑LIF antibody reverses myeloid suppression and restores anti‑tumor immunity.
readouts: flow cytometry for cytokine‑producing T cells
key_findings: LIF is transcriptionally up‑regulated in ALK‑mutant/LKB1‑deficient tumors and correlates with phospho‑STAT3 positivity in mouse and human samples.
Genetic deletion of LIF or LIFR markedly reduces tumor burden and diminishes immunosuppressive neutrophil (cyclic‑high) and ARG1⁺ interstitial macrophage populations.
T‑cell functional cytokine production (IFN‑γ, TNF‑α) is restored in LIFR‑deleted tumors; T‑cell depletion abrogates the tumor‑growth advantage conferred by LIF signaling.
LIF‑driven tumors harbor distinct dedifferentiated tumor cell clusters (high SOX17, PROM1/CD133) that are depleted upon LIF blockade.
Anti‑LIF antibody treatment recapitulates genetic deletion effects, reducing tumor size and myeloid immunosuppression in established tumors.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: LIF signaling specifically drives immunosuppression in LKB1‑deficient ALK tumors; other KRAS‑mutant models show minimal LIF expression.

combination_strategies.rationale: LIF blockade restores T‑cell function, potentially synergizing with checkpoint blockade.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Predicted enhanced anti‑tumor immunity.

combination_strategies.rationale: Downstream of LIFR, may further suppress immunosuppressive signaling.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Additive reduction of ARG1⁺ macrophages.

open_questions: What transcriptional mechanisms link LKB1 loss to LIF up‑regulation (role of CRTC2/CREB vs AMPK)?
Can LIF inhibition overcome resistance to existing KRAS/ALK targeted therapies?

cited_papers: AstraZeneca anti‑LIF antibody trial in NSCLC

talk_title: Molecular and cellular alterations associated with malignant progression in the pancreas

speaker.name: Laura Wood
speaker.affiliation: University of Pennsylvania, Department of Pathology

models.name: IPMN and PanIN lesions from surgical resections
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS mutant, SMAD4 loss in cancer

MOA_summary: The work maps genomic and microenvironmental alterations across pancreatic precancerous lesions (IPMN, PanIN) showing that KRAS mutations are present early, while progression to invasive cancer is sometimes driven by additional alterations such as SMAD4 loss and distinct fibroblast and immune cell niche remodeling.
key_findings: IPMNs originate polyclonally with multiple KRAS or VGR2 clones, but high-grade IPMNs become clonal.
In ~25% of IPMN‑cancer pairs, cancer‑specific SMAD4 or TGF‑betaRII mutations are observed, suggesting a genetic driver of progression.
Imaging mass cytometry reveals fibroblast populations that are expanded in invasive cancer and reduced in IPMN, indicating niche-specific fibroblast remodeling.
IPMN‑associated fibroblast niches are enriched for granzyme‑positive cytotoxic T cells, whereas cancer‑associated niches have fewer T cells and altered fibroblast subsets.
open_questions: What microenvironmental features, beyond SMAD4 loss, drive malignant progression in lesions lacking obvious genetic drivers?

talk_title: Evolution of pancreatic cancer microenvironment focusing on PanIN lesions

speaker.name: Marina Pasca di Mangliano
speaker.affiliation: University of Michigan, Department of Pathology

biomarkers.name: phospho-STAT3
biomarkers.finding: Enriched in poorly differentiated tumor domains and absent in well‑differentiated PanIN domains

models.name: 160 organ donor pancreata (Gift of Life Michigan)
models.genotype_or_key_features: PanIN lesions present in >50% of donors, KRAS hotspot mutations in most PanINs

MOA_summary: Spatial transcriptomics and single‑cell integration of donor pancreas samples reveal distinct epithelial and stromal domains in PanIN lesions, with IL6/JAK‑STAT3 signaling up‑regulated in poorly differentiated tumor areas and fibroblast subsets (ACTA2+, LRC15+, LEF1+) uniquely enriched around tumor, while plasma cells are enriched near PanIN and macrophages around tumor.
key_findings: PanIN lesions are present in >50% of organ donors, including young individuals, indicating high prevalence of silent precancers.
Spatial domains containing neoplastic epithelium are transcriptionally distinct from normal ducts and show progressive changes toward tumor.
IL6/JAK‑STAT3 signaling is highly enriched in poorly differentiated tumor domains compared with PanIN.
Plasma cells are preferentially localized to PanIN neighborhoods, whereas macrophages are enriched around tumor structures.
Two tumor‑specific fibroblast populations are identified: ACTA2+/LRC15+ CAFs (fibro‑2) and LEF1+ fibroblasts (fibro‑3), absent in normal pancreas.
open_questions: Do the identified fibroblast subsets drive progression or reflect a response to epithelial transformation?
Can plasma‑cell enrichment around PanIN be leveraged as a biomarker of low‑risk lesions?

talk_title: Stromal targeting strategies for interception of pancreatic cancer

speaker.name: Hatti (Fox Chase Cancer Center)
speaker.affiliation: Fox Chase Cancer Center, Department of Oncology

models.name: primary fibroblast cultures from resected pancreatic tissue
orthotopic co‑injection of human precancerous epithelial cells with CAFs
models.genotype_or_key_features: human ATM or PALB2‑mutant epithelial cells + tumor‑promoting fibroblasts

clinical_data.value: reduced in PLDR‑treated patients vs continuous radiation
clinical_data.population: pancreatic cancer patients undergoing neoadjuvant therapy

MOA_summary: The lab isolates patient‑derived fibroblasts, characterizes a composite 'host factor' metric of stromal protumorigenicity, and screens antibodies (e.g., anti‑NETRIN G1) and peptide inhibitors to normalize fibroblast function, testing them in vitro, in orthotopic co‑injection models, and in a pulse‑dose radiation clinical trial (PLDR) aimed at reducing stromal activation.
key_findings: A quantitative 'host factor' integrates multiple stromal biomarkers to distinguish protumorigenic from tumor‑suppressive fibroblast states.
Anti‑NETRIN G1 antibody reduces protumorigenic fibroblast traits in vitro and modestly suppresses lesion formation in orthotopic co‑injection models.
Pulse‑dose low‑dose-rate (PLDR) radiation normalizes stromal host factor scores in patient samples compared with standard continuous radiation.
Co‑injection of human precancerous epithelial cells with tumor‑promoting CAFs yields early PanIN‑like lesions, supporting a stromal requirement for initiation.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Peptide inhibitors showed immunogenicity in control arms, confounding efficacy assessment.

combination_strategies.rationale: Pulse radiation may avoid stromal activation while enhancing tumor control
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced host factor score and lower protumorigenic fibroblast markers

combination_strategies.rationale: Targeting a fibroblast‑derived pro‑tumorigenic ligand to normalize stroma
combination_strategies.phenotype: Decreased collagen deposition and fibro‑2 marker expression in vitro

open_questions: Which specific stromal pathways downstream of NETRIN G1 mediate protumorigenic activity?
Can the host factor metric predict patient response to stromal‑targeted therapies?

patent_or_ip_hints: US2025001234A1 targeting NETRIN G1 in pancreatic stroma

talk_title: ARCH-DR multimodal single-cell profiling of esophageal adenocarcinoma

speaker.name: Nick Navin
speaker.affiliation: University of California, San Francisco, Department of Pathology

MOA_summary: ARCH-DR is a high‑throughput archival DNA‑RNA co‑sequencing chemistry that simultaneously captures copy‑number alterations, somatic mutations and full‑length transcriptomes from thousands of single cells in fresh, frozen or FFPE esophageal adenocarcinoma specimens, enabling reconstruction of genetic lineages and phenotypic plasticity.
key_findings: Analyzed 12 EAC patients (≈34,000 cells) with ARCH‑DR, revealing patient‑specific transcriptional clusters driven by copy‑number dosage effects.
Identified three epithelial lineages (esophageal, gastric, intestinal) within individual tumors, with most subclones displaying mixed lineage phenotypes, indicating high cellular plasticity.
Detected ecDNA amplifications of EGFR and ERBB2 that segregated with distinct genetic subclones and showed expression saturation at ~20 copies.
Found that ~51% of genes exhibit dosage‑sensitive expression correlating with copy‑number changes, while the remainder are dosage‑insensitive.
Observed that ~20% of somatic mutations are associated with altered gene expression, highlighting mutation‑driven transcriptional effects.
open_questions: What molecular mechanisms underlie the observed dosage‑insensitive genes in EAC?
Do specific ecDNA events influence therapeutic response or resistance?
How do non‑cancer stromal cells acquire chromosome X/Y losses and what functional impact do they have on tumor immunity?

talk_title: PTA enables uniform single‑cell genome amplification and reveals hidden mutational diversity in pediatric leukemia

models.name: pediatric leukemia cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: multiple diagnostic and post‑treatment samples, single‑cell WGS (2,000‑4,000 mutations per cell)

clinical_data.value: detected in >90% of post‑treatment resistant clones

MOA_summary: Primary Template-directed Amplification (PTA) adds irreversible terminators to a Phi29‑based whole‑genome amplification, producing uniformly covered single‑cell genomes that retain accurate allele frequencies, enabling high‑sensitivity SNV and copy‑number detection, which was applied to pediatric leukemia to uncover a hidden mutational burden and clonal drug‑resistance mechanisms.
key_findings: PTA achieves ~90% detection of known germline variants with allele‑frequency distribution centered at 50%, outperforming MDA and PCR‑based methods.
Single‑cell sequencing of pediatric leukemia revealed 2,000–4,000 mutations per cell, far exceeding the <1,000 mutations observed in bulk sequencing.
De‑novo mutational signatures (SBS2/13 APOBEC) were identified at the single‑cell level, showing heterogeneous signature activity across clones.
Post‑treatment MRD analysis uncovered JAK2‑mutant subclones that were undetectable in bulk but expanded after chemotherapy, suggesting a genetic basis for resistance.
Integration of surface‑marker index sorting with PTA enabled mapping of phenotypic (surface protein) and genotypic (SNV/CNV) traits onto phylogenetic trees.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: JAK2‑mutant clones were selectively enriched after chemotherapy, indicating a potential resistance driver.
open_questions: Can PTA‑derived mutational signatures predict therapeutic response in real time?
What additional genomic events cooperate with JAK2 mutations to drive MRD persistence?

talk_title: SKY‑Met: high‑throughput single‑cell DNA methylation and multi‑omic profiling

speaker.name: Andrew Addy
speaker.affiliation: Oregon Health & Science University, Department of Molecular Medicine

models.name: AML cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: single‑cell methylome + ATAC co‑assay from bone‑marrow samples

MOA_summary: SKY‑Met combines combinatorial indexing with nucleosome disruption to achieve genome‑wide bisulfite‑free single‑cell DNA methylation profiling at high throughput; the platform also supports simultaneous ATAC‑seq or whole‑genome sequencing, enabling multi‑omic interrogation of epigenetic state, chromatin accessibility and copy‑number in thousands of cells from frozen or fixed tissues.
key_findings: Generated a 140,000‑cell methylome atlas of the human cerebral cortex, revealing cell‑type‑specific CpG methylation patterns and non‑CG methylation dynamics in neuronal subtypes.
Applied SKY‑Met to pancreatic ductal adenocarcinoma, achieving >90% CpG coverage per cell and enabling copy‑number profiling from frozen tumor specimens.
Demonstrated a joint methylome‑ATAC co‑assay on ~8% of 140,000 bone‑marrow cells, providing concordant cell‑type clustering from both modalities.
Detected a balanced translocation in a PDAC subclone using combined single‑cell genome + Hi‑C reads, illustrating the ability to capture structural variants alongside epigenetic information.

combination_strategies.rationale: to link chromatin accessibility with DNA methylation in the same cell
combination_strategies.phenotype: concordant cell‑type clustering from both modalities

combination_strategies.rationale: to capture 3D genome architecture alongside methylation
combination_strategies.phenotype: identification of subclone‑specific balanced translocation

open_questions: How can methylation‑based cell‑type markers be optimized for tumor sub‑clustering where CG methylation is less discriminative?
What is the functional impact of non‑CG methylation observed in rare tumor‑associated neuronal‑like cells?

talk_title: Precision medicine in childhood CNS tumors and high‑risk brain tumors

speaker.name: David Ziegler
speaker.affiliation: Sydney Children's Hospital and Cancer Institute

targets.dependency_context: pediatric cancers with CDK4/6 pathway activation

biomarkers.name: TRK fusion
biomarkers.finding: Identified in a high‑grade glioma infant; guided use of larotrectinib with dramatic clinical response

biomarkers.name: MYC amplification
biomarkers.finding: Associated with worse overall survival in high‑risk CNS tumors (median OS 12 mo vs 21 mo for non‑amplified)

models.name: Zero Childhood Cancer Program cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 71% of high‑risk patients had a therapeutic target; 5% diagnostic change

clinical_data.population: high‑risk brain tumor patients with matched therapy

MOA_summary: Ziegler described a national whole‑genome and methylation profiling program that identifies actionable alterations (e.g., NTRK fusions, MYC amplification) in high‑risk pediatric brain tumors; matched targeted agents such as larotrectinib produce disease stabilization or objective responses, and patients receiving precision‑guided therapy show significantly improved progression‑free and overall survival compared with unguided treatment.
key_findings: 71% of high‑risk CNS tumor patients harbored a therapeutic target; 5% had a diagnostic revision based on genomics.
Precision‑guided therapy conferred a 44% reduction in hazard of death (HR 0.56, 95% CI 0.38‑0.82) versus unguided therapy.
Patients with MYC‑amplified tumors had markedly worse OS (median 12 mo vs 21 mo) but still derived benefit from matched therapy.
A TRK‑fusion infant with refractory high‑grade glioma achieved rapid functional recovery and radiographic remission after larotrectinib.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: TRK inhibitors are highly selective with minimal off‑target effects, making them ideal for infants.
MYC amplification remains a poor prognostic marker despite targeted therapy, highlighting need for combination approaches.

combination_strategies.rationale: Combine targeted agents with standard cytotoxic therapy to overcome multiple pathway activation
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved disease control in high‑risk patients

open_questions: How can combination regimens be optimized to target multiple concurrent drivers in high‑risk brain tumors?
What is the optimal timing for delivering matched targeted agents (early vs salvage) to maximize survival benefit?

talk_title: Precision medicine in childhood bone sarcomas: osteosarcoma and Ewing sarcoma subtyping

speaker.name: Catherine Janeway
speaker.affiliation: Boston Children's Hospital and Dana‑Farber Cancer Institute

biomarkers.name: MYC amplification
biomarkers.finding: Associated with worse OS (median 1.5 yr vs 5.4 yr) in osteosarcoma

biomarkers.name: MYC protein overexpression
biomarkers.finding: Median OS 1.8 yr vs 5.4 yr for low expression

biomarkers.name: STAG2 mutation
biomarkers.finding: Increased risk of relapse in Ewing sarcoma (HR ~1.5 in multivariable model)

models.name: Dana‑Farber PROFILE & GAIN‑ICAD2 studies
models.genotype_or_key_features: ~2,500 pediatric solid tumors sequenced with OncoPanel

models.name: MSKCC osteosarcoma validation cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: Localized osteosarcoma patients, MYC copy‑number assessed by fold‑change

models.name: COG Ewing sarcoma phase‑III trials
models.genotype_or_key_features: 354 FFPE samples analyzed with low‑pass WGS (Trans‑eq) and STAG2 IHC

MOA_summary: Janeway identified MYC amplification (and high MYC protein) as a high‑risk biomarker in osteosarcoma, correlating with markedly reduced overall survival, and demonstrated that STAG2 mutations predict relapse in Ewing sarcoma, establishing biologic subtypes that can guide risk‑adapted therapy and future targeted approaches.
key_findings: MYC amplification occurs in ~10% of osteosarcoma and predicts significantly worse OS (median 1.5 yr vs 5.4 yr).
High MYC protein expression (IHC H‑score >150) independently associates with poor survival; combined amplification + high protein identifies the highest‑risk subgroup (median OS 1 yr).
In a validation cohort, MYC amplification confirmed as adverse prognostic factor for both OS and EFS.
STAG2 mutation is the strongest molecular predictor of relapse in localized Ewing sarcoma (multivariable HR ≈ 1.5).
Patients without any recurrent alterations have >90% recurrence‑free survival, suggesting a de‑intensification opportunity.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: MYC amplification likely resides on extrachromosomal DNA, complicating direct targeting.
STAG2‑mutated Ewing sarcoma shows resistance to standard chemotherapy, underscoring need for novel agents.

combination_strategies.with_agent: standard MAP chemotherapy (doxorubicin, cisplatin, high‑dose methotrexate)
combination_strategies.rationale: Assess whether MYC‑amplified tumors benefit from intensified or targeted addition
combination_strategies.phenotype: Potentially improved response in high‑risk osteosarcoma

combination_strategies.rationale: MYC‑driven replication stress may sensitize to DNA damage response inhibition
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression in MYC‑amp models

open_questions: Can MYC‑amplified osteosarcoma be effectively targeted with emerging MYC or CDK4/6 inhibitors?
What is the optimal therapeutic regimen for STAG2‑mutated Ewing sarcoma—targeted agents, immunotherapy, or intensified chemotherapy?

talk_title: Molecular subtyping of osteosarcoma and Ewing sarcoma for outcome prediction

speaker.name: Jane Wei
speaker.affiliation: Dana‑Farber Cancer Institute / Boston Children’s Hospital

biomarkers.name: MYC amplification
biomarkers.finding: Median OS 1.5 yr (amp) vs 5.4 yr (non‑amp) in osteosarcoma

biomarkers.name: MYC protein high expression
biomarkers.finding: Median OS 1.8 yr (high) vs 5.4 yr (low)

biomarkers.name: STAG2 mutation
biomarkers.finding: Increased relapse risk in Ewing sarcoma (HR ≈ 1.5)

models.name: MSKCC osteosarcoma validation cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: Localized disease, MYC copy‑number by fold‑change

models.name: COG Ewing sarcoma Trans‑eq cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 354 FFPE samples, low‑pass WGS + STAG2 IHC

MOA_summary: Wei integrated targeted sequencing and IHC data to define biologic subtypes in osteosarcoma (MYC amplification + high protein) and Ewing sarcoma (STAG2 mutation), demonstrating that these molecular alterations independently predict poorer survival and can be used to stratify patients for risk‑adapted therapy.
key_findings: MYC amplification occurs in ~10% of osteosarcoma and predicts markedly inferior OS (median 1.5 yr).
High MYC protein expression alone also predicts poor survival; combined amplification + high protein identifies the highest‑risk subgroup (median OS 1 yr).
In a separate MSKCC cohort, MYC amplification confirmed as adverse prognostic factor for OS and EFS.
STAG2 mutation is the strongest molecular predictor of relapse in localized Ewing sarcoma, independent of other clinical factors.
Patients lacking recurrent alterations have >90% recurrence‑free survival, supporting potential de‑intensification strategies.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: MYC amplification likely resides on extrachromosomal DNA, limiting direct druggability.
STAG2‑mutated Ewing sarcoma shows resistance to standard chemotherapy, indicating need for novel agents.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target downstream of MYC‑driven cell‑cycle activation
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic growth inhibition in MYC‑amp osteosarcoma models

combination_strategies.rationale: MYC‑driven replication stress may sensitize to DNA repair inhibition
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced tumor regression in xenograft models

open_questions: Can MYC‑amplified osteosarcoma be effectively targeted with emerging MYC or CDK4/6 inhibitors in clinical trials?
What therapeutic strategies best overcome STAG2‑mediated chemoresistance in Ewing sarcoma?

talk_title: Slide Tags spatial transcriptomics and lineage tracing in tumor ecosystems

models.name: KP tracer lung tumor model
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS-driven lung tumor with engineered barcodes

MOA_summary: Slide Tags uses photo-cleavable DNA barcodes on spatial arrays to label nuclei, providing true single-cell resolution spatial transcriptomics; combined with lineage barcoding and CNV detection to map tumor clone spatial niches and infer expansion dynamics.
key_findings: Slide Tags achieves true single-cell resolution with median >3,000 UMIs per cell, outperforming Visium HD in melanoma specimens.
Distinct transcriptional tumor cell states in melanoma correspond to spatially segregated CNV clones and differential immune neighborhoods.
Neural crest-like tumor state associates with SPP1+ macrophages and fibroblasts, showing reduced CD8 T‑cell clonality.
Lineage tracing in KP mouse tumors reveals spatially coherent subclones with active expansion linked to Arg1+ immunosuppressive macrophages and hypoxia.
Metastasis‑seeding subclones share hypoxic and immunosuppressive niche signatures with primary tumor regions.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: SPP1 protects neural crest‑like melanoma cells from interferon and TNF‑mediated killing.
Immune‑suppressive niches limit T‑cell clone expansion in specific tumor regions.
open_questions: Can T‑cell receptor specificities be matched to tumor subclone antigens in situ?
What are the precise molecular mechanisms by which SPP1 confers cytokine resistance?

talk_title: Decoding tissue architecture in pancreatic cancer using SEGER and Wormhole

models.name: Basal cell supermouse model
models.genotype_or_key_features: Cre-inducible DTR cassette in basal PDAC cells

MOA_summary: SEGER assigns transcripts to nuclei using AI‑driven graph embeddings, eliminating segmentation errors; Wormhole transforms high‑dimensional niche matrices into a scalable latent space, enabling trajectory and interaction analysis of spatial neighborhoods.
key_findings: SEGER improves transcript‑to‑cell assignment over geometric methods, preserving continuous cell‑state manifolds.
Wormhole latent captures dominant variation as a gastric‑to‑progenitor axis and associated niche remodeling.
p53 loss sustains a progenitor‑like niche with immunosuppressive TNC fibroblasts and Arg1+ macrophages in early PDAC.
Ablation of basal cells (4% of tumor mass) triggers collapse of the entire tumor, accompanied by loss of immunosuppressive macrophages and fibroblasts and influx of T/NK cells.
CRISPR‑mediated deletion of CSF2 specifically in basal cells phenocopies basal cell ablation, implicating CSF2 as a master niche signal.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Basal cells act as niche architects; their removal destabilizes tumor ecosystem.

combination_strategies.rationale: KRAS‑dependent progenitor state maintenance; inhibition reduces progenitor niche.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Loss of progenitor cells and associated immunosuppressive niche.

open_questions: Can the Wormhole niche embedding be scaled to patient‑level analyses for prognostic stratification?
What upstream regulators control CSF2 expression in basal PDAC cells?

talk_title: Spatial transcriptomics and proteomics reveal organization of glioma and head‑and‑neck cancer
tumor_types: head and neck squamous cell carcinoma
MOA_summary: Combines Visium spatial transcriptomics (55 µm spots) with CODEX multiplexed protein imaging to map cancer cell states and microenvironmental interactions, revealing spatial coherence, hypoxia‑driven layering in glioma and microenvironment‑dependent partial EMT patterns in head‑and‑neck cancer.
key_findings: In GBM, mesenchymal‑hypoxia tumor cells form spatially coherent patches; high hypoxia correlates with structured tissue organization.
GBM spatial layers: necrotic/hypoxic core → mesenchymal/hypoxia → inflammatory macrophages → vascular repair → neurodevelopmental tumor cells → normal brain parenchyma.
IDH‑mutant gliomas show less structure overall; low‑grade tumors retain normal white‑gray matter boundaries that act as barriers to malignant cell infiltration.
In head‑and‑neck cancer, partial EMT programs are either edge‑enriched (fibroblast‑derived TGF‑β) or core‑enriched (myeloid‑derived oncostatin M), influencing immune infiltration.
Oncostatin M and TGF‑β both induce partial EMT genes in vitro, but spatial context determines dominant driver in vivo.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Partial EMT at tumor core associates with reduced lymphocyte infiltration, suggesting stronger immunosuppression.

combination_strategies.rationale: Reduce core‑enriched partial EMT and restore immune infiltration
combination_strategies.phenotype: Predicted decrease in EMT gene expression in tumor core

combination_strategies.rationale: Target edge‑enriched EMT driven by fibroblasts
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced EMT at invasive front

open_questions: What determines whether a head‑and‑neck tumor adopts edge‑ vs core‑enriched EMT phenotypes?
Can the white‑gray matter barrier in low‑grade glioma be therapeutically reinforced to limit tumor spread?

talk_title: Spatial Transcriptomics Profiling of Colorectal Cancer Tumors in Alaska Native People: Discovery of Prognostic Biomarkers

biomarkers.name: SPINK4
biomarkers.finding: Upregulated in epithelial compartment of tumor center in patients who did not die from colorectal cancer.

biomarkers.name: MUQ2
biomarkers.finding: Upregulated in epithelial compartment of tumor center in patients who did not die from colorectal cancer.

biomarkers.name: HBA2
biomarkers.finding: Upregulated in epithelial compartment of invasive margin in patients who die from colorectal cancer.

biomarkers.name: Interferon alpha and gamma response pathway
biomarkers.finding: Enriched in epithelial compartment (tumor center and invasive margin) in patients who did not die from colorectal cancer.

biomarkers.name: Epithelial-mesenchymal transition pathways
biomarkers.finding: Enriched in epithelial and stromal compartments of invasive margin in patients who die from colorectal cancer.

biomarkers.name: Plasma cells
biomarkers.finding: Increased in epithelial compartment at tumor center in patients who did not die from colorectal cancer.

biomarkers.name: Mast cells
biomarkers.finding: Increased in stromal compartment at tumor center in patients who die from colorectal cancer.

models.name: Alaska Native colorectal cancer patients
models.genotype_or_key_features: 217 patients with FFPE tissue blocks, matched for sex, tumor stage, and tumor sites. Deaths matched 1:2 to non-deaths.

MOA_summary: This study utilized spatial transcriptomics (GeoMix) to profile colorectal cancer tumors in Alaska Native people, identifying prognostic biomarkers associated with mortality. Key findings include differential gene expression (SPINK4, MUQ2, HBA2) and pathway enrichment (interferon response, EMT) in tumor and stromal compartments, as well as altered cell type abundance (plasma cells, mast cells), correlating with patient survival outcomes.
key_findings: Identified spatially specific markers tied to colorectal cancer-specific mortality in Alaska Native people.
In the tumor center epithelial compartment, downregulation of MILK2 and SPINK4, and interferon alpha and gamma pathways were associated with not dying from colorectal cancer.
In the tumor center, a decrease in plasma cells and an increase in mast cells were observed in patients who did not die from colorectal cancer.
In the invasive margin epithelial compartment, an increase in HBA2 expression was observed in patients who died from colorectal cancer.
Epithelial-mesenchymal transition pathways were enriched in the invasive margin (epithelial and stromal compartments) in patients who died from colorectal cancer.

talk_title: Highly Multiplexed Immunoassays Enable Discovery of Multi-Marker EV Surface Signatures as Novel Biomarkers

biomarkers.name: CEA (soluble)
biomarkers.finding: 16% of stage IV CRC patients were undetectable.

biomarkers.name: CEA on EV surface
biomarkers.finding: Detectable in stage IV CRC patients where soluble CEA was undetectable.

biomarkers.name: CD73, CD13, CEA triplet signature
biomarkers.finding: Recurrent components in CRC-secreted EVs, with prognostic value.

biomarkers.name: Epithelial cell markers (CEA, E-cadherin, EpCAM)
biomarkers.finding: Reflect epithelial cell origin in CRC EVs.

biomarkers.name: Stromal cell markers
biomarkers.finding: Reflect stromal cell origin in CRC EVs.

biomarkers.name: CD73
biomarkers.finding: Found on both stromal and epithelial populations.

models.name: Colorectal cancer patients
models.genotype_or_key_features: Three independent cohorts: 1 cross-sectional (Bob Coffey lab), 2 longitudinal (Andy Nixon lab).

models.name: Diffi, HCT15
models.genotype_or_key_features: Colorectal carcinoma cell lines used for assay validation.

MOA_summary: This presentation introduces a novel combinatorial immunosequencing platform for highly multiplexed profiling of extracellular vesicle (EV) surface signatures. The technology enables the discovery of multi-marker combinations that can distinguish cell origins and provide prognostic information, demonstrating improved sensitivity over single markers and showing consistent findings across multiple colorectal cancer cohorts.
key_findings: Soluble CEA was undetectable in 16% of stage IV CRC patients, while the same molecule on EV surfaces was detectable.
The combinatorial immunosequencing platform can detect rare EV subpopulations (1 in 10 million) with high sensitivity and a four-log dynamic range.
Three-marker combinations consistently discriminated between epithelial and stromal cell origins in CRC EVs across three independent cohorts.
Several multi-marker EV surface signatures showed FDR significant prognostic value in longitudinal CRC cohorts.
CD73, CD13, and CEA were identified as recurrent components in CRC-secreted EVs with prognostic potential.
open_questions: Plans for validation of findings.
Potential for early detection using multi-marker EV signatures.

talk_title: Differences in Survival Outcomes Among Metastatic Colorectal Cancer Patients Across Fibroblast Growth Factor Receptor Two and Three Expression Levels

biomarkers.name: FGFR2 gene expression
biomarkers.finding: Associated with worse overall survival across treatment arms (HR 1.28, median survival difference 7.4 months).

biomarkers.name: FGFR2 gene expression
biomarkers.finding: Predictive of overall survival in patients treated with cetuximab (HR 1.56, median survival difference 12 months).

biomarkers.name: FGFR3 gene expression
biomarkers.finding: Predictive of overall survival in patients treated with cetuximab (HR 0.59, median survival difference 12.4 months).

biomarkers.name: FGFR2 SNP RS-3135718
biomarkers.finding: Predictive of progression-free and overall survival in patients treated with cetuximab. Homozygous variant associated with improved survival (median survival difference 3 months vs WT).

models.name: CALGB SWOG 80405 Phase III clinical trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: Newly diagnosed metastatic colorectal cancer patients randomized to chemotherapy + cetuximab or chemotherapy + bevacizumab. Covariates adjusted for: KRAS mutation, BRAF mutation, MSI status, sidedness, age, sex.

models.name: CRC cell lines
models.genotype_or_key_features: Used for preclinical validation of FGFR2 inhibition.

clinical_data.metric: Median Survival Difference (FGFR2 high vs low)
Overall Survival (FGFR2 high vs low, cetuximab arm)
Median Survival Difference (FGFR2 high vs low, cetuximab arm)
Overall Survival (FGFR3 high vs low, cetuximab arm)
Median Survival Difference (FGFR3 high vs low, cetuximab arm)
Overall Survival (FGFR2 SNP RS-3135718 homozygous variant vs WT, cetuximab arm)

MOA_summary: This study investigated the prognostic and predictive value of FGFR2 and FGFR3 gene expression and FGFR2 SNPs in metastatic colorectal cancer patients from the CALGB SWOG 80405 trial. FGFR2 high expression was associated with worse survival overall and specifically in the cetuximab arm, while FGFR3 high expression and a specific FGFR2 SNP (RS-3135718) were associated with improved survival in the cetuximab arm, suggesting differential roles in response to EGFR inhibition.
key_findings: FGFR2 high expression was prognostic for worse overall survival in mCRC patients (HR 1.28).
FGFR2 high expression was predictive of worse overall survival in the cetuximab arm (HR 1.56).
FGFR3 high expression was predictive of improved overall survival in the cetuximab arm (HR 0.59).
FGFR2 SNP RS-3135718 variant allele was predictive of improved PFS and OS in the cetuximab arm.
FGFR2 inhibition decreased cell viability in CRC cell lines, reiterating its criticality for cell survival.
open_questions: Why do FGFR2 and FGFR3 behave differently despite being in the same family?
Underlying mechanisms for FGFR3's association with improved survival in the cetuximab arm.

talk_title: Mapping of Spatial Organization of B Cells and Immune Signaling in Triple Negative Breast Cancer to Characterize Response to Immunotherapy

biomarkers.name: Germinal center B cells
biomarkers.finding: Associated with immunotherapy response on-treatment.

biomarkers.name: Plasma memory B cells
biomarkers.finding: Associated with immunotherapy response on-treatment.

biomarkers.name: Naive B cells
biomarkers.finding: Associated with immunotherapy response on-treatment.

biomarkers.name: B activated niche
biomarkers.finding: Significantly associated with immunotherapy response across neighborhood scales.

biomarkers.name: Germinal center niche
biomarkers.finding: Trends towards association with immunotherapy response.

biomarkers.name: Plasma-maturing niche
biomarkers.finding: Trends towards association with immunotherapy response.

biomarkers.name: Plasma-rich niche interacting with CD15, MHC-1 and 2 epithelial cells
biomarkers.finding: Associated with immunotherapy response at baseline (immunotherapy arm).

biomarkers.name: B cells from B activated niche interacting with CD4, CD8, plasma, and B cells
biomarkers.finding: Associated with immunotherapy response on-treatment.

biomarkers.name: Naive B cells enrichment
biomarkers.finding: Most abundant phenotype enriched on-treatment in chemoimmunotherapy arm, also enriched in chemotherapy arm.

biomarkers.name: Cytokine enrichment within niches
biomarkers.finding: Persistent feature in chemoimmunotherapy responders post-treatment.

models.name: NEOCHIP study
models.genotype_or_key_features: 280 early-stage triple-negative breast cancer patients randomized to chemoimmunotherapy or chemotherapy. Samples collected at baseline, on-treatment, and post-treatment.

MOA_summary: This study investigated the role of B cells and cytokine signaling in triple-negative breast cancer (TNBC) response to immunotherapy using imaging mass cytometry (IMC). Findings indicate that B cell densities, specific B cell niches (e.g., B activated niche), and their interactions with other immune cells are significantly associated with immunotherapy response. The spatial organization of B cells and cytokine enrichment within niches appear to be key drivers of treatment efficacy.
key_findings: Several B cell populations (germinal center B, plasma memory, naive B) are associated with immunotherapy response on-treatment.
Specific B cell niches (germinal center, B activated, plasma-rich, plasma-maturing) were identified.
The B activated niche and its interactions with other immune cells are predictive of immunotherapy response.
B cells are more significantly impacted post-immunotherapy compared to other TME cells.
Cytokine enrichment within niches is a persistent feature in immunotherapy responders across treatment time points.
open_questions: How do B cells and plasma cells contribute to immunotherapy response, given the traditional focus on T cells?
The role of antibodies produced by plasma cells in immunotherapy response.
Potential for targeting B cells or their niches in immunotherapy.

talk_title: AI-Powered Deep Visual Proteomics (DVP) Links Pancreatic Precursor Biology to PDAC Survival Phenotypes

biomarkers.name: Cancer-associated normal ducts vs. incidental normal ducts
biomarkers.finding: Cancer-associated ducts show enrichment of proteins reflecting oxidative stress, metabolic reprogramming, and immune editing.

biomarkers.name: Inflammation and antigen presentation pathways
biomarkers.finding: Upregulated early in normal ducts, all the way to low-grade PANA, then sharply drop in high-grade PANA, suggesting immune evasion.

biomarkers.name: KRAS mutations
biomarkers.finding: Identified hotspot mutations in normal ducts, ADMs, and PANA lesions, allowing genotype-phenotype correlation.

models.name: Pancreatic precursor lesions and PDAC cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: Cohort 1: Incidental normal ducts and ADMs, low-grade PANAs. Cohort 2: Cancer-associated normal ducts, low-grade and high-grade PANAs, tumor cells. Isolated <100 phenotype-matched epithelial cells.

MOA_summary: This study applied AI-powered Deep Visual Proteomics (DVP) to analyze pancreatic precursor lesions and PDAC. By integrating high-resolution microscopy with mass spectrometry-based spatial proteomics, the researchers identified early molecular changes in normal ducts associated with cancer development, including immune editing and metabolic reprogramming. The technology also enabled the detection of KRAS mutations directly from proteomic data, linking genotype to phenotype and revealing insights into immune evasion in high-grade PANA.
key_findings: Cancer-associated normal ducts show distinct proteomic profiles compared to incidental normal ducts, including oxidative stress, metabolic reprogramming, and immune editing.
Inflammation and antigen presentation pathways are upregulated early in pancreatic precursor lesions but drop sharply in high-grade PANA, indicating immune evasion.
KRAS hotspot mutations can be identified from proteomic data, enabling genotype-phenotype correlation in precursor lesions.
The study identified a 'cancer field effect' where normal ducts near tumors acquire immune signatures.
DVP identified distinct proteomic differences between spatially adjacent regions within tumors, highlighting tumor heterogeneity.
open_questions: Translating these findings into clinical diagnostics or therapeutics.
Further exploration of the identified immune evasion mechanisms.

talk_title: Spatial Immune Multiomics Refines Prognostication in Early-Stage Estrogen Receptor-Positive Breast Cancer

speaker.name: Zach Kinsella
speaker.affiliation: Royal College of Surgeons in Ireland

targets.dependency_context: inflamed epithelia in ER+ breast cancer
inflamed epithelia and stroma in ER+ breast cancer
inflamed epithelia in ER+ breast cancer
inflamed epithelia in ER+ breast cancer
intermediate recurrence score ER+ breast cancer, chemo arm

biomarkers.name: Inflamed TME and epithelia
biomarkers.finding: Associated with poorer outcome in patients receiving chemotherapy in the intermediate recurrence score group.

biomarkers.name: Stromal CD8 density
biomarkers.finding: Predictive marker of chemo inferiority in intermediate recurrence score ER+ breast cancer.

biomarkers.name: Epithelial CD8 density
biomarkers.finding: Conferred poorer outcome in patients randomized to chemotherapy.

biomarkers.name: Tregs fluidity
biomarkers.finding: More fluid in inflamed TME, likely to infiltrate epithelia.

models.name: Irish patients from TAILORx trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: Early-stage ER-positive, HER2-negative breast cancer patients with intermediate recurrence score (11-25). Randomized to hormone therapy alone or chemoendocrine therapy.

clinical_data.population: Intermediate recurrence score ER+ breast cancer

clinical_data.metric: Median survival time difference (90th percentile CD8 density)
clinical_data.population: Intermediate recurrence score ER+ breast cancer

MOA_summary: This study utilized spatial multiomics (IHC, spatial transcriptomics, spatial proteomics) on early-stage ER+ breast cancer patients from the TAILORx trial to refine prognostication in the intermediate recurrence score group. Findings reveal that inflamed immune microenvironments are associated with dysfunctional responses, including upregulation of checkpoints and downregulation of ER signaling. High stromal CD8 density was identified as a predictive marker of chemo inferiority, suggesting that immune infiltration patterns can refine treatment decisions.
key_findings: Four TME types and three epithelial types were identified, with immune types not significantly captured by Oncotype DX Recurrence Score.
Inflamed epithelia were characterized by downregulation of ESR1 and suppression of DNA repair pathways.
Inflamed TME and epithelia were prognostic in the intermediate recurrence score group, with chemotherapy showing poorer outcomes.
High stromal CD8 density was a predictive marker of chemo inferiority in the intermediate recurrence score group.
Increasing CD8 density conferred poorer outcome on chemotherapy, adding prognostic information to clinical variables.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Inflamed states are transcriptomically wired to more dysfunctions, such as expression of checkpoints, exhaustion transcripts, M2-like macrophage, and remodeling genes, as well as dedifferentiation.
open_questions: How to translate these findings into a clinical assay, potentially using foundational models for TIL scoring.
The role of chemotherapy in ER+ breast cancer with high CD8 density.
The relationship between CD8 density and response to immunotherapy in triple-negative breast cancer.

talk_title: Unbiased Phenotyping of CRISPR Edited Tumors with Preserved Map and Subcellular Imaging of the Mouse Whole Transcriptome, 22,000 Plex

targets.dependency_context: mouse lung tumors treated with PD1

biomarkers.name: Predicted human CCAM6 expression
biomarkers.finding: Identified specific tumors with high predicted human CCAM6 expression in mouse models, correlating with a switch in histology resembling human CCAM6-high tumors.

biomarkers.name: STK11 knockout phenotype
biomarkers.finding: Associated with cold TME and fibrotic signature.

biomarkers.name: beta2m knockout phenotype
biomarkers.finding: Associated with low T cells and cold TME.

biomarkers.name: beta-catenin knockout phenotype
biomarkers.finding: Associated with hot TME and T cell activity.

biomarkers.name: SOX1 knockout phenotype
biomarkers.finding: Associated with hot TME and T cell activity.

biomarkers.name: PTPN2 knockout phenotype
biomarkers.finding: Associated with hot TME and T cell activity.

models.name: PertiMap platform
models.genotype_or_key_features: Scaled in vivo perturbation platform using protein barcodes (ProCode CRISPRs) in mouse lungs. Generated >600 genetic perturbations across 4 therapeutic interventions.

MOA_summary: This presentation describes the PertiMap platform, which uses CRISPR-edited, barcoded tumors in mice to generate large-scale in vivo perturbation data. This data is then 'humanized' using the MAIRS model, which projects human biology onto mouse H&E images, enabling the discovery of novel therapeutic targets and combinations. The approach allows for unbiased phenotyping of genetic perturbations and identification of drivers of tumor biology and response to therapy.
key_findings: The PertiMap platform enables scaled in vivo genetic perturbation and multimodal tissue profiling.
The MAIRS model can project human biology onto mouse H&E images, effectively 'humanizing' preclinical models.
MAIRS identified tumors with high predicted human CCAM6 expression in mouse models, correlating with specific histological changes.
The embedding space of perturbations revealed distinct phenotypic clusters associated with immune infiltration, fibrosis, and metabolic signatures.
VEGFR1 knockout tumors treated with PD1 showed a shift towards a hot TME, suggesting a rationale for combination therapy.

combination_strategies.rationale: VEGFR1 knockout tumors treated with PD1 showed a drastic shift towards a hot TME.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Hot TME

combination_strategies.rationale: Identified a gene knockout that suggests a rationale for combination with PD1.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Unknown

open_questions: How to validate the predicted human gene expression in mouse models.
The application of MAIRS to humanized mouse models with both mouse and human cells.
The number of genes that can be identified in the inferred spatial transcriptomes.

talk_title: Pragmatic trial of mailed FIT versus FIT-DNA outreach in community health centers

speaker.name: Jennifer Haas
speaker.affiliation: Massachusetts General Hospital, Center for Primary Care Research

models.name: CARE-CCRS pragmatic trial cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: screening-eligible adults age 45-75 at 8 CHCs in Boston/LA + 1 tribal site

MOA_summary: Mailed outreach using either annual FIT kits or triennial FIT‑DNA kits, combined with text reminders and manufacturer‑provided navigation, modestly increased colorectal cancer screening uptake (28% vs 23% at 90 days) in underserved community health centers, though follow‑up colonoscopy rates remained suboptimal.
key_findings: FIT‑DNA arm achieved 28% screening completion at 90 days versus 23% for FIT (p<0.05).
The modest advantage persisted at 180 days (≈27% vs 22%).
Higher screening rates were observed in Los Angeles sites for FIT‑DNA, driven by lower baseline screening.
Hispanic and Medicaid/uninsured participants showed greater uptake of FIT‑DNA, likely reflecting clinic‑level differences.
Colonoscopic follow‑up after abnormal results was low across all sites, with Boston showing the highest rates.

talk_title: DISRUPT: AI‑driven patient‑trial matching to improve equity in cancer trial enrollment

speaker.name: Nina Bachael
speaker.affiliation: Mount Sinai Cancer Center, Associate Director of Community Engaged and Equity Research

models.name: DISRUPT enrollment cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: patients at treatment decision points across three NYC cancer centers

MOA_summary: An EMR‑based algorithm identifies patients at treatment decision points, matches them to open trials, and delivers bilingual trial summaries to clinicians and patients before the decision visit, resulting in higher minority enrollment (58%) and a 27% enrollment rate among eligible patients, surpassing historical benchmarks.
key_findings: Enrolled 579 patients, of whom 58% self‑identified as minority, reversing typical under‑representation.
Eligibility filtering reduced manual chart review workload by 35%.
Among eligible patients, 27% enrolled in a trial, compared with ~22% at NCI centers.
No racial/ethnic differences were observed in patient attitudes toward trials or in physician‑reported trial discussions.
High refusal rate due to emotional burden at decision points limited overall enrollment.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Patients often declined participation due to emotional stress at treatment decision points.
open_questions: How can the algorithm be integrated prospectively into trial design and IRB planning to predict accrual feasibility?

talk_title: Multimodal navigation to increase lung cancer screening in the I‑95 corridor

speaker.name: Robert Winn
speaker.affiliation: VCU Massey Comprehensive Cancer Center, Director of Lung Cancer Health Disparities Research

models.name: Southeastern Consortium Lung Screening cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: African‑American adults eligible for low‑dose CT screening across VA, NC, SC

clinical_data.population: screen‑eligible African‑American adults in I‑95 corridor

MOA_summary: A multimodal, multilevel navigation system—combining community activators, certified community health workers, and clinical nurse navigators—was deployed to identify, engage, and guide African‑American adults in the I‑95 corridor through low‑dose CT lung‑cancer screening, resulting in enrollment of 220 participants and collection of >5,000 biospecimens.
key_findings: Identified 675 African‑American individuals eligible for lung‑cancer screening; 220 (≈33%) enrolled after navigation.
Collected >5,000 biospecimens and contacted >3,000 community members, demonstrating feasibility of large‑scale outreach.
Multimodal navigation (community activators + CHWs + nurse navigators) improved screening uptake compared with prior single‑navigator models.
Rural sites required intensive door‑to‑door outreach; lack of mobile screening vans limited reach in remote areas.
Financial‑toxicity navigation was planned but not fully implemented due to resource constraints.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Transportation barriers and limited colonoscopy capacity reduced follow‑up compliance.

combination_strategies.rationale: to overcome transportation barriers in remote rural communities
combination_strategies.phenotype: potentially higher screening completion in frontier areas

open_questions: How can sustainable funding be secured to maintain the multilevel navigation workforce after grant termination?
What is the optimal balance of high‑tech (AI risk scoring) and high‑touch (community navigation) interventions for maximal impact?

session_title: Strengthening the Oncology Trial Ecosystem through Operational and Regulatory Innovation
talk_title: Survey of US Oncology Trial Site Activation Timelines

speaker.name: Mark Stewart
speaker.affiliation: Friends of Cancer Research, Vice President of Science Policy

models.name: not specified
models.genotype_or_key_features: registrational oncology trials, Phase 3 (2020-2024), self‑reported company data

clinical_data.comparator: South Korea ~45 days; other regions 100‑125 days

MOA_summary: Mark Stewart presented analysis of a multi‑company survey of registrational oncology trials (2020‑2024) showing that US sites open the first site quickly (~150 days) but lag substantially in scaling to 75% of planned sites (>300 days) and in enrolling the first patient (~80 days). He linked these delays to cumulative protocol amendments, each adding 30‑60 days, and highlighted systemic bottlenecks across regulatory review, IRB processes, contracting, and site readiness.
key_findings: US first site activation median 150 days, faster than Europe and China.
Time to activate 75% of planned US sites exceeds 300 days, whereas South Korea achieves ~45 days and other regions 100‑125 days.
First patient in US takes ~80 days versus 40‑50 days in non‑US regions.
Each additional protocol amendment version adds approximately 30‑60 days to overall study start time.
Trials with three or more protocol versions experience an extra 70‑80 days delay compared with the original version.

talk_title: Stress and mutation dependency reshape CAF heterogeneity via HSF1 and oxidative stress pathways

biomarkers.name: BRCA1/2 mutation status
biomarkers.finding: BRCA-mutated tumors showed higher stromal HSF1, ATF4, XBP1 expression and increased iCAF proportions

models.name: in vitro cancer cell lines (BRCA mutant vs WT)
syngeneic mouse tumor models
models.genotype_or_key_features: BRCA-mutated breast, pancreatic, ovarian, prostate tumors

models.name: ex vivo CAF–NK cell co-cultures
models.genotype_or_key_features: triple‑negative breast cancer CAFs vs normal fibroblasts

MOA_summary: Cancer cell BRCA mutations drive upregulation of stress‑responsive transcription factors (HSF1, NRF2, ATF4, XBP1) in adjacent stromal fibroblasts, reprogramming them toward an immune‑regulatory iCAF phenotype that suppresses NK‑cell cytotoxicity and promotes tumor progression.
key_findings: BRCA‑mutated tumors exhibit higher stromal HSF1, ATF4, and XBP1 expression compared with BRCA‑wild‑type tumors.
HSF1‑dependent signaling converts fibroblasts to iCAFs that down‑regulate NK‑cell activating receptors (NKG2D, DNAM1).
CAF‑mediated internalization of NK‑cell ligands (CD155, ULBP1) impairs NK‑cell cytotoxicity in ex vivo co‑culture assays.
In vivo NK‑cell depletion or CAF enrichment increases stromal content and reduces metastasis control in mouse models.
High nectin‑2 expression on CAFs correlates with poorer overall survival in breast cancer tissue microarrays.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: NK‑cell inhibition by CAFs appears irreversible on the experimental timescale.
HSF1 inhibition may sensitize tumors to PARP inhibitors in BRCA‑mutated contexts.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target cancer cells while simultaneously inhibiting stress‑driven CAF reprogramming
combination_strategies.phenotype: Potential synergistic tumor regression in BRCA‑mutated models

open_questions: Does targeting HSF1 in CAFs reverse established iCAF phenotypes in vivo?
What metabolic cues (e.g., lipids) drive CAF‑mediated NK‑cell suppression?
Can similar stress‑response pathways be exploited in other tumor types beyond BRCA‑mutated cancers?

talk_title: Chronic stress, glucocorticoids, and neutrophil NETs promote metastatic outgrowth

biomarkers.name: circulating NET DNA (cfDNA)
biomarkers.finding: DNase treatment reduced plasma NET DNA and lowered lung metastasis in stressed mice

models.name: chronic unpredictable mild stress model
models.genotype_or_key_features: daily varied stressors (tail pinch, noise, food deprivation)

models.name: pancreatic cancer patient plasma glucocorticoid analysis

MOA_summary: Chronic stress elevates glucocorticoids, which activate the glucocorticoid receptor in neutrophils, driving excessive NET formation that remodels the lung microenvironment, expands ECM, suppresses T‑cell infiltration, and creates a permissive niche for dormant disseminated tumor cells to proliferate and form metastases.
key_findings: Restraint stress increased circulating corticosterone and produced a ~4‑fold rise in lung metastases.
Chronic unpredictable mild stress also elevated corticosterone and enhanced metastatic burden despite milder hormone rise.
Stressed lungs showed increased ECM (fibronectin), reduced T‑cell infiltration, and expanded neutrophils.
Neutrophils from stressed mice formed markedly more NETs ex vivo; this required neutrophil‑intrinsic glucocorticoid receptor signaling.
DNase treatment abolished NET DNA, reduced ECM deposition, and returned metastatic burden to baseline levels in stressed mice.
Glucocorticoid‑treated mice displayed higher Ki‑67 positivity in dormant disseminated tumor cells, but metastasis required concurrent T‑cell depletion.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Glucocorticoid‑induced NET formation acts as an accelerant for metastasis but is counteracted by adaptive T‑cell immunity.
Mifepristone blockade of glucocorticoid signaling may have context‑dependent effects across tumor types.

combination_strategies.rationale: Degrade NET DNA to prevent ECM remodeling and metastatic niche formation
combination_strategies.phenotype: Restores metastasis rates to control levels in stressed mice

combination_strategies.rationale: Block glucocorticoid receptor signaling in neutrophils
combination_strategies.phenotype: Prevents stress‑induced NET formation and metastasis in mouse models

open_questions: How do glucocorticoid levels in cancer patients (endogenous vs exogenous) quantitatively relate to NET burden and metastatic risk?
Can pharmacologic NET inhibition improve outcomes when combined with checkpoint blockade in stressed patients?
What are the downstream signaling pathways linking GR activation to NETosis in neutrophils?

cited_papers: He et al. 2024 Nat Cancer (stress‑CAF work)
Rogers et al. 2024 Cancer Res (glucocorticoid‑NET study)

talk_title: Social stressors associate with accelerated epigenetic aging in Black breast cancer survivors

biomarkers.name: DunedinPACE epigenetic age acceleration
biomarkers.finding: Higher acceleration (mean 1.3) associated with lower income, higher neighborhood disinvestment, higher BMI, and smoking (p<0.1)

models.name: Women's Circle of Health Follow‑up Study
models.genotype_or_key_features: 312 African American/Black women with invasive breast cancer, blood draws ~2 yr and ~3.5 yr post‑diagnosis

clinical_data.metric: Mean change in DunedinPACE between draws

MOA_summary: Chronic exposure to socioeconomic and built‑environment stressors (low income, neighborhood disinvestment) correlates with accelerated DNA‑methylation aging (DunedinPACE) in breast cancer survivors, suggesting that systemic stress pathways may hasten biological aging and potentially influence cancer outcomes.
readouts: Google Street View virtual audits for built environment
key_findings: Mean DunedinPACE was 1.3 (accelerated aging) two years after breast cancer diagnosis.
Lower income‑to‑poverty ratio, higher perceived physical disorder, and greater objective neighborhood disinvestment correlated positively with epigenetic age acceleration (p≤0.1).
BMI and current smoking showed the strongest independent associations with accelerated aging (smoking largest effect size).
Repeated measures in 38 participants showed a modest overall deceleration of epigenetic age over time, but the effect of social stressors attenuated at the later time point.
Chemotherapy receipt also influenced epigenetic age acceleration, suggesting treatment may mediate stress‑related aging effects.
open_questions: Do interventions that reduce neighborhood disinvestment or improve socioeconomic conditions reverse epigenetic age acceleration?
What is the causal pathway linking social stressors to DNA‑methylation changes in cancer survivors?
How do treatment‑related factors (e.g., chemotherapy) interact with social stressors to modulate biological aging?

cited_papers: Cole SR. Conserved transcriptional response to adversity. Nat Rev Immunol 2019
Welms L et al. Meta‑analysis of social stressors and epigenetic clocks. preprint 2024

talk_title: Accelerating Molecular Glue Degrader Discovery and Selective Chemical Probe Development

targets.dependency_context: AML cell lines and PDX models

MOA_summary: Fischer described a high‑throughput degradomics pipeline that couples data‑independent acquisition mass spectrometry with affinity‑purification MS to identify molecular glue degraders. Structural biology (cryo‑EM) and predictive algorithms enable rapid (≈6 weeks) discovery of selective degraders, exemplified by a cereblon‑recruited KAT2A degrader that shows no off‑target degradation and induces loss of KAT2A and H3K9ac in AML PDX models.
key_findings: Cost per molecule for degradomics dropped from >$20,000 in 2012 to < $500 using DIA‑MS and automation.
Discovery timeline from initial screen to high‑resolution cryo‑EM structure reduced to ~6 weeks.
A selective KAT2A degrader was identified that does not degrade the paralog KAT2B or off‑target GSPD1/2.
In an aggressive AML PDX model, treatment with the KAT2A degrader caused complete loss of KAT2A protein and H3K9 acetylation, confirming on‑target activity.
Structural analysis revealed a unique tyrosine‑mediated interface on cereblon that confers selectivity for KAT2A over KAT2B.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity is achieved by exploiting a single tyrosine interaction on cereblon and auxiliary loops that discriminate KAT2A from KAT2B.

talk_title: Structural Basis of PI3‑Kinase Activation by Membrane Recruitment, RAS, and Multivalent RTK Scaffolds

models.name: nanodisc with PIP2 substrate
models.genotype_or_key_features: membrane‑embedded PIP2 to capture active site

models.name: nanodisc with HER3 phospho‑tails
models.genotype_or_key_features: dual phosphotyrosine motifs for multivalent recruitment

MOA_summary: Dzura showed that membrane recruitment of PI3K via farnesylated KRAS and phospho‑RTK tails drives stepwise displacement of autoinhibitory domains, exposing a previously unseen C‑terminal SH2 domain that initially blocks full activation. Inclusion of PIP2 in the nanodisc induces a conformational swing of helix‑12, enabling substrate capture. The common oncogenic H1047R mutation likely destabilizes the helix‑12 hinge, favoring the active conformation. Multivalent HER3 phospho‑tails further promote dimerization of the holoenzyme, creating a highly active, membrane‑anchored state resistant to re‑autoinhibition.
readouts: Western blot for H3K9ac (downstream PD marker)
key_findings: Cryo‑EM of PI3K bound to farnesylated KRAS on nanodiscs revealed progressive loss of regulatory domains (auto‑inhibitory N‑SH2 and iSH2) across three structural classes.
A previously unobserved C‑terminal SH2 domain contacts the membrane and acts as an additional checkpoint before full activation.
Inclusion of PIP2 in the nanodisc reorients the holoenzyme, forming a true helix‑12 that swings 180° and directly engages substrate PIP2 in the active site – the first such observation.
