The H1047R oncogenic mutation likely disrupts a hinge interaction (His1047‑Trp1051), facilitating helix‑12 swing and membrane association, explaining its activating effect.
Multivalent HER3 phospho‑tails induce a dimeric PI3K configuration at the nanodisc edge, preventing re‑assembly of the auto‑inhibited state and representing a fully active dimeric species.
open_questions: What is the precise molecular identity of the extra density assigned to the C‑terminal SH2 domain, and how does its membrane interaction regulate activation?
Does membrane curvature influence the propensity of PI3K to dimerize, and how might this affect signaling in different cellular contexts?

talk_title: Conformational Landscapes of Protein Kinases Reveal Low‑Populated States for Selective Inhibition

models.name: solution NMR of Src family kinases
models.genotype_or_key_features: active, inactive, and intermediate conformations (~5‑10% population)

MOA_summary: Kalodimos demonstrated that protein kinases sample multiple low‑populated conformational states in solution. Using high‑field NMR, three states were characterized for ABL (active, inactive‑1, inactive‑2) and for Src (active, fully inactive, and a novel intermediate). The intermediate state exhibits steric clashes with ADP, explaining its role in rapid ADP release and processive phosphorylation. Gleevec’s selectivity arises from binding the rare inactive‑2 state of ABL, and resistance mutations destabilize this state. Structural insight into these invisible states enables rational design of highly selective inhibitors via virtual docking of billions of compounds or experimental DEL screening.
key_findings: ABL kinase populates three conformations: a ground active state and two low‑populated inactive states (~5% each).
Gleevec binds selectively to the inactive‑2 state, explaining its high specificity and the mechanism of many resistance mutations that destabilize this state.
Src family kinases also sample an intermediate conformation (~5%) that has markedly reduced ADP affinity, identifying it as the key state for rapid ADP release during processive phosphorylation.
Mutations that destabilize the intermediate state abolish processive phosphorylation by Src and Lck, confirming its functional relevance.
VirtualFlow docking of >70 billion compounds against the inactive‑2 ABL structure yielded nanomolar inhibitors with promising selectivity profiles.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Resistance mutations in ABL (e.g., T315I) act by depleting the inactive‑2 population rather than directly blocking Gleevec binding.
open_questions: Can the identified intermediate state be stabilized pharmacologically across other kinases to achieve broad selectivity?
What are the kinetic and energetic determinants that govern the population distribution of these low‑populated states in vivo?

talk_title: CAR T‑cell consolidation therapy for older ALL patients and BAFF‑R CAR for CD19‑negative relapse

speaker.name: Steve Forman
speaker.affiliation: City of Hope, Department of Hematology and Oncology, Duarte, CA

biomarkers.name: PH positivity
biomarkers.finding: Patients with PH‑positive disease (n=10) achieved molecular remission after BAFF‑R CAR infusion

biomarkers.name: MRD (ClonoSEQ)
biomarkers.finding: Two molecular relapses detected by ClonoSEQ; both patients were retreated and proceeded to transplant

models.name: Phase 1 CD19 CAR consolidation trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: Adults >55 yr, in first remission after standard chemotherapy, prior blinatumomab exposure

models.name: First‑in‑human BAFF‑R CAR trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: Relapsed CD19‑negative ALL or B‑cell lymphoma, BAFF‑R expression ≥10% by IHC

clinical_data.population: Adults with relapsed ALL receiving CD19 CAR
CD19 CAR in first remission (low disease burden)
Older ALL patients treated with CD19 CAR consolidation

clinical_data.metric: Objective response (CR/PR) in BAFF‑R CAR lymphoma cohort
clinical_data.population: Patients with active B‑cell lymphoma receiving BAFF‑R CAR

MOA_summary: CD19 CAR T cells with a naive‑phenotype enrichment expand, contract, and persist for 4–5 months, trafficking to CSF and providing durable remission in older ALL patients when given in first remission; BAFF‑R CAR T cells target an essential B‑cell survival receptor, showing expansion, low neurotoxicity, and clinical activity in CD19‑negative relapsed B‑cell malignancies.
key_findings: In a cohort of 48 older ALL patients, CD19 CAR achieved 90% complete remission with no dose‑limiting toxicities and markedly reduced grade ≥2 CRS (17% vs 80% in relapse setting).
CAR T cells were detectable in CSF of every treated patient, indicating CNS trafficking and no observed CNS relapses.
Two molecular relapses were identified by ClonoSEQ; both patients were successfully retreated and proceeded to allogeneic transplant.
First‑in‑human BAFF‑R CAR demonstrated expansion comparable to CD19 CAR, grade 1 toxicities only, and ≥70% objective responses in lymphoma patients.
BAFF‑R expression persisted in ~66% of CD19‑negative ALL relapses, supporting its role as a salvage target.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: CD19 loss or epitope alteration accounted for ~30% of post‑blinatumomab relapses, necessitating BAFF‑R targeting.
Variable BAFF‑R antigen density in ALL (≈80% of cases) may limit efficacy in low‑expressors.

combination_strategies.rationale: Enhance CAR T‑cell engraftment and expansion
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved in vivo persistence and reduced toxicity

open_questions: Will longer‑term follow‑up confirm durability of remission beyond 2 years in older ALL patients?
Can BAFF‑R CAR be combined with CD19 CAR or bispecific antibodies to prevent antigen escape?

talk_title: Engineering TCR‑engineered T cells to overcome TGF‑β–driven dysfunction in solid tumors

speaker.name: Phil Greenberg
speaker.affiliation: Fred Hutchinson Cancer Research Center, Seattle, WA

targets.dependency_context: renal cell carcinoma, ovarian, pancreatic, glioblastoma

biomarkers.name: ctDNA
biomarkers.finding: Complete clearance in 2 of 4 KRAS‑TCR patients; relapse observed at day 60–100 in one patient

biomarkers.name: CEA
biomarkers.finding: Marked decline correlating with tumor shrinkage in KRAS‑TCR treated pancreatic cancer patients

models.name: KRAS G12V TCR trial (A11‑restricted)
models.genotype_or_key_features: HLA‑A2+, KRAS G12V+, n=4 treated, dose‑escalation

models.name: Mesothelin TCR trial (pancreatic cancer)
models.genotype_or_key_features: 9 patients, stable disease in 6, no PR/CR

models.name: KPC genetically engineered mouse model
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS G12D; Trp53 R172H; pancreatic adenocarcinoma

models.name: PDX models of RCC, ovarian, pancreatic, glioblastoma

clinical_data.value: ≥30% reduction in target lesion in 2 of 4 KRAS‑TCR patients
high in tumor‑resident engineered T cells vs low in blood

MOA_summary: Engineered TCR T cells targeting mesothelin or KRAS G12V infiltrate pancreatic tumors but acquire a TGF‑β–driven, mitigated exhaustion program; CRISPR knockout of the TGF‑β‑responsive transcription factor IKZF3 (Aeolus) reduces both TGF‑β and exhaustion signatures but induces apoptosis, which can be rescued by synthetic fusion proteins delivering pro‑survival signals. HIT CARs replace the scFv with antibody heavy/light chains to create HLA‑independent, ultra‑sensitive receptors that eradicate low‑density CD70‑expressing tumors in PDX models, outperforming conventional CARs.
key_findings: Mesothelin‑specific TCR T cells showed tumor infiltration but displayed a dominant TGF‑β–driven exhaustion signature distinct from blood T cells.

IKZF3 (Aeolus) knockout eliminated the TGF‑β signature and mitigated exhaustion but caused activation‑induced cell death, which was rescued by engineered fusion proteins delivering IL‑2 or FasL‑derived survival signals.
HIT CD70 CARs demonstrated superior sensitivity, achieving tumor eradication in renal cell carcinoma PDXs with low CD70 expression where conventional CARs failed.
KRAS G12V‑specific TCRs engineered with CD8αβ and CD28 co‑stimulation induced durable tumor regressions and biomarker (CEA, ctDNA) declines in 3 of 4 patients.
Spatial analysis revealed that exhausted engineered T cells reside in tumor cores surrounded by TGF‑β, whereas functional T cells localize to tertiary lymphoid structures near vasculature.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: TGF‑β in the pancreatic TME drives a unique exhaustion program not seen in melanoma TILs.
CD70 expression is epigenetically silenced in kidney‑site RCC PDXs via EZH2‑mediated H3K27me3, leading to antigen loss and resistance to conventional CARs.

combination_strategies.rationale: Restore avidity of CD4‑engineered TCRs to match CD8 T cells
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced tumor killing and cytokine production in vitro

combination_strategies.rationale: Provide secondary signal to CD4 TCR‑engineered cells
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved persistence and tumor clearance in mouse models

combination_strategies.with_agent: Synthetic fusion proteins (e.g., TGF‑β/IL‑2 chimeric receptor)
combination_strategies.rationale: Convert suppressive TGF‑β signals into proliferative IL‑2 signals
combination_strategies.phenotype: Sustained cytotoxicity and proliferation over 150 h in vitro

open_questions: Can Aeolus knockout combined with survival‑signal fusion proteins achieve durable persistence without apoptosis in patients?
What is the optimal balance of TGF‑β signaling to maintain stemness versus exhaustion in solid‑tumor TCR therapies?
Will HIT CARs maintain specificity and safety in vivo given their ultra‑high sensitivity to low antigen density?

talk_title: Novel CAR designs: 1XX, HIT, and 1A platforms for enhanced potency and sensitivity

targets.dependency_context: renal cell carcinoma, ovarian, pancreatic, glioblastoma
renal cell carcinoma, ovarian, breast, glioblastoma

clinical_data.population: Patients with refractory DLBCL receiving 25 M 1XX CAR T cells

clinical_data.metric: Tumor burden reduction (HIT CD70 CAR, RCC PDX)
clinical_data.value: Complete eradication in kidney model at 5–10 M cells
clinical_data.population: Renal cell carcinoma PDX with low CD70

MOA_summary: The 1XX CAR removes two of three CD3ζ ITAMs, delivering a calibrated activation signal that enables durable complete remissions with a 25‑million‑cell dose and reduced cytokine toxicity; HIT CARs fuse antibody heavy/light chains to a TCR scaffold, creating HLA‑independent receptors with ultra‑high antigen sensitivity that overcome low‑density CD70 expression; 1A CAR incorporates the pre‑TCR cytoplasmic domain, driving YBX1‑mediated global translation enhancement, resulting in potent, long‑lasting effector function with minimal exhaustion.
key_findings: 1XX CD19 CAR achieved a 75% complete remission rate in refractory DLBCL using only 25 M cells, with markedly lower CRS/ICANS compared to standard products.
HIT CD70 CAR eradicated renal cell carcinoma PDXs with low CD70 expression, whereas conventional CD70 CARs failed even at 10 M cell doses.
1A CD70 CAR outperformed classic 28ζ CARs in orthotopic kidney, ovarian, breast, and glioblastoma models, showing sustained tumor control and reduced exhaustion markers.
YBX1 phosphorylation uniquely sustained translation efficiency in 1A CAR T cells, linking enhanced ribosomal biogenesis to prolonged effector function.
Epigenetic silencing (EZH2‑mediated H3K27me3) of CD70 in kidney‑site tumors creates a microenvironment‑dependent antigen loss that is overcome by the heightened sensitivity of HIT CARs.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: HIT CARs’ ultra‑sensitivity may increase risk of on‑target off‑tumor effects; low CD70 expression detected in esophageal epithelium raises safety considerations.
1XX CAR’s reduced ITAM signaling mitigates cytokine release but may limit maximal cytotoxic potency in high‑antigen‑density settings.

combination_strategies.rationale: Prevent epigenetic silencing of CD70 to enhance antigen availability for HIT CARs
combination_strategies.phenotype: Increased CD70 surface density and improved tumor clearance in RCC PDX

combination_strategies.with_agent: IL‑2 cytokine support via TGF‑β/IL‑2 chimeric receptor
combination_strategies.rationale: Provide pro‑survival signaling to 1XX or 1A CAR T cells
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced persistence and reduced apoptosis in mouse models

open_questions: Will the 1XX CAR’s lower activation threshold be sufficient for high‑antigen‑density tumors without compromising efficacy?
What safety monitoring is required for HIT CARs given potential low‑level CD70 expression in normal tissues such as esophagus?
Can YBX1 modulation be therapeutically leveraged to boost 1A CAR function without inducing off‑target translation effects?

talk_title: Evolution of PARP inhibitors and lessons for synthetic lethality development

biomarkers.name: RAD51 foci
biomarkers.finding: Low RAD51 foci levels correlated with tumor regression in PARP inhibitor-treated patient-derived models

biomarkers.name: HRD genomic scar
biomarkers.finding: HRD scar associated with improved PFS on olaparib maintenance in first-line ovarian cancer

models.name: patient-derived xenograft cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: various HR alterations, RAD51 foci measured

MOA_summary: PARP inhibitors trap PARP1 on DNA, preventing repair of single-strand breaks that convert to double-strand breaks during replication; in HR-deficient tumors (e.g., BRCA1/2 loss) this leads to lethal genomic instability, while normal cells retain homologous recombination capacity, providing a therapeutic window. Trapping potency, dose intensity, and duration of target inhibition dictate clinical efficacy.
key_findings: PARP1 trapping, not just catalytic inhibition, is essential for monotherapy activity; talazoparib and sarucaparib show stronger trapping and greater efficacy than rucaparib.
Complete (>90%) PARP inhibition is required for tumor regression; sub‑therapeutic dosing leads to tumor escape and resistance.
BRCA1/2 mutation type influences response; hypomorphic C61G BRCA1 mutants confer reduced sensitivity compared to null alleles.
Acquired resistance is driven by HR re‑activation, evidenced by RAD51 foci re‑appearance in >90% of resistant models.
Dose‑intensity correlates with long‑term tumor control; continuous high‑dose olaparib prevents escape, whereas intermittent dosing increases residual tumor burden.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: PARP1 L77P mutation abolishes trapping and confers resistance to olaparib and talazoparib.
Reversion mutations restoring HR function lead to RAD51 foci resurgence and PARP inhibitor resistance.

combination_strategies.rationale: Synergistic DNA damage in HRD tumors
combination_strategies.phenotype: enhanced tumor regression in preclinical models

combination_strategies.rationale: Potential synthetic lethal interaction in BRCA‑mutant context
combination_strategies.phenotype: preclinical synergy observed

open_questions: What is the optimal dosing schedule to maximize PARP trapping while minimizing toxicity?
Can functional HRD assays (e.g., STRIDE) replace static genomic scar tests for patient selection?

talk_title: Targeting ARID1A/ARID1B synthetic lethality and SMARCA2/SMARCA4 paralog dependencies
MOA_summary: Selective degradation of ARID1B via VHL or cereblon-recruited PROTACs eliminates the compensatory paralog in ARID1A-deficient cancers, leading to loss of BAF complex function and tumor cell death, while sparing normal cells that retain ARID1A. Similarly, inhibition of SMARCA2 in SMARCA4-mutant tumors exploits paralog synthetic lethality, causing chromatin remodeling failure and tumor regression, with enhanced anti‑tumor immunity when combined with PD‑1 blockade.
key_findings: ARID1B degraders achieve nanomolar potency and >10‑fold selectivity over ARID1A in biochemical and cellular assays.
Cereblon‑based ARID1B degraders show superior potency and no off‑target neo‑substrate activity across the proteome.
Ternary complex crystal structures reveal a ligand twist that enhances cooperativity, informing SAR for both VHL and CRBN series.
SMARCA2 inhibitor LY4050784 (909) combined with anti‑PD‑1 yields 6/6 complete responses in a BRG1‑knockout syngeneic mouse model and induces durable immune memory.
Combination of SMARCA2 inhibition with PD‑1 blockade is well tolerated (no weight loss) and produces sustained tumor control.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: ARID1B degraders show a clear hook effect at high concentrations, requiring careful dose optimization.
No degradation of ARID1A observed, confirming paralog selectivity.

combination_strategies.rationale: SMARCA2 inhibition may increase tumor immunogenicity in SMARCA4‑mutant cold tumors
combination_strategies.phenotype: complete tumor regressions and immune memory in mouse model

open_questions: Can ARID1B degraders achieve sufficient oral bioavailability for clinical use?
What biomarkers best predict response to SMARCA2 inhibition beyond SMARCA4 loss?

cited_papers: Ballen et al. 2026 Nature Chemical Biology

talk_title: Quantitative molecular imaging to enable personalized radiopharmaceutical therapy

speaker.name: Dr. Delphine Chen
speaker.affiliation: Fred Hutch Cancer Center, Department of Nuclear Medicine and Radiology

biomarkers.name: SSTR uptake relative to liver
biomarkers.finding: Patients with uptake ≥ liver were eligible for lutetium-177 dotatate; post‑hoc analysis showed similar benefit across uptake levels

biomarkers.name: PSMA PET SUV quartile
biomarkers.finding: Higher SUV quartile correlated with improved radiographic PFS and OS in VISION trial

biomarkers.name: FDG metabolic tumor volume
biomarkers.finding: Lower FDG MTV associated with better response to lutetium PSMA therapy

MOA_summary: Quantitative PET/SPECT imaging of SSTR and PSMA ligands enables patient selection and dosimetry for beta‑emitting radiopharmaceuticals; higher tumor uptake (SUV) predicts better outcomes, while FDG metabolic burden inversely correlates with response, supporting personalized dosing and treatment planning.
key_findings: Post‑hoc NETTER‑1/2 analyses showed similar survival benefit across low, intermediate, and high somatostatin receptor uptake levels.
VISION trial post‑hoc quartile analysis demonstrated that patients in the highest PSMA PET SUV quartile had the best radiographic PFS and OS.
Low FDG metabolic tumor volume predicted superior response to lutetium‑PSMA therapy, independent of PSMA uptake intensity.
Single‑time‑point dosimetry at ~24 h can estimate tumor absorbed dose, but lesion doses decrease with successive cycles.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Approximately 30% of VISION patients were non‑responders, associated with low PSMA uptake, TP53 mutations, or discordant FDG‑positive/PSMA‑negative disease.
Somatostatin analog co‑administration can increase tumor SUV by reducing background liver/spleen activity.

combination_strategies.rationale: enhance tumor radiosensitivity and increase absorbed dose
combination_strategies.phenotype: improved response in subset of patients receiving sensitizer

combination_strategies.rationale: reduce background organ uptake, improve tumor‑to‑background ratio
combination_strategies.phenotype: higher tumor SUV observed

open_questions: What is the optimal imaging time point for accurate dosimetry across different radiopharmaceuticals?
Can standardized single‑time‑point dosimetry replace multi‑time‑point protocols without loss of predictive power?

talk_title: Clinical advances and opportunities in prostate cancer using alpha and beta emitting radiopharmaceuticals

speaker.name: Dr. Neil Tonk
speaker.affiliation: University of Pennsylvania, Department of Radiation Oncology

biomarkers.name: PSMA PET SUV
biomarkers.finding: Uptake above liver required for VISION eligibility; higher SUV predicts better response

biomarkers.name: FDG PET discordance
biomarkers.finding: FDG‑positive/PSMA‑negative lesions associated with poorer outcomes

biomarkers.name: TP53 mutation
biomarkers.finding: Somatic TP53 mutations correlated with reduced benefit from PSMA‑RPT

models.name: PSMA‑ADDITION
models.genotype_or_key_features: hormone‑sensitive prostate cancer on ADT + ARPI

clinical_data.population: heavily pretreated APMR receiving actinium‑225 PSMA

MOA_summary: Beta‑emitting lutetium‑177 PSMA‑617 delivers cross‑fire radiation to PSMA‑positive prostate cancer lesions, improving OS and PFS; alpha‑emitting actinium‑225 PSMA provides high‑LET, short‑range DNA double‑strand breaks, achieving deep PSA responses but with high salivary gland toxicity. Combination strategies aim to exploit complementary mechanisms and overcome resistance.
key_findings: VISION trial showed a 4‑month OS benefit (median 11 mo vs 7 mo) for lutetium‑177 PSMA‑617 in APMR.
Approximately 30% of patients were non‑responders, linked to low PSMA uptake, FDG‑PSMA discordance, or TP53 mutations.
Actinium‑225 PSMA therapy yielded median OS 16 mo in heavily pretreated patients, with 70% experiencing xerostomia after one cycle.
Early‑line trials (PSMA‑4, PSMA‑ADDITION) improve radiographic PFS but have not yet shown OS advantage, likely due to crossover.
Alpha emitters may overcome radioresistance in high‑grade disease and micrometastases, while beta emitters are suited for bulk disease.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Low PSMA expression and FDG‑positive/PSMA‑negative lesions predict resistance to PSMA‑RPT.
TP53 somatic mutations associated with poorer outcomes.

combination_strategies.rationale: enhance radiation‑induced DNA damage
combination_strategies.phenotype: preclinical synergy, ongoing phase 1 trials

combination_strategies.rationale: potential immunogenic cell death from high‑LET alpha radiation
combination_strategies.phenotype: early phase trials exploring safety and response

combination_strategies.rationale: sequential therapy to target AR‑driven disease before/after RPT
combination_strategies.phenotype: PSMA‑4 trial showed delayed progression

open_questions: Optimal sequencing of alpha versus beta radiopharmaceuticals in the same patient.
How to mitigate salivary gland toxicity from actinium‑225 PSMA.

talk_title: Preclinical and translational strategies to combine radiopharmaceuticals with other cancer therapies

speaker.name: Dr. Zach Morris
speaker.affiliation: University of Wisconsin, Department of Radiation Medicine

targets.dependency_context: broad tumor types via membrane incorporation

models.name: Phase I NM‑600 + checkpoint inhibitor study
models.genotype_or_key_features: recurrent head and neck cancer, renal dose limit 23 Gy

MOA_summary: NM‑600 incorporates into tumor cell membranes, delivering Y‑90 beta radiation; when combined with checkpoint blockade, sub‑maximal tumor doses preserve immune cells and enhance systemic anti‑tumor immunity. Astatine‑211 PARP‑targeted alpha therapy induces DNA double‑strand breaks and up‑regulates PARP1, creating a positive feedback loop that increases tumor uptake and promotes immune priming.
key_findings: Personalized dosimetry using NM‑600 allowed subtraction of radiopharmaceutical dose from external beam plans, enabling safe delivery of combined therapy in head‑and‑neck cancer.
Low‑to‑moderate Y‑90 NM‑600 doses synergized with anti‑CTLA‑4, enhancing local and systemic tumor control and generating immune memory.
Alpha‑emitting actinium showed superior immune priming (increased TCR clonality) in poorly immunogenic tumors compared with beta emitters.

Heterogeneous intratumoral dose distribution (mixed high/low uptake) activated multiple immune pathways (type‑I IFN, MHC up‑regulation) and improved survival in mouse models, an effect lost with homogeneous dosing.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: High‑dose beta radiation can cause systemic lymphopenia, reducing checkpoint inhibitor efficacy.
Fractionated dosing mitigates GI toxicity for astatine‑211 agents in preclinical models.

combination_strategies.rationale: Additive tumor dose while sparing overlapping normal tissues
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved local control in recurrent head‑and‑neck cancer

combination_strategies.rationale: Radiation‑induced immunogenic cell death enhances checkpoint efficacy
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression in preclinical models

combination_strategies.rationale: Low‑dose radiopharmaceuticals increase antigen presentation without depleting T cells
combination_strategies.phenotype: Strong synergistic responses in solid tumor xenografts

open_questions: What is the optimal dose heterogeneity profile to maximize immune priming while preserving normal tissue?
How should dosing intervals be timed between radiopharmaceuticals and immunotherapies to avoid lymphocyte depletion?

cited_papers: Patel et al. 2024 Cancer Immunology Research

talk_title: Non-enzymatic HDAC-mediated repression reveals selective stress adaptive mechanisms for cancer therapy
MOA_summary: This talk describes the discovery and characterization of E6R, a non-enzymatic inhibitor of the Sin3-Rpd3L (HDAC1/2) complex in yeast and neuroblastoma cells. Unlike traditional HDAC inhibitors, E6R disrupts protein-protein interfaces, leading to a distinct transcriptomic signature. In neuroblastoma, E6R induces cell cycle arrest via p53/E2F signaling and integrated stress response, achieving therapeutic effects without the broad cytotoxicity of enzymatic HDAC inhibitors.
key_findings: E6R is a non-enzymatic inhibitor of the Sin3-Rpd3L complex, identified through a yeast-based reporter assay screening 52,000 compounds.
E6R exhibits a distinct transcriptomic signature compared to enzymatic HDAC inhibitors (TSA) and complex knockouts (Rpd3, Sin3).
In neuroblastoma cells, E6R reduces invasion without significant cytotoxicity, unlike TSA.
E6R affects 14x fewer genes than TSA in neuroblastoma cells, indicating higher transcriptional selectivity.
E6R induces cell cycle arrest via p53/E2F signaling and repression of G2M checkpoint genes.
E6R specifically activates integrated stress response and pro-apoptotic pathways, including ATF4 targets like DDIT3 and GDF15.
Single-cell RNA sequencing shows E6R treatment mirrors DMSO control in cell state composition.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: E6R shows a broad tolerance window in neuroblastoma cells, unlike TSA which is cytotoxic at low concentrations.
open_questions: Is ATF4 activity a direct outcome of SIN3 regulation or an indirect effect?

talk_title: Molecular Glue Degrader of the ARNT Protein

targets.dependency_context: VHL-deficient clear cell renal cell carcinoma

biomarkers.name: ARNT expression
biomarkers.finding: Knockdown of ARNT leads to tumor growth regression in A498 system.

biomarkers.name: HIF-2 alpha pathway modulation
biomarkers.finding: NEO811 treatment modulates VEGF and NDRG1.

models.name: 7860 xenograft system
models.genotype_or_key_features: VHL deficient, relies on HIF-2 alpha signaling

models.name: XP490 PDX line
models.genotype_or_key_features: low HIF-2 alpha, high HIF-1 alpha, resistant to HIF-2 alpha inhibitors

MOA_summary: NEO811 is a potent and selective molecular glue degrader of ARNT (HIF1 beta) for clear cell renal cell carcinoma (ccRCC). ARNT is central to HIF signaling, angiogenesis, proliferation, and immune suppression. NEO811 demonstrates dose-dependent tumor growth inhibition as a single agent, shows synergistic effects with belzutifan, and retains activity in models of acquired and intrinsic resistance to HIF-2 alpha inhibitors, supporting its clinical development.
key_findings: ARNT knockdown in A498 cells leads to tumor growth regression, validating ARNT as a target in ccRCC.
NEO811 is a potent ARNT degrader with low nanomolar activity, dependent on cereblon, and shows a favorable selectivity window.
NEO811 demonstrates dose-dependent tumor growth inhibition in the 7860 xenograft model, with >50% regression at 3 mg/kg.
NEO811 shows synergistic tumor growth regression when combined with belzutifan.
NEO811 retains activity in a model of acquired resistance to HIF-2 alpha inhibitors (G323E mutation).
NEO811 shows activity in the XP490 PDX model, which is intrinsically resistant to HIF-2 alpha inhibitors.
NEO811 is orally bioavailable and well-tolerated in preclinical studies.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: NEO811 retains activity in models of acquired resistance to HIF-2 alpha inhibitors (G323E mutation).
NEO811 shows activity in a PDX model intrinsically resistant to HIF-2 alpha inhibitors (low HIF-2 alpha, high HIF-1 alpha).

combination_strategies.rationale: Targeting the same pathway (HIF-2 alpha) to potentially hit pathways harder.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Significantly more tumor growth regression than any single agent.

talk_title: Molecular Glue Degrader Targeting the CCNE1 Protein

biomarkers.name: CCNE1 amplification
biomarkers.finding: Activity of MRT55811 is exclusively observed in CCNE1 amplified cell lines.

biomarkers.name: Rb phosphorylation
biomarkers.finding: MRT55811 treatment leads to Rb dephosphorylation.

biomarkers.name: S-phase progression
biomarkers.finding: MRT55811 treatment completely suppresses S-phase.

biomarkers.name: P16 expression
biomarkers.finding: Low P16 expression in CCNE1 amplified cancers correlates with sensitivity to MRT55811.

models.name: MKN1 gastric cancer cell line xenograft
ovarian and breast cancer cell line xenografts

MOA_summary: MRT55811 is a first-in-class molecular glue degrader targeting CCNE1, an oncogene amplified in various cancers. It induces co-degradation of CCNE1 and CDK2, leading to Rb dephosphorylation, cell cycle arrest, and anti-proliferative effects specifically in CCNE1-amplified cancers. This mechanism differentiates it from CDK2 inhibitors and suggests a favorable therapeutic window.
key_findings: MRT55811 is a potent and selective CCNE1 degrader, exclusively active in CCNE1 amplified cell lines.
MRT55811 induces co-degradation of CCNE1 and CDK2, leading to Rb dephosphorylation and S-phase arrest.
The degrader forms a quaternary complex involving CRBN, glue, CCNE1, and CDK2.
MRT55811 shows potent anti-proliferative activity in CCNE1 amplified cell lines, with GI50s in the nanomolar range.
MRT55811 demonstrates tumor regression in xenograft models of CCNE1 amplified gastric, ovarian, and breast cancers.
MRT55811 shows an edge over CDK2 inhibitors in preclinical models.
The compound is well-tolerated in preclinical studies.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: MRT55811 shows no activity in non-amplified cell lines, suggesting high cancer specificity.
The compound's activity is dependent on CCNE1 amplification, differentiating it from CDK2 inhibitors which show broader activity.

talk_title: Molecular Glue Degrader of the RNA Binding Protein HuR

biomarkers.name: HuR degradation
biomarkers.finding: HUR degrader two achieves 96% degradation at 10 uM.

biomarkers.name: BRAF mutation
biomarkers.finding: Strong positive correlation between HuR dependency and BRAF mutation status.

biomarkers.name: Cell viability
biomarkers.finding: HUR degrader selectively reduced cell viability in BRAF mutant CRC cell lines.

biomarkers.name: MAPK signaling
biomarkers.finding: HUR degrader confirmed pathway inhibition in resistant cells.

models.name: CRBN WT and KO cells
models.genotype_or_key_features: Used for screening and validation of CRBN dependency.

models.name: BRAF mutant CRC cell lines
models.genotype_or_key_features: Used for proof of concept study.

models.name: BRAF mutant CRC PDX model
models.genotype_or_key_features: Used for in vivo efficacy studies.

MOA_summary: Degron Therapeutics has developed DG6498, a potent and selective molecular glue degrader of the RNA-binding protein HuR, targeting BRAF-mutant colorectal cancer. HuR is an undruggable oncogenic driver. DG6498 induces CRBN-dependent degradation of HuR, showing efficacy in BRAF-mutant CRC models and overcoming resistance to BRAF inhibitors. The compound has favorable PK/PD properties and is currently in Phase 1 clinical development.
readouts: in vivo tumor growth inhibition assay
key_findings: A molecular glue degrader of HuR was identified through proteomic screening of a proprietary compound library.
HUR degrader two exhibits potent HuR degradation (DC50 = 3.8 nM, 96% max degradation) dependent on CRBN and the proteasome.
Cryo-EM structure reveals the ternary complex formation between CRBN, MGD, and HuR.
HuR dependency is strongly correlated with BRAF mutation status, making BRAF-mutant CRC a key indication.
The HuR degrader selectively reduces cell viability in BRAF-mutant CRC cell lines.
The HuR degrader overcomes resistance to dabrafenib in BRAF-mutant CRC models.
DG6498, a lead compound, shows robust and sustained HuR degradation and favorable PK/ADME properties.
DG6498 is currently in Phase 1 clinical development for BRAF-mutant CRC and other solid tumors.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: The HuR degrader overcomes resistance to dabrafenib in BRAF-mutant CRC models.
BRAF wild-type cell lines remained resistant to HuR degradation despite efficient HuR degradation.

talk_title: BBO10203, a RAS PI3 Breaker, Inhibits Phospho-AKT Signaling in HER2-Amplified Models Through Inhibition of Non-Canonical RAS Signaling

biomarkers.name: Phospho-AKT
biomarkers.finding: BBO10203 shows potent single-digit nanomolar complete suppression of phospho-AKT in HER2-amplified lines.

biomarkers.name: RAS isoform knockdown
biomarkers.finding: Non-canonical RAS isoform RS2 knockdown reduces phospho-AKT, but combination with RS and RS2 is needed for maximal effect.

biomarkers.name: HER2 amplification
biomarkers.finding: HER2-amplified lines are particularly sensitive to disruption of RAS-PI3K interaction.

MOA_summary: BBO10203 is a covalent inhibitor that breaks the RAS-PI3K interaction, specifically targeting HER2-amplified cancers. It potently suppresses phospho-AKT signaling by inhibiting non-canonical RAS isoforms (RS2) and canonical RAS signaling. BBO10203 demonstrates strong monotherapy efficacy and combination potential with HER2-targeted agents, offering a new therapeutic strategy for HER2-amplified tumors.
key_findings: HER2-amplified cell lines are particularly sensitive to disruption of the RAS-PI3K interaction by BBO10203.
BBO10203 achieves potent single-digit nanomolar suppression of phospho-AKT in HER2-amplified lines.
Non-canonical RAS isoforms, particularly RS2, play a significant role in driving phospho-AKT signaling in HER2-amplified cells.
BBO10203 inhibits both non-canonical and canonical RAS signaling pathways.
BBO10203 shows strong monotherapy tumor regression in HER2-amplified KYSC esophageal CDX model.
BBO10203 demonstrates strong combination potential with HER2-targeted agents like tucatinib and trastuzumab.
BBO10203 outperforms tucatinib as a single agent in HER2-amplified N87 CDX model.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Non-canonical RAS proteins play a large and important role in driving the majority of the phospho-AKT signaling, but canonical signaling is still important to inhibit.
HER2 mutant lines resistant to traditional therapies do not show as much combination benefit.

combination_strategies.rationale: Orthogonal inhibition of HER2 signaling.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Strong regressions in BT474 cells and N87 CDX model.

combination_strategies.rationale: Orthogonal inhibition of HER2 signaling.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Strong regressions in BT474 cells and N87 CDX model.

combination_strategies.rationale: Vertical pathway inhibition.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Not specified

combination_strategies.rationale: Vertical pathway inhibition.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Not specified

combination_strategies.rationale: Combination in CRC.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Not specified

open_questions: The exact mechanism for how the breaker interferes with the RS2-HER2 complex formation is an active area of investigation.

talk_title: Catalytic Inhibitors of the KAT2A/B Protein in Multiple Myeloma

biomarkers.name: H3K9 acetylation
biomarkers.finding: KAT2I treatment phenocopies the loss of H3K9 acetylation seen in KAT2A/B double knockout cells.

biomarkers.name: MYC target genes
biomarkers.finding: KAT2I treatment leads to downregulation of MYC target genes.

biomarkers.name: E2F target genes
biomarkers.finding: KAT2I treatment leads to downregulation of E2F target genes.

biomarkers.name: Interferon signatures
biomarkers.finding: KAT2I treatment leads to upregulation of interferon signatures and ISGs.

biomarkers.name: IRF7
biomarkers.finding: KAT2I treatment increases IRF7 and IRF9 protein levels.

models.name: KAT2A/B knockout cell lines
models.genotype_or_key_features: Used to confirm H3K9 acetylation biomarker.

models.name: multiple myeloma syngeneic model
models.genotype_or_key_features: Used for combination studies with immune therapies.

MOA_summary: Pfizer has developed KAT2I, a first-in-class catalytic inhibitor of KAT2A/B, targeting multiple myeloma. KAT2A/B are key components of the SAGA complex, driving oncogenic transcription. KAT2I treatment leads to downregulation of MYC and E2F targets, and upregulates interferon responses, sensitizing myeloma cells to immune killing. The compound shows robust efficacy as monotherapy and in combination with standard of care agents and T-cell engagers.
key_findings: Multiple myeloma cells show a strong dependency on the SAGA complex and KAT2A/B.
KAT2I is a potent, selective, and orally bioavailable catalytic inhibitor of KAT2A/B.
KAT2I treatment leads to dose-dependent reduction of H3K9 acetylation, mimicking genetic loss of KAT2A/B.
KAT2I shows broad activity across a panel of multiple myeloma cell lines.
KAT2I downregulates MYC and E2F target genes, contributing to tumor-intrinsic response.
KAT2I upregulates interferon signatures and ISGs, potentially sensitizing tumors to immune cell killing.
KAT2I demonstrates synergistic activity with lenalidomide and pomalidomide in vitro.
Combination of KAT2I with pomalidomide and dexamethasone shows benefit in the OPM2 xenograft model.
KAT2I enhances the efficacy of a BCMA CD3 bispecific antibody in a multiple myeloma syngeneic model.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: SAGA complex and KAT2A/B show selective dependencies in hematopoietic lineages, including multiple myeloma.

combination_strategies.rationale: Standard of care in multiple myeloma.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic scores observed in vitro.

combination_strategies.rationale: Standard of care in multiple myeloma.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic scores observed in vitro; combination benefit in OPM2 xenograft.

combination_strategies.rationale: Standard of care in multiple myeloma.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Combination benefit in OPM2 xenograft.

combination_strategies.rationale: Enhance T-cell mediated killing.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Extended tumor growth delay and improved survival in syngeneic model.

open_questions: Mechanism of IRF activation after KAT2A/B inhibition is under investigation, potentially involving non-resolution of R-loops.

talk_title: Discovery of a New Class of Mutant-Targeted Catalytic RAS Oncogene Inhibitors

biomarkers.name: RAS GTP levels
biomarkers.finding: RMO-55 produces potent and near complete reduction in cellular RAS GTP levels.

biomarkers.name: Phospho-ERK
biomarkers.finding: RMO-55 leads to potent reduction in phospho-ERK levels.

biomarkers.name: Cell proliferation
biomarkers.finding: RMO-55 robustly inhibits proliferation in G12 mutant cell lines, with greater effect than deraxasip.

biomarkers.name: KRAS amplification
biomarkers.finding: RMO-55 shows activity in KRAS amplified models and overcomes resistance to deraxasip.

models.name: G12 mutant cell lines
models.genotype_or_key_features: Panel of over 800 cell lines profiled.

models.name: G12C non-small cell lung cancer model
KRAS overexpressing cell lines
models.genotype_or_key_features: Used to model KRAS amplification resistance.

models.name: HaCaT keratinocyte cell line
models.genotype_or_key_features: Normal skin cell line used for selectivity assessment.

MOA_summary: RMO-55 is a novel catalytic RAS inhibitor that mimics natural GAPs by stimulating GTP hydrolysis of oncogenic RAS G12 mutants, restoring them to the GDP state. This mechanism leads to potent inhibition of RAS signaling and cell proliferation, with a wider therapeutic window than steric inhibitors. RMO-55 demonstrates deep and durable tumor regressions in preclinical models and shows activity in settings of acquired resistance, supporting its clinical development.
key_findings: RMO-55 is a new class of mutant-targeted catalytic RAS inhibitor that stimulates GTP hydrolysis of G12 mutant RAS.
RMO-55 produces rapid and near-complete reduction in G12V GTP levels biochemically.
RMO-55 causes potent and near complete reduction in cellular RAS GTP levels and phospho-ERK.
RMO-55 robustly inhibits proliferation in G12 mutant cell lines, with greater efficacy than deraxasip.
RMO-55 shows a reduced effect on wild-type cell lines compared to deraxasip.
RMO-55 demonstrates potent inhibition of tumor RAS pathway signaling with significant selectivity over normal tissue.
RMO-55 drives deep and durable tumor regressions in G12D pancreatic, G12C NSCLC, and G12V colorectal models.
RMO-55 shows activity in KRAS amplified models and overcomes resistance to deraxasip.
RMO-55 restores tumor control in tumors that have acquired resistance to RAS on inhibitors.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: RMO-55 shows a reduced effect on wild-type cell lines compared to deraxasip.
RMO-55 shows activity in KRAS amplified models and overcomes resistance to deraxasip.
RMO-55 is active in settings of acquired resistance to RAS on multi-selective inhibition.

combination_strategies.rationale: Investigating combination potential.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Not specified

open_questions: The exact mechanism of how RMO-55 interferes with the complex formation is an active area of investigation.
Exploring resistance mechanisms to RMO-55, including sensitivity to SIPA expression levels.

talk_title: CQ0736 polysaccharide‑based KRAS‑targeted therapy via macropinocytosis

targets.dependency_context: KRAS‑mutant lung, pancreatic and colorectal CDX/PDX models

MOA_summary: CQ0736 is a dextran‑based conjugate bearing docetaxel and gamma‑linolenic acid that exploits enhanced macropinocytosis in KRAS‑driven tumors for selective tumor accumulation; the conjugate releases docetaxel intracellularly, inducing apoptosis while avoiding P‑gp efflux, resulting in durable tumor regression across KRAS G12C, G12D and wild‑type models.
key_findings: CQ0736 achieved sustained intratumoral docetaxel exposure for 96 h versus free DTX.
In KRAS G12C lung CDX, weekly CQ0736 reduced tumor volume 3.6‑fold vs RMC and 5.2‑fold vs adagrasib at day 28.
CQ0736 showed durable tumor control without resistance in KRAS G12C PDX (16.5‑fold reduction vs RMC at day 49).
No significant body‑weight loss or hematologic toxicity observed across CDX/PDX studies.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Reduced P‑gp mediated efflux via gamma‑linolenic acid
Macropinocytosis‑mediated uptake 5‑10 × higher in KRAS‑mutant vs wild‑type cells
open_questions: What is the exact half‑life of CQ0736 in humans?
Can the polysaccharide platform be adapted to other cytotoxic payloads?

talk_title: Orally bioavailable small‑molecule MYC translation inhibitors (WDC‑623)

biomarkers.name: c-MYC protein level
biomarkers.finding: Reduced >100‑fold potency to single‑digit nM (WDC‑623) versus ~1 µM for hit

clinical_data.value: significant inhibition at 5 mg/kg BID oral

MOA_summary: WDC‑623 binds to a regulatory element in the MYC 5'‑UTR, stabilizing an inhibitory RNA structure that blocks translation initiation; rapid loss of MYC protein (half‑life ~30 min) leads to selective repression of MYC‑driven transcriptional programs while sparing non‑MYC short‑half‑life proteins.
key_findings: Structure‑guided hit‑to‑lead improved potency >100‑fold to single‑digit nM (WDC‑623).
WDC‑623 reduced c‑MYC protein within 30 min without affecting MYC mRNA levels.
Proteomics showed c‑MYC and its direct targets CDC25A/B were the most repressed proteins after 2 h treatment.
Oral BID dosing (5 mg/kg) produced significant tumor growth inhibition in SCLC CDX and 125 % TGI in PDAC xenograft with no weight loss.
Compound activity is nutrient‑dependent; high nutrient levels (normal plasma) blunt MYC repression, suggesting tumor‑selective activity.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity driven by nutrient‑dependent translation control; minimal effect on L‑MYC or other short‑half‑life proteins.
open_questions: Can the nutrient‑dependent mechanism be leveraged to improve therapeutic window in patients?
What is the chronic safety profile of sustained MYC translation inhibition?

talk_title: Intramolecular glue Cbl‑B inhibitors (NX‑1607) advancing to Phase 1

biomarkers.name: phospho‑HS1
biomarkers.finding: dose‑dependent increase correlating with efficacy in mouse models

clinical_data.value: robust efficacy at 10–30 mg/kg daily in mouse models
clinical_data.population: CT26, TC1, TNBC, B‑cell lymphoma models

clinical_data.value: enhanced survival in multiple tumor models

MOA_summary: NX‑1607 binds to the closed conformation pocket at the interface of Cbl‑B’s three domains, acting as an intramolecular glue that locks Cbl‑B in an inactive state, thereby releasing the brake on T‑cell and NK‑cell activation; the resulting immune activation synergizes with anti‑PD‑1 therapy to produce tumor regression.
key_findings: High‑throughput screen of 250 k compounds yielded a single Cbl‑B hit (NRX‑001) with 30 µM potency.
Enantiomeric resolution identified the active S‑enantiomer; crystal structure revealed binding to a buried pocket near Tyr363.
Medicinal chemistry optimization produced NX‑1607 with sub‑nanomolar Kd (0.4 nM) and excellent oral bioavailability.
NX‑1607 monotherapy achieved tumor regression at 10–30 mg/kg daily in colorectal, TNBC, and B‑cell lymphoma models.
Combination with anti‑PD‑1 showed synergistic survival benefit across multiple syngeneic models.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity for Cbl‑B over other E3 ligases demonstrated by lack of Src inhibition in primary screen.
No activity observed in Cbl‑B knockout mice, confirming on‑target mechanism.

combination_strategies.rationale: Cbl‑B inhibition releases T‑cell brake, enhancing checkpoint blockade efficacy
combination_strategies.phenotype: significant survival extension vs anti‑PD‑1 alone

open_questions: Can NX‑1607 be combined safely with other immune agonists (e.g., CD40 agonists)?
What is the long‑term safety of chronic Cbl‑B inhibition in humans?

talk_title: NEO811, a first‑in‑class ARNT molecular glue degrader for clear cell RCC

biomarkers.name: HIF transcriptional activity
biomarkers.finding: potent inhibition superior to belzutifan

clinical_data.value: significant regression after 21 days daily dosing
minimal degradation of SALL4, CK1α, Ikaros compared with earlier hits

MOA_summary: NEO811 binds cereblon and creates a neo‑substrate interface that recruits ARNT, inducing a conformational rearrangement that enables ubiquitination and proteasomal degradation; loss of ARNT disrupts HIF‑α heterodimer transcription, leading to potent anti‑tumor activity in VHL‑deficient RCC.
key_findings: Structure‑guided design converted initial hit (compound A) into selective ARNT degrader (compound C) with reduced off‑target cereblon neo‑substrate degradation.
NEO811 demonstrated >80 % ARNT degradation at low nanomolar concentrations in cellular assays.
In vivo, once‑daily oral dosing for 21 days produced marked tumor regression in ccRCC xenografts with good tolerability.
NEO811 showed superior potency to FDA‑approved HIF‑2α inhibitor belzutifan in HIF transcriptional inhibition assays.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Off‑target degradation of SALL4, CK1α, Ikaros mitigated by steric optimization of cereblon‑binding moiety.
open_questions: Will ARNT degradation overcome resistance to current VEGF/HIF‑2α inhibitors?
What is the safety profile of chronic ARNT loss in normal tissues?

talk_title: DNA‑templated spatially controlled degraders (DTAG) for CDK4/6‑Cyclin D1

clinical_data.comparator: DNA alone, ligand‑only, other DTAG versions

MOA_summary: DTAG‑v1 uses a 20‑bp double‑stranded DNA scaffold to position a CDK4/6 inhibitor and an E3 ligase recruiter at defined distances; the optimal geometry (ligand on major groove) enables formation of a productive ternary complex that simultaneously degrades CDK4, CDK6 and the undruggable Cyclin D1, demonstrating that spatial programming via DNA can control target selectivity.
readouts: Western blot of CDK4/6 and Cyclin D1
key_findings: Four DNA‑templated PROTAC geometries showed distinct degradation profiles; version 1 (major‑groove placement) degraded CDK4, CDK6 and Cyclin D1.
Molecular dynamics revealed that productive ternary complexes correlated with negative ΔG values and close proximity of CDK4/6 to the E3 ligase.
In vivo, DTAG‑v1 achieved robust tumor regression in U251 xenografts with no significant body‑weight loss.
The platform is modular: swapping the CDK4/6 ligand for CDK9 or CDK2 inhibitors generated degraders for other cyclin‑CDK complexes without re‑synthesizing the DNA scaffold.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity driven by DNA‑defined geometry; larger spacer (version 3) failed to induce degradation.
open_questions: Can DTAG be adapted for systemic delivery and nuclease stability in vivo?
Will DNA‑based degraders trigger innate immune pathways (cGAS‑STING)?

talk_title: BH‑501284, a pseudo‑irreversible pan‑KRAS switch‑II inhibitor

targets.dependency_context: KRAS‑mutant (G12V, G12D, G12C) cell lines and CDX models

biomarkers.name: p‑ERK
biomarkers.finding: sustained inhibition after washout (90 % retained at 24 h)

clinical_data.population: KRAS mutant solid tumor cell lines

clinical_data.value: deep regression at 5 mg/kg BID in H358 CDX
59 % TGI at 100 mg/kg BID for AMG 410 comparator

MOA_summary: BH‑501284 binds the KRAS switch‑II pocket in both GDP‑ and GTP‑bound states with picomolar affinity and a residence time >54 h, rigidifying KRAS and preventing RAF interaction; the pseudo‑irreversible kinetic profile yields sustained pathway inhibition after drug washout.
key_findings: SPR showed picomolar Kd and >54 h residence time on GDP‑bound KRAS G12D, outperforming AMG 410 (1.4 h).
Washout experiments demonstrated >90 % p‑ERK inhibition retained 24 h after removal, comparable to covalent G12C inhibitor.
Potent activity across KRAS G12V, G12D, G12C (IC50 <3 nM) and KRAS‑amplified lines (IC50 ~1.5 nM); inactive on NRAS/HRAS mutants.
Oral BID dosing as low as 5 mg/kg achieved deep tumor regressions in multiple KRAS mutant CDX models.
Only ~2‑fold shift in IC50 when EGFR ligand added, and synergistic with cetuximab in long‑term growth assays.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity over NRAS/HRAS confirmed; modest activity on KRAS G12R absent.
Pseudo‑irreversible binding may mitigate rapid resistance emergence.

combination_strategies.rationale: EGFR‑mediated re‑activation of KRAS signaling is a known resistance mechanism
combination_strategies.phenotype: synergistic inhibition of long‑term cell growth

open_questions: Will the long residence time translate to favorable human PK/PD?
Can BH‑501284 overcome acquired resistance to covalent KRAS G12C inhibitors?

talk_title: Proteomics‑guided discovery of TRIM21‑mediated multimeric degradation (ACE compounds)
MOA_summary: ACE compounds act as molecular glues that convert the E3 ligase TRIM21 into a degrader of nuclear pore complex proteins; binding is NUP98‑dependent, leading to ordered degradation of inner‑ring NPC subunits followed by outer‑ring components, demonstrating that multimeric assembly state governs TRIM21‑mediated proteasomal clearance.
key_findings: Phenotypic screen identified three ACE compounds that cause IFN‑γ‑dependent cell death.
CRISPR screen highlighted TRIM21 as essential for ACE activity.
Proteomics revealed selective degradation of inner‑ring NPC proteins, reversible by proteasome inhibition.
Time‑course proteomics showed hierarchical degradation: inner‑ring NPC subunits degraded within 4 h, outer‑ring later, nuclear basket unchanged at 12 h.
BioID proximity labeling demonstrated ACE‑dependent recruitment of TRIM21 to NPC complexes via NUP98; NUP98 knockout abolished recruitment.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Selectivity limited to multimeric NPC assemblies; monomeric proteins are spared.
Dependence on NUP98 suggests potential resistance via NUP98 loss.
open_questions: Can TRIM21‑based glues be harnessed to degrade pathogenic protein condensates in vivo?
What are the immunological consequences of chronic TRIM21 activation?

talk_title: MUTA tetravalent antibody platform for next-generation ADCs
MOA_summary: The MUTA platform creates a tetravalent, multi-epitope antibody that simultaneously binds two tumor antigens (e.g., MUC16 and NaPi2b) to improve internalization, efficacy, and safety of ADCs, with site-specific conjugation and a hydrophilic linker to enhance plasma stability and therapeutic window.
key_findings: MUDA tetravalent ADCs outperformed monospecific ADCs in lung, colon, head and neck xenograft models, showing greater tumor regression.
PK in monkeys demonstrated stable plasma exposure up to 9 mg/kg with reversible reticulocyte decrease only at the highest dose, indicating an improved safety margin.
AquaLink hydrophilic linker maintained ADC stability without increasing hydrophobicity, improving plasma stability and bystander activity.

talk_title: High-throughput drug screening and single-cell network analysis for IDH mutant glioma
MOA_summary: Single-cell regulatory network analysis identifies state-specific master regulator programs in IDH mutant glioma; high-throughput drug screening (OncoTreat) predicts compounds that invert these programs (e.g., cisplatin for AC-like, DS‑651b for NPC-like, micofenolate for OC-like), enabling rational combination strategies validated in patient-derived slice explants.
key_findings: Three conserved malignant cell states (AC-like, NPC-like, OC-like) were defined across oligodendroglioma and astrocytoma with distinct master regulator programs.
OncoTreat identified state-specific drug vulnerabilities: DNA damage repair inhibitors for AC-like, MAPK pathway inhibitors for NPC-like, and purine synthesis inhibitors for OC-like.
Preliminary ex vivo slice experiments suggest serum biomarkers can detect early state shifts, supporting translational potential.
open_questions: Will the state-specific drug predictions translate into durable clinical responses in patients?
How stable are the identified master regulator programs over disease progression and treatment?

talk_title: Serum hyaluronic acid as a predictive biomarker for T‑DXD‑induced interstitial lung disease

biomarkers.name: Serum hyaluronic acid (HA)
biomarkers.finding: HA AUC 80% in preclinical model; AUC 74% (single) and 93% (panel) in pilot clinical cohort for predicting ADC‑ILD

clinical_data.value: 0% in young mice vs 40% in adult mice

MOA_summary: Age‑dependent accumulation of T‑DXD in adult lungs leads to alveolar macrophage activation, endothelial HYAL2 up‑regulation, extracellular matrix remodeling and release of hyaluronic acid fragments into serum, which serve as an early non‑invasive biomarker for ADC‑induced interstitial lung disease.
key_findings: Adult mice develop T‑DXD‑induced ILD (40%) whereas young mice are protected (0%).
Adult lungs show higher T‑DXD accumulation, increased mature alveolar macrophages, elevated CTSB and SPP1 expression, and endothelial HYAL2 up‑regulation.
Serum HA levels rise significantly in adult mice with ILD and predict ILD in a pilot clinical cohort (AUC 74% alone, 93% with fibrosis panel).
Early HA elevation precedes radiographic ILD, suggesting utility for pre‑emptive monitoring.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target perivascular macrophages to mitigate ILD
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced macrophage‑mediated lung injury in preclinical models

open_questions: Can HA biomarker be validated prospectively in larger, multi‑center trials?
What is the mechanistic basis for age‑dependent macrophage maturation affecting ADC uptake?

talk_title: Co‑clinical trial of datopotamab deruxtecan in patient‑derived xenografts

speaker.name: Dr. Dhruv Chachad
speaker.affiliation: MD Anderson Cancer Center, Department of Investigational Cancer Therapeutics

models.name: 23 successful PDXs from Tropion‑PanTumor01 trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: various tumor types, variable Trop‑2 expression

clinical_data.value: significant tumor volume reduction and extended time to doubling vs monotherapies

MOA_summary: Datopotamab deruxtecan (ADC targeting Trop‑2) delivers a topoisomerase‑1 inhibitor payload; co‑clinical PDX models recapitulate patient responses and reveal synergistic antitumor activity when combined with gemcitabine, likely via enhanced DNA damage in Trop‑2‑positive tumors.
key_findings: Dato induced tumor regression and prolonged time to doubling across multiple PDX models, mirroring patient outcomes (78.6% concordance).
Gemcitabine synergized with Dato in PDXs derived from patients with progressive disease, leading to greater tumor control than either agent alone.
Biopsy take rate was 47% overall, demonstrating feasibility of generating co‑clinical PDXs from basket trial specimens.

combination_strategies.rationale: Enhance DNA damage in Trop‑2‑positive tumors and overcome resistance
combination_strategies.phenotype: Greater tumor regression and extended time to doubling in resistant PDXs

open_questions: What biomarkers predict which patients will benefit from the Dato‑gemcitabine combination?
Can the co‑clinical platform be scaled to test other rational combinations in real time?

talk_title: Inducer‑amplifier strategy targeting p53‑mutant cancers
MOA_summary: A two‑step inducer‑amplifier approach: TAS‑102 induces DNA damage that accumulates in p53‑mutant cells; PARP inhibition blocks single‑strand break repair, enhancing damage; subsequent WEE1 inhibition forces G2‑arrested cells into mitotic catastrophe, achieving selective tumor cell death with low toxicity.
key_findings: TAS‑102 plus PARP inhibitor synergistically kills p53‑mutant cells in vitro and reduces tumor growth in PDX models.
Adding WEE1 inhibitor after the inducer‑amplifier increases cell death from 30% to ~80% specifically in p53‑mutant cells.
The sequential regimen is well tolerated in preclinical models and shows early signs of efficacy in a phase‑1 trial.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Wild‑type p53 cells recover after drug washout, whereas mutant cells retain G2 arrest and undergo death.

combination_strategies.rationale: Block single‑strand break repair to amplify DNA damage induced by TAS‑102
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic cytotoxicity in p53‑mutant cells

combination_strategies.rationale: Force G2‑arrested p53‑mutant cells into mitosis, causing mitotic catastrophe
combination_strategies.phenotype: Cell death ↑ to ~80% in mutant cells

open_questions: Which p53 mutation subtypes respond best to the sequential regimen?
Can biomarkers of BER activity predict patient selection?

talk_title: KBD‑1111: a selective, long‑acting, brain‑penetrant PARP‑1 inhibitor

clinical_data.value: near complete at 10 mg/kg Q2W in DLD1 xenograft
dose‑dependent reduction in intracranial model (0.3‑3 mg/kg QW)
minimal at QW dosing; slight at QD

MOA_summary: KBD‑1111 is a highly selective PARP‑1 inhibitor (9,000‑fold over PARP‑2) that traps PARP‑1, exhibits long half‑life, penetrates the CNS (CSF AUC 38.6 ng·h/mL, Kpu 0.26), and demonstrates potent antitumor activity with reduced hematologic toxicity, supporting Q2W dosing.
key_findings: KBD‑1111 shows IC50 0.36 nM against PARP‑1 and >9,000‑fold selectivity over PARP‑2.
In BRCA‑deficient xenografts, 10 mg/kg Q2W achieved near‑complete tumor inhibition, superior to AZD5305.
Brain penetration (Kpu 0.26) enabled tumor regression in intracranial models with weekly dosing.
Hematologic toxicity was markedly reduced compared to olaparib, especially with QW dosing.
open_questions: Will the CNS penetration translate into clinical efficacy for brain metastases?
What patient populations (HRD vs non‑HRD) will benefit most from the Q2W schedule?

talk_title: Comparative analysis of TEAD palmitoylation inhibitor vs PPI in KRAS‑mutant cancers
MOA_summary: TEAD central‑pocket palmitoylation inhibitors (CPI) block TEAD palmitoylation, while TEAD‑YAP/TAZ PPI inhibitors directly disrupt the protein‑protein interaction; both suppress TEAD‑driven transcription, but PPI achieves greater YAP eviction and target gene repression, leading to more durable synergy with KRAS inhibitors in vitro, though in vivo both modalities show comparable tumor control.
key_findings: PPI (IAG 933) combined with KRAS G12C inhibitor delayed resistance and resensitized resistant cells more effectively than CPI (AZD0751) in vitro.
Both TEAD inhibitors suppressed KRAS‑induced TEAD target gene up‑regulation, but PPI achieved greater YAP chromatin eviction.
In NSCLC xenografts, both CPI and PPI enhanced tumor regression with KRAS inhibition, but no additive benefit was observed in KRAS G12D pancreatic models.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: CPI showed partial resistance delay; PPI provided more durable suppression of TEAD targets.

combination_strategies.rationale: Prevent TEAD‑mediated adaptive resistance to KRAS blockade
combination_strategies.phenotype: Extended in‑vitro response and resensitization of resistant cells (PPI superior)

combination_strategies.rationale: Same as above for G12D context
combination_strategies.phenotype: PPI provided durable effect in long‑term assays

open_questions: Why does the superior in‑vitro activity of PPI not translate into greater in‑vivo efficacy?
Can dosing schedules or formulation improve the in‑vivo advantage of PPI over CPI?

talk_title: Targeting RAS and RAF in MSS Metastatic Colorectal Cancer

speaker.name: Scott Kopetz
speaker.affiliation: University of Texas MD Anderson Cancer Center, Department of Gastrointestinal Medical Oncology

targets.dependency_context: BRAF V600E mutant MSS colorectal cancer
MSS colorectal cancer receiving BRAF inhibition
KRAS G12C mutant MSS colorectal cancer
KRAS G12D mutant MSS colorectal cancer

biomarkers.name: ctDNA clearance of BRAF V600E clone
biomarkers.finding: Undetectable ctDNA at 6 weeks increased from 35% (control) to 45% (EC) to 67% (EC+FOLFOX)

biomarkers.name: Cytolytic score (granzyme/perforin)
biomarkers.finding: Low cytolytic score associated with greater benefit from encorafenib+cetuximab (higher ORR and PFS)

models.name: BEACON
models.genotype_or_key_features: BRAF V600E mutant MSS colorectal cancer, second/third line

models.name: BreakWater
models.genotype_or_key_features: BRAF V600E mutant MSS colorectal cancer, first line, arms: EC+FOLFOX, EC alone, standard chemo

models.name: multiple KRAS/BRAF resistant PDX models
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS/NRAS/MAP2K1 mutations, MET amplification, subclonal resistance

clinical_data.population: BRAF V600E mutant MSS colorectal cancer, first line
BRAF V600E mutant MSS colorectal cancer, first line
BRAF V600E mutant MSS colorectal cancer, first line
BRAF V600E mutant MSS colorectal cancer, first line
BRAF V600E mutant MSS colorectal cancer, first line
BRAF V600E mutant MSS colorectal cancer, first line
KRAS G12C mutant MSS colorectal cancer
KRAS G12C mutant MSS colorectal cancer
KRAS G12C mutant MSS colorectal cancer
KRAS G12C mutant MSS colorectal cancer

MOA_summary: Encouraging BRAF V600E inhibition in MSS colorectal cancer requires concurrent EGFR blockade to prevent feedback re‑activation of MAPK signaling; adding chemotherapy (FOLFOX) further suppresses emergent resistant clones and reduces ctDNA‑detectable resistance mutations, leading to higher response rates and prolonged PFS/OS. Parallel KRAS allele‑specific inhibitors (G12C, G12D) show modest activity alone, which is markedly enhanced when combined with EGFR antibodies, reflecting the same feedback loop. Emerging degraders and pan‑RAS approaches aim to overcome subclonal resistance mechanisms such as MET amplification, KRAS/NRAS secondary mutations, and YAP/TEAD activation.
key_findings: BreakWater first‑line BRAF V600E + EGFR inhibition plus FOLFOX achieved ORR 67% vs 37% with standard chemotherapy (HR for PFS 0.53, HR for OS 0.49).
Median OS doubled from 15.1 months (control) to 30.3 months with EC+FOLFOX in BRAF V600E MSS colorectal cancer.
ctDNA clearance of the BRAF V600E clone at 6 weeks increased from 35% (control) to 45% (EC) and 67% (EC+FOLFOX), correlating with improved outcomes.
Acquired resistance mutations (KRAS, NRAS, MAP2K1, MET amplification) were subclonal and more frequent after EC alone than after EC+FOLFOX.
KRAS G12C inhibitor adagrasib combined with cetuximab yielded ORR 34% and PFS 6.9 months; elacestrant monotherapy gave ORR 47% (up to 62% with EGFR inhibitor).
High baseline cell‑cycle signaling predicts emergence of secondary resistance mutations; low cytolytic score predicts greater benefit from BRAF‑EGFR therapy.
Pre‑clinical data show cross‑resistance between prior chemotherapy and BRAF‑EGFR therapy, supporting first‑line use of the combination.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Secondary KRAS/NRAS/MAP2K1 mutations and MET amplifications emerge at progression, often subclonal (≈1% of tumor cells).
High baseline cell‑cycle transcriptional activity predicts acquisition of resistance mutations.
YAP/TEAD signaling up‑regulation observed in paired biopsies after KRAS inhibition, suggesting a bypass pathway.

combination_strategies.rationale: Blocks EGFR‑mediated MAPK re‑activation that limits BRAF inhibitor efficacy.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression; ORR 67% with EC+FOLFOX vs 37% control.

combination_strategies.rationale: Suppresses emergent resistant clones and reduces ctDNA‑detectable resistance mutations.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved PFS (12.8 mo vs 7 mo) and OS (30.3 mo vs 15.1 mo).

combination_strategies.rationale: KRAS inhibition may prime tumor microenvironment for immune activation.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Early data suggest increased lymphocyte clustering and immune activation in paired biopsies.

combination_strategies.rationale: Degrader plus EGFR inhibition superior to BRAF‑EGFR combo in pre‑clinical models.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced tumor regression in PDX models.

open_questions: What is the optimal sequencing of BRAF‑EGFR therapy with cytotoxic chemotherapy to minimize cross‑resistance?
Can pan‑RAS or KRAS G12D inhibitors achieve durable responses without EGFR blockade, or is EGFR inhibition always required?
What are the mechanisms driving the indolent biology of post‑BreakWater resistant tumors (PFS2), and can they be therapeutically exploited?

talk_title: Epidemiology and clinical impact of chemosensory dysfunction in cancer patients

speaker.name: Valentina Parma
speaker.affiliation: Monell Chemical Senses Center; University of Pennsylvania Department of Otolaryngology

models.name: NHANES representative sample
models.genotype_or_key_features: 3,000+ US adults, cancer history, self-reported smell/taste dysfunction

models.name: Smell & Taste Association of North America survey

clinical_data.metric: both smell and taste dysfunction in NHANES
odds reduction for life satisfaction with smell dysfunction
odds reduction for life satisfaction with taste dysfunction

MOA_summary: Epidemiologic analyses of large population cohorts reveal that chemosensory dysfunction is highly prevalent in cancer patients (≈60% smell, 93% taste) and is associated with substantial reductions in quality of life, increased frailty, malnutrition, and higher risk of comorbidities such as heart failure and cognitive decline.
key_findings: Approximately 60% of cancer patients report smell dysfunction and 93% report taste dysfunction.
NHANES data show 14% prevalence of taste dysfunction, 22% smell dysfunction, and 10% both in the general US adult population.
Smell dysfunction reduces odds of a satisfying life by 40%; taste dysfunction reduces it by 60% (NHANES).
During chemotherapy cycles, taste dysfunction peaks at days 4‑7 and improves by the end of the cycle.
Clinician awareness is low: only ~15% can distinguish taste from flavor, yet 44% of clinicians consider taste changes very important to patients.
open_questions: How can routine EMR integration improve detection and management of chemosensory dysfunction?
What are the most effective, evidence‑based interventions for specific dysgeusia phenotypes?

talk_title: Molecular mechanisms of targeted therapy‑induced taste dysfunction and development of taste organoid platforms

speaker.name: Linda Barlow
speaker.affiliation: University of Colorado, Taste & Smell Center

models.name: wild‑type C57BL/6
models.genotype_or_key_features: treated with smoothened antagonist, Wnt beta‑catenin manipulation, cabozantinib

models.name: mouse taste organoids (Lgr5+ progenitors)
models.genotype_or_key_features: derived from circumvallate papillae, GFP‑Lgr5 reporter

models.name: human taste organoids
models.genotype_or_key_features: derived from donor circumvallate papillae, <50 y, no cancer

MOA_summary: Targeted cancer therapies disrupt taste homeostasis via distinct pathways: Wnt/β‑catenin activation skews progenitor output toward type I cells and reduces sweet perception; Hedgehog inhibition blocks progenitor differentiation causing loss of taste buds; KIT inhibition by anti‑angiogenic TKIs impairs sweet cell fate and promotes bitter differentiation, leading to dysgeusia.
key_findings: Wnt/β‑catenin signaling is required for taste progenitor proliferation; hyperactivation increases type I cells and modestly type II cells without affecting sour cells.
Loss of Wnt signaling leads to progressive loss of taste buds, especially in anterior tongue, correlating with reduced sweet behavior in mice.
Smoothened antagonism (Hedgehog inhibition) eliminates taste buds and diminishes gustatory nerve responses, but does not affect progenitor proliferation.
Overexpression of SHH drives excess taste bud formation, indicating Hedgehog promotes differentiation rather than proliferation.
Cabozantinib treatment reduces sweet‑cell markers and increases bitter‑cell markers in mouse taste organoids and in vivo, phenocopying KIT deletion in type II precursors.
Human circumvallate papillae can be harvested from organ donors and cultured into organoids containing all taste cell types, establishing a platform for translational testing.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: KIT inhibition selectively impairs sweet cell fate while promoting bitter differentiation, suggesting off‑target selectivity contributes to dysgeusia.

combination_strategies.rationale: Rescue progenitor proliferation during Wnt inhibition
combination_strategies.phenotype: Restores type I/II cell balance and sweet perception in mice

combination_strategies.rationale: Promote differentiation of progenitors when smoothened antagonists are used clinically
combination_strategies.phenotype: Partial restoration of taste bud numbers and nerve responses

open_questions: Do human taste organoids recapitulate drug‑induced taste alterations observed in patients?
Can selective modulation of KIT signaling mitigate dysgeusia without compromising anti‑angiogenic efficacy?

talk_title: Implementation of the Genie Data Model at Providence Cancer Institute
MOA_summary: The GP3 pipeline programmatically extracts source attributes from Providence's Snowflake clinical data warehouse, maps them to the Genie Data Model (GDM) via SQL transformations, validates and stages the data, and exports it for downstream use, dramatically reducing manual abstraction time and improving data quality.
key_findings: Programmatic extraction reduced case abstraction time from ~8 hours per case to minutes for thousands of patients.
Automated schema validation and record-level consistency checks identified implausible values (e.g., out-of-range weights) for rapid correction.
Lineage parsing of SQL code automatically documented source provenance for each GDM attribute, facilitating reproducibility.
AI-assisted coding (GitHub Copilot) accelerated development of mapping scripts and improved code quality.
The pipeline achieved near‑real‑time generation of GDM‑aligned tables, enabling rapid data sharing across sites.

talk_title: Senescent fibroblast SASP drives chemotherapy‑induced peripheral neuropathy and can be reversed by senolytics or MK2 inhibition

targets.dependency_context: senescent fibroblasts in peripheral skin of paclitaxel‑treated mice
senescent fibroblasts driving SASP in CIPN

models.name: C57BL/6 paclitaxel‑treated
models.genotype_or_key_features: peripheral skin fibroblasts p16‑positive, CIPN phenotype

models.name: nude rat with human stem cell transplant (for later stem‑cell work)

MOA_summary: Chemotherapy (paclitaxel) induces p16‑positive senescent fibroblasts in peripheral skin; these cells secrete a SASP regulated by MK2 kinase that causes epidermal denervation and behavioral neuropathy. Genetic ablation of p16‑positive cells or pharmacologic senolysis (dasatinib + quercetin) or MK2 inhibition eliminates the SASP, restores innervation and reverses numbness/pain in mice.
readouts: single‑cell RNA‑seq of peripheral skin fibroblasts
key_findings: Paclitaxel treatment increased p16 mRNA and protein in peripheral skin but not in dorsal root ganglia or sciatic nerve.
Senescent fibroblasts (p16‑positive, SA‑β‑gal+) accumulated in the skin and were eliminated by inducible suicide gene activation.
Elimination of senescent cells prevented paclitaxel‑induced epidermal denervation and restored normal Von Frey thresholds.
Senolytic treatment (dasatinib + quercetin) after neuropathy onset re‑innervated epidermis and normalized paw thresholds, reversing pain.
Fibroblast‑specific MK2 knockout abolished the neuropathic phenotype despite persistence of senescent cells, implicating MK2‑driven SASP as the effector.
Human skin biopsies from three CIPN patients showed increased p16 and senescent fibroblast markers, mirroring mouse findings.

combination_strategies.rationale: Kill p16‑positive senescent fibroblasts to remove SASP source
combination_strategies.phenotype: Restored innervation and normalized Von Frey thresholds after neuropathy onset

combination_strategies.rationale: Block SASP production in senescent fibroblasts without killing them
combination_strategies.phenotype: Prevents denervation and behavioral numbness in paclitaxel‑treated mice
combination_strategies.stage: clinical dose‑escalation (metastatic breast cancer) but preclinical for CIPN

open_questions: Why does chemotherapy‑induced senescence occur selectively in peripheral fibroblasts rather than neurons or DRG cells?

talk_title: Long‑term morbidity and mortality in childhood cancer survivors: prevalence, frailty, and socioeconomic impacts

models.name: St. Jude Lifetime Cohort Study
models.genotype_or_key_features: ≈2,800 adult survivors of pediatric cancer, median age 32, >20 yr from diagnosis

clinical_data.value: ≈25% by age 40 compared with US population

clinical_data.metric: average number of chronic conditions by age 50
frailty hazard ratio for early death
psychological hardship (concern about paying for care)

MOA_summary: Large‑scale survivorship cohort analyses reveal that treatment‑related exposures (radiation, alkylators, surgery) and socioeconomic deprivation synergistically drive multimorbidity, frailty, and excess mortality in adult survivors of childhood cancer, with genetic predisposition (pathogenic germline variants) further increasing risk of subsequent neoplasms.
readouts: clinical examinations (cardiac echo, pulmonary function, neurocognitive testing)
patient‑reported outcome surveys (financial hardship, quality of life)
key_findings: By age 40, childhood cancer survivors have a 25% excess mortality relative to the US population.
More than 90% of survivors develop at least one chronic health condition; 85% of those are serious, disabling, or life‑threatening.
Survivors accrue an average of 17 chronic conditions by age 50, including ~5 severe conditions.
Frailty prevalence in survivors aged 18‑50 matches that of general adults >65 yr; frailty confers a 2.6‑fold higher risk of early death.
Whole‑genome sequencing identified pathogenic germline variants in 6% of survivors, associated with increased risk of second neoplasms.
Financial hardship is pervasive: 22% report material hardship, 51% psychological hardship, and 33% behavioral hardship, correlating with higher anxiety, depression, and suicidal ideation.
open_questions: How can precision survivorship interventions (e.g., targeted screening, lifestyle programs) be tailored to specific genetic risk profiles?
What are the mechanistic links between epigenetic age acceleration and specific organ‑system morbidities in this population?

talk_title: Functional significance of YTHDF m6A readers in AML and multiple myeloma

speaker.name: Kamil Krance
speaker.affiliation: Institute of Cancer Research, London, Hemato‑Oncology

targets.dependency_context: AML and multiple myeloma, correlates with LSC activity

models.name: YTHDF1/3 double knockout
models.genotype_or_key_features: global deletion of Ythdf1 and Ythdf3

models.name: primary AML samples (n≈3 shown)
models.genotype_or_key_features: various AML subtypes, including MLL, AF9, TP53 mutant

models.name: THP‑1 liver engraftment model
models.genotype_or_key_features: human AML PDX in NSG mice

models.name: multiple myeloma cell lines (various)
models.genotype_or_key_features: diverse MM lines with different mutations

MOA_summary: YTHDF2 and YTHDF3 are cytosolic m6A readers that promote decay of methylated transcripts such as TNFRSF1B; genetic deletion stabilizes these transcripts, leading to heightened TNF‑mediated apoptosis and loss of leukemic stem cell activity in AML. Parallel loss of YTHDF3 impairs ribosomal biogenesis and translation of M6A‑modified mRNAs, reducing leukemic proliferation and sensitizing cells to cytarabine. Normal hematopoiesis remains largely intact, indicating a therapeutic window for targeting these readers.
key_findings: YTHDF2 is overexpressed in AML across sub‑genetic subtypes and correlates with leukemic stem cell activity.
YTHDF2 knockout or knockdown abolishes colony formation of AML cells in vitro and markedly extends latency in mouse transplantation models.
Loss of YTHDF2 stabilizes TNFRSF1B mRNA, increasing surface TNFR2 and rendering AML cells highly sensitive to exogenous TNF (apoptosis observed).
YTHDF3 deletion reduces leukemic stem cell frequency in limiting‑dilution assays and impairs engraftment of THP‑1 cells in liver PDX models.
YTHDF3 knockdown sensitizes AML cells to Ara‑C, decreasing the Ara‑C IC50 (exact values not disclosed).
Inducible YTHDF2 tryptophan‑to‑alanine (WA) mutant recapitulates knockout phenotypes, confirming the M6A‑binding pocket is essential for function.
A prototype small‑molecule YTHDF inhibitor (IC50 ≈ 11 µM) suppresses AML colony formation in a dose‑dependent manner, with partial rescue by YTHDF knockdown.

combination_strategies.rationale: YTHDF3 loss increases sensitivity to standard AML chemotherapy
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced Ara‑C IC50 and enhanced apoptosis in AML cell lines

combination_strategies.rationale: Stabilized TNFR2 after YTHDF2 loss sensitizes cells to TNF‑mediated death
combination_strategies.phenotype: Robust apoptosis of YTHDF2‑deficient AML cells upon TNF treatment
