combination_strategies.rationale: Pharmacologic blockade of YTHDF2 mimics genetic loss
combination_strategies.phenotype: Dose‑dependent inhibition of AML colony formation (IC50 ≈ 11 µM)

open_questions: What specific mutations, if any, occur in YTHDF family genes in patient AML cohorts?
Will combined YTHDF inhibition with Ara‑C lead to emergent resistance mechanisms?
What are the long‑term effects of dual YTHDF1/2 or YTHDF1/3 inhibition on normal hematopoietic stem cell function?

talk_title: YTHDF2 and YTHDC1 in tumor immunity and chromatin regulation of plastic cancers

speaker.name: Chuan He
speaker.affiliation: University of Chicago, Department of Chemistry, Howard Hughes Medical Investigator

targets.dependency_context: neuroendocrine prostate and small cell lung cancers with lineage plasticity

models.name: radiation + anti‑PD‑L1 combination model
models.genotype_or_key_features: wild‑type vs Ythdf2‑KO mice treated with IR and PD‑L1 blockade

models.name: patient‑derived xenografts of neuroendocrine prostate cancer

MOA_summary: YTHDF2 promotes decay of M6A‑modified transcripts in myeloid‑derived suppressor cells, enhancing their immunosuppressive function after radiation; its loss stabilizes anti‑inflammatory RNAs, reduces MDSC migration, and synergizes with radiation and anti‑PD‑L1 to boost CD8⁺ T‑cell activity. YTHDC1 binds M6A‑modified promoter and enhancer RNAs (including LTR elements) in solid tumors, recruiting SETD1A/YY1 to open chromatin and drive transcription of lineage‑plastic, proliferative programs; inhibition of YTHDC1 collapses this transcriptional program and depletes cycling tumor cells.
readouts: flow cytometry (CD8⁺ T cells, MDSC markers)
key_findings: YTHDF2 knockout in MDSCs diminishes their suppressive capacity, reduces IL‑10 production, and enhances CD8⁺ T‑cell infiltration after irradiation.
Combining YTHDF2 loss with radiation and anti‑PD‑L1 yields synergistic tumor control in mouse models, dependent on CD8⁺ T cells.
YTHDC1 inhibition (tool compound) markedly reduces viability of neuroendocrine prostate and small‑cell lung cancer cells in vitro and suppresses tumor growth in PDX models.
Loss of YTHDC1 leads to global chromatin closing (reduced DNase I signal, lower H3K27ac/H3K4me3) and down‑regulation of ~6,000 genes linked to LTR‑derived promoter RNAs.
Proteomics identifies SETD1A and YY1 as YTHDC1 interactors mediating transcriptional activation at open chromatin sites.
Single‑cell sequencing after YTHDC1 inhibition shows depletion of the cycling tumor cell population while non‑cycling cells persist.

combination_strategies.rationale: Radiation induces NF‑κB‑driven YTHDF2 expression that sustains MDSC immunosuppression
combination_strategies.phenotype: YTHDF2 loss + IR markedly reduces tumor growth compared with IR alone

combination_strategies.rationale: YTHDF2‑deficient MDSCs fail to suppress CD8⁺ T cells, enhancing checkpoint efficacy
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression in mouse models

combination_strategies.rationale: Targeting YTHDC1‑mediated chromatin opening suppresses lineage‑plastic tumor cells
combination_strategies.phenotype: Significant tumor control in neuroendocrine prostate and SCLC PDX models

open_questions: Does YTHDF2 influence DNA damage repair pathways (e.g., homologous recombination) in irradiated tumors?
What post‑translational modifications of YTHDC1 dictate its switch from transcriptional repression (retrotransposon sites) to activation (promoter sites) in cancer?
Can YTHDC1 inhibition synergize with PARP inhibitors or other epigenetic drugs in neuroendocrine cancers?

talk_title: Precision diets targeting serine/glycine metabolism and combination with PHGDH inhibition

speaker.name: Karen Valston
speaker.affiliation: University of California, San Diego, Department of Molecular Medicine

targets.dependency_context: KRAS-driven lung and pancreatic tumors with PHGDH upregulation
Serine/glycine‑depleted cells become dependent on folate transporter

biomarkers.name: GDF15
biomarkers.finding: GDF15 levels were lower in mice on serine/glycine‑free diet, correlating with reduced cachexia

biomarkers.name: IL6
biomarkers.finding: IL6 was reduced in serine/glycine‑restricted mice compared with controls

MOA_summary: Limiting dietary serine and glycine reduces circulating one‑carbon metabolites, impairing nucleotide synthesis and antioxidant capacity in tumor cells, which slows growth in serine‑dependent GEM and organoid models. Tumors that up‑regulate de novo serine synthesis via PHGDH become resistant, but combined PHGDH inhibition restores sensitivity, especially when paired with radiotherapy that induces ROS.
key_findings: Serine/glycine‑free diet reduced tumor growth and metastasis in KRAS/LKB1 lung and KRAS/p53 pancreatic GEM models (p<0.01).
Combination of serine/glycine restriction with radiotherapy markedly increased tumor cell death compared with either modality alone (p<0.001).
PHGDH‑amplified tumors were resistant to dietary serine depletion, but addition of a PHGDH inhibitor restored tumor growth inhibition (tumor volume reduction 45%, p<0.01).
CRISPR dropout identified folate transporter as a synthetic lethal partner with serine/glycine depletion; knockdown abolished growth of resistant organoids.
Mice on serine/glycine‑free diet showed transient weight loss but no organ toxicity; cachexia markers GDF15 and IL6 were significantly lower (GDF15 ↓30%, IL6 ↓25%).
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Tumors with high PHGDH expression bypass exogenous serine/glycine dependence, rendering diet alone ineffective.
Adipocyte‑derived serine can rescue tumor cells; adipocyte‑specific PHGDH knockout lowers circulating serine.

combination_strategies.rationale: Both increase oxidative stress; serine/glycine depletion sensitizes cells to ROS‑mediated killing
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression in KRAS/LKB1 lung model (p<0.001)

combination_strategies.rationale: Blocks de novo serine synthesis that confers resistance to dietary restriction
combination_strategies.phenotype: Restores diet‑induced growth inhibition in PHGDH‑amplified breast cancer cells (45% tumor volume reduction)

open_questions: Will obesity‑induced adipocyte serine production diminish the efficacy of serine‑restricted diets?
What are the long‑term effects of chronic serine/glycine restriction on normal tissue function and immune responses?

talk_title: Exercise‑derived extracellular vesicles suppress breast cancer metastasis via MTSS1 and NME2

targets.dependency_context: Exercise EV‑enriched protein that inhibits migration of 41.2 breast cancer cells
Exercise EV‑enriched protein that reduces breast cancer cell migration

models.name: orthotopic 41.2 breast tumor model
models.genotype_or_key_features: female mice, voluntary wheel running vs locked wheel

models.name: exercise EV injection model
models.genotype_or_key_features: tumor‑bearing mice receiving EVs from exercised donors

MOA_summary: Voluntary exercise increases the number and protein cargo of circulating small extracellular vesicles. Proteomic profiling identified MTSS1 and NME2 as enriched in exercise EVs; these proteins suppress EMT‑associated pathways, reducing breast cancer cell migration and metastasis when delivered via EVs, recapitulating the anti‑tumor effect of physical activity.
key_findings: Mice with voluntary wheel access ran ~7 km/night and showed ~35% reduction in primary tumor volume compared with locked‑wheel controls (p<0.01).
Metastatic lesions were reduced by >70% in running mice; mice running >6.5 km/day had the greatest metastasis‑free survival (p<0.001).
Exercise‑derived EVs injected into non‑running tumor‑bearing mice reproduced the tumor‑suppressive effect (tumor volume ↓30%, p<0.01).
Proteomic analysis identified 322 proteins significantly up‑regulated in exercise EVs; MTSS1 and NME2 were selected as anti‑metastatic candidates.
Overexpression of MTSS1 or NME2 in 41.2 cells inhibited migration in scratch assays by 50%–60% (p<0.001).

combination_strategies.rationale: Exercise EVs may enhance anti‑metastatic effects without increasing immune exhaustion
combination_strategies.phenotype: Preliminary data suggest additive tumor growth inhibition

open_questions: What is the optimal intensity and duration of exercise to maximize anti‑metastatic EV cargo?
Can exercise EVs be engineered or concentrated for therapeutic use in patients unable to exercise?

talk_title: Diet‑microbiome interactions in colorectal cancer prevention and the coffee‑microbiome trial

speaker.name: Minyang Song
speaker.affiliation: Harvard T.H. Chan School of Public Health, Department of Nutrition

models.name: Nurses Health Study II
models.genotype_or_key_features: 20,000 women, longitudinal diet, stool, and health data

models.name: Coffee intervention trial (Stage I‑III CRC)
models.genotype_or_key_features: 80 patients randomized to encapsulated coffee (6 g/day) vs placebo for 3 months

MOA_summary: Diet shapes gut microbial composition; unhealthy diets enrich CRC‑associated taxa (Fusobacterium, Bacteroides) while healthy diets promote butyrate‑producing microbes. Coffee consumption strongly enriches Lawsonia intracellularis, altering microbial metabolites and plasma metabolome, which is associated with improved survival in CRC patients.
key_findings: Microbiome changes are modest in incident polyps (AUC 0.52) but become pronounced in prevalent polyps (AUC 0.67) and strongest in established CRC (AUC 0.85).
A CRC‑microbial diet score derived from CRC‑associated microbes correlates positively with Western diet (r=0.62) and inversely with prudent diet (r=‑0.58).
Higher CRC‑microbial diet score is associated with increased colon cancer risk after multivariable adjustment (HR 1.34 per SD, p=0.004).
Coffee intake after CRC diagnosis is linked to a 30% reduction in mortality (HR 0.70, 95% CI 0.55‑0.89) independent of caffeine content.
Coffee consumption is the strongest single dietary factor predicting a distinct microbiome signature, driven by enrichment of Lawsonia intracellularis.
open_questions: Does the coffee‑induced Lawsonia enrichment directly mediate survival benefit, or is it a surrogate for broader metabolic changes?
Can early‑life diet‑microbiome interventions prevent polyp formation in high‑risk groups such as Lynch syndrome carriers?

talk_title: Mutational signatures reveal hidden mutagenic and promoter exposures across cancers

speaker.name: Sir Mike Stratton
speaker.affiliation: Wellcome Sanger Institute, Senior Group Leader

targets.dependency_context: renal clear cell cancer in SE Europe

models.name: Mutagraphs cancer cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 5,000 participants, 8 cancer types, whole genome sequenced

models.name: Mouse carcinogen exposure study
models.genotype_or_key_features: 20 putative human carcinogens, tumor incidence assessed

MOA_summary: Stratton described how whole-genome sequencing enables extraction of mutational signatures that act as permanent footprints of past carcinogen exposures, revealing both known mutagens (tobacco, aristolochic acids, colibactin) and many silent promoter agents that drive clonal expansion without increasing point mutation burden.
key_findings: Esophageal squamous carcinoma shows 10-20-fold geographic incidence variation but no distinct mutational signature, suggesting non‑mutagenic promoters drive risk.
Renal clear cell cancer in SE Europe harbors SBS22A/B signatures linked to aristolochic acid exposure in ~70% of cases.
A novel SBS12 signature is found exclusively in Japan, cause unknown.
Colorectal cancer early‑onset cases have >30% prevalence of SBS88 (colibactin) signature, present in normal colon of ~40% of UK children.
Only 3 of 20 tested carcinogens produced mutational signatures in mouse tumors; the rest act as promoters/selectogens.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Promoter agents can drive tumorigenesis without increasing mutation burden, highlighting a non‑mutagenic route to cancer.
open_questions: What are the specific agents responsible for the geographic esophageal cancer disparity?
Can promoter activity be quantified in human epidemiological cohorts?

talk_title: Promotion and selectogen mechanisms in tumor initiation and clonal competition

speaker.name: Alan Balmain
speaker.affiliation: University of California, San Francisco, Professor

targets.dependency_context: DMBA‑initiated skin tumors promoted by TPA
spontaneous clones in normal mouse skin
spontaneous clones, selected in HRAS‑knockout mice

models.name: DMBA/TPA skin promotion model
models.genotype_or_key_features: single DMBA initiation (HRAS codon 61 mutation) followed by TPA promotion

models.name: HRAS knockout mice
models.genotype_or_key_features: HRAS null, used to reveal KRAS G13R tumorigenic potential

MOA_summary: Balmain demonstrated that a single mutagenic initiation event (DMBA‑induced HRAS Q61L) remains dormant for months, and promotion by TPA selects specific mutant clones (HRAS Q61L) to expand into tumors, while many spontaneous RAS mutations expand transiently but are eliminated unless HRAS is absent, highlighting clonal competition and promoter selectivity.
key_findings: DMBA induces a permanent HRAS Q61L initiation mutation that can remain silent for a year before promotion triggers rapid tumor formation.
Only HRAS Q61L clones persist and give tumors after TPA promotion; other RAS mutants expand transiently then disappear.
In HRAS knockout mice, KRAS G13R clones, normally non‑tumorigenic, give rise to tumors under TPA promotion.
Promoter agents can act without increasing point‑mutation burden, supporting the selectogen concept.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Spontaneous KRAS G12D/G13R clones are initially promoted but later disappear, indicating selective pressures that limit tumorigenic potential.
open_questions: What molecular pathways mediate the disappearance of spontaneously promoted clones?
Can human promoter exposures be identified using analogous clonal competition signatures?

talk_title: Epigenetic‑oncogenic cooperation reveals tissue‑specific therapeutic vulnerabilities

speaker.name: Karen Kochowski
speaker.affiliation: Harvard Medical School / Brigham and Women's Hospital, Professor

biomarkers.name: GATA3 expression
biomarkers.finding: Induced by EZH2+AKT inhibition; loss predicts resistance

biomarkers.name: BMF expression
biomarkers.finding: Cooperatively up‑regulated; knockout abolishes tumor regression

biomarkers.name: Mesenchymal signature
biomarkers.finding: Enriched in resistant TNBC lines; predicts lack of differentiation response

MOA_summary: Kochowski showed that simultaneous inhibition of EZH2 (PRC2) and oncogenic signaling (AKT, KRAS, PTEN, HER2) forces tumor cells into a differentiated, luminal‑like state via GATA3 induction and triggers an involution‑like apoptotic program mediated by JAK1‑STAT3‑BMF, leading to robust tumor regression in multiple tissue contexts.
readouts: IHC for GATA3, CK8/14, cleaved PARP
key_findings: EZH2 + AKT inhibition kills ~60% of TNBC models synergistically, with no single‑agent activity.
Combination induces GATA3, shifts cells to mature luminal phenotype, and suppresses basal/stem markers.
BMF is the top up‑regulated pro‑apoptotic gene; its knockout blocks tumor regression.
Predictive transcriptional signature (basal vs mesenchymal) separates sensitive from resistant TNBC lines.
Similar EZH2‑RAS or EZH2‑AKT combos are effective in KRAS‑mutant colorectal, PTEN‑null prostate, and HER2‑positive breast cancers, each exploiting tissue‑specific differentiation pathways.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Mesenchymal transcriptional programs confer resistance by preventing GATA3‑mediated differentiation.
HRAS knockout mice reveal that loss of competing oncogene can unmask otherwise silent KRAS clones.

combination_strategies.rationale: EZH2 inhibition derepresses differentiation genes; AKT inhibition blocks survival signaling, together inducing luminal differentiation and apoptosis.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression in TNBC xenografts and GEM models

combination_strategies.rationale: EZH2 inhibition sensitizes KRAS‑mutant colorectal cancer to MAPK blockade by promoting differentiation.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Tumor shrinkage in KRAS‑mutant CRC models

combination_strategies.rationale: In PTEN‑null prostate cancer, EZH2 inhibition removes epigenetic repression while mTORC1 blockade cuts metabolic support.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Regression of castration‑resistant prostate xenografts

combination_strategies.rationale: EZH2 inhibition reprograms HER2‑positive breast cancer to a state vulnerable to HER2 blockade.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced response in resistant HER2+ models

open_questions: Can the GATA3‑based predictive signature be prospectively validated in clinical trials?
What are the upstream regulators that determine why some tumors adopt a mesenchymal vs basal state?

talk_title: Mechanistic dissection of cancer cachexia: brain‑tumor communication and metabolic drivers

speaker.name: Eileen White
speaker.affiliation: Rutgers Cancer Institute / Princeton University, Deputy Director

targets.dependency_context: appetite suppression via GFRA1 neuronal circuit

biomarkers.name: GDF15
biomarkers.finding: Elevated in 'inflamed' cachexia subtype (red group) with high symptom burden

biomarkers.name: IL‑6
biomarkers.finding: Higher in inflamed cachexia patients; correlates with worse survival

biomarkers.name: Kynurenine/Serotonin ratio
biomarkers.finding: Cachectic mice show high kynurenine, low serotonin; SSRI restores serotonin without affecting weight loss

models.name: KPC pancreatic cancer model
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS G12D; TP53 R172H; develops cachexia

models.name: PDAC orthotopic implantation
models.genotype_or_key_features: Pancreatic tumor induces weight loss, reduced locomotion

models.name: UNCOVER & Lotus cachexia studies
models.genotype_or_key_features: n≈2,000 cancer patients, blood biomarkers, symptom questionnaires

clinical_data.value: GDF15 median 2.5‑fold higher in red subtype vs orange

MOA_summary: White described how tumor‑derived cytokines (IL‑6, GDF15) act on distinct brain circuits to suppress appetite and induce apathy, while metabolic reprogramming in cachectic mice diverts tryptophan toward the kynurenine pathway, lowering serotonin and driving depression; pharmacologic restoration of serotonin improves mood but does not reverse muscle/fat wasting, indicating separable mechanisms.
key_findings: High‑fat diet worsens cachexia in mouse models via tumor‑derived prostaglandin E2 signaling through the vagus nerve.
Cachectic mice exhibit low serotonin and high kynurenine; SSRI treatment restores serotonin and improves depressive‑like behavior without affecting weight loss.
Two cachexia subtypes identified in patients: inflamed (high GDF15, IL‑6, severe symptoms, poorer survival) vs non‑inflamed (weight loss without systemic inflammation).
Longitudinal patient data reveal dynamic weight trajectories; some patients cycle between loss and stabilization.
Brain‑tumor communication pathways (GDF15‑GFRA1, IL‑6‑dopamine suppression) are key drivers of appetite loss and apathy.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Serotonin restoration improves mood but does not impact the catabolic muscle/fat loss, indicating distinct downstream effectors.

combination_strategies.rationale: Block central appetite‑suppressing cytokine signaling to improve food intake
combination_strategies.phenotype: Potential increase in food intake; not yet tested in combination with SSRI

combination_strategies.rationale: Shift tryptophan metabolism away from kynurenine toward serotonin
combination_strategies.phenotype: Expected reduction in kynurenine, increase in serotonin; may improve mood and possibly muscle catabolism

open_questions: Can targeting the GDF15‑GFRA1 neuronal circuit improve both appetite and muscle mass?
What molecular mechanisms link elevated kynurenine to muscle wasting beyond depression?

talk_title: Neutrophil extracellular traps drive tumor necrosis and metastasis

speaker.name: Jose Drover
speaker.affiliation: Cancer Macroenvironment Lab, Francis Crick Institute

MOA_summary: Tumor-derived signals induce a myeloid skew producing LY6C-low vascular-restricted neutrophils that aggregate in tumor vessels, form NETs, occlude perfusion, create downstream hypoxia and necrosis, and promote perinecrotic tumor cell EMT and metastasis.
key_findings: Tumors induce LY6C‑low neutrophils that are restricted to the vasculature and form abundant NETs.
Neutrophil aggregates block vessel perfusion, leading to downstream hypoxia and necrosis in mouse tumor models.
Genetic or pharmacologic inhibition of NET formation (disulfiram, DNase I, platelet‑neutrophil interaction blockade) reduces tumor necrosis and lung metastasis.
Perinecrotic tumor cells upregulate hypoxia, EMT, and TGF‑β pathways, enhancing metastatic traits.

combination_strategies.rationale: inhibits NET formation
combination_strategies.phenotype: reduced tumor necrosis and metastasis in mice

combination_strategies.rationale: degrades NET DNA
combination_strategies.phenotype: decreased necrosis and metastatic spread

open_questions: What molecular cues trigger the emergence of LY6C‑low neutrophils beyond emergency myelopoiesis?
Do NETs in necrotic zones directly modulate tumor cell signaling beyond hypoxia?

talk_title: Ischemic tumor regions reprogram macrophage metabolism to suppress T‑cell killing

biomarkers.name: N1‑acetylspermidine
biomarkers.finding: 100‑fold increase in ischemic macrophage supernatants; suppresses T‑cell killing of tumor cells

MOA_summary: In ischemic tumor regions, macrophages upregulate arginase‑1, diverting ornithine into polyamine synthesis; excess polyamines are secreted as N1‑acetylspermidine, which tumor cells convert via PAOX to a metabolite that impairs antigen‑specific T‑cell cytotoxicity without altering MHC‑I expression.
key_findings: Ischemic macrophages express high ARG1 and secrete N1‑acetylspermidine.
N1‑acetylspermidine suppresses antigen‑specific T‑cell killing of tumor cells in vitro.
PAOX in tumor cells is required for the immunosuppressive effect; PAOX knockout restores T‑cell killing.
Adoptive transfer of antigen‑specific T cells dramatically reduces growth of PAOX‑deficient tumors.

combination_strategies.rationale: prevent conversion of acetylated polyamine to immunosuppressive metabolite
combination_strategies.phenotype: restores T‑cell killing in co‑culture

open_questions: Does N1‑acetylspermidine affect other immune subsets beyond CD8 T cells?
Can targeting ARG1 or PAOX improve immunotherapy efficacy in vivo?

talk_title: Tumor‑derived angiopoietin‑like 7 drives necrotic core formation and metastatic dissemination
MOA_summary: Tumor cells upregulate ANGPTL7 in necrotic cores, promoting dilated intratumoral vessels, neutrophil NET formation, and TGF‑β enrichment, which together facilitate perinecrotic tumor cell EMT and release of CTC clusters.
key_findings: Necrotic core size in rat 4T1 tumors spikes between days 22‑27 and correlates with acute CTC surge.
Tumor‑derived ANGPTL7 is highly expressed in necrotic cores; knockdown reduces necrosis, dilated vessels, CTC clusters, and lung metastasis.
ANGPTL7 knockout lowers total TGF‑β and pSMAD signaling in tumors, shifting macrophage polarization toward inflammatory phenotype.
Human breast cancer analysis shows reparative necrosis changes (distinct from coagulative necrosis) predict poor survival, especially with lymphovascular invasion.

combination_strategies.rationale: prevent necrotic core formation and downstream metastasis
combination_strategies.phenotype: reduced necrosis, CTCs, and lung mets in mice

open_questions: Can pharmacologic ANGPTL7 blockade improve outcomes when combined with chemotherapy or immunotherapy?
What is the precise mechanism linking ANGPTL7 to TGF‑β activation in the necrotic niche?

talk_title: Translational rheostat driven by 4E‑BP1 regulates cancer metastasis

targets.dependency_context: 4E-BP1 proficient vs deficient breast cancer cells

MOA_summary: Loss of 4E‑BP1 suppresses breast cancer metastasis by reducing translation of weak mRNAs such as pannexin‑1; paradoxically, 4E‑BP1 activation promotes early metastatic steps (invasion, anoikis resistance) through a Goldilocks level of mTORC1 signaling that selectively up‑regulates translation of long 5'‑UTR, uORF‑rich transcripts.
key_findings: 4E‑BP1 double knockout mice crossed with polyoma middle T breast cancer showed larger primary tumors but dramatically reduced lung metastases compared with wild‑type controls.
Re‑expression of 4E‑BP1 rescued metastatic potential, whereas a constitutively active 4E‑BP1‑4A mutant failed to do so, indicating that precise mTORC1‑mediated tuning of 4E‑BP1 is required.

4E‑BP1‑proficient cells displayed increased invasion and anoikis resistance in vitro, while tail‑vein injection showed no difference in outgrowth, pinpointing early metastatic steps as the affected stage.
Translatome analysis revealed that 4E‑BP1‑dependent up‑regulated mRNAs have longer 5'‑UTRs and more upstream open reading frames, exemplified by pannexin‑1 protein increase without RNA level change.

Serum‑dose and mTOR inhibitor titration experiments demonstrated a biphasic “Goldilocks” response where intermediate mTORC1 activity maximized pannexin‑1 translation, whereas strong inhibition reduced invasion.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Constitutively active 4E‑BP1‑4A mutant reduced invasion, suggesting that hyper‑activation of the translational brake can be anti‑metastatic.
Strong mTORC1 inhibition decreased invasion, indicating that complete pathway blockade may avoid unintended promotion of metastasis.
open_questions: What molecular mechanism allows 4E‑BP1‑dependent mRNAs with long 5'‑UTRs to be selectively up‑regulated under intermediate mTORC1 signaling?
Can pharmacologic modulation of the 4E‑BP1 rheostat be exploited therapeutically without compromising primary tumor control?

talk_title: tRNA U34 modification by ELP3 shapes cancer proteome via dicodon regulation
MOA_summary: ELP3‑mediated U34 modification of a subset of tRNAs enhances elongation of codons requiring these modified tRNAs; loss of ELP3 causes ribosome stalling on dicodons that pair a U34‑dependent codon with a neighboring codon, leading to reduced protein output of cell‑cycle genes despite increased polysome association.
key_findings: CRISPR‑mediated loss of ELP3 reduces U34 tRNA modification and impairs proliferation of transformed but not non‑transformed cells.
Proteomics showed down‑regulation of many proteins despite higher polysome association, indicating elongation blockade.
Dicodon analysis (PosNet) explained ~30% of translational regulation, double the ~15% explained by single‑codon usage alone.
Six specific dicodons containing a U34‑dependent codon were enriched in down‑regulated proteins; closer spacing of dicodons correlated with stronger repression.
mRNAs enriched for these dicodons also possessed longer 5'‑UTRs and lower initiation efficiency, linking initiation and elongation control.
Knockdown of ELP3 increased mitotic defects and activated the integrated stress response (eEF2α‑P, ATF4) without rescuing protein output.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: ELP3 loss selectively impairs cancer cell proliferation while sparing normal cells, suggesting a therapeutic window.
open_questions: What additional RNA features beyond dicodons contribute to the remaining unexplained translational regulation?
Can pharmacologic inhibition of ELP3 or modulation of U34 tRNA modification be achieved safely in vivo?

talk_title: Selective translation of CaMK2D drives drug tolerance and metastasis in ER‑positive breast cancer

biomarkers.name: CaMK2D protein
biomarkers.finding: High CaMK2D protein levels in post‑hormone‑therapy tumors correlated with worse overall survival (HR 1.8, p=0.004) in a cohort of 400 ER‑positive patients

models.name: MCF7, T47D, MCF7‑TAMR
models.genotype_or_key_features: ERα positive; some with ERα RNA‑binding mutant

models.name: ERα‑positive breast cancer PDX
models.genotype_or_key_features: treated with fulvestrant ± verapamil or zotatifin

clinical_data.comparator: control TSC cells (5/8 mice with liver/brain mets)

MOA_summary: ERα binds the 3'‑UTR of CaMK2D mRNA while eIF4A engages a structured 5'‑UTR G‑quadruplex; this looping enhances translation of CaMK2D during drug‑tolerant states, driving calcium‑dependent survival, invasion and metastasis, which can be blocked by calcium channel inhibition (verapamil) or eIF4A inhibition (zotatifin).
key_findings: Therapy‑surviving cells (TSC) exposed to high‑dose fulvestrant up‑regulate translation of CaMK2D without changes in mRNA levels, as shown by ribosome profiling and proteomics.
CaMK2D protein, but not its mRNA, correlates with poor prognosis in a 400‑patient ER‑positive breast cancer cohort.
ERα directly binds the 3'‑UTR of CaMK2D; loss of this RNA‑binding activity reduces TSC formation, which can be rescued by CaMK2D overexpression.
eIF4A interacts more strongly with ERα in tamoxifen‑resistant cells; eIF4A inhibition by zotatifin suppresses CaMK2D translation and eliminates TSCs in vitro and in PDX models.
Combination of fulvestrant with verapamil or zotatifin markedly delays tumor regrowth and prevents metastasis to liver and brain in mouse models, whereas single agents show modest effects.
Knockdown of CaMK2D abolishes the metastatic capability of TSCs in tail‑vein injection assays.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: CaMK2D up‑regulation is specific to therapy‑surviving cells and not observed in naïve cancer cells.
eIF4A inhibition selectively targets the translational program of TSCs without affecting bulk tumor proliferation.

combination_strategies.rationale: Calcium channel blockade should suppress CaMK2D‑driven survival of tolerant cells
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced TSC colony formation in vitro; modest tumor growth delay as single agent

combination_strategies.rationale: Molecular glue inhibition of eIF4A blocks translation of CaMK2D and other structured 5'‑UTR oncogenes
combination_strategies.phenotype: Strong tumor growth inhibition (≈70% reduction) and complete abrogation of metastasis in PDX when combined with fulvestrant

open_questions: What additional mRNAs are co‑regulated with CaMK2D via the ERα‑eIF4A looping mechanism?
Can biomarkers of eIF4A activity predict response to zotatifin in patients receiving endocrine therapy?

patent_or_ip_hints: US20250012345A1 – ERα‑mediated translation control of CaMK2D

talk_title: Phase 1 trial of trastuzumab deruxtecan (DS-8201A) plus olaparib in HER2‑expressing advanced solid tumors

biomarkers.name: HER2 IHC 2+
biomarkers.finding: required for enrollment; majority of patients expressed HER2 IHC 2+

models.name: CTEP Trial 10355 dose‑escalation cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: HER2‑positive endometrial or ovarian cancer, median 3.5 prior lines

clinical_data.value: 3 DLTs at DS‑8201A 5.4 mg/kg + olaparib 100 mg BID continuous

MOA_summary: Trastuzumab deruxtecan delivers a potent exatecan TOP1‑inhibitor payload to HER2‑expressing tumor cells; concurrent PARP inhibition with olaparib blocks repair of TOP1‑DNA cleavage complexes, enhancing DNA damage and tumor cell death while staggered dosing reduces systemic hematologic toxicity.
key_findings: Objective response rate of 54% (confirmed ORR 46%) observed in 28 heavily pre‑treated HER2‑positive endometrial and ovarian cancer patients.
Clinical benefit rate reached 67% with median duration of response 11.2 months.
Staggered (gap) olaparib schedule (days 8‑14) was feasible and reduced Grade 3‑4 hematologic toxicities compared with continuous olaparib dosing.
Six‑month PFS estimated at 88.2% and median PFS 15.2 months despite median of 3.5 prior therapy lines.
Pneumonitis occurred in 7 patients (all low‑grade) and was not dose‑dependent.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Responses were seen across HER2 IHC 1+ to 2+ levels, indicating activity even in low HER2 expression.
Prior PARP inhibitor exposure did not preclude response, but detailed subgroup analysis was limited.

combination_strategies.rationale: PARP inhibition blocks repair of TOP1‑DNA cleavage complexes generated by the ADC payload, synergizing DNA damage.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved ORR and reduced hematologic toxicity with staggered schedule versus continuous dosing.

open_questions: Will the combination outperform trastuzumab deruxtecan monotherapy in a randomized trial?
What is the optimal PARP inhibitor dose and schedule to maximize synergy while minimizing toxicity?

talk_title: First‑in‑human study of SOS 6010, an EGFR‑targeted ADC, in advanced nasopharyngeal carcinoma

targets.dependency_context: Advanced NPC, pre‑treated with platinum and immunotherapy

models.name: Phase 1 NPC expansion cohort (abstract CTO36)
models.genotype_or_key_features: 56 enrolled, 54 efficacy‑evaluable; EGFR expression not required

clinical_data.value: high incidence; specific rates not quantified

MOA_summary: SOS 6010 is a humanized IgG1 ADC that binds EGFR, is internalized, and releases a high‑potency, membrane‑permeable TOP1‑inhibitor payload (GS1) via a cleavable linker, causing DNA damage and tumor cell death, with a by‑stander effect in EGFR‑negative cells.
key_findings: Overall ORR of 31.5% in heavily pre‑treated NPC, with higher response at the 4.8 mg/kg dose (36.6%).
Median PFS of 7.5 months and median OS of 14.9 months (4.2 mg/kg cohort; OS not reached at 4.8 mg/kg).
Grade 3+ hematologic toxicities were the most common adverse events, but treatment discontinuation was low.
No clear correlation between baseline EGFR expression level and response, suggesting a possible by‑stander effect.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Responses observed in patients with low or absent EGFR expression, indicating potential by‑stander activity.
High‑grade hematologic toxicities reflect the TOP1 payload class effect.

combination_strategies.rationale: Potential synergistic immune activation with DNA damage‑induced immunogenic cell death.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Planned first‑line combination trials in NPC.

open_questions: Will EGFR expression or EGFR‑targeted monoclonal antibody pretreatment predict response to SOS 6010?
Can the hematologic toxicity be mitigated with growth‑factor support?

talk_title: First‑in‑human Phase 1 study of QLS‑5132, a claudin‑6‑targeting ADC, in platinum‑resistant ovarian cancer

models.name: Phase 1 QLS‑5132 dose‑escalation and expansion (PROC cohort)
models.genotype_or_key_features: 28 patients, 92.9% ovarian cancer, median 3 prior lines, 89.3% prior bevacizumab, 22 received PARP inhibitor

clinical_data.metric: ORR (overall response rate) across all dose levels

MOA_summary: QLS‑5132 couples a humanized anti‑claudin‑6 antibody to a TOP1‑inhibitor payload via a stable hydrazone linker (DAR 8); selective binding to claudin‑6‑positive tumor cells leads to internalization and release of the payload, causing DNA damage and tumor regression with a therapeutic window due to minimal normal tissue expression of claudin‑6.
key_findings: Confirmed ORR of 55.6% at the 4.8 mg/kg dose in platinum‑resistant ovarian cancer.
Median relative dose intensity 99.8% and PK exposure increased proportionally from 1.6 to 6.4 mg/kg.
Grade 3+ adverse events occurred in 32.1% of patients; most were mild/moderate GI or hematologic toxicities.
Responses were observed even in two patients with undetectable CLDN6 expression, suggesting possible by‑stander effect.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: No clear correlation between CLDN6 expression level and response; activity seen in CLDN6‑negative tumors.

combination_strategies.rationale: Potential synergistic immune activation after DNA damage induced by TOP1 payload.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Planned first‑line combination trials in NPC and other solid tumors.

open_questions: What is the mechanistic basis for responses in CLDN6‑negative tumors?
Will earlier‑line use improve efficacy given potential up‑regulation of CLDN6 after platinum exposure?

talk_title: ARTEMIS‑101: Phase 1 study of REVSYBOO (B7‑H3 ADC) plus atezolizumab in pre‑treated non‑squamous NSCLC

targets.dependency_context: Advanced non‑squamous NSCLC without actionable genomic alterations

biomarkers.name: B7‑H3 expression
biomarkers.finding: Efficacy observed across low and high B7‑H3 levels

biomarkers.name: PD‑L1 TPS
biomarkers.finding: ORR 62.5% in PD‑L1‑positive vs 33.3% in PD‑L1‑negative subgroup

models.name: ARTEMIS‑101 expansion cohort (NSCLC)
models.genotype_or_key_features: 40 patients, median 1 prior line, 75% prior immunotherapy, non‑squamous NSCLC without EGFR/ALK/ROS1 alterations

MOA_summary: Tagretucumab (REVSYBOO) is a B7‑H3‑directed ADC delivering a tropomodulin‑1 TOP1 inhibitor via a protease‑cleavable linker (DAR 4); combined with atezolizumab, PD‑L1 blockade may enhance anti‑tumor immunity while the ADC induces DNA damage in B7‑H3‑positive tumor cells.
key_findings: Confirmed ORR of 47.1% (n=34) with median PFS of 14 months in heavily pre‑treated NSCLC.
Efficacy observed across B7‑H3 expression levels and irrespective of prior immunotherapy exposure.
Grade 3+ TRAEs occurred in 70% of patients, predominantly hematologic; ILD was low‑grade and infrequent.
12‑month PFS rate of 67.7% exceeds historical benchmarks for second‑line docetaxel or REVSYBOO monotherapy.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Higher ORR in PD‑L1‑positive subgroup (62.5%) suggests additive benefit of checkpoint inhibition.
Responses also seen in patients with primary resistance to prior immunotherapy.

combination_strategies.rationale: Checkpoint blockade may synergize with ADC‑induced immunogenic cell death and overcome resistance.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved ORR and PFS versus historical monotherapy data.

open_questions: Will the combination demonstrate superiority over standard second‑line chemotherapy in a randomized phase 3 trial?
What biomarkers (B7‑H3, PD‑L1, tumor mutational burden) best predict benefit?

talk_title: Therapy‑induced mutagenesis underlying clonal hematopoiesis and second cancers in pediatric cancer survivors
MOA_summary: Sun's group used deep targeted sequencing and single‑cell mutational signature analysis to show that therapy‑related clonal hematopoiesis is accelerated in pediatric cancer survivors, with STAT3 hotspot mutations in Hodgkin lymphoma survivors linked to a SBS25 signature caused by procapsid exposure, and distinct cell‑type enrichment patterns (e.g., CD4/CD8 T cells). They also demonstrated elevated mutation burdens and therapy‑associated signatures (cisplatin, nitrogen mustard) in secondary neoplasms, linking specific chemotherapies to mutational signatures and structural alterations such as NF2 disruption in meningioma.
key_findings: Accelerated clonal hematopoiesis observed in ~3000 pediatric cancer survivors versus controls.
Therapy‑related STAT3 hotspot mutations in Hodgkin lymphoma survivors are 3‑10‑fold enriched in CD4/CD8 T cells and show 100% confidence of SBS25 signature linkage to procapsid exposure.
DNMT3A mutations display cell‑type specific enrichment (monocytes vs double‑negative CD3+ T cells).
Secondary neoplasms (breast, meningioma, thyroid) exhibit elevated SNV/indel burdens and cisplatin‑related signatures, confirming long‑term mutagenic imprint of childhood therapy.
In vitro cyclophosphamide treatment of MCF10A cells increases mutation burden (~600 SNVs) and reproduces SBS5‑like flat signatures.
NF2 structural disruption and kinase fusions are enriched in radiation‑associated secondary meningioma and thyroid cancers, respectively.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: STAT3 mutant T‑cell clones may contribute to immune dysfunction and lifelong graft‑versus‑host‑like risk in Hodgkin lymphoma survivors.
Platinum‑induced SBS5‑like signatures appear dose‑dependent but are attenuated in combination with cytarabine.

combination_strategies.rationale: Assess interaction of platinum with non‑mutagenic chemotherapy
combination_strategies.phenotype: slightly reduced mutation burden compared with platinum alone

open_questions: Can dosage and timing of specific chemotherapies be quantitatively linked to SBS burden in individual survivors?
What is the functional impact of STAT3‑mutant T‑cell clones on long‑term immune competence?

talk_title: Liver‑specific platinum mutagenesis in children revealed by duplex (NanoSeq) sequencing

speaker.name: Sam Benatti
speaker.affiliation: Wellcome Sanger Institute / Cambridge Children Research Institute

models.name: Pediatric hepatoblastoma resections
models.genotype_or_key_features: 155 normal liver biopsies from children treated with platinum +/- SDS

clinical_data.value: present in 6/6 platinum‑exposed recurrent hepatoblastoma tumors

MOA_summary: Benatti's team applied duplex (NanoSeq) sequencing to normal pediatric liver tissue after platinum chemotherapy, uncovering a novel liver‑specific mutational signature distinct from canonical platinum signatures; this signature correlates with exposure, is absent in unexposed liver, and is detectable in recurrent hepatoblastoma tumors, indicating pervasive platinum‑induced mutagenesis and potential functional driver‑like mutations in normal liver cells.
key_findings: Normal liver tissue from platinum‑treated children shows mutation burdens comparable to adult liver, far exceeding untreated pediatric liver.
A novel liver‑specific platinum mutational signature was identified, distinct from known SBS31/35 signatures.
The liver‑specific signature is present in all platinum‑exposed normal liver samples and in six recurrent hepatoblastoma tumors previously treated with cisplatin.
Cell‑line experiments confirm the signature appears in liver tumor cells exposed to cisplatin but not in breast cancer cells, indicating tissue specificity.
Approximately 1,500 coding variants with potential driver impact were detected in normal liver, most attributable to the novel signature.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: The liver‑specific signature is absent after carboplatin alone, suggesting differential mutagenic mechanisms between platinum agents.
Signature detection depends on tissue type, highlighting organ‑specific DNA repair or adduct processing.

combination_strategies.rationale: Assess whether ototoxicity‑preventing chelator modulates platinum‑induced mutagenesis
combination_strategies.phenotype: no clear reduction of signature observed

open_questions: What molecular mechanisms underlie the liver‑specific platinum signature?
Do the identified driver‑like mutations contribute to late‑onset liver disease or secondary malignancies?

talk_title: Single‑cell whole‑genome and transcriptome analysis of mutagenesis in hematopoietic stem cells of childhood cancer survivors

biomarkers.name: SBS87 (thiopurine) signature
biomarkers.finding: present in a subset of HSCs from ALL patients treated with 6‑MP

biomarkers.name: Platinum signature
biomarkers.finding: detected in HSCs of survivors exposed to cisplatin/carboplatin

models.name: Childhood cancer survivor cohort with therapy‑related AML
models.genotype_or_key_features: samples at diagnosis, post‑treatment, and AML onset

models.name: Umbilical cord blood HSCs
models.genotype_or_key_features: treated in vitro with cyclophosphamide (mafiosfamide) or 6‑MP

MOA_summary: Van Boxtel's group combined primary‑template‑directed amplification (PTA) single‑cell whole‑genome sequencing with matched transcriptomics to quantify chemotherapy‑induced mutagenesis in hematopoietic stem cells; they identified platinum‑ and thiopurine‑specific signatures (SBS31/35, SBS87) in subsets of cells, showed that mutation burden rises after therapy but can regress over years, and linked differential expression of DNA‑repair and apoptotic pathways to cells bearing these signatures, suggesting treatment‑specific transcriptional programs modulate mutagenesis.
key_findings: All examined ALL survivors show increased mutation burden post‑treatment (up to 3‑fold), driven by platinum and thiopurine signatures.
Thiopurine (SBS87) signature appears only in a subset of HSCs, whereas platinum signatures are more ubiquitous.
Mutation burden can decline over a decade, returning to baseline levels, suggesting selective loss or replacement of heavily mutated clones.
Differential expression analysis reveals enrichment of DNA‑repair, apoptosis, and autophagy pathways in cells with high thiopurine or clock‑like mutagenesis, without differences in cell‑cycle phase.
In vitro exposure of cord‑blood HSCs to mafosfamide (active cyclophosphamide) recapitulates a flat, clock‑like signature, confirming drug‑specific mutagenic patterns.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Cells lacking the thiopurine signature show higher expression of DNA‑repair genes, suggesting a protective transcriptional program.
Clonality of HSCs remains polyclonal post‑treatment, indicating no dominant expansion of mutated clones.

combination_strategies.rationale: Assess mutagenic potential of antiviral drugs used during HSCT
combination_strategies.phenotype: identified novel mutational signatures in HSCT patients

open_questions: What molecular pathways determine why some HSCs acquire high mutation loads while others remain protected?
Can targeted modulation of identified DNA‑repair pathways reduce chemotherapy‑induced mutagenesis in vivo?

talk_title: Aspirin paradox in colorectal cancer initiation versus metastasis

speaker.name: Omer Yilmaz
speaker.affiliation: Koch Institute for Integrative Cancer Research, MIT; Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center, Boston

biomarkers.name: PIK3CA exon 9/20 mutation
biomarkers.finding: Aspirin started early after diagnosis reduced death in mutant tumors but not wild‑type

biomarkers.name: Stage 4 disease
biomarkers.finding: Aspirin increased overall mortality >2‑fold in stage 4 colorectal cancer (ASPREE)

models.name: AKP (APC‑K‑RAS‑p53) colorectal organoids
models.genotype_or_key_features: APC loss, KRAS G12D, TP53 mutation

clinical_data.population: stage 4 colorectal cancer patients on aspirin

MOA_summary: Aspirin lowers tumor‑cell prostaglandin E2 (PGE2) by inhibiting PTGS1/2, which reduces metastatic seeding but paradoxically enhances outgrowth of established liver micrometastases via a hepatic stellate cell (HSC)–NK cell circuit; loss of tumor‑derived PGE2 expands NK‑mediated clearance, whereas aspirin‑mediated PGE2 depletion permits HSC‑driven NK suppression, promoting metastatic growth.
key_findings: Genetic ablation of PTGS1/2 in AKP organoids reduced PGE2 and decreased number of liver metastases but increased size of each lesion.
Low‑dose aspirin recapitulated PTGS1/2 loss, lowering primary tumor PGE2 and enlarging liver metastatic lesions.
HPGD knockout (high PGE2) organoids produced fewer and smaller liver metastases; pharmacologic 15‑PGDH inhibition (SW033291) mirrored this effect.
Metastatic outgrowth advantage of PTGS‑deficient cells required an intact immune system; NK‑cell depletion abolished the size increase, implicating NK cells as mediators.
Depletion of hepatic stellate cells (HSCs) removed the anti‑metastatic effect of high PGE2, indicating HSC‑derived signals (e.g., CXCL12) modulate NK activity.
Clinical ASPREE data show aspirin increases mortality >2‑fold in stage 4 colorectal cancer, supporting a stage‑dependent pro‑metastatic effect.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Aspirin’s anti‑metastatic benefit is lost in advanced disease where tumor‑derived PGE2 is already low, leading to paradoxical promotion of outgrowth.
NK‑cell dependence suggests resistance may arise in immunocompromised or NK‑deficient microenvironments.

combination_strategies.rationale: Increase tumor PGE2 to suppress metastatic outgrowth via NK activation
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced number and size of liver metastases in portal seeding model

combination_strategies.rationale: Counteract aspirin‑mediated NK suppression in liver
combination_strategies.phenotype: Predicted restoration of anti‑metastatic immunity

open_questions: What specific HSC‑derived factors (beyond CXCL12) mediate NK suppression in low‑PGE2 environments?
Can selective modulation of platelet‑derived thromboxane A2 replicate aspirin’s preventive effect without promoting metastasis?
How does route of aspirin administration (systemic vs. local) influence hepatic PGE2 dynamics and metastasis?

talk_title: Platelet activation as a biomarker for aspirin chemoprevention in cancer

biomarkers.name: 11‑dehydro thromboxane B2 (UTXM)
biomarkers.finding: Baseline levels higher in adjuvant GI cancer patients vs healthy; suppressed by aspirin during run‑in; rise again in placebo arm

biomarkers.name: PIK3CA exon 9/20 mutation
biomarkers.finding: Aspirin improves disease‑free survival in mutant colorectal cancer (ASCOT trial HR≈0.5)

models.name: ADD‑ASPIRIN
models.genotype_or_key_features: adjuvant patients, 4 tumor types, aspirin 100 mg vs 300 mg vs placebo

MOA_summary: Low‑dose aspirin irreversibly acetylates platelet COX‑1, suppressing thromboxane A2 production and platelet activation; reduced platelet‑derived pro‑tumor signals lower early adenoma formation and may enhance immune clearance of micrometastases. Urinary 11‑dehydro‑TXB2 (UTXM) reflects platelet activation and predicts aspirin benefit, especially in PIK3CA‑mutant colorectal cancers.
key_findings: Platelet‑specific COX‑1 knockout mice crossed with ApcMin develop fewer and smaller adenomas, linking platelet COX‑1 to early carcinogenesis.
Urinary UTXM levels are elevated in adjuvant GI cancer patients at baseline and decrease with aspirin run‑in, rising again in placebo arm.
PIK3CA exon 9/20 mutations identify colorectal cancer patients who derive a ~50% disease‑free survival benefit from aspirin (ASCOT trial).
ADD‑ASPIRIN trial shows dose‑response suppression of UTXM with 100 mg and 300 mg aspirin, supporting biomarker‑guided dosing.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Higher UTXM levels correlate with BMI, smoking, and inflammatory markers, suggesting lifestyle‑driven resistance to aspirin benefit.
PIK3CA‑wild‑type tumors do not show aspirin‑mediated survival advantage, indicating molecular selectivity.

combination_strategies.rationale: Enhance aspirin efficacy in PIK3CA‑mutant tumors by targeting downstream signaling
combination_strategies.phenotype: Potential additive improvement in disease‑free survival

open_questions: Can longitudinal UTXM monitoring guide aspirin dose adjustments in real time?
What is the mechanistic link between platelet activation and NK/T‑cell anti‑tumor activity?
Will aspirin benefit extend to non‑GI cancers with high platelet activation?

talk_title: Targeting COX‑2/PGE2 and PI3K pathways in colorectal cancer prevention

speaker.name: Raymond DuBois
speaker.affiliation: Medical University of South Carolina, Hollings Cancer Center

biomarkers.name: COX‑2 expression
biomarkers.finding: Higher COX‑2 levels in adenomas predict greater response to celecoxib

biomarkers.name: PD‑1 positivity on CD8 T cells
biomarkers.finding: Celecoxib treatment reduces PD‑1 expression and increases total CD8 T‑cell infiltrates in mouse adenomas

models.name: ASCULT
models.genotype_or_key_features: stage B/C colorectal cancer, aspirin 200 mg vs placebo

models.name: Alaska trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: PIK3CA‑mutant colorectal cancer, aspirin 160 mg vs placebo

MOA_summary: COX‑2–derived PGE2 drives adenoma formation and immune evasion via EP4‑mediated PI3K‑AKT‑NF‑κB activation, up‑regulating PD‑1 on CD8 T cells and macrophages; celecoxib or EP4 antagonism reduces PGE2, lowers PD‑1 expression, expands effector T‑cell infiltrates, and synergizes with PI3K inhibition to suppress tumorigenesis.
key_findings: Platelet‑specific COX‑1 knockout reduces adenoma number and size in ApcMin mice, linking platelet COX‑1 to early tumorigenesis.
Celecoxib (400‑1600 mg/day) markedly decreases adenoma burden in mouse models and in FAP patients, with ~80% response in a subset.
EP4 antagonist AE3208 mirrors celecoxib’s effect, confirming PGE2‑EP4 as the critical downstream axis.
PGE2 up‑regulates PD‑1 on CD8 T cells and macrophages; celecoxib treatment lowers PD‑1 and increases total T‑cell infiltrates in mouse adenomas.
PIK3CA mutations co‑occur with COX‑2 overexpression; aspirin/celecoxib improves DFS in PIK3CA‑mutant colorectal cancer (HR 0.5).
Combination of PI3K inhibitor with celecoxib yields additive tumor suppression in preclinical models.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: High‑dose celecoxib required for COX‑2 inhibition may cause cardiovascular toxicity; low‑dose aspirin primarily affects platelet COX‑1.
Only ~20% of FAP patients show robust adenoma regression, suggesting genetic or microenvironmental resistance.

combination_strategies.rationale: Co‑targeting PI3K and COX‑2/PGE2 pathways addresses synergistic oncogenic signaling
combination_strategies.phenotype: Additive reduction in adenoma number and size in mouse models

combination_strategies.rationale: Directly block PGE2 signaling downstream of COX‑2
combination_strategies.phenotype: Similar adenoma suppression as celecoxib with potentially fewer cardiovascular effects

open_questions: What determines the subset of patients who respond dramatically to COX‑2 inhibition?
Can EP4 antagonists replace NSAIDs/COX‑2 inhibitors to avoid cardiovascular risk?
How does PGE2‑mediated PD‑1 up‑regulation interact with checkpoint inhibitor therapy?

talk_title: Adoptive T cell immunotherapy strategies and preclinical findings

models.name: TIL therapy vs ipilimumab trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: 84 patients per arm, relapsed after checkpoint blockade

MOA_summary: Adoptive transfer of expanded tumor-infiltrating lymphocytes (TILs) or genetically engineered T cells (TCR or CAR) provides large numbers of tumor-specific cytotoxic T cells that can directly kill cancer cells; CAR constructs redirect T cells to surface antigens such as CD19 or mesothelin, while TCR engineering targets intracellular antigens like NY-ESO-1. Preclinical data show that Th17-polarized CD4 T cells can synergize with endogenous CD8 responses to achieve durable tumor regression in the B16-F10 melanoma model.
key_findings: TIL therapy achieved a 6‑month PFS of 52.7% versus ipilimumab in 84 relapsed patients (Phase II trial).
CD19 CAR T cells cleared CD19‑positive leukemia in bone marrow with durable remission observed >170 days post‑infusion.
Th17‑polarized CD4 T cells, when adoptively transferred into B16‑F10 melanoma‑bearing mice, produced superior tumor control compared with Th0, Th1, or Th9 subsets.
RNA‑seq of Th17‑treated tumors revealed up‑regulation of chemokines and antigen‑presentation pathways, suggesting recruitment of endogenous CD8 T cells.

talk_title: Clinical mechanisms of response and resistance to cancer immunotherapy
MOA_summary: Zhamarin described how tumor antigens are presented to T cells, leading to activation, proliferation, and tumor cell lysis, and outlined mechanisms of primary and adaptive resistance including loss of antigen presentation, interferon signaling suppression, and immune-excluded microenvironments.
key_findings: Primary resistance is associated with tumors lacking T cell infiltration (cold tumors) due to absent antigen presentation or immunosuppressive microenvironment.
Adaptive resistance involves upregulation of PD-L1 driven by IFN‑γ from activated T cells, creating an exhausted T cell phenotype.
Proximity analysis of PD‑1+ T cells to PD‑L1+ cells within 30‑50 µm predicts response to checkpoint blockade in endometrial cancer.
Tumor mutational burden and MSI‑high status correlate with higher response rates to PD‑1 inhibitors, but ~30% of high‑TMB patients do not respond.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Loss of MHC expression or interferon‑γ signaling in tumor cells leads to immune editing and re‑emergence of cold phenotypes.
Tumor‑derived cytokines and chemokines can recruit suppressive immune cells, contributing to exclusion.
open_questions: How can we reliably measure functional T cell–tumor cell interactions in routine clinical samples?
What minimal biomarker panels can replace complex spatial analyses for predicting checkpoint inhibitor response?

talk_title: Dual-compartment targeting GPNMB CAR T cells for glioblastoma

speaker.name: Seela Singh
speaker.affiliation: Ontario Institute for Cancer Research, Toronto, Canada

models.name: BT972-derived GSC PDX
models.genotype_or_key_features: GPNMB+ glioma stem cells, murine macrophage-deficient NOD scid mice

MOA_summary: GPNMB is co‑expressed on glioma stem cells and immunosuppressive tumor‑associated macrophages; a GPNMB‑directed CAR T cell simultaneously eliminates both compartments, leading to complete tumor clearance in orthotopic GBM models and durable survival without skin toxicity.
key_findings: GPNMB is up‑regulated in GBM and enriched on both glioma stem cells and M2‑like macrophages.
CRISPR knockout of GPNMB reduces proliferation of patient‑derived GSCs and shifts tumor cell states away from mesenchymal programs.
Intracranial delivery of 1 × 10⁶ GPNMB CAR T cells eradicated orthotopic GBM PDX tumors and cleared GPNMB‑positive macrophages.
In syngeneic GL261 mice, GPNMB CAR T induced complete remission without observable skin toxicity.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Antigen escape observed in a minority of mice with tumor recurrence.
CAR T efficacy reduced in models with low GPNMB expression on macrophages.
open_questions: Can combining GPNMB CAR T with checkpoint blockade prevent antigen escape?
What is the optimal dosing schedule to maintain efficacy while minimizing neurotoxicity?

talk_title: Macrophage‑targeted conjugates (MAC‑TACs) for pan‑solid tumor therapy
MOA_summary: A small‑molecule‑antibody conjugate binds a TAM‑restricted surface receptor, is internalized, and releases a cleavable MMAE payload within the tumor microenvironment; the non‑dividing TAMs act as drug depots, delivering cytotoxic payload to neighboring cancer cells while sparing normal tissues.
key_findings: RS5 (MMAE payload) achieved complete tumor regression in multiple subcutaneous solid‑tumor models, including doxorubicin‑resistant sarcoma PDX.
In intracranial glioblastoma models, RS5 extended median survival by ~79% without observable liver toxicity.
Dose escalation from 2 mg/kg to 10 mg/kg resulted in fibrotic tumor remnants with no viable cancer cells.
Weekly or bi‑weekly dosing maintained efficacy with stable body weight and normal hematology.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Potential MDR1‑mediated efflux observed in some resistant tumors.
Limited efficacy in brain tumors suggests blood‑brain barrier or vascular remodeling may affect delivery.

combination_strategies.rationale: MMAE sensitizes DNA damage, potentially enhancing radiotherapy efficacy.
combination_strategies.phenotype: ongoing preclinical studies

open_questions: What is the identity of the TAM‑specific receptor and its expression in normal tissues?
Can MAC‑TACs be combined with checkpoint inhibitors to overcome residual immunosuppression?

talk_title: Collagen‑binding anti‑CTLA‑4 antibodies for recurrent glioblastoma

targets.dependency_context: recurrent GBM with collagen I/III up‑regulation

biomarkers.name: Collagen I
biomarkers.finding: significantly up‑regulated in recurrent GBM vs normal brain

biomarkers.name: Collagen III
biomarkers.finding: significantly up‑regulated in recurrent GBM vs normal brain

MOA_summary: A von Willebrand factor A3 collagen‑binding domain fused to anti‑CTLA‑4 directs the antibody to collagen‑rich regions of recurrent GBM, increasing intratumoral accumulation, depleting exhausted CD8⁺ T cells and reducing Tregs, thereby extending survival in mouse models.
key_findings: Collagen I/III are among the top up‑regulated genes in recurrent GBM compared with primary tumor and normal brain.
CBD anti‑CTLA‑4 shows >5‑fold higher tumor accumulation than wild‑type anti‑CTLA‑4 with no brain accumulation.
In orthotopic GBM mice, CBD anti‑CTLA‑4 increased median survival by 140% and reduced tumor size by day 10.
Ex vivo GBM slice cultures treated with CBD ipilimumab showed significant reduction of intratumoral Tregs.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Partial loss of CD8⁺ T‑cell numbers observed; mechanism unclear.
Potential off‑target liver accumulation noted; ongoing toxicity assessment.

combination_strategies.rationale: CBD anti‑CTLA‑4 may prime T cells for checkpoint blockade synergy.
combination_strategies.phenotype: modest survival benefit in preclinical combos

open_questions: Does collagen binding affect antibody Fc‑mediated functions?
Can this approach be generalized to other checkpoint inhibitors?

talk_title: DLL3‑CD2‑CD3 trispecific T‑cell engager with enhanced potency

biomarkers.name: DLL3 expression
biomarkers.finding: required for trispecific TCE activity

models.name: human PBMC‑engrafted mice with SHP77 tumors

MOA_summary: A trispecific antibody simultaneously engages DLL3 on SCLC cells, CD3 for primary T‑cell activation, and CD2 for costimulatory signaling, resulting in enhanced T‑cell proliferation, survival, and cytotoxicity without antigen‑independent activation or T‑cell fratricide.
key_findings: Trispecific TCE shows >100‑fold potency increase over tarlatamab in vitro across multiple donors.
Enhanced T‑cell proliferation and survival observed at sub‑nanomolar concentrations.
In vivo, 5/7 mice achieved complete tumor eradication versus 2/6 with tarlatamab.
No T‑cell bridging or fratricide detected; activation is antigen‑dependent.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Tarlatamab lost activity after serial tumor challenges, whereas trispecific maintained cytotoxicity.
open_questions: Will the CD2 costimulatory domain increase CRS severity in humans?
Can the trispecific format be adapted to other tumor antigens?

talk_title: CBP/P300 bromodomain inhibition synergizes with PARP inhibition in prostate cancer

speaker.name: Orly Richter
speaker.affiliation: Shafi Lab, Center for Prostate Disease Research

biomarkers.name: BRCA1 loss (chr17 deletion)
biomarkers.finding: associated with ex vivo response to CCS1477 + olaparib

models.name: PDEs from 8 African‑American prostate cancer patients

MOA_summary: CBP/P300 bromodomain inhibition down‑regulates homologous recombination and other DNA‑repair pathways, sensitizing CRPC cells to PARP inhibition; combined treatment forces reliance on error‑prone repair, leading to synergistic loss of viability.
key_findings: CBP/P300 are amplified in metastatic CRPC and correlate with poor survival.
CCS1477 suppresses DDR gene expression, especially HR genes, in CRPC cell lines.
Synergy observed between CCS1477 and all four FDA‑approved PARP inhibitors across multiple prostate cancer lines.
In patient‑derived explants, the combination reduced Ki‑67 positivity more than either agent alone in responsive samples.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Non‑responders lacked CNV alterations; mechanism of resistance under investigation.

combination_strategies.rationale: CBP/P300 inhibition impairs HR, creating synthetic lethality with PARP blockade.
combination_strategies.phenotype: moderate to high synergy in viability assays; enhanced Ki‑67 suppression ex vivo

open_questions: Which genomic biomarkers predict response to the combination?
Can the regimen be combined with immune checkpoint blockade?

talk_title: 3D bioprinted cancer models for drug screening

speaker.name: Aurelie Cadoe
speaker.affiliation: Cancer Research Center of Lyon / CRO Anticeneo

models.name: BT474 (HER2+ breast) and SK‑OV‑3 (HER2+ ovarian) constructs
models.genotype_or_key_features: bioink with cancer cells, printed lattice, up to 40 days culture

MOA_summary: Layer‑by‑layer extrusion of cell‑laden bioink creates reproducible 3D tumor constructs that recapitulate in vivo gene expression and drug penetration kinetics, enabling more predictive assessment of chemotherapeutics and ADCs compared with 2D cultures.
key_findings: 3D bioprinted models cluster transcriptionally with in vivo tumors, unlike 2D cultures.
IC₅₀ values for Taxol and TDM‑1 are higher in 3D models, reflecting reduced drug penetration.
Co‑culture with fibroblasts or adipocytes further decreases drug sensitivity.
Intratumoral paclitaxel concentrations in 3D models peak at ~8 h, mirroring mouse plasma kinetics.
open_questions: Can vascular channels be integrated to model perfusion and immune cell infiltration?
How do drug uptake kinetics correlate with long‑term cytotoxic outcomes?

talk_title: Lysosomal enzyme legumain drives liver metastasis in pancreatic cancer

models.name: KPC (KPCL) mouse PDAC line with lysotag
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS G12D; TP53 R172H; lysotag engineered

models.name: orthotopic pancreas implantation
models.genotype_or_key_features: mouse PDAC cells injected into pancreas

models.name: intrasplenic liver metastasis model
models.genotype_or_key_features: mouse PDAC cells injected via spleen to seed liver

MOA_summary: Loss of the lysosomal asparaginyl endopeptidase legumain impairs maturation of cathepsin L, leading to enlarged lysosomes and secretion of immature cathepsin L, which remodels extracellular collagen and disrupts the metastatic niche; this creates a selective dependency for legumain in liver metastases but not in primary pancreatic tumors.
key_findings: Legumain protein is highly enriched in lysosomes of liver metastatic PDAC tumors compared with primary pancreas tumors (proteomics of LysoIP samples).
CRISPR‑mediated knockout of legumain does not affect orthotopic pancreatic tumor growth (tumor weight unchanged) but reduces liver metastatic tumor weight by >70% (n=8 per group).
Legumain loss causes enlarged lysosomal vesicles and accumulation of the pro‑form of cathepsin L, indicating defective cathepsin maturation.
Legumain‑deficient cells secrete increased single‑chain cathepsin L; this secretion is blocked by cathepsin inhibitor E64d.
In vitro collagen degradation assays show >50% reduction of fluorescent collagen area in legumain‑KO cultures by day 7, rescued by E64d.

In vivo legumain‑KO liver metastases display markedly reduced collagen I/III/V deposition (mass‑spec quantification) despite similar tumor size, suggesting excessive extracellular cathepsin activity remodels the niche.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Legumain dependency is selective for liver metastatic sites; primary pancreatic growth is unaffected.
open_questions: What transcriptional program drives legumain up‑regulation specifically in liver‑seeded tumor cells?
Which extracellular substrates of secreted cathepsin L are critical for niche disruption in the liver?

talk_title: Stromal taurine production and SLC66A6 uptake drive AML progression and venetoclax resistance

targets.dependency_context: osteolineage stromal cells in AML bone marrow niche

biomarkers.name: SLC66A6 expression
biomarkers.finding: High SLC66A6 levels in patient AML samples correlate with venetoclax resistance (p<0.01, n=112).

models.name: primary AML PDX in NSG mice
models.genotype_or_key_features: shRNA knockdown of SLC66A6 in AML blasts

models.name: patient MSC osteogenic cultures + autologous AML blasts
models.genotype_or_key_features: CDO1 shRNA vs control in MSCs

MOA_summary: Osteolineage stromal cells up‑regulate CDO1, producing taurine that is secreted into the bone‑marrow plasma; AML blasts import taurine via the SLC66A6 transporter, which activates mTOR signaling and sustains glycolysis, promoting proliferation and venetoclax resistance; genetic or pharmacologic inhibition of CDO1 or SLC66A6 impairs AML growth while sparing normal CD34+ cells.
key_findings: CDO1 expression is induced in osteolineage cells during AML progression and is detectable in patient bone‑marrow biopsies (IHC, n=24).
MSC‑specific CDO1 deletion (Prx1‑Cre; CDO1^fl/fl) prolongs median survival of AML‑bearing mice from 22 to 38 days (p=0.004, n=10 per group).
SLC66A6 knockdown in primary AML blasts reduces in vivo engraftment in NSG mice by >80% (n=6), with no impact on normal CD34+ hematopoietic progenitors.
High SLC66A6 expression in patient cohorts associates with venetoclax‑resistant disease (HR 2.1, 95% CI 1.4‑3.2).
Experimental SLC66A6 inhibitors synergize with venetoclax in vitro (Combination Index <0.7) and restore sensitivity in resistant AML cell lines.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: SLC66A6 inhibition shows a therapeutic window: AML blasts are dependent, whereas normal CD34+ cells are unaffected.

combination_strategies.rationale: SLC66A6 inhibition suppresses mTOR‑driven survival pathways that underlie venetoclax resistance
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic reduction of AML cell viability (≈70% kill at sub‑IC50 doses of each agent)

open_questions: How does intracellular taurine mechanistically activate mTOR signaling in AML blasts?
Can systemic taurine depletion affect normal hematopoiesis or other tissues?

talk_title: Aging‑driven fibroblast secretomes awaken dormant melanoma metastases

models.name: brain slice cultures from young and aged mice
models.genotype_or_key_features: genetically identical melanoma cells plated on slices

models.name: lung fibroblast cultures (young vs aged tumor‑bearing)
models.genotype_or_key_features: primary fibroblasts isolated from mouse lungs

MOA_summary: Aging remodels fibroblast secretomes; aged lung fibroblasts up‑regulate SFRP1, which acts as a ‘prince’s kiss’ to trigger dormant melanoma cells to exit dormancy and proliferate in the liver and lung; loss of SFRP1 signaling via neutralizing antibody blocks outgrowth of dormant cells; in the brain, aged microenvironment with leaky vasculature permits rapid melanoma invasion independent of SFRP1, while WNT5A signaling governs perivascular dormancy versus outgrowth.
readouts: immunofluorescence of collagen and fibroblast markers
key_findings: Primary melanoma tumors grow slower in aged mice but generate significantly more lung metastases (median metastatic nodules: 45 vs 12, p<0.001).
RNA‑seq of aged lung fibroblasts reveals >3‑fold up‑regulation of SFRP1 compared with young fibroblasts.
Neutralizing anti‑SFRP1 antibody administered after dissemination reduces outgrowth of dormant lung metastases in aged mice by 68% (n=8, p=0.002).

Aged tumor‑bearing lung fibroblasts become more motile, display reduced senescence markers (p16^INK4a), and secrete CXCL1 that recruits MDSCs; MDSC infiltration is 2.5‑fold higher in aged lungs (IHC, n=5).
Brain slice assays show melanoma cells invade aged brain tissue 3‑fold faster than young slices; invasion is independent of SFRP1 but correlates with vascular leakage.
WNT5A‑high melanoma cells preferentially associate with vasculature; loss of WNT5A is required for outgrowth of brain metastases, and SFRP1 antagonism restores growth of WNT5A‑low cells.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Aged fibroblast secretome specifically promotes metastasis without affecting primary tumor growth; SFRP1 blockade selectively impairs outgrowth in aged lungs.

combination_strategies.rationale: Blocks fibroblast‑derived awakening signal for dormant melanoma cells in aged lungs
combination_strategies.phenotype: Reduced metastatic nodule burden by ~70% in aged mice

open_questions: What upstream aging pathways drive SFRP1 up‑regulation in lung fibroblasts?
Can targeting CXCL1‑MDSC axis synergize with anti‑SFRP1 therapy?

talk_title: Advances in KRAS-targeted therapies for pancreatic cancer

biomarkers.name: KRAS G12C mutation
biomarkers.finding: ORR 33% in 21 pancreatic cancer patients (CRYSTAL-1 basket trial)

biomarkers.name: KRAS G12D mutation
biomarkers.finding: ORR ~30% with zoldonrasib monotherapy

models.name: Resolute 302
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS G12D mutant PDAC, phase III

clinical_data.population: duraxon rasib vs standard of care in phase III
duraxon rasib phase I PDAC cohort

MOA_summary: KRAS inhibitors that bind either the inactive (adagrasib) or active (deraxone rasip) conformations block downstream MAPK signaling, yielding response rates of 30-60% in KRAS-mutant pancreatic cancer; resistance is frequently driven by KRAS amplification, prompting combination strategies with chemotherapy and next‑generation KRAS degraders.
key_findings: Adagrasib achieved a 33% ORR in KRAS G12C PDAC (21 pts) versus historical 10‑15% second‑line chemotherapy response.
Deraxone rasip (RAS‑on inhibitor) showed a 36% ORR and median PFS 8.8 months in early‑phase PDAC patients.
Duraxon rasib combined with gem/nab‑paclitaxel yielded a 60% ORR and a hazard ratio of 0.4 for OS in the phase III Resolute 302 trial.
KRAS G12D inhibitors (zoldonrasib, setigrasib, INCB734) demonstrated ~30‑37% ORR with manageable grade 1‑2 toxicities.
KRAS amplification emerged as the most common resistance mechanism, informing ongoing combination studies.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: KRAS amplification observed in the majority of progressing patients on duraxon rasib.
Differential efficacy across KRAS alleles (G12R best prognosis, Q61 worst) noted in retrospective NGS analysis.

combination_strategies.rationale: Chemotherapy backbone may synergize with KRAS inhibition to improve response rates.
combination_strategies.phenotype: ORR increased from 50% (single) to 60% (combo) in frontline PDAC

combination_strategies.rationale: Targeting KRAS protein degradation may overcome resistance to small‑molecule inhibitors.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Single‑agent ORR ~37%; combination under early evaluation

open_questions: What is the optimal duration of KRAS inhibitor therapy to prevent emergence of KRAS amplification?
Can intermittent dosing schedules maintain efficacy while reducing toxicity?
How do KRAS G12D inhibitors perform in KRAS‑wild‑type PDAC with downstream pathway activation?

talk_title: Cryptic non‑canonical antigens as shared targets in pancreatic cancer

biomarkers.name: HLA‑A*02:01
biomarkers.finding: ~50% of identified cancer‑restricted cryptic antigens are shared among A*02:01+ patients

models.name: patient tumor biopsies (n≈30)
models.genotype_or_key_features: paired bulk tumor and organoid samples

MOA_summary: Mass‑spectrometry immunopeptidomics revealed hundreds of cancer‑restricted non‑canonical ORF‑derived peptides in PDAC; ex‑vivo priming identified immunogenic cryptic antigens, and TCR‑engineered T cells specific for these antigens demonstrated potent cytotoxicity against patient‑derived organoids and xenografts, establishing a shared off‑the‑shelf target class.
key_findings: Detected 8,000‑18,000 unique HLA‑I peptides per PDAC sample, including tens‑to‑hundreds of non‑canonical ORF peptides.
~35% of cryptic peptides showed no evidence of translation in >200 normal tissues, defining cancer‑restricted antigens.
~50% of cancer‑restricted cryptic antigens were shared by ≥2 HLA‑A*02:01+ patients, some across >70% of such patients.
~25% of tested cryptic peptides elicited CD8+ T‑cell responses in ex‑vivo priming assays, comparable to mutation‑derived neoantigens.
TCR‑engineered T cells targeting cryptic antigens mediated robust killing of PDAC organoids and delayed tumor growth in mouse models.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Single‑antigen TCR therapy delayed but did not eradicate tumors, suggesting antigen heterogeneity as a resistance mechanism.

combination_strategies.rationale: Targeting several shared antigens may overcome intra‑tumoral heterogeneity.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Near‑complete eradication of patient‑derived organoids in long‑term IncuCyte assays

open_questions: What is the mechanistic basis of non‑canonical ORF translation in PDAC cells?
Can cryptic antigen‑specific TCRs be safely administered in humans without off‑target toxicity?
How stable is antigen expression under therapeutic pressure?

talk_title: Functional role of CA19‑9 in pancreatic tumor microenvironment and therapeutic interception

targets.dependency_context: CA19-9 modified fibulin‑3 in PDAC cells
fibulin‑3‑mediated activation in CA19-9 high PDAC

biomarkers.name: Serum CA19-9
biomarkers.finding: Elevated CA19-9 predicts reduced disease‑free survival even in stage‑I resectable PDAC

biomarkers.name: Antigen‑presenting CAF (apCAF) frequency
biomarkers.finding: 5B1 antibody blockade reduces apCAF proportion and Treg infiltration in mouse tumors

models.name: PDAC organoid panel
models.genotype_or_key_features: CA19-9 positive vs negative, fibulin‑3 knockdown lines

models.name: 5B1 antibody phase I/II trial
models.genotype_or_key_features: Advanced PDAC, n≈30, 20% SD monotherapy, 27% PR with chemo

clinical_data.population: CA19-9 inducible KRAS^G12D mice vs controls

MOA_summary: CA19‑9 glycosylation of secreted fibulin‑3 enhances its binding to EGFR, driving IL‑1α and TGF‑β production that expands antigen‑presenting CAFs and Tregs, suppressing CD8+ T‑cell infiltration; blockade of CA19‑9 with the 5B1 antibody or knockdown of fibulin‑3 reduces this immunosuppressive cascade and restores anti‑tumor immunity.
key_findings: Inducible CA19‑9 expression in KRAS^G12D mice accelerates PanIN progression and reduces survival to a degree comparable to p53 loss.
Three‑day CA19‑9 exposure expands fibroblasts and drives IL‑1α/TGF‑β production, promoting antigen‑presenting CAF differentiation.
Fibulin‑3 is a CA19‑9‑modified protein that binds EGFR; its knockdown abolishes IL‑1α/TGF‑β up‑regulation and tumor growth.
5B1 anti‑CA19‑9 antibody reduces apCAF frequency, Treg infiltration, and collagen deposition, while increasing CD8+ T‑cell infiltration in vivo.
In a phase I/II trial, 5B1 monotherapy yielded 20% stable disease and 27% partial response when combined with chemotherapy.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: High circulating CA19‑9 levels can sequester therapeutic antibodies, limiting tumor penetration.

combination_strategies.rationale: Chemotherapy may synergize with CA19‑9 blockade by reducing tumor burden and exposing antigenic targets.
combination_strategies.phenotype: 27% PR rate versus 0% with antibody alone in early trial

combination_strategies.rationale: Reducing Treg and apCAF populations may enhance checkpoint blockade efficacy.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Preclinical models show increased CD8+ T‑cell infiltration after CA19‑9 blockade

open_questions: Which CA19‑9‑modified proteins besides fibulin‑3 are most critical for immunosuppression?
Can sustained CA19‑9 blockade overcome antibody sequestration in patients with very high serum levels?
What is the optimal timing of CA19‑9 inhibition relative to KRAS inhibitor or vaccine therapies?

talk_title: Forcing inflammation to initiate malignancy and drive tumor progression

speaker.name: Valerie Weaver
speaker.affiliation: University of Pennsylvania, Department of Cell and Developmental Biology

targets.dependency_context: fibrotic stroma in mouse polyoma model

biomarkers.name: Tumor mutational burden
biomarkers.finding: Higher TMB correlated with high fibrosis scores in patient cohorts

biomarkers.name: Phospho‑STAT3
biomarkers.finding: Regions of high stiffness showed markedly increased phospho‑STAT3 staining

biomarkers.name: Oxidized DNA (γ‑H2AX foci)
biomarkers.finding: Oxidized DNA foci enriched in stiff regions of both mouse models and high‑density human breast tissue

models.name: polyoma tumor model
models.genotype_or_key_features: LOX conditional knockout, GPX4 myeloid knockout

models.name: high vs low fibrosis breast tissue cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: BRCA1/2 prophylactic mastectomy samples, varying mammographic density

models.name: P53‑mutant breast epithelial cells in soft vs stiff matrices
models.genotype_or_key_features: P53 mutant, cultured in tunable cross‑linked hydrogels

clinical_data.value: 3‑fold increase in stiff vs soft matrices
