MOA_summary: Weaver demonstrated that increased matrix stiffness drives chronic inflammation via phospho‑STAT3 activation in myeloid cells, leading to elevated lipid peroxidation and accumulation of aldehyde‑derived DNA damage. Genetic or pharmacologic reduction of LOX or GPX4 lowers fibrosis, diminishes ROS‑derived aldehydes, and reduces DNA double‑strand breaks and metastatic burden in mouse models, linking mechanotransduction to mutational load and tumor progression.
key_findings: High fibrosis scores in breast tissue correlate with increased tumor mutational burden and DNA double‑strand breaks.
LOX conditional knockout or antibody blockade reduces collagen cross‑linking, tissue stiffness, and metastatic incidence in polyoma mouse models.
Stiff matrices elevate phospho‑STAT3 and pro‑tumor myeloid infiltration, which drives lipid peroxidation and aldehyde‑mediated DNA damage.
GPX4 loss in myeloid cells amplifies lipid aldehyde accumulation and DNA damage even in soft environments, promoting metastasis.
In prophylactic BRCA1/2 high‑density breast tissues, regions of greatest stiffness show heightened oxidative DNA lesions despite absence of overt tumor.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Stiffness‑induced DNA damage may select for clones with defective nucleotide excision repair, potentially limiting efficacy of DNA‑damage‑based therapies.

combination_strategies.rationale: Reduce collagen cross‑linking and tissue stiffness to lower inflammation‑driven DNA damage
combination_strategies.phenotype: 30‑40% reduction in metastatic lesions in polyoma model

combination_strategies.rationale: Test whether augmenting lipid aldehyde accumulation accelerates tumor progression
combination_strategies.phenotype: Increased DNA oxidation and metastasis even in soft matrices

open_questions: Does chronic stiffness directly induce specific mutational signatures beyond frameshift enrichment?
Can pharmacologic targeting of STAT3 or lipid aldehyde scavengers mitigate stiffness‑driven tumor initiation in high‑risk women?
What is the relative contribution of stiffness versus hormonal/genetic risk factors (e.g., BRCA status) in early DNA damage accumulation?

talk_title: Stiffness‑induced copy number variation across pan‑cancer

speaker.name: Dennis Discher
speaker.affiliation: University of Pennsylvania, Department of Bioengineering

targets.dependency_context: high collagen I expression correlates with CNV burden across TCGA tumors

models.name: H23 lung cancer cells (RFP‑lamin B1 reporter)
models.genotype_or_key_features: monoallelic GFP/RFP reporter for chromosome 5 loss

models.name: B16 melanoma spheroids in agarose gels
models.genotype_or_key_features: stiffness 0–30 kPa, Cre reporter for chromosome loss

models.name: TCGA pan‑cancer (≈10,000 patients)
models.genotype_or_key_features: collagen I transcript levels, CNV, LOH, LOI metrics

clinical_data.population: H23 cells in soft vs 30 kPa agarose

clinical_data.metric: CNV burden vs collagen I expression

MOA_summary: Discher showed that increasing extracellular matrix stiffness elevates heritable chromosome loss rates by mechanically constraining mitotic rounding, leading to spindle compression, prolonged mitosis, and micronucleus formation. This mechanotransduction‑driven mitotic error produces copy‑number losses (and later gains) that scale with collagen‑I‑driven tissue stiffness across multiple cancer types.
key_findings: Across TCGA, tumor collagen I transcript levels strongly correlate with total copy‑number variation burden.
In 3D spheroids, mutation (chromosome loss) rate per division increases ~10‑fold when stiffness rises from 0 to 30 kPa.
Stiff agarose gels suppress proliferation but increase per‑division mutation frequency, yielding a super‑Poisson variance pattern.
Myosin II knockdown exacerbates mutation rates in stiff environments, implicating contractile rounding forces in genome stability.
Micronuclei frequency is higher in stiff spheroids and in the cores of tumors, linking mechanical confinement to missegregation.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Stiffness‑induced chromosome loss preferentially affects tumor suppressor loci (e.g., chromosome 17p/TP53) before oncogene gains.

combination_strategies.rationale: Mimic spindle shortening observed under mechanical confinement
combination_strategies.phenotype: Similar increase in reporter loss as stiff 3D culture

combination_strategies.rationale: Test dependence of rounding forces on mutation rate
combination_strategies.phenotype: Further elevation of chromosome loss in stiff gels

open_questions: Do specific CNV patterns induced by stiffness preferentially target tumor suppressor genes versus oncogenes?
Can pharmacologic modulation of cell contractility reduce stiffness‑driven genomic instability in vivo?
How does stiffness‑induced CNV interact with existing driver mutations (e.g., KRAS, TP53) to shape tumor evolution?

talk_title: Spatial ecosystem analysis reveals mechanobiology‑driven relapse niches in breast cancer

targets.dependency_context: high‑risk ER+ HER2‑ breast tumors (8p12 amplification)
tumor‑dense niche zero associated with relapse

biomarkers.name: Niche‑zero cancer cell density
biomarkers.finding: High density predicts significantly worse 20‑year relapse‑free survival (HR ≈ 2.1, p<0.001)

biomarkers.name: Perivascular‑like cell / fibroblast co‑localization
biomarkers.finding: Enrichment in niche zero correlates with relapse (p=0.004)

biomarkers.name: Stiffness map (AFM‑CNN)
biomarkers.assay: atomic force microscopy with deep learning
biomarkers.finding: Top 20% stiffness values in TMAs double the risk of 20‑year relapse

models.name: 336 ER+ HER2‑ breast cancer TMA cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: 20‑year follow‑up, integrated copy‑number subtypes, spatial proteomics & transcriptomics

models.name: ER+ breast cancer organoids
models.genotype_or_key_features: used for functional validation of niche interactions

clinical_data.value: Patients in top 20% stiffness have 2.5‑fold higher relapse risk

MOA_summary: Curtis integrated multi‑modal spatial data (MIBI proteomics, CosMX transcriptomics, AFM‑derived stiffness maps, and glycomics) to define a tumor‑dense niche (niche zero) where cancer cells co‑localize with perivascular‑like cells and fibroblasts within a stiff, collagen‑rich microenvironment. This spatial ecosystem predicts poor long‑term outcomes independent of genomic risk classifiers, suggesting that mechanobiology‑driven ECM remodeling creates vascular niches that bypass VEGF signaling and foster relapse.
key_findings: Spatial niche zero (high cancer cell density) is a strong independent predictor of 20‑year relapse in ER+ HER2‑ breast cancer.
Perivascular‑like cells and fibroblasts co‑localize within niche zero, forming collagen‑rich vascular niches associated with relapse.
Stiffness measured by AFM‑CNN correlates with niche zero prevalence and doubles relapse risk in the top 20% stiffness group.
Integrative analysis shows that genomic high‑risk subtypes (focal CN amplifications) acquire distinct microenvironmental signatures, refining risk stratification.
Spatial glycomics reveals enrichment of COL6A1, COL3A1, COL1A2 in tumor‑dense regions, linking specific collagen isoforms to aggressive niches.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: ECM‑driven vascular niches appear VEGF‑independent, potentially conferring resistance to anti‑angiogenic therapies.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target VEGF‑independent vascular stabilization mediated by collagen‑rich niches
combination_strategies.phenotype: Hypothesized to disrupt niche‑zero perfusion and reduce relapse

combination_strategies.rationale: FGFR1 amplifications co‑occur with high‑risk ER+ tumors
combination_strategies.phenotype: Potentially synergistic with niche‑targeted therapies

open_questions: Can therapeutic modulation of tissue stiffness (e.g., LOX inhibition) remodel niche zero and improve outcomes?
What are the molecular drivers of VEGF‑independent vessel stabilization in collagen‑rich niches?
How generalizable are these spatial relapse signatures to other tumor types (e.g., ovarian, pancreatic)?

talk_title: Historical context and biological implications of extrachromosomal DNA in cancer

targets.dependency_context: tumor suppressor loss enabling ecDNA formation

MOA_summary: Michiel described how extrachromosomal DNA (ecDNA) forms via double-strand breaks or chromothripsis, lacks centromeres leading to random segregation, creates high copy-number heterogeneity, and drives rapid oncogene amplification, transcriptional hyperactivity, and therapeutic resistance.
key_findings: ecDNA particles are large (1–5 Mb), chromatinized but highly accessible, and absent in normal tissues.
ecDNA amplifies oncogenes (e.g., EGFR, MYC) leading to rapid copy-number changes upon drug treatment and withdrawal.
ecDNA formation is linked to p53 loss and can arise during high-grade dysplasia, predicting progression to invasive cancer.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: ecDNA enables rapid adaptation to targeted therapy by altering copy number, allowing switch to alternative oncogenes.
ecDNA suppresses cGAS‑STING signaling, contributing to immune evasion.
open_questions: What specific enzymatic dependencies create synthetic lethal opportunities for ecDNA‑positive tumors?
Can ecDNA be directly targeted or its segregation be disrupted therapeutically?

talk_title: Computational tools for ecDNA detection and structural reconstruction

models.name: AmpliconSuite pipeline
models.genotype_or_key_features: analysis of WGS data for focal amplifications

MOA_summary: Bafna presented AmpliconSuite, a graph‑based pipeline that parses whole‑genome sequencing to identify focal high‑copy amplifications, classify them as ecDNA cycles, breakage‑fusion‑bridge events, or linear amplicons, and reconstruct possible ecDNA structures using long‑read and short‑read data.
key_findings: AmpliconSuite has processed >60,000 WGS samples, providing first‑level ecDNA classification.
In lung cancer cohorts, ~20% of patients harbor computationally confident ecDNA, matching prior pan‑cancer estimates.
Long‑read data resolve ambiguous cycles, enabling validation of complex ecDNA structures (e.g., PC3DM, Polo3.20).
Experimental CRISPR‑Cas cuts and multi‑FISH confirm predicted ecDNA structures in cell lines.
open_questions: How to reliably distinguish reintegrated ecDNA (HSR) from native intrachromosomal amplifications at single‑cell resolution?
Can graph‑based signatures predict functional impact of ecDNA on drug resistance?

talk_title: Longitudinal whole‑genome analysis of ecDNA in TRACERx lung cancer cohort

targets.dependency_context: ERBB2 ecDNA in EGFR‑TKI resistant metastatic NSCLC

biomarkers.name: ecDNA presence
biomarkers.finding: Patients with ecDNA have significantly worse overall survival and higher extra‑thoracic metastasis rates

models.name: TRACERx early‑stage NSCLC (n≈130)
models.genotype_or_key_features: multi‑regional WGS, longitudinal primary and metastatic samples

clinical_data.metric: overall survival hazard ratio for ecDNA+ vs ecDNA‑

MOA_summary: Ghanayem showed that ecDNA, identified via AmpliconArchitect in longitudinal whole‑genome data, is frequently truncal in NSCLC, often harbors oncogenes (e.g., ERBB2) or immune‑modulatory genes, correlates with aggressive clinical phenotypes, rapid metastatic growth, and resistance to EGFR‑TKI therapy.
key_findings: ~20% of early‑stage NSCLC tumors contain ecDNA; prevalence rises to ~30% in metastatic lesions.
ecDNA is predominantly truncal, present across all sampled regions, and usually carries at least one oncogene.
Patients with ecDNA have worse overall survival, higher rates of extra‑thoracic metastasis, and faster lesion growth on imaging.
In a case of EGFR‑mutant NSCLC, ERBB2 ecDNA emerged at progression and was present in all resistant metastases, suggesting a role in TKI resistance.
Unichromosomal ecDNA dominate, but multi‑chromosomal ecDNA also occur, often co‑amplifying immune‑related genes.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: ecDNA emergence coincides with acquisition of resistance to EGFR inhibitors, likely via oncogene switching.
ecDNA harboring immune‑evasion genes (e.g., HLA pathway) associates with reduced T‑cell infiltration.

combination_strategies.rationale: non‑invasive detection of ecDNA emergence to guide early therapeutic intervention
combination_strategies.phenotype: potentially earlier identification of resistance mechanisms

open_questions: Can ecDNA be targeted directly or its segregation disrupted to overcome resistance?
What is the functional contribution of co‑amplified immune genes on ecDNA to tumor immune evasion?

talk_title: GV20-0251 anti-IGSF8 antibody phase 1/2 study in melanoma

speaker.name: Dr. Memi
speaker.affiliation: UCLA Angeles Clinic and Research Institute

biomarkers.name: IGSF8 expression
biomarkers.finding: High IGSF8 expression correlated with response (3/9 primary resistant melanoma patients responded)

biomarkers.name: PD-L1
biomarkers.finding: Inverse relationship with IGSF8; responders were PD-L1 negative

MOA_summary: GV20-0251 is an IgG4 anti-IGSF8 antibody that blocks IGSF8, enhancing NK cell cytotoxicity and dendritic cell antigen presentation, leading to tumor regression especially in IGSF8-high, PD-L1-negative melanoma.
key_findings: GV20-0251 monotherapy yielded an ORR of 33.3% and DCR of 77.8% in primary ICI‑resistant melanoma (n=9).
Responses were observed in patients with high tumor IGSF8 expression and low PD‑L1.
The antibody showed a favorable safety profile with no dose‑limiting toxicities and a 26‑day half‑life.
Pharmacodynamics demonstrated full target occupancy on circulating T cells at >3 mg/kg and increased intratumoral NK and CD8 T‑cell infiltration.

combination_strategies.rationale: Preclinical synergy showing enhanced tumor regression with combined IGSF8 blockade and PD‑1 inhibition.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced tumor regression in mouse models

open_questions: What is the receptor/ligand for IGSF8 on NK and T cells?
Will higher doses (10‑20 mg/kg) improve durability of responses?

talk_title: First-in-human Phase 1 study of PLN101-095 dual integrin inhibitor with pembrolizumab
tumor_types: head and neck squamous cell carcinoma

biomarkers.name: plasma interferon gamma
biomarkers.finding: Elevated after 14 days of monotherapy and associated with response

biomarkers.name: PD-L1 expression
biomarkers.finding: Increased after 14 days of monotherapy

models.name: PLN101-095 Phase 1 dose escalation
models.genotype_or_key_features: advanced solid tumors refractory to prior ICI

MOA_summary: PLN101-095 blocks integrin αVβ8/αVβ1, preventing local activation of TGF‑β in the tumor microenvironment, thereby reducing immunosuppression and enhancing IFN‑γ–driven anti‑tumor immunity, especially when combined with pembrolizumab.
key_findings: Overall ORR was 19% (4/16) with disease control rate of 56% in the dose‑escalation cohort.
In patients with secondary ICI resistance, ORR increased to 30% and DCR to 60%.
Responses included a complete response in cholangiocarcinoma and partial responses in NSCLC and melanoma.
Pharmacodynamics showed dose‑proportional exposure and >24‑hour IC75 coverage at ≥1000 mg BID.
Elevated plasma IFN‑γ and PD‑L1 after 14 days correlated with clinical responses.

combination_strategies.rationale: Integrin inhibition reduces TGF‑β–mediated immune suppression, potentially restoring sensitivity to PD‑1 blockade.
combination_strategies.phenotype: ORR 30% and DCR 60% in ICI‑resistant cohort

open_questions: Does baseline interferon‑γ signature predict response to PLN101‑095?
What is the impact of tumor MSS vs MSI status on efficacy?

talk_title: Phase I study of AMG 355 anti‑CCR8 antibody alone and with pembrolizumab

speaker.name: Dr. Marwan Fakhry
speaker.affiliation: City of Hope National Medical Center

biomarkers.name: CCR8 IPS score
biomarkers.finding: Median baseline IPS ~5%; no clear correlation with response

MOA_summary: AMG 355 is an afucosylated IgG1 anti‑CCR8 antibody engineered for enhanced FcγRIIIa binding, depleting CCR8‑positive intratumoral Tregs via ADCC, thereby reducing immunosuppression.
key_findings: AMG 355 monotherapy yielded a single partial response (melanoma) and limited activity overall.
Combination with pembrolizumab produced one complete response (gastric) and modest disease control (33%).
Pharmacodynamic analyses showed robust CCR8+ cell depletion in tumor biopsies across dose levels.
Safety profile was favorable with ≤5% grade 3 toxicities; infusion reactions were common but mild.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Limited efficacy may reflect heterogeneous CCR8 expression or insufficient Treg depletion in some tumors.

combination_strategies.rationale: CCR8‑mediated Treg suppression may limit PD‑1 blockade efficacy; depletion could synergize.
combination_strategies.phenotype: 1 CR and modest DCR improvement

open_questions: Why did CCR8 depletion not translate into higher response rates?
Is peripheral CCR8+ Treg depletion required for activity?

talk_title: Safety of prolonged atezolizumab exposure in alveolar soft part sarcoma

speaker.name: Dr. Alice Chen
speaker.affiliation: National Cancer Institute, Division of Cancer Treatment and Diagnosis

MOA_summary: Atezolizumab, an anti‑PD‑L1 antibody, provides durable checkpoint inhibition in ASPS, with long‑term treatment (>2 years) showing low incidence of new grade 2‑3 immune‑related adverse events.
key_findings: Extended atezolizumab treatment (>2 years) was feasible with only 3 of 17 patients developing new grade 2‑3 toxicities.
No grade 4‑5 immune‑related events were observed after prolonged exposure.
Median treatment duration reached 124 cycles in the longest‑treated patient.
Overall response rate remained at 37% consistent with earlier reports.
open_questions: What is the efficacy of re‑treating patients after a drug holiday?
Are there biomarkers to predict which ASPS patients benefit from extended therapy?

talk_title: Phase I study of DKY709 IKZF2 degrader alone and with PDR001

clinical_data.value: 0% below 10 mg, up to 28% at higher doses

MOA_summary: DKY709 is a cereblon‑recruiting molecular glue that selectively degrades IKZF2 (Helios) in regulatory T cells, reducing their suppressive function and enhancing effector T‑cell activity; combined with PD‑1 blockade (PDR001) to further potentiate anti‑tumor immunity.
key_findings: Robust IKZF2 degradation (>90%) observed in peripheral Tregs at doses ≥6 mg.
Monotherapy yielded 2 confirmed PRs and 1 CR in melanoma; combination added 3 PRs across tumor types.
Peripheral neuropathy was dose‑dependent, occurring mainly >10 mg but reversible with dose adjustments.
Recommended Phase 2 dose established at 6 mg QD for both monotherapy and combination.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Peripheral neuropathy emerged as a dose‑limiting toxicity without clear mechanistic link to IKZF2 degradation.

combination_strategies.rationale: IKZF2 degradation reduces Treg suppression, potentially synergizing with PD‑1 blockade.
combination_strategies.phenotype: ORR 9.7% and DCR 37.5% in combination arm

open_questions: Is the neuropathy directly related to IKZF2 loss in neurons?
Would lower intermittent dosing retain efficacy while reducing neuropathy?

talk_title: Phase 2 trial of tislelizumab with anti‑TIM‑3 and anti‑LAG‑3 in recurrent/metastatic HNSCC
tumor_types: head and neck squamous cell carcinoma

biomarkers.name: PD-L1 CPS
biomarkers.finding: Patients with CPS >20 had higher ORR (44.4%) with tislelizumab monotherapy

clinical_data.value: 3 in LAG‑3 arm, 2 in triple arm
5.4 months (TIM‑3 combo) vs 1.4 months (monotherapy) in CPS 1‑19 subgroup

MOA_summary: Tislelizumab blocks PD‑1, while cirgelicimab and LBA‑007 inhibit TIM‑3 and LAG‑3 respectively, aiming to overcome adaptive resistance by targeting multiple immune checkpoints in HNSCC.
key_findings: Overall ORR was similar across all arms (25‑27.5%).
Complete responses were observed only in LAG‑3‑containing arms (3 CRs).
In PD‑L1 low (CPS 1‑19) subgroup, combination arms showed numerically longer median PFS (5.4 mo) than monotherapy (1.4 mo).
Safety was comparable; most AEs were grade 1‑2 with no additive toxicity in combinations.

combination_strategies.rationale: Block TIM‑3 mediated exhaustion to enhance PD‑1 blockade.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Numerically longer PFS in low PD‑L1 subgroup

combination_strategies.rationale: Target LAG‑3 to further relieve T‑cell inhibition.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Three complete responses observed

open_questions: Why did combinations not improve ORR despite PFS signals?
Would enrichment for low PD‑L1 patients reveal clearer benefit?

talk_title: 10‑year survival and cfDNA methylation in melanoma brain metastases treated with ipilimumab + nivolumab

biomarkers.name: tumor fraction (TF)
biomarkers.finding: Patients with baseline TF below median had higher 10‑yr OS

biomarkers.name: t‑methylation score
biomarkers.finding: Low baseline t‑methylation score associated with longer OS; decreases at week 12 correlated with survival

models.name: NIBITANTO Phase III
models.genotype_or_key_features: melanoma with asymptomatic brain mets, no prior systemic therapy

MOA_summary: Combined CTLA‑4 and PD‑1 blockade synergistically activates T‑cells, leading to durable intracranial and systemic responses in melanoma brain metastases; cfDNA tumor fraction and methylation scores serve as surrogate biomarkers of long‑term survival.
key_findings: 10‑year OS was 32% with ipilimumab + nivolumab versus 30% with fuio‑mustine and 7% with ipilimumab + fuio‑mustine.
Melanoma‑specific 10‑yr OS reached 36% in the combination arm.
Low baseline tumor fraction and t‑methylation scores predicted superior survival.
Decreases in TF and t‑methylation at week 12 correlated with longer overall survival.

combination_strategies.rationale: Dual checkpoint blockade overcomes adaptive resistance and improves intracranial control.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Long‑term survival benefit in brain metastasis patients

open_questions: Can cfDNA TF and methylation scores be used prospectively to select patients for dual checkpoint therapy?
What is the mechanistic basis for the observed biomarker changes during treatment?

talk_title: Digital Tissue Twins: AI‑Powered Prediction of Single‑Cell Spatial Omics from H&E Slides

models.name: MD Anderson spatial transcriptomics
models.genotype_or_key_features: 23 LUAD patients, medium-resolution spatial gene expression

MOA_summary: Li's group developed AI models (iSTAR, iSCALE, AURORA) that learn the relationship between H&E morphology and spatial gene expression, using bulk RNA‑seq as a dynamic‑range constraint to predict single‑cell resolution transcriptomic maps and cell‑type annotations across whole‑slide images.
key_findings: AURORA achieved the highest Pearson correlation and lowest false‑positive rate among competing methods when predicting gene expression on held‑out LUAD samples (5 patients).
Virtual prediction of 4,000 TCGA LUAD slides reproduced known tumor pathology patterns (e.g., KRT8 expression).
Mature TLS area quantified by AURORA correlated with improved patient survival in TCGA LUAD.
Higher tumor‑TLS co‑localization was associated with better cancer survival.
Spatial cuff structures identified by AURORA predicted poorer survival, recapitulating findings from Wang et al. Cancer Cell.
open_questions: How will AURORA perform on treated or therapy‑resistant tumor samples where dynamic range may shift?
Can virtual predictions be extended to infer subclonal genetic alterations?

talk_title: Rosie: Generative AI for In‑Silico Spatial Proteomics from H&E Slides
MOA_summary: Rosie trains a hierarchical deep‑learning model on paired H&E and CODEX images to impute multiplex protein expression at 8 µm resolution, enabling rapid, low‑cost generation of in‑silico spatial proteomics maps.
key_findings: Rosie generates in‑silico CODEX images for up to 50 protein markers in minutes for <$1 cost, matching key structures seen in experimental CODEX.
Performance across external validation sites remained robust, with Pearson correlations ranging from high (99th percentile) to moderate (25th percentile).
Imputed proteomic maps enable accurate segmentation of tissue structures (stroma, tumor epithelium, endothelium) comparable to ground‑truth CODEX.
open_questions: How does Rosie generalize to novel tissue types or rare biomarkers not represented in the training set?
What are the limits of sub‑cellular resolution for accurate protein quantification?

talk_title: Virtual Biotech and AI‑Driven Spatial Omics for Therapeutic Strategy Design

biomarkers.name: CD276 expression
biomarkers.finding: high CD276 expression in fibroblasts associated with poorer survival and reduced therapy response

models.name: Virtual Biotech AI agents
models.genotype_or_key_features: integrates single‑cell atlases, spatial transcriptomics, genetics, clinical trial data

clinical_data.population: lung cancer patients with high CD276

MOA_summary: The virtual biotech platform uses AI agents to integrate multi‑omic and clinical data, identifying CD276 (B7H3) as a fibroblast‑derived immune checkpoint; agents propose an ADC (CD276‑DXd) strategy, later validated by an FDA breakthrough designation for a similar ADC.
key_findings: AI agents identified CD276 highly expressed in tumor‑associated fibroblasts in lung cancer.
Spatial analysis showed CD276‑high fibroblast neighborhoods depleted of immune cells.
High CD276 expression correlated with poorer overall and disease‑specific survival.
Agents independently designed a CD276‑DXd ADC strategy, later mirrored by Merck's FDA‑breakthrough ADC.

combination_strategies.rationale: Target CD276 on fibroblasts to deliver cytotoxic payload directly to immunosuppressive niche
combination_strategies.phenotype: preclinical efficacy predicted; clinical breakthrough designation achieved

open_questions: Can AI‑derived single‑cell features (tau score, bimodality) predict trial success across other indications?
How robust is the CD276 fibroblast target across diverse ethnic and molecular subpopulations?

talk_title: Multiplexed functional assay of BRCA2 missense variants in HAP1 cells

targets.dependency_context: DNA repair viability assay in HAP1 haploid cells

models.name: HAP1
models.genotype_or_key_features: haploid human leukemia line, BRCA2 endogenous locus edited

MOA_summary: A multiplexed assay of variant effects (MAVE) was performed in haploid HAP1 cells where BRCA2 missense variants were introduced at the endogenous locus; cells were cultured and viability ratios (day 5 vs day 14) were sequenced to generate functional scores that distinguish loss‑of‑function (pathogenic strong) from benign variants, enabling calibration to ACMG classification and risk association.
key_findings: 502 missense variants (≈10‑11% of all missense) were classified as pathogenic strong, far exceeding the 30‑50 pathogenic missense in ClinVar.
Sensitivity and specificity of the BRCA2 MAVE against known standards were 93‑95% across multiple metrics.
Integration of functional scores with ACMG criteria reduced VUS from ~7,000 to 608 variants (≈90% reclassification).
Combined analysis of two independent BRCA2 MAVEs (HAP1 and mouse ESC) using an integrated VarCall model captured ~95% of variants and further reduced VUS to 396.
Case‑control analysis of pathogenic‑strong missense variants yielded an odds ratio of 4.5 for breast/ovarian cancer risk, comparable to truncating mutations (OR ~5.5).
Demonstrated that combining MAVEs improves variant classification accuracy and clinical utility.
open_questions: How do functional scores translate across different cell types (e.g., primary mammary epithelial cells vs HAP1)?
Can replication‑fork specific assays be integrated to capture BRCA2 functions beyond homologous recombination?

talk_title: Saturation MAVE of Lynch syndrome mismatch‑repair genes and clinical risk integration

models.name: haploid human cell line (e.g., HAP1)
models.genotype_or_key_features: knockout of each MMR gene, transduced with variant libraries

models.name: All of Us biobank cohort
models.genotype_or_key_features: population‑scale genotype data linked to colorectal and endometrial cancer outcomes

MOA_summary: Saturation MAVE libraries covering >73,000 missense variants across the four Lynch‑syndrome mismatch‑repair genes were introduced into haploid cells; 6‑thioguanine treatment selects for loss‑of‑function variants, and deep sequencing quantifies variant depletion, producing functional scores that correlate with clinical pathogenicity and cancer risk.
key_findings: Generated functional maps for >73,000 missense variants across MSH2, MLH1, MSH6, and PMS2.
Proline substitution patterns validated assay performance, showing expected loss‑of‑function in ordered protein regions.
47 of 48 clinically classified missense variants (independent of functional evidence) were concordant with MAVE results, providing strong evidence for assay reliability.
Retrospective reclassification of 10 individuals in Ambry Genetics database as Lynch syndrome carriers based on MAVE data.
Population‑scale analysis linked loss‑of‑function missense scores to increased colorectal cancer risk, recapitulating known penetrance differences (high for MLH1/MSH2, lower for MSH6/PMS2).
AlphaMissense predictor overestimates pathogenicity (≈40% vs 12% loss‑of‑function by MAVE) highlighting need for calibration.
Pairwise double‑mutant screens revealed epistatic interactions where a mildly deleterious variant (e.g., K618A in MLH1) amplifies the functional impact of other missense changes.
open_questions: How to systematically capture and interpret phased double‑mutant haplotypes in clinical sequencing data?
What is the contribution of non‑viability phenotypes (e.g., replication‑fork stress) to pathogenicity that may be missed by the 6‑TG assay?
Can functional scores be integrated with polygenic risk scores to refine individual cancer risk predictions?

talk_title: Inducing viral mimicry to turn cold small cell lung cancer hot via DHX9, TREX1 and spliceosome inhibition

speaker.name: Israel Cañadas
speaker.affiliation: Fox Chase Cancer Center, Assistant Professor

biomarkers.name: PD-L1
biomarkers.finding: PD-L1 expression induced after DHX9 or spliceosome inhibition in vitro

biomarkers.name: CD8 T cell infiltration
biomarkers.finding: Increased CD8+ T cells in tumors with DHX9 knockdown or spliceosome inhibitor treatment

models.name: immunocompetent small cell lung cancer model
models.genotype_or_key_features: DHX9 knockdown or TREX1 knockout, treated with anti-PD-1

models.name: human small cell lung cancer cell lines (e.g., HCT9-AR)

MOA_summary: Inhibition or genetic loss of DHX9, TREX1, or spliceosome components leads to accumulation of dsRNA, DNA‑RNA hybrids and crRNA, activating MDA5/MAVS and cGAS‑STING pathways, inducing type I interferon, cytokine production and necroptosis, which remodels the tumor microenvironment, increases CD8+ T‑cell infiltration and sensitizes small cell lung cancer to anti‑PD‑1 therapy.
key_findings: DHX9 knockdown in small cell lung cancer cells increases dsRNA and DNA‑RNA hybrids, activating STING and MDA5 pathways.
DHX9 loss reduces tumor growth and enhances CD8+ T‑cell infiltration in immunocompetent mice.
Combination of DHX9 knockdown with anti‑PD‑1 dramatically improves tumor control and survival versus anti‑PD‑1 alone.
Spliceosome inhibition (Pladienolide B) induces crRNA accumulation, ZBP1‑dependent necroptosis, and tumor regression.
Spliceosome inhibitor plus anti‑PD‑1 shows synergistic tumor suppression; effect is lost in CBP1‑deficient mice.
TREX1 is up‑regulated in chemotherapy‑resistant SCLC cells; its knockdown restores interferon signaling and sensitizes cells to cisplatin and T‑cell activation.
Viral mimicry can be chronic, leading to immune evasion; targeting TREX1 or DHX9 may overcome this tolerance.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Chronic viral mimicry can induce immune evasion via p53 loss and tolerance to cytosolic nucleases.
TREX1 up‑regulation in chemo‑resistant cells suppresses cGAS‑STING signaling, contributing to therapy resistance.

combination_strategies.rationale: Viral mimicry increases antigen presentation and PD‑L1 expression, making tumors more responsive to checkpoint blockade.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression and prolonged survival in mouse SCLC models

combination_strategies.rationale: TREX1 knockdown restores cGAS‑STING activation and DNA damage response, sensitizing cells to DNA‑damaging agents.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced cisplatin efficacy in TREX1‑deficient SCLC xenografts

open_questions: What determines the balance between dsRNA and crRNA formation after spliceosome inhibition?
How does viral mimicry in tumor‑infiltrating lymphocytes affect autoimmunity or systemic immunity?
Can CBP1 expression levels predict response to spliceosome‑targeted therapies?

talk_title: Epigenetic regulation of viral mimicry and its impact on immunotherapy response

targets.alteration: wild-type vs R175H mutant vs null

biomarkers.name: type I interferon signature
biomarkers.finding: Induced by DNMTi and HDACi across multiple tumor models

biomarkers.name: PD‑L1 expression
biomarkers.finding: Upregulated after epigenetic therapy, correlating with improved checkpoint response

biomarkers.name: M2 macrophage abundance
biomarkers.finding: Reduced after combined DNMTi + ADAR1 knockdown in ovarian cancer model

models.name: DNMTi + anti‑PD‑1 in NKT cell lymphoma (phase II)
high‑grade serous ovarian cancer survivors
models.genotype_or_key_features: long‑term vs short‑term survivors, all TP53 mutant

clinical_data.value: significantly longer in high TE expression cluster (C1)
clinical_data.population: high‑grade serous ovarian cancer long‑term survivors

clinical_data.value: significantly longer in high TE expression cluster
improved when DNMTi combined with anti‑PD‑1 in melanoma preclinical model

MOA_summary: DNMT and HDAC inhibition demethylates and opens chromatin, reactivating transposable element transcription that forms dsRNA or DNA‑RNA hybrids, which are sensed by MDA5/MAVS and cGAS‑STING pathways, driving type I interferon signaling, upregulating antigen presentation and PD‑L1, and reshaping the tumor microenvironment to enhance checkpoint blockade efficacy; ADAR1 editing dampens this response, and TP53 status modulates TE expression and drug responsiveness.
readouts: IHC for PD‑L1 and immune infiltrates
key_findings: DNMT inhibitors reactivate endogenous retroviruses, generating dsRNA that triggers innate immunity.
Combining DNMTi with HDACi amplifies TE transcription and interferon responses.
ADAR1 knockdown synergizes with DNMTi to improve survival and shift macrophage polarization in ovarian cancer models.
Wild‑type TP53 binds to LTRs and enhances TE transcription upon DNMTi; TP53 loss reduces DNA methylation globally, increasing TE expression.
TP53‑null cells show the strongest interferon response to DNMTi, whereas R175H mutants are less responsive.
High TE expression in long‑term ovarian cancer survivors correlates with increased immune infiltration and better survival, independent of BRCA/HRD status.
Clinical trials of DNMTi + checkpoint blockade show mixed results; notable activity in NKT cell lymphoma and melanoma.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Chronic TE expression can lead to interferon desensitization and immune evasion.
ADAR1-mediated RNA editing reduces dsRNA immunogenicity, limiting viral mimicry efficacy.

combination_strategies.rationale: Viral mimicry upregulates antigen presentation and PD‑L1, sensitizing tumors to checkpoint blockade.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved tumor control and survival in preclinical melanoma and ovarian models

combination_strategies.rationale: HDAC inhibition synergizes with DNMTi to further increase TE transcription.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Higher interferon scores than DNMTi alone

combination_strategies.rationale: Blocks RNA editing that dampens dsRNA sensing, enhancing interferon response.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Extended survival and M1 macrophage polarization in ovarian cancer model

open_questions: How does TP53 status mechanistically influence DNMTi‑induced TE activation beyond methylation changes?
What are the determinants of chronic versus acute viral mimicry and their impact on therapy resistance?
Can TE expression be used prospectively to stratify patients for epigenetic‑immunotherapy combinations?

talk_title: Viral mimicry enhances cancer vaccine efficacy via interferon‑dependent tumor remodeling

targets.dependency_context: tumor cells for viral mimicry induction

biomarkers.name: type I interferon score
biomarkers.finding: Azacitidine induces strong IFN response in tumor cells, dependent on MAVS/STING

biomarkers.name: MHC class I expression
biomarkers.finding: Increased on tumor cells only when viral mimicry is intact

biomarkers.name: vaccine‑specific CD8+ T‑cell clones
biomarkers.finding: Clonal expansion in TME requires tumor‑intrinsic viral mimicry

models.name: B16‑F10 melanoma
models.genotype_or_key_features: wild‑type, MAVS‑/‑ or STING‑/‑ tumors; vaccinated with mRNA neoantigen

MOA_summary: Azacitidine demethylates tumor DNA, reactivating transposable elements that generate dsRNA and DNA‑RNA hybrids, activating MDA5/MAVS and cGAS‑STING pathways, leading to type I interferon production, upregulation of MHC‑I and PD‑L1, and remodeling of myeloid populations; this creates a permissive microenvironment for vaccine‑elicited T cells to infiltrate, expand, and mediate tumor control, an effect lost when MAVS or STING are knocked out in tumor cells.
key_findings: Azacitidine strongly induces transposable element transcription and a type I interferon response in B16 melanoma, dependent on MAVS and STING.
Vaccination alone fails therapeutically, but combined with azacitidine leads to tumor control and improved survival across multiple tumor models.
The combination increases tumor cell MHC‑I expression and expands vaccine‑specific CD8+ T‑cell clones within the TME.
MAVS/STING knockout tumors do not respond to the combination, confirming the necessity of tumor‑intrinsic viral mimicry.
Single‑cell analysis reveals increased cDC1 dendritic cells and reduced immunosuppressive Arg1+ macrophages only when viral mimicry is intact.
Combination therapy promotes proliferation of CD8+ T cells and clonal expansion of vaccine‑specific TCRs.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Tumors lacking MAVS or STING are resistant to the vaccine‑azacitidine synergy, highlighting dependence on innate sensing pathways.

combination_strategies.rationale: Viral mimicry upregulates antigen presentation and creates an inflamed TME, enabling vaccine‑elicited T cells to function.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression and survival benefit in multiple syngeneic models

combination_strategies.rationale: Azacitidine‑induced PD‑L1 upregulation may sensitize tumors to PD‑1 inhibition.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved responses in NKT cell lymphoma patients (clinical observation)

open_questions: Can STING agonists substitute for azacitidine to achieve similar vaccine synergy?
What is the durability of myeloid repolarization after transient azacitidine treatment?
Will this strategy translate to human tumors with heterogeneous MAVS/STING status?

session_title: Where Expected Pathways, Innovation, Safety, and Patient Access Collide
talk_title: Clinical advances and patient experiences with KRAS inhibitors

speaker.name: Ignacio Garrido Laguna
speaker.affiliation: Moffitt Cancer Center, Chair of the Department of Early Therapeutics Development

biomarkers.name: KRAS mutation status
biomarkers.finding: Patients with KRAS G12C, G12D, G12V, or KRAS amplification were enrolled in respective trials

biomarkers.name: ctDNA KRAS amplification
biomarkers.finding: Emergence of KRAS amplification detected by ctDNA was proposed as a resistance mechanism

models.name: FMC6236 first‑in‑human study
models.genotype_or_key_features: KRAS‑mutant metastatic pancreatic cancer, patient enrolled Nov 2022, low‑dose level

clinical_data.metric: response rate in endometrial G12D cohort

MOA_summary: KRAS inhibitors bind either the GDP‑bound or GTP‑bound conformations of mutant KRAS, blocking downstream MAPK signaling and inducing tumor regression. Pan‑KRAS agents target multiple KRAS alleles, while allele‑specific inhibitors (e.g., QTX‑3034) selectively inhibit G12D. Tricomplex inhibitors form a ternary complex with KRAS and an adaptor, achieving potent inhibition across alleles. Early clinical data show high response and disease‑control rates in KRAS‑driven pancreatic, colorectal, and gastro‑esophageal cancers.
key_findings: A patient with metastatic KRAS‑mutant pancreatic cancer achieved a complete response after enrollment in the FMC6236 first‑in‑human study (Nov 2022) and remains in remission.
Early‑phase KRAS inhibitor trials report overall response rates around 30% and disease‑control rates of 80‑90% in pancreatic cancer, markedly exceeding historical chemotherapy control rates.
Pan‑KRAS inhibitors show activity in KRAS‑amplified gastro‑esophageal cancers but face pharmacokinetic challenges and limited efficacy against GTP‑bound KRAS.
Allele‑specific G12D inhibitor QTX‑3034 demonstrated a signal in a small endometrial cancer cohort, highlighting the need for mutation‑focused development.
Resistance mechanisms such as KRAS amplification can be detected by ctDNA, suggesting adaptive dosing or combination strategies.
resistance_or_selectivity_notes: Pan‑KRAS agents are limited to the GDP‑bound form, resulting in reduced activity against GTP‑bound KRAS and necessitating combination with upstream EGFR, SHP2, or SOS1 inhibitors.
Higher IC50 values for GTP‑bound KRAS limit efficacy of many pan‑KRAS inhibitors against certain mutations (e.g., G12R).
Emergence of KRAS amplification in ctDNA is proposed as a resistance mechanism that may require dose escalation of pan‑KRAS degraders.

combination_strategies.rationale: Pan‑KRAS inhibitors target GDP‑bound KRAS; EGFR blockade reduces upstream signaling that can reactivate KRAS.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Synergistic tumor regression in preclinical models; proposed for clinical testing.

combination_strategies.rationale: Inhibits upstream RAS activation, complementing pan‑KRAS inhibition of GDP‑bound KRAS.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Enhanced pathway suppression in cell line studies.

combination_strategies.rationale: Blocks nucleotide exchange, increasing the proportion of KRAS in GDP‑bound state for pan‑KRAS agents.
combination_strategies.phenotype: Improved response rates in early‑phase combination cohorts.

open_questions: Do we need additional KRAS inhibitors that are not differentiated pre‑clinically from existing agents?
How can platform or master‑protocol trials with shared control arms be implemented without compromising sponsor interests?
What surrogate endpoints (e.g., ctDNA clearance) can reliably replace overall survival in transformative KRAS‑targeted therapies?
